hazard function for the *j*th group. In this model, the coefficients of
the *p* explanatory variables, $\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\ldots,\beta_{p}$,
are constant over the *g* groups, but there is a different baseline
hazard function for each group. This is a *stratified Cox regression
model* in which the *g* groups define the separate strata. These models
are considered in greater detail in [Chapter 11](#chapter11.xhtml).

On fitting the stratified model, the corresponding estimated survivor
function for the *i*th patient in the *j*th group is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{ij}(t) = \left\{ {{\hat{S}}_{0j}(t)} \right\}^{\exp({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij})},}
\end{array}$$

where ${\hat{S}}_{0j}(t)$ is the estimated baseline survivor function
for individuals in the *j*th group. If the groups can be assumed to act
proportionately on the hazard function, a common baseline hazard would
be fitted and a group effect included in the model. Then, $h_{0j}(t)$ is
replaced by $\exp(g_{j})h_{0}(t)$, where *g*~*j*~ is the effect of the
*j*th group. In general, it is less restrictive to allow the group
effects to vary over time by using a stratified model.

The risk-adjusted survivor function for each group can be found from the
stratified model by averaging the values of the estimated survivor
functions at each of the event times, across []{#chapter3.xhtml_p98
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 98. " role="doc-pagebreak"}the individuals
in each group. The average risk-adjusted survivor function at time *t*
is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{j}(t) = \frac{1}{n_{j}}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n_{j}}{\hat{S}}_{ij}}(t),}
\end{array}$$

for individuals in the *j*th group.

An alternative approach is to average the values of each explanatory
variable for individuals in each group, and to then use these values to
estimate the group-specific survivor functions. Writing
${\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}}}_{j}$ for the vector of average values
of the *p* variables across the individuals in the *j*th group, the
corresponding estimated survivor function for an 'average individual' in
group *j* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\widetilde{S}}_{j}(t) = \left\{ {{\hat{S}}_{0j}(t)} \right\}^{\exp({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'{\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}}}_{j})}.}
\end{array}$$

Although this approach is widely used, it can be criticised on a number
of grounds. Averaging the values of explanatory variables is not
appropriate for categorical variables. For example, suppose that the two
levels of a variable associated with the presence or absence of diabetes
are coded as 1 and 0, respectively, and that 10% of the individuals on
whom data are available are diabetic. Setting the average of the
indicator variable to be 0.1 does not lead to a meaningful
interpretation of the survivor function at this value of diabetic
status. Also, even with continuous explanatory variables, the set of
average values of the explanatory variables within a group may not
correspond to a realistic combination of values, and the survivor
function for an average individual may be very different from the
patterns of the individual survivor functions.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam3_16" role="doc-tip">

[Example 3.16]{.boxnum} [Comparison of two treatments for prostatic
cancer]{.boxht}

Consider the data from [Example 1.4](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_4) on the
survival times of 38 prostatic cancer patients in two treatment groups.
In [Example 3.6](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_6), the explanatory variables
*Size* and *Index* were found to affect the hazard of death, and so a
stratified Cox regression model that contains these two variables is
fitted. The estimated survivor function for the *i*th patient in the
*j*th treatment group, for *j* = 1 (placebo) and 2 (DES), is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{ij}(t) = \left\{ {{\hat{S}}_{0j}(t)} \right\}^{\exp({\hat{\eta}}_{ij})},}
\end{array}$$

where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\eta}}_{ij} = 0.0673\, Size_{ij} + 0.6532\, Index_{ij},}
\end{array}$$

and ${\hat{S}}_{0j}(t)$ is the estimated baseline survivor function for
the *j*th treatment group. Averaging the estimated survivor functions at
each event time over the patients in each group gives the risk-adjusted
survivor functions shown in [Figure
3.8](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_8){#chapter3.xhtml_b3fig3_8}. Also shown in
this figure are the unadjusted Kaplan-Meier estimates of the survivor
functions for each group.

<figure id="fig3_8" class="image">
<img src="images/fig3_8.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter3.xhtml_b3fig3_8" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">3.8</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">(i) Unadjusted and (ii) risk-adjusted
survivor functions for prostatic cancer patients on DES (<span
class="middle">.......</span>) and placebo (—).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

This figure shows how the risk adjustment process has diminished the
treatment difference. Of course, this is also seen by comparing the
unadjusted hazard ratio for a patient on DES relative to placebo (0.14)
with the corresponding value adjusted for tumour size and Gleason index
(0.33), although this latter analysis assumes proportional hazards for
the two treatments.

</aside>
:::
::::

:::::::: section
## 3.12 Concordance, predictive ability, and explained variation {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12 .head1}

Measures that summarise the agreement between observed survival times
and model-based predictions, and the extent to which explanatory
variables explain observed variability in survival data, quantify the
predictive power of a fitted model. Many such measures have been
proposed, and in this section, some of the most useful are described.

::: section
### 3.12.1 []{#chapter3.xhtml_p99 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 99. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Measures of concordance {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_1 .head2}

The agreement, or *concordance*, between the ranks of observed and
predicted survival times summarise the potential of a fitted model to
discriminate between individuals, by separating those with longer
survival times from those with shorter times. Measures of concordance
take values between 0 and 1, corresponding to perfect discordance and
perfect concordance, respectively. Values around 0.5 are obtained when a
model has no predictive ability, and models with a reasonable degree of
predictive ability will tend to have values greater than 0.7.

A particular measure of concordance is the *c*-statistic described by
[Harrell, Lee and Mark
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib168){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib168 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. This statistic is an estimate of the probability
that, for any two individuals, the one with the shortest survival time
is the one with the greatest hazard of death. To calculate this
statistic, consider all possible pairs of survival times, where either
both members of the pair have died, or where one member of the pair dies
before the censored survival time of the other. Pairs in which both
individuals have censored survival times, or where the survival time of
one exceeds the censored survival time of the other, are not included.
If in a pair where both individuals have died, the model-based predicted
survival time is greater for the individual who lived longer, the two
individuals are said to be *concordant*.

In a proportional hazards model, an individual in a pair who is
predicted to have the greatest survival time will be the one with the
lower hazard of death at a given time, the higher estimated survivor
function at a given time, or the lower value of the risk score. For
pairs where just one individual dies, and one individual has a time that
is censored after the survival time of the other member of the pair, the
individual with the censored time has survived longer than the other,
and so it can be determined whether the two members of such a pair are
concordant. The *c*-statistic is obtained by dividing the number of
concordant pairs by the number of all possible pairs being considered.

Since pairs where both individuals are censored, or where the censored
survival time of one occurs before the death time of the other, do not
contribute to its value, the *c*-statistic is affected by the pattern of
censoring. This considerable disadvantage is overcome in a statistic
proposed by [Gönen and Heller
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib151){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib151 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. Their measure of concordance is an estimate of
the probability that, for any two individuals, the survival time of one
exceeds the survival time of the other, conditional on the individual
with the longer survival time having the lower risk score.

[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p100 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 100. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}To obtain this statistic, let
${\hat{\eta}}_{i} = {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$
be the risk score for the *i*th individual. A pair of survival times,
$(T_{i},T_{j})$, say, is then concordant if the individual with the
lower risk score has the longer survival time. The probability of
concordance is then
$K = \text{P}(T_{i} > T_{j}\mid\eta_{i}\leqslant\eta_{j})$, and an
estimate of this concordance probability, $\hat{K}$, is the Gönen and
Heller measure of the predictive value of a model. This estimate is
given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{K} = \frac{2}{n(n - 1)}\underset{i < j}{\sum\sum}\left\{ {\frac{I\left\{ ({\hat{\eta}}_{j} - {\hat{\eta}}_{i}) < 0 \right\}}{1 + \exp({\hat{\eta}}_{j} - {\hat{\eta}}_{i})} + \frac{I\left\{ ({\hat{\eta}}_{i} - {\hat{\eta}}_{j}) < 0 \right\}}{1 + \exp({\hat{\eta}}_{i} - {\hat{\eta}}_{j})}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where the indicator function $I( \cdot )$ is such that
$I\left\{ X < 0 \right\} = 1$ if $X < 0$ and zero otherwise. In
addition, the standard error of $\hat{K}$ can be obtained, so that the
precision of $\hat{K}$ can be assessed, although details will not be
given here.
:::

::: section
### 3.12.2 Predictive ability {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_2 .head2}

A measure of the ability of a fitted model to separate individuals, or
groups of individuals, according to the values of the risk score, is the
*D*-statistic described by [Royston and Sauerbrei
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib327){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib327 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. This statistic also summarises the predictive
ability of a model and is closely related to the variance of the risk
scores.

To obtain this statistic, the values of the risk score of a fitted Cox
regression model,
${\hat{\eta}}_{i} = {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$,
are first ordered from smallest value to largest, so that the *i*th of
the *n* ordered values is
${\hat{\eta}}_{(i)} = {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{(i)}$,
where
${\hat{\eta}}_{(1)} < {\hat{\eta}}_{(2)} < \cdots < {\hat{\eta}}_{(n)}$.
The *i*th of these ordered values is then associated with a quantity
$z_{(i)}$ that is an approximation to the expected value of the *i*th
order statistic of a standard normal distribution in a sample of *n*
observations. This is usually referred to as a *normal score*, and the
most widely used score is
$z_{(i)} = \Phi^{- 1}\left\{ (i - 3/8)/(n + 1/4) \right\}$, where
$\Phi^{- 1}( \cdot )$ is the inverse standard normal distribution
function. A Cox regression model that contains the variable formed from
the scaled normal scores, $z_{(i)}/\left. \sqrt{}(8/\pi) \right.$, is
then fitted to the survival data. The resulting estimate of the
coefficient of this variable is the *D*-statistic, and the fitted model
also yields the standard error of *D*.
:::

::: section
### 3.12.3 Explained variation in the Cox regression model {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_3 .head2}

In linear regression analysis, the proportion of variation in the
response variable that is accounted for by the explanatory variables is
widely used to summarise the explanatory power of a model. This
statistic is usually denoted by *R*^2^. For a general linear model with
*p* explanatory variables, the *i*th value of a response variable, *Y*,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{y_{i} = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x_{1i} + \cdots + \beta_{p}x_{pi} + \epsilon_{i},}
\end{array}$$[(3.33)]{.equationnum}

where *β*~0~ is a constant and $\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\ldots,\beta_{p}$
are the coefficients of *p* explanatory variables, $x_{ji}$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, and *ϵ*~*i*~ is a residual or error term. On fitting
this model, the *R*^2^-statistic is defined as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{R^{2} = \frac{\text{Model}\,\text{SS}}{\text{Total}\,\text{SS}} = \frac{\text{Model}\,\text{SS}}{\text{Model}\,\text{SS} + \text{Residual}\,\text{SS}}.}
\end{array}$$

In this equation, the variation in the response variable that is
explained by the model is summarised in the Model Sum of Squares (SS),
$\sum\limits_{i}{({\hat{y}}_{i} - \overline{y})}^{2}$, and expressed as
a proportion of the total variation in the data, represented by the
Total Sum of Squares, $\sum\limits_{i}{(y_{i} - \overline{y})}^{2}$,
where $\overline{y} = n^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i}y_{i}}$ and ${\hat{y}}_{i}$
is the *i*th fitted value, given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{y}}_{i} = {\hat{\beta}}_{0} + {\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{1i} + \cdots + {\hat{\beta}}_{p}x_{pi}.}
\end{array}$$[(3.34)]{.equationnum}

The Total SS can be partitioned into the Model SS and the Residual SS,
$\sum\limits_{i}{(y_{i} - {\hat{y}}_{i})}^{2}$, which represents the
unexplained variation. The larger the value of *R*^2^, the greater the
proportion of variation in the response variable that is accounted for
by the model.

[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p101 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 101. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The *R*^2^-statistic for the general linear model
can also be expressed in the form

$$\begin{array}{r}
{R^{2} = \frac{{\hat{V}}_{M}}{{\hat{V}}_{M} + s^{2}},}
\end{array}$$[(3.35)]{.equationnum}

where ${\hat{V}}_{M} = \text{Model}\,\text{SS}/(n - 1)$ is an estimate
of the variation in the data explained by the model and is the sample
variance of the *n* fitted values ${\hat{y}}_{i}$ in Equation (3.34).
Also, *s*^2^ is an estimate of the residual variation, where
$s^{2} = \text{Residual}\,\text{SS}/(n - 1)$, rather than the usual
unbiased estimator with divisor $n - p - 1$. The quantity
${\hat{V}}_{M}$ can also be expressed in matrix form as
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{S}}\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$,
where $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$ is the vector of estimated
coefficients of the *p* explanatory variables in the fitted regression
model, and $\mathbf{\mathbf{S}}$ is the variance-covariance matrix of
the explanatory variables. This matrix is formed from the sample
variance of each of the *p* explanatory variables,
${(n - 1)}^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i}{(x_{ij} - {\overline{x}}_{j})}^{2}}$,
on its diagonals, and the sample covariance terms,
${(n - 1)}^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i}{(x_{ij} - {\overline{x}}_{j})}}(x_{ij'} - {\overline{x}}_{j'})$,
as the off-diagonal terms, for $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and
$j,j' = 1,2,\ldots,p$ for $j \neq j'$, and where ${\overline{x}}_{j}$ is
the sample mean of the *j*th variable.

In the analysis of survival data, a large number of different measures
of the proportion of variation in the data that is explained by a fitted
Cox regression model have been proposed. However, published reviews of
these measures, based on extensive simulation studies, suggest that
three particular statistics have desirable properties. All of them take
values between 0 and 1, are largely independent of the degree of
censoring, are not affected by the scale of the survival data, and
increase in value as explanatory variables are added to the model. These
are described in the next section.
:::

::: section
### 3.12.4 Measures of explained variation {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_4 .head2}

Consider a Cox regression model for the hazard of death at time *t* in
the *i*th individual,
$h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t)$,
where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is the vector of values of *p*
explanatory variables, $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ is the vector of their
unknown coefficients, and $h_{0}(t)$ is the baseline hazard function.
One of the earliest suggestions for an *R*^2^ type statistic, due to
Kent and O'Quigley (1988), is similar in form to the *R*^2^-statistic
for linear regression analysis in Equation (3.35). This statistic is
defined by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{R_{P}^{2} = \frac{{\hat{V}}_{P}}{{\hat{V}}_{P} + \pi^{2}/6},}
\end{array}$$[(3.36)]{.equationnum}

where
${\hat{V}}_{P} = {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{S}}\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$
is an estimate of the variation in the risk score,
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, between the
*n* individuals, $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$ is the vector of
parameter estimates in the fitted Cox regression model, and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{S}}$ is the variance-covariance matrix of the
explanatory variables. The quantity ${\hat{V}}_{P}$ is most easily
calculated as the sample variance of the *n* values of the risk score
from the fitted Cox regression model. The reason for including the term
$\pi^{2}/6$ in Equation (3.36) in place of *s*^2^ in Equation (3.35)
will be explained later in [Section 5.14](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_14) of
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml).

The statistic $R_{D}^{2}$ proposed by [Royston and Sauerbrei
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib327){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} is
also based on an estimate of the variation in the risk score between
individuals and is closely related to their *D*-statistic, a measure of
predictive ability defined in [Section
3.12.2](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_2). To obtain this statistic, the value
of *D* is scaled to give $D_{0} = D/\left. \sqrt{}(8/\pi) \right.$. The
$R_{D}^{2}$ measure of explained variation for a Cox regression model is
then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{R_{D}^{2} = \frac{D_{0}^{2}}{D_{0}^{2} + \pi^{2}/6}.}
\end{array}$$[(3.37)]{.equationnum}

This has a similar form to the $R_{P}^{2}$-statistic, and indeed,
$D_{0}^{2}$ can also be regarded as an estimate of the variation between
the values of the risk score. If $R_{D}^{2}$ is to be obtained directly,
the unscaled normal scores, $z_{(i)}$ in [Section
3.12.2](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_2), can be used in the Cox regression
model and the resulting coefficient is then *D*~0~ in Equation (3.37).

[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p102 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 102. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Another statistic from Kent and O'Quigley (1988) is
based on the 'distance' between the model of interest and the model that
has no explanatory variables. This latter model is known as the null
model and is such that the hazard function for each individual is simply
the baseline hazard, $h_{0}(t)$. This statistic is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{R_{W}^{2} = 1 - \exp( - \widetilde{W}),}
\end{array}$$

where $\widetilde{W}$, the distance measure, is an estimate of the
expected value of the likelihood ratio statistic, defined as
$W = - 2\,\text{E}\,\left\{ \log L(0) - \log L(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}) \right\}$,
for comparing the maximised log-likelihood under the fitted model,
$\log L(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})$ with that of the null model,
$\log L(0)$. This is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\widetilde{W} = 2\left\lbrack {(1 - \widetilde{\omega})\Psi(1) + \log\Gamma(\widetilde{\omega}) + \log\left\{ {n^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\exp}( - \widetilde{\omega}z_{i})} \right\}} \right\rbrack,}
\end{array}$$

where
$z_{i} = {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'(\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} - \mathbf{\overline{x}})$,
$\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$ is the vector of parameter estimates in
the fitted Cox regression model, $\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}}$ is the
vector of mean values of the *p* explanatory variables, and
$\Gamma(\widetilde{\omega}) = {\int_{0}^{\infty}u^{\widetilde{\omega} - 1}}e^{- u}\,\text{d}u$
is a gamma function. Also, $\widetilde{\omega}$ is the value of *ω* that
satisfies the non-linear equation

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\Psi(1) - \Psi(\omega) + {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\frac{\exp( - \omega z_{i})}{{\sum\limits_{l = 1}^{n}\exp}( - \omega z_{l})}}z_{i} = 0,}
\end{array}$$

where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the digamma function, defined by the series
expansion

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\Psi(\omega) = - \lambda + {\sum\limits_{j = 0}^{\infty}\frac{\omega - 1}{(1 + j)(\omega + j)}},}
\end{array}$$

so that $\Psi(1) = - \lambda$, and $\lambda = 0.577216$ is Euler\'s
constant. This is a complicated statistic to calculate, but it has been
included here as it performs well, and can now be obtained using several
software packages for survival analysis.
:::

::: section
### 3.12.5 Model validation {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_5 .head2}

The methods described in [Sections 3.12.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_1) to
[3.12.4](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_4) summarise the predictive ability of
a Cox regression model, and the variation in a set of survival data that
is explained by a fitted model. However, these can lead to an
over-optimistic assessment of the strength of a model if the same data
has been used both to fit the model and to validate it. For this reason,
measures of predictive ability and explained variation are best
determined from fitting the model to data that has not been used in the
modelling process. If an independent data set, such as data from another
region or country is available, then these data should be used for the
validation process. This is referred to as *external validation*.

In many cases, external validation will not possible. A data-splitting
procedure, based on partitioning the data at random into a modelling and
a validation data set, may then be used. The model is then determined
from the modelling data set and validated on the remaining data, leading
to an *internal validation* process. Depending on the size of the data
set, up to 50% of the data may be set aside for validation purposes,
although for smaller sets of data, this proportion may be somewhere
between 20% and 40%. In any case, this validation data set should
contain a sufficient number of events, and a general guideline is that
at least 100 events are needed.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam3_17" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 3.17]{.boxnum}](#chapter5.xhtml_b3exam3_17){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival of multiple myeloma patients]{.boxht}

In this example, various measures of predictive ability and explained
variation will be compared for the data on the survival times of
multiple myeloma patients, presented in [Example
1.3](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_3). In [Example
3.5](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_5), the most satisfactory Cox regression
model for the hazard of []{#chapter3.xhtml_p103 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 103. " role="doc-pagebreak"}death contained the
explanatory variables *Hb* (serum haemoglobin) and *Bun* (blood urea
nitrogen).

The concordance between model and data, summarised by the value of the
*c*-statistic is 0.67 (se = 0.053) for the selected model and 0.71 (se =
0.048) for the full model. Gönen and Heller\'s measure of concordance is
0.67 (se = 0.041) for the selected model and 0.68 (se = 0.040) for the
full model. In this example, the measures of the discriminatory power of
the two models are very similar because there is relatively little
censoring, and so most of the pairs of survival times can be used in the
computation of the *c*-statistic. There is also very little difference
in the value of either statistic for the selected and full model,
confirming that the predictive ability of the selected model is
comparable to that of the full model. However a value of 0.67 for the
Gönen and Heller statistic indicates that the model is not particularly
good at discriminating between the survival times of the individuals in
this study.

The *D*-statistic for the fitted model is 1.008 with a standard error of
0.351. The statistic has a value that is about three times its standard
error, indicating that some prognostic separation is achieved with the
fitted model. The value of this statistic for the full model is only
slightly greater, at 1.236 (se = 0.346), suggesting that the additional
variables do little to improve the quality of the model.

Turning to measures of explained variation, the values of the different
statistics are shown in [Table
3.20](#chapter3.xhtml_tbl3_20){#chapter3.xhtml_b3tbl3_20} for both the
selected model that contains *Hb* and *Bun*, and the full model that
contains *Age, Sex, Bun, Ca, Hb, Pcells* and *Protein*.

  Measure       *R*^2^ (selected model)   *R*^2^ (full model)
  ------------- ------------------------- ---------------------
  $R_{P}^{2}$   0.27                      0.31
  $R_{D}^{2}$   0.20                      0.27
  $R_{W}^{2}$   0.30                      0.34

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [3.20]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter3.xhtml_b3tbl3_20){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Measures of explained variation on fitting the
  selected and full models]{.table-title} {#tbl3_20}

The three different measures of explained variation have broadly similar
values for both models. On the basis of these statistics, we conclude
that about 30% of the variation in the survival times is explained by
the levels of serum haemoglobin and blood urea nitrogen. Equivalently,
70% of the variation remains unexplained by the model, suggesting that
there is considerable room for improvement. It may be, for example, that
additional explanatory variables need to be measured and incorporated in
the modelling process.

</aside>
:::
::::::::

::::::: section
## 3.13 Time-dependent ROC curves {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_13 .head1}

Measures of concordance described in [Section
3.12.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_1) provide an overall assessment of the
association between observed survival times and the risk score in a
fitted model. However, the short term predictive ability of a model may
not necessarily be the same as that for longer term survival, and so
time-dependent measures are required. Receiver operating characteristic
(ROC) curves are widely used in summarising the accuracy of predictions
in a fitted logistic regression model. This section therefore begins
with a brief summary of their use in this context, before developing
time-dependent versions that can be used with survival data.

::: section
### 3.13.1 Sensitivity and specificity {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_13_1 .head2}

When using the result of a diagnostic procedure to distinguish
individuals with some disease or condition from those without, two types
of error can occur. First, the test procedure could
[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p104 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 104. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}lead to someone who has the disease being labelled
as disease-free. Second, an individual who does not have the disease
could be identified as actually having the disease. For a diagnostic
test procedure that is clinically accurate, the probabilities of these
two types of error will be small. The probability of correctly
identifying someone who has the disease is called the *sensitivity* of
the test, while the probability of correctly identifying someone as
being disease-free is called the *specificity*. Sensitivity is sometimes
referred to as the *true-positive rate* while ($1 -$ specificity), the
probability that a disease-free individual is identified as having the
disease, is the *false-positive rate*. These concepts are illustrated in
the following example.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam3_18" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 3.18]{.boxnum}](#chapter3.xhtml_b3exam3_18){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Performance of a lateral flow device in detecting
COVID-19]{.boxht}

This example uses data obtained on the performance of the Innova lateral
flow device (LFD) for identifying infection with COVID-19. The data are
from a post-marketing surveillance study reported by [Wolf, Hulmes and
Hopkins (2021)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib419){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib419
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and carried out by Public Health
England and Oxford University between November, 2020 and January, 2021.
A total of 8223 individuals were classified according to whether or not
they had a positive result on a reverse transcription polymerase chain
reaction (RT-PCR) test, the best available, or 'gold standard', test for
diagnosing COVID-19. These individuals were also classified according to
whether or not the quicker and cheaper test using the LFD gave a
positive result, and summary data are shown in [Table
3.21](#chapter3.xhtml_tbl3_21){#chapter3.xhtml_b3tbl3_21}.

  PCR Result   LFD Positive   LFD Negative   Total
  ------------ -------------- -------------- -------
  Positive     339             338            677
  Negative      21            7525           7546
  Total        360            7863           8223

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [3.21]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter3.xhtml_b3tbl3_21){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Number of individuals with a positive or
  negative result from a LFD test compared to their RT-PCR
  status]{.table-title} {#tbl3_21}

From this table, the sensitivity of the LFD test is 339/677, that is
50.1%. The specificity is 7525/7546, that is 99.7%, and so the
false-positive rate is 0.3%, which is extremely low. On the basis of
this summary data, the LFD device is only about 50% effective in
correctly identifying COVID-19 infection, but nearly 100% effective in
identifying someone as disease-free.

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 3.13.2 Modelling the probability of disease {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_13_2 .head2}

Whether or not an individual has a particular disease or condition, or
the risk of developing the disease in future, can often be assessed
using a biological marker, or *biomarker*, to measure disease presence
or progress. Many biomarkers are in common use as part of screening or
diagnostic procedures, and examples include serum bilirubin level as an
indicator of liver disease, prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level used
in screening for prostate cancer, and blood sugar level as a marker of
diabetes mellitus. These biomarkers can be measured accurately and are
observations on a continuous scale of measurement, where a higher
biomarker value is more indicative of disease. The result of a
diagnostic test is then based on whether the marker value exceeds a
threshold value, in which case an individual is classified as having the
disease in question.

When a biomarker is used as a diagnostic aid, the dependence of the
probability of disease on the value of the biomarker can be summarised
using a []{#chapter3.xhtml_p105 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 105. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}linear logistic model. In this process, the
response variable is binary, and indicates whether or not an individual
has the disease in question, with a value of zero being recorded if an
individual is disease-free and unity otherwise. Writing *p*~*i*~ for the
probability of disease in the *i*th of *n* individuals, a linear
logistic model for *p*~*i*~ is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{logit}\,(p_{i}) = \log\left( \frac{p_{i}}{1 - p_{i}} \right) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x_{i},}
\end{array}$$[(3.38)]{.equationnum}

where *x*~*i*~ is the value of the biomarker for the *i*th individual.
Fitting this model to the binary response variable leads to estimates
${\hat{\beta}}_{0}$, ${\hat{\beta}}_{1}$ of the *β*-parameters in the
model. A model-based estimate of the logistic transform of the
probability that the *i*th individual has the disease is then
${\hat{\beta}}_{0} + {\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{i}$, and the corresponding
estimated probability that the *i*th individual is diseased,
${\hat{p}}_{i}$, is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{p}}_{i} = \frac{\exp({\hat{\beta}}_{0} + {\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{i})}{1 + \exp({\hat{\beta}}_{0} + {\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{i})}.}
\end{array}$$

These estimated disease probabilities will vary across diseased and
disease-free individuals with different values of *x*~*i*~. For a
diagnostic test based on a binary disease marker, such as the result of
a lateral flow test in [Example
3.18](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_18){#chapter3.xhtml_b3exam3_18}, the value
of *x*~*i*~ in Equation (3.38) will be either 0 or 1, corresponding to a
negative or positive flow test result, respectively. The fitted
probabilities will then be equal to the observed proportions of diseased
and disease-free individuals with a positive test result, leading
directly to estimates of the sensitivity and specificity of the test.

When using a biomarker with continuous values, such as serum bilirubin
level, a threshold value of *x*~*i*~ is required to declare whether or
not an individual is diseased or disease-free on the basis of their test
result. The threshold value to use in a diagnostic test will be guided
by the sensitivity and specificity of the biomarker at different
thresholds. The threshold value for an ideal test will lead to small
values of the sensitivity and specificity, but some trade-off between
them is generally required. For example, if it is very important not to
misdiagnose anyone that has the disease, as in the context of [Example
3.18](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_18), a high sensitivity might be required at
the expense of a lower specificity.

Rather than using a single biomarker, a measure of the risk of disease
may be based on characteristics of an individual. These include
demographic variables such as age and gender, clinical measures of
well-being and biomarkers. Additional explanatory variables are then
included in the model of Equation (3.38). The linear part of the model
then becomes
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x_{1i} + \beta_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \beta_{k}x_{ki}$,
where $x_{1i},x_{2i},\ldots,x_{ki}$ are the values of *k* variables,
including any biomarkers. Fitting the model
$\log\left\{ p_{i}/(1 - p_{i}) \right\} = \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$
leads to an estimate of the disease probability for the *i*th
individual, ${\hat{p}}_{i}$, and their risk score,
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$. Again,
threshold values are needed for the estimated disease probability or the
risk score, to determine whether or not an individual has the disease in
question. The sensitivity and specificity of a diagnostic procedure
based on the biomarker value or risk score at various thresholds can
then be estimated.
:::

::: section
### 3.13.3 ROC curves {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_13_3 .head2}

Once the sensitivity and specificity of a biomarker or risk score have
been estimated for a range of threshold values, a useful graphical
summary is formed by plotting sensitivity against (1 - specificity), for
the different thresholds. This is known as a *receiver-operating
characteristic curve* or ROC curve, a name coined by radio engineers in
the Second World War in a different context. The ROC curve summarises
how well a diagnostic test, biomarker or risk score distinguishes
between individuals with and without the disease. The curve is
particularly useful in showing the trade-off between sensitivity and
specificity when different threshold values are being compared.

An optimal value of the threshold would be such that there is a
sensitivity and specificity of 100%, corresponding to perfect true- and
false-positive rates, leading to a point in the top left-hand corner of
the ROC curve. On the other hand, a straight line relationship
[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p106 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 106. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}between sensitivity and (1 - specificity) indicates
a test with equal true-positive and false-positive probabilities, that
has no predictive ability. Some idealised ROC curves are shown in
[Figure 3.9](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_9){#chapter3.xhtml_b3fig3_9}.

<figure id="fig3_9" class="image">
<img src="images/fig3_9.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter3.xhtml_b3fig3_9" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">3.9</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Idealised ROC curves for models with
differing predictive ability.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In this figure, the straight line corresponds to a model with no
discriminatory power. The curves above this line are what might be
obtained for a biomarker or risk score with an increasing ability to
distinguish between individuals with and without the disease.

A useful overall summary of the ROC curve is the area under the curve
(AUC), and this can be used to compare the predictive power of
alternative models for the probability of disease. The area under an ROC
curve measures the concordance between a binary response variable and
model-based fitted probabilities. The greater the AUC value, the better
the test is at distinguishing those with the disease from those without.
The AUC will always lie between zero and unity, where these extreme
values correspond to a model whose predictions are always incorrect or
one that is always correct. A value of 0.5 corresponds to a model with
no discriminatory power.

ROC curves provide a well-established method for summarising the
predictive ability of a model for the dependence of the probability that
an individual has a disease on values of a biomarker or risk score.
However, the use of ROC curves now extends well beyond diagnostic
testing, as they provide a convenient way of summarising the agreement
between the observed and fitted values of a binary response variable.
:::

::: section
### 3.13.4 Time-dependent ROC curves {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_13_4 .head2}

The ability of biomarker measured at some time point to distinguish
between individuals who may develop a particular disease in the period
after the biomarker has been measured from those who do not is an
important consideration. In situations where the outcome is the time to
an event, such as the occurrence of a disease or death from a particular
condition, the sensitivity and specificity of a diagnostic test may
change in the time period where individuals are being followed up. The
ROC curve is then time-dependent and will need to be estimated at
specific times in the follow-up period. The possibility that the time to
an event such as disease occurrence or death may be censored has also to
be taken into account.

[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p107 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 107. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Estimates of the sensitivity and specificity of a
time-dependent outcome can be obtained in the following manner. Let *T*
be the variable associated with the time to an event such as the
occurrence of a disease or death, and let $D(t)$ be the status of an
individual at time *t*, where $D(t) = 0$ if $T < t$ and $D(t) = 1$ if
$T\geqslant t$. We will refer to the variable $D(t)$ as the disease
status, where $D(t) = 0$ for individuals who are disease-free at time
*t*. However, $D(t)$ could also denote whether or not an individual has
developed some characteristic, or who is alive or dead at time *t*.

The variable associated with the biomarker or risk score will be denoted
by *X*, where larger values of *X* are more indicative of the presence
of disease. The sensitivity of a diagnostic test at time *t*, when a
value *c* is used as the threshold value of a biomarker or risk score,
is the probability that $X > c$ for individuals with the disease at time
*t* and is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{sens}(t,c) = \text{P}(X > c\mid D(t) = 1).}
\end{array}$$

Similarly, the specificity is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{spec}(t,c) = \text{P}(X\leqslant c\mid D(t) = 0).}
\end{array}$$

These two probabilities can be evaluated using a result from conditional
probability according to which the probability of an event *A*
conditional on an event *B* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \text{P}(A\, \middle| \, B) = \frac{\left. \text{P}(B\, \middle| \, A)\text{P}(A) \right.}{\text{P}(B)}. \right.
\end{array}$$[(3.39)]{.equationnum}

This result is known as Bayes' theorem and underpins the Bayesian
approach to the analysis of survival data described in [Chapter
16](#chapter16.xhtml) and is derived in [Section
16.1](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_1) of that chapter. Using Equation (3.39),
the sensitivity of a diagnostic procedure can be written

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(X > c\mid D(t) = 1) = \frac{\text{P}(D(t) = 1\mid X > c)\text{P}(X > c)}{\text{P}(D(t) = 1)},}
\end{array}$$

and the probabilities $\text{P}(D(t) = 1\mid X > c)$ and
$\text{P}(D(t) = 1)$ can be obtained from the survivor function of *T*,
$S(t) = \text{P}(T\geqslant t)$. The sensitivity is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\left\{ 1 - S(t\mid X > c) \right\}\text{P}(X > c)}{1 - S(t)},}
\end{array}$$[(3.40)]{.equationnum}

where $S(t\mid X > c)$ is the survivor function for those individuals
with $X > c$. In the same way, the specificity is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{S(t\mid X\leqslant c)\text{P}(X\leqslant c)}{S(t)}.}
\end{array}$$[(3.41)]{.equationnum}

The sensitivity and specificity can be estimated using the Kaplan-Meier
estimate of the survivor function, $\hat{S}(t)$, obtained from the
possibly censored event times of these individuals, and the subsets of
them with $X > c$ or $X\leqslant c$. Also, $\text{P}(X\leqslant c)$ can
be estimated from the proportion of individuals with marker values less
than or equal to *c*, ${\hat{p}}_{X}(c)$, and $\text{P}(X > c)$ is
estimated by $1 - {\hat{p}}_{X}(c)$. The resulting estimates of the
sensitivity and specificity are

$$\begin{aligned}
{\hat{\text{sens}}(t,c)} & {= \frac{\left\{ 1 - \hat{S}(t\mid X > c) \right\}\left\{ 1 - {\hat{p}}_{X}(c) \right\}}{1 - \hat{S}(t)},}
\end{aligned}$$[(3.42)]{.equationnum}

$$\begin{aligned}
{\hat{\text{spec}}(t,c)} & {= \frac{\hat{S}(t\mid X\leqslant c)\,{\hat{p}}_{X}(c)}{\hat{S}(t)}.}
\end{aligned}$$[(3.43)]{.equationnum}

These estimates can now be used to construct a time-dependent ROC curve
for chosen values of the time to disease or death.

[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p108 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 108. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The area under a time-dependent ROC curve provides
an overall summary of the ability of a model to distinguish between
diseased and disease-free individuals at a given time. The larger the
value of this area, the better the prognostic performance of the model.
This statistic can be used to identify the time points at which the
predictive ability of the model is maximised and to compare models based
on different biomarkers, or different formulations of a risk score.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam3_19" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 3.19]{.boxnum}](#chapter3.xhtml_b3exam3_19){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival of multiple myeloma patients]{.boxht}

In the study concerning the survival times of patients diagnosed with
multiple myeloma, first given in [Example 1.3](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_3)
of [Chapter 1](#chapter1.xhtml), the relevant explanatory variables were
found in [Example 3.5](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_5) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml) to be serum haemoglobin level, *Hb*, and blood urea
nitrogen, *Bun*. The risk score will be used as the marker variable *X*
in Equations (3.42) and (3.43), where the value of the score for the
*i*th individual is $- 0.1336\, Hb_{i} + 0.0186\, Bun_{i}$. This can be
calculated for each of the 48 patients for use as the biomarker variable
*X*. The predictive ability of the marker at times of 6, 12, 18 and 24
months will be evaluated, and the threshold values *c* will be taken to
be each of the 48 values of the risk score. The Kaplan-Meier estimate of
the survivor function for the set of 48 patients, and that for the
subset of patients with a risk score that is greater than *c* or less
than or equal to *c*, for each given threshold value, *c*, is then
determined. Estimated sensitivities and specificities are then found
from Equations (3.42) and (3.43), leading to the time-dependent ROC
curves for the chosen times. These are shown in [Figure
3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_10){#chapter3.xhtml_b3fig3_10}, together with
the line of equal values of sensitivity and (1 - specificity).

<figure id="fig3_10" class="image">
<img src="images/fig3_10.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter3.xhtml_b3fig3_10" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">3.10</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Time-dependent ROC curves for multiple
myeloma data obtained using the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor
function, at times 6 months (- - -), 12 months (– - –), 18 months (<span
class="math inline">⋯⋯</span>) and 24 months (<span
class="math inline">−−</span>).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

This figure shows that the predictive power of the risk score diminishes
with time, as the estimated ROC curves get closer to the line where
sensitivity = (1 - specificity). The risk score best predicts death at
six months after diagnosis. This is confirmed by the areas under the ROC
curves, which are 0.793, 0.730, 0.686 and 0.600 at 6, 12, 18 and 24
months, respectively.

</aside>

Time-dependent ROC curves based on Kaplan-Meier estimates of the
survivor functions suffer from two difficulties. First, the sensitivity
and (1 - specificity) estimated using Equations []{#chapter3.xhtml_p109
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 109. " role="doc-pagebreak"}(3.42) and
(3.43), are not necessarily decreasing functions of the marker values.
This feature can be seen in [Figure 3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_10).
Second, the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function conditional
on $X > c$ assumes that the censoring does not depend on *X*. If the
rigour with which patients are followed up is influenced by the marker
value, this assumption may not be valid. As a result, an alternative
procedure based on estimating the joint distribution of *X* and *T* is
often used in practice.

Let *x*~*i*~ be the value of the biomarker for the *i*th individual for
whom the observed survival time is *t*~*i*~, and let *δ*~*i*~ be the
censoring indicator so that $\delta_{i} = 1$ if *t*~*i*~ is an event
time and zero otherwise. The joint probability
$S(t,c) = \text{P}(T > t,X > c)$ can be estimated by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t,c) = n^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{\hat{S}}_{\omega}}(t\mid X = x_{i})I(x_{i} > c),}
\end{array}$$

where the indicator function $I( \cdot )$ is such that
$I(x_{i} > c) = 1$ if $x_{i} > c$ and 0 otherwise, and
${\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t\mid X = x_{i})$ is a smoothed estimate of the
conditional survivor function that depends on a smoothing parameter *ω*.
A suitable smoothed estimate is the weighted Kaplan-Meier estimate,
where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t\mid X = x_{i}) = {\prod\limits_{s}\left\{ {1 - \frac{{\sum\limits_{j}K_{\omega}}(x_{i},x_{j})I(t_{j} = s)\delta_{j}}{{\sum\limits_{j}K_{\omega}}(x_{i},x_{j})I(t_{j}\geqslant s)}} \right\}},}
\end{array}$$[(3.44)]{.equationnum}

and the product is over all unique event times that are less than or
equal to *t*. In this expression, $K_{\omega}(x_{i},x_{j})$ is a
nearest-neighbour kernel smoothing function that gives greater weight to
values of marker variables *x*~*i*~, *x*~*j*~ that are close together.
This function is given by

$$\begin{array}{l}
{K_{\omega}(x_{i},x_{j}) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
1 & {\text{~if~}\left| \,{\hat{p}}_{X}(x_{i}) - {\hat{p}}_{X}(x_{j}) \middle| < \omega, \right.} \\
0 & {\text{~otherwise},}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

where $p_{X}(x) = \text{P}(X\leqslant x)$ and ${\hat{p}}_{X}(x)$ its
estimate, so that ${\hat{p}}_{X}(x_{i})$ is the proportion of *X* values
that are less than or equal to *x*~*i*~. Also, $0 < 2\omega < 1$, which
means that the proportion of marker values in the near neighbourhood is
$2\omega$, where *ω* must be less than 0.5. In fact to ensure
consistency of the estimate ${\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t,c)$, *ω* must be less
than $n^{- 1/3}$. The greater the value of *ω*, the greater the degree
of smoothing, and a value of 0.05 is often used.

The sensitivity and specificity can then be found from Equations (3.40)
and (3.41), using the results that
$S(t\mid X > c)\text{P}(X > c) = S(t,c)$,
$S(t\mid X\leqslant c)\text{P}(X\leqslant c) = \text{P}(T > t,X\leqslant c)$
and $\text{P}(T > t\mid X\leqslant c) = S(t) - S(t,c)$. This leads to
estimates

$$\begin{aligned}
{\hat{\text{sens}}(t,c)} & {= \frac{1 - {\hat{p}}_{X}(c) - {\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t,c)}{1 - {\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t, - \infty)},}
\end{aligned}$$[(3.45)]{.equationnum}

$$\begin{aligned}
{\hat{\text{spec}}(t,c)} & {= 1 - \frac{{\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t,c)}{{\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t, - \infty)},}
\end{aligned}$$[(3.46)]{.equationnum}

where
${\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t, - \infty) = {\hat{S}}_{\omega}(t) = n^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i}{\hat{S}}_{\omega}}(t\mid X = x_{i})$.
Time-dependent ROC curves can now be constructed using Equations (3.45)
and (3.46).

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam3_20" role="doc-tip">

[Example 3.20]{.boxnum} [Survival of multiple myeloma patients]{.boxht}

In [Example 3.19](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_19){#chapter3.xhtml_b3exam3_19},
the Kaplan-Meier method was used to obtain time-dependent ROC curves for
data on survival following a diagnosis of multiple myeloma. The nearest
neighbour method of estimation is now illustrated. Using the smoothed
weighted Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function, given in
Equation (3.44) with $\omega = 0.05$, the resulting estimates of the
time-dependent ROC curves are shown in [Figure
3.11](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_11){#chapter3.xhtml_b3fig3_11}.[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p110
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 110. " role="doc-pagebreak"}

<figure id="fig3_11" class="image">
<img src="images/fig3_11.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter3.xhtml_b3fig3_11" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">3.11</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Time-dependent ROC curves for multiple
myeloma data obtained using the nearest-neighbour estimate at times 6
months (- - -), 12 months (– - –), 18 months (<span
class="middle">.......</span>) and 24 months
(--).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In this figure, estimates of the sensitivity and (1 - specificity) are
strictly increasing, but the curves do not differ materially from those
shown in [Figure 3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_10). The corresponding areas
under the curves are 0.753, 0.683, 0.690 and 0.609. These are a little
less than the areas under the time-dependent ROC curves in [Figure
3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_10), but it is still the case that the risk
score best predicts death within six months.

</aside>

Although the use of time-dependent ROC curves has been developed in the
context of diagnostic testing, they provide a useful means of
summarising the concordance between observed event times and a fitted
model at different time horizons.
:::
:::::::

::: section
## 3.14 Proportional hazards modelling and the log-rank test {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_14 .head1}

The Cox regression model can be used to test the null hypothesis that
there is no difference between the hazard functions for two groups of
survival times, as illustrated in [Example
3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_3){#chapter3.xhtml_b3exam3_3}. This modelling
approach therefore provides an alternative to the log-rank test in this
situation. However, there is a close connection between the two
procedures, which is explored in greater detail in this section.

Following the notation used in [Section
2.6.2](#chapter2.xhtml_sec2_6_2), and summarised in [Table
2.7](#chapter2.xhtml_tbl2_7){#chapter3.xhtml_b3tbl2_7}, the two groups
will be labelled Group I and Group II, respectively. The numbers of
individuals in the two groups who die at the *j*th ordered death time,
$t_{(j)}$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,r$, will be denoted by $d_{1j}$ and $d_{2j}$,
respectively. Similarly, the numbers of individuals at risk in the two
groups at time $t_{(j)}$, that is, the numbers who are alive and
uncensored just prior to this time, will be denoted $n_{1j}$ and
$n_{2j}$, respectively.

Now let *X* be an indicator variable that is unity when an individual is
in Group I and zero when an individual is in Group II. The Cox
regression model for the *i*th individual can be written as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = e^{\beta x_{i}}h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where *x*~*i*~ is the value of *X* for the *i*th individual,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$. When there are no tied observations, that is, when
$d_{j} = d_{1j} + d_{2j} = 1$, this model can be fitted by finding that
value $\hat{\beta}$ which maximises the likelihood function in Equation
(3.4). Denoting the value of []{#chapter3.xhtml_p111 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 111. " role="doc-pagebreak"}*X* for the individual who
dies at $t_{(j)}$ by $x_{(j)}$, the likelihood function is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L(\beta) = {\prod\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\frac{\exp(\beta x_{(j)})}{{\sum\limits_{l = 1}^{n_{j}}\exp}(\beta x_{l})}},}
\end{array}$$[(3.47)]{.equationnum}

since there are $n_{j} = n_{1j} + n_{2j}$ individuals in the risk set,
$R(t_{(j)})$, at time $t_{(j)}$, and the corresponding log-likelihood
function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\beta) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\beta}x_{(j)} - {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\log}\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{l = 1}^{n_{j}}\exp}(\beta x_{l})} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

Since $x_{(j)}$ is zero for individuals in Group II, the first summation
in this expression is over the death times in Group I, and so is simply
$d_{1}\beta$, where $d_{1} = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}d_{1j}}$ is the
total number of deaths in Group I. Also,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{l = 1}^{n_{j}}\exp}(\beta x_{l}) = n_{1j}e^{\beta} + n_{2j},}
\end{array}$$

and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\beta) = d_{1}\beta - {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\log}\left\{ {n_{1j}e^{\beta} + n_{2j}} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(3.48)]{.equationnum}

The maximum likelihood estimate of *β* can be found by maximising this
expression with respect to *β*, using a non-linear optimisation routine.
Then, the null hypotheses that $\beta = 0$ can be tested by comparing
the value of $- 2\log L(\hat{\beta})$ with $- 2\log L(0)$. This latter
quantity is simply $2{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\log}n_{j}$.

Computation of $\hat{\beta}$ can be avoided by using a *score test* of
the null hypothesis that $\beta = 0$. This test procedure, which is
outlined in [Appendix A](#appA.xhtml_appA), is based on the test
statistic $u^{2}(0)/i(0)$, where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{u(\beta) = \frac{\partial\log L(\beta)}{\partial\beta}}
\end{array}$$

is the efficient score, and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{i(\beta) = - \frac{\partial^{2}\log L(\beta)}{\partial\beta^{2}}}
\end{array}$$

is Fisher\'s (observed) information function. Under the null hypothesis
that $\beta = 0$, $u^{2}(0)/i(0)$ has a chi-squared distribution on one
degree of freedom. Now, from Equation (3.48),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\partial\log L(\beta)}{\partial\beta} = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\left( {d_{1j} - \frac{n_{1j}e^{\beta}}{n_{1j}e^{\beta} + n_{2j}}} \right)},}
\end{array}$$

and

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial^{2}\log L(\beta)}{\partial\beta^{2}} & {= - {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\frac{(n_{1j}e^{\beta} + n_{2j})n_{1j}e^{\beta} - {(n_{1j}e^{\beta})}^{2}}{{(n_{1j}e^{\beta} + n_{2j})}^{2}}}} \\
 & {= - {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\frac{n_{1j}n_{2j}e^{\beta}}{{(n_{1j}e^{\beta} + n_{2j})}^{2}}}.}
\end{aligned}$$

The efficient score and information function, evaluated at $\beta = 0$,
are therefore given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{u(0) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\left( {d_{1j} - \frac{n_{1j}}{n_{1j} + n_{2j}}} \right)},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p112 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 112. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{i(0) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\frac{n_{1j}n_{2j}}{{(n_{1j} + n_{2j})}^{2}}}.}
\end{array}$$

These are simply the expressions for *U*~*L*~ and *V*~*L*~ given in
Equations (2.20) and (2.22) of [Chapter 2](#chapter2.xhtml), for the
special case where there are no tied survival times, that is, where
$d_{j} = 1$ for $j = 1,2,\ldots,r$.

When there are tied observations, the likelihood function in Equation
(3.47) has to be replaced by one that allows for ties. In particular, if
the likelihood function in Equation (3.12) is used, the efficient score
and information function are exactly those given in Equations (2.20) and
(2.22). Hence, when there are tied survival times, the log-rank test
corresponds to using the score test for the discrete proportional
hazards model due to [Cox (1972)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib77){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. In practice, the *P*-value that results from this
score test will not usually differ much from that obtained from
comparing the values of the statistic $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for the models
with and without a term corresponding to the treatment effect. This was
noted in the discussion of [Example 3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_3). Of
course, one advantage of using the Cox regression model in the analysis
of such data is that it leads directly to an estimate of the hazard
ratio.
:::

::: section
## 3.15 Further reading {#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_15 .head1}

Comprehensive introductions to statistical modelling in the context of
linear regression analysis are given by [Draper and Smith
(1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib104){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib104 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Montgomery, Peck and Vining
(2021)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib266){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib266 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. McCullagh and Nelder (1989) include a chapter on
models for survival data in their encyclopaedic survey of generalised
linear modelling.

The proportional hazards model for survival data, in which the baseline
hazard function remains unspecified, was proposed by [Cox
(1972)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib77){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. This
paper introduced the notion of partial likelihood, which was
subsequently considered in greater detail by [Cox
(1975)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib78){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib78 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. See also the contributions to the discussion of
[Cox (1972)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib77){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} by
[Kalbfleisch and Prentice
(1972)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib196){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib196 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Breslow
(1972)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib42){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib42 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. A detailed review of the model, and extensions of
it, is contained in [Therneau and Grambsch
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib380){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib380 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

Introductions to the Cox regression model intended for medical
researchers have been given by [Christensen
(1987)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib63){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib63 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Elashoff
(1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib112){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib112 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Tibshirani
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib386){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib386 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. More recent accounts are given in the textbooks
referenced in [Section 1.6](#chapter1.xhtml_sec1_6) of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml). In particular, [Hosmer, Lemeshow and May
(2008)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib183){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib183 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} include a careful discussion on model development
and the interpretation of model-based parameter estimates.

A detailed treatment of ties in survival data is given in [Kalbfleisch
and Prentice (2002) and Lawless
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib198){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib198 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}; see also [Breslow (1972) and Peto
(1972)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The
estimate of the baseline survivor function, denoted by
${\hat{S}}_{0}(t)$ in [Section 3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_10), was
introduced by [Kalbfleisch and Prentice
(1973)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib197){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and is
also described in [Kalbfleisch and Prentice
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib198){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The
estimate $S_{0}^{*}(t)$ was presented by [Breslow
(1972](#refbib.xhtml_refbib42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, 1974),
although it was derived using a different argument from that used in
[Section 3.10.2](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_10_2).

Model formulation and strategies for model selection are discussed in
books on linear regression analysis. [Harrell
(2015)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib167){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib167 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} addresses many practical issues in model building
and illustrates the process using extensive case studies involving
survival data. See also [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml) of [Chatfield
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib57){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib57 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml) of [Cox and Snell
(1981)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib83){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib83 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Miller
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib262){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib262 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describes a wide range of procedures for
identifying suitable subsets of variables to use in linear regression
modelling. What has come to be known as Akaike\'s information criterion
was introduced by [Akaike
(1974)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib9){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib9 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. It is widely used in time series analysis and
described in books on this subject, such as [Chatfield and Xing
(2019)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib58){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib58 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. The use of the *BIC* statistic in modelling
survival data was described by [Volinsky and Raftery
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib398){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib398 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. The hierarchic principle is fully discussed by
[Nelder (1977)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib275){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib275
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and in [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml) of
[McCullagh and Nelder
(1989)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib253){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib253 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p113 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 113. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The lasso was first proposed by [Tibshirani
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib387){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib387 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Tibshirani
(1997)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib387){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} showed
how the method can be used for variable selection in the Cox model. An
efficient algorithm for computing lasso estimates was described by
[Goeman (2010)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib147){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib147
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. This algorithm is now implemented in
the R package [penalized]{.monospace} for fitting penalised Cox
regression models; see [Goeman, Meijer and Chaturvedi
(2022)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib148){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib148 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. The piecewise linearity property of the lasso was
established by [Efron et al.
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib110){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib110 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Yuan and Lin
(2006)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib423){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib423 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} introduced the group lasso to handle categorical
variables.

The use of fractional polynomials in regression modelling was described
by [Royston and Altman
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib323){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib323 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}; see also [Sauerbrei and Royston
(1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib332){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib332 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. The Box-Tidwell transformation, referred to in
the context of fractional polynomials, was introduced by [Box and
Tidwell (1962)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib40){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib40
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

Methods for the calculation of the adjusted survivor function have been
reviewed by [Nieto and Coresh
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib279){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib279 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and in his comments on this paper, [Zahl
(1997)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib424){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib424 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} outlines some related approaches. The method is
also described in [Zhang et al.
(2007)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib426){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib426 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, who present a SAS macro for its implementation.
[Neuberger et al.
(1986)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib278){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib278 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} used the method based on averaging the values of
explanatory variables to summarise observational data from a liver
transplant programme, which was subsequently referred to in Thomsen,
Keiding and [Altman
(1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib12){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib12 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

A considerable number of measures of explained variation have been
proposed, and there have been several reviews that compare and contrast
their properties. The most extensive reviews are those of [Schemper and
Stare (1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib335){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib335
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Hielscher et al.
(2010)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib176){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib176 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and the two consecutive papers of
[Choodari-Oskooei, Royston and Parmar
(2012)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib61){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib61 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. The *R*^2^ measures described in this chapter
were introduced by Kent and O'Quigley (1988) and [Royston and Sauerbrei
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib327){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The
links between these and other measures of explained variation are also
described in [Royston
(2006)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib322){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib322 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Heinzl
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib173){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib173 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describes SAS macros for the calculation of Kent
and O'Quigley\'s $R_{W}^{2}$-statistic. Harrell\'s *c*-statistic is
described in [Harrell, Lee and Mark
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib168){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and a
preferred measure of concordance was introduced by [Gönen and Heller
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib151){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. One
further measure of explained variation, due to [Stare, Perme and
Henderson (2011)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib364){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib364
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, can be used in proportional hazards
modelling, and has the advantage of being applicable to some of the
other models presented in later chapters. This statistic is based on
explaining variability in the ranks of observations, and R software for
calculating the statistic is available.

A key paper on time-dependent ROC curves is [Heagerty, Lumley and Pepe
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib171){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib171 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, who described the two methods for estimating this
curve described in [Section 3.13](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_13). [Akritas
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib10){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib10 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} provides the nearest neighbour smoothing method. A
comprehensive review of many methods for estimating time-dependent ROC
curves is given in [Kamarudin, Cox and Kolamunnage-Dona
(2017)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib199){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib199 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. The logistic regression model is described in
detail in [Collett
(2003)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib70){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib70 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and tutorials on evaluating diagnostic tests and
time varying predictive ability include [Pepe, Leisenring and Rutter
(1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib286){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib286 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Pepe, Longton and Janes
(2009)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib287){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib287 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

One issue that has not been discussed in this chapter is how to handle
missing values of explanatory variables. A method of *multiple
imputation* based on *fully conditional specification*, also known as
*multiple imputation by chained equations*, is a widely used technique
for dealing with this common problem. The multiple imputation process
involves generating a number of sets of imputed values for each
explanatory variable with missing values. The parameter estimates on
fitting a given model to each imputed data set are then combined to give
overall estimates, with standard errors that allow for uncertainty in
the imputed values. Advances in software development mean that this
technique can now be used routinely in conjunction with commonly
available software packages, including SAS, Stata and R. The tutorial
paper of [White, Royston and Wood
(2011)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib412){#chapter3.xhtml_b3refbib412 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describes the method, its limitations and possible
dangers, and also includes a comprehensive reference
list.[]{#chapter3.xhtml_p114 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 114 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}
:::

[]{#chapter4.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter4.xhtml_p115 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 115. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 4]{.chapnum}[Model checking in the Cox regression model]{.chaptitle} {#chapter4.xhtml_chapter4 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-4](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-4){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

After a model has been fitted to an observed set of survival data, the
adequacy of the model needs to be assessed. Indeed, the use of
diagnostic procedures for model checking is an essential part of the
modelling process.

In some situations, careful inspection of an observed set of data may
lead to the identification of certain features, such as individuals with
unusually large or small survival times. However, unless there are only
one or two explanatory variables, a visual examination of the data may
not be very revealing. The situation is further complicated by
censoring, in that the occurrence of censored survival times make it
difficult to judge aspects of model adequacy, even in the simplest of
situations. Visual inspection of the data should therefore be
supplemented by diagnostic procedures for detecting inadequacies in a
fitted model.

Once a model has been fitted, there are a number of aspects of the fit
that need to be studied. For example, the model must include an
appropriate set of explanatory variables from those measured in the
study, and we will need to check that the correct functional form of
these variables has been used. It might be important to identify
observed survival times that are greater than would have been
anticipated, or individuals whose explanatory variables have an undue
impact on particular hazard ratios. Also, some means of checking the
assumption of proportional hazards might be required.

Many model-checking procedures are based on quantities known as
*residuals*. These are values that can be calculated for each individual
in the study, and have the feature that their behaviour is known, at
least approximately, when the fitted model is satisfactory. A number of
residuals have been proposed for use in connection with the Cox
regression model, and this chapter begins with a review of some of
these. The use of residuals in assessing specific aspects of model
adequacy is then discussed in subsequent sections.

::::::::: section
## 4.1 Residuals for the Cox regression model {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_1 .head1}

Throughout this section, we will suppose that the survival times of *n*
individuals are available, where *r* of these are death times and the
remaining $n - r$ are right-censored. We further suppose that a Cox
regression model has been fitted to the survival times, and that the
linear component of the model contains *p* explanatory variables,
$X_{1},X_{2},\ldots,X_{p}$. The fitted hazard function for the *i*th
individual, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, is therefore

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = \exp({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}){\hat{h}}_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} = {\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{1i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + {\hat{\beta}}_{p}x_{pi}$
is the value of the risk score for that individual and
${\hat{h}}_{0}(t)$ is the estimated baseline hazard function.

::: section
### 4.1.1 []{#chapter4.xhtml_p116 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 116. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Cox-Snell residuals {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_1_1 .head2}

The residual that is most widely used in the analysis of survival data
is the *Cox-Snell residual*, so called because it is a particular
example of the general definition of residuals given by [Cox and Snell
(1968)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib82){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib82 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

The Cox-Snell residual for the *i*th individual, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, is
given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Ci} = \exp({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}){\hat{H}}_{0}(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$[(4.1)]{.equationnum}

where ${\hat{H}}_{0}(t_{i})$ is an estimate of the baseline cumulative
hazard function at time *t*~*i*~, the observed survival time of that
individual. In practice, the Nelson-Aalen estimate given in Equation
(3.29) is generally used. Note that from Equation (3.25), the Cox-Snell
residual, $r_{Ci}$, is the value of
${\hat{H}}_{i}(t_{i}) = - \log{\hat{S}}_{i}(t_{i})$, where
${\hat{H}}_{i}(t_{i})$ and ${\hat{S}}_{i}(t_{i})$ are the estimated
values of the cumulative hazard and survivor functions of the *i*th
individual at *t*~*i*~.

This residual can be derived from a general result in mathematical
statistics on the distribution of a function of a random variable.
According to this result, if *T* is the random variable associated with
the survival time of an individual, and $S(t)$ is the corresponding
survivor function, then the random variable $Y = - \log S(T)$ has an
exponential distribution with unit mean, irrespective of the form of
$S(t)$. The proof of this result is outlined in the following paragraph,
which can be omitted without loss of continuity.

From a general result, if $f_{X}(x)$ is the probability density function
of the random variable *X*, the density of the random variable
$Y = g(X)$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f_{Y}(y) = f_{X}\left\{ g^{- 1}(y) \right\}/\left| \frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}x} \right|,}
\end{array}$$

where $f_{X}\left\{ g^{- 1}(y) \right\}$ is the density of *X* expressed
in terms of *y*. Using this result, the probability density function of
the random variable $Y = - \log S(T)$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f_{Y}(y) = f_{T}\left\{ {S^{- 1}(e^{- y})} \right\}/\left| \frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} \right|,}
\end{array}$$[(4.2)]{.equationnum}

where $f_{T}(t)$ is the probability density function of *T*. Now,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = \frac{\text{d}\left\{ - \log S(t) \right\}}{\text{d}t} = \frac{f_{T}(t)}{S(t)},}
\end{array}$$

and when the absolute value of this function is expressed in terms of
*y*, the derivative becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{f_{T}\left\{ {S^{- 1}(e^{- y})} \right\}}{S\left\{ {S^{- 1}(e^{- y})} \right\}} = \frac{f_{T}\left\{ {S^{- 1}(e^{- y})} \right\}}{e^{- y}}.}
\end{array}$$

Finally, on substituting for the derivative in Equation (4.2), we find
that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f_{Y}(y) = e^{- y},}
\end{array}$$

which, from Equation (5.3) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), is the
probability density function of an exponential random variable, *Y*,
with unit mean.

The next and crucial step in the argument is as follows. If the model
fitted to the observed data is satisfactory, a model-based estimate of
the survivor function for the *i*th individual at *t*~*i*~, the survival
time of that individual, will be close to the corresponding true value
$S_{i}(t_{i})$. This suggests that if the correct model has been fitted,
the values ${\hat{S}}_{i}(t_{i})$ will have properties similar to those
of $S_{i}(t_{i})$. Then, the negative logarithms of the estimated
survivor functions, $- \log{\hat{S}}_{i}(t_{i})$, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$,
will behave as *n* observations from a unit exponential distribution.
These estimates are the Cox-Snell residuals.

[]{#chapter4.xhtml_p117 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 117. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}If the observed survival time for an individual is
right-censored, the corresponding value of the residual is also
right-censored. The residuals will therefore be a censored sample from
the unit exponential distribution, and a test of this assumption
provides a test of model adequacy, to which we return in [Section
4.2.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_2_1).

The Cox-Snell residuals, $r_{Ci}$, have properties that are quite
dissimilar to those of residuals used in linear regression analysis. In
particular, they will not be symmetrically distributed about zero and
they cannot be negative. Furthermore, since the Cox-Snell residuals are
assumed to have an exponential distribution when an appropriate model
has been fitted, they have a highly skew distribution and the mean and
variance of the *i*th residual will both be unity.
:::

::: section
### 4.1.2 Modified Cox-Snell residuals {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_1_2 .head2}

Censored observations lead to residuals that cannot be regarded on the
same footing as residuals derived from uncensored observations. We might
therefore seek to modify the Cox-Snell residuals so that explicit
account can be taken of censoring.

Suppose that the *i*th survival time is a censored observation,
$t_{i}^{*}$, and let *t*~*i*~ be the actual, but unknown, survival time,
so that $t_{i} > t_{i}^{*}$. The Cox-Snell residual for this individual,
evaluated at the censored survival time, is then given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Ci} = {\hat{H}}_{i}(t_{i}^{*}) = - \log{\hat{S}}_{i}(t_{i}^{*}),}
\end{array}$$

where ${\hat{H}}_{i}(t_{i}^{*})$ and ${\hat{S}}_{i}(t_{i}^{*})$ are the
estimated cumulative hazard and survivor functions, respectively, for
the *i*th individual at the censored survival time.

If the fitted model is correct, then the values $r_{Ci}$ can be taken to
have a unit exponential distribution. The cumulative hazard function of
this distribution increases linearly with time, and so the greater the
value of the survival time *t*~*i*~ for the *i*th individual, the
greater the value of the Cox-Snell residual for that individual. It then
follows that the residual for the *i*th individual at the actual
(unknown) failure time, ${\hat{H}}_{i}(t_{i})$, will be greater than the
residual evaluated at the observed censored survival time.

To take account of this, Cox-Snell residuals can be modified by the
addition of a positive constant Δ, which can be called the *excess
residual*. Modified Cox-Snell residuals are therefore of the form

$$\begin{array}{l}
{r_{Ci}' = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
r_{Ci} & \text{for~uncensored~observations,} \\
{r_{Ci} + \Delta} & \text{for~censored~observations,}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

where $r_{Ci}$ is the Cox-Snell residual for the *i*th observation,
defined in Equation (4.1). It now remains to identify a suitable value
for Δ. For this, we use the *lack of memory property* of the exponential
distribution.

To demonstrate this property, suppose that the random variable *T* has
an exponential distribution with mean $\lambda^{- 1}$, and consider the
probability that *T* exceeds $t_{0} + t_{1}$, $t_{1}\geqslant 0$,
conditional on *T* being at least equal to *t*~0~. From the standard
result for conditional probability given in [Section
3.3.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3_1), this probability is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(T\geqslant t_{0} + t_{1}\mid T\geqslant t_{0}) = \frac{\text{P}(T\geqslant t_{0} + t_{1}\,\text{and}\, T\geqslant t_{0})}{\text{P}(T\geqslant t_{0})}.}
\end{array}$$

The numerator of this expression is simply
