variables in the model whose coefficients vary with time, or if there
are explanatory variables that are time-dependent, the proportional
hazards assumption will be violated. We therefore require techniques
that can be used to detect whether there is some form of time dependency
in particular covariates, after allowing for the effects of explanatory
variables that are known, or expected to be, independent of time.

In this section, a straightforward plot that can be used in advance of
model fitting is first described, and this is followed by a description
of how diagnostics and test statistics derived from a fitted model can
be used in examining the proportional hazards assumption.

::: section
### 4.4.1 The log-cumulative hazard plot {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_1 .head2}

In the Cox regression model, the hazard of death at any time *t* for the
*i*th of *n* individuals is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(4.17)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is the vector of values of explanatory
variables for that individual, $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ is the
corresponding vector of coefficients, and $h_{0}(t)$ is the baseline
hazard function. Integrating both sides of this equation over *t* gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\int_{0}^{t}h_{i}}(u)\,\text{d}u = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}){\int_{0}^{t}h_{0}}(u)\,\text{d}u,}
\end{array}$$

and so, using Equation (1.7),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{H_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})H_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $H_{i}(t)$ and $H_{0}(t)$ are the cumulative hazard functions.
Taking logarithms of each side of this equation, we get

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log H_{i}(t) = \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \log H_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

from which it follows that differences in the log-cumulative hazard
functions do not depend on time. This means that if the log-cumulative
hazard functions for individuals with different values of their
explanatory variables are plotted against time, the curves so formed
will be parallel if the proportional hazards model in Equation (4.17) is
valid. This provides the basis of a widely used diagnostic for assessing
the validity of the proportional hazards assumption. It turns out that
plotting the log-cumulative hazard functions against the logarithm of
*t*, rather than *t* itself, is a useful diagnostic in parametric
modelling, and so this form of plot is generally used; see [Section
5.2](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_2) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml) for further
details on the use of this log-cumulative hazard plot.

To use this plot, the survival data are first grouped according to the
levels of one or more factors. If continuous variables are to feature in
this analysis, their values will first []{#chapter4.xhtml_p140
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 140. " role="doc-pagebreak"}need to be
grouped in some way to give a categorical variable. The Kaplan-Meier
estimate of the survivor function of the data in each group is then
obtained. A log-cumulative hazard plot, that is, a plot of the logarithm
of the estimated cumulative hazard function against the logarithm of the
survival time, will yield parallel curves if the hazards are
proportional across the different groups. This method is informative,
and simple to operate when there is a small number of factors, and a
reasonable number of observations at each level. On the other hand, the
plot will be based on very few observations at the later survival times,
and in more highly structured data sets, a different approach needs to
be taken.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam4_9" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 4.9]{.boxnum}](#chapter17.xhtml_b4exam4_9){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival of multiple myeloma patients]{.boxht}

We again use the data on the survival times of 48 patients with multiple
myeloma to illustrate the log-cumulative hazard plot. In particular we
will investigate whether the assumption of proportional hazards is valid
for the variable *Hb*, which is associated with the serum haemoglobin
level. Because this is a continuous variable, we first need to
categorise the values of *Hb*. This will be done in the same manner as
in [Example 3.8](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_8){#chapter4.xhtml_b4exam3_8} of
[Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), where four groups were defined with values
of *Hb* which are such that $Hb\leqslant 7$, $7 < Hb\leqslant 10$,
$10 < Hb\leqslant 13$ and $Hb > 13$. The patients are then grouped
according to their haemoglobin level, and the Kaplan-Meier estimate of
the survivor function is obtained for each of the four groups. From this
estimate, the estimated log-cumulative hazard is formed using the
relation $\hat{H}(t) = - \log\hat{S}(t)$, from Equation (1.8) of
[Chapter 1](#chapter1.xhtml), and plotted against the values of
$\log t$. The resulting log-cumulative hazard plot is shown in [Figure
4.13](#chapter4.xhtml_fig4_13){#chapter4.xhtml_b4fig4_13}.

<figure id="fig4_13" class="image">
<img src="images/fig4_13.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter4.xhtml_b4fig4_13" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">4.13</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Log-cumulative hazard plot for multiple
myeloma patients in four groups defined by <span
class="math inline"><em>H</em><em>b</em> ≤ 7</span> (∙), <span
class="math inline">7 &lt; <em>H</em><em>b</em> ≤ 10</span> (■), <span
class="math inline">10 &lt; <em>H</em><em>b</em> ≤ 13</span> (▲) and
<span class="math inline"><em>H</em><em>b</em> &gt; 13</span>
(∗).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

This figure indicates that the plots for $Hb\leqslant 7$,
$7 < Hb\leqslant 10$ and $Hb > 13$ are roughly parallel. The plot for
$10 < Hb\leqslant 13$ is not in line with the others, although this
impression results from relatively large cumulative hazard estimates at
the longest survival times experienced by patients in this group. This
plot takes no account of the values of the other variable, *Bun*, and it
could be that the survival times of the individuals in the third *Hb*
group have been affected by their *Bun* values. Overall, there is little
reason to doubt the proportional hazards assumption.

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 4.4.2 []{#chapter4.xhtml_p141 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 141. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Use of Schoenfeld residuals {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_2 .head2}

The Schoenfeld residuals, defined in [Section
4.1.5](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_1_5), are particularly useful in evaluating
the assumption of proportional hazards after fitting a Cox regression
model. [Grambsch and Therneau
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib154){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have
shown that the expected value of the *i*th scaled Schoenfeld residual,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, for the *j*th explanatory variable in the model,
*X*~*j*~, $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, denoted $r_{Sji}^{*}$, is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{E}\,(r_{Sji}^{*}) \approx \beta_{j}(t_{i}) - {\hat{\beta}}_{j},}
\end{array}$$[(4.18)]{.equationnum}

where $\beta_{j}(t)$ is taken to be a time-varying coefficient of
*X*~*j*~, $\beta_{j}(t_{i})$ is the value of this coefficient at the
survival time of the *i*th individual, *t*~*i*~, and ${\hat{\beta}}_{j}$
is the estimated value of *β*~*j*~ in the fitted Cox regression model.
Note that these residuals are only defined at event times.

Equation (4.18) suggests that a plot of the values of
$r_{Sji}^{*} + {\hat{\beta}}_{j}$, or equivalently just the scaled
Schoenfeld residuals, $r_{Sji}^{*}$, against the observed survival times
should give information about the form of the time-dependent coefficient
of *X*~*j*~, $\beta_{j}(t)$. In particular, a horizontal line will
suggest that the coefficient of *X*~*j*~ is constant, and the
proportional hazards assumption is satisfied. A smoothed curve can be
superimposed on this plot to aid interpretation, as in the plots of
martingale residuals against the values of explanatory variables,
described in [Section 4.2.3](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_2_3).

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam4_10" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 4.10]{.boxnum}](#chapter4.xhtml_b4exam4_10){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Infection in patients on dialysis]{.boxht}

The data on catheter removal times for patients on dialysis, first given
in [Example 4.1](#chapter4.xhtml_exam4_1), are now used to illustrate
the use of the scaled Schoenfeld residuals in assessing non-proportional
hazards. The scaled Schoenfeld residuals for the variables *Age* and
*Sex* were given in [Table 4.2](#chapter4.xhtml_tbl4_2), and plotting
these values against the removal times gives the graphs shown in [Figure
4.14](#chapter4.xhtml_fig4_14){#chapter4.xhtml_b4fig4_14}. The smoothed
curves deviate little from horizontal lines, and in neither plot is
there any suggestion of non-proportional hazards.

<figure id="fig4_14" class="image">
<img src="images/fig4_14.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter4.xhtml_b4fig4_14" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">4.14</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of scaled Schoenfeld residuals for
<em>Age and Sex.</em></span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 4.4.3 Tests for non-proportional hazards {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_3 .head2}

The graphical method for assessing the assumption of proportional
hazards, described in [Section 4.4.2](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_2), leads
to a formal test procedure. From the result given in Equation (4.18),
the expected value of the *i*th scaled Schoenfeld residual,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, for the *j*th explanatory variable, *X*~*j*~,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, depends on $\beta_{j}(t_{i})$, the value of a
time-varying coefficient of *X*~*j*~ at time *t*~*i*~. A test of the
proportional hazards assumption can then be based on testing
[]{#chapter4.xhtml_p142 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 142. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}whether there is a linear relationship between
$\,\text{E}\,(r_{Sji}^{*})$ and some function of time. If there is
evidence that $\,\text{E}\,(r_{Sji}^{*})$ is time-dependent, the
hypothesis of proportional hazards would be rejected.

For a particular explanatory variable *X*~*j*~, linear dependence of the
coefficient of *X*~*j*~ on time can be expressed by taking
$\beta_{j}(t_{i}) = \beta_{j} + \nu_{j}(t_{i} - \overline{t})$, where
*ν*~*j*~ is an unknown regression coefficient. This leads to a linear
regression model with
$\,\text{E}\,(r_{Sji}^{*}) = \nu_{j}(t_{i} - \overline{t})$, and a test
of whether the slope *ν*~*j*~ is zero leads to a test of whether the
coefficient of *X*~*j*~ is time-dependent and hence of proportional
hazards with respect to *X*~*j*~. Letting
$\tau_{i},\tau_{2},\ldots,\tau_{d}$ be the *d* observed death times
across all *n* individuals in the data set, [Grambsch and Therneau
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib154){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} show
that an appropriate test statistic is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\left\{ {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{d}(}\tau_{i} - \overline{\tau})r_{Sji}^{*} \right\}^{2}}{d\,\text{var}\,({\hat{\beta}}_{j}){\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{d}{(\tau_{i} - \overline{\tau})}^{2}}},}
\end{array}$$[(4.19)]{.equationnum}

where $\overline{\tau} = d^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{d}\tau_{i}}$ is
the sample mean of the observed death times. Under the null hypothesis
that the slope is zero, this statistic has a *χ*^2^ distribution on 1
d.f., and significantly large values of Expression (4.19) lead to
rejection of the proportional hazards assumption for the *j*th
explanatory variable.

An overall or global test of the proportional hazards assumption across
all the *p* explanatory variables included in a Cox regression model is
obtained by aggregating the individual test statistics in Expression
(4.19). This leads to the statistic

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{{(\mathbf{\mathbf{\tau}} - \overline{\tau})}'\mathbf{\mathbf{S}}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})\mathbf{\mathbf{S}}'(\mathbf{\mathbf{\tau}} - \overline{\tau})}{{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{d}{(\tau_{i} - \overline{\tau})}^{2}}/d},}
\end{array}$$[(4.20)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{\tau}} = {(\tau_{1},\tau_{2},\ldots,\tau_{d})}'$
is the vector formed from the *d* event times and $\mathbf{\mathbf{S}}$
is the $d \times p$ matrix whose columns are the (unscaled) Schoenfeld
residuals for the *j*th explanatory variable, so that the *j*th column
of $\mathbf{\mathbf{S}}$ is the vector
${(r_{Sj1},r_{Sj2},\ldots,r_{Sjd})}'$, and
$\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})$ is the
variance-covariance matrix of the estimated coefficients of the
explanatory variables in the fitted Cox regression model. The test
statistic in Expression (4.20) has a *χ*^2^ distribution on *p* d.f.
when the assumption of proportional hazards across all *p* explanatory
variables is true. This test is known as the *Grambsch and Therneau test
of proportional hazards* and is sometimes more enigmatically referred to
as the *zph* test.

The test statistics in Expressions (4.19) and (4.20) can be adapted to
other time-scales by replacing the *τ*~*i*~, $i = 1,2,\ldots,d$, by
transformed values of the death times. For example, using logarithms of
the death times, rather than the times themselves, would allow linearity
in the coefficient of *X*~*j*~ to be assessed on a logarithmic scale.
The *τ*~*i*~ can also be replaced by the rank order of the death times,
or by the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function at each event
time. Plots of scaled Schoenfeld residuals against time, discussed in
[Section 4.4.2](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_2), may indicate which of these
possible options is the most appropriate.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam4_11" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 4.11]{.boxnum}](#chapter4.xhtml_b4exam4_11){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Infection in patients on dialysis]{.boxht}

We now illustrate tests of proportional hazards using the data on
catheter removal times for patients on dialysis. The variances of the
estimated coefficients of the variables *Age* and *Sex* in the fitted
Cox regression model are 0.000688 and 1.20099, respectively, the sum of
squares of the 12 event times is 393418.92, and the numerator of
Expression (4.19) is calculated from the scaled Schoenfeld residuals in
[Table 4.2](#chapter4.xhtml_tbl4_2). The values of the test statistic
are 0.811 ($P = 0.368$) and 0.224 ($P = 0.636$) for *Age* and *Sex*,
respectively. In neither case is there evidence against the proportional
hazards assumption, as might have been expected from the graphical
analysis in [Example
4.10](#chapter4.xhtml_exam4_10){#chapter4.xhtml_b4exam4_10}.

Matrix multiplication is required to obtain the numerator of the global
test for proportional hazards in Expression (4.20), and leads to
26578.805, from which the Grambsch and []{#chapter4.xhtml_p143
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 143. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Therneau test
statistic is 0.811. This has a *χ*^2^ distribution on 2 d.f. leading to
a *P*-value of 0.667. Again there is no reason to doubt the validity of
the proportional hazards assumption.

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 4.4.4 Adding a time-dependent variable {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_4 .head2}

Specific forms of departure from proportional hazards can be
investigated by adding a *time-dependent variable* to the Cox regression
model. Full details on the use of time-dependent variables in modelling
survival data are given in [Chapter 8](#chapter8.xhtml), but in this
section, the procedure is described in a particular context.

Consider a survival study in which each patient has been allocated to
one of two groups, corresponding to a standard treatment and a new
treatment. Interest may then centre on whether the ratio of the hazard
of death at time *t* in one treatment group relative to the other, is
independent of survival time. A proportional hazards model for the
hazard function of the *i*th individual in the study is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\beta_{1}x_{1i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(4.21)]{.equationnum}

where $x_{1i}$ is the value of an indicator variable *X*~1~ that is zero
for the standard treatment and unity for the new treatment. The relative
hazard of death at any time for a patient on the new treatment, relative
to one on the standard, is then $e^{\beta_{1}}$, which is independent of
the survival time.

Now define a time-dependent explanatory variable *X*~2~, where
$X_{2} = X_{1}t$. If this variable is added to the model in Equation
(4.21), the hazard of death at time *t* for the *i*th individual becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\beta_{1}x_{1i} + \beta_{2}x_{2i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(4.22)]{.equationnum}

where $x_{2i} = x_{1i}t$ is the value of $X_{1}t$ for the *i*th
individual. The relative hazard at time *t* is now

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\exp(\beta_{1} + \beta_{2}t),}
\end{array}$$[(4.23)]{.equationnum}

since $X_{2} = t$ under the new treatment, and zero otherwise. This
hazard ratio depends on *t*, and the model in Equation (4.22) is no
longer a proportional hazards model. In particular, if $\beta_{2} < 0$,
the relative hazard decreases with time. This means that the hazard of
death on the new treatment, relative to that on the standard, decreases
with time. If $\beta_{1} < 0$, the superiority of the new treatment
becomes more apparent as time goes on. On the other hand, if
$\beta_{2} > 0$, the relative hazard of death on the new treatment
increases with time, reflecting an increasing risk of death on the new
treatment relative to the standard. In the particular case where
$\beta_{2} = 0$, the relative hazard is constant at $e^{\beta_{1}}$.
This means that a test of the hypothesis that $\beta_{2} = 0$ is a test
of the assumption of proportional hazards. The situation is illustrated
in [Figure 4.15](#chapter4.xhtml_fig4_15){#chapter4.xhtml_b4fig4_15}.

<figure id="fig4_15" class="image">
<img src="images/fig4_15.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter4.xhtml_b4fig4_15" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">4.15</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the relative hazard, <span
class="math inline">exp (<em>β</em><sub>1</sub> + <em>β</em><sub>2</sub><em>t</em>)</span>,
against <em>t</em>, for different values of
<em>β</em><sub>2</sub>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In order to aid both the computation and interpretation of the
parameters in the model of Equation (4.22), the variable *X*~2~ can be
defined in terms of the deviation from some time, *t*~0~. The estimated
values of *β*~1~ and *β*~2~ will then tend to be less highly correlated,
and the numerical algorithm for maximising the appropriate likelihood
function will be more stable. If *X*~2~ is taken to be such that
$X_{2} = X_{1}(t - t_{0})$, the value of *X*~2~ is $t - t_{0}$ for the
new treatment and zero for the standard. The relative hazard now becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\exp\left\{ \beta_{1} + \beta_{2}(t - t_{0}) \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

In the model of Equation (4.22), with $x_{2i} = x_{1i}(t - t_{0})$, the
quantity $e^{\beta_{1}}$ is the hazard of death at time *t*~0~ for an
individual on the new treatment relative to one on the standard. In
practical applications, *t*~0~ will generally be chosen to provide a
convenient interpretation for the time at which this relative hazard is
applicable. For example, taking *t*~0~ to be the []{#chapter4.xhtml_p144
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 144. " role="doc-pagebreak"}mean or median
survival time means that $\exp({\hat{\beta}}_{1})$ is the estimated
relative hazard of death at this time.

A similar model can be used to detect whether the coefficient of a
continuous variate has a coefficient that depends on time. Suppose that
*X* is such a variate, and we wish to examine whether there is any
evidence that the coefficient of *X* is linearly dependent on time. To
do this, the term *Xt* is added to the model that includes *X*. The
hazard of death at time *t* for the *i*th individual is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\beta_{1}x_{i} + \beta_{2}x_{i}t)h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where *x*~*i*~ is the value of *X* for that individual. The hazard of
death at time *t* for an individual for whom $X = x_{i} + 1$, relative
to an individual for whom $X = x_{i}$, is then
$\exp(\beta_{1} + \beta_{2}t)$, as in Equation (4.23).

The time-dependent variables considered in this section have
coefficients that are linearly dependent on time. A similar approach can
be used when a coefficient is a non-linear function of time. For
example, $\log t$ might be used in place of *t* in the definition of the
time-dependent variable *X*~2~, used in Equation (4.22). In this version
of the model, a test of the hypothesis that $\beta_{2} = 0$ is a test of
proportional hazards, where the alternative hypothesis is that the
hazard ratio is dependent on the logarithm of time. Using $\log t$ in
the definition of a time-dependent variable is also helpful when the
numerical values of the survival times are large, such as when survival
in a long-term study is measured in days. There may then be
computational problems associated with calculating the value of
$\exp(\beta_{2}x_{2i})$ in Equation (4.22), which are resolved by using
$\log t$ in place of *t* in the definition of *X*~2~.

Models that include the time-dependent variable *X*~2~ cannot be fitted
by treating *X*~2~ in the same manner as other explanatory variables in
the model. The reason for this is that this variable will have different
values at different death times, complicating the calculation of the
denominator of the partial likelihood function in Equation (3.4). Full
details on the fitting process will be deferred to [Chapter
8](#chapter8.xhtml). However, inferences about the effect of
time-dependent variables on the hazard function can be evaluated as for
other variables. In particular, the change in the value of the
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic can be compared to percentage points of the
chi-squared distribution to test the significance of the variable. This
is therefore a formal test of proportional
hazards.[]{#chapter4.xhtml_p145 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 145. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam4_12" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 4.12]{.boxnum}](#chapter17.xhtml_b4exam4_12){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Infection in patients on dialysis]{.boxht}

The data on catheter removal times, used in [Examples
4.10](#chapter4.xhtml_exam4_10) and
[4.11](#chapter4.xhtml_exam4_11){#chapter4.xhtml_b4exam4_11}, will now
be used to illustrate how the proportional hazards assumption can be
tested using time-dependent variates.

We begin by fitting the Cox regression model containing just *Age* and
*Sex*, which leads to a value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ of 34.468. We now
define terms that are the products of these variables with time, namely
$Tage = Age \times t$ and $Tsex = Sex \times t$. These variables are
then added to the model. Note that we cannot simply form these products
from the observed survival times of the patients, since the
model-fitting process requires that these values be computed for
different values of *t*; see [Chapter 8](#chapter8.xhtml) for details on
this.

When the variable *Tage* is added to the model that contains *Age* and
*Sex*, the value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ reduces to 32.006, but this
reduction is not significant at the 5% level ($P = 0.117$). The
reduction in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ when *Tsex* is added to the model that has
*Age* and *Sex* is only 0.364 ($P = 0.546$). This analysis confirms that
there is no reason to doubt the assumption of proportional hazards in
respect of the variables *Age* and *Sex*.

</aside>
:::
:::::::

::: section
## 4.5 Recommendations {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_5 .head1}

In this chapter, a variety of diagnostics have been presented. Which
should be used on a routine basis and which are needed when a more
thorough assessment of model adequacy is required?

In terms of assessing the overall fit of a model, a plot of the deviance
residuals against the risk score gives information on observations that
are not well fitted by the model, and their relation to the set of
values of the explanatory variables. This diagnostic is generally more
informative than the cumulative, or log-cumulative, hazard plot of the
Cox-Snell residuals. Plots of residuals against the survival times, the
rank order of the survival times, or explanatory variables may also be
useful.

Plots of residuals might be supplemented by influence diagnostics. When
the inference to be drawn from a model centres on one or two particular
parameters, the delta-beta statistic for those parameters, will be the
most relevant. Plots of these values against the rank order of survival
times will then be useful. To investigate whether there are observations
that have an influence on the set of parameter estimates, or risk score,
the diagnostic based on the absolute values of the elements of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{l}}_{\text{max}}$ is probably the most suitable. Plots
of these values against the rank order of survival times will be
informative, but plots against particular explanatory variables might
also be revealing. An initial assessment of the validity of the
proportional hazards assumption can be made from log-cumulative hazard
plots. However, plots based on the scaled Schoenfeld residuals, and
associated tests of non-proportionality are more helpful. The Grambsch
and Therneau test can be used for an overall test of the proportional
hazards assumption.

Once a suitable model has been determined, and satisfactory results have
been obtained from the model checking process, the final stage in the
model building process is to review the model critically, in conjunction
with subject matter experts. This may lead to further revisions to the
model to ensure that it will be accepted as an appropriate summary of
the underlying survival data.
:::

::: section
## 4.6 Further reading {#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_6 .head1}

General introductions to model checking in linear models are included in
[Draper and Smith
(1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib104){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib104 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Montgomery, Peck and Vining
(2021)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib266){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib266 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Cook and Weisberg
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib73){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib73 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} give a more detailed account of the theory
underlying residuals and influence diagnostics in a number of
situations. [Atkinson
(1985)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib27){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib27 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describes model checking in linear models from a
practical []{#chapter4.xhtml_p146 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 146. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}viewpoint, and [McCullagh and Nelder
(1989)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib253){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib253 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Aitkin et al.
(1989)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib7){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib7 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} discuss this topic in the context of generalised
linear models.

Many textbooks devoted to the analysis of survival data, and
particularly those of [Cox and Oakes
(1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib81){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib81 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Hosmer, Lemeshow and May
(2008)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib183){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib183 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Lawless
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib229){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib229 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Kalbfleisch and Prentice
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib198){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib198 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, include sections on the use of residuals.
[Hinkley, Reid and Snell
(1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib178){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib178 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Hastie and Tibshirani
(1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib170){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib170 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} also include brief discussions on methods for
assessing the adequacy of models fitted to survival data.

Early articles on the use of residuals in checking the adequacy of
survival models include [Kay
(1977)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib202){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib202 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Crowley and Hu
(1977)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib89){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. These
papers include a discussion on the Cox-Snell residuals, which are based
on the general definition of residuals given by [Cox and Snell
(1968)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib82){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
[Crowley and Storer
(1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib90){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib90 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} showed empirically that the cumulative hazard plot
of the residuals is not particularly good at identifying inadequacies in
the fitted model. See also [Crowley and Storer
(1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib90){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a
practical application of the methods. Reviews of diagnostic procedures
in survival analysis were given in the mid-1980s by [Kay
(1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib203){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib203 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Day
(1985)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib97){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib97 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

Martingale residuals were proposed by [Barlow and Prentice
(1988)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib30){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib30 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. Essentially the same residuals were proposed by
[Lagakos (1981)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib222){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and their use is discussed by [Therneau, Grambsch
and Fleming (1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib381){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Henderson and Milner
(1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib175){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib175 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. Deviance residuals were also introduced in
[Therneau, Grambsch and Fleming
(1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib381){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The
Schoenfeld residuals for the Cox model were proposed by [Schoenfeld
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib341){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. In
accounts of survival analysis based on the theory of counting processes,
[Fleming and Harrington
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib126){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib126 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Therneau and Grambsch
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib380){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib380 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} show how different types of residual can be used,
and give detailed practical examples. Two other types of residual,
introduced by [Nardi and Schemper
(1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib274){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib274 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, are particularly suitable for the detection of
outlying survival times.

Influence diagnostics for the Cox regression model have been considered
by many authors, but the major papers are those of [Cain and Lange
(1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib54){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Reid
and Crépeau (1985)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib313){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib313
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Storer and Crowley
(1985)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib365){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib365 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Pettitt and Bin Daud
(1989)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib295){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and
[Weissfeld (1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib407){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib407
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. [Pettitt and Bin Daud
(1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib296){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib296 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} show how time-dependence in the Cox proportional
hazards model can be detected by smoothing the Schoenfeld residuals. The
LOWESS smoother was introduced by [Cleveland
(1979)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

Some other methods for evaluating survival models, not mentioned in this
chapter, have been proposed by [Cox
(1979)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib79){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib79 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Arjas
(1988)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib25){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib25 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Gray
(1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib155){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib155 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describes the use of smoothed estimates of
cumulative hazard functions in evaluating the fit of a Cox model. Lin,
Wei and Ying (2003) describe graphical and numerical methods for
checking the adequacy of a Cox regression model, based on cumulative
sums of martingale residuals.

Most of the diagnostic procedures presented in this chapter rely on an
informal evaluation of tabular or graphical presentations of particular
statistics. In addition to these procedures, a variety of significance
tests have been proposed that can be used to assess the goodness of fit
of the model. Examples include the methods of [Schoenfeld
(1980)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib339){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib339 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Andersen
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib15){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib15 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Nagelkerke, Oosting and Hart
(1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib272){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib272 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Ciampi and Etezadi-Amoli
(1985)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib65){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib65 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, Moreau, O'Quigley and Mesbah (1985), [Gill and
Schumacher (1987)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib145){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib145
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, O'Quigley and Pessione (1989), Quantin
et al. (1996), [Grønnesby and Borgan
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib160){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib160 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Verweij, van Houwelingen and Stijnen
(1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib396){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib396 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. Reviews of some of these goodness of fit tests
for the Cox regression model are included in [Lin and Wei
(1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib236){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib236 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Quantin et al.
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib306){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib306 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. Many of these tests involve statistics that are
quite complicated, and the procedures are not widely available in
computer software for survival analysis. A more simple procedure for
evaluating the overall fit of a model has been proposed by [May and
Hosmer (1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib251){#chapter4.xhtml_b4refbib251
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

[]{#chapter5.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter5.xhtml_p147 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 147. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 5]{.chapnum}[Parametric regression models]{.chaptitle} {#chapter5.xhtml_chapter5 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-5](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-5){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

When the Cox regression model is used in the analysis of survival data,
there is no need to assume a particular form of probability distribution
for the survival times. As a result, the hazard function is not
restricted to a specific functional form, and the model has flexibility
and widespread applicability. On the other hand, if the assumption of a
particular probability distribution for the data is valid, inferences
based on such an assumption will be more precise. In particular,
estimates of quantities such as relative hazards and median survival
times will tend to have smaller standard errors than they would in the
absence of a distributional assumption. Models in which a specific
probability distribution is assumed for the survival times are known as
*parametric models* and are the subject of this chapter.

A probability distribution that plays a central role in the analysis of
survival data is the Weibull distribution, introduced by W. Weibull in
1951 in the context of industrial reliability testing. Indeed, this
distribution is as central to the parametric analysis of survival data
as the normal distribution is in linear modelling. Models based on the
Weibull distribution are therefore considered in some detail in
[Sections 5.5](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_5) to [5.7](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_7).

Although the proportional hazards model finds widespread applicability
in the analysis of survival data, there are relatively few probability
distributions for the survival times that can be used with this model.
Moreover, the distributions that are available, principally the Weibull
and Gompertz distributions, lead to hazard functions that either
increase or decrease over time. A model that encompasses a wider range
of survival time distributions is the *accelerated failure time model*,
described in [Sections 5.10](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_10) to
[5.13](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_13). In circumstances where the proportional
hazards assumption is not tenable, models based on this general family
may prove to be fruitful. Again, the Weibull distribution may be adopted
for the distribution of survival times in the accelerated failure time
model, but some other probability distributions are also available. This
chapter therefore begins with a brief survey of distributions for
survival data, that may be used in parametric models. One other general
family of survival models, known as the *proportional odds model*, may
be useful in some circumstances. This model is described in [Section
5.15](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_15).

::::::::::: section
## 5.1 Models for the hazard function {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1 .head1}

Once a distributional model for survival times has been specified in
terms of a probability density function, the corresponding survivor and
hazard functions can be obtained from the relations

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = 1 - {\int_{0}^{t}f}(u)\,\text{d}u,}
\end{array}$$

and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)} = - \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left\{ \log S(t) \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where $f(t)$ is the probability density function of the survival times.
These relationships were derived in [Section
1.4](#chapter1.xhtml_sec1_4). An alternative approach is to specify a
functional form for the []{#chapter5.xhtml_p148 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 148. " role="doc-pagebreak"}hazard function, from
which the survivor function and probability density functions can be
determined from the equations

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = \exp\left\{ {- H(t)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(5.1)]{.equationnum}

and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = h(t)S(t) = - \frac{\text{d}S(t)}{\text{d}t},}
\end{array}$$

where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{H(t) = {\int_{0}^{t}h}(u)\,\text{d}u}
\end{array}$$

is the integrated hazard function.

::: section
### 5.1.1 The exponential distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_1 .head2}

The simplest model for the hazard function is to assume that it is
constant over time. The hazard of death at any time after the time
origin of the study is then the same, irrespective of the time that has
elapsed. Under this model, the hazard function may be written as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \lambda,}
\end{array}$$

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$. The parameter *λ* is a positive constant
that can be estimated by fitting the model to observed survival data.
From Equation (5.1), the corresponding survivor function is

$$\begin{aligned}
{S(t)} & {= \exp\left\{ {- {\int_{0}^{t}\lambda}\,\text{d}u} \right\},} \\
 & {= e^{- \lambda t},}
\end{aligned}$$[(5.2)]{.equationnum}

and so the implied probability density function of the survival times is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \lambda e^{- \lambda t},}
\end{array}$$[(5.3)]{.equationnum}

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$. This is the probability density function of
a random variable *T* that has an *exponential distribution* with a mean
of $\lambda^{- 1}$. It is sometimes convenient to write
$\mu = \lambda^{- 1}$, so that the hazard function is $\mu^{- 1}$, and
the survival time distribution has a mean of *μ*. However, the former
specification of the hazard function will generally be used in this
book.

The median of the exponential distribution, $t(50)$, is such that
$S\left\{ t(50) \right\} = 0.5$, that is,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\exp\left\{ - \lambda t(50) \right\} = 0.5,}
\end{array}$$

so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(50) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\log 2.}
\end{array}$$

More generally, the *p*th percentile of the survival time distribution
is the value $t(p)$ such that $S\left\{ t(p) \right\} = 1 - (p/100)$,
and using Equation (5.2), this is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(p) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right).}
\end{array}$$

A plot of the hazard function for three values of *λ*, namely 1.0, 0.1
and 0.01, is given in [Figure
5.1](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_1){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_1}, and the
corresponding probability density functions are shown in [Figure
5.2](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_2){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_2}. For these values
of *λ*, the means of the corresponding exponential distributions are 1,
10 and 100, and the median survival times are 0.69, 6.93 and 69.31,
respectively.

<figure id="fig5_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Hazard functions for exponential
distributions with <span class="math inline"><em>λ</em>=</span> 1.0, 0.1
and 0.01.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig5_2" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_2.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_2" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.2</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Probability density functions for exponential
distributions with <span class="math inline"><em>λ</em>=</span> 1.0, 0.1
and 0.01.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: section
### 5.1.2 []{#chapter5.xhtml_p149 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 149. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The Weibull distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_2 .head2}

In practice, the assumption of a constant hazard function, or
equivalently of exponentially distributed survival times, is rarely
tenable. A more general form of hazard function is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1},}
\end{array}$$[(5.4)]{.equationnum}

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$, a function that depends on two parameters
*λ* and *γ*, which are both greater than zero. In the particular case
where $\gamma = 1$, the hazard function takes a constant value *λ*, and
the survival times have an exponential distribution. For other values of
*γ*, the hazard function increases or decreases monotonically, that is,
it does not change direction. The shape of the hazard function depends
critically on the value of *γ*, and so *γ* is known as
[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p150 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 150. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}the *shape parameter*, while the parameter *λ* is a
*scale parameter*. The general form of this hazard function for
different values of *γ* is shown in [Figure
5.3](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_3){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_3}.

<figure id="fig5_3" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_3.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_3" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.3</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">The form of the Weibull hazard function,
<span
class="math inline"><em>h</em>(<em>t</em>) = <em>λ</em><em>γ</em><em>t</em><sup><em>γ</em> − 1</sup></span>,
for different values of <em>γ</em>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

For this particular choice of hazard function, the survivor function is
given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = \exp\left\{ {- {\int_{0}^{t}\lambda}\gamma u^{\gamma - 1}\,\text{d}u} \right\} = \exp( - \lambda t^{\gamma}).}
\end{array}$$[(5.5)]{.equationnum}

The corresponding probability density function is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}\exp( - \lambda t^{\gamma}),}
\end{array}$$

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$, which is the density of a random variable
that has a *Weibull distribution* with scale parameter *λ* and shape
parameter *γ*. This distribution will be denoted $W(\lambda,\gamma)$.
The right-hand tail of this distribution is longer than the left-hand
one, and so the distribution is positively skewed.

The mean, or expected value, of a random variable *T* that has a
$W(\lambda,\gamma)$ distribution can be shown to be given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{E}\,(T) = \lambda^{- 1/\gamma}\Gamma(\gamma^{- 1} + 1),}
\end{array}$$

where $\Gamma(x)$ is the gamma function defined by the integral

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\Gamma(x) = {\int_{0}^{\infty}u^{x - 1}}e^{- u}\,\text{d}u.}
\end{array}$$[(5.6)]{.equationnum}

The value of this integral is $(x - 1)!$, and so for integer values of
*x* it can easily be calculated. The function is also defined for
non-integer values of *x*, and can then be evaluated as a standard
function in many software packages.

Since the Weibull distribution is skewed, a more appropriate, and more
tractable, summary of the location of the distribution is the median
survival time. This is the value $t(50)$ such that
$S\left\{ t(50) \right\} = 0.5$, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\exp\left\{ {- \lambda{\lbrack t(50)\rbrack}^{\gamma}} \right\} = 0.5,}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p151 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 151. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(50) = \left\{ {\frac{1}{\lambda}\log 2} \right\}^{1/\gamma}.}
\end{array}$$

More generally, the *p*th percentile of the Weibull distribution,
$t(p)$, is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(p) = \left\{ {\frac{1}{\lambda}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right)} \right\}^{1/\gamma}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.7)]{.equationnum}

The median and other percentiles of the Weibull distribution are
therefore much simpler to compute than the mean of the distribution.

The hazard function and corresponding probability density function for
Weibull distributions with a median of 20, and shape parameters
$\gamma = 0.5$, 1.5 and 3.0, are shown in [Figures
5.4](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_4){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_4} and
[5.5](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_5){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_5}, respectively.
The corresponding value of the scale parameter, *λ*, for these three
Weibull distributions is 0.15, 0.0078 and 0.000087, respectively.

<figure id="fig5_4" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_4.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_4" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.4</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Hazard functions for a Weibull distribution
with a median of 20 and <span class="math inline"><em>γ</em>=</span>
0.5, 1.5 and 3.0.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig5_5" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_5.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_5" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.5</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Probability density functions for a Weibull
distribution with a median of 20 and <span
class="math inline"><em>γ</em>=</span> 0.5, 1.5 and
3.0.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

Since the Weibull hazard function can take a variety of forms, depending
on the value of the shape parameter, *γ*, and appropriate summary
statistics can be easily obtained, this distribution is widely used in
the parametric analysis of survival data.
:::

::: section
### 5.1.3 The log-logistic distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_3 .head2}

One limitation of the Weibull hazard function is that it is a monotonic
function of time. However, situations in which the hazard function
changes direction can arise. For example, following a heart transplant,
a patient faces an increasing hazard of death over the first ten days or
so after the transplant, while the body adapts to the new organ. The
hazard then decreases with time as the patient recovers. In situations
such as this, a unimodal hazard function may be appropriate.

A particular form of unimodal hazard is the function

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \frac{e^{\theta}\kappa t^{\kappa - 1}}{1 + e^{\theta}t^{\kappa}},}
\end{array}$$[(5.8)]{.equationnum}

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p152 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 152. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}for $0\leqslant t < \infty$, $\kappa > 0$. This
hazard function decreases monotonically if $\kappa\leqslant 1$, but if
$\kappa > 1$, the hazard has a single mode. The survivor function
corresponding to the hazard function in Equation (5.8) is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = \left\{ {1 + e^{\theta}t^{\kappa}} \right\}^{- 1},}
\end{array}$$[(5.9)]{.equationnum}

and the probability density function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \frac{e^{\theta}\kappa t^{\kappa - 1}}{{(1 + e^{\theta}t^{\kappa})}^{2}}.}
\end{array}$$

This is the density of a random variable *T* that has a *log-logistic
distribution*, with parameters $\theta,\kappa$. The distribution is so
called because the variable $\log T$ has a *logistic distribution*, a
symmetric distribution whose probability density function is very
similar to that of the normal distribution.

The *p*th percentile of the log-logistic distribution is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(p) = \left( \frac{p\, e^{- \theta}}{100 - p} \right)^{1/\kappa},}
\end{array}$$

and so the median of the distribution is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(50) = e^{- \theta/\kappa}.}
\end{array}$$

The hazard functions for log-logistic distributions with a median of 20
and $\kappa = 0.5$, 2.0 and 5.0 are shown in [Figure
5.6](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_6){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_6}. The
corresponding values of *θ* for these distributions are $- 1.5$, $- 6.0$
and $- 15.0$, respectively.

<figure id="fig5_6" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_6.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_6" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.6</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Hazard functions for a log-logistic
distribution with a median of 20 and <span
class="math inline"><em>κ</em>=</span> 0.5, 2.0 and
5.0.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: section
### 5.1.4 The lognormal distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_4 .head2}

The *lognormal distribution* is also defined for random variables that
take positive values, and so may be used as a model for survival data. A
random variable, *T*, is said to have a []{#chapter5.xhtml_p153
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 153. " role="doc-pagebreak"}lognormal
distribution, with parameters *μ* and *σ*, if $\log T$ has a normal
distribution with mean *μ* and variance *σ*^2^. The probability density
function of *T* is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \frac{1}{\sigma\left. \sqrt{}(2\pi) \right.}t^{- 1}\exp\left\{ {- {(\log t - \mu)}^{2}/2\sigma^{2}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$, $\sigma > 0$, from which the survivor and
hazard functions can be derived. The survivor function of the lognormal
distribution is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = 1 - \Phi\left( \frac{\log t - \mu}{\sigma} \right),}
\end{array}$$[(5.10)]{.equationnum}

where $\Phi( \cdot )$ is the standard normal distribution function,
given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\Phi(z) = \frac{1}{\left. \sqrt{}(2\pi) \right.}{\int_{- \infty}^{z}\exp}\left( {- u^{2}/2} \right)\text{d}u.}
\end{array}$$

The *p*th percentile of the distribution is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(p) = \exp\left\{ {\sigma\Phi^{- 1}(p/100) + \mu} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where $\Phi^{- 1}(p/100)$, the *p*th percentile of the standard normal
distribution, is sometimes called the *probit* of $p/100$. In
particular, the median survival time under this distribution is simply
$t(50) = e^{\mu}$.

The hazard function can be found from the relation $h(t) = f(t)/S(t)$.
This function is zero when $t = 0$, increases to a maximum and then
decreases to zero as *t* tends to infinity. The fact that the survivor
and hazard functions can only be expressed in terms of integrals limits
the usefulness of this model. Moreover, in view of the similarity of the
normal and logistic distributions, the lognormal model will tend to be
very similar to the log-logistic model.
:::

::: section
### 5.1.5 The Gompertz distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_5 .head2}

The Gompertz distribution was introduced by Gompertz in 1825 as a model
for human mortality and is now used in demography and the biological
sciences. The hazard function []{#chapter5.xhtml_p154 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 154. " role="doc-pagebreak"}of the distribution is
given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \lambda e^{\theta t},}
\end{array}$$

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$, and $\lambda > 0$. In the particular case
where $\theta = 0$, the hazard function has a constant value, *λ*, and
the survival times then have an exponential distribution. The parameter
*θ* determines the shape of the hazard function, positive values leading
to a hazard function that increases with time. The hazard function can
also be expressed as $h(t) = \exp(\alpha + \theta t)$, which shows that
the log-hazard function is linear in *t*. On the other hand, from
Equation (5.4), the Weibull log-hazard function is linear in $\log t$.
Like the Weibull hazard function, the Gompertz hazard increases or
decreases monotonically.

The survivor function of the Gompertz distribution is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = \exp\left\{ {\frac{\lambda}{\theta}(1 - e^{\theta t})} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and the corresponding density function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \lambda e^{\theta t}\exp\left\{ {\frac{\lambda}{\theta}(1 - e^{\theta t})} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

The *p*th percentile is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(p) = \frac{1}{\theta}\log\left\{ {1 - \frac{\theta}{\lambda}\log\left( \frac{100 - p}{100} \right)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

from which the median survival time is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t(50) = \frac{1}{\theta}\log\left\{ {1 + \frac{\theta}{\lambda}\log 2} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

A plot of the Gompertz hazard function for distributions with a median
of 20 and $\theta = - 0.2,0.02$ and 0.05 is shown in [Figure
5.7](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_7){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_7}. The
corresponding values of *λ* are 0.141, 0.028 and 0.020.

<figure id="fig5_7" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_7.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_7" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.7</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Hazard functions for a Gompertz distribution
with a median of 20 and <span class="math inline"><em>θ</em>=</span>
-0.2, 0.02 and 0.05.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: section
### 5.1.6 []{#chapter5.xhtml_p155 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 155. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The gamma distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_6 .head2}

The probability density function of a *gamma distribution* with mean
$\rho/\lambda$ and variance $\rho/\lambda^{2}$ is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \frac{\lambda^{\rho}t^{\rho - 1}e^{- \lambda t}}{\Gamma(\rho)},}
\end{array}$$[(5.11)]{.equationnum}

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$, $\lambda > 0$, and $\rho > 0$. As for the
lognormal distribution, the survivor function of the gamma distribution
can only be expressed as an integral, and we write

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = 1 - \Gamma_{\lambda t}(\rho),}
\end{array}$$

where $\Gamma_{\lambda t}(\rho)$ is known as the *incomplete gamma
function*, given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\Gamma_{\lambda t}(\rho) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(\rho)}{\int_{0}^{\lambda t}u^{\rho - 1}}e^{- u}\,\text{d}u.}
\end{array}$$

The hazard function for the gamma distribution is then
$h(t) = f(t)/S(t)$. This hazard function increases monotonically if
$\rho > 1$ and decreases if $\rho < 1$, and tends to *λ* as *t* tends to
∞.

When $\rho = 1$, the gamma distribution reduces to the exponential
distribution described in [Section 5.1.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_1), and
so this distribution, like the Weibull distribution, includes the
exponential distribution as a special case. Indeed, the gamma
distribution is quite similar to the Weibull, and inferences based on
either model will often be very similar.

A generalisation of the gamma distribution is actually more useful than
the gamma distribution itself, since it includes the Weibull and
lognormal distributions as special cases. This model, known as the
*generalised gamma distribution*, may therefore be used to discriminate
between alternative parametric models for survival data.

The probability density function of the generalised gamma distribution
is an extension of the gamma density in Equation (5.11), that includes
an additional parameter, *θ*, where $\theta > 0$, and is defined by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \frac{\theta\lambda^{\rho\theta}t^{\rho\theta - 1}\exp\left\{ - {(\lambda t)}^{\theta} \right\}}{\Gamma(\rho)},}
\end{array}$$

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$. The survivor function for this distribution
is again defined in terms of the incomplete gamma function and is given
by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = 1 - \Gamma_{{(\lambda t)}^{\theta}}(\rho),}
\end{array}$$

and the hazard function is again found from $h(t) = f(t)/S(t)$. This
distribution leads to a wide range of shapes for the hazard function,
governed by the parameter *θ*. This parameter is therefore termed the
*shape parameter* of the distribution. When $\rho = 1$, the distribution
becomes the Weibull, when $\theta = 1$, the gamma, and as
$\left. \rho\rightarrow\infty \right.$, the lognormal.
:::

::: section
### 5.1.7 The inverse Gaussian distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_7 .head2}

The *inverse Gaussian distribution* is a flexible model that has some
important theoretical properties. The probability density function of
the distribution which has mean *μ* and scale parameter *λ* is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \left( \frac{\lambda}{2\pi t^{3}} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\exp\left\{ \frac{\lambda(t - \mu^{2})}{2\mu^{2}t} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

for $0\leqslant t < \infty$, and $\lambda > 0$. The corresponding
survivor function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = \Phi\left\{ {\left( {1 - t\mu^{- 1}} \right)\left. \sqrt{}\left( {\lambda t^{- 1}} \right) \right.} \right\} - \exp(2\lambda/\mu)\,\Phi\left\{ {- \left( {1 + t\mu^{- 1}} \right)\left. \sqrt{}\left( {\lambda t^{- 1}} \right) \right.} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p156 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 156. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and the hazard function is found from the ratio of
the density and survivor functions. However, the complicated form of the
survivor function makes this distribution difficult to work with.
:::

::: section
### 5.1.8 Some other distributions {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_8 .head2}

Although a number of distributions for survival data have already been
considered in some detail, there are others that can be useful in
specific circumstances.

When the hazard of death is expected to increase or decrease with time
in the short term, and to then become constant, a hazard function that
follows a *general exponential curve* or *Mitscherlich curve* may be
appropriate. We would then take the hazard function to be

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \theta - \beta e^{- \gamma t},}
\end{array}$$

where $\theta > 0$, $\beta > 0$ and $\gamma > 0$. This is essentially a
Gompertz hazard function, defined in [Section
5.1.5](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_5), with an additional constant. The
general shape of this function is depicted in [Figure
5.8](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_8){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_8}. This function
has a value of $\theta - \beta$ when $t = 0$ and increases to a
horizontal asymptote at a hazard of *θ*. Similarly the function

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \theta + \beta e^{- \gamma t},}
\end{array}$$

<figure id="fig5_8" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_8.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_8" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.8</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">An asymptotic hazard function, where <span
class="math inline"><em>h</em>(<em>t</em>) = <em>θ</em> − <em>β</em><em>e</em><sup>−<em>γ</em><em>t</em></sup></span>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

where $\theta > 0$, $\beta > 0$ and $\gamma > 0$, could be used to model
a hazard which decreases from $\theta + \beta$ to a horizontal asymptote
at *θ*.

Using Equation (1.6), the corresponding survivor function can be found,
from which the probability density function can be obtained. The
probability distribution corresponding to this specification of the
hazard function is known as the *Gompertz-Makeham distribution*.

To model a hazard function that decreases and then increases
symmetrically about the minimum value, a quadratic hazard function might
be suitable. Thus, if

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \theta + \beta t + \gamma t^{2},}
\end{array}$$

for values of $\theta,\beta$ and *γ* which give the required shape of
hazard and ensure that $h(t)\geqslant 0$, explicit forms for the
survivor function and probability density function can be obtained.

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p157 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 157. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Another form of hazard function that decreases to a
single minimum and increases thereafter is the 'bathtub' hazard. The
model with

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = \alpha t + \frac{\beta}{1 + \gamma t}}
\end{array}$$

provides a straightforward representation of this form of hazard, and
corresponding expressions for the survivor and density functions can be
found.
:::
:::::::::::

::: section
## 5.2 Assessing the suitability of a parametric model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_2 .head1}

Prior to fitting a model based on an assumed parametric form for the
hazard function, a preliminary study of the validity of this assumption
should be carried out. One approach would be to estimate the hazard
function using the methods outlined in [Section
2.3](#chapter2.xhtml_sec2_3). If the hazard function was reasonably
constant over time, this would indicate that the exponential
distribution might be a suitable model for the data. On the other hand,
if the hazard function increased or decreased monotonically with
increasing survival time, a model based on the Weibull distribution
would be indicated.

A more informative way of assessing whether a particular distribution
for the survival times is plausible is to compare the survivor function
for the data with that of a chosen model. This is greatly helped by
transforming the survivor function to produce a plot that should give a
straight line if the assumed model is appropriate.

Suppose that a single sample of survival data is available, and that a
Weibull distribution for the survival times is contemplated. Since the
survivor function for a Weibull distribution, with scale parameter *λ*
and shape parameter *γ*, is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = \exp\left\{ {- \lambda t^{\gamma}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

taking the logarithm of $S(t)$, multiplying by $- 1$, and taking
logarithms a second time, gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\left\{ {- \log S(t)} \right\} = \log\lambda + \gamma\log t.}
\end{array}$$[(5.12)]{.equationnum}

We now substitute the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function,
$\hat{S}(t)$, for $S(t)$ in Equation (5.12). If the Weibull assumption
is tenable, $\hat{S}(t)$ will be 'close' to $S(t)$, and a plot of
$\log\left\{ - \log\hat{S}(t) \right\}$ against $\log t$ would then give
an approximately straight line. From Equation (1.8), the cumulative
hazard function, $H(t)$, is $- \log S(t)$ and so
$\log\left\{ - \log S(t) \right\}$ is the log-cumulative hazard. A plot
of the values of $\log\left\{ - \log\hat{S}(t) \right\}$ against
$\log t$ is a log-cumulative hazard plot, introduced in [Section
4.4.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_1) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml).

If the log-cumulative hazard plot gives a straight line, the plot can be
used to provide a rough estimate of the two parameters of the Weibull
distribution. Specifically, from Equation (5.12), the intercept and
slope of the straight line will be $\log\lambda$ and *γ*, respectively.
Thus, the slope of the line in a log-cumulative hazard plot gives an
estimate of the shape parameter, and the exponent of the intercept
provides an estimate of the scale parameter. Note that if the slope of
the log-cumulative hazard plot is close to unity, the survival times
could be modelled using an exponential distribution.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_1" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 5.1]{.boxnum}](#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Time to discontinuation of the use of an
IUD]{.boxht}

In [Example 2.3](#chapter2.xhtml_exam2_3){#chapter5.xhtml_b2exam2_3},
the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function, $\hat{S}(t)$, for
the data on the time to discontinuation of an intrauterine device was
obtained. A log-cumulative hazard plot for these data, that is, a plot
of $\log\left\{ - \log\hat{S}(t) \right\}$ against $\log t$, is shown in
[Figure 5.9](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_9){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_9}.

<figure id="fig5_9" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_9.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_9" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.9</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Log-cumulative hazard plot for the data from
<a href="#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_1" id="chapter5.xhtml_b5exam1_1">Example
1.1</a>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The plot indicates that there is a straight line relationship between
the log-cumulative hazard and $\log t$, confirming that the Weibull
distribution is an appropriate model for the discontinuation times. From
the graph, the intercept of the line is approximately $- 6.0$ and the
slope is approximately 1.25. Approximate estimates of the parameters of
the Weibull []{#chapter5.xhtml_p158 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 158. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}distribution are therefore
$\lambda^{*} = \exp( - 6.0) = 0.002$ and $\gamma^{*} = 1.25$. The
estimated value of *γ*, the shape parameter of the Weibull distribution,
is quite close to unity, suggesting that the discontinuation times might
be adequately modelled by an exponential distribution.

</aside>

A similar procedure can be used to assess the suitability of the
log-logistic distribution, using a transformation of the survivor
function that leads to a straight line plot. From Equation (5.9), the
odds of surviving beyond time *t* are

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{S(t)}{1 - S(t)} = e^{- \theta}t^{- \kappa},}
\end{array}$$

and so the log-odds of survival beyond *t* can be expressed as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\left\{ \frac{S(t)}{1 - S(t)} \right\} = - \theta - \kappa\log t.}
\end{array}$$

If the survivor function for the data is estimated using the
Kaplan-Meier estimate, and the estimated log-odds of survival beyond *t*
are plotted against $\log t$, a straight line plot will be obtained if a
log-logistic model for the survival times is suitable. Estimates of the
parameters of the log-logistic distribution, *θ* and *κ*, can be
obtained from the intercept and slope of the straight line plot.

The suitability of other parametric models can be investigated along
similar lines. For example, from the survivor function of the lognormal
distribution, given in Equation (5.10),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\Phi^{- 1}\left\{ 1 - S(t) \right\} = \frac{\log t - \mu}{\sigma},}
\end{array}$$

and so a plot of $\Phi^{- 1}\left\{ 1 - \hat{S}(t) \right\}$ against
$\log t$ should give a straight line, if the lognormal model is
appropriate. The slope and intercept of this line provide estimates of
$\sigma^{- 1}$ and $- \mu/\sigma$, respectively.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_2" role="doc-tip">

[Example 5.2]{.boxnum} [Time to discontinuation of the use of an
IUD]{.boxht}

In [Example 5.1](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_1){#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_1}, a
log-cumulative hazard plot was used to evaluate the fit of the Weibull
distribution to the data on times to discontinuation of an intrauterine
device (IUD), given []{#chapter5.xhtml_p159 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 159. " role="doc-pagebreak"}in [Example
1.1](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_1). We now consider whether the log-logistic
distribution is appropriate. A plot of
$\log\left\{ \hat{S}(t)/\lbrack 1 - \hat{S}(t)\rbrack \right\}$ against
$\log t$ for the data on the times to discontinuation of an IUD is shown
in [Figure 5.10](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_10){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_10}.

<figure id="fig5_10" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_10.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_10" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.10</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">A plot of the estimated log-odds of
discontinuation after <em>t</em> against <span
class="math inline">log <em>t</em></span> for the data from <a
href="#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_1">Example 1.1</a>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

From this plot, it appears that the relationship between the estimated
log-odds of discontinuing use of the contraceptive after time *t*, and
$\log t$, is reasonably straight. This suggests that a log-logistic
model could be used to model the observed data.

Notice that there is very little difference in the extent of departure
from linearity in the plots of [Figures 5.9](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_9) and
[5.10](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_10). This means that either the Weibull
distribution or the log-logistic distribution is likely to be
satisfactory, even though the estimated hazard function under these two
distributions may be quite different. Indeed, when survival data are
obtained for a relatively small number of individuals, as in this
example, there will often be little to choose between alternative
distributional models for the data. The model that is the most
convenient for the purpose in hand will then be adopted.

Informal estimates of unknown parameters in a probability distribution
for survival times, obtained using graphical methods, can also be used
to estimate functions of these, such as the median of the distribution.
However, this does not lead to a measure of the precision with which the
quantities have been estimated. A more formal way of fitting parametric
models to survival data is developed in the next section.

</aside>
:::

:::: section
## 5.3 Fitting a parametric model to a single sample {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_3 .head1}

Parametric models can be fitted to an observed set of survival data
using the method of maximum likelihood, outlined in [Section
3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). Consider
first the situation where actual survival times have been observed for
*n* individuals, so that there are no censored observations. If the
probability density function of the random variable associated with
survival time is $f(t)$, the likelihood of the *n* observations
$t_{1},t_{2},\ldots,t_{n}$ is simply the product

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}f}(t_{i}).}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p160 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 160. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}This likelihood will be a function of the unknown
parameters in the probability density function, and the maximum
likelihood estimates of these parameters are those values for which the
likelihood function is a maximum. In practice, it is generally more
convenient to work with the logarithm of the likelihood function. Those
values of the unknown parameters in the density function that maximise
the log-likelihood are of course the same values that maximise the
likelihood function itself.

We now consider the more usual situation where the survival data
includes one or more censored survival times. Specifically, suppose that
*r* of the *n* individuals die at times $t_{1},t_{2},\ldots,t_{r}$, and
that the survival times of the remaining $n - r$ individuals,
$t_{1}^{*},t_{2}^{*},\ldots,t_{n - r}^{*}$, are right-censored. The *r*
death times contribute a term of the form

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{j = 1}^{r}f}(t_{j})}
\end{array}$$

to the overall likelihood function. Naturally, we cannot ignore
information about the survival experience of the $n - r$ individuals for
whom a censored survival time has been recorded. If a survival time is
censored at time *t*^\*^, say, we know that the lifetime of the
individual is at least *t*^\*^, and the probability of this event is
$\text{P}(T\geqslant t^{*})$, which is $S(t^{*})$. Thus, each censored
observation contributes a term of this form to the likelihood of the *n*
observations. The total likelihood function is therefore

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{j = 1}^{r}f}(t_{j}){\prod\limits_{l = 1}^{n - r}S}(t_{l}^{*}),}
\end{array}$$[(5.13)]{.equationnum}

in which the first product is taken over the *r* death times and the
second over the $n - r$ censored survival times.

More compactly, suppose that the data are regarded as *n* pairs of
observations, where the pair for the *i*th individual is
$(t_{i},\delta_{i})$, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$. In this notation, *δ*~*i*~ is
an indicator variable that takes the value zero when the survival time
*t*~*i*~ is censored, and unity when *t*~*i*~ is an uncensored survival
time. The likelihood function can then be written as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {f(t_{i})} \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}\left\{ {S(t_{i})} \right\}^{1 - \delta_{i}}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.14)]{.equationnum}

This function, which is equivalent to that in Expression (5.13), can
then be maximised with respect to the unknown parameters in the density
and survivor functions.

An alternative expression for this likelihood function can be obtained
by writing Expression (5.14) in the form

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ \frac{f(t_{i})}{S(t_{i})} \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}S(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$

so that, from Equation (1.4) of [Chapter 1](#chapter1.xhtml), this
becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {h(t_{i})} \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}S(t_{i}).}
\end{array}$$[(5.15)]{.equationnum}

This version of the likelihood function is particularly useful when the
probability density function has a complicated form, as it often does.
Estimates of the unknown parameters in this likelihood function are then
found by maximising the logarithm of the likelihood
function.[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p161 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 161. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}

::: section
### 5.3.1 Likelihood function for randomly censored data {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_3_1 .head2}

A more careful derivation of the likelihood function in Equation (5.14)
is given in this section, which shows the relevance of the assumption of
independent censoring, referred to in [Section
1.2.1](#chapter1.xhtml_sec1_2_1) of [Chapter 1](#chapter1.xhtml).

Suppose that survival data for a sample of *n* individuals is a mixture
of event times and right-censored observations. Denote the observed time
for the *i*th individual by *t*~*i*~, and let *δ*~*i*~ be the
corresponding event indicator, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, so that
$\delta_{i} = 1$ if *t*~*i*~ is an event time, and $\delta_{i} = 0$ if
the time is censored.

The random variable associated with the event time of the *i*th
individual will be denoted by *T*~*i*~. The censoring times will be
assumed to be random, and *C*~*i*~ will denote the random variable
associated with the time to censoring. The value *t*~*i*~ is then an
observation on the random variable $Y_{i} = \text{min}(T_{i},C_{i})$.
The density and survivor functions of *T*~*i*~ will be denoted by
$f_{T_{i}}(t)$ and $S_{T_{i}}(t)$, respectively. Also, $f_{C_{i}}(t)$
and $S_{C_{i}}(t)$ will be used to denote the density and survivor
functions of the random variable associated with the censoring time,
*C*~*i*~.

We now consider the probability distribution of the pair
$(Y_{i},\delta_{i})$ for censored and uncensored observations,
respectively. Consider first the case of a censored observation, so that
$\delta_{i} = 0$. The joint distribution of *Y*~*i*~ and *δ*~*i*~ is
described by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(Y_{i} = t,\delta_{i} = 0) = \text{P}(C_{i} = t,T_{i}\geqslant t).}
\end{array}$$

This joint probability is a mixture of continuous and discrete
components, but to simplify the presentation, $\text{P}(T_{i} = t)$, for
example, will be understood to be the probability density function of
*T*~*i*~. The distribution of the event time, *T*~*i*~, is now assumed
to be independent of that of the censoring time, *C*~*i*~. Then,

$$\begin{aligned}
{\text{P}(C_{i} = t,T_{i}\geqslant t)} & {= \text{P}(C_{i} = t)\,\text{P}(T_{i}\geqslant t),} \\
 & {= f_{C_{i}}(t)S_{T_{i}}(t),}
\end{aligned}$$

so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(Y_{i} = t,\delta_{i} = 0) = f_{C_{i}}(t)S_{T_{i}}(t).}
\end{array}$$

Similarly, for an uncensored observation,

$$\begin{aligned}
{\text{P}(Y_{i} = t,\delta_{i} = 1)} & {= \text{P}(T_{i} = t,C_{i}\geqslant t),} \\
 & {= \text{P}(T_{i} = t)\,\text{P}(C_{i}\geqslant t),} \\
 & {= f_{T_{i}}(t)S_{C_{i}}(t),}
\end{aligned}$$

again assuming that the distributions of *C*~*i*~ and *T*~*i*~ are
independent. Putting these two results together, the joint probability,
or likelihood, of the *n* observations, $t_{1},t_{2},\ldots,t_{n}$, is
therefore

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left. {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ f_{T_{i}}( \right.}t_{i})S_{C_{i}}(t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}\left\{ f_{C_{i}}(t_{i})S_{T_{i}}(t_{i}) \right\}^{1 - \delta_{i}},}
\end{array}$$

which can be written as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}f_{C_{i}}}{(t_{i})}^{1 - \delta_{i}}S_{C_{i}}{(t_{i})}^{\delta_{i}} \times {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}f_{T_{i}}}{(t_{i})}^{\delta_{i}}S_{T_{i}}{(t_{i})}^{1 - \delta_{i}}.}
\end{array}$$

On the assumption of independent censoring, the first product in this
expression will not involve any parameters that are relevant to the
distribution of the survival times, and so can be regarded as a
constant. The likelihood of the observed data is then proportional to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}f_{T_{i}}}{(t_{i})}^{\delta_{i}}S_{T_{i}}{(t_{i})}^{1 - \delta_{i}},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p162 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 162. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}which was given in Expression (5.14) of this
chapter.

It can also be shown that when the study has a fixed duration, so that
individuals who have not experienced an event by the end of the study
are censored, the same likelihood function is obtained. Details are not
given here, but see [Klein and Moeschberger
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib212){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib212 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} or [Lawless
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib229){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib229 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, for example.
:::
::::

:::::: section
## 5.4 Fitting exponential and Weibull models {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4 .head1}

We now consider fitting exponential and Weibull distributions to a
single sample of survival data.

::: section
### 5.4.1 Fitting the exponential distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4_1 .head2}

Suppose that the survival times of *n* individuals,
