$t_{1},t_{2},\ldots,t_{n}$, are assumed to have an exponential
distribution with mean $\lambda^{- 1}$. Further suppose that the
survival times of *r* individuals are uncensored, and that the remaining
$n - r$ times are right-censored.

For the exponential distribution,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \lambda e^{- \lambda t},\text{\quad\quad}S(t) = e^{- \lambda t},}
\end{array}$$

and on substituting into Expression (5.14), the likelihood function for
the *n* observations is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L(\lambda) = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left( {\lambda e^{- \lambda t_{i}}} \right)^{\delta_{i}}}\left( e^{- \lambda t_{i}} \right)^{1 - \delta_{i}},}
\end{array}$$

where *δ*~*i*~ is zero if the survival time of the *i*th individual is
censored and unity otherwise. After some simplification,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L(\lambda) = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\lambda^{\delta_{i}}}e^{- \lambda t_{i}},}
\end{array}$$[(5.16)]{.equationnum}

and the corresponding log-likelihood function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\lambda) = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log\lambda - \lambda{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}}.}
\end{array}$$

Since the data contain *r* deaths,
${\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}} = r$ and the log-likelihood
function becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\lambda) = r\log\lambda - \lambda{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}}.}
\end{array}$$

We now need to identify the value $\hat{\lambda}$, for which the
log-likelihood function is a maximum. Differentiation with respect to
*λ* gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\text{d}\log L(\lambda)}{\text{d}\lambda} = \frac{r}{\lambda} - {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}},}
\end{array}$$

and equating the derivative to zero and evaluating it at $\hat{\lambda}$
gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\lambda} = r/{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}}}
\end{array}$$[(5.17)]{.equationnum}

for the maximum likelihood estimator of *λ*.

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p163 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 163. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The mean of an exponential distribution is
$\mu = \lambda^{- 1}$, and so the maximum likelihood estimator of *μ* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\mu} = {\hat{\lambda}}^{- 1} = \frac{1}{r}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}}.}
\end{array}$$

This estimator of *μ* is the total time survived by the *n* individuals
in the data set divided by the number of deaths observed. The estimator
therefore has intuitive appeal as an estimate of the mean lifetime from
censored survival data.

The standard error of either $\hat{\lambda}$ or $\hat{\mu}$ can be
obtained from the second derivative of the log-likelihood function,
using a result from the theory of maximum likelihood estimation given in
[Appendix A](#appA.xhtml_appA). Differentiating $\log L(\lambda)$ a
second time gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\lambda)}{\text{d}\lambda^{2}} = - \frac{r}{\lambda^{2}},}
\end{array}$$

and so the asymptotic variance of $\hat{\lambda}$ is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\lambda}) = \left\{ {- \,\text{E}\,\left( \frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\lambda)}{\text{d}\lambda^{2}} \right)} \right\}^{- 1} = \frac{\lambda^{2}}{r}.}
\end{array}$$

Consequently, the standard error of $\hat{\lambda}$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda}) = \hat{\lambda}/\left. \sqrt{}r. \right.}
\end{array}$$[(5.18)]{.equationnum}

This result could be used to obtain a confidence interval for the mean
survival time. In particular, the limits of a $100(1 - \alpha)$ %
confidence interval for *λ* are
$\hat{\lambda} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda})$, where
$z_{\alpha/2}$ is the upper $\alpha/2$-point of the standard normal
distribution.

In presenting the results of a survival analysis, the estimated survivor
and hazard functions, and the median and other percentiles of the
distribution of survival times, are useful. Once an estimate of *λ* has
been found, all these functions can be estimated using the results given
in [Section 5.1.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_1). In particular, under the
assumed exponential distribution, the estimated hazard function is
$\hat{h}(t) = \hat{\lambda}$ and the estimated survivor function is
$\hat{S}(t) = \exp( - \hat{\lambda}t)$. In addition, the estimated *p*th
percentile is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(p) = \frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right),}
\end{array}$$[(5.19)]{.equationnum}

and the estimated median survival time is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(50) = {\hat{\lambda}}^{- 1}\log 2.}
\end{array}$$[(5.20)]{.equationnum}

The standard error of an estimate of the *p*th percentile of the
distribution of survival times can be found using the result for the
approximate variance of a function of a random variable given in
Equation (2.8) of [Chapter 2](#chapter2.xhtml). According to this
result, an approximation to the variance of a function
$g(\hat{\lambda})$ of $\hat{\lambda}$ is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,\left\{ g(\hat{\lambda}) \right\} \approx \left\{ \frac{\text{d}g(\hat{\lambda})}{\text{d}\hat{\lambda}} \right\}^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\lambda}).}
\end{array}$$[(5.21)]{.equationnum}

Using this result, the approximate variance of the estimated *p*th
percentile is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\} \approx \left\{ {- \frac{1}{{\hat{\lambda}}^{2}}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right)} \right\}^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\lambda}).}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p164 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 164. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Taking the square root, we get

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\} = \frac{1}{{\hat{\lambda}}^{2}}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right)\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda}),}
\end{array}$$

and on substituting for $\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda})$ from Equation
(5.18) and $\hat{t}(p)$ from Equation (5.19), we find

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\} = \hat{t}(p)/\left. \sqrt{}r. \right.}
\end{array}$$[(5.22)]{.equationnum}

In particular, the standard error of the estimated median survival time
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(50) \right\} = \hat{t}(50)/\left. \sqrt{}r. \right.}
\end{array}$$[(5.23)]{.equationnum}

Confidence intervals for a true percentile are best obtained from
exponentiating the confidence limits for the logarithm of the
percentile. This procedure ensures that confidence limits for the
percentile will be non-negative. Again making use of the result in
Equation (5.21), the standard error of $\log\hat{t}(p)$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \log\hat{t}(p) \right\} = \hat{t}{(p)}^{- 1}\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and after substituting for $\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\}$
from Equation (5.22), this standard error becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \log\hat{t}(p) \right\} = 1/\left. \sqrt{}r. \right.}
\end{array}$$

Using this result, $100(1 - \alpha)$ % confidence limits for the
100*p*th percentile are
$\exp\left\{ \log\hat{t}(p) \pm z_{\alpha/2}/\left. \sqrt{}r \right\} \right.$,
that is,
$\hat{t}(p)\exp\left\{ \pm z_{\alpha/2}/\left. \sqrt{}r \right\} \right.$,
where $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the upper $\alpha/2$-point of the standard
normal distribution.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_3" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 5.3]{.boxnum}](#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_3){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Time to discontinuation of the use of an
IUD]{.boxht}

In this example, the data of [Example 1.1](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_1) on
the times to discontinuation of an IUD for 18 women are analysed under
the assumption of a constant hazard of discontinuation. An exponential
distribution is therefore fitted to the discontinuation times. For these
data, the total of the observed and right-censored discontinuation times
is 1046 days, and the number of uncensored times is 9. Therefore, using
Equation (5.17), $\hat{\lambda} = 9/1046 = 0.0086$, and the standard
error of $\hat{\lambda}$ from Equation (5.18) is
$\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda}) = 0.0086/\left. \sqrt{}9 = 0.0029 \right.$.
The estimated hazard function is therefore $\hat{h}(t) = 0.0086$,
$t > 0$, and the estimated survivor function is
$\hat{S}(t) = \exp( - 0.0086\, t)$. The estimated hazard and survivor
functions are shown in [Figures
5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_11){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_11} and
[5.12](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_12){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_12},
respectively.

<figure id="fig5_11" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_11.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_11" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.11</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated hazard function on fitting the
exponential distribution.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig5_12" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_12.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_12" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.12</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated survivor function on fitting the
exponential distribution.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

Estimates of the median and other percentiles of the distribution of
discontinuation times can be found from [Figure
5.12](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_12), but more accurate estimates are obtained
from Equation (5.19). In particular, using Equation (5.20), the median
discontinuation time is 81 days, and an estimate of the 90th percentile
of the distribution of discontinuation times is, from Equation (5.19),
$\hat{t}(90) = \log 10/0.0086 = 267.61$. This means that on the
assumption that the risk of discontinuing the use of an IUD is
independent of time, 90% of women will have a discontinuation time of
less than 268 days.

From Equation (5.23), the standard error of the estimated median time to
discontinuation is $80.56/\left. \sqrt{}9 \right.$, that is, 26.85 days.
The limits of a 95% confidence interval for the true median
discontinuation time are

$$\begin{array}{r}
{80.56\exp\left\{ \pm 1.96/\left. \sqrt{}9 \right\}, \right.}
\end{array}$$

and so the interval is from 42 days to 155 days. Confidence intervals
for other percentiles can be calculated in a similar manner.

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 5.4.2 []{#chapter5.xhtml_p165 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 165. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Fitting the Weibull distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4_2 .head2}

The survival times of *n* individuals are now taken to be a censored
sample from a Weibull distribution with scale parameter *λ* and shape
parameter *γ*. Suppose that there are *r* deaths among the *n*
individuals and $n - r$ right-censored survival times. We can again use
Expression (5.14) to obtain the likelihood of the sample data. The
probability density, survivor and hazard function of a
$W(\lambda,\gamma)$ distribution are given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}\exp( - \lambda t^{\gamma}),\quad S(t) = \exp( - \lambda t^{\gamma}),\quad h(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p166 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 166. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and so, from Expression (5.14), the likelihood of
the *n* survival times is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {\lambda\gamma t_{i}^{\gamma - 1}\exp( - \lambda t_{i}^{\gamma})} \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}\left\{ {\exp( - \lambda t_{i}^{\gamma})} \right\}^{1 - \delta_{i}},}
\end{array}$$

where *δ*~*i*~ is zero if the *i*th survival time is censored and unity
otherwise. Equivalently, from Expression (5.15), the likelihood function
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {\lambda\gamma t_{i}^{\gamma - 1}} \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}\exp( - \lambda t_{i}^{\gamma}).}
\end{array}$$

This is regarded as a function of *λ* and *γ*, the unknown parameters in
the Weibull distribution, and so can be written $L(\lambda,\gamma)$. The
corresponding log-likelihood function is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\lambda,\gamma) = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log(\lambda\gamma) + (\gamma - 1){\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log t_{i} - \lambda{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}^{\gamma}},}
\end{array}$$

and noting that ${\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}} = r$, the
log-likelihood becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\lambda,\gamma) = r\log(\lambda\gamma) + (\gamma - 1){\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log t_{i} - \lambda{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}^{\gamma}}.}
\end{array}$$

The maximum likelihood estimates of *λ* and *γ* are found by
differentiating this function with respect to *λ* and *γ*, equating the
derivatives to zero, and evaluating them at $\hat{\lambda}$ and
$\hat{\gamma}$. The resulting equations are

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{r}{\hat{\lambda}} - {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}^{\hat{\gamma}}} = 0,}
\end{array}$$[(5.24)]{.equationnum}

and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{r}{\hat{\gamma}} + {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log t_{i} - \hat{\lambda}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}^{\hat{\gamma}}}\log t_{i} = 0.}
\end{array}$$[(5.25)]{.equationnum}

From Equation (5.24),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\lambda} = r/{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}^{\hat{\gamma}}},}
\end{array}$$[(5.26)]{.equationnum}

and on substituting for $\hat{\lambda}$ in Equation (5.25), we get the
equation

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{r}{\hat{\gamma}} + {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log t_{i} - \frac{r}{\sum\limits_{i}t_{i}^{\hat{\gamma}}}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}^{\hat{\gamma}}}\log t_{i} = 0.}
\end{array}$$[(5.27)]{.equationnum}

This is a non-linear equation in $\hat{\gamma}$, which can only be
solved numerically using an iterative procedure. Once the estimate,
$\hat{\gamma}$, which satisfies Equation (5.27), has been found,
Equation (5.26) can be used to obtain $\hat{\lambda}$.

In practice, a numerical method, such as the Newton-Raphson procedure,
is used to find the values $\hat{\lambda}$ and $\hat{\gamma}$ which
maximise the likelihood function simultaneously. This procedure was
described in [Section 3.3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3_3) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml), in connection with fitting the Cox regression
model. In that section it was noted that an important by-product of the
Newton-Raphson procedure is an approximation to the variance-covariance
matrix of the parameter estimates, from which their standard errors can
be obtained.

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p167 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 167. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Once estimates of the parameters *λ* and *γ* have
been found from fitting the Weibull distribution to the observed data,
percentiles of the survival time distribution can be estimated using
Equation (5.7). The estimated *p*th percentile of the distribution is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(p) = \left\{ {\frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right)} \right\}^{1/\hat{\gamma}},}
\end{array}$$[(5.28)]{.equationnum}

and so the estimated median survival time is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(50) = \left\{ {\frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}}\log 2} \right\}^{1/\hat{\gamma}}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.29)]{.equationnum}

An expression for the standard error of a percentile of the Weibull
distribution, and a corresponding confidence interval, is derived in
[Section 5.4.3](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4_3).
:::

::: section
### 5.4.3 Standard error of a percentile of the Weibull distribution {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4_3 .head2}

The standard error of the estimated *p*th percentile of the Weibull
distribution with scale parameter *λ* and shape parameter *γ*,
$\hat{t}(p)$, is most easily found from the variance of
$\log\hat{t}(p)$. Now, from Equation (5.28),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\hat{t}(p) = \frac{1}{\hat{\gamma}}\log\left\{ {{\hat{\lambda}}^{- 1}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\hat{t}(p) = \frac{1}{\hat{\gamma}}\left\{ {c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{c_{p} = \log\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right).}
\end{array}$$

This is a function of two parameter estimates, $\hat{\lambda}$ and
$\hat{\gamma}$.

To obtain the variance of $\log\hat{t}(p)$, we use the general result
that the approximate variance of a function
$g({\hat{\theta}}_{1},{\hat{\theta}}_{2})$ of two parameter estimates,
${\hat{\theta}}_{1}$, ${\hat{\theta}}_{2}$, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left( \frac{\partial g}{\partial{\hat{\theta}}_{1}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,({\hat{\theta}}_{1}) + \left( \frac{\partial g}{\partial{\hat{\theta}}_{2}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,({\hat{\theta}}_{2}) + 2\left( {\frac{\partial g}{\partial{\hat{\theta}}_{1}}\frac{\partial g}{\partial{\hat{\theta}}_{2}}} \right)\,\text{cov}\,({\hat{\theta}}_{1},{\hat{\theta}}_{2}).}
\end{array}$$[(5.30)]{.equationnum}

This is an extension of the result given in Equation (2.8) of [Chapter
2](#chapter2.xhtml) for the approximate variance of a function of a
single random variable. Using Equation (5.30),

$$\begin{aligned}
{\,\text{var}\,\left\{ {\log\hat{t}(p)} \right\}} & {\approx \left( \frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial\hat{\lambda}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\lambda}) + \left( \frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial\hat{\gamma}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\gamma})} \\
 & {\text{\quad\quad} + 2\frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial\hat{\lambda}}\frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial\hat{\gamma}}\,\text{cov}\,(\hat{\lambda},\hat{\gamma}).}
\end{aligned}$$

Now, the derivatives of $\log\hat{t}(p)$ with respect to $\hat{\lambda}$
and $\hat{\gamma}$ are given by

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial\hat{\lambda}} & {= - \frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}},} \\
\frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial\hat{\gamma}} & {= - \frac{c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda}}{{\hat{\gamma}}^{2}},}
\end{aligned}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p168 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 168. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and so the approximate variance of $\log\hat{t}(p)$
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{1}{{\hat{\lambda}}^{2}{\hat{\gamma}}^{2}}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\lambda}) + \frac{\left( {c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda}} \right)^{2}}{{\hat{\gamma}}^{4}}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\gamma}) + \frac{2\left( {c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda}} \right)}{\hat{\lambda}{\hat{\gamma}}^{3}}\,\text{cov}\,(\hat{\lambda},\hat{\gamma}).}
\end{array}$$[(5.31)]{.equationnum}

The variance of $\hat{t}(p)$ itself is found from the result in Equation
(2.8) of [Chapter 2](#chapter2.xhtml), from which

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\} \approx \hat{t}{(p)}^{2}\,\text{var}\,\left\{ \log\hat{t}(p) \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(5.32)]{.equationnum}

and using Expression (5.31),

$$\begin{aligned}
{\,\text{var}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\}} & {\approx \frac{\hat{t}{(p)}^{2}}{{\hat{\lambda}}^{2}{\hat{\gamma}}^{4}}\left\{ {{\hat{\gamma}}^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\lambda}) + {\hat{\lambda}}^{2}\left( {c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\gamma})}~ \right.} \\
 & {\text{\quad\quad}\left. ~{+ 2\hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}\left( {c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda}} \right)\,\text{cov}\,(\hat{\lambda},\hat{\gamma})\,} \right\}.}
\end{aligned}$$

The standard error of $\hat{t}(p)$ is the square root of this
expression, given by

$$\begin{aligned}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ {\hat{t}(p)} \right\}} & {= \frac{\hat{t}(p)}{\hat{\lambda}{\hat{\gamma}}^{2}}\left\{ {{\hat{\gamma}}^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\lambda}) + {\hat{\lambda}}^{2}\left( {c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\gamma})}~ \right.} \\
 & {\text{\quad\quad} + \left. ~{2\hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}\left( {c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda}} \right)\,\text{cov}\,(\hat{\lambda},\hat{\gamma})\,} \right\}^{\frac{1}{2}}.}
\end{aligned}$$[(5.33)]{.equationnum}

Note that for the special case of the exponential distribution, where
the shape parameter, *γ*, is equal to unity, the standard error of the
estimated *p*th percentile from Equation (5.33) is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\hat{t}(p)}{\hat{\lambda}}\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda}).}
\end{array}$$

Now, using Equation (5.18),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda}) = \hat{\lambda}/\left. \sqrt{}r, \right.}
\end{array}$$

where *r* is the number of death times in the data set, and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\} = \hat{t}(p)/\left. \sqrt{}r, \right.}
\end{array}$$

as in Equation (5.22).

A $100(1 - \alpha)$ % confidence interval for the *p*th percentile of a
Weibull distribution is found from the corresponding confidence limits
for $\log t(p)$. These limits are

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\hat{t}(p) \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \log\hat{t}(p) \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where $\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \log\hat{t}(p) \right\}$ is, from Equation
(5.32), given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \log\hat{t}(p) \right\} = \frac{1}{\hat{t}(p)}\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(5.34)]{.equationnum}

and $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the upper $\alpha/2$-point of the standard normal
distribution. The corresponding $100(1 - \alpha)$ % confidence interval
for the *p*th percentile, $t(p)$, is then
$\hat{t}(p)\exp\left\lbrack {\pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \log\hat{t}(p) \right\}} \right\rbrack$.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_4" role="doc-tip">

[Example 5.4]{.boxnum} [Time to discontinuation of the use of an
IUD]{.boxht}

In [Example 5.1](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_1), it was found that an
exponential distribution provides a satisfactory model for the data on
the discontinuation times of 18 IUD users. For comparison, a Weibull
distribution will be fitted to the same data set. This distribution is
fitted using computer []{#chapter5.xhtml_p169 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 169. " role="doc-pagebreak"}software, and from the
resulting output, the estimated scale parameter of the distribution is
found to be $\hat{\lambda} = 0.000454$, while the estimated shape
parameter is $\hat{\gamma} = 1.676$. The standard errors of these
estimates are given by $\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda}) = 0.000965$ and
$\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\gamma}) = 0.460$, respectively. Note that
approximate confidence limits for the shape parameter, *γ*, found using
$\hat{\gamma} \pm 1.96\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\gamma})$, include unity,
suggesting that the exponential distribution would provide a
satisfactory model for the discontinuation times.

The estimated hazard and survivor functions are obtained by substituting
these estimates into Equations (5.4) and (5.5), whence

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{h}(t) = \hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}t^{\hat{\gamma} - 1},}
\end{array}$$

and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{S}(t) = \exp\left( {- \hat{\lambda}t^{\hat{\gamma}}} \right).}
\end{array}$$

These two functions are shown in [Figures
5.13](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_13){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_13} and
[5.14](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_14){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_14}.

<figure id="fig5_13" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_13.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_13" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.13</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated hazard function on fitting the
Weibull distribution.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig5_14" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_14.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_14" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.14</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated survivor function on fitting the
Weibull distribution.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

Although percentiles of the discontinuation time can be read from the
estimated survivor function in [Figure 5.14](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_14),
they are better estimated using Equation (5.28). Hence, under the
Weibull distribution, the median discontinuation time can be estimated
using Equation (5.29) and is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(50) = \left\{ {\frac{1}{0.000454}\log 2} \right\}^{1/1.676} = 79.27.}
\end{array}$$

As a check, notice that this is perfectly consistent with the value of
the discontinuation time corresponding to $\hat{S}(t) = 0.5$ in [Figure
5.14](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_14). The standard error of this estimate,
from Equation (5.33) is, after much arithmetic, found to be

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(50) \right\} = 15.795.}
\end{array}$$

In order to obtain a 95% confidence interval for the median
discontinuation time, the standard error of $\log\hat{t}(50)$ is
required. From Equation (5.34),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \log\hat{t}(50) \right\} = \frac{15.795}{79.272} = 0.199,}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p170 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 170. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and so the required confidence limits for the log
median discontinuation time are $\log 79.272 \pm 1.96 \times 0.199$,
that is, $(3.982,4.763)$. The corresponding interval estimate for the
true median discontinuation time, found from exponentiating these
limits, is $(53.64,117.15)$. This means that there is a 95% chance that
the interval from 54 days to 117 days includes the true value of the
median discontinuation time. This interval is rather wide because of the
small number of actual discontinuation times in the data set.

It is interesting to compare these results with those found in [Example
5.3](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_3){#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_3}, where the
discontinuation times were modelled using an exponential distribution.
The estimated median survival times are very similar, at 80.6 days for
the exponential and 79.3 days for the Weibull model. However, the
standard error of the estimated median survival time is 26.8 days when
the times are assumed to have an exponential distribution, and only 15.8
days under the Weibull model. The median is therefore estimated more
precisely when the discontinuation times are assumed to have a Weibull
distribution.

Other percentiles of the discontinuation time distribution, and
accompanying standard errors and confidence intervals, can be found in a
similar fashion. For example, the 90th percentile, that is, the time
beyond which 10% of those in the study continue with the use of the IUD,
is 162.23 days, and 95% confidence limits for the true percentile are
from 95.41 to 275.84 days. Notice that the width of this confidence
interval is larger than that for the median discontinuation time,
reflecting the fact that the median is more precisely estimated than
other percentiles.

</aside>
:::
::::::

::::: section
## 5.5 The Weibull model for the comparison of two groups {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_5 .head1}

We saw in [Section 3.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_1) that a convenient
general model for comparing two groups of survival times is the
proportional hazards model. Here, the two groups will be labelled Group
I and Group II, and *X* will be an indicator variable that takes the
value zero if an individual is in Group I and unity if an individual is
in Group II. Under the proportional hazards model, the hazard of death
at time *t* for the *i*th individual is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = e^{\beta x_{i}}h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(5.35)]{.equationnum}

where *x*~*i*~ is the value of *X* for the *i*th individual.
Consequently, the hazard at time *t* for an individual in Group I is
$h_{0}(t)$, and that for an individual in Group II is $\psi h_{0}(t)$,
where []{#chapter5.xhtml_p171 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 171. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}$\psi = \exp(\beta)$. The quantity *β* is then the
logarithm of the ratio of the hazard for an individual in Group II, to
that of an individual in Group I.

We will now make the additional assumption that the survival times of
the individuals in Group I have a Weibull distribution with scale
parameter *λ* and shape parameter *γ*. Using Equation (5.35), the hazard
function for the individuals in this group is $h_{0}(t)$, where
$h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$. Now, also from Equation
(5.35), the hazard function for those in Group II is $\psi h_{0}(t)$,
that is, $\psi\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$. This is the hazard function
for a Weibull distribution with scale parameter $\psi\lambda$ and shape
parameter *γ*. We therefore have the result that if the survival times
of individuals in one group have a Weibull distribution with shape
parameter *γ*, and the hazard of death at time *t* for an individual in
the second group is proportional to that of an individual in the first,
the survival times of those in the second group will also have a Weibull
distribution with shape parameter *γ*. The Weibull distribution is then
said to have the *proportional hazards property*. This property is
another reason for the importance of the Weibull distribution in the
analysis of survival data.

::: section
### 5.5.1 Exploratory analysis {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_5_1 .head2}

When a single sample of survival times has a Weibull distribution
$W(\lambda,\gamma)$, the log-cumulative hazard plot, described in
[Section 5.2](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_2), will give a straight line with
intercept $\log\lambda$ and slope *γ*. It then follows that if the
survival times in a second group have a $W(\psi\lambda,\gamma)$
distribution, as they would under the proportional hazards model in
Equation (5.35), the log-cumulative hazard plot will give a straight
line, also of slope *γ*, but with intercept $\log\psi + \log\lambda$. If
the estimated log-cumulative hazard function is plotted against the
logarithm of the survival time for individuals in two groups, parallel
straight lines would mean that the assumptions of a proportional hazards
model and Weibull survival times were tenable. The vertical separation
of the two lines provides an estimate of $\beta = \log\psi$, the
logarithm of the relative hazard.

If the two lines in a log-cumulative hazard plot are essentially
straight, but not parallel, this means that the shape parameter, *γ*, is
different in the two groups, and the hazards are no longer proportional.
If the lines are not particularly straight, the Weibull model may not be
appropriate. However, if the curves can be taken to be parallel, this
would mean that the proportional hazards model is valid, and the Cox
regression model discussed in [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml) might be more
satisfactory.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_5" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 5.5]{.boxnum}](#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_5){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

In this example, we investigate whether the Weibull proportional hazards
model is likely to be appropriate for the data of [Example
1.2](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_2){#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam1_2} on the survival
times of breast cancer patients. These data relate to women classified
according to whether their tumours were positively or negatively
stained. The Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor functions for the
women in each group were shown in [Figure
2.9](#chapter2.xhtml_fig2_9){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig2_9}. From these
estimates, the log-cumulative hazards can be estimated and plotted
against $\log t$. The resulting log-cumulative hazard plot is shown in
[Figure 5.15](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_15){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_15}.

<figure id="fig5_15" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_15.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_15" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.15</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Log-cumulative hazard plot for women with
tumours that were positively stained (∗) and negatively stained
(∙).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In this figure, the lines corresponding to the two staining groups are
reasonably straight. This means that the assumption of Weibull
distributions for the survival times of the women in each group is quite
plausible. Moreover, the gradients of the two lines are very similar,
which means that the proportional hazards model is valid. The vertical
separation of the two lines provides an estimate of the log relative
hazard. From [Figure 5.15](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_15), the vertical
distance between the two straight lines is approximately 1.0, and so a
rough estimate of the hazard ratio is $e^{1.0} = 2.72$. Women in the
positively stained group would appear to have nearly three times the
risk of death at any time compared to those in the negatively stained
group. More accurate estimates of the relative hazard will be obtained
from fitting exponential and Weibull models to the data of this example,
in [Examples 5.6](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_6){#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_6}
and [5.7](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_7){#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_7}.

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 5.5.2 []{#chapter5.xhtml_p172 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 172. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Fitting the model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_5_2 .head2}

The proportional hazards model in Equation (5.35) can be fitted using
the method of maximum likelihood. To illustrate the process, we consider
the situation where the survival times in each group have an exponential
distribution.

Suppose that the observations from *n*~1~ individuals in Group I can be
expressed as $(t_{i1},\delta_{i1})$, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n_{1}$, where
$\delta_{i1}$ takes the value zero if the survival time of the *i*th
individual in that group is censored, and unity if that time is a death
time. Similarly, let $(t_{i'2},\delta_{i'2})$, $i' = 1,2,\ldots,n_{2}$,
be the observations from the *n*~2~ individuals in Group II. For
individuals in Group I, the hazard function will be taken to be *λ*, and
the probability density function and survivor function are given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t_{i1}) = \lambda e^{- \lambda t_{i1}},\text{\quad\quad}S(t_{i1}) = e^{- \lambda t_{i1}}.}
\end{array}$$

For those in Group II, the hazard function is $\psi\lambda$, and the
probability density function and survivor function are

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(t_{i'2}) = \psi\lambda e^{- \psi\lambda t_{i'2}},\text{\quad\quad}S(t_{i'2}) = e^{- \psi\lambda t_{i'2}}.}
\end{array}$$

Using Equation (5.14), the likelihood of the $n_{1} + n_{2}$
observations, $L(\psi,\lambda)$, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n_{1}}\left\{ {\lambda e^{- \lambda t_{i1}}} \right\}^{\delta_{i1}}}\left\{ e^{- \lambda t_{i1}} \right\}^{1 - \delta_{i1}}{\prod\limits_{i' = 1}^{n_{2}}\left\{ {\psi\lambda e^{- \psi\lambda t_{i'2}}} \right\}^{\delta_{i'2}}}\left\{ e^{- \psi\lambda t_{i'2}} \right\}^{1 - \delta_{i'2}},}
\end{array}$$

which simplifies to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n_{1}}\lambda^{\delta_{i1}}}e^{- \lambda t_{i1}}{\prod\limits_{i' = 1}^{n_{2}}{(\psi\lambda)}^{\delta_{i'2}}}e^{- \psi\lambda t_{i'2}}.}
\end{array}$$

If the numbers of actual death times in the two groups are *r*~1~ and
*r*~2~, respectively, then $r_{1} = {\sum\limits_{i}\delta_{i1}}$ and
$r_{2} = {\sum\limits_{i'}\delta_{i'2}}$, and the log-likelihood
function is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\psi,\lambda) = r_{1}\log\lambda - \lambda{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n_{1}}t_{i1}} + r_{2}\log(\psi\lambda) - \psi\lambda{\sum\limits_{i' = 1}^{n_{2}}t_{i'2}}.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p173 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 173. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Now write *T*~1~ and *T*~2~ for the total known
time survived by the individuals in Groups I and II, respectively. Then,
*T*~1~ and *T*~2~ are the totals of uncensored and censored survival
times in each group, so that the log-likelihood function becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\psi,\lambda) = (r_{1} + r_{2})\log\lambda + r_{2}\log\psi - \lambda(T_{1} + \psi T_{2}).}
\end{array}$$

In order to obtain the values $\hat{\psi},\hat{\lambda}$ for which this
function is a maximum, we differentiate with respect to *ψ* and *λ*, and
set the derivatives equal to zero. The resulting equations that are
satisfied by $\hat{\psi},\hat{\lambda}$ are

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{r_{2}}{\hat{\psi}} - \hat{\lambda}T_{2} = 0,}
\end{array}$$[(5.36)]{.equationnum}

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{r_{1} + r_{2}}{\hat{\lambda}} - (T_{1} + \hat{\psi}T_{2}) = 0.}
\end{array}$$[(5.37)]{.equationnum}

From Equation (5.36),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\lambda} = \frac{r_{2}}{\hat{\psi}T_{2}},}
\end{array}$$

and on substituting for $\hat{\lambda}$ in Equation (5.37) we get

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\psi} = \frac{r_{2}T_{1}}{r_{1}T_{2}}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.38)]{.equationnum}

Then, from Equation (5.36),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\lambda} = r_{1}/T_{1}.}
\end{array}$$

Both of these estimates have an intuitive justification. The estimated
value of *λ* is the reciprocal of the average time survived by
individuals in Group I, while the estimated relative hazard,
$\hat{\psi}$, is the ratio of the average times survived by the
individuals in the two groups.

The asymptotic variance-covariance matrix of the parameter estimates is
the inverse of the information matrix, whose elements are found from the
second derivatives of the log-likelihood function; see [Appendix
A](#appA.xhtml_appA). We have that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\psi,\lambda)}{\text{d}\psi^{2}} = - \frac{r_{2}}{\psi^{2}},\frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\psi,\lambda)}{\text{d}\lambda^{2}} = - \frac{r_{1} + r_{2}}{\lambda^{2}},\frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\psi,\lambda)}{\text{d}\lambda\text{d}\psi} = - T_{2},}
\end{array}$$

and the information matrix is the matrix of negative expected values of
these partial derivatives. The only second derivative for which
expectations need to be obtained is the derivative with respect to *λ*
and *ψ*, for which E$(T_{2})$ is required. This is straightforward when
the survival times have an exponential distribution, but as shown in
[Section 5.1.2](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_2), the expected value of a
survival time that has a Weibull distribution is much more difficult to
calculate. For this reason, the information matrix is usually
approximated by using the observed values of the negative second partial
derivatives. The observed information matrix is thus

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}(\psi,\lambda) = \left( \begin{array}{ll}
{r_{2}/\psi^{2}} & T_{2} \\
T_{2} & {(r_{1} + r_{2})/\lambda^{2}}
\end{array} \right),}
\end{array}$$

and the inverse of this matrix is

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\frac{1}{(r_{1} + r_{2})r_{2} - T_{2}^{2}\psi^{2}\lambda^{2}}\left( \begin{array}{ll}
{cc(r_{1} + r_{2})\psi^{2}} & {- T_{2}\psi^{2}\lambda^{2}} \\
{- T_{2}\psi^{2}\lambda^{2}} & {r_{2}\lambda^{2}}
\end{array} \right).}
\end{array}$$

The standard errors of $\hat{\psi}$ and $\hat{\lambda}$ are found by
substituting $\hat{\psi}$ and $\hat{\lambda}$ for *ψ* and *λ* in this
matrix, and taking square roots. Thus, the standard error of
$\hat{\psi}$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\psi}) = \sqrt{\frac{(r_{1} + r_{2}){\hat{\psi}}^{2}}{(r_{1} + r_{2})r_{2} - T_{2}^{2}{\hat{\psi}}^{2}{\hat{\lambda}}^{2}}}.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p174 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 174. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}On substituting for $\hat{\psi}$ and
$\hat{\lambda}$ in the denominator of this expression, this standard
error simplifies to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\psi}\sqrt{\frac{r_{1} + r_{2}}{r_{1}r_{2}}}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.39)]{.equationnum}

Similarly, the standard error of $\hat{\lambda}$ turns out to be given
by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\lambda}) = \hat{\lambda}/\left. \sqrt{}r_{1}. \right.}
\end{array}$$

The standard error of these estimates cannot be used directly in the
construction of confidence intervals for *ψ* and *λ*. The reason for
this is that the values of both parameters must be positive and their
estimated values will tend to have skewed distributions. This means that
the assumption of normality, used in constructing a confidence interval,
would not be justified. The distribution of the logarithm of an estimate
of either *ψ* or *λ* is much more likely to be symmetric, and so
confidence limits for the logarithm of the parameter are found using the
standard error of the logarithm of the parameter estimate. The resulting
confidence limits are then exponentiated to give an interval estimate
for the parameter itself.

The standard error of the logarithm of a parameter estimate can be found
using the general result given in Equation (5.21). Thus, the approximate
variance of $\log\hat{\psi}$ is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(\log\hat{\psi}) \approx {\hat{\psi}}^{- 2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\psi}),}
\end{array}$$

and so the standard error of $\log\hat{\psi}$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,(\log\hat{\psi}) \approx {\hat{\psi}}^{- 1}\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\psi}) = \sqrt{\frac{r_{1} + r_{2}}{r_{1}r_{2}}}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.40)]{.equationnum}

A $100(1 - \alpha)$ % confidence interval for the logarithm of the
relative hazard has limits
$\log\hat{\psi} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\text{se}\,(\log\hat{\psi})$, and
confidence limits for the hazard ratio *ψ* are found by exponentiating
these limits for $\log\psi$. If required, a confidence interval for *λ*
can be found in a similar manner.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_6" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 5.6]{.boxnum}](#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_6){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

The theoretical results developed in this section will now be
illustrated using the data on the survival times of breast cancer
patients. The survival times for the women in each group are assumed to
have exponential distributions, so that the hazard of death, at any
time, for a woman in the negatively stained group is a constant value,
*λ*, while that for a woman in the positively stained group is
$\psi\lambda$, where *ψ* is the hazard ratio.

From the data given in [Table
1.2](#chapter1.xhtml_tbl1_2){#chapter5.xhtml_b5tbl1_2} of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml), the numbers of death times in the negatively and
positively stained groups are, respectively, $r_{1} = 5$ and
$r_{2} = 21$. Also, the total time survived in each group is
$T_{1} = 1652$ and $T_{2} = 2679$ months. Using Equation (5.38), the
estimated hazard of death for a woman in the positively stained group,
relative to one in the negatively stained group, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\psi} = \frac{21 \times 1652}{5 \times 2679} = 2.59,}
\end{array}$$

so that a woman in the positively stained group has about two and a half
times the risk of death at any given time, compared to a woman whose
tumour was negatively stained. This is consistent with the estimated
value of *ψ* of 2.72 from the graphical procedure used in [Example
5.5](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_5){#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_5}.

Next, using Equation (5.39), the standard error of the estimated hazard
ratio is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\psi}) = 2.59\sqrt{\frac{5 + 21}{5 \times 21}} = 1.289.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p175 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 175. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In order to obtain a 95% confidence interval for
the true relative hazard, the standard error of $\log\hat{\psi}$ is
required. Using Equation (5.40), this is found to be given by
$\,\text{se}\,(\log\hat{\psi}) = 0.498$, and so 95% confidence limits
for $\log\psi$ are $\log(2.59) \pm 1.96\,\text{se}\,(\log\hat{\psi})$,
that is, $0.952 \pm (1.96 \times 0.498)$. The confidence interval for
the log relative hazard is $( - 0.024,1.927)$, and the corresponding
interval estimate for the relative hazard itself is
$(e^{- 0.024},e^{1.927})$, that is, $(0.98,6.87)$. This interval only
just includes unity, and suggests that women with positively stained
tumours have a poorer prognosis than those whose tumours were negatively
stained. This result is consistent with the result of the log-rank test
in [Example 2.12](#chapter2.xhtml_exam2_12){#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam2_12},
where a *P*-value of 0.061 was obtained on testing the hypothesis of no
group difference.

</aside>

In practice, computer software is used to fit a parametric models to two
groups of survival data, assuming proportional hazards. When the model
in Equation (5.35) is fitted, estimates of *β, λ* and *γ*, and their
standard errors, can be obtained from the resulting output. Further
calculation may then be needed to obtain an estimate of the relative
hazard, and the standard error of this estimate. In particular, the
estimated hazard ratio would be obtained as
$\hat{\psi} = \exp(\hat{\beta})$ and $\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\psi})$ found
from the equation

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\psi}) = \exp(\hat{\beta})\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\beta}),}
\end{array}$$

a result that follows from Equation (5.21).

The median and other percentiles of the survival time distributions in
the two groups can be estimated from the values of $\hat{\lambda}$ and
$\hat{\psi}$. For example, from Equation (5.28), the estimated *p*th
percentile for those in Group I is found from

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(p) = \left\{ {\frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right)} \right\}^{1/\hat{\gamma}},}
\end{array}$$

and that for individuals in Group II is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(p) = \left\{ {\frac{1}{\hat{\psi}\hat{\lambda}}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right)} \right\}^{1/\hat{\gamma}}.}
\end{array}$$

An expression similar to that in (5.33) can be used to obtain the
standard error of an estimated percentile for individuals in each group,
once the variances and covariances of the parameter estimates in the
model have been found. Specific results for the standard error of
percentiles of the survival time distributions in each of the two groups
will not be given. Instead, the general expression for the standard
error of the *p*th percentile after fitting a Weibull model, given in
Equation (5.33), may be used.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_7" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 5.7]{.boxnum}](#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_7){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

In [Example 5.5](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_5), a Weibull proportional
hazards model was found to be appropriate for the data on the survival
times of two groups of breast cancer patients. Under this model, the
hazard of death at time *t* is $\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$ for a
patient whose tumour is negatively stained and
$\psi\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$ for a patient for whom it is
positively stained.

The estimated value of the shape parameter of the fitted Weibull
distribution is $\hat{\gamma} = 0.937$. The estimated scale parameter
for women in Group I is $\hat{\lambda} = 0.00414$ and that for women in
Group II is $\hat{\lambda}\hat{\psi} = 0.0105$. The estimated hazard
ratio under this Weibull model is $\hat{\psi} = 2.55$, which is not very
different from the value obtained in [Example
5.6](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_6), on the assumption of exponentially
distributed survival times.

Putting $\hat{\gamma} = 0.937$ and $\hat{\lambda} = 0.00414$ in Equation
(5.29) gives 235.89 for the median survival time of those in Group I.
The estimated median survival time for women in Group II is found by
putting $\hat{\gamma} = 0.937$ and $\hat{\lambda} = 0.0105$ in that
equation, and gives 87.07 for the estimated median survival time. The
median survival time for women with a tumour that
[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p176 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 176. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}was positively stained is about one-third that of
those with a tumour that was negatively stained.

Using the general result for the standard error of the median survival
time, given in Equation (5.33), the standard error of the two medians is
found by taking $p = 50$, $\hat{\gamma} = 0.937$ and
$\hat{\lambda} = 0.00414$ and 0.0105 in turn. They turn out to be
114.126 and 20.550, respectively.

As in [Section 5.4.3](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4_3), 95% confidence limits
for the true median survival times for each group of women are best
obtained by working with the logarithm of the median. The standard error
of $\log\hat{t}(50)$ is found using Equation (5.34), from which

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(50) \right\} = \frac{1}{\hat{t}(50)}\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(50) \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

Confidence limits for $\log t(50)$ are then exponentiated to give the
corresponding confidence limits for $t(50)$ itself.

In this example, 95% confidence intervals for the true median survival
times of the two groups of women are $(91.4,608.9)$ and $(54.8,138.3)$,
respectively. Notice that the confidence interval for the median
survival time of patients with positive staining is much narrower than
that for women with negative staining. This is due to there being a
relatively small number of uncensored survival times in the women whose
tumours were negatively stained.

</aside>
:::
:::::

::::::: section
## 5.6 The Weibull proportional hazards model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_6 .head1}

The model in Equation (5.35) for the comparison of two groups of
survival data can easily be generalised to give a model that is similar
in form to the Cox regression model described in [Section
3.1.2](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_1_2). Suppose that the values
$x_{1},x_{2},\ldots,x_{p}$ of *p* explanatory variables,
$X_{1},X_{2},\ldots,X_{p}$, are recorded for each of *n* individuals.
Under the proportional hazards model, the hazard of death at time *t*
for the *i*th individual is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\beta_{1}x_{1i} + \beta_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \beta_{p}x_{pi})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(5.41)]{.equationnum}

for $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$. Although this model has a similar appearance to
that given in Equation (3.3), there is one fundamental difference, which
concerns the specification of the baseline hazard function $h_{0}(t)$.
In the Cox regression model, the form of $h_{0}(t)$ is unspecified, and
the shape of the function is essentially determined by the actual data.
In the model being considered in this section, the survival times are
assumed to have a Weibull distribution, and this imposes a particular
parametric form on $h_{0}(t)$.

Consider an individual for whom the values of the *p* explanatory
variables in the model of Equation (5.41) are all equal to zero. The
hazard function for such an individual is $h_{0}(t)$. If the survival
time of this individual has a Weibull distribution with scale parameter
*λ* and shape parameter *γ*, then their hazard function is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}.}
\end{array}$$

Using Equation (5.41), the hazard function for the *i*th individual in
the study is then given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1},}
\end{array}$$[(5.42)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ stands for
$\beta_{1}x_{1i} + \beta_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \beta_{p}x_{pi}$. From the
form of this hazard function, we can see that the survival time of the
*i*th individual in the study has a Weibull distribution with scale
parameter $\lambda\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})$
and shape parameter *γ*. This again is a manifestation of the
proportional hazards property of the Weibull distribution. This result
shows that the effect of the explanatory variates in the model is to
alter the scale parameter of the distribution, while the shape parameter
remains constant.

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p177 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 177. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The survivor function corresponding to the hazard
function given in Equation (5.42) is found using Equation (1.6), and
turns out to be

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})\lambda t^{\gamma}} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.43)]{.equationnum}

::: section
### 5.6.1 Fitting the model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_6_1 .head2}

The Weibull proportional hazards model is fitted by constructing the
likelihood function of the *n* observations, and maximising this
function with respect to the unknown parameters,
$\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\ldots,\beta_{p},\lambda$ and *γ*. Since the hazard
function and survivor function differ for each individual, the
likelihood function in Expression (5.15) is now written as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {h_{i}(t_{i})} \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}S_{i}(t_{i}).}
\end{array}$$[(5.44)]{.equationnum}

The logarithm of the likelihood function, rather than the likelihood
itself, is maximised with respect to the unknown parameters, and from
Expression (5.44), this is

$$\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\lbrack\delta_{i}\log h_{i}(t_{i}) + \log S_{i}(t_{i})\rbrack.$$

On substituting for $h_{i}(t_{i})$ and $S_{i}(t_{i})$ from Equations
(5.42) and (5.43), the log-likelihood becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\lbrack {\delta_{i}\left\{ \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \log(\lambda\gamma) + (\gamma - 1)\log t_{i} \right\} - \lambda\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})t^{\gamma}} \right\rbrack},}
\end{array}$$

which can be written as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\lbrack {\delta_{i}\left\{ \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \log(\lambda\gamma) + \gamma\log t_{i} \right\} - \lambda\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})t^{\gamma}} \right\rbrack} - {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log t_{i}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.45)]{.equationnum}

The final term in this expression,
$- {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log t_{i}$, does not involve any
of the unknown parameters, and can be omitted from the likelihood. The
resulting log-likelihood function is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\lbrack {\delta_{i}\left\{ \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \log(\lambda\gamma) + \gamma\log t_{i} \right\} - \lambda\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})t^{\gamma}} \right\rbrack},}
\end{array}$$[(5.46)]{.equationnum}

which differs from that obtained from the full log-likelihood, given in
Expression (5.45), by the value of
${\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\log t_{i}$. When computer software
is used to fit the Weibull proportional hazards model, the
log-likelihood is generally computed from Expression (5.46). This
expression will also be used in the examples given in this book.

Computer output from fitting parametric proportional hazards models
generally includes the standard errors of the parameter estimates, from
which confidence intervals for relative hazards and the median and other
percentiles of the survival time distribution can be found.
Specifically, suppose that the estimates of the parameters in the model
of Equation (5.42) are
${\hat{\beta}}_{1},{\hat{\beta}}_{2},\ldots,{\hat{\beta}}_{p},\hat{\lambda}$
and $\hat{\gamma}$. The estimated survivor function for the *i*th
individual in the study, for whom the values of the explanatory
variables in the model are $x_{1i},x_{2i},\ldots,x_{pi}$, is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- \exp({\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{1i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + {\hat{\beta}}_{p}x_{pi})\hat{\lambda}t^{\hat{\gamma}}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and the corresponding estimated hazard function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = \exp({\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{1i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + {\hat{\beta}}_{p}x_{pi})\hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}t^{\hat{\gamma} - 1}.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p178 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 178. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Both of these functions are continuous functions of
time, *t*, and can be estimated and plotted against *t* for individuals
with particular values of the explanatory variables in the model.

Generalising the result in Equation (5.28) to the situation where the
Weibull scale parameter is
$\lambda\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})$, the
estimated *p*th percentile of the survival time distribution for an
individual, whose vector of explanatory variables is
$\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(p) = \left\{ {\frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}\exp({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})}\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right)} \right\}^{1/\hat{\gamma}}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.47)]{.equationnum}

The estimated median survival time for such an individual is therefore

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{t}(50) = \left\{ \frac{\log 2}{\hat{\lambda}\exp({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})} \right\}^{1/\hat{\gamma}}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.48)]{.equationnum}

The standard error of $\hat{t}(p)$ and corresponding interval estimates
for $t(p)$, are derived in [Section 5.6.2](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_6_2).
:::

::: section
### 5.6.2 Standard error of a percentile in the Weibull model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_6_2 .head2}

As in [Section 5.4.3](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4_3), the standard error of
the estimated *p*th percentile of the survival time distribution for the
Weibull proportional hazards model, $\hat{t}(p)$, given in Equation
(5.47), is found from the variance of $\log\hat{t}(p)$. Writing

$$\begin{array}{r}
{c_{p} = \log\log\left( \frac{100}{100 - p} \right),}
\end{array}$$

we have from Equation (5.47),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\hat{t}(p) = \frac{1}{\hat{\gamma}}\left( {c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda} - {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}} \right).}
\end{array}$$[(5.49)]{.equationnum}

This is a function of the $p + 2$ parameter estimates $\hat{\lambda}$,
$\hat{\gamma}$ and
${\hat{\beta}}_{1},{\hat{\beta}}_{2},\ldots,{\hat{\beta}}_{p}$, and the
approximate variance of this function can be found using a further
generalisation of the result in Equation (5.30). This generalisation is
best expressed in matrix form.

Suppose that $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}$ is a vector formed from
*k* parameter estimates, ${\hat{\theta}}_{1}$,
${\hat{\theta}}_{2},\ldots,{\hat{\theta}}_{k}$, and that
$g(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$ is a function of these *k*
estimates. The approximate variance of
$g(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$ is then given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,\left\{ g(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}) \right\} \approx \mathbf{\mathbf{d}}{(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})}'\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})\,\mathbf{\mathbf{d}}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}),}
\end{array}$$[(5.50)]{.equationnum}

where $\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$ is the
$k \times k$ variance-covariance matrix of the estimates in
$\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}$, and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{d}}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$ is the
*k*-component vector whose *i*th element is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left. ~\frac{\partial g(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})}{\partial{\hat{\theta}}_{i}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}},}
\end{array}$$

for $i = 1,2,\ldots,k$.

We now write $\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}$ for the $(p + 2) \times (p + 2)$
variance-covariance matrix of $\hat{\lambda}$, $\hat{\gamma}$ and
${\hat{\beta}}_{1},{\hat{\beta}}_{2},\ldots,{\hat{\beta}}_{p}$. Next,
from Equation (5.49), the derivatives of $\log\hat{t}(p)$ with respect
to these parameter estimates are

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial\hat{\lambda}} & {= - \frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}},} \\
\frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial\hat{\gamma}} & {= - \frac{c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda} - {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}{{\hat{\gamma}}^{2}},} \\
\frac{\partial\log\hat{t}(p)}{\partial{\hat{\beta}}_{j}} & {= - \frac{x_{ji}}{\hat{\gamma}},}
\end{aligned}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p179 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 179. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}for $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, where $x_{ji}$ is the *j*th
component of $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$. The vector
$\mathbf{\mathbf{d}}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$ in Equation (5.50)
can be expressed as $- {\hat{\gamma}}^{- 1}\mathbf{\mathbf{d}}_{0}$,
where $\mathbf{\mathbf{d}}_{0}$ is a vector with components
${\hat{\lambda}}^{- 1}$,
${\hat{\gamma}}^{- 1}\left\{ c_{p} - \log\hat{\lambda} - {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} \right\}$,
and $x_{1i},x_{2i},\ldots,x_{pi}$. Then, the standard error of
$\log\hat{t}(p)$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ {\log\hat{t}(p)} \right\} = {\hat{\gamma}}^{- 1}\left. \sqrt{}\left( {\mathbf{\mathbf{d}}_{0}'\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}\mathbf{\mathbf{d}}_{0}} \right), \right.}
\end{array}$$[(5.51)]{.equationnum}

from which the standard error of $\hat{t}(p)$ itself is obtained using

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,\left\{ \hat{t}(p) \right\} = \hat{t}(p)\,\text{se}\,\left\{ {\log\hat{t}(p)} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.52)]{.equationnum}

Notice that for the null model that contains no explanatory variables,
the standard error of $\hat{t}(p)$ in Equation (5.52) reduces to the
result in Equation (5.33).

Confidence limits for $\log t(p)$, can be found from the standard error
of $\log\hat{t}(p)$, shown in Equation (5.51), and a confidence interval
for $t(p)$ is obtained by exponentiating these limits.
:::

::: section
### 5.6.3 Log-linear form of the model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_6_3 .head2}

Most computer software for fitting the Weibull proportional hazards
model uses a different form of the model from that presented in [Section
5.6](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_6). The reason for this will be given [Section
5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_11), but for the moment we observe that the
hazard function in Equation (5.42) is expressed as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \frac{1}{\sigma t}\exp\left( \frac{\log t - \mu - \alpha_{1}x_{1i} - \alpha_{2}x_{2i} - \cdots - \alpha_{p}x_{pi}}{\sigma} \right),}
\end{array}$$

and the corresponding survivor function in Equation (5.43) is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- \exp\left( \frac{\log t - \mu - \alpha_{1}x_{1i} - \alpha_{2}x_{2i} - \cdots - \alpha_{p}x_{pi}}{\sigma} \right)} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

In these equations, $\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2},\ldots,\alpha_{p}$ are the
coefficients of the *p* explanatory variables in the model, whose values
are $x_{1i},x_{2i},\ldots,x_{pi}$ for the *i*th of *n* individuals, and
*μ* and *σ* are unknown parameters. The correspondence between these two
representations of the model is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\lambda = \exp( - \mu/\sigma),\quad\gamma = \sigma^{- 1},\quad\beta_{j} = - \alpha_{j}/\sigma,}
\end{array}$$[(5.53)]{.equationnum}

for $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, and *μ, σ* are often termed the *intercept* and
*scale parameter*, respectively.

Generally speaking, it will be more straightforward to use the
log-linear form of the model to estimate the hazard and survivor
functions, percentiles of the survival time distribution, and their
standard errors. The relevant expressions are given later as Equations
(5.66), (5.67) and (5.69) in [Section 5.12](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_12).

This representation of the model makes it difficult to obtain confidence
intervals for a log-hazard ratio, *β*, in a proportional hazards model,
since only the standard error of the estimate of *α* is given in the
output. In particular, on fitting the Weibull proportional hazards
model, output generally provides the estimated value of
$\alpha = - \sigma\beta$, $\hat{\alpha}$, and the standard error of
$\hat{\alpha}$. The corresponding estimate of *β* is easily found from
$\hat{\beta} = - \hat{\alpha}/\hat{\sigma}$, but the standard error of
$\hat{\beta}$ is more complicated to calculate.

To obtain the standard error of $\hat{\beta}$, we can use the result
given in Equation (5.30) of [Section 5.4.3](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4_3).
Using this result, the approximate variance of the function

$$\begin{array}{r}
{g(\hat{\alpha},\hat{\sigma}) = - \frac{\hat{\alpha}}{\hat{\sigma}}}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p180 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 180. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left( \frac{\partial g}{\partial\hat{\alpha}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\alpha}) + \left( \frac{\partial g}{\partial\hat{\sigma}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\sigma}) + 2\left( {\frac{\partial g}{\partial\hat{\alpha}}\frac{\partial g}{\partial\hat{\sigma}}} \right)\,\text{cov}\,(\hat{\alpha},\hat{\sigma}).}
\end{array}$$[(5.54)]{.equationnum}

The derivatives of $g(\hat{\alpha},\hat{\sigma})$, with respect to
$\hat{\alpha}$ and $\hat{\sigma}$, are given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\partial g}{\partial\hat{\alpha}} = - \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}},\text{\quad\quad}\frac{\partial g}{\partial\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{\hat{\alpha}}{{\hat{\sigma}}^{2}},}
\end{array}$$

and so using Expression (5.54),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,\left( {- \frac{\hat{\alpha}}{\hat{\sigma}}} \right) \approx \left( {- \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\alpha}) + \left( \frac{\hat{\alpha}}{{\hat{\sigma}}^{2}} \right)^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\sigma}) + 2\left( {- \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}}} \right)\left( \frac{\hat{\alpha}}{{\hat{\sigma}}^{2}} \right)\,\text{cov}\,(\hat{\alpha},\hat{\sigma}).}
\end{array}$$

After some algebra, the approximate variance becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{1}{{\hat{\sigma}}^{4}}\left\{ {{\hat{\sigma}}^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\alpha}) + {\hat{\alpha}}^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\sigma}) - 2\hat{\alpha}\hat{\sigma}\,\text{cov}\,(\hat{\alpha},\hat{\sigma})} \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(5.55)]{.equationnum}

and the square root of this function is the standard error of
$\hat{\beta}$.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_8" role="doc-tip">

[Example 5.8]{.boxnum} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

The computation of a standard error of a log-hazard ratio is now
illustrated using the data on the survival times of two groups breast
cancer patients. In this example, computer output from fitting the
log-linear form of the Weibull proportional hazards model is used to
illustrate the estimation of the hazard ratio, and calculation of the
standard error of the estimate.

On fitting the model that contains the treatment effect, represented by
a variable *X*, where $X = 0$ for a woman with negative staining and
$X = 1$ for positive staining, we find that the estimated coefficient of
*X* is $\hat{\alpha} = - 0.9967$. Also, the estimates of *μ* and *σ* are
given by $\hat{\mu} = 5.8544$ and $\hat{\sigma} = 1.0668$, respectively.
The estimated log-hazard ratio for a woman with positive staining,
($X = 1$) relative to a woman with negative staining ($X = 0$) is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{\beta} = - \frac{- 0.9967}{1.0668} = 0.9343.}
\end{array}$$

The corresponding hazard ratio, $\exp(\hat{\beta})$, is 2.55, as in
[Example 5.7](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_7).

The standard errors of $\hat{\alpha}$ and $\hat{\sigma}$ are generally
included in standard computer output and are 0.5441 and 0.1786,
respectively. The estimated variances of $\hat{\alpha}$ and
$\hat{\sigma}$ are therefore 0.2960 and 0.0319, respectively. The
covariance between $\hat{\alpha}$ and $\hat{\sigma}$ can generally be
found from computer software, although it is not usually part of the
default output. It is found to be $- 0.0213$.

Substituting for $\hat{\alpha}$, $\hat{\sigma}$ and their variances and
covariance in Expression (5.55), we get

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\beta}) \approx 0.2498,}
\end{array}$$

and so the standard error of $\hat{\beta}$ is given by
$\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\beta}) = 0.4998$. This can be used in the
construction of confidence intervals for the corresponding true hazard
ratio.

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 5.6.4 Exploratory analysis {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_6_4 .head2}

Graphical procedures for assessing whether data can be modelled by a
Weibull distribution, and whether the proportional hazards assumption is
valid, such as those described in [Sections 5.2](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_2)
and [5.5.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_5_1), work perfectly well when we are
faced with a single sample of survival data, or when the number of
groups is small and there is a reasonably large number of
[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p181 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 181. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}individuals in each group. But in situations where
there are a small number of death times distributed over a relatively
large number of groups, it may not be possible to estimate the survivor
function, and hence the log-cumulative hazard function, for each group.

As an example, consider the data on the survival times of patients with
hypernephroma, given in [Table
3.6](#chapter3.xhtml_tbl3_6){#chapter5.xhtml_b5tbl3_6}. Here,
individuals are classified according to age group and whether or not a
nephrectomy has been performed, giving six combinations of age group and
nephrectomy status. To examine the assumption of a Weibull distribution
for the survival times in each group, and the assumption of proportional
hazards across the groups, a log-cumulative hazard plot would be
required for each group. The number of patients in each age group who
have not had a nephrectomy is so small that the survivor function cannot
be properly estimated in these groups. If there were more individuals in
the study who had died and not had a nephrectomy, it would be possible
to construct a log-cumulative hazard plot. If this plot featured six
parallel straight lines, the Weibull proportional hazards model is
likely to be satisfactory.

When a model contains continuous variables, their values will first need
to be grouped before a log-cumulative hazard plot can be obtained. This
may also result in there being insufficient numbers of individuals in
some groups to enable the log-cumulative hazard function to be
estimated.

The only alternative to using each combination of factor levels in
constructing a log-cumulative hazard plot is to ignore some of the
factors. However, the resulting plot can be very misleading. For
example, suppose that patients are classified according to the levels of
two factors, *A* and *B*. The log-cumulative hazard plot obtained by
grouping the individuals according to the levels of *A* ignoring *B*, or
according to the levels of *B* ignoring *A*, may not give cause to doubt
the Weibull or proportional hazards assumptions. However, if the
log-cumulative hazard plot is obtained for individuals at each
combination of levels of *A* and *B*, the plot may not feature a series
of four parallel lines. By the same token, the log-cumulative hazard
plot obtained when either *A* or *B* is ignored may not show sets of
parallel straight lines, but when a plot is obtained for all
combinations of *A* and *B*, parallel lines may result. This feature is
illustrated in the following example.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_9" role="doc-tip">

[Example 5.9]{.boxnum} [A numerical illustration]{.boxht}

Suppose that a number of individuals are classified according to the
levels of two factors, *A* and *B*, each with two levels, and that their
survival times are as shown in [Table
5.1](#chapter5.xhtml_tbl5_1){#chapter5.xhtml_b5tbl5_1}.

+-------------------+----------------------+
| $A = 1$           | $A = 2$              |
+=========+=========+==========+===========+
| $B = 1$ | $B = 2$ | $B = 1$  | $B = 2$   |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 59      | 10      | 88       |  25[^53]  |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 20      |  4      |  70[^54] | 111       |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 71      | 16      | 54       | 152       |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 33      | 18      | 139      |   86      |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 25      | 19      | 31       | 212       |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 25      | 35      | 59       |  187[^55] |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 15      | 11      | 111      |   54      |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 53      |         | 149      | 357       |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
| 47      |         | 30       | 301       |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
|         |         | 44       | 195       |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+
|         |         | 25       |           |
+---------+---------+----------+-----------+

: [[[Table]{.label}
[5.1]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter5.xhtml_b5tbl5_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Artificial data on the survival times of 37
patients classified according to the levels of two factors, *A* and
*B*]{.table-title} {#tbl5_1}

The log-cumulative hazard plot shown in [Figure
5.16](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_16){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_16} is derived
from the individuals classified according to the two levels of *A*,
ignoring the level of factor *B*. The plot in []{#chapter5.xhtml_p182
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 182. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Figure
5.17](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_17){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_17} is from
individuals classified according to the two levels of *B*, ignoring the
level of factor *A*.

<figure id="fig5_16" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_16.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_16" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.16</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Log-cumulative hazard plot for individuals
for whom <span class="math inline"><em>A</em> = 1</span> (∗) and <span
class="math inline"><em>A</em> = 2</span> (∙).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig5_17" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_17.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_17" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.17</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Log-cumulative hazard plot for individuals
for whom <span class="math inline"><em>B</em> = 1</span> (∗) and <span
class="math inline"><em>B</em> = 2</span> (∙).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

From [Figure 5.16](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_16) there is no reason to doubt
the assumption of a Weibull distribution for the survival times at the
two levels of *A*, and the assumption of proportional hazards is clearly
tenable. However, the crossed lines on the plot shown as [Figure
5.17](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_17) strongly suggest that the hazards are not
proportional when individuals are classified according to the levels of
*B*. A different picture emerges when the 37 survival times are
classified according to the levels of both *A* and *B*. The
log-cumulative hazard plot based on the four groups is shown in [Figure
5.18](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_18){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_18}. The four
parallel lines show that there is no doubt about the validity of the
proportional hazards assumption across the groups.

<figure id="fig5_18" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_18.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_18" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.18</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Log-cumulative hazard plot for individuals in
the groups defined by the four combinations of levels of <em>A</em> and
<em>B</em>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p183 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 183. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this example, the reason why the log-cumulative
hazard plot for *B* ignoring *A* is misleading is that there is an
interaction between *A* and *B*. An examination of the data reveals
that, on average, the difference in the survival times of patients for
whom $B = 1$ and $B = 2$ is greater when $A = 2$ than when $A = 1$.

</aside>

Even when a log-cumulative hazard plot gives no reason to doubt the
assumption of a Weibull proportional hazards model, the validity of the
fitted model will need to be examined using the methods to be described
in [Chapter 7](#chapter7.xhtml). When it is not possible to use a
log-cumulative hazard plot to explore whether a Weibull distribution
provides a reasonable model for the survival times, a procedure based on
the Cox regression model, described in [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml),
might be helpful. Essentially, a Cox regression model that includes all
the relevant explanatory variables is fitted, and the baseline hazard
function is estimated, using the procedure described in [Section
3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_10). A plot of this function may suggest
whether or not the assumption of a Weibull distribution is tenable. In
particular, if the estimated baseline hazard function in the Cox model
is increasing or decreasing, the Weibull model may provide a more
concise summary of the baseline hazard function than the Cox regression
model. Because the estimated baseline hazard function for a fitted Cox
model can be somewhat irregular, comparing the estimated baseline
cumulative hazard or the baseline survivor function, under the fitted
Cox regression model, with that of the Weibull model may be more
fruitful.
:::
:::::::

::: section
## 5.7 Comparing alternative Weibull proportional hazards models {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_7 .head1}

In order to ascertain which explanatory variables should be included in
a Weibull proportional hazards model, alternative models need to be
compared. Comparisons between different Weibull models can be made using
methods analogous to those for the Cox regression model described in
[Section 3.5](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_5).

Suppose that one model contains a subset of the explanatory variables in
another, so that the two models are nested. The two models can then be
compared on the basis of the statistic $- 2\log\hat{L}$, where $\hat{L}$
is the maximised value of the likelihood function under the fitted
model. For a model that contains *p* explanatory variables, the sample
likelihood is a []{#chapter5.xhtml_p184 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 184. " role="doc-pagebreak"}function of $p + 2$
unknown parameters, $\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\ldots,\beta_{p},\lambda$ and
*γ*. The maximised likelihood is then the value of this function when
these parameters take their estimates,
${\hat{\beta}}_{1},{\hat{\beta}}_{2},\ldots,{\hat{\beta}}_{p},\hat{\lambda}$
and $\hat{\gamma}$.

More specifically, suppose that one model, Model (1), say, contains *p*
explanatory variables, $X_{1},X_{2},\ldots,X_{p}$, and another model,
Model (2), contains an additional *q* explanatory variables,
$X_{p + 1},X_{p + 2},\ldots,X_{p + q}$. The estimated hazard functions
for the *i*th of *n* individuals under these two models are as shown
below:

  ------------ ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Model (1):   $h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{1i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + {\hat{\beta}}_{p}x_{pi} \right\}\hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}t^{\hat{\gamma} - 1}$
  Model (2):   $h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{1i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + {\hat{\beta}}_{p + q}x_{p + q,i} \right\}\hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}t^{\hat{\gamma} - 1}$
  ------------ ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

where $x_{1i},x_{2i},\ldots,x_{p + q,i}$ are the values of the $p + q$
explanatory variables for the *i*th individual. The maximised
likelihoods under Model (1) and Model (2) will be denoted by
${\hat{L}}_{1}$ and ${\hat{L}}_{2}$, respectively. The difference
between the values of $- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{1}$ and $- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{2}$,
that is, $- 2\left\{ \log{\hat{L}}_{1} - \log{\hat{L}}_{2} \right\}$,
then has an approximate chi-squared distribution with *q* degrees of
freedom, under the null hypothesis that the coefficients of the
additional *q* variates in Model (2) are all equal to zero. If the
