by $\hat{\mu} = 7.661$, $\hat{\sigma} = 0.338$,
${\hat{\alpha}}_{1} = - 0.029$, ${\hat{\alpha}}_{2} = - 0.293$ and
${\hat{\alpha}}_{3} = 0.573$. The values of the *α*\'s suggests that the
survival time tends to be shorter for larger values of the tumour size
and tumour index, and longer for individuals assigned to the active
treatment.

Using Equation (5.70), the fitted survivor function for the *i*th
patient is

$$\begin{aligned}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t)} & {= \left\lbrack {1 + \exp\left\{ \frac{\log t - \hat{\mu} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}Size_{i} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}Index_{i} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{3}Treat_{i}}{\hat{\sigma}} \right\}} \right\rbrack^{- 1},}
\end{aligned}$$

which can be written in the form

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = \left\{ {1 + t^{1/\hat{\sigma}}\exp\left( {\hat{\zeta}}_{i} \right)} \right\}^{- 1},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p206 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 206. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\zeta}}_{i} = \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}}\left\{ {- \hat{\mu} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}Size_{i} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}Index_{i} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{3}Treat_{i}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

that is,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\zeta}}_{i} = - 22.645 + 0.085\, Size_{i} + 0.865\, Index_{i} - 1.693\, Treat_{i}.}
\end{array}$$

The corresponding estimated hazard function can be found by
differentiating the estimated cumulative hazard function,
${\hat{H}}_{i}(t) = - \log{\hat{S}}_{i}(t)$, with respect to *t*. This
gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}t}\left\{ {1 + t^{- 1/\hat{\sigma}}\exp\left( {- {\hat{\zeta}}_{i}} \right)} \right\}^{- 1},}
\end{array}$$

a result that can also be obtained directly from the hazard function
given in Equation (5.71).

The estimated time ratio for an individual in the treated group,
relative to an individual in the control group, is $e^{0.573} = 1.77$.
The interpretation of this result is that after allowing for the size
and index of the tumour, the effect of the treatment with
diethylstilbestrol is to nearly double the survival time. This effect
might be of clinical importance, even though it is not statistically
significant. However, before accepting this interpretation, the adequacy
of the fitted model should be checked using suitable diagnostics.

A confidence interval for the time ratio is found by exponentiating the
confidence limits for the logarithm of the time ratio. In this example,
the logarithm of the time ratio for the treatment effect is the
estimated coefficient of *Treat* in the model for the hazard function,
$- 0.573$, and the standard error of this estimate is 0.473. Thus, a 95%
confidence interval for the time ratio has limits of
$\exp\left\{ 0.573 \pm 1.96 \times 0.473 \right\}$, and the required
interval is from 0.70 to 4.48. Notice that this interval estimate
includes unity, which is consistent with the earlier finding of a
non-significant treatment difference.

Finally, in terms of the parameterisation of the model in [Section
5.1.3](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_3), the fitted hazard function for the
*i*th patient, $i = 1,2,\ldots,38$, is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = e^{- {\hat{\eta}}_{i}}{\hat{h}}_{0}(e^{- {\hat{\eta}}_{i}}t),}
\end{array}$$

where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\eta}}_{i} = - 0.029\, Size_{i} - 0.293\, Index_{i} + 0.573\, Treat_{i},}
\end{array}$$

and from Equation (5.8),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{0}(t) = \frac{e^{\hat{\theta}}\hat{\kappa}t^{\hat{\kappa} - 1}}{1 + e^{\hat{\theta}}t^{\hat{\kappa}}}.}
\end{array}$$

The estimated parameters in this form of the estimated baseline hazard
function, ${\hat{h}}_{0}(t)$, are given by $\hat{\theta} = - 22.644$ and
$\hat{\kappa} = 2.956$, and a graph of this function is shown in [Figure
5.24](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_24){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_24}.

<figure id="fig5_24" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_24.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_24" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.24</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated baseline hazard function for the
fitted log-logistic model (—) and a fitted Weibull model (<span
class="middle">.......</span>).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

This figure indicates that the baseline hazard is increasing over time.
Comparison with the baseline hazard function for a fitted Weibull model,
also shown in this figure, indicates that under the log-logistic model,
the estimated baseline hazard function does not increase quite so
rapidly.

</aside>
:::

:::: section
## 5.14 Measures of explained variation in parametric models {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_14 .head1}

Measures of explained variation in the Cox regression model were
described in [Section 3.12](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml). In this section, a general measure of explained
variation is described for use with parametric accelerated failure time
models. From Equation (5.61), this model has

$$\begin{array}{r}
{Y_{i} = \log T_{i} = \mu + \alpha_{1}x_{1i} + \alpha_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \alpha_{p}x_{pi} + \sigma\epsilon_{i},}
\end{array}$$

which is similar in form to the general linear model in Equation (3.33)
of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). However, the *ϵ*~*i*~ term in the
general linear model is replaced by $\sigma\epsilon_{i}$, where *σ* is
the scale parameter in []{#chapter5.xhtml_p207 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 207. " role="doc-pagebreak"}the accelerated failure
time model and *ϵ*~*i*~ has a distribution that depends on the assumed
distribution for the survival times. The analogue of the
*R*^2^-statistic in Equation (3.35) is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{R_{P}^{2} = \frac{{\hat{V}}_{P}}{{\hat{V}}_{P} + {\hat{\sigma}}^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\epsilon_{i})},}
\end{array}$$[(5.75)]{.equationnum}

where, as in [Section 3.12.4](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_4) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml), ${\hat{V}}_{P}$ is the sample variance of the risk
scores in the fitted accelerated failure time model and
${\hat{\sigma}}^{2}$ is the estimated scale parameter.

In the particular case of the Weibull distribution,
$\,\text{var}\,(\epsilon_{i}) = \pi^{2}/6$, from [Section
5.12.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_12_1). This measure of explained variation
can also be used for a Cox proportional hazards model by taking
$\sigma = 1$, and this was the $R_{P}^{2}$-statistic in Equation (3.35)
of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml).

When an accelerated failure time model is fitted to survival times that
have a log-logistic distribution,
$\,\text{var}\,(\epsilon_{i}) = \pi^{2}/3$ in Equation (5.75), and for
lognormal survival times $\,\text{var}\,(\epsilon_{i}) = 1$.

The $R_{D}^{2}$-statistic, also described in [Section
3.12.4](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_4) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), can
be adapted for use with parametric survival models in a similar manner.
This leads to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{R_{D}^{2} = \frac{D_{0}^{2}}{D_{0}^{2} + {\hat{\sigma}}^{2}\,\text{var}\,(\epsilon_{i})},}
\end{array}$$

where *D*~0~ is the coefficient of the variable associated with the
normal scores in a fitted Cox regression model that only contains this
term.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_16" role="doc-tip">

[Example 5.16]{.boxnum} [Survival of multiple myeloma patients]{.boxht}

To illustrate the use of measures of explained variation in a Weibull
model for survival data, consider the data on the survival times of
patients with multiple myeloma, for which the values of three
*R*^2^-statistics on fitting Cox regression models were given in
[Example 3.17](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_17){#chapter5.xhtml_b3exam3_17}.
For a Weibull model containing the variables *Hb* and *Bun*,
$R_{P}^{2} = 0.25$ and $R_{D}^{2} = 0.23$. For the model that contains
all 7 variables, $R_{P}^{2} = 0.30$ and $R_{D}^{2} = 0.34$. These values
are quite similar to those obtained on fitting corresponding Cox
regression models, so that the Cox and Weibull models have similar
explanatory power.

</aside>

::: section
### 5.14.1 []{#chapter5.xhtml_p208 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 208. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Predictive ability of a parametric model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_14_1 .head2}

The use of time-dependent receiver-operating characteristic curves in
assessing the predictive ability of a Cox regression model was described
in [Section 3.13.4](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_13_4) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml). This technique can in fact be used with any model
for survival data, once the risk score has been estimated. No new
principles are involved.
:::
::::

:::: section
## 5.15 The proportional odds model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_15 .head1}

In the general proportional odds model, the odds of an individual
surviving beyond some time *t* are expressed as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{S_{i}(t)}{1 - S_{i}(t)} = e^{\eta_{i}}\frac{S_{0}(t)}{1 - S_{0}(t)},}
\end{array}$$[(5.76)]{.equationnum}

where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\eta_{i} = \beta_{1}x_{1i} + \beta_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \beta_{p}x_{pi}}
\end{array}$$

is a linear combination of the values of *p* explanatory variables,
*X*~1~, $X_{2},\ldots$, *X*~*p*~, measured on the *i*th individual, and
$S_{0}(t)$, the baseline survivor function, is the survivor function for
an individual whose explanatory variables all take the value zero.

In this model, the explanatory variables act multiplicatively on the
odds of survival beyond *t*. The logarithm of the ratio of the odds of
survival beyond *t* for the *i*th individual, relative to an individual
for whom the explanatory variables are all equal to zero, is therefore
just *η*~*i*~. The model is therefore a linear model for the log-odds
ratio.

Now consider the particular case of a two-group study, in which
individuals receive either a standard treatment or new treatment. Let
the single indicator variable *X* take the value zero if an individual
is on the standard treatment and unity if on the new. The odds of the
*i*th individual surviving beyond time *t* is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{S_{i}(t)}{1 - S_{i}(t)} = e^{\beta x_{i}}\frac{S_{0}(t)}{1 - S_{0}(t)},}
\end{array}$$

where *x*~*i*~ is the value of *X* for the *i*th individual,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$. Thus, if $S_{N}(t)$ and $S_{S}(t)$ are the survivor
functions for individuals on the new and standard treatments,
respectively,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{S_{N}(t)}{1 - S_{N}(t)} = e^{\beta}\frac{S_{S}(t)}{1 - S_{S}(t)},}
\end{array}$$

and the log-odds ratio is simply *β*. The parameters in the linear
component of the model therefore have an immediate interpretation.

As for the proportional hazards model, a non-parametric estimate of the
baseline hazard function can be obtained. The model is then fitted by
estimating the *β*-parameters in the linear component of the model, and
the baseline survivor function, from the data. Fully parametric versions
of the proportional odds model can be derived by using a specific
probability distribution for the survival times. One such model is
described below in [Section 5.15.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_15_1).

A particularly important property of the proportional odds model
concerns the ratio of the hazard function for the *i*th individual to
the baseline hazard, $h_{i}(t)/h_{0}(t)$. It can be shown that this
ratio converges from the value $e^{- \eta_{i}}$ at time $t = 0$, to
unity at $t = \infty$. To show this, the model in Equation (5.76) can be
rearranged to give

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = S_{0}(t)\left\{ {e^{- \eta_{i}} + (1 - e^{- \eta_{i}})S_{0}(t)} \right\}^{- 1},}
\end{array}$$

and taking logarithms, we get

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log S_{i}(t) = \log S_{0}(t) - \log\left\{ {e^{- \eta_{i}} + (1 - e^{- \eta_{i}})S_{0}(t)} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.77)]{.equationnum}

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p209 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 209. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Using the general result from Equation (1.5), the
hazard function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = - \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\log S_{i}(t),}
\end{array}$$

and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = h_{0}(t) - \frac{(1 - e^{- \eta_{i}})f_{0}(t)}{e^{- \eta_{i}} + (1 - e^{- \eta_{i}})S_{0}(t)},}
\end{array}$$

after differentiating both sides of Equation (5.77) with respect to *t*,
where $f_{0}(t)$ is the baseline probability density function. After
some rearrangement, this equation becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = h_{0}(t) - \frac{f_{0}(t)}{{(e^{\eta_{i}} - 1)}^{- 1} + S_{0}(t)}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.78)]{.equationnum}

From Equation (1.4), we also have that $h_{0}(t) = f_{0}(t)/S_{0}(t)$
and substituting for $f_{0}(t)$ in Equation (5.78) gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = h_{0}(t)\left\{ {1 - \frac{S_{0}(t)}{{(e^{\eta_{i}} - 1)}^{- 1} + S_{0}(t)}} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

Finally, after further rearrangement, the hazard ratio is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{h_{i}(t)}{h_{0}(t)} = \left\{ {1 + (e^{\eta_{i}} - 1)S_{0}(t)} \right\}^{- 1}.}
\end{array}$$

As *t* increases from 0 to ∞, the baseline survivor function decreases
monotonically from 1 to 0. When $S_{0}(t) = 1$, the hazard ratio is
$e^{- \eta_{i}}$ and as *t* increases to ∞, the hazard ratio converges
to unity.

In practical applications, it is common for the hazard functions
obtained for patients in two or more groups to converge with time. For
example, in a follow-up study of patients in a clinical trial, the
effect on survival of the treatment, or the initial stage of disease,
may wear off. Similarly, in studies where a group of patients with some
disease are being compared with a control group of disease-free
individuals, an effective cure of the disease would lead to the survival
experience of each group becoming more similar over time. This suggests
that the proportional odds model, with its property of convergent hazard
functions, might be of considerable value. However, there are two
reasons why this general model has not been widely used in practice. The
first of these is that computer software is not generally available for
fitting a proportional odds model in which the baseline survivor
function is of unspecified form. The second is that the model is likely
to give similar results to a Cox regression model that includes a
time-dependent variable to produce non-proportional hazards. This
particular approach to modelling survival data with non-proportional
hazards was outlined in [Section 4.4.3](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_3) and is
considered more fully in [Chapter 8](#chapter8.xhtml).

::: section
### 5.15.1 The log-logistic proportional odds model {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_15_1 .head2}

If survival times for individuals are assumed to have a log-logistic
distribution, the baseline survivor function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{0}(t) = \left\{ {1 + e^{\theta}t^{\kappa}} \right\}^{- 1},}
\end{array}$$

where *θ* and *κ* are unknown parameters. The baseline odds of survival
beyond time *t* are then given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{S_{0}(t)}{1 - S_{0}(t)} = e^{- \theta}t^{- \kappa}.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p210 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 210. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The odds of the *i*th individual surviving beyond
time *t* are therefore

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{S_{i}(t)}{1 - S_{i}(t)} = e^{\eta_{i} - \theta}t^{- \kappa},}
\end{array}$$

and so the survival time of the *i*th individual has a log-logistic
distribution with parameters $\theta - \eta_{i}$ and *κ*. The
log-logistic distribution therefore has the *proportional odds
property*, and this distribution is the natural one to use in
conjunction with the proportional odds model. In fact, this is the only
distribution to share both the accelerated failure time property and the
proportional odds property.

This result also means that the estimated *β*-coefficients in the linear
component of the proportional odds model in Equation (5.76) can be
obtained by multiplying the *α*-coefficients in the log-logistic
accelerated failure time model of Equation (5.70) by
$\hat{\kappa} = {\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}$, where $\hat{\sigma}$ is the
estimated value of the parameter *σ*. The coefficients of the
explanatory variables under the proportional odds model can then be
obtained from those under the accelerated failure time model, and vice
versa. The results of a survival analysis based on a proportional odds
model can therefore be interpreted in terms of a time ratio,
acceleration factor, or the ratio of the odds of survival beyond some
time, whichever is the more convenient.

As for other models for survival data, the proportional odds model can
be fitted using the method of maximum likelihood. Alternative models may
then be compared on the basis of the statistic $- 2\log\hat{L}$.

In a two-group study, a preliminary examination of the likely
suitability of the model can easily be undertaken. The log-odds of the
*i*th individual surviving beyond time *t* are

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\left\{ \frac{S_{i}(t)}{1 - S_{i}(t)} \right\} = \beta x_{i} - \theta - \kappa\log t,}
\end{array}$$

where *x*~*i*~ is the value of an indicator variable that takes the
value zero if an individual is in one group and unity if in the other.
The Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function is then obtained for
the individuals in each group and the estimated log-odds of survival
beyond time *t*,
$\log\left\{ {{\hat{S}}_{i}(t)/\lbrack 1 - {\hat{S}}_{i}(t)\rbrack} \right\}$,
are plotted against $\log t$. If the plot shows two parallel straight
lines, this would indicate that the log-logistic model was appropriate.
If the lines were straight but not parallel, this would suggest that the
parameter *κ* in the model was not the same for each treatment group.
Parallel curves in this plot suggest that although the proportional odds
assumption is valid, the survival times cannot be taken to have a
log-logistic distribution.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam5_17" role="doc-tip">

[Example 5.17]{.boxnum} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

In this example to illustrate the use of the proportional odds model,
the model is fitted to the data on the survival times of breast cancer
patients. In order to assess the likely suitability of the proportional
odds model, the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function for women
with negatively and positively stained tumours is computed. For the two
groups of women, the log-odds of survival beyond time *t* are estimated
and plotted against $\log t$. The resulting graph is shown in [Figure
5.25](#chapter5.xhtml_fig5_25){#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_25}. The lines are
reasonably straight and parallel, and so we go on to use the
log-logistic proportional odds model to summarise these data.

<figure id="fig5_25" class="image">
<img src="images/fig5_25.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter5.xhtml_b5fig5_25" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">5.25</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated values of the log-odds of survival
beyond <em>t</em> plotted against <span
class="math inline">log <em>t</em></span> for women with positively
stained (∗) and negatively stained (∙) tumours.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The model can be fitted using software for fitting the log-logistic
accelerated failure time model. In [Example
5.14](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_14){#chapter5.xhtml_b5exam5_14}, this latter
model was fitted to the data on the survival of breast cancer patients.
The estimated values of *κ* and *θ* in the proportional odds model are
1.243 and $- 6.787$, respectively, the same as those in the accelerated
failure time model. However, the estimated value of *β* in the linear
component of the proportional odds model is
$\hat{\beta} = - 1.149 \times 1.243 = - 1.428$. This is an estimate of
the logarithm of the ratio of the odds of a positively stained woman
surviving beyond time *t*, relatively to one who is negatively stained.
The corresponding odds ratio is $e^{- 1.428} = 0.24$, so that the odds
of a []{#chapter5.xhtml_p211 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 211. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}woman surviving beyond *t* are about four times
greater if that woman has a negatively stained tumour.

</aside>
:::
::::

::: section
## 5.16 Modelling cure rates {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_16 .head1}

In survival analysis, it is generally assumed that all individuals will
eventually experience the end point of interest, if the follow-up period
is long enough. This is certainly the case if the end point is death
from any cause. However, in some studies, a substantial proportion of
individuals may not have experienced the end point before the end of the
study. This may be because the treatment has effectively cured the
patient. For example, in a cancer trial, interest may centre on a
comparison of two treatments, where the end point is death from a
particular type of cancer. If the treatment is curative, there will be a
number of patients who do not die from the cancer during the course of a
relatively long follow-up period. This can lead to a larger proportion
of censored observations than is usually encountered, which is sometimes
referred to as *heavy censoring*. However, strictly speaking, an
individual who does not die during the follow-up period has a censored
time, whereas those who are cured cannot die from the disease under
study.

Individuals who have been cured, or more generally, those who can no
longer experience the event under study, will eventually fail for some
reason, but may remain alive for a very long time period. In this
situation, the survivor function estimated from a group of individuals
will tend to level off at a value greater than zero. It may then be
assumed that the population consists of a mixture of individuals: those
who are susceptible to the end point, and those who are not. The latter
then correspond to the cured individuals, and the proportion with
prolonged survival is called the *cured fraction*. Standard methods of
survival analysis can then be adapted, so that the probability of cure
is modelled simultaneously with the time to the event.

Models for the time to an event can be extended to incorporate a cured
fraction, *π*, the probability of being cured. In a fully parametric
model, the survivor function becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S(t) = (1 - \pi)S_{n}(t) + \pi,}
\end{array}$$[(5.79)]{.equationnum}

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p212 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 212. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where $S_{n}(t)$ is the survivor function of the
non-cured individuals. In Equation (5.79), $S(t)$ is the overall
survivor function for a group consisting of cured and non-cured
individuals, and the model is termed a *parametric mixture model*. As
$\left. t\rightarrow\infty \right.$, the survivor function of non-cured
individuals tends to zero, and so, from Equation (5.79), $S(t)$ tends to
*π*, the probability of cure. The corresponding hazard function for the
whole group is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h(t) = - \frac{\text{d}\log S(t)}{\text{d}t} = \frac{f_{n}(t)}{S_{n}(t) + \pi/(1 - \pi)},}
\end{array}$$[(5.80)]{.equationnum}

where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f_{n}(t) = - \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}S_{n}(t)}
\end{array}$$

is the density function of the non-cured individuals.

If a proportional hazards model can be assumed for the survival time of
the non-cured individuals, the hazard of death at time *t* in such an
individual is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{ni}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{n0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} = \beta_{1}x_{1i} + \beta_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \beta_{p}x_{pi}$
is a linear combination of the values of *p* explanatory variables,
$X_{1},X_{2},\ldots,X_{p}$, measured on this individual, and $h_{n0}(t)$
is the baseline hazard function of the non-cured individuals. The
survivor function for the non-cured individuals is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{ni}(t) = {\lbrack S_{n0}(t)\rbrack}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})},}
\end{array}$$

where
$S_{n0}(t) = \exp\left\{ - {\int_{0}^{t}h_{n0}}(u)\,\text{d}u \right\}$
is their baseline survivor function.

In addition, the probability of being cured may depend on a number of
explanatory variables, especially those relating to treatment group,
denoted $Z_{1},Z_{2},\ldots,Z_{p}$. The dependence of the probability
that the *i*th of *n* individuals is cured on these variables can then
be modelled by taking the logistic transformation of the cured fraction
to be a linear combination of their values,
$z_{1i},z_{2i},\ldots,z_{pi}$. Then,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{logit}\,(\pi_{i}) = \log\left( \frac{\pi_{i}}{1 - \pi_{i}} \right) = \phi_{0} + \phi'\mathbf{\mathbf{z}}_{i},}
\end{array}$$

for $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, where
$\phi'\mathbf{\mathbf{z}}_{i} = \phi_{0} + \phi_{1}z_{1i} + \phi_{2}z_{2i} + \cdots + \phi_{p}z_{pi}$
and $\phi_{0},\phi_{1},\ldots,\phi_{p}$ are unknown parameters. The cure
probability is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\pi_{i} = \left\{ {1 + \exp( - \phi'\mathbf{\mathbf{z}}_{i})} \right\}^{- 1}.}
\end{array}$$

The same explanatory variables may feature in both the survival model
for the non-cured individuals and the model for the probability of cure.
Also, in contrast to a survival model, the model for the cure
probability will generally include a constant term *ϕ*~0~, which is the
logistic transformation of a common cure probability, when there are no
other explanatory variables in the model.

Now suppose that the survival times for non-cured individuals can be
modelled using a Weibull distribution, with baseline hazard function
given by $h_{n0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$. The hazard function
for the non-cured individuals is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{ni}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1},}
\end{array}$$[(5.81)]{.equationnum}

and the corresponding survivor function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{ni}(t) = {\lbrack\exp( - \lambda t^{\gamma})\rbrack}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})} = \exp\left\{ - e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}\lambda t^{\gamma} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.82)]{.equationnum}

As for other models described in this chapter, the model that
incorporates a cured fraction can be fitted using the method of maximum
likelihood. Suppose that the data consist []{#chapter5.xhtml_p213
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 213. " role="doc-pagebreak"}of *n* survival
times $t_{1},t_{2},\ldots,t_{n}$, and that *δ*~*i*~ is the event
indicator for the *i*th individual so that $\delta_{i} = 1$ if the *i*th
individual dies and zero otherwise. From Equation (5.44), the likelihood
function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\phi,\lambda,\gamma) = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {h_{i}(t_{i})} \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}S_{i}(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$

where $h_{i}(t_{i})$ and $S_{i}(t_{i})$ are found by substituting
$h_{ni}(t_{i})$ and $S_{ni}(t_{i})$ from Equations (5.81) and (5.82)
into Equations (5.79) and (5.80).

The corresponding log-likelihood function,
$\log L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\phi,\lambda,\gamma)$ can then be
maximised using computer software for numerical optimisation. This
process leads to estimates
$\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\phi},\hat{\lambda},\hat{\gamma}$ of
the unknown parameters and their standard errors. In addition, models
with different explanatory variables in either the model for the
probability of cure or the survival models for non-cured individuals,
can be compared using the values of the statistic
$- 2\log L(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\phi},\hat{\lambda},\hat{\gamma})$
in the usual way.

A number of extensions to this model are possible. For example, an
accelerated failure time model can be used instead of a proportional
hazards model for the non-cured individuals.
:::

::: section
## 5.17 Effect of covariate adjustment {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_17 .head1}

We conclude this chapter with an illustration of an important feature
that can be encountered when developing parametric models for survival
data. In linear regression analysis, one effect of adding covariates to
a model is to reduce the residual mean square, and hence increase the
precision of estimates based on the model, such as a treatment effect.
The estimated treatment effect, adjusted for explanatory variables, will
then have a smaller standard error than the unadjusted effect. In
modelling survival data, the inclusion of relevant explanatory variables
often has a negligible effect on standard errors of parameter estimates.
Indeed, the standard error of an estimated treatment effect, for
example, may even be larger after adjusting for covariates. Essentially,
this is because the treatment effect does not have the same
interpretation in models with and without covariates, a point made by
[Ford, Norrie and Ahmadi
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib127){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib127 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. Nevertheless, it is important to include relevant
explanatory variables in the model, and to check that the fitted model
is appropriate, in order to ensure that a proper estimate of the
treatment effect is obtained.

To illustrate this in a little more detail, suppose that *t*~*i*~ is the
observed value of the random variable *T*~*i*~, that is associated with
the survival time of the *i*th of *n* individuals, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$.
We will consider the situation where there are two treatment groups,
with $n/2$ individuals in each group, and where there is a further
explanatory variable whose values are available for each individual. The
two explanatory variables will be labelled $X_{1},X_{2}$, where *X*~1~
refers to the treatment effect, and takes the value 0 or 1. The values
of $X_{1},X_{2}$ for the *i*th individual will be denoted
$x_{1i},x_{2i}$, respectively, and we will write
$z_{ji} = x_{ji} - {\overline{x}}_{j}$, for $j = 1,2$, where
${\overline{x}}_{j}$ is the sample mean of the values of the explanatory
variable *X*~*j*~. A proportional hazards model will be adopted for the
dependence of the hazard of death at time *t*, for the *i*th individual,
on the values $z_{1i}$, $z_{2i}$, in which the baseline hazard is a
constant value, *λ*. Consequently, the hazard function can be expressed
in the form

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \lambda\exp(\beta_{1}z_{1i} + \beta_{2}z_{2i}),}
\end{array}$$[(5.83)]{.equationnum}

and under this model, the survival times are exponentially distributed,
with means
$\left\{ \lambda\exp(\beta_{1}z_{1i} + \beta_{2}z_{2i}) \right\}^{- 1}$.
Using results given in [Section 5.12.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_12_1), this
model may also be expressed in accelerated failure time form as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log T_{i} = \mu - \beta_{1}z_{1i} - \beta_{2}z_{2i} + \epsilon_{i},}
\end{array}$$[(5.84)]{.equationnum}

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p214 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 214. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where $\mu = - \log\lambda$ and *ϵ*~*i*~ has a
Gumbel distribution, that is $\log\epsilon_{i}$ has a unit exponential
distribution. The model represented in Equations (5.83) or (5.84) will
be referred to as Model (1).

Using the results for maximum likelihood estimation given in [Appendix
A](#appA.xhtml_appA), it can be shown that the approximate variance of
the estimated treatment effect, ${\hat{\beta}}_{1}$, in Model (1), is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,({\hat{\beta}}_{1}) = \frac{1}{\lbrack 1 - \left\{ \,\text{corr}\,(z_{1},z_{2}) \right\}^{2}\rbrack{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}z_{1i}^{2}}},}
\end{array}$$

where $\,\text{corr}\,(z_{1},z_{2})$ is the sample correlation between
the values $z_{1i}$ and $z_{2i}$. Since $z_{1i}$ is either $- 0.5$ or
0.5, and there are equal numbers of individuals in each group,
${\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}z_{1i}^{2}} = n/4$, and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,({\hat{\beta}}_{1}) = \frac{4}{n\lbrack 1 - \left\{ \,\text{corr}\,(z_{1},z_{2}) \right\}^{2}\rbrack}.}
\end{array}$$[(5.85)]{.equationnum}

Now consider the model that only includes the variable associated with
the treatment effect, *X*~1~, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \lambda\exp(\beta_{1}z_{1i}),}
\end{array}$$[(5.86)]{.equationnum}

or equivalently,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log T_{i} = \mu - \beta_{1}z_{1i} + \epsilon_{i},}
\end{array}$$[(5.87)]{.equationnum}

where again $\log\epsilon_{i}$ has a unit exponential distribution. The
model described by Equations (5.86) or (5.87) will be referred to as
Model (2). In this model, the approximate variance of
${\hat{\beta}}_{1}$ is given by
$\,\text{var}\,({\hat{\beta}}_{1}) = 4n^{- 1}$. Since the term
$1 - \left\{ \,\text{corr}\,(z_{1},z_{2}) \right\}^{2}$ in Equation
(5.85) is always less than or equal to unity, the variance of
${\hat{\beta}}_{1}$ in Model (1) is at least equal to that of Model (2).
Consequently, the addition of the explanatory variable *X*~2~ to Model
(1) cannot decrease the variance of the estimated treatment effect.

The reason for this is that Model (1) and Model (2) cannot both be valid
for the same data set. If Model (1) is correct, and Model (2) is
actually fitted, the residual term in Equation (5.87) is not *ϵ*~*i*~
but $\epsilon_{i} - \beta_{2}z_{2i}$. Similarly, if Model (2) is
correct, but Model (1) is actually fitted, we cannot assume that the
logarithm of *ϵ*~*i*~ in Equation (5.84) has a unit exponential
distribution. Moreover, the parameter *β*~1~ is now estimated less
precisely because a redundant parameter, *β*~2~, is included in the
model.

More detailed analytic and simulation studies are given in the paper by
[Ford, Norrie and Ahmadi (1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib127){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, which confirm the general point that the
inclusion of explanatory variables in models for survival data cannot be
expected to increase the precision of an estimated treatment effect.
:::

::: section
## 5.18 Further reading {#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_18 .head1}

The properties of the exponential, Weibull and other continuous
distributions are presented in [Johnson, Kotz and Balakrishnan
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib193){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib193 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Chhikara and Folks
(1989)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib60){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib60 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} give a detailed study of the inverse Gaussian
distribution. Use of the quadratic hazard function was discussed by
[Gaver and Acar
(1979)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib134){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib134 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and the bathtub hazard function was proposed by
[Hjorth (1980)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib179){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib179
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

A thorough discussion of the theory of maximum likelihood estimation is
included in [Barnett
(1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib31){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib31 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Cox and Hinkley
(1974)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib80){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib80 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and a useful summary of the main results is
contained in [Hinkley, Reid and Snell
(1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib178){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib178 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. Numerical methods for obtaining maximum
likelihood estimates, and the Newton-Raphson procedure in particular,
are described by [Everitt
(1987)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib116){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib116 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Thisted
(1988)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib383){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib383 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, for example; see also the description in [Section
3.3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3_3) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). [Byar
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib52){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib52 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} provides a comparison of the Cox and Weibull
proportional hazards models.

[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p215 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 215. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}[Cox and Oakes
(1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib81){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib81 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} show that the Weibull distribution is the only one
to have both the proportional hazards property and the accelerated
failure time property. They also demonstrate that the log-logistic
distribution is the only one that shares the accelerated failure time
property and the proportional odds property. One other distribution with
the proportional hazards property is the *Pareto distribution*. This
model is rarely used in practice, but see [Davis and Feldstein
(1979)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib96){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib96 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} for further details.

A description of the log-linear model for survival data is contained in
many of the major textbooks on survival analysis; see in particular [Cox
and Oakes (1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib81){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Kalbfleisch and Prentice
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib198){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib198 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Klein and Moeschberger
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib212){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} or
[Lawless (2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib229){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

A non-parametric version of the accelerated failure time model, which
does not require the specification of a probability distribution for the
survival data, has been introduced by [Wei
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib403){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib403 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. This paper, and the published discussion, [Fisher
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib124){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib124 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, includes comments on whether the accelerated
failure time model should be used more widely in the analysis of
survival data.

The application of the accelerated failure time and proportional odds
models to the analysis of reliability data is described by [Crowder et
al. (1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib88){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib88
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The general proportional odds model
for survival data was introduced by [Bennett
(1983a)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib32){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib32 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and Bennett (1983b) describes the log-logistic
proportional odds model. The model has been further developed by [Yang
and Prentice
(1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib422){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib422 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

Papers that define and compare measures of explained variation for the
Weibull model include those of Kent and O'Quigley (1988) and [Royston
and Sauerbrei
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib327){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib327 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, cited in [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). [Chan, Xu
and Chambers (2018)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib56){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib56
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study the performance of the
$R_{P}^{2}$ measure of explained variation in Equation (5.75) in
different accelerated failure time models.

A more general way of modelling survival data is to use a family of
distributions for survival times, which includes the Weibull and
log-logistic as special cases. The choice between alternative
distributions can then be made within a likelihood framework. In
particular, the exponential, Weibull, log-logistic, lognormal and gamma
distributions are special cases of the generalised *F*-distribution
described by [Kalbfleisch and Prentice
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib198){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
However, this methodology will only tend to be informative in the
analysis of data sets in which the number of death times is relatively
large.

Estimators of the hazard function based on kernel smoothing are
described by [Ramlau-Hansen
(1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib311){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib311 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and in the text of [Klein and Moeschberger
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib212){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
Parametric mixture models that incorporate cured fractions, and their
extension to semi-parametric models, have been described by a number of
authors, including [Farewell
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib117){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib117 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Kuk and Chen
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib219){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib219 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Taylor
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib375){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib375 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Sy and Taylor
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib372){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib372 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Peng and Dear
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib285){#chapter5.xhtml_b5refbib285 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.[]{#chapter5.xhtml_p216 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 216 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}
:::

[]{#chapter6.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter6.xhtml_p217 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 217. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 6]{.chapnum}[Flexible parametric models]{.chaptitle} {#chapter6.xhtml_chapter6 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-6](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-6){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

The parametric models for survival analysis described in [Chapters
5](#chapter5.xhtml) lead to baseline hazard functions that depend on a
small number of unknown parameters, and so have a limited ability to
capture the underlying form of a baseline hazard. In contrast, the main
advantage of the Cox regression model is often perceived to be the
flexibility of the baseline hazard function, since it can accommodate
the pattern needed for any particular data set. This advantage of the
Cox model is often overplayed. Since the baseline hazard function in a
Cox model is estimated from the data to give a step function with jumps
at each event time, it can behave very erratically, as illustrated in
[Example 3.14](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_14){#chapter6.xhtml_b3exam3_14} of
[Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). Also, the estimated survivor function for
an individual is constant between event times, so that it may not be
possible to estimate the survival rate at a precise time. Moreover,
survival rates at times beyond the longest event time in a data set
cannot be estimated. Flexible parametric models bridge the gap between
the Cox semi-parametric model and fully parametric models, and can be of
great value when standard parametric models do not provide a
satisfactory depiction of an underlying baseline hazard or survivor
function.

::: section
## 6.1 Piecewise exponential model {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1 .head1}

In the Cox regression model, the hazard function for the *i*th of *n*
individuals is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is the vector of values of *p*
explanatory variables, $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ is the vector of their
coefficients and $h_{0}(t)$ is the baseline hazard function. The model
is fitted by maximising a partial likelihood function that does not
depend on $h_{0}(t)$, and this leads to estimates of the *β*s.
Subsequently, the hazard, cumulative hazard and survivor functions can
be estimated by stepwise functions that change at event times. We now
consider a version of this model in which the baseline hazard function
is explicitly modelled using a parameter for each interval between the
event times.

Suppose that the survival times from *n* individuals consists of *r*
ordered event times $t_{(1)} < t_{(2)} < \cdots < t_{(r)}$, and $n - r$
right-censored times. In a model where the baseline hazard is constant
between adjacent event times, we can set

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{0}(t) = \lambda_{j},}
\end{array}$$[(6.1)]{.equationnum}

for $t_{(j - 1)}\leqslant t < t_{(j)}$, where $\lambda_{j} > 0$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,r$, and $t_{(0)} = 0$. Since $\lambda_{j} > 0$, the
model is often parameterised by setting $\lambda_{j} = \exp(\phi_{j})$
and regarding the *ϕ*s as the unknown parameters in the baseline hazard.
The hazard function in Equation (6.1) is piecewise constant in each time
interval, and so corresponds to fitting individual exponential models in
each interval. The model is therefore referred to as a *piecewise
exponential model*. The time points where the hazard function changes
are $t_{(1)},t_{(2)},\ldots,t_{(r - 1)}$ and these are known as the *cut
points* or *knots*.

[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p218 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 218. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}To estimate the unknown parameters in this model,
we can use the method of maximum likelihood, described in [Section
3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). The
likelihood function for a fully parametric model is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {h_{i}(t_{i})} \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}S_{i}(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$[(6.2)]{.equationnum}

first given in Expression (5.15) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml), where
$h_{i}(t_{i})$ and $S_{i}(t_{i})$ are the hazard and survivor functions
for the *i*th individual at time *t*~*i*~, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and
*δ*~*i*~ is the event indicator. On integrating the baseline hazard
function in Equation (6.1), the cumulative baseline hazard is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{H_{0}(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\lambda_{j}}\Delta_{j}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(6.3)]{.equationnum}

where

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\Delta_{j}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
0 & {\,\,\, t < t_{(j - 1)},} \\
{t - t_{(j - 1)}} & {\,\,\, t_{(j - 1)}\leqslant t < t_{(j)},} \\
{t_{(j)} - t_{(j - 1)}} & {\,\,\, t\geqslant t_{(j)}.}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

This cumulative hazard function consists of straight line segments
within each interval. The corresponding cumulative hazard function for
the *i*th individual is
$H_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})H_{0}(t)$,
and the survivor function can then be found using the relationship
$S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - H_{i}(t) \right\}$, given in Equation (1.6) of
[Chapter 1](#chapter1.xhtml).

In principle, the unknown parameters that form the baseline hazard
function, $\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2},\ldots,\lambda_{r}$, can be estimated
alongside the *β*s by maximising the logarithm of the likelihood
function in Equation (6.2). When there is a separate parameter *λ* for
each inter-event time in the data, substantial numerical problems can
arise when attempting to maximise this log-likelihood function,
particularly when there is a relatively large number of event times.
However, fast and efficient algorithms for achieving this are now
available. These were primarily developed for handling interval-censored
data, to be described in [Chapter 9](#chapter9.xhtml), but since right
censoring is a special case of interval censoring, the same algorithms
can be used here. Even so, the methods remain computationally demanding.
In addition, the usual way of constructing the standard error of an
estimate, based on the information matrix and described in [Appendix
A](#appA.xhtml_appA), is unstable, and simulation methods are generally
used. The simulation process known as *bootstrapping* involves sampling
*n* observations from the data set, with replacement, and estimating the
unknown parameters for this sample. This process is repeated a large
number of times, perhaps 500 or more, and the sample standard deviation
of the each estimate is calculated from the individual bootstrap
estimates. These standard deviations are then used to summarise the
precision of an estimated parameter.

Aside from computational difficulties, having a separate parameter for
each inter-event time does not really provide an effective summary of
the underlying hazard. It is preferable and simpler to adopt fewer time
intervals when constructing a piecewise constant baseline hazard. The
actual number of intervals will depend on the size of the data set, but
usually, no more than 10 will be needed. There is also the issue of
where to position the end points of the intervals. A commonly employed
approach is to divide the time period of the study into intervals that
each contain similar numbers of event times. Alternatively, it may be
possible to choose the time intervals in such a way that they are narrow
where the hazard varies markedly and wider when the hazard changes more
slowly. An informed choice of interval boundaries should lead to
reasonable approximations for any baseline hazard.

The use of a piecewise exponential model is illustrated in the following
example.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam6_1" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 6.1]{.boxnum}](#chapter6.xhtml_b6exam6_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Treatment of hypernephroma]{.boxht}

A study leading to data on the survival times of 36 patients with
hypernephroma was described in [Example
3.4](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_4){#chapter6.xhtml_b6exam3_4} of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml). A Cox regression model that includes age group and
[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p219 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 219. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}nephrectomy status was fitted and the estimated
hazard function was given in [Example 3.14](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_14).
The hazard ratio for nephrectomy status is $\exp( - 1.412) = 0.244$ and
the hazard ratios for those aged between 60 and 70 and those over 70,
relative to those aged less than 60, are $\exp(0.013) = 1.013$ and
$\exp(1.342) = 3.825$, respectively.

On fitting a piecewise exponential model with constant hazards between
the 23 ordered event times, the resulting hazard ratios for nephrectomy
status and age group are respectively 0.215, 1.124 and 4.518, which are
quite similar to those obtained from the Cox regression model. The
estimated values of the *λ*-parameters in the baseline hazard function
are broadly similar to those given [Table
3.19](#chapter3.xhtml_tbl3_19){#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl3_19} of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml), although both sets of estimates are very imprecise.
The hazard function for the fitted piecewise exponential model is shown
in [Figure 6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_1){#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_1}. This
has a similar pattern to that for the Cox model, given in [Figure
3.4](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_4){#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig3_4} of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml).

<figure id="fig6_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig6_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">6.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Baseline hazard functions for a piecewise
exponential with jumps at event times (<span
class="middle">.......</span>) and a piecewise exponential model with
five intervals (—).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The estimated baseline hazard function has 22 different parameters and
is grossly overparameterised for a data set of this size. For
illustration, a piecewise exponential model with just five intervals is
also fitted, where the times 12, 26, 56 and 84 months define the
intervals. These four cut points, or knots, between the shortest and
longest event times of 5 and 115 months, correspond approximately to
quintiles of the event times. The hazard ratios for nephrectomy status
and age group are now 0.236, 1.179 and 4.944, respectively, which are
very similar to those found for the model with 22 intervals.

These two piecewise exponential models can be compared using the *AIC*
statistic, defined in Equation (3.14) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml).
The number of unknown parameters for the model with 22 intervals, *q*,
is $22 + 3 = 25$ and for this model, $AIC = 298.70$. For the model with
five intervals, the number of parameters is 8 and $AIC = 283.48$. The
simpler model is therefore preferred. Note that the *AIC* statistic for
the fitted Cox regression model is formed from a maximised partial
likelihood and so cannot be compared to the *AIC* values for piecewise
exponential models, which are based on the full likelihood.

[Figure 6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_1) suggests that the five individual
hazards in each interval are quite similar, so that the baseline hazard
may in fact be constant. The data would then have an exponential
distribution and on fitting this model, the constant baseline hazard is
0.058. The value of []{#chapter6.xhtml_p220 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 220. " role="doc-pagebreak"}the corresponding *AIC*
statistic is 298.76, so that this model does not fit these data as well
as the more complex piecewise model with five intervals.

</aside>

It is difficult to discern pattern in the estimated baseline hazard
function in a fitted Cox regression model, as the hazard at each event
time is determined from the number of events and the number at risk at
that time. Moreover, in practical applications, it is highly unlikely
that an underlying hazard function will be stepwise. A smoothed estimate
of this function is therefore desirable. This can be achieved by
applying a smoothing process to the estimated cumulative baseline
hazard, followed by numerical differentiation to give a smooth estimate
of the hazard function itself. Although this estimate is useful in a
descriptive analysis, it is not straightforward to use the fitted curve
to estimate survival rates from the fitted model, nor to validate the
fit of such a curve. A more satisfactory approach is to use models for a
hazard based on smooth parametric functions, and these are described in
subsequent sections.
:::

::::: section
## 6.2 Modelling using spline functions {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_2 .head1}

Spline functions are widely used in biomedical research to model
non-linear functions of time. They are constructed by dividing the time
scale into a number of intervals and fitting a polynomial function of
the survival time, *t*, within each interval. This is done in such a way
that the functions are constrained to join smoothly at the interval
boundaries, which are known as *knots*. The set of knots defining the
intervals is known as the *knot sequence*. In the simplest case, we take
the smallest and largest values of *t* and divide the range into two
halves. There would then be two *boundary knots* at the extreme values
of *t* and one *internal knot* between them. Increasing the number of
internal knots leads to a more complex curve. The degree of a spline
function is defined to be the degree of the constituent polynomial
functions. Spline functions of degree three, also known as cubic
splines, are generally used as they provide a compromise between
simplicity and flexibility. They are also straightforward to calculate,
numerically stable, and lead to smooth curves, and so we will restrict
attention to these.

As an illustration of a spline function, suppose that the range of
values of *t* is from *t*~*min*~ to *t*~*max*~, and that one internal
knot is specified at the point where $t = t_{1}$. A cubic expression in
*t* is then defined for $t_{\min}\leqslant t\leqslant t_{1}$ and for
$t_{1}\leqslant t\leqslant t_{\max}$. These two cubic expressions are
then constrained to have a smooth join at the internal knot *t*~1~ to
give a cubic spline. Now consider a cubic spline function over the range
of a variable *t* with *m* internal knots at $t_{1},t_{2},\ldots,t_{m}$,
and where the minimum and maximum values of *t* form boundary knots at
*t*~*min*~ and *t*~*max*~. There will then be $m + 1$ intervals to which
cubic expressions are fitted. Each individual cubic function, called a
*basis function*, is constrained to join neighbouring functions smoothly
at the internal knots. The number of basis functions needed increases
with the number of internal knots, and a linear combination of these
basis functions is a spline function. The unknown coefficients of the
basis functions that make up the spline function are estimated in the
model fitting process.

There are many different types of spline function, but we will restrict
attention to those that are most commonly used in modelling survival
data.

::: section
### 6.2.1 B-splines {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_2_1 .head2}

A B-spline function of degree *k* with *m* internal knots is constructed
from $m + k + 1$ basis functions, each of degree *k*. Any linear
combination of these basis functions leads to a smooth curve that can be
used to model specific functions of *t*, such as a hazard or survivor
[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p221 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 221. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}function. A B-spline function of *t* is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{B(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m + k + 1}\alpha_{j}}B_{j,k}(t),\quad t_{\min}\leqslant t\leqslant t_{\max},}
\end{array}$$[(6.4)]{.equationnum}

where $B_{j,k}(t)$, is the *j*th basis function of degree *k* with
coefficients *α*~*j*~, $j = 1,2,\ldots,m + k + 1$.

A cubic spline function, where $k = 3$ in Equation (6.4), is then given
by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{B(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m + 4}\alpha_{j}}B_{j,3}(t),\quad t_{\min}\leqslant t\leqslant t_{\max},}
\end{array}$$[(6.5)]{.equationnum}

which has $m + 4$ basis functions $B_{j,3}(t)$ of degree three. The
$m + 4$ unknown *α*-parameters in this function are estimated by fitting
this function to observed data.

The *j*th basis function for a cubic spline, $B_{j,3}(t)$, is defined
using a recurrence relationship, but first the knot sequence is
augmented by adding three lower and three upper boundary knots to allow
the recurrence relationship to be fully defined. The full knot sequence
is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{t_{\min},\, t_{\min},\, t_{\min},\, t_{\min},\, t_{1},\, t_{2},\,\ldots,\, t_{m},\, t_{\max},\, t_{\max},\, t_{\max},\, t_{\max},}
\end{array}$$

and these $m + 8$ augmented knots will now be denoted
$\tau_{1},\tau_{2},\ldots,\tau_{m + 8}$. For each knot in this sequence,
a recursive relationship is used to obtain the B-spline basis functions,
$B_{j,3}(t)$. First, set

$$\begin{array}{l}
{B_{j,0}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
1 & {\,\,\,\text{if~}\tau_{j}\leqslant t < \tau_{j + 1},} \\
0 & {\,\,\,\text{otherwise,}}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

to give the *j*th basis function of degree zero. Then, basis functions
of degrees 1, 2 and 3 are obtained using the recurrence relationship

$$\begin{array}{r}
{B_{j,k + 1}(t) = \omega_{j,k + 1}(t)B_{j,k}(t) + \left\{ 1 - \omega_{j + 1,k + 1}(t) \right\} B_{j + 1,k}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(6.6)]{.equationnum}

where

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\omega_{j,k}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
{(t - \tau_{j})/(\tau_{j + k} - \tau_{j})} & {\,\,\,\text{if~}\tau_{j + k} \neq \tau_{j},} \\
0 & {\,\,\,\text{otherwise,}}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

for $j = 1,2,\ldots,m + 4$. By setting $k = 0,1,2$ in turn in Equation
(6.6), this recursive process leads to the $m + 4$ B-spline basis
functions of degree three, $B_{j,3}(t)$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,m + 4$. On
substituting these functions in Equation (6.5), we obtain a cubic
B-spline function that can be fitted to observed data. This recursion
argment is illustrated in [Example
6.2](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_2){#chapter6.xhtml_b6exam6_2}.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam6_2" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 6.2]{.boxnum}](#chapter6.xhtml_b6exam6_2){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Illustration of a spline function]{.boxht}

To illustrate the process of constructing a B-spline, suppose that a
data set contains survival times in the range from 0 to 10 units. Also,
suppose that a cubic B-spline function of time *t* is to be constructed
with one internal knot, so that $m = 1$ in Equation (6.5). This knot
will be taken to be at the midpoint of the time scale, where $t = 5$.
The boundary knots are at 0 and 10, and these are repeated to give the
augmented knot sequence $(0,0,0,0,5,10,10,10,10)$.

The spline function will have five basis functions, $B_{j,3}(t)$,
constructed using the recurrence relationship in Equation (6.6), by
taking $k = 0,1,2$ and $j = 1,2,\ldots,5$. After much algebra, the basis
functions are found to be as follows:

$$\begin{array}{ll}
{B_{1,3}(t)} & {= \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
{( - t^{3} + 15t^{2} - 75t + 125)/125} & {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, 0\leqslant t < 5} \\
0 & {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, 5\leqslant t < 10}
\end{array}~ \right.} \\
 & \\
{B_{2,3}(t)} & {= \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
{t(7t^{2} - 90t + 300)/500} & {\,\,\,\,\, 0\leqslant t < 5} \\
{( - t^{3} + 30t^{2} - 300t + 1000)/500} & {\,\,\,\,\, 5\leqslant t < 10}
\end{array}~ \right.} \\
 & \\
{B_{3,3}(t)} & {= \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
{t^{2}( - 2t + 15)/250} & {\,\,\,\,\,\,\, 0\leqslant t < 5} \\
{(2t^{3} - 45t^{2} + 300t - 500)/250} & {\,\,\,\,\,\,\, 5\leqslant t < 10}
\end{array}~ \right.} \\
 & \\
{B_{4,3}(t)} & {= \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
{t^{3}/500} & {\,\, 0\leqslant t < 5} \\
{( - 7t^{3} + 120t^{2} - 600t + 1000)/500} & {\,\, 5\leqslant t < 10}
\end{array}~ \right.} \\
 & \\
{B_{5,3}(t)} & {= \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
0 & {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, 0\leqslant t < 5} \\
{(t^{3} - 15t^{2} + 75t - 125)/125} & {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, 5\leqslant t < 10}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p222 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 222. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Each of these basis functions is a cubic expression
in *t* defined over the range $(0,10)$, with smooth joins at the
position of the internal knot. These basis functions are shown as dotted
lines in [Figure 6.2](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_2){#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_2}.
This figure shows how the cubic expressions either side of the internal
knot at $t = 5$ join smoothly.

<figure id="fig6_2" class="image">
<img src="images/fig6_2.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_2" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">6.2</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Cubic B-spline basis functions (<span
class="middle">.......</span>) when there is one internal knot and a
fitted spline curve (—).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

Now suppose that in using the spline function
$B(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{5}\alpha_{j}}B_{j,3}(t)$ to model a
function of *t*, estimates of the five coefficients of the spline basis
functions, $\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2},\ldots,\alpha_{5}$, are respectively
$0.8, - 0.3,1.2,0.5$ and 0.4. The resulting fitted function is shown in
[Figure 6.2](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_2) as a solid line. The B-spline
function shown in this figure decreases at first before increasing to
peak at around $t = 6$, and then decreasing more slowly. This figure
also illustrates how a linear combination of the values of the basis
functions leads to a smooth spline function. Different sets of values
for the *α*-coefficients will lead to different spline curves. Spline
functions constructed in this way can be used to represent a wide
variety of functional forms and are particularly useful in modelling a
baseline hazard function or its logarithm.

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 6.2.2 Restricted cubic splines {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_2_2 .head2}

A polynomial spline, such as a B-spline, can behave erratically at
extremes of the range of observed values of a survival time *t*, that
is, in the vicinity of the boundary knots. In addition, the B-spline
function is not defined beyond these boundary knots. This limits the
[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p223 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 223. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}usefulness of the model in predicting outside the
range of observed values of *t*. This can be resolved by using a
*restricted* or *natural* cubic spline, where the range of values of *t*
is again divided into $m + 1$ intervals by using *m* internal knots and
a cubic expression fitted within each interval and constrained to join
smoothly at the knots. In addition, a linear term in *t* is assumed for
$t < k_{\min}$ and $t > k_{\max}$. This leads to a slightly more simple
representation than that of a B-spline model, as fewer basis functions
are required for a given number of internal knots. [Figure
6.3](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_3){#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_3} illustrates a
restricted cubic spline with boundary knots at *k*~*min*~ and *k*~*max*~
and an internal knot at *k*~1~.

<figure id="fig6_3" class="image">
<img src="images/fig6_3.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_3" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">6.3</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Restricted cubic spline with an internal knot
at <em>k</em><sub>1</sub> and boundary knots at
<em>k</em><sub><em>min</em></sub> and
<em>k</em><sub><em>max</em></sub>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

When there are *m* internal knots, a restricted cubic spline has *m*
basis functions, denoted $\nu_{1}(t),\nu_{2}(t),\ldots,\nu_{m}(t)$, and
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}t + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m}\gamma_{j + 1}}\nu_{j}(t).}
\end{array}$$[(6.7)]{.equationnum}

In this expression, the *j*th basis function, $\nu_{j}(t)$, is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\nu_{j}(t) = {(t - k_{j})}_{+}^{3} - \lambda_{j}{(t - k_{\min})}_{+}^{3} - (1 - \lambda_{j}){(t - k_{\max})}_{+}^{3},}
\end{array}$$[(6.8)]{.equationnum}

where *k*~*j*~ is the *j*th internal knot, $j = 1,2,\ldots,m$,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\lambda_{j} = \frac{k_{\max} - k_{j}}{k_{\max} - k_{\min}},}
\end{array}$$[(6.9)]{.equationnum}

and ${(t - a)}_{+}^{3} = \max\left\{ 0,{(t - a)}^{3} \right\}$ for any
value of *a*, so that ${(t - a)}_{+}^{3}$ is the greater of zero and
${(t - a)}^{3}$. This spline function has $m + 2$ unknown
*γ*-parameters, estimated from fitting the spline function to observed
data. The special case where $m = 0$, that is where there are no
internal knots, leads to a restricted cubic spline that is simply a
linear function of *t*, $\gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}t$.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam6_3" role="doc-tip">

[Example 6.3]{.boxnum} [Illustration of a spline function]{.boxht}

In [Example 6.2](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_2), a B-spline function was
constructed for data consisting of survival times, *t*, between 0 and 10
units with one internal knot at $t = 5$ units. In this example, an
expression for the corresponding restricted cubic spline function is
obtained. The boundary knots are $k_{\min} = 0$ and $k_{\max} = 10$,
respectively, and the internal knot is at $k_{1} = 5$. A restricted
cubic spline with one internal knot has just the one basis function, and
from Equation (6.8), this is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\nu_{1}(t) = {(t - 5)}_{+}^{3} - \lambda_{1}{(t - 0)}_{+}^{3} - (1 - \lambda_{1}){(t - 10)}_{+}^{3},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p224 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 224. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where from Equation (6.9),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\lambda_{1} = (10 - 5)/(10 - 0) = 0.5.}
\end{array}$$

The basis function is therefore

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\nu_{1}(t) = {(t - 5)}_{+}^{3} - 0.5t^{3} - 0.5{(t - 10)}_{+}^{3},}
\end{array}$$

for $t\geqslant 0$, so that

$$\begin{array}{ll}
{\nu_{1}(t)} & {= \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
{- 0.5t^{3}} & {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, 0\leqslant t < 5,} \\
{{(t - 5)}^{3} - 0.5t^{3}} & {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, 5\leqslant t < 10.}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

For $t > 10$,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\nu_{1}(t) = {(t - 5)}^{3} - 0.5t^{3} - 0.5{(t - 10)}^{3},}
\end{array}$$

which reduces to $\nu_{1}(t) = 375 - 75t$, so that $\nu_{1}(t)$, and
hence the restricted spline function, is linear in *t* beyond the upper
boundary knot.

For any values of *γ*~0~, *γ*~1~ and *γ*~2~ in Equation (6.7), the
restricted cubic spline function is
$\gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}t + \gamma_{2}\nu_{1}(t)$, which is linear for
$t > 10$ and a cubic expression for $0\leqslant t < 5$ and
$5\leqslant t < 10$. In contrast to the B-spline function, a restricted
cubic spline is defined for all values of *t*, not just those between
the boundary knots.

</aside>

The spline functions described here can be used in modelling either the
hazard or cumulative hazard functions and lead to extremely flexible
parametric models for survival data.
:::
:::::

::: section
## 6.2.3 Number and position of the knots {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_2_3 .head1i}

Whatever spline function is used, the number and position of the knots
will have some effect on the form of the fitted function. Although some
authors have proposed using automatic or data driven procedures to
determine where knots should be placed, others caution against this, on
the grounds that they can be unduly affected by local features of the
data set, and that it is difficult to take account of this when
constructing standard errors of parameter estimates. Instead, it is
usually recommended that boundary knots are the smallest and largest
values of the observed event times in a data set, with internal knots
taken to give equal spacing between percentiles of the distribution of
uncensored event times. Specifically, one internal knot would be placed
at the median of uncensored values of observed survival times, two
internal knots would be placed at the 33% and 67% percentiles, three
knots would be placed at the quartiles, and so on. Usually, no more than
4 or 5 knots will be needed in practical applications.

To determine the number of knots to be used, we start with just the two
boundary knots. A model with one internal knot is then fitted, and
compared to the model with zero knots. Next, a model with two internal
knots is then fitted and compared to that with one knot, and so on. As
the position of the knots changes as their number is increased, for
example from one at the 50th percentile to two at the 33rd and 67th
percentiles of the distribution of the event times, models with
different numbers of knots are not necessarily nested. This means that
the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic cannot generally be used to determine
whether increasing the number of knots gives a significant improvement
in the fit. Instead, the *AIC* statistic, introduced in [Section
3.6.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_6_1) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), can be
used. The *AIC* statistic is $- 2\log\hat{L} + 2q$, where *q* is the
total number of unknown parameters in the fitted model, including those
associated with the spline functions. For example, when a restricted
cubic spline model with *m* internal knots is used in a model for
survival times that contains *p* explanatory variables, there will be
*pβ*-parameters and $m + 2$ *γ*-coefficients in the spline function, so
[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p225 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 225. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}that $q = p + m + 2$. Models with the smallest
values of the *AIC* statistic are generally the most suitable, and so
the number of knots is increased until the *AIC* statistic cannot be
reduced any further. Models can be compared in a similar manner using
the *BIC* statistic, also defined in [Section
3.6.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_6_1) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml).
:::

::: section
## 6.3 Flexible models for the hazard function {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_3 .head1}

Spline models for the baseline hazard function provide a flexible
approach to parametric modelling. Consider a proportional hazards model
where the hazard of an event at time *t* for the *i*th of *n*
individuals is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\eta_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

and $\eta_{i} = \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, where
the *β*s are coefficients of *p* explanatory variables. To ensure that
fitted hazard functions are always non negative, models are built from
the logarithm of the baseline hazard function. If a cubic B-spline
function of time with *m* internal knots is used for the log-baseline
hazard, $\log h_{0}(t)$, then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log h_{0}(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m + 4}\alpha_{j}}B_{j,3}(t),}
\end{array}$$

using the spline function in Equation (6.5). The spline model for the
logarithm of the hazard function for the *i*th individual is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log h_{i}(t) = \eta_{i} + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m + 4}\alpha_{j}}B_{j,3}(t).}
\end{array}$$[(6.10)]{.equationnum}

This model can be fitted by maximising the likelihood function of
observed data, given in Equation (6.2). For this, the survivor function
$S_{i}(t)$ is needed, and can be obtained by first integrating the
baseline hazard function to give the cumulative baseline hazard
$H_{0}(t)$. This has to be done numerically, as a B-spline on the
log-hazard scale is being used. Then
$S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - e^{\eta_{i}}H_{0}(t) \right\}$.

The modelling procedure proceeds by fitting models with increasing
numbers of knots, as described in [Section
6.2.3](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_2_3). For each model, a measure of model
adequacy, such as the *AIC* or *BIC* statistic, is used to identify
models with the smallest values of these statistics.

An important special case of this model is obtained using B-spline basis
functions of degree zero, so that $k = 0$ in Equation (6.4). In this
case, there are $m + 1$ spline basis functions, $B_{j,0}(t)$, that are
unity for $t_{j}\leqslant t < t_{j + 1}$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,m + 1$, with
$t_{1} = t_{\min}$ and $t_{m + 2} = t_{\max}$, and zero elsewhere. The
B-spline function is then constant in each of the $m + 1$ time
intervals. The corresponding model for the hazard function is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{0}(t) = \exp\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m + 1}\alpha_{j}}B_{j,0}(t)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and so $h_{0}(t) = \exp(\alpha_{j})$ in the *j*th time interval,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,m + 1$. This is another form of the piecewise
exponential model described in [Section 6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1),
and corresponds to a Cox regression model when the number and position
of the knots is that of the event times.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam6_4" role="doc-tip">

[Example 6.4]{.boxnum} [Treatment of hypernephroma]{.boxht}

In [Example 6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_1){#chapter6.xhtml_b6exam6_1},
piecewise exponential hazard functions were fitted to the data on
patients with hypernephroma. We now consider modelling the baseline
hazard with a parametric model based on a spline function, using the
model in Equation (6.10). A proportional hazards model that contains age
group and nephrectomy status is fitted, in which the
[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p226 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 226. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}log-baseline hazard function is a B-spline function
of degree three. On fitting models with increasing numbers of internal
knots, situated at percentiles of the event times, we find the value of
the *AIC* statistic is smallest for a model with two internal knots. The
fitted baseline hazard function is the exponential of a linear
combination of six basis functions, given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{0}(t) = \exp\left\{ {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}B_{1,3}(t) + {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}B_{2,3}(t) + \cdots + {\hat{\alpha}}_{6}B_{6,3}(t) \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where the functions $B_{j,3}(t),j = 1,2,\ldots,6$, are cubic expressions
in *t*. This leads to a rather complicated function of *t* that is shown
in [Figure 6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_4){#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_4}.

<figure id="fig6_4" class="image">
<img src="images/fig6_4.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_4" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">6.4</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Baseline hazard function on fitting a
B-spline model to the data on survival times of patients with
hypernephroma</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The estimated baseline hazard function in this figure has peaks at
around 10 and 40 days, and declines thereafter. These two peaks can just
about be seen in the plot of the baseline hazard function for the
piecewise exponential regression model in [Figure
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_1). For this model, the hazard ratio for
nephrectomy status is 0.250, and those for patients aged 60-70 and over
70 relative to those aged less than 60, are 0.962 and 3.974,
respectively. These are very similar to those found on fitting the
piecewise exponential model in [Example 6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_1),
and so the precise form of the estimated baseline hazard function has
little impact on estimated hazard ratios. However, the value of the
*AIC* statistic for the B-spline model with two internal knots is
292.83, which is larger than that found on fitting a piecewise
exponential model with four internal knots in [Example
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_1). Consequently, the spline based hazard
function does not lead to an improved fit.

</aside>
:::

::: section
## 6.4 Flexible models for the log-cumulative hazard function {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_4 .head1}

When a spline model is used to model a hazard function, there is always
the risk of unusual behaviour near the shortest and longest event times.
Models developed for the log-cumulative hazard or survivor function are
less susceptible to this.

Flexible models for the log-cumulative hazard function are commonly
based on restricted cubic splines described in [Section
6.2.2](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_2_2), and in the particular case of there
being no internal knots, the model reduces to a Weibull model. To see
this, the Weibull hazard function at time *t* for the *i*th of *n*
individuals is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p227 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 227. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is the vector of
values of *p* explanatory variables with coefficients
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ and the baseline hazard function is
$h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$. The parameters *λ* and *γ*
determine the scale and shape of the underlying Weibull distribution.
The corresponding cumulative hazard function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{H_{i}(t) = {\int_{0}^{t}h_{i}}(u)\,\text{d}u = \exp(\eta_{i})\lambda t^{\gamma},}
\end{array}$$

where $\eta_{i} = \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, and
so the log-cumulative hazard function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log H_{i}(t) = \eta_{i} + \log\lambda + \gamma\log t.}
\end{array}$$

Setting $\gamma_{0} = \log\lambda$, $\gamma_{1} = \gamma$ and
$y = \log t$, the log-cumulative hazard function for the Weibull model
can be written

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log H_{i}(t) = \eta_{i} + \gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}y.}
\end{array}$$

This formulation shows that the log-cumulative hazard function is linear
in $y = \log t$.

From Equation (6.7), a restricted cubic spline model for the logarithm
of the baseline cumulative hazard with *m* internal knots, in terms of
$y = \log t$, can be written as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log H_{0}(t) = \gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}y + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m}\gamma_{j + 1}}\nu_{j}(y).}
\end{array}$$[(6.11)]{.equationnum}

In this equation, for the *j*th internal knot at *k*~*j*~,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,m$,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\nu_{j}(y) = {(y - k_{j})}_{+}^{3} - \lambda_{j}{(y - k_{\min})}_{+}^{3} - (1 - \lambda_{j}){(y - k_{\max})}_{+}^{3},}
\end{array}$$[(6.12)]{.equationnum}

*k*~*min*~ and *k*~*max*~ are boundary knots,
${(y - a)}_{+}^{3} = \max\left\{ 0,{(y - a)}^{3} \right\}$, and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\lambda_{j} = \frac{k_{\max} - k_{j}}{k_{\max} - k_{\min}}.}
\end{array}$$

The *j*th basis function $\nu_{j}(y)$ can be expressed less succinctly
as

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\nu_{j}(y) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
0 & {y < k_{\min},} \\
{- \lambda_{j}{(y - k_{\min})}^{3}} & {k_{\min}\leqslant y\leqslant k_{j},} \\
{{(y - k_{j})}^{3} - \lambda_{j}{(y - k_{\min})}^{3}} & {k_{j}\leqslant y\leqslant k_{\max},} \\
{{(y - k_{j})}^{3} - \lambda_{j}{(y - k_{\min})}^{3} - (1 - \lambda_{j}){(y - k_{\max})}^{3}} & {y > k_{\max}.}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

When $y > k_{\max}$, the expression for $\nu_{j}(y)$ simplifies to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{(k_{\max} - k_{j})(k_{\min} - k_{j})(3y - k_{\min} - k_{j} - k_{\max}),}
\end{array}$$

which confirms that the basis function is a linear function of *y* when
$y > k_{\max}$, and a cubic function of *y* for values of *y* between
*k*~*min*~ and *k*~*max*~.

The cumulative hazard function for the *i*th individual in a
proportional hazards model is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{H_{i}(t) = {\int_{0}^{t}\exp}(\eta_{i})h_{0}(u)\,\text{d}u = \exp(\eta_{i})H_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

and so a restricted cubic spline model for $\log H_{i}(t)$ is given by
