$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log H_{i}(t) = \eta_{i} + \gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}y + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m}\gamma_{j + 1}}\nu_{j}(y),}
\end{array}$$[(6.13)]{.equationnum}

where the *γ*-parameters are estimated along with the *β*-parameters
when fitting the model to observed data. The model defined by Equation
(6.13) was first proposed by Royston and []{#chapter6.xhtml_p228
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 228. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Parmar (2002)
and is often referred to as the *Royston and Parmar model*. This
extended parametric form of the log-cumulative hazard function means
that the survival times no longer have a Weibull distribution under this
model, nor any other recognisable distribution, although the model still
assumes proportional hazards amongst the explanatory variables.

The model in Equation (6.13) can also be expressed in terms of the
survivor function, $S_{i}(t)$, using the result
$S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - H_{i}(t) \right\}$ to give

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - \exp\lbrack\eta_{i} + \gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}y + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m}\gamma_{j + 1}}\nu_{j}(y)\rbrack \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(6.14)]{.equationnum}

Equivalently,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \left\{ S_{0}(t) \right\}^{\exp(\eta_{i})},}
\end{array}$$

where $S_{0}(t) = \exp\left\{ - H_{0}(t) \right\}$ is the baseline
survivor function and $H_{0}(t)$ is found from Equation (6.11). One
reason why it is often advantageous to use splines on the log-cumulative
hazard scale is that the corresponding survivor function is readily
obtained.

A model for the hazard function, $h_{i}(t) = \exp(\eta_{i})h_{0}(t)$,
can also be found from the corresponding parametric model for the
baseline hazard function, $h_{0}(t)$. Differentiating $\log H_{0}(t)$
with respect to *t*, gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\text{d}\,\log H_{0}(t)}{\text{d}\, t} = \frac{1}{H_{0}(t)}\frac{\text{d}\, H_{0}(t)}{\text{d}\, t} = \frac{1}{H_{0}(t)}h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

so that the baseline hazard function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{0}(t) = H_{0}(t)\frac{\text{d}\,\log H_{0}(t)}{\text{d}\, t}.}
\end{array}$$

The derivative of $\log H_{0}(t)$ in Equation (6.11) with respect to *t*
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\text{d}\,\log H_{0}(t)}{\text{d}\, t} = \frac{\text{d}\,\log H_{0}(t)}{\text{d}\, y}\,\frac{\text{d}\, y}{\text{d}\, t} = \left\{ \gamma_{1} + \gamma_{2}\nu_{1}'(y) + \cdots + \gamma_{m + 1}\nu_{m}'(y) \right\} t^{- 1},}
\end{array}$$

where

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\nu_{j}'(y) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
0 & {y\leqslant k_{\min},} \\
{- 3\lambda_{j}{(y - k_{\min})}^{2}} & {k_{\min}\leqslant y\leqslant k_{j},} \\
{3{(y - k_{j})}^{2} - 3\lambda_{j}{(y - k_{\min})}^{2}} & {k_{j}\leqslant y\leqslant k_{\max},} \\
{3{(y - k_{j})}^{2} - 3\lambda_{j}{(y - k_{\min})}^{2} - 3(1 - \lambda_{j}){(y - k_{\max})}^{2}} & {y\geqslant k_{\max},}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

is the first derivative of $\nu_{j}(y)$ with respect to $y = \log t$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,m$. Consequently,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = e^{\eta_{i}}h_{0}(t) = e^{\eta_{i}}\left\{ {\gamma_{1} + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m}\gamma_{j + 1}}\nu_{j}'(y)} \right\} t^{- 1}H_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(6.15)]{.equationnum}

where $H_{0}(t)$ is given in Equation (6.11), is a fully parametric
proportional hazards model in which the baseline hazard is a smooth
function of $t = e^{y}$ with $m + 2$ unknown parameters,
$\gamma_{0},\gamma_{1},\ldots,\gamma_{m + 1}$.

The model for the log-cumulative hazard function in Equation (6.13), can
be fitted using the method of maximum likelihood. The likelihood
function is again that in Equation (6.2), and the survivor and hazard
functions for the *i*th individual at time *t*~*i*~, $S_{i}(t_{i})$ and
$h_{i}(t_{i})$, are given in Equations (6.14) and (6.15). The logarithm
of this likelihood function can be maximised using standard optimisation
routines, and this leads to fitted survivor and hazard functions that
are smooth functions of the survival time *t*. The fitting process also
leads to []{#chapter6.xhtml_p229 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 229. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}standard errors of the parameter estimates and
functions of them, such as estimates of the hazard and survivor
functions at any given time.

Experience in using these parametric models suggests that the number of
knots is barely affected by covariate adjustment. This means that a
variable selection process can be based on standard Weibull or Cox
models. Once the explanatory variables for inclusion in the Royston and
Parmar model have been determined, restricted cubic spline models
containing these variables that have increasing numbers of internal
knots can be fitted. Again, preferred models are those with the smallest
values of the *AIC* or *BIC* statistics.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam6_5" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 6.5]{.boxnum}](#chapter6.xhtml_b6exam6_5){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Recurrence-free survival in breast cancer
patients]{.boxht}

A cohort study of breast cancer in a large number of hospitals was
carried out by the German Breast Cancer Study Group to compare three
cycles of chemotherapy with six cycles, and also to investigate the
effect of additional hormonal treatment consisting of a daily dose of 30
mg of tamoxifen over two years. The patients in the study had primary
histologically proven non-metastatic node-positive breast cancer who had
been treated with mastectomy. The response variable of interest is
recurrence-free survival, which is the time from entry to the study
until a recurrence of the cancer or death. Earlier analyses of the data
had shown that recurrence-free survival was not affected by the number
of cycles of chemotherapy, and so only the factor associated with
whether or not a patient received tamoxifen is included in this example.
In addition to this treatment factor, data were available on patient
age, menopausal status, size and grade of the tumour, number of positive
lymph nodes, progesterone and oestrogen receptor status. [Schumacher et
al. (1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib344){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib344
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} give the background to the study.

The data in this example relate to data from 41 centres and 686 patients
with complete data, and were included in [Sauerbrei and Royston
(1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib332){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib332 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. The variables in this data set are as follows:

  ----------- -----------------------------------------------------------
  *Id*:       Patient number
  *Treat*:    Hormonal treatment (0 = no tamoxifen, 1 = tamoxifen)
  *Age*:      Patient age (years)
  *Men*:      Menopausal status (1 = premenopausal, 2 = postmenopausal)
  *Size*:     Tumour size (mm)
  *Grade*:    Tumour grade (1, 2, 3)
  *Nodes*:    Number of positive lymph nodes
  *Prog*:     Progesterone receptor status (femtomoles)
  *Oest*:     Oestrogen receptor status (femtomoles)
  *Time*:     Recurrence-free survival time (days)
  *Status*:   Event indicator (0 = censored, 1 = relapse or death)
  ----------- -----------------------------------------------------------

Data for 20 of the 686 patients in this study are shown in [Table
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_tbl6_1){#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl6_1}. In analysing
these data, we might be interested in the treatment effect after
adjusting for other explanatory variables, and in estimating the
adjusted hazard functions for the two treatment groups.

  *Id*   *Treat*   *Age*   *Men*   *Size*   *Grade*   *Nodes*   *Prog*   *Oest*   *Time*   *Status*
  ------ --------- ------- ------- -------- --------- --------- -------- -------- -------- ----------
   1     0         70      2       21       2         3         48       66       1814     1
   2     1         56      2       12       2         7         61       77       2018     1
   3     1         58      2       35       2         9         52       271      712      1
   4     1         59      2       17       2         4         60       29       1807     1
   5     0         73      2       35       2         1         26       65       772      1
   6     0         32      1       57       3         ​​​24        0        13       448      1
   7     1         59      2       8        2         2         181      0        2172     0
   8     0         65      2       16       2         1         192      25       2161     0
   9     0         80      2       39       2         ​​​30        0        59       471      1
  10     0         66      2       18       2         7         0        3        2014     0
  11     1         68      2       40       2         9         16       20       577      1
  12     1         71      2       21       2         9         0        0        184      1
  13     1         59      2       58       2         1         154      101      1840     0
  14     0         50      2       27       3         1         16       12       1842     0
  15     1         70      2       22       2         3         113      139      1821     0
  16     0         54      2       30       2         1         135      6        1371     1
  17     0         39      1       35       1         4         79       28       707      1
  18     1         66      2       23       2         1         112      225      1743     0
  19     1         69      2       25       1         1         131      196      1781     0
  20     0         55      2       65       1         4         312      76       865      1

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [6.1]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl6_1){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Recurrence-free survival times of 20 breast
  cancer patients]{.table-title} {#tbl6_1}

As an initial step in the analysis of these data, the suitability of a
Weibull model is investigated using a log-cumulative hazard plot,
stratified by treatment. Although the presence of additional explanatory
variables hinders the interpretation of such a plot for just one factor,
the graph shown in [Figure
6.5](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_5){#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_5} does not exhibit
straight lines for the two treatment groups. The assumption of a Weibull
distribution for the survival times is therefore not appropriate.
However, the vertical separation of two curves in this plot appears
constant suggesting that the proportional hazards assumption for the
treatment effect is valid.

<figure id="fig6_5" class="image">
<img src="images/fig6_5.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_5" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">6.5</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Log-cumulative hazard plot for the women not
on tamoxifen (∙) and those in the tamoxifen group
(∗).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

At this stage, a variable selection process may be used to determine
which of the other explanatory factors, *Age, Men, Size, Grade, Nodes,
Prog*, and *Oest*, are needed in the model in addition to *Treat*, but
in this example, all of them will be included. Royston and Parmar
[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p230 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 230. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}models with increasing numbers of internal knots
are then fitted. [Table
6.2](#chapter6.xhtml_tbl6_2){#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl6_2} gives the value
of the *AIC* statistic for the models fitted, where the model with zero
knots is the standard Weibull model. There is a large reduction in the
value of the *AIC* statistic on fitting a restricted cubic spline with
one internal knot, indicating that this model is a substantial
improvement on the standard Weibull model. On adding a second and third
knot, the *AIC* statistic is reduced further, but the decrease is much
less than that when one knot is added. On adding a fourth internal knot,
the *AIC* statistic increases. This analysis shows that the
[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p231 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 231. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}best fitting model has three knots, although this
is not a marked improvement on that with just one knot. The fitted model
with three knots is most simply expressed in terms of the estimated
log-cumulative hazard function for the *i*th patient, given by

$$\begin{aligned}
{\log{\hat{H}}_{i}(t)} & {= {\hat{\beta}}_{1}Treat_{i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{2}Age_{i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{3}Men_{i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{4}Size_{i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{5}Grade_{i}} \\
 & {\text{\quad\quad} + {\hat{\beta}}_{6}Nodes_{i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{7}Prog_{i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{8}Oest_{i} + \log{\hat{H}}_{0}(t),}
\end{aligned}$$

  Number of Knots   *AIC*
  ----------------- --------
  0                 5182.4
  1                 5147.9
  2                 5147.1
  3                 5146.1
  4                 5148.2

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [6.2]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl6_2){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Values of the *AIC* statistic for models with up
  to four internal knots]{.table-title} {#tbl6_2}

where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log{\hat{H}}_{0}(t) = {\hat{\gamma}}_{0} + {\hat{\gamma}}_{1}y + {\hat{\gamma}}_{2}\nu_{1}(y) + {\hat{\gamma}}_{3}\nu_{2}(y) + {\hat{\gamma}}_{4}\nu_{3}(y)}
\end{array}$$

is the baseline cumulative hazard function for a model with three
internal knots, $y = \log t$ and the functions
$\nu_{1}(y),\nu_{2}(y),\nu_{3}(y)$ are defined in Equation (6.12).

A visual summary of the fit of the Royston and Parmar models is shown in
[Figure 6.6](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_6){#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_6}. This
figure shows the adjusted baseline survivor function on fitting a Cox
regression model that contains the 8 explanatory variables, shown as a
step function. In addition, the adjusted baseline survivor function for
a Weibull model, and models with one and three internal knots, are
shown. This figure confirms that the underlying risk-adjusted baseline
survivor function from the Cox model is not well fitted by a Weibull
model. A Royston and Parmar model with one knot tracks the estimated Cox
baseline survivor function much more closely, and that with three knots
gives an improved performance at the longer survival times.

<figure id="fig6_6" class="image">
<img src="images/fig6_6.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_6" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">6.6</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Risk-adjusted survivor functions for a fitted
Cox regression model (—), Weibull model (- . -) and Royston-Parmar
models with one (<span class="math inline">⋯⋯⋯</span>) and three (- - -)
knots.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#chapter6.xhtml_p232 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 232. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The estimated values of the parameters and their
standard errors for the Royston and Parmar model with three knots, are
given in [Table 6.3](#chapter6.xhtml_tbl6_3){#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl6_3}.
\>Also shown in this table are the estimated *β*-parameters in a fitted
Cox model and their standard errors. The estimates and their standard
errors for the two models are very similar. The adjusted hazard ratio
for a patient on tamoxifen, relative to one who is not, is 0.71 under
both models, so that the hazard of recurrence of cancer or death is
lower for patients on tamoxifen.

+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
|           | Estimate     | $\,\text{se}\,$ |   | Estimate   | $\,\text{se}\,$ |
|           |              | (Estimate)      |   |            | (Estimate)      |
+===========+==============+=================+===+============+=================+
| *β*~1~    | --0.3386     | 0.1290          |   | --0.3372   | 0.1290          |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *β*~2~    | --0.0096     | 0.0093          |   | --0.0094   | 0.0093          |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+

+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *β*~4~    | 0.0077       | 0.0040          |   | 0.0077     | 0.0039          |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *β*~5~    | 0.2824       | 0.1059          |   | 0.2801     | 0.1061          |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *β*~6~    | 0.0497       | 0.0074          |   | 0.0499     | 0.0074          |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *β*~7~    | --0.0022     | 0.0006          |   | --0.0022   | 0.0006          |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *β*~8~    | 0.0002       | 0.0004          |   | 0.0002     | 0.0004          |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *γ*~0~    | $-$ 20.4691  | 3.2958          |   |            |                 |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *γ*~1~    | 2.9762       | 0.5990          |   |            |                 |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *γ*~2~    | --0.4832     | 0.5873          |   |            |                 |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *γ*~3~    | 1.4232       | 0.8528          |   |            |                 |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+
| *γ*~4~    | --0.9450     | 0.4466          |   |            |                 |
+-----------+--------------+-----------------+---+------------+-----------------+

: [[[Table]{.label}
[6.3]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl6_3){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Parameter estimates and their standard errors for
a Royston and Parmar model with three knots and a Cox regression
model]{.table-title} {#tbl6_3}

The Royston and Parmar model has the advantage of providing a parametric
estimate of the baseline hazard. The fitted baseline hazard function,
adjusted for the eight explanatory variables, for the Weibull model and
the Royston and Parmar spline models with one and three knots, is shown
in [Figure 6.7](#chapter6.xhtml_fig6_7){#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_7}. The
Royston and Parmar model indicates that the underlying hazard function
is unimodal, and so it is not surprising that the Weibull model is a
poor fit.

<figure id="fig6_7" class="image">
<img src="images/fig6_7.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter6.xhtml_b6fig6_7" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">6.7</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Adjusted baseline hazard functions for a
fitted Weibull model (—) and a Royston-Parmar model with one (<span
class="middle">.......</span>) and three knots (- -
-).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

</aside>
:::

::: section
## 6.5 []{#chapter6.xhtml_p233 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 233. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Flexible proportional odds models {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_5 .head1}

Parametric models based on cubic splines can also be used in conjunction
with proportional odds models described in [Section
5.15](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_15) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml) The
general proportional odds model for the odds of survival beyond time
*t*, $S_{i}(t)/\left\{ 1 - S_{i}(t) \right\}$, is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{S_{i}(t)}{1 - S_{i}(t)} = \exp\left\{ \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} \right\}\frac{S_{0}(t)}{1 - S_{0}(t)},}
\end{array}$$

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is the vector of values of the
explanatory variables for the *i*th individual and $S_{0}(t)$ is the
baseline survivor function, that is the survivor function for an
individual whose explanatory variables all take the value zero. If a
log-logistic model with parameters *θ, κ* is assumed for the survival
times, as in [Section 5.15.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_15_1), the log-odds
of survival beyond time *t* is, from Equation (5.9),
$S(t) = \left\{ {1 + e^{\theta}t^{\kappa}} \right\}^{- 1}$. Taking this
to be the baseline survivor function, $S_{0}(t)$ is then such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\left( \frac{S_{0}(t)}{1 - S_{0}(t)} \right) = - \theta - \kappa\log t = \gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}y,}
\end{array}$$

where $\gamma_{0} = - \theta$, $\gamma_{1} = - \kappa$, and the model is
linear in $y = \log t$. Extending this model to incorporate non-linear
terms to give a restricted cubic spline with *m* internal knots, and
using the same notation as in Equation (6.13), we get

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\left( \frac{S_{0}(t)}{1 - S_{0}(t)} \right) = \gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}y + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m}\gamma_{j + 1}}\nu_{j}(y),}
\end{array}$$

which is analogous to the expression for the log-cumulative baseline
hazard function in Equation (6.11). The proportional odds model that
includes spline terms can then be expressed as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log\left( \frac{S_{i}(t)}{1 - S_{i}(t)} \right) = \eta_{i} + \gamma_{0} + \gamma_{1}y + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m}\gamma_{j + 1}}\nu_{j}(y),}
\end{array}$$

where $\eta_{i} = \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$.

This flexible proportional odds model is used in just the same way as
the flexible proportional hazards model and is particularly suited to
situations where the hazard function is unimodal. The model can also be
expressed in terms of the odds of an event occurring before time *t*,
$\log\lbrack F(t)/\left\{ 1 - F(t) \right\}\rbrack$, and as this is
simply $- \log\lbrack S(t)/\left\{ 1 - S(t) \right\}\rbrack$, the
resulting parameter estimates will only differ in sign.

The model is fitted using the method of maximum likelihood, and leads to
an estimate of the survivor function. Estimates of the corresponding
hazard and cumulative hazard functions can then straightforwardly be
obtained.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam6_6" role="doc-tip">

[Example 6.6]{.boxnum} [Recurrence-free survival in breast cancer
patients]{.boxht}

The unimodal hazard function identified in [Example
6.5](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_5){#chapter6.xhtml_b6exam6_5} suggests that a
log-logistic model may be a better fit to the recurrence-free survival
times for women with breast cancer. [Table
6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_tbl6_4){#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl6_4} gives the value
of the *AIC* statistic for the model for the log-odds of surviving
beyond time *t* that has a flexible baseline survivor function, where
the model with zero knots is the standard proportional odds model. As in
[Example 6.5](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_5), the explanatory variables *Age,
Men, Size, Grade, Nodes, Prog* and *Oest* are included in the model, in
addition to the treatment factor, *Treat*. Notice that the values of the
*AIC* statistic behave somewhat erratically for models with 1, 2 and 3
knots, with the value of the statistic increasing slightly on adding a
fourth knot. This feature can occur when the models being fitted are
barely distinguishable, and since these models have increasing numbers
of knots at different locations, not all of them are nested. In this
case, a model for the log-odds of surviving []{#chapter6.xhtml_p234
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 234. " role="doc-pagebreak"}beyond time *t*
that contains three knots is the best fit, although the fit of this
model is very similar to that of the model with just one knot. Moreover,
since the *AIC* statistics in [Table 6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_tbl6_4) are
smaller than they are for the corresponding proportional hazards models
in [Table 6.2](#chapter6.xhtml_tbl6_2), the proportional odds model is
preferred.

  Number of Knots   *AIC*
  ----------------- --------
  0                 5154.0
  1                 5135.4
  2                 5135.8
  3                 5134.1
  4                 5136.2

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [6.4]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter6.xhtml_b6tbl6_4){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Values of the *AIC* statistic for models with up
  to four internal knots]{.table-title} {#tbl6_4}

For the model with three knots, the estimated parameter associated with
the treatment effect is 0.5287. The ratio of the odds of surviving
beyond time *t* for a patient on tamoxifen, relative to one who is not,
is $\exp(0.5287) = 1.70$, and so the odds of a patient on tamoxifen
surviving beyond any given time are 1.7 times that for a patient who has
not received that treatment. This result is entirely consistent with the
corresponding hazard ratio for the treatment effect, given in [Example
6.5](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_5).

</aside>
:::

::: section
## 6.6 Further reading {#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_6 .head1}

The piecewise exponential model was introduced by [Breslow
(1974)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib43){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib43 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, who pointed out that it is equivalent to the Cox
regression model with constant hazards between each death time. The
model was described in greater detail by [Friedman
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib129){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib129 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, although [Lawless
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib229){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib229 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} includes a less mathematical treatment. Efficient
numerical optimisation methods for fitting the piecewise exponential
model include the expectation-maximisation (EM) algorithm described in
[Dempster, Laird and Rubin
(1977)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib100){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib100 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, the Iterative Convex Minorant Algorithm described
by [Groeneboom and Wellner
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib159){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib159 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and a combination of the EM and ICM algorithms
described by [Wellner and Zahn
(1997)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib410){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib410 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

A detailed account of spline functions is given by [de Boor
(2001)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib98){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib98 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, while [Racine
(2021)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib308){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib308 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Perperoglou et al.
(2019)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib289){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib289 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} provide accessible overviews of spline functions
in regression models. The use of cubic splines in regression models was
described by [Durrleman and Simon
(1989)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib106){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib106 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and Sleeper and Harrington (1989). An overview of
B-splines is included in [Eilers and Marx
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib111){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib111 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and additive B-spline models for the baseline
hazard function are described in [Rosenberg
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib321){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib321 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Royston and Parmar
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib325){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib325 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describe flexible parametric models for both
proportional hazards and proportional odds models based on a restricted
cubic spline. A comprehensive account of the model, and its
implementation in Stata, is included in [Royston and Lambert
(2011)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib324){#chapter6.xhtml_b6refbib324 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

[]{#chapter7.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter7.xhtml_p235 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 235. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 7]{.chapnum}[Model checking in parametric models]{.chaptitle} {#chapter7.xhtml_chapter7 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-7](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-7){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

Diagnostic procedures for the assessment of model adequacy are as
important in parametric modelling as they are when the Cox regression
model is used in the analysis of survival data. Procedures based on
residuals are particularly relevant, and so we begin this chapter by
defining residuals for parametric models, some of which stem from those
developed for the Cox regression model, described in [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml). This is followed by a summary of graphical
procedures for assessing the suitability of models fitted to data that
are assumed to have a Weibull, log-logistic or lognormal distribution.
Other ways of examining the fit of a parametric regression model are
then considered, along with methods for the detection of influential
observations. This chapter concludes with a summary of how the
assumption of proportional hazards can be examined after fitting the
Weibull proportional hazards model.

:::::::: section
## 7.1 Residuals for parametric models {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1 .head1}

Suppose that *T*~*i*~ is the random variable associated with the
survival time of the *i*th individual, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and that
$x_{1i},x_{2i},\ldots,x_{pi}$ are the values of *p* explanatory
variables, $X_{1},X_{2},\ldots,X_{p}$, for this individual. Assuming an
accelerated failure time model for *T*~*i*~, we have that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log T_{i} = \mu + \alpha_{1}x_{1i} + \alpha_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \alpha_{p}x_{pi} + \sigma\epsilon_{i},}
\end{array}$$

where *ϵ*~*i*~ is a random variable with a probability distribution that
depends on the distribution adopted for *T*~*i*~, and *μ, σ* and
*α*~*j*~, $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, are unknown parameters. If the observed
survival time of the *i*th individual is censored, the corresponding
residual will also be censored, complicating the interpretation of these
quantities.

::: section
### 7.1.1 Standardised residuals {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_1 .head2}

A natural form of residual to adopt in accelerated failure time
modelling is the *standardised residual* defined by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Si} = \left\{ {\log t_{i} - \hat{\mu} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}x_{1i} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}x_{2i} - \cdots - {\hat{\alpha}}_{p}x_{pi}} \right\}/\hat{\sigma},}
\end{array}$$[(7.1)]{.equationnum}

where *t*~*i*~ is the observed survival time of the *i*th individual,
and $\hat{\mu}$, $\hat{\sigma}$, ${\hat{\alpha}}_{j}$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, are the estimated parameters in the fitted
accelerated failure time model. This residual has the appearance of a
quantity of the form 'observation$\, - \,$ fitted value', and would be
expected to have the same distribution as that of *ϵ*~*i*~ in the
accelerated failure time model, if the model were correct. For example,
if a Weibull distribution is adopted for *T*~*i*~, the $r_{Si}$ would be
expected to behave as if they were a possibly censored sample from a
Gumbel distribution, if the fitted model is correct. The estimated
survivor function of the residuals would then be similar to the survivor
function of *ϵ*~*i*~, that is, $S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon)$. Using the
general result in [Section 4.1.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_1_1) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml), $- \log S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon)$ has a unit
exponential distribution, and so it follows that
$- \log S_{\epsilon_{i}}(r_{Si})$ will have an approximate unit
exponential distribution, if the []{#chapter7.xhtml_p236 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 236. " role="doc-pagebreak"}fitted model is
appropriate. This provides the basis for a diagnostic plot that may be
used in the assessment of model adequacy, described in [Section
7.2.4](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_2_4).
:::

::: section
### 7.1.2 Cox-Snell residuals {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_2 .head2}

The Cox-Snell residuals that were defined for the Cox regression model
in [Section 4.1.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_1_1) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml) are essentially the estimated values of the
cumulative hazard function for the *i*th observation, at the
corresponding event time, *t*~*i*~. Residuals that have a similar form
may also be used in assessing the adequacy of parametric models. The
main difference is that now the survivor and hazard functions are
parametric functions that depend on the distribution adopted for the
survival times.

The Cox-Snell residuals for a parametric model are defined by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Ci} = {\hat{H}}_{i}(t_{i}) = - \log{\hat{S}}_{i}(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$[(7.2)]{.equationnum}

where ${\hat{H}}_{i}(t_{i})$ is the estimated cumulative hazard
function, and ${\hat{S}}_{i}(t_{i})$ is the estimated survivor function,
evaluated at *t*~*i*~. The estimated survivor function for the *i*th
individual, on fitting an accelerated failure time model, from Equation
(5.65) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = S_{\epsilon_{i}}\left( \frac{\log t - \hat{\mu} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}x_{1i} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}x_{2i} - \cdots - {\hat{\alpha}}_{p}x_{pi}}{\hat{\sigma}} \right),}
\end{array}$$[(7.3)]{.equationnum}

where $S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon)$ is the survivor function of *ϵ*~*i*~
in the accelerated failure time model, ${\hat{\alpha}}_{j}$ is the
estimated coefficient of $x_{ji}$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, and $\hat{\mu}$,
$\hat{\sigma}$ are the estimated values of *μ* and *σ*. The form of
$S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon)$ for some commonly used distributions for
*T*~*i*~ was described in [Section 5.12](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_12) of
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml).

As in the context of the Cox regression model, the Cox-Snell residuals
can be taken to have a unit exponential distribution when the correct
model has been fitted, with censored observations leading to censored
residuals; see [Section 4.1.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_1_1) for details.
The Cox-Snell residuals in Equation (7.2) are also very closely related
to the standardised residuals in Equation (7.1), since from Equation
(7.3), we see that $r_{Ci} = - \log S_{\epsilon_{i}}(r_{Si})$.
Assessment of whether the standardised residuals have a particular
distribution is therefore equivalent to assessing whether the
corresponding Cox-Snell residuals have a unit exponential distribution.
:::

::: section
### 7.1.3 Martingale residuals {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_3 .head2}

The martingale residuals provide a measure of the difference between the
observed number of deaths in the interval $(0,t_{i})$, which is either 0
or 1, and the number predicted by the model. Observations with unusually
large martingale residuals are not well fitted by the model. The
analogue of the martingale residual, defined for the Cox regression
model in Equation (4.6) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml), is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Mi} = \delta_{i} - r_{Ci},}
\end{array}$$[(7.4)]{.equationnum}

where *δ*~*i*~ is the event indicator for the *i*th observation, so that
*δ*~*i*~ is unity if that observation is an event and zero if censored,
and now $r_{Ci}$ is the Cox-Snell residual given in Equation (7.2). For
reasons given in [Section 7.2.1](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_2_1), the
martingale residuals for a parametric accelerated failure time model sum
to zero, but are not symmetrically distributed about zero. Strictly
speaking, it is no longer appropriate to refer to these residuals as
martingale residuals since the derivation of them, based on martingale
methods, does not carry over to the accelerated failure time model.
However, for semantic convenience, we will continue to refer to the
quantities in Equation (7.4) as martingale residuals.
:::

::: section
### 7.1.4 []{#chapter7.xhtml_p237 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 237. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Deviance residuals {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_4 .head2}

The deviance residuals, which were first presented in Equation (4.7) of
[Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml), can be regarded as an attempt to make the
martingale residuals symmetrically distributed about zero and are
defined by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Di} = \text{sgn}(r_{Mi})\left\lbrack {- 2\left\{ {r_{Mi} + \delta_{i}\log(\delta_{i} - r_{Mi})} \right\}} \right\rbrack^{\frac{1}{2}}.}
\end{array}$$[(7.5)]{.equationnum}

This definition leads to deviance residuals that will often be similar
to those from a comparable Cox regression model. However, the values of
$r_{Di}$ in Equation (7.5) will generally have the opposite sign from
the standardised residuals, $r_{Si}$, in Equation (7.1). This is because
the parametric accelerated failure time model is based on the logarithm
of a survival time, rather than a hazard function. It is therefore more
appropriate for the deviance residuals for an accelerated failure time
model to be defined as the values $- r_{Di}$. This definition is used in
some software packages for survival analysis. It is also important to
note that the deviance residuals in Equation (7.5) are not components of
the deviance for the fitted parametric model, but nonetheless it will be
convenient to continue to refer to them as deviance residuals.
:::

::: section
### 7.1.5 Score residuals {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_5 .head2}

Score residuals, which parallel the score residuals, or Schoenfeld
residuals, used in connection with the Cox regression model, can be
defined for any parametric model. The score residuals are the components
of the derivatives of the log-likelihood function, with respect to the
unknown parameters, *μ, σ* and *α*~*j*~, $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, and
evaluated at the maximum likelihood estimates of these parameters,
$\hat{\mu}$, $\hat{\sigma}$ and ${\hat{\alpha}}_{j}$. From Equation
(5.74) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), the log-likelihood function for
*n* observations is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}},\mu,\sigma) = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {- \delta_{i}\log(\sigma t_{i}) + \delta_{i}\log f_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i}) + (1 - \delta_{i})\log S_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i})} \right\}},}
\end{array}$$

where
$z_{i} = (\log t_{i} - \mu - \alpha_{1}x_{1i} - \alpha_{2}x_{2i} - \cdots - \alpha_{p}x_{pi})/\sigma$,
$f_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon)$ and $S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon)$ are the
density and survivor functions of *ϵ*~*i*~, respectively, and *δ*~*i*~
is the event indicator.

Differentiating this log-likelihood function with respect to the
parameters *μ, σ* and *α*~*j*~, for $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, gives the
derivatives

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial\log L}{\partial\mu} & {= \sigma^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}g}(z_{i}),} \\
\frac{\partial\log L}{\partial\sigma} & {= \sigma^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {z_{i}g(z_{i}) - \delta_{i}} \right\}},} \\
\frac{\partial\log L}{\partial\alpha_{j}} & {= \sigma^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}x_{ji}}g(z_{i}),}
\end{aligned}$$

where the function $g(z_{i})$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{g(z_{i}) = \frac{(1 - \delta_{i})f_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i})}{S_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i})} - \frac{\delta_{i}f_{\epsilon_{i}}'(z_{i})}{f_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i})},}
\end{array}$$[(7.6)]{.equationnum}

and $f_{\epsilon_{i}}'(z_{i})$ is the derivative of
$f_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i})$ with respect to *z*~*i*~. The *i*th component
of each derivative, evaluated at the maximum likelihood estimates of the
unknown parameters, is then the score residual for the corresponding
term. Consequently, from the definition of the standardised residual in
Equation (7.1), the *i*th score residual for *μ* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}g(r_{Si}),}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter7.xhtml_p238 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 238. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}that for the scale parameter, *σ*, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}\left\{ {r_{Si}\, g(r_{Si}) - \delta_{i}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and that for the *j*th explanatory variable in the model, *X*~*j*~, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Uji} = {\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}x_{ji}\, g(r_{Si}).}
\end{array}$$

Of these, the score residuals for *X*~*j*~ are the most important, and
as in [Section 4.1.6](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_1_6) are denoted $r_{Uji}$.
Specific expressions for these residuals are given in the sequel for
some particular parametric models. Because the sums of score residuals
are the derivatives of the log-likelihood function at its maximum, these
residuals must sum to zero.
:::
::::::::

::::::: section
## 7.2 Residuals for particular parametric models {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_2 .head1}

In this section, the form of the residuals for parametric models based
on Weibull, log-logistic and lognormal distributions for the survival
times are described.

::: section
### 7.2.1 Weibull distribution {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_2_1 .head2}

The residuals described in [Section 7.1](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1) may be
used in conjunction with either the proportional hazards or the
accelerated failure time representations of the Weibull model. We begin
with the proportional hazards model described in [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), according to which the hazard of death at time *t*
for the *i*th individual is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\beta_{1}x_{1i} + \beta_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \beta_{p}x_{pi})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$ is the baseline hazard
function. The corresponding estimate of the cumulative hazard function
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{H}}_{i}(t) = \exp({\hat{\beta}}_{1}x_{1i} + {\hat{\beta}}_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + {\hat{\beta}}_{p}x_{pi})\hat{\lambda}t^{\hat{\gamma}}}
\end{array}$$

which are the Cox-Snell residuals, defined in Equation (7.2).

In the accelerated failure time version of the model, *ϵ*~*i*~ has a
Gumbel distribution, with survivor function

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon) = \exp( - e^{\epsilon}).}
\end{array}$$[(7.7)]{.equationnum}

The standardised residuals are then as given in Equation (7.1), and if
an appropriate model has been fitted, these will be expected to behave
as a possibly censored sample from a Gumbel distribution. This is
equivalent to assessing whether the Cox-Snell residuals, defined below,
have a unit exponential distribution.

The Cox-Snell residuals, $r_{Ci} = - \log S_{\epsilon_{i}}(r_{Si})$,
are, from Equation (7.7), simply the exponentiated standardised
residuals, that is $r_{Ci} = \exp(r_{Si})$. These residuals lead
immediately to the martingale and deviance residuals for the Weibull
model, using Equations (7.4) and (7.5).

The score residuals for the Weibull model are found from the general
results in [Section 7.1.5](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_5). Here,
$S_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i}) = \exp( - e^{z_{i}})$ from Equation (7.7), and
differentiating $S_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i})$ and multiplying by $- 1$ gives
$f_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i}) = e^{z_{i}}\exp( - e^{z_{i}})$. Also,
$f_{\epsilon_{i}}'(z_{i}) = (1 - e^{z_{i}})f_{\epsilon_{i}}(z_{i})$, and
substitution into Equation (7.6) leads to
$g(z_{i}) = e^{z_{i}} - \delta_{i}$ after some algebra. The *i*th score
residual for the *j*th explanatory variable in the model, *X*~*j*~, is
then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Uji} = {\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}x_{ji}\left( {e^{r_{Si}} - \delta_{i}} \right),}
\end{array}$$

where $r_{Si}$ is the *i*th standardised residual and *δ*~*i*~ the event
indicator.

We also note that the *i*th score residual for *μ* is
${\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}(e^{r_{Si}} - \delta_{i})$, which is
${\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}(r_{Ci} - \delta_{i})$. Since these score residuals
sum to zero, it follows that the sum of the martingale residuals,
defined in Equation (7.4), must be zero in the Weibull model.
:::

::: section
### 7.2.2 []{#chapter7.xhtml_p239 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 239. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Log-logistic distribution {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_2_2 .head2}

In the log-logistic accelerated failure time model, the random variable
*ϵ*~*i*~ has a logistic distribution with survivor function

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon) = \left( {1 + e^{\epsilon}} \right)^{- 1}.}
\end{array}$$

Accordingly, the standardised residuals, obtained from Equation (7.1),
should behave as a sample from a logistic distribution, if the fitted
model is correct. Equivalently, the Cox-Snell residuals for the
log-logistic accelerated failure time model are given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Ci} = - \log S_{\epsilon_{i}}\left( r_{Si} \right),}
\end{array}$$

that is,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Ci} = \log\left\{ {1 + \exp\left( r_{Si} \right)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where $r_{Si}$ is the *i*th standardised residual. The score residuals
are found from the general results in [Section
7.1.5](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_5), and using Equation (7.6) we find that
the *i*th score residual for the *j*th explanatory variable in the model
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Uji} = {\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}x_{ji}\left\{ \frac{\exp(r_{Si}) - \delta_{i}}{1 + \exp(r_{Si})} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$
:::

::: section
### 7.2.3 Lognormal distribution {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_2_3 .head2}

If the survival times are assumed to have a lognormal distribution, then
*ϵ*~*i*~ in the log-linear formulation of the accelerated failure time
model is normally distributed. The estimated survivor function for the
*i*th individual, from Equation (5.73), is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = 1 - \Phi\left( \frac{\log t - \hat{\mu} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}x_{1i} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}x_{2i} - \cdots - {\hat{\alpha}}_{p}x_{pi}}{\hat{\sigma}} \right),}
\end{array}$$

and so the Cox-Snell residuals become

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Ci} = - \log\left\{ {1 - \Phi\left( r_{Si} \right)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where, as usual, $r_{Si}$ is the *i*th standardised residual in Equation
(7.1). Again the martingale and deviance residuals are obtained from
these, and the score residuals are obtained from the results in [Section
7.1.5](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_5). Specifically, the *i*th score residual
for *X*~*j*~, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Uji} = {\hat{\sigma}}^{- 1}\left\{ {\frac{(1 - \delta_{i})f_{\epsilon_{i}}(r_{Si})}{1 - \Phi(r_{Si})} + \delta_{i}r_{Si}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where $f_{\epsilon_{i}}(r_{Si})$ is the standard normal density function
at $r_{Si}$, and $\Phi(r_{Si})$ is the corresponding distribution
function.
:::

::: section
### 7.2.4 Analysis of residuals {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_2_4 .head2}

In the analysis of residuals after fitting a parametric model to
survival data, one of the most useful plots is based on comparing the
distribution of the Cox-Snell residuals with the unit exponential
distribution. As noted in [Section 7.1.1](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_1),
this is equivalent to comparing the distribution of the standardised
residuals to that of the random variable *ϵ*~*i*~ in the log-linear form
of the accelerated failure time model. This comparison is made using a
cumulative hazard, or log-cumulative hazard, plot of the residuals, as
shown in [Section 4.2.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_2_1) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml), where the use of this plot in connection with
residuals after fitting the Cox regression []{#chapter7.xhtml_p240
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 240. " role="doc-pagebreak"}model was
described. In summary, the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor
function of the Cox-Snell residuals, denoted $\hat{S}(r_{Ci})$, is
obtained, and $- \log\hat{S}(r_{Ci})$ is plotted against $r_{Ci}$. A
straight line with unit slope and zero intercept will suggest that the
fitted model is appropriate. Alternatively, a log-cumulative hazard plot
of the residuals, obtained by plotting
$\log\left\{ - \log\hat{S}(r_{Ci}) \right\}$ against $\log r_{Ci}$, will
also give a straight line with unit slope and passing through the
origin, if the fitted survival model is satisfactory.

In [Section 4.2.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_2_1), substantial criticisms
were levied against the use of this plot. However, these criticisms do
not have as much force for residuals derived from parametric models. The
reason for this is that the non-parametric estimate of the baseline
cumulative hazard function, used in the Cox regression model, is now
replaced by an estimate of a parametric function. This function usually
depends on just two parameters, *μ* and *σ*, and so fewer parameters are
being estimated when an accelerated failure time model is fitted to
survival data. The Cox-Snell residuals for a parametric model are
therefore much more likely to be approximated by a unit exponential
distribution, when the correct model has been fitted.

Other residual plots that are useful include index plots of martingale
or deviance residuals, which can be used to identify observations not
well fitted by the model. A plot of martingale or deviance residuals
against the survival times, the rank order of the times, or explanatory
variables, shows whether there are particular times, or particular
values of explanatory variables, for which the model is not a good fit.
Plots of martingale or deviance residuals against the estimated
acceleration factor,
$\exp( - {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})$, the
time ratio,
$\exp({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})$, or
simply the estimated linear component of the accelerated failure time
model, ${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, also
provide information about the relationship between the residuals and the
likely survival time of an individual. Those with large values of the
estimated acceleration factor will tend to have shorter survival times.
Index plots of score residuals, or plots of these residuals against the
survival times, or the rank order of the survival times, might be
examined in a more comprehensive assessment of model adequacy.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam7_1" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 7.1]{.boxnum}](#chapter7.xhtml_b7exam7_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Chemotherapy in ovarian cancer patients]{.boxht}

In [Example 5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_11){#chapter7.xhtml_b7exam5_11}
of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), data on the survival times of patients
with ovarian cancer were presented. The data were analysed using a
Weibull proportional hazards model, and the model chosen contained
variables corresponding to the age of the woman, *Age*, and the
treatment group to which the woman was assigned, *Treat*. In the
accelerated failure time representation of the model, the estimated
survivor function for the *i*th woman is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = S_{\epsilon_{i}}\left( \frac{\log t - \hat{\mu} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}\, Age_{i} - {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}\, Treat_{i}}{\hat{\sigma}} \right),}
\end{array}$$

where $S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon) = \exp( - e^{\epsilon})$, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- \exp\left( \frac{\log t - 10.4254 + 0.0790\, Age_{i} - 0.5615\, Treat_{i}}{0.5489} \right)} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

The standardised residuals are the values of

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Si} = (\log t_{i} - 10.4254 + 0.0790\, Age_{i} - 0.5615\, Treat_{i})/0.5489,}
\end{array}$$

for $i = 1,2,\ldots,26$, and these are given in [Table
7.1](#chapter7.xhtml_tbl7_1){#chapter7.xhtml_b7tbl7_1}. Also given are
the values of the Cox-Snell residuals, which for the Weibull model, are
such that $r_{Ci} = \exp(r_{Si})$.

  Patient   $r_{Si}$    $r_{Ci}$   Patient   $r_{Si}$    $r_{Ci}$
  --------- ----------- ---------- --------- ----------- ----------
   1        $- 1.320$   0.267      14        $- 1.782$   0.168
   2        $- 1.892$   0.151      15        $- 0.193$   0.825
   3        $- 2.228$   0.108      16        $- 1.587$   0.204
   4        $- 2.404$   0.090      17        $- 0.917$   0.400
   5        $- 3.270$   0.038      18        $- 1.771$   0.170
   6        $- 0.444$   0.642      19        $- 1.530$   0.217
   7        $- 1.082$   0.339      20        $- 2.220$   0.109
   8        $- 0.729$   0.482      21        $- 0.724$   0.485
   9         0.407      1.503      22        $- 1.799$   0.165
  10         0.817      2.264      23         0.429      1.535
  11        $- 1.321$   0.267      24        $- 0.837$   0.433
  12        $- 0.607$   0.545      25        $- 1.287$   0.276
  13        $- 1.796$   0.166      26        $- 1.886$   0.152

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [7.1]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter7.xhtml_b7tbl7_1){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Values of the standardised and Cox-Snell
  residuals for 26 ovarian cancer patients]{.table-title} {#tbl7_1}

A cumulative hazard plot of the Cox-Snell residuals is given in [Figure
7.1](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_1){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_1}. In this figure,
the plotted points lie on a line that has an intercept and slope close
to zero and unity, respectively. However, there is some evidence of a
systematic deviation from the straight line, giving some cause for
concern about the adequacy of the fitted model.

<figure id="fig7_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Cumulative hazard plot of the Cox-Snell
residuals.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

Plots of the martingale and deviance residuals, $r_{Mi}$ and $r_{Di}$,
against the rank order of the survival times are shown in [Figures
7.2](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_2){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_2} and
[7.3](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_3){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_3}, respectively.
Both of these plots show []{#chapter7.xhtml_p241 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 241. " role="doc-pagebreak"}a slight tendency for
observations with longer survival times to have smaller residuals, but
these are also the observations that are censored.

<figure id="fig7_2" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_2.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_2" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.2</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the martingale residuals against rank
order of survival time.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig7_3" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_3.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_3" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.3</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the deviance residuals against rank
order of survival time.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The graphs in [Figure
7.4](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_4){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_4} show the score
residuals for the two variables in the model, *Age* and *Treat*, plotted
against the rank order of the survival times. The plot of the score
residuals for *Age* shows that there are three observations with
relatively large residuals. These correspond to patients 14, 4 and 26 in
the original data set given in [Table
5.6](#chapter5.xhtml_tbl5_6){#chapter7.xhtml_b7tbl5_6}. However, there
does not appear to be anything unusual about these observations. The
score residual for *Treat* for patient 26 is also somewhat larger than
the others. This points to the fact that the model is not a good fit to
the data from patients 14, 4 and 26.

<figure id="fig7_4" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_4.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_4" class="backlink"

role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.4</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Score residuals plotted against rank order of
survival time for <em>Age</em> and
<em>Treat</em>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

</aside>
:::
:::::::

::: section
## 7.3 Comparing observed and fitted survivor functions {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_3 .head1}

In parametric modelling, the estimated survivor function is a continuous
function of the survival time, *t*, and so this function can be plotted
for particular values of the explanatory []{#chapter7.xhtml_p242
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 242. " role="doc-pagebreak"}variables
included in the model. When there is just a single sample of survival
data, with no explanatory variables, the fitted survivor function can be
compared directly with the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor
function, described in [Section 2.1.2](#chapter2.xhtml_sec2_1_2) of
[Chapter 2](#chapter2.xhtml). If the fitted survivor function is close
to the Kaplan-Meier estimate, which is a step function, the fitted model
is an appropriate summary of the data. Similarly, suppose that the model
incorporates one or two factors that classify individuals according to
treatment group, or provide a cross-classification of treatment group
and gender. For each group of individuals defined by the combinations of
levels of the factors in the model, the fitted survivor function can
then be compared with the corresponding Kaplan-Meier estimate of the
survivor function.

In situations where the values of a number of explanatory variables are
recorded, groups of individuals are formed from the values of the
estimated linear component of the fitted []{#chapter7.xhtml_p243
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 243. " role="doc-pagebreak"}model. For the
*i*th individual, whose values of the explanatory variables in the model
are $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, this is just the risk score in a
proportional hazards model,
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, or the value
of ${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ in an
accelerated failure time model. The following discussion is based on the
risk score, where large positive values correspond to a greater hazard,
but it could equally well be based on the linear component of an
accelerated failure time model, or the value of the acceleration factor
or time ratio.

The values of the risk score for each individual are arranged in
increasing order, and these values are used to divide the individuals
into a number of groups. For example, if three groups were used, there
would be individuals with low, medium and high values of the risk score.
The actual number of groups formed in this way will depend on the size
of the database. For larger datasets, five or even seven groups might be
constructed. In particular, with five groups, there would be 20% of the
individuals in each group; those with the lowest and highest values of
the risk score would be at low and high risk, respectively, while the
middle 20% would be at medium risk.

The next step is to compare the observed and fitted survivor functions
in each of the groups. Suppose that ${\hat{S}}_{ij}(t)$ is the
model-based estimate of the survivor function for the *i*th individual
in the *j*th group. The average fitted survivor function is then
obtained for each group, or just the groups with the smallest, middle
and highest risk scores, from

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\overline{S}}_{j}(t) = \frac{1}{n_{j}}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n_{j}}{\hat{S}}_{ij}}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where *n*~*j*~ is the number of observations in the *j*th group. The
value of ${\overline{S}}_{j}(t)$ would be obtained for a range of *t*
values, so that a plot of the values of ${\overline{S}}_{j}(t)$ against
*t*, for each value of *j*, yields a smooth curve. The corresponding
observed survivor function for a particular group is the Kaplan-Meier
estimate of the survivor function for the individuals in that group.
Superimposing these two sets of estimates gives a visual representation
of the agreement between the observed and fitted survivor functions.
This procedure is analogous to that described in [Section
3.11.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_11_1) for the Cox regression model.

Using this approach, it is often easier to detect departures from the
fitted model, than from plots based on residuals. However, the procedure
can be criticised for using the same fitted model to define the groups,
and to obtain the estimated survivor function for each group. If the
database is sufficiently large, the survivor function could be estimated
from half of the data, and the fit of the model evaluated on the
remaining half. Also, since the []{#chapter7.xhtml_p244 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 244. " role="doc-pagebreak"}method is based on the
values of the risk score, no account is taken of differences between
individuals who have different sets of values of the explanatory
variables, but just happen to have the same value of the risk score.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam7_2" role="doc-tip">

[Example 7.2]{.boxnum} [Chemotherapy in ovarian cancer patients]{.boxht}

In this example, we examine the fit of a Weibull proportional hazards
model to the data on the survival times of 26 women, following treatment
for ovarian cancer. A Weibull model that contains the variables *Age*
and *Treat* is fitted, as in [Example 5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_11),
so that the fitted survivor function for the *i*th individual is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- e^{{\hat{\eta}}_{i}}\hat{\lambda}t^{\hat{\gamma}}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(7.8)]{.equationnum}

where ${\hat{\eta}}_{i} = 0.144\, Age_{i} - 1.023\, Treat_{i}$ is the
risk score, $i = 1,2,\ldots,26$. This is equivalent to the accelerated
failure time representation of the model, used in [Example
7.1](#chapter7.xhtml_exam7_1){#chapter7.xhtml_b7exam7_1}. The values of
${\hat{\eta}}_{i}$ are then arranged in ascending order and divided into
three groups, as shown in [Table
7.2](#chapter7.xhtml_tbl7_2){#chapter7.xhtml_b7tbl7_2}.

+----------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| Group    | Risk Score                                                |
+==========+===========+===========+===========+===========+===========+
| 1 (low   | 4.29 (14) | 4.45 (2)  | 4.59 (20) | 5.15 (22) | 5.17 (5)  |
| risk)    |           |           |           |           |           |
+----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+
|          | 5.17 (19) | 5.31 (17) | 5.59 (4)  | 5.59 (12) |           |
+----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+
| 2        | 5.87 (16) | 6.02 (13) | 6.16 (8)  | 6.18 (18) | 6.31 (26) |
| (medium  |           |           |           |           |           |
| risk)    |           |           |           |           |           |
+----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+
|          | 6.45 (6)  | 6.45 (9)  | 6.45 (11) | 7.03 (25) |           |
+----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+
| 3 (high  | 7.04 (15) | 7.04 (24) | 7.17 (7)  | 8.19 (23) |           |
| risk)    |           |           |           |           |           |
+----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+
|          | 8.48 (1)  | 9.34 (3)  | 9.63 (10) | 9.63 (21) |           |
+----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+

: [[[Table]{.label}
[7.2]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter7.xhtml_b7tbl7_2){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Values of the risk score, with the patient number
in parentheses, for three groups of ovarian cancer
patients]{.table-title} {#tbl7_2}

The next step is to obtain the average survivor function for each group
by averaging the values of the estimated survivor function, in Equation
(7.8), for the patients in the three groups. This is done for
$t = 0,1,\ldots,1230$, and the three average survivor functions are
shown in [Figure 7.5](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_5){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_5}.
The Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function for the individuals
in each of the three groups shown in [Table 7.2](#chapter7.xhtml_tbl7_2)
is then calculated, and this is also shown in [Figure
7.5](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_5).

<figure id="fig7_5" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_5.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_5" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.5</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the observed and fitted survivor
functions for patients of low (<span class="math inline">⋯⋯</span>),
medium (- - -) and high (—) risk. The observed survivor function is the
step function.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

From this plot, we see that the model is a good fit to the patients in
the high-risk group. For those in the middle group, the agreement
between the observed and fitted survivor functions is not that good, as
the fitted model leads to estimates of the survivor function that are a
little too high. In fact, the patients in this group have the largest
values of the martingale residuals, which also indicates that the death
times of these individuals are not adequately summarised by the fitted
model. There is only one death among the individuals in the low-risk
group, and so little can be said about the fit of the model to this set
of patients.

</aside>
:::

::::: section
## 7.4 Identification of influential observations {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_4 .head1}

As when fitting the Cox regression model, it will be important to
identify observations that exert an undue influence on particular
parameter estimates, or on the complete set of parameter estimates.
These two aspects of influence are considered in turn in this section.

A number of influence diagnostics for the Weibull proportional hazards
model can be derived from the accelerated failure time representation of
the model, but they can also be adapted for use with other models in
this family. These diagnostics are computed from the estimates of all
$p + 2$ parameters in the model, and their variance-covariance matrix.
For convenience, the vector of $p + 2$ parameters will be denoted by
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, so that []{#chapter7.xhtml_p245 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 245. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}' = (\mu,\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2},\ldots,\alpha_{p},\sigma)$.
The vector ${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}}'$ will be used to denote
the corresponding vector of estimates of the parameters.

::: section
### 7.4.1 Influence of observations on a parameter estimate {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_4_1 .head2}

An approximation to the change in the estimated value of *θ*~*j*~, the
*j*th component of the vector $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, on omitting
the *i*th observation, $\Delta_{i}{\hat{\theta}}_{j}$, is the *j*th
component of the $(p + 2) \times 1$ vector

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})\,\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}.}
\end{array}$$[(7.9)]{.equationnum}

In Expression (7.9), $\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$ is
the estimated variance-covariance matrix of the parameters in
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, and $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$ is the
$(p + 2) \times 1$ vector of values of the first partial derivatives of
the log-likelihood for the *i*th observation, with respect to the
$p + 2$ parameters in $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, evaluated at
$\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}$. The vector $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$
is therefore the vector of values of the score residuals for the *i*th
observation, defined in [Section 7.1.5](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_5).

The quantities $\Delta_{i}{\hat{\alpha}}_{j}$ are components 2 to
$p - 1$ of the vector in Expression (7.9), which we will continue to
refer to as delta-betas rather than as delta-alphas. These values may be
standardised through division by the standard error of
${\hat{\alpha}}_{j}$, leading to standardised delta-betas. Index plots
or plots of the standardised or unstandardised values of
$\Delta_{i}{\hat{\alpha}}_{j}$ provide informative summaries of this
aspect of influence.
:::

::: section
### 7.4.2 Influence of observations on the set of parameter estimates {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_4_2 .head2}

The influence of each of *n* observations on the set of parameter
estimates in a parametric survival model can be measured using the
likelihood displacement. This was described in [Section
4.3](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_3) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml) in
connection with the Cox regression model and is simply the difference
between the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ values for a model fitted to the full data
set and that fitted to the data after excluding the *i*th row, for
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$. This statistic is most easily obtained as the
diagonal elements of the matrix
$\mathbf{\mathbf{U}}\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})\,\mathbf{\mathbf{U}}'$,
where $\mathbf{\mathbf{U}}$ is the $n \times (p + 2)$ matrix with
elements $\partial\log L_{i}/\partial\theta_{j}$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,p + 2$, *θ*~*j*~ is the *j*th parameter estimate in the
parameter vector $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$,
$\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$ is the
variance-covariance matrix of the parameter estimates,
[]{#chapter7.xhtml_p246 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 246. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and *L*~*i*~ is the likelihood contribution of the
*i*th observation. Relatively large values of this statistic suggest
that the corresponding observation influences the parameter estimates in
the fitted model.

Two other summary measures of the influence of the *i*th observation on
the set of parameters that make up the vector $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$
have been proposed by [Hall, Rogers and Pregibon
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib162){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib162 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. These are the statistics *F*~*i*~ and *C*~*i*~.
The quantity *F*~*i*~ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{F_{i} = \frac{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}'\mathbf{\mathbf{R}}^{- 1}\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}}{(p + 2)\left\{ 1 - \mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}'\mathbf{\mathbf{R}}^{- 1}\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i} \right\}},}
\end{array}$$[(7.10)]{.equationnum}

where the $(p + 2) \times (p + 2)$ matrix $\mathbf{\mathbf{R}}$ is the
cross-product matrix of score residuals, that is,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{R}} = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}}\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}'$.
Equivalently,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{R}} = \mathbf{\mathbf{U}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{U}}$, where
$\mathbf{\mathbf{U}}$ is the $n \times (p + 2)$ matrix whose *i*th row
is the transpose of the vector of score residuals,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}'$. An alternative measure of the influence of
the *i*th observation on the set of parameter estimates is the statistic

$$\begin{array}{r}
{C_{i} = \frac{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}'\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}}{\left\{ 1 - \mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}'\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i} \right\}^{2}}.}
\end{array}$$[(7.11)]{.equationnum}

The statistics *F*~*i*~ and *C*~*i*~ will typically have values that are
quite different from each other. However, in each case a relatively
large value of the statistic will indicate that the corresponding
observation is influential. Exactly how such observations influence the
estimates would need to be investigated by omitting that observation
from the data set and refitting the model.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam7_3" role="doc-tip">

[Example 7.3]{.boxnum} [Chemotherapy in ovarian cancer patients]{.boxht}

We now go on to investigate whether there are any influential
observations in the data on the survival times following chemotherapy
treatment for ovarian cancer. The unstandardised delta-betas for *Age*
and *Treat*, plotted against the rank order of the survival times, are
shown in [Figures 7.6](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_6){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_6}
and [7.7](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_7){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_7}.

<figure id="fig7_6" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_6.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_6" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.6</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the delta-betas for <em>Age</em>
against rank order of survival time.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig7_7" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_7.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_7" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.7</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the delta-betas for <em>Treat</em>
against rank order of survival time.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In [Figure 7.6](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_6), two observations have
relatively large values of the delta-beta for *Age*. These occur for
patients 4 and 5 in the original data set. Both women have short
survival times, and in addition one is relatively old at 74 years and
the other relatively young at 43 years. The delta-betas for *Treat*
displayed in [Figure 7.7](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_7) show no unusual
features.

[]{#chapter7.xhtml_p247 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 247. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}We next investigate the influence of each
observation on the set of parameter estimates. A plot of the likelihood
displacement statistic against the rank order of the survival times is
shown in [Figure 7.8](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_8){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_8}.

<figure id="fig7_8" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_8.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_8" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.8</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the likelihood displacement statistic
against rank order of survival time.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The values of *F*~*i*~ and *C*~*i*~, defined in Equations (7.10) and
(7.11), are plotted against the rank order of the survival times in
[Figures 7.9](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_9){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_9} and
[7.10](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_10){#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_10}. [Figure
7.9](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_9) clearly shows that the observation
corresponding to patient 5 is influential, and that the influence of
patients 1, 4, 14 and 26 should be investigated in greater detail.
[Figure 7.10](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_10) strongly suggests that the data
from patients 5 and 26 is influential.

<figure id="fig7_9" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_9.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_9" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.9</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the <em>F</em>-statistic against rank
order of survival time.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig7_10" class="image">
<img src="images/fig7_10.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter7.xhtml_b7fig7_10" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">7.10</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Plot of the <em>C</em>-statistic against rank
order of survival time.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The linear component of the fitted hazard function in the model fitted
to all 26 patients is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{0.144\, Age_{i} - 1.023\, Treat_{i},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter7.xhtml_p248 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 248. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}while that on omitting each of observations 1, 4,
5, 14 and 26 in turn is as follows:

  ----------------------------- ---------------------------------------
  Omitting patient number 1:    $0.142\, Age_{i} - 1.016\, Treat_{i}$
  Omitting patient number 4:    $0.175\, Age_{i} - 1.190\, Treat_{i}$
  Omitting patient number 5:    $0.177\, Age_{i} - 0.710\, Treat_{i}$
  Omitting patient number 14:   $0.149\, Age_{i} - 1.318\, Treat_{i}$
  Omitting patient number 26:   $0.159\, Age_{i} - 0.697\, Treat_{i}$
  ----------------------------- ---------------------------------------

[]{#chapter7.xhtml_p249 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 249. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}These results show that the effect of omitting the
data from patient 1 on the parameter estimates is small. When the data
from patient 4 are omitted, the estimated coefficient of *Age* is most
affected, whereas when the data from patient 14 are omitted, the
coefficient of *Treat* is changed the most. On leaving out the data from
patients 5 and 26, both estimates are considerably affected.

The hazard ratio for a patient on the combined treatment ($Treat = 2$),
relative to one on the single treatment ($Treat = 1$), is estimated by
$e^{- 1.023} = 0.36$, when the model is fitted to all 26 patients. When
the observations from patients 1, 4, 5, 14 and 26 are omitted in turn,
the estimated age-adjusted hazard ratios are 0.36, 0.30, 0.49, 0.27 and
0.50, respectively. The data from patients 5 and 26 clearly have the
greatest effect on the estimated hazard ratio; in each case the estimate
is increased, and the magnitude of the treatment effect is diminished.
Omission of the data from patients 4 or 14 decreases the estimated
hazard ratio, thereby increasing the estimated treatment difference.

</aside>
:::
:::::

::: section
## 7.5 Testing proportional hazards in the Weibull model {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_5 .head1}

The Weibull model is most commonly used as a parametric proportional
hazards model, and so it will be important to test that the proportional
hazards assumption is tenable. In [Section
4.4.3](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_3) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml), it was
shown how a time-dependent variable can be used in testing
proportionality of hazards in the Cox regression model. Parametric
models containing time-dependent variables are more complicated, and
because software for fitting such models is not widely available,
further details on this approach will not be given here.

In the Weibull model, the assumption of proportional hazards across a
number of groups, *g*, say, corresponds to the assumption that the shape
parameter *γ* in the baseline hazard function is the same in each group.
One way of testing this assumption is to fit a separate Weibull model to
each of the *g* groups, where the linear component of the model is the
same in each case. The models fitted to the data from each group will
then have different shape parameters as well as different scale
parameters. The values of the statistic $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for each of
these *g* separate models are then summed to give a value of
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ for a model that has different shape parameter for each
group. Denote this by $- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{1}$. We then combine the *g*
sets of data and fit a Weibull proportional hazards model that includes
the factor associated with the group effects and interactions between
this factor and other terms in the model. This model then corresponds to
there being a common shape parameter for each group. The inclusion of
group effects in the model leads to there being different scale
parameters for each group. The value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for this model,
$- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{0}$, say, is then compared with
$- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{1}$. The difference between the values of these two
statistics is the change in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ due to constraining the
Weibull shape parameters to be equal, and can be compared with a
chi-squared distribution on $g - 1$ degrees of freedom. If the
difference is not significant, the assumption of proportional hazards is
justified.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam7_4" role="doc-tip">

[Example 7.4]{.boxnum} [Chemotherapy in ovarian cancer patients]{.boxht}

Data from the study of survival following treatment for ovarian cancer,
given in [Example 5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_11) of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), are now used to illustrate the procedure for
testing the assumption that the Weibull shape parameter is the same for
the patients in each of the two treatment groups. The first step is to
