fit a Weibull proportional hazards model that contains *Age* alone to
the data from the women in each treatment group. When such a model is
fitted to the data from those on the single chemotherapy treatment, the
value of the statistic $- 2\log\hat{L}$ is 22.851, while that for the
women on the combined treatment is 16.757. The sum of the two values of
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ is 39.608, which is the value of the statistic for a
Weibull model with different shape parameters for the two treatment
groups, and different coefficients of *Age* for each group. For the
model with different age effects for each treatment group, a
[]{#chapter7.xhtml_p250 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 250. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}treatment effect, and common shape parameter, the
value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ is 39.708. The change in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ on
constraining the shape parameters to be equal is therefore 0.10, which
is not significant when compared with a chi-squared distribution on one
degree of freedom. The two shape parameters may therefore be taken to be
equal.

</aside>

Some alternatives to the proportional hazards model are described in
[Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), and further comments on how to deal with
situations in which the hazards are not proportional are given in
[Chapter 11](#chapter11.xhtml).
:::

::: section
## 7.6 Further reading {#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_6 .head1}

There have been relatively few publications on model checking in
parametric survival models, compared to the literature on model checking
in the Cox regression model. Residuals and influence measures for the
Weibull proportional hazards model are described by [Hall, Rogers and
Pregibon (1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib162){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Hollander and Proschan
(1979)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib182){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib182 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} show how to assess whether a sample of censored
observations is drawn from a particular probability distribution.
[Weissfeld and Schneider
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib409){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib409 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describe and illustrate a number of residuals that
can be used in conjunction with parametric models for survival data.
[Cohen and Barnett
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib69){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib69 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describe how the interpretation of cumulative
hazard plots of residuals can be helped by the use of simulated
envelopes for the plots. Influence diagnostics for use in parametric
survival modelling are given by [Weissfeld and Schneider
(1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib407){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib407 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Escobar and Meeker
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib115){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib115 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. These papers involve arguments based on local
influence, a topic that is explored in general terms by [Cook
(1986)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib72){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib72 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and reviewed in [Rancel and Sierra
(2001)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib312){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib312 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. A method for testing the assumptions of
proportional hazards and accelerated failure times against a general
model for the hazard function is presented by [Ciampi and Etezadi-Amoli
(1985)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib65){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib65 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. An interesting application of parametric
modelling, based on data on times to reoffending by prisoners released
on parole, which incorporates elements of model checking, is given by
[Copas and Heydari
(1997)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib76){#chapter7.xhtml_b7refbib76 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

[]{#chapter8.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter8.xhtml_p251 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 251. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 8]{.chapnum}[Time-dependent variables]{.chaptitle} {#chapter8.xhtml_chapter8 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-8](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-8){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

When explanatory variables are incorporated in a model for survival
data, the values taken by such variables are those recorded at the time
origin of the study. For example, consider the study to compare two
treatments for prostatic cancer first described in [Example
1.4](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_4){#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam1_4} of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml). Here, the age of a patient, serum haemoglobin
level, size of the tumour, value of the Gleason index, and of course the
treatment group, were all recorded at the time when a patient was
entered into the study. The impact of these variables on the hazard of
death is then evaluated.

In many studies that generate survival data, individuals are monitored
for the duration of the study. During this period, the values of certain
explanatory variables may be recorded on a regular basis. Thus, in the
example on prostatic cancer, the size of the tumour and other variables,
may be recorded at frequent intervals. If account can be taken of the
values of explanatory variables as they evolve, a more satisfactory
model for the hazard of death at any given time would be obtained. For
example, in connection with the prostatic cancer study, more recent
values of the size of the tumour may provide a better indication of
future life expectancy than the value at the time origin.

Variables whose values change over time are known as *time-dependent
variables*, and in this chapter we see how such variables can be
incorporated in models used in the analysis of survival data.

:::: section
## 8.1 Types of time-dependent variables {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_1 .head1}

It is useful to consider two types of variable that change over time,
which may be referred to as *internal variables* and *external
variables*.

Internal variables relate to a particular individual in a study, and can
only be measured while a patient is alive. Such data arises when
repeated measurements of certain characteristics are made on a patient
over time, and examples include measures of lung function such as vital
capacity and peak flow rate, white blood cell count, systolic blood
pressure and serum cholesterol level. Variables that describe changes in
the status of a patient are also of this type. For example, following a
bone marrow transplant, a patient may be susceptible to the development
of graft versus host disease. A binary explanatory variable that denotes
whether or not the patient is suffering from this life-threatening side
effect at any given time, is a further example of an internal variable.
In each case, such variables reflect the condition of the patient and
their values may well be associated with the survival time of the
patient.

On the other hand, external variables are time-dependent variables that
do not necessarily require the survival of a patient for their
existence. A particular type of external variable is one that changes in
such a way that its value will be known in advance at any future time.
Examples include the dose of a drug that is to be varied in a
predetermined manner during the course of a study, or planned changes to
the type of immunosuppressant to be used following organ
transplantation.

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p252 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 252. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Another type of external variable is one that
exists totally independently of any particular individual, such as the
level of atmospheric sulphur dioxide, or air temperature. Changes in the
values of such quantities may well have an effect on the lifetime of
individuals, as in studies concerning the management of patients with
certain types of respiratory disease.

::: section
### 8.1.1 Time-dependent coefficients {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_1_1 .head2}

Time-dependent variables also arise in situations where the coefficient
of a time-constant explanatory variable is a function of time. In
[Section 3.9](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_9) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml),
it was explained that the coefficient of an explanatory variable in the
Cox proportional hazards model is a log-hazard ratio, and under this
model the hazard ratio is constant over time. If this hazard ratio were
in fact a function of time, then the coefficient of the explanatory
variable that varies with time is referred to as a *time-varying
coefficient*. In this case, the log-hazard ratio is not constant and so
we no longer have a proportional hazards model.

More formally, suppose that the coefficient of an explanatory variable,
*X*, is a linear function of time, *t*, so that we may write the term as
$\beta tX$. This means that the log-hazard ratio corresponding to a unit
increase in the value of *X* is $\beta t$, a linear function of time. A
term of this form was introduced into the model in order to test the
assumption of proportional hazards in [Section
4.4.3](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_3) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml). This
term can also be written as $\beta X(t)$, where $X(t) = Xt$ is a
time-dependent variable. In general, suppose that a model includes the
explanatory variable, *X*, with a time-varying coefficient of the form
$\beta(t)$. The corresponding term in the model would be $\beta(t)X$,
which can be expressed as $\beta X(t)$. In other words, a term that
involves a time-varying coefficient can be expressed as a time-dependent
variable with a constant coefficient. However, if $\beta(t)$ is a
non-linear function of one or more unknown parameters, for example
$\beta_{0}\exp(\beta_{1}t)$, the term is not so easily fitted in a
model.

Such a variable cannot be included directly in a Cox regression model
that is being fitted by maximising the partial likelihood function. To
see why, consider a model that contains the variable $X_{1} = X_{0}t$,
where *X*~0~ is a variable in the data set, and *X*~1~ is formed as the
product of this variable with the survival time. If *X*~1~ is included
as a variable in the model, in the same way as any time-fixed
explanatory variable, the coefficient of *X*~1~ is likely to be
significantly less than zero. This might be interpreted as meaning that
there is a lower hazard of death at any time for individuals with higher
values of *X*~1~, or equivalently, individuals with larger *X*~1~
survive longer. But of course, individuals who survive longer have
larger values of the survival time, and hence of *X*~1~. So it is no
surprise that *X*~1~ is a significant predictor of survival. Instead,
*X*~1~ is incorporated as a time-dependent variable in the model.

All these different types of time-dependent variables can be introduced
into the Cox regression model, in the manner described in the following
section.
:::
::::

:::: section
## 8.2 Modelling with time-dependent variables {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_2 .head1}

According to the Cox proportional hazards model described in [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml), the hazard of death at time *t* for the *i*th of
*n* individuals in a study can be written in the form

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{p}\beta_{j}}x_{ji}} \right\} h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where *β*~*j*~ is the coefficient of $x_{ji}$, the baseline value of the
*j*th explanatory variable, *X*~*j*~, $j = 1,2,\ldots$, *p*, for the
*i*th individual, and $h_{0}(t)$ is the baseline hazard function.
Generalising this model to the situation in which some or all of the
explanatory variables are time-dependent, we write $x_{ji}(t)$ for the
value of the *j*th explanatory variable at time *t*, in the *i*th
[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p253 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 253. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}individual. The Cox regression model then becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{p}\beta_{j}}x_{ji}(t)} \right\} h_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$[(8.1)]{.equationnum}

In this model, the baseline hazard function, $h_{0}(t)$, is interpreted
as the hazard function for an individual for whom all the variables are
zero at the time origin, and remain at this same value through time.

Since the values of the variables $x_{ji}(t)$ in the model given in
Equation (8.1) depend on the time *t*, the relative hazard
$h_{i}(t)/h_{0}(t)$ is also time-dependent. This means that the hazard
of death at time *t* is no longer proportional to the baseline hazard,
and the model is no longer a proportional hazards model.

To provide an interpretation of the *β*-parameters in this model,
consider the ratio of the hazard functions at time *t* for two
individuals, the *r*th and *s*th, say. This is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{h_{r}(t)}{h_{s}(t)} = \exp\left\lbrack {\beta_{1}\left\{ x_{1r}(t) - x_{1s}(t) \right\} + \cdots + \beta_{p}\left\{ x_{pr}(t) - x_{ps}(t) \right\}} \right\rbrack.}
\end{array}$$

The coefficient *β*~*j*~, $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, can therefore be
interpreted as the log-hazard ratio for two individuals whose value of
the *j*th explanatory variable at a given time *t* differs by one unit,
with the two individuals having the same values of all the other $p - 1$
variables at that time.

::: section
### 8.2.1 Fitting models with time-dependent variables {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_2_1 .head2}

When the Cox regression model is extended to incorporate time-dependent
variables, the partial log-likelihood function, from Equation (3.6) in
[Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), can be generalised to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{p}\beta_{j}}x_{ji}(t_{i}) - \log{\sum\limits_{l \in R(t_{i})}\exp}\left( {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{p}\beta_{j}}x_{jl}(t_{i})} \right)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(8.2)]{.equationnum}

in which $R(t_{i})$ is the risk set at time *t*~*i*~, the death time of
the *i*th individual in the study, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and *δ*~*i*~ is
an event indicator that is zero if the survival time of the *i*th
individual is censored and unity otherwise. This expression can then be
maximised to give estimates of the *β*-parameters.

In order to use the model in Equation (8.1) in this maximisation
process, the variables in the model must have known values at each death
time for all individuals in the risk set at time *t*~*i*~. This is no
problem for external variables whose values are preordained, but it may
be a problem for external variables that exist independently of the
individuals in a study, and certainly for internal variables.

To illustrate the problem, consider a trial of two maintenance therapies
for patients who have suffered a myocardial infarct. The serum
cholesterol level of such patients may well be measured at the time when
a patient is admitted to the study, and at regular intervals of time
thereafter. This variable is then a time-dependent variable and will be
denoted $X(t)$. It is then plausible that the hazard of death for any
particular patient, the *i*th, say, at time *t*, $h_{i}(t)$, is more
likely to be influenced by the value of the explanatory variable $X(t)$
at time *t*, than its value at the time origin, where $t = 0$.

Now suppose that the *i*th individual dies at time *t*~*i*~ and that
there are two other individuals, labelled *r* and *s*, in the risk set
at time *t*~*i*~. We further suppose that individual *r* dies at time
*t*~*r*~, where $t_{r} > t_{i}$, and that the survival time of
individual *s, t*~*s*~, is censored at some time after *t*~*r*~. The
situation is illustrated graphically in [Figure
8.1](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_1){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_1}. In this figure,
the vertical dotted lines refer to points in patient time when the value
of $X(t)$ is measured.

<figure id="fig8_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Survival times of three patients in patient
time.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p254 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 254. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}If individuals *r* and *s* are the only two in the
risk set at time *t*~*i*~, and *X* is the only explanatory variable that
is measured, the contribution of the *i*th individual to the partial
log-likelihood function in Expression (8.2) will be

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\beta x_{i}(t_{i}) - \log{\sum\limits_{l}\exp}\left\{ \beta x_{l}(t_{i}) \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where $x_{i}(t_{i})$ is the value of $X(t)$ for the *i*th individual at
their death time, *t*~*i*~, and *l* in the summation takes the values
*i, r*, and *s*. This expression is therefore equal to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\beta x_{i}(t_{i}) - \log\left\{ {e^{\beta x_{i}(t_{i})} + e^{\beta x_{r}(t_{i})} + e^{\beta x_{s}(t_{i})}} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

This shows that the value of the time-dependent variable $X(t)$ is
needed at the death time of the *i*th individual, and at time *t*~*i*~
for individuals *r* and *s*. In addition, the value of the variable
$X(t)$ will be needed for individuals *r* and *s* at *t*~*r*~, the death
time of individual *r*.

For terms in a model that are explicit functions of time, such as
interactions between time and a variable or factor measured at baseline,
there is no difficulty in obtaining the values of the time-dependent
variables at any time for any individual. Indeed, it is usually
straightforward to incorporate such variables in the Cox model when
using statistical software that has facilities for dealing with
time-dependent variables. For other variables, such as serum cholesterol
level, the values of the time-dependent variable at times other than
that at which it was measured has to be approximated.

In a Cox regression model that contains time-dependent variables, the
hazard of death at some time *t* can only depend on values of the
time-dependent variable up to time *t*. The model cannot see into the
future! This means that the last recorded value of an explanatory
variable is used whenever the value of that variable is needed at a
particular time.

When the value of a variable has been recorded for an individual at a
number of time points, it might be tempting to use the value closest to
a given time, or to use some form of interpolation between the times.
[Figure 8.2](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_2){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_2}
illustrates these possibilities.

<figure id="fig8_2" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_2.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_2" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.2</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Values of a time-dependent variable at
intermediate times.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In this figure, the continuous curve depicts the actual value of a
time-dependent variable at any time, and the dotted vertical lines
signify times when the variable is actually measured. If the value of
the variable is required at time *t* in this figure, we should use the
value []{#chapter8.xhtml_p255 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 255. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}at P, the last recorded value of the variable,
rather than the value at R, the value closest to *t*, or the value at Q,
the linearly interpolated value between P and R. Using the value of the
variable at either R or Q, rather than at P, may lead to incorrect
models because at time *t*, the value of the variable at R and Q will
not be known.

Some categorical variables may be such that individuals can only
progress through the levels of the variable in a particular direction.
For example, the performance status of an individual may only be
expected to deteriorate, so that the value of this categorical variable
might only change from 'good' to 'fair' and from 'fair' to 'poor'. As
another example, following a biopsy, a variable associated with the
occurrence of a tumour will take one of two values, corresponding to
absence and presence. It might then be very unlikely for the status to
change from 'present' to 'absent' in consecutive biopsies.

Anomalous changes in the values of time-dependent variables can be
detected by plotting the values of the variable against time for each
patient. This may then lead to a certain amount of data editing. For
example, consider the plot in [Figure
8.3](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_3){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_3}, which shows the
biopsy result, absent or present, for a particular patient at a number
of time points. In this diagram, at least one of the observations made
at times *t*~*A*~ and *t*~*B*~ must be incorrect. The observation at
*t*~*A*~ might then be changed to 'absent' or that at *t*~*B*~ to
'present'.

<figure id="fig8_3" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_3.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_3" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.3</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Values of a time-dependent categorical
variable.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

A variable that changes over time in an entirely predictable manner is
the age of an individual, and increasing age might be expected to be
relevant in a long-term study. However, if age is included as a linear
term in a model, the form of the partial likelihood means that changes
in age do not feature in this likelihood function. To see this, suppose
that the variable *X* relates to an individual\'s age in years at the
start of a study, so that their age at time *t* is a time-dependent
variable $X(t) = X + t$. If the age of the *i*th individual at the
outset of the study is *x*~*i*~, the dependence of the hazard function
at time *t* on their current age, can be modelled using

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ \beta(x_{i} + t) \right\} h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p256 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 256. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}from Equation (8.1), where $h_{0}(t)$ is a baseline
hazard function. The corresponding partial log-likelihood function, from
Equation (8.2), is then

$$\begin{aligned}
 & {{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\left\{ \beta(x_{i} + t_{i}) - \log{\sum\limits_{l \in R(t_{i})}\exp}\left\lbrack {\beta(x_{l} + t_{i})} \right\rbrack \right\},} \\
 & {= {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\left\{ \beta x_{i} + \beta t_{i} - \log{\sum\limits_{l \in R(t_{i})}\exp}(\beta x_{l} + \beta t_{i}) \right\},} \\
 & {= {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\left\{ \beta x_{i} - \log{\sum\limits_{l \in R(t_{i})}\exp}(\beta x_{l}) \right\}.}
\end{aligned}$$

This is just the partial log-likelihood for a Cox regression model that
only includes the baseline value of age. There is therefore nothing to
be gained by including the actual age at time *t* as a linear term in
the model.
:::
::::

::: section
## 8.3 Coding of time-dependent variables {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_3 .head1}

Models that include time-dependent variables can be fitted by maximising
the partial log-likelihood function in Equation (8.2). This can be
accomplished using software that enables the value of any time-dependent
variable to be determined at different event times in the data set, as
explained in [Section 8.2.1](#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_2_1).

However, a much more flexible approach is to express the data for an
individual in the form of a series of time-intervals over which the
values of all explanatory variables are constant. The event indicator is
then set to zero for all intervals except the final interval. The upper
limit of the final interval is the event time, or censoring time, of the
individual, and the value of the event indicator is set to unity if
there is an event at the end of that interval, and zero otherwise.
Variables associated with the lower and upper limits of each time
interval, *Start* and *Stop*, say, are then used to specify survival
experience, and the variable *Status* denotes whether or not an event
has occurred at each stop time. To avoid ambiguity, an interval that
includes a time *t* is such that $Start < t\leqslant Stop$, so that this
interval would include the value $t = Stop$ but not $t = Start$. The
survival data are then []{#chapter8.xhtml_p257 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 257. " role="doc-pagebreak"}represented by a trio of
values (*Start, Stop, Status*) for each interval, and this format of
data is called the *counting process* or *(start, stop, status) format*.
Many software packages for survival analysis can process data expressed
in this general form.

If a model that only contains time-constant explanatory variables is
fitted to data prepared in this way, the resulting partial likelihood
function is the same as that for the original data. This is explained
more fully in [Section 13.1](#chapter13.xhtml_sec13_1) of [Chapter
13](#chapter13.xhtml), where further details on the counting process
format, and its application to other areas of survival analysis, are
given.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_1" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 8.1]{.boxnum}](#chapter13.xhtml_b8exam8_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Illustration of the counting process
format]{.boxht}

Patients suffering from liver disease tend to have serum albumin levels
that decrease over time. The albumin level at any given time may then be
a better indicator of the hazard of death at that time than the value
recorded at the start of a study. Suppose that in a study of survival
from diagnosis of liver disease, the survival time of a particular
patient is 379 days. The baseline albumin level of this patient is 3.8
g/dL and this is subsequently measured at 88, 172, 245 and 357 days. The
recorded values on these days were 3.4, 2.9, 3.0 and 2.3 g/dL,
respectively, so that the value recorded at 88 days applies from day 89
up to and including day 172 when a subsequent measurement was made. The
data for this patient in counting process format are shown in [Table
8.1](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_1){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_1}.

  Observation   Time   Time Interval       Albumin   *Start*   *Stop*   *Status*
  ------------- ------ ------------------- --------- --------- -------- ----------
  1              0       $(0,88\rbrack$    3.8        0         88      0
  2              88     $(88,172\rbrack$   3.4        88       172      0
  3             172    $(172,245\rbrack$   2.9       172       245      0
  4             245    $(245,357\rbrack$   3.0       245       357      0
  5             357    $(357,379\rbrack$   2.3       357       379      1

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.1]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_1){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Survival time and status for a patient with
  values of a time-dependent variable in counting process
  format]{.table-title} {#tbl8_1}

The data for this patient now has 5 rows. If the patient was still alive
at 379 days, and this was the last day on which they were in the study,
the value of the variable *Status* would be replaced by zero. This
process is then repeated for all patients in the study and a Cox
regression model with the time-dependent variable serum albumin is then
fitted to this extended data set.

</aside>
:::

::: section
## 8.4 Estimation of the cumulative hazard and survivor functions {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_4 .head1}

After a Cox regression model that includes time-dependent variables has
been fitted, the baseline cumulative hazard function, $H_{0}(t)$ and the
corresponding baseline survivor function, $S_{0}(t)$, can be estimated.
This involves an adaptation of the results given in [Section
3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_10){#chapter8.xhtml_b3exam3_10} of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml) to cope with the additional complication of
time-dependent variables, in which the values of the explanatory
variables need to be updated with their time-specific values. In
particular, the Nelson-Aalen estimate of the baseline cumulative hazard
function, given in Equation (3.29), becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t) = - \log{\widetilde{S}}_{0}(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{k}\frac{d_{j}}{{\sum\limits_{l \in R(t_{(j)})}\exp}\left\{ {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{l}(t) \right\}}},}
\end{array}$$[(8.3)]{.equationnum}

for $t_{(k)}\leqslant t < t_{(k + 1)}$, $k = 1,2,\ldots,r - 1$, where
$\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{l}(t)$ is the vector of values of the explanatory
variables for the *l*th individual at time *t*, and *d*~*j*~ is the
number of events at the *j*th ordered event time, $t_{(j)}$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,r$. Similar modifications can be made to the other
results in [Section 3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_10). With this
modification, computation of the summation over the risk set is
[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p258 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 258. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}much more complicated, since for every event time,
$t_{(j)}$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,r$, the value of each time-dependent
variable, for all individuals in the risk set, is needed at that event
time.

Having obtained an estimate of the cumulative hazard function, the
corresponding baseline hazard function can be estimated using Equation
(3.31), and an estimate of the baseline survivor function is
${\widetilde{S}}_{0}(t) = \exp\left\{ - {\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t) \right\}$.

The survivor function for a particular individual is much more difficult
to estimate. This is because the result that $S_{i}(t)$ can be expressed
as a power of the baseline survivor function, $S_{0}(t)$, given in
Equation (3.26) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), no longer holds.
Instead, the survivor function for the *i*th individual is obtained from
the integrated hazard function, which, from Equation (1.7) in [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml), is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- {\int_{0}^{t}\exp}\left( {{\sum_{j = 1}^{p}\beta_{j}}x_{ji}(u)} \right)h_{0}(u)\,\text{d}u} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(8.4)]{.equationnum}

This survivor function therefore depends not only on the baseline hazard
function $h_{0}(t)$, but also on the values of the time-dependent
variables over the interval from 0 to *t*. The survivor function may
therefore depend on future values of the time-dependent variables in the
model, which will generally be unknown. However, approximate conditional
probabilities of surviving a certain time interval can be found from the
probability that an individual survives over an interval of time, from
*t* to $t + h$, say, conditional on being alive at time *t*. This
probability is $\text{P}(T_{i}\geqslant t + h\mid T_{i}\geqslant t)$,
where *T*~*i*~ is the random variable associated with the survival time
of the *i*th individual. Using the standard result for conditional
probability, given in [Section 3.3.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3_1) of
[Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), this probability becomes
$\text{P}(T_{i}\geqslant t + h)/\text{P}(T_{i}\geqslant t)$, which is
the ratio of the survivor functions at times $t + h$ and *t*, that is,
$S_{i}(t + h)/S_{i}(t)$. We now assume that any time-dependent variable
remains constant through this interval, so that from Equation (8.4), the
approximate conditional probability is

$$\begin{aligned}
{P_{i}(t,t + h)} & {= \frac{\exp\left\{ {- \exp\left( {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{p}\beta_{j}}x_{ji}(t)} \right){\int_{0}^{t + h}h_{0}}(u)\,\text{d}u} \right\}}{\exp\left\{ {- \exp\left( {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{p}\beta_{j}}x_{ji}(t)} \right){\int_{0}^{t}h_{0}}(u)\,\text{d}u} \right\}},} \\
 & {= \exp\left\lbrack {- \left\{ {H_{0}(t + h) - H_{0}(t)} \right\}\exp\left( {{\sum_{j = 1}^{p}\beta_{j}}x_{ji}(t)} \right)} \right\rbrack,}
\end{aligned}$$

where $H_{0}(t)$ is the baseline cumulative hazard function. An estimate
of this approximate conditional probability of surviving through the
interval $(t,t + h)$ is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\widetilde{P}}_{i}(t,\, t + h) = \exp\left\lbrack {- \left\{ {{\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t + h) - {\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t)} \right\}\exp\left( {{\sum_{j = 1}^{p}{\hat{\beta}}_{j}}x_{ji}(t)} \right)} \right\rbrack,}
\end{array}$$[(8.5)]{.equationnum}

where ${\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t)$ is the estimated baseline cumulative
hazard function obtained on fitting the Cox regression model with *p*
possibly time-dependent variables with values $x_{ji}(t)$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, for the *i*th individual, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and
${\hat{\beta}}_{j}$ is the estimated coefficient of the *j*th
time-dependent variable. This result was given by [Altman and De Stavola
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib13){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib13 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

Corresponding estimates of the conditional probability of an event in
the interval $(t,t + h)$ are $1 - {\widetilde{P}}_{i}(t,t + h)$, and
these quantities can be used to obtain an estimate of the expected
number of events in each of a number of successive intervals of width
*h*. Comparing these values with the observed number of events in these
intervals leads to an informal assessment of model adequacy.
:::

::::: section
## 8.5 Model comparison and validation {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_5 .head1}

Models for survival data that include time-dependent variables can be
compared in the same manner as Cox proportional hazards models, using
the procedure described in [Section 3.5](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_5)
[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p259 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 259. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). In particular, the
model-fitting process leads to a maximised partial likelihood function,
from which the value of the statistic $- 2\log\hat{L}$ can be obtained.
Changes in the value of this statistic between alternative nested models
may then be compared to percentage points of the chi-squared
distribution, with degrees of freedom equal to the difference in the
number of *β*-parameters being fitted. For this reason, the
model-building strategies discussed in [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml)
apply equally in situations where there are time-dependent variables.

::: section
### 8.5.1 Comparison of treatments {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_5_1 .head2}

In order to examine the magnitude of a treatment effect after taking
account of variables that change over time, the value of
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ for a model that contains the time-dependent variables
and any other prognostic factors is compared with that for the model
that contains the treatment term, in addition to these other variables.
But in this analysis, if no treatment effect is revealed, one
explanation could be that the time-dependent variable has *masked* the
treatment difference.

To fix ideas, consider the example of a study to compare two cytotoxic
drugs in the treatment of patients with leukaemia. Here, a patient\'s
survival time may well depend on subsequent values of that patient\'s
white blood cell count. If the effect of the treatment is to increase
white blood cell count, no treatment difference will be identified after
including white blood cell count as a time-dependent variable in the
model. On the other hand, the treatment effect may appear in the absence
of this variable. An interpretation of this is that the time-dependent
variable has accounted for the treatment difference, and so provides an
explanation as to how the treatment has been effective.

In any event, much useful information will be gained from a comparison
of the results of an analysis that incorporates time-dependent variables
with an analysis that uses baseline values alone.
:::

::: section
### 8.5.2 Assessing model adequacy {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_5_2 .head2}

After fitting a model that includes time-dependent variables, a number
of the techniques for evaluating the adequacy of the model, described in
[Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml), can be implemented. In particular, an
overall martingale residual can be computed for each subject, from an
adaptation of the result in Equation (4.6). The martingale residual for
the *i*th subject is now

$$\begin{array}{r}
{r_{Mi} = \delta_{i} - \exp\left\{ {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}(t_{i}) \right\}{\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}(t_{i})$ is the vector of values of
explanatory variables for the *i*th individual, which may be
time-dependent, evaluated at *t*~*i*~, the event time of that
individual. Also, $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$ is the vector of
coefficients, *δ*~*i*~ is the event indicator that takes the value unity
if *t*~*i*~ is an event and zero otherwise, and
${\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t_{i})$ is the estimated baseline cumulative hazard
function at *t*~*i*~, obtained from Equation (8.3). The deviance
residuals may also be computed from the martingale residuals, using
Equation (4.7) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml).

The plots described in [Section 4.2.2](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_2_2) of
[Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml) will often be helpful. In particular, an
index plot of the martingale residuals will enable outlying observations
to be identified. However, diagnostic plots for assessing the functional
form of covariates, described in [Section
4.2.3](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_2_3), turn out to be not so useful when a
time-dependent variable is being studied. This is because there will
then be a number of values of the time-dependent covariate for any one
individual, and it is not clear what the martingale residuals for the
null model should be plotted against.

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p260 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 260. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}For detecting influential values, the delta-betas,
introduced in [Section 4.3.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_3_1) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml), provide a helpful means of investigating the effect
of each observation on individual parameter estimates. Changes in the
value of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic, on omitting each observation in
turn, can give valuable information about the effect of each observation
on the set of parameter estimates.
:::
:::::

:::: section
## 8.6 Some applications of time-dependent variables {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_6 .head1}

One application of time-dependent variables is in connection with
evaluating the assumption of proportional hazards. This was discussed in
detail in [Section 4.4.3](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_3) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml). In this application, a variable formed from the
product of an explanatory variable, *X*, and time, *t*, is added to the
linear part of the Cox model, and the null hypothesis that the
coefficient of *Xt* is zero is tested. If this estimated coefficient is
found to be significantly different from zero, there is evidence that
the assumption of proportional hazards is not valid.

In many circumstances, the waiting time from the occurrence of some
catastrophic event until a patient receives treatment may be strongly
associated with the patient\'s survival. For example, in a study of
factors affecting the survival of patients who have had a myocardial
infarct, the time from the infarct to when the patient arrives in
hospital may be crucial. Some patients may die before receiving
treatment, while those who arrive at the hospital soon after their
infarct will tend to have a more favourable prognosis than those for
whom treatment is delayed. It will be important to take account of this
aspect of the data when assessing the effects of other explanatory
variables on the survival times of these patients.

In a similar example, [Crowley and Hu
(1977)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib89){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib89 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} show how a time-dependent variable can be used in
organ transplantation studies. Here, one feature of interest is the
effect of a transplant on the patient\'s survival time. Suppose that in
a study on the effectiveness of a particular type of organ transplant, a
patient is judged to be suitable for a transplant at some time *t*~0~.
They then wait some period of time until a suitable donor organ is
found, and if the patient survives this period, they receive a
transplant at time *t*~1~.

In studies of this type, the survival times of patients who have
received a transplant cannot be compared with those who have not had a
transplant in the usual way. The reason for this is that in order to
receive a transplant, a patient must survive the waiting time to
transplant. Consequently, the group who survive to the time of
transplant is not directly comparable with the group who receive no such
transplant. Similarly, it is not possible to compare the times that the
patients who receive a transplant survive after the transplant with the
survival times of the group not receiving a transplant. Here, the time
origin would be different for the two groups, and so they are not
comparable at the time origin. This means that it is not possible to
identify a time origin from which standard methods for survival analysis
can be used.

The solution to this problem is to introduce a time-dependent variable
$X_{1}(t)$, which takes the value zero if a patient has not received a
transplant at time *t*, and unity otherwise. Adopting a Cox regression
model, the hazard of death for the *i*th individual at time *t* is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ \eta_{i} + \beta_{1}x_{1i}(t) \right\} h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where *η*~*i*~ is a linear combination of the explanatory variables that
are not time-dependent, whose values have been recorded at the time
origin for the *i*th individual, and $x_{1i}(t)$ is the value of *X*~1~
for that individual at time *t*.

Under this model, the hazard function is $\exp(\eta_{i})h_{0}(t)$ for
patients who do not receive a transplant before time *t*, and
$\exp\left\{ \eta_{i} + \beta_{1} \right\} h_{0}(t)$ thereafter. The
effect of a transplant on the patient\'s survival experience is then
reflected in *β*~1~. In particular, for two patients who have the same
values of other explanatory variables in a model, $e^{\beta_{1}}$ is the
hazard of death at time *t* for the patient who receives a transplant
before time *t*, relative to the hazard at []{#chapter8.xhtml_p261
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 261. " role="doc-pagebreak"}that time for
the patient who does not. Values of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ can be compared
after fitting the models with and without the time-dependent variable
*X*~1~. A significant difference in these values means that the
transplant has an effect on survival.

In a refinement to this model, [Cox and Oakes
(1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib81){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib81 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} suggested that the term $\beta_{1}x_{1i}(t)$ be
replaced by
$\beta_{1} + \beta_{2}\exp\left\{ - \beta_{3}(t - t_{1}) \right\}$ for
patients receiving a transplant at time *t*~1~. In this model, the
effect of the transplant is to increase the hazard to some value
$\exp(\eta_{i} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2})h_{0}(t)$ immediately after the
transplant, when $t = t_{1}$, and to then decrease the hazard
exponentially to $\exp(\eta_{i} + \beta_{1})h_{0}(t)$, which is less
than the initial hazard $\exp(\eta_{i})h_{0}(t)$ if $\beta_{1} < 0$. See
[Figure 8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_4){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_4}, which
shows graphically the behaviour of the hazard ratio,
$h_{i}(t)/h_{0}(t)$, for a transplant patient for whom *η*~*i*~ is the
linear component of the model. Although this is an attractive model, it
does have the disadvantage that specialist software is required to fit
it.

<figure id="fig8_4" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_4.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_4" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.4</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">The hazard ratio <span
class="math inline">exp {<em>η</em><sub><em>i</em></sub> + <em>β</em><sub>1</sub> + <em>β</em><sub>2</sub><em>e</em><sup>−<em>β</em><sub>3</sub>(<em>t</em> − <em>t</em><sub>1</sub>)</sup>}</span>,
<span class="math inline"><em>t</em> &gt; <em>t</em><sub>1</sub></span>,
for individual <em>i</em> who receives a transplant at
<em>t</em><sub>1</sub>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In situations where a particular explanatory variable is changing
rapidly, new variables that reflect such changes may be defined. The
dependence of the hazard on the values of such variables can then be
explored. For example, in an oncological study, the percentage increase
in the size of a tumour over a period of time might be a more suitable
prognostic variable than either the size of the tumour at the time
origin, or the time-dependent version of that variable. If this route is
followed, the computational burden of fitting time-dependent variables
can be avoided.

::: section
### 8.6.1 Some examples {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_6_1 .head2}

In this section, three examples of survival analyses that involve
time-dependent variables are given. In the first, data from a study
concerning the use of bone marrow transplantation in the treatment of
leukaemia is used to illustrate how a variable that is associated with
the state of a patient, and whose value changes over the follow-up
period, can be included in a model. In the second example, data from
[Example 5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_11){#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam5_11} of
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml) on the comparison of two chemotherapy
treatments for ovarian cancer are analysed to explore whether age has a
time-varying coefficient. The third example is designed to illustrate
how information on a time-dependent explanatory variate recorded during
the follow-up period can be incorporated in a model for survival times.
Studies in which the values of certain explanatory
[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p262 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 262. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}variables are recorded regularly throughout the
follow-up period of each patient generate large sets of data. For this
reason, artificial data from a small number of individuals will be used
in [Example 8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_4){#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam8_4} to
illustrate the methodology.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_2" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 8.2]{.boxnum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam8_2){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Bone marrow transplantation in the treatment of
leukaemia]{.boxht}

Patients suffering from acute forms of leukaemia often receive a bone
marrow transplant. This provides the recipient with a new set of parent
blood-forming cells, known as stem cells, which in turn produce a supply
of healthy red and white blood cells. [Klein and Moeschberger
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib212){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib212 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describe a multicentre study of factors that
affect the prognosis for leukaemia patients treated in this manner. This
study involved patients suffering from acute lymphoblastic leukaemia
(ALL) and acute myelocytic leukaemia (AML), with those suffering from
AML being further divided into low-risk and high-risk, according to
their status at the time of transplantation. The survival time from the
date of the transfusion is available for each patient, together with the
values of a number of explanatory variables concerning the
characteristics of the donor and recipient, and adverse events that
occurred during the recovery process. Before the bone marrow transplant,
patients were treated with a combination of cyclophosphamide and
busulfan, in order to destroy all abnormal blood cells. The time taken
for the blood platelets to return to a normal level is then an important
variable in terms of the prognosis for a patient, and so the values of
this variable were also recorded.

This example is based on the data from just one hospital, St Vincent\'s
in Sydney, Australia. The observed survival time of each patient was
recorded in days, together with the values of an event indicator that is
unity if a patient died, and zero if the patient was still alive at the
end of the study period. The prognostic variables to be used in this
example concern the disease group, the ages of the patient and the bone
marrow donor, an indicator variable that denotes whether the platelet
count returned to a normal level of $40 \times 10^{9}$ per litre, and
the time taken to return to this value. Two patients, numbers 7 and 21,
died before the platelet count had returned to normal, and so for these
patients, no value is given for the time to return to a normal platelet
count. The variables recorded are therefore as follows:

  -------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------
  *Time*:        Survival time in days
  *Status*:      Event indicator (0 = censored, 1 = event)
  *Group*:       Disease group (1 = ALL, 2 = low-risk AML, 3 = high-risk AML)
  *Page*:        Age of patient
  *Dage*:        Age of donor
  *Precovery*:   Platelet recovery indicator (0 = no, 1 = yes)
  *Ptime*:       Time in days to return of platelets to normal level (if *Precovery* = 1)
  -------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------

The data used in this example are given in [Table
8.2](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_2){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_2}.

  Patient   *Time*   *Status*   *Group*   *Page*   *Dage*   *Precovery*   *Ptime*
  --------- -------- ---------- --------- -------- -------- ------------- ---------
   1        1199     0          1         24       40       1             29
   2        1111     0          1         19       28       1             22
   3        530      0          1         17       28       1             34
   4        1279     1          1         17       20       1             22
   5        110      1          1         28       25       1             49
   6        243      1          1         37       38       1             23
   7        86       1          1         17       26       0             
   8        466      1          1         15       18       1             100 
   9        262      1          1         29       32       1             59
  10        1850     0          2         37       36       1              9
  11        1843     0          2         34       32       1             19
  12        1535     0          2         35       32       1             21
  13        1447     0          2         33       28       1             24
  14        1384     0          2         21       18       1             19
  15        222      1          2         28       30       1             52
  16        1356     0          2         33       22       1             14
  17        1136     0          3         47       27       1             15
  18        845      0          3         40       39       1             20
  19        392      1          3         43       50       1             24
  20        63       1          3         44       37       1             16
  21        97       1          3         48       56       0             
  22        153      1          3         31       25       1             59
  23        363      1          3         52       48       1             19

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.2]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_2){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times of patients following bone marrow
  transplantation]{.table-title} {#tbl8_2}

The aim of the analysis of these data is to examine whether there are
any differences between the survival times of patients in the three
disease groups, after adjusting for prognostic variables. In order to
investigate the effect of the time taken for platelets to return to
their normal level on patient survival, a time-dependent variable,
$Plate(t)$, is defined. This variable takes the value zero at times *t*
when the platelets have not returned to normal levels, and then switches
to unity once a normal level has been achieved. Formally,

$$\begin{array}{l}
{Plate(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
0 & {\text{~if~}t < \text{~time~at~which~platelets~returned~to~normal,}} \\
1 & {\text{~if~}t\geqslant\text{~time~at~which~platelets~returned~to~normal,}}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

so that $Plate(t) = 0$ for all *t* when a patient dies before platelet
recovery.

We first fit a Cox proportional hazards model that contains the
variables associated with the age of the patient and donor, *Page* and
*Dage*. When either of these variables is added []{#chapter8.xhtml_p263
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 263. " role="doc-pagebreak"}on their own or
in the presence of the other, there is no significant reduction in the
value of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic.

The time-dependent variable $Plate(t)$ is now added to the null model.
The value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ is reduced from 67.13 to 62.21, a
reduction of 4.92 on 1 d.f., which is significant at the 5% level
($P = 0.026$). This suggests that time to platelet recovery does affect
survival. After allowing for the effects of this variable, there is
still no evidence that the hazard of death is dependent on the age of
the patient or donor.

The estimated coefficient of $Plate(t)$ in the model that contains this
variable alone is $- 2.696$, and the fact that this is negative
indicates that there is a greater hazard of death at any given time for
a patient whose platelets are not at a normal level. The hazard ratio at
any given time is $\exp( - 2.696) = 0.067$, and so a patient whose
platelets have recovered to normal at a given time has about
one-fifteenth the risk of death at that time. However, a 95% confidence
interval for the corresponding true hazard ratio is $(0.006,0.751)$,
which shows that the point estimate of the relative risk is really quite
imprecise.

To quantify the effect of disease group on survival, the change in
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ when the factor *Group* is added to the model that
contains the time-dependent variable $Plate(t)$ is 6.49 on 2 d.f., which
is significant at the 5% level ($P = 0.039$). The parameter estimates
associated with disease group show that the hazard of death is much
greater for those suffering from ALL and those in the high-risk group of
AML sufferers. The hazard ratios for an ALL patient relative to a
low-risk AML patient is 7.97 and that for a high-risk AML patient
relative to a low-risk one is 11.77.

For the model that contains the factor *Group* and the time-dependent
variable $Plate(t)$, the estimated baseline cumulative hazard and
survivor functions are given in [Table
8.3](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_3){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_3}. These have been
obtained using the estimate of the baseline cumulative hazard function
given in Equation (8.3).

  Time, *t*   ${\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t)$   ${\widetilde{S}}_{0}(t)$   ${\widetilde{S}}_{1}(t)$
  ----------- -------------------------- -------------------------- --------------------------
    0         0.0000                     1.0000                     1.0000
   63         0.1953                     0.8226                     0.9810
   86         0.3962                     0.6728                     0.9618
   97         0.6477                     0.5232                     0.9383
  110         1.2733                     0.2799                     0.8823
  153         1.9399                     0.1437                     0.8264
  222         2.6779                     0.0687                     0.7685
  243         3.4226                     0.0326                     0.7143
  262         4.2262                     0.0146                     0.6600
  363         5.0987                     0.0061                     0.6057
  392         6.0978                     0.0022                     0.5491
  466         7.2663                     0.0007                     0.4895
  1279        13.0700                    0.0000                     0.2766

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.3]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_3){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Estimated baseline cumulative hazard,
  ${\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t)$, baseline survivor function,
  ${\widetilde{S}}_{0}(t)$, and survivor function for an ALL patient
  with $Plate(t) = 1$, ${\widetilde{S}}_{1}(t)$]{.table-title} {#tbl8_3}

</aside>

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p264 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 264. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this table, ${\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t)$ and
${\widetilde{S}}_{0}(t)$ are the estimated cumulative hazard and
survivor functions for an individual with ALL and for whom the platelet
recovery indicator, $Plate(t)$, remains at zero throughout the study.
Also given in this table are the values of the estimated survivor
function for an individual with ALL, but for whom $Plate(t) = 1$ for all
values of *t*, denoted ${\widetilde{S}}_{1}(t)$. Since the value of
$Plate(t)$ is zero for each patient at the start of the study, and for
most patients this changes to unity at some later point in time, these
two estimated survivor functions illustrate the effect of platelet
recovery at any specific time. For example, the probability of an ALL
patient surviving beyond 97 days is only 0.52 if their platelets have
not recovered to a normal level by this time. On the other hand, if such
a patient has experienced platelet recovery by this time, they would
have an estimated survival probability of 0.94. The estimated survivor
function for an ALL patient whose platelet recovery status changes at
some time *t*~0~ from 0 to 1 can also be obtained from [Table
8.3](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_3), since this will be
${\widetilde{S}}_{0}(t)$ for $t\leqslant t_{0}$ and
${\widetilde{S}}_{1}(t)$ for $t > t_{0}$. Estimates of the survivor
function may also be obtained for individuals in the other disease
groups.

In this illustration, the data from two patients who died before their
platelet count had reached a normal level have a substantial impact on
inferences about the effect of platelet recovery. If patients 7 and 21
are omitted from the database, the time-dependent variable is no longer
significant when added to the null model ($P = 0.755$). The conclusion
about the effect of platelet recovery time on survival is therefore
dramatically influenced by the data for these two patients.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_3" role="doc-tip">

[Example 8.3]{.boxnum} [Chemotherapy in ovarian cancer patients]{.boxht}

Consider again the data on patient survival following diagnosis of
ovarian cancer, given in [Example 5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_11) of
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml). When a Cox proportional hazards model that
contains the variables *Age*, the age of a patient at the time origin,
and *Treat*, the treatment group, is fitted to the data on the survival
times of patients with ovarian cancer, the estimated hazard function for
the *i*th of 26 patients in the study is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ 0.147\, Age_{i} - 0.796\, Treat_{i} \right\} h_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$

The value of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic for this model is 54.148.

We now fit a model that contains *Age, Treat* and a term with a
time-varying coefficient of *Age*. This will be represented by the
time-dependent variable *Tage*, whose values are formed from the product
of *Age* and the survival time *t*, that is, $Tage = Age \times t$.
Since the []{#chapter8.xhtml_p265 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 265. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}values of *Tage* are dependent upon *t*, this
time-dependent variable cannot be fitted in the same manner as *Age* and
*Treat*, as explained in [Section 8.1.1](#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_1_1). When
*Tage* is added to the model, the fitted hazard function becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ 0.216\, Age_{i} - 0.664\, Treat_{i} - 0.0002\, Age_{i}\, t \right\} h_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$

Under this model, the hazard of death at *t* for a patient of a given
age on the combined treatment ($Treat = 2$), relative to one of the same
age on the single treatment ($Treat = 1$), is $\exp( - 0.664) = 0.52$,
which is not very different from the value of 0.45 found using the model
that does not contain the variable *Tage*. However, the log-hazard ratio
for a patient aged *a*~2~ years, relative to one aged *a*~1~ years, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{0.216(a_{2} - a_{1}) - 0.0002(a_{2} - a_{1})t}
\end{array}$$

at time *t*. This model therefore allows the log-hazard ratio for *Age*
to be linearly dependent on survival time.

The value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for the model that contains *Age, Treat*
and *Tage* is 53.613. The change in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ on adding the
variable *Tage* to a model that contains *Age* and *Treat* is therefore
0.53, which is not significant ($P = 0.465$). We therefore conclude that
the time-dependent variable *Tage* is not in fact needed in the model,
and so the age effect does not depend on time.

</aside>
<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_4" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 8.4]{.boxnum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam8_4){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Data from a cirrhosis study]{.boxht}

Although the data to be used in this example are artificial, it is
useful to provide a background against which these data can be
considered. Suppose therefore that 12 patients have been recruited to a
study on the treatment of cirrhosis of the liver. The patients are
randomised to receive either a placebo or a new treatment that will be
referred to as Liverol. Six patients are allocated to Liverol and six to
the placebo. At the time when the patient is entered into the study, the
age and baseline value of the patient\'s bilirubin level are recorded.
The natural logarithm of the bilirubin value (in *μ*mol/l) will be used
in this analysis, and the variables measured are summarised below:

  ----------- ------------------------------------------------
  *Time*:     Survival time of the patient in days
  *Status*:   Event indicator (0 = censored, 1 = uncensored)
  *Treat*:    Treatment group (0 = placebo, 1 = Liverol)
  *Age*:      Age of the patient in years
  *Lbr*:      Logarithm of bilirubin level
  ----------- ------------------------------------------------

The values of these variables are given in [Table
8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_4){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_4}.

  Patient   *Time*   *Status*   *Treat*   *Age*   *Lbr*
  --------- -------- ---------- --------- ------- -------
   1        281      1          0         46      3.2
   2        604      0          0         57      3.1
   3        457      1          0         56      2.2
   4        384      1          0         65      3.9
   5        341      0          0         73      2.8
   6        842      1          0         64      2.4
   7        1514     1          1         69      2.4
   8        182      0          1         62      2.4
   9        1121     1          1         71      2.5
  10        1411     0          1         69      2.3
  11        814      1          1         77      3.8
  12        1071     1          1         58      3.1

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.4]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_4){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times of 12 patients in a study on
  cirrhosis of the liver]{.table-title} {#tbl8_4}

Patients are supposed to return to the clinic three, six and twelve
months after the commencement of treatment, and yearly thereafter. On
these occasions, the bilirubin level is again measured and recorded.
Data are therefore available on how the bilirubin level changes in each
patient throughout the duration of the study. [Table
8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_5){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_5} gives the values
of the logarithm of the bilirubin value at each time in the follow-up
period for each patient.

  Patient   Follow-up Time   Log(bilirubin)
  --------- ---------------- ----------------
  1          47              3.8
            184              4.9
            251              5.0
  2          94              2.9
            187              3.1
            321              3.2
  3          61              2.8
             97              2.9
            142              3.2
            359              3.4
            440              3.8
  4          92              4.7
            194              4.9
            372              5.4
  5          87              2.6
            192              2.9
            341              3.4
  6          94              2.3
            197              2.8
            384              3.5
            795              3.9
  7          74              2.9
            202              3.0
            346              3.0
            917              3.9
            1411             5.1
  8          90              2.5
            182              2.9
  9         101              2.5
            410              2.7
            774              2.8
            1043             3.4
  10        182              2.2
            847              2.8
            1051             3.3
            1347             4.9
  11        167              3.9
            498              4.3
  12        108              2.8
            187              3.4
            362              3.9
            694              3.8

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.5]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_5){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Follow-up times and *log*(bilirubin) values for
  the 12 patients in the cirrhosis study]{.table-title} {#tbl8_5}

In taking log(bilirubin) to be a time-dependent variable, the value of
the variate is that recorded at the most recent follow-up visit, for
each patient. In this calculation, the change to a new value will be
assumed to take place immediately after the reading was taken, so that
patient 1, for example, is assumed to have a log(bilirubin) value of 3.2
for any time *t* when $t\leqslant 47$, 3.8 for $47 < t\leqslant 184$,
4.9 for $184 < t\leqslant 251$, and 5.0 for $251 < t\leqslant 281$. The
values of *Lbr* for a given individual then follow a step function in
which the values are assumed constant between any two adjacent time
points.

The data are first analysed using the baseline log(bilirubin) value
alone. A Cox proportional hazards model is used, and the values of
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ on fitting particular models are as shown in [Table
8.6](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_6){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_6}.

  Terms in Model   $- 2\log\hat{L}$
  ---------------- ------------------
  null model       25.121
  *Age*            22.135
  *Lbr*            21.662
  *Age, Lbr*       18.475

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.6]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_6){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Values of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for models without a
  time-dependent variable]{.table-title} {#tbl8_6}

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p266 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 266. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Both *Age* and *Lbr* appear to be needed in the
model, although the evidence for including *Age* as well as $Lbr$ is not
very strong. When *Treat* is added to the model that contains *Age* and
*Lbr*, the reduction in the value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ is 5.182 on 1 d.f.
This is significant at the 5% level ($P = 0.023$). The coefficient of
*Treat* is $- 3.052$, indicating that the drug Liverol is effective in
reducing the hazard of death. Indeed, other things being equal, Liverol
reduces the hazard of death by a factor of 0.047.

We now analyse these data, taking the log(bilirubin) values to be
time-dependent. Let *Lbrt* be the time-dependent variate formed from the
values of log(bilirubin). The values of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ on fitting Cox
regression models to the data are then given in [Table
8.7](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_7){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_7}.

  Terms in Model   $- 2\log\hat{L}$
  ---------------- ------------------
  null model       25.121
  *Age*            22.135
  *Lbrt*           12.050
  *Age, Lbrt*      11.145

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.7]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_7){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Values of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for models with a
  time-dependent variable]{.table-title} {#tbl8_7}

It is clear from this table that the hazard function depends on the
time-dependent variable *Lbrt*, and that after allowing for this, the
effect of *Age* is slight. We therefore add the treatment effect *Treat*
to the model that contains *Lbrt* alone. The effect of this is that
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ is reduced from 12.050 to 10.676, a reduction of 1.374
on 1 d.f. This reduction is not significant ($P = 0.241$) leading to the
conclusion that after taking account of the dependence of the hazard of
death on the evolution of the log(bilirubin) values, no treatment effect
is discernible. The estimated hazard function for the *i*th individual
is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ 3.605\, Lbr_{i}(t) - 1.479\, Treat_{i} \right\} h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $Lbr_{i}(t)$ is the value of log(bilirubin) for this patient at
time *t*. The estimated ratio of the hazard of death at time *t* for two
individuals on the same treatment who have values of *Lbr* that differ
by 0.1 units at *t* is $e^{0.3605} = 1.43$. This means that the
individual whose log(bilirubin) value is 0.1 units greater has close to
a 50% increase in the hazard of death at time *t*.

One possible explanation for the difference between the results of these
two analyses is that the effect of the treatment is to change the values
of the bilirubin level, so that after changes in these values over time
have been allowed for, no treatment effect is visible.

The baseline cumulative hazard function may now be estimated for the
model that contains the time-dependent variable *Lbrt* and *Treat*. The
estimated values of this function are tabulated in [Table
8.8](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_8){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_8}.

  Follow-up Time $(t)$   ${\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t)$
  ---------------------- --------------------------
    0                    0.000
  281                    $0.009 \times 10^{- 6}$
  384                    $0.012 \times 10^{- 6}$
  457                    $0.541 \times 10^{- 6}$
  814                    $0.908 \times 10^{- 6}$
  842                    $1.577 \times 10^{- 6}$
  1071                   $3.318 \times 10^{- 6}$
  1121                   $6.007 \times 10^{- 6}$
  1514                   $6.053 \times 10^{- 6}$

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.8]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_8){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Estimated baseline cumulative hazard function,
  ${\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t)$, for the cirrhosis study]{.table-title}
  {#tbl8_8}

This table shows that the cumulative hazard function is increasing in a
non-linear fashion, which indicates that the baseline hazard is not
constant, but increases with time. The corresponding baseline survivor
function could be obtained from this estimate. However, a model with
$Lbrt = 0$ for all *t* is not at all easy to interpret, and so the
estimated survivor function is obtained for an individual for whom
$Lbrt = 3$. This function is shown in [Figure
8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_5){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_5}, for a patient in
either treatment group.[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p267 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 267. Table on this page. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p268 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 268. Table on this page. " role="doc-pagebreak"}

<figure id="fig8_5" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_5.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_5" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.5</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated survivor function for a patient
with <span
class="math inline"><em>L</em><em>b</em><em>r</em> = 3</span>, for all
<em>t</em>, who is on placebo (—) or Liverol (<span
class="middle">.......</span>).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p269 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 269. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}This figure clearly shows that patients on the
placebo have a much poorer prognosis than those on Liverol.

Finally, we illustrate how estimates of the conditional probabilities of
survival from time *t* to time $t + 360$ days can be obtained, using the
result given in Equation (8.5). Using the values of the time-dependent
variable given in [Tables 8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_4) and
[8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_5), the values of
${\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{p}{\hat{\beta}}_{j}}x_{ji}(t)$, the prognostic
index at time *t* for the *i*th patient, $i = 1,2,\ldots,12$, can be
calculated for $t = 0$, 360, 720, 1080, and 1440. For this calculation,
the log(bilirubin) value at one of these times is taken to be the value
recorded at the immediately preceding follow-up time. [Table
8.8](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_8) is then used to obtain the values of
${\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t + 360) - {\widetilde{H}}_{0}(t)$, and then
${\widetilde{P}}_{i}(t,t + 360)$ is obtained from Equation (8.5). The
full set of results will not be given here, but as an example, the
estimated approximate conditional probabilities of surviving through
consecutive intervals of 360 days, for patients 1 and 7, are shown in
[Table 8.9](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_9){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_9}.

  Time Interval   ${\widetilde{P}}_{1}(t,t + h)$   ${\widetilde{P}}_{7}(t,t + h)$
  --------------- -------------------------------- --------------------------------
    0--           0.999                            1.000
  360--           0.000                            0.994
  720--           0.000                            0.969
  1080--          0.000                            0.457
  1440--          0.045                            0.364

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [8.9]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_9){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Approximate conditional survival probabilities
  for patients 1 and 7]{.table-title} {#tbl8_9}

Note that because these estimates are survival probabilities conditional
on being alive at the start of an interval, they are not necessarily
decreasing functions of time. These estimates again show that patients
on Liverol have a greater probability of surviving for a period of one
year, if they are alive at the start of that year, than patients on the
placebo. Finally, the values of $1 - {\widetilde{P}}_{i}(t,t + h)$ are
approximate estimates of the probability of death within each interval,
conditional on a patient being alive at the start. Summing these
estimates over all 12 patients leads to the values 0.02, 2.46, 5.64,
6.53, 3.16, for each of the five intervals, respectively. These can be
compared to the observed numbers of deaths in each interval, which are
1, 2, 3, 1 and 1, respectively. There is therefore a tendency for the
model to overestimate the numbers of deaths, but because of the small
size of the data set, this does not provide a very reliable assessment
of the predictive power of the model.

</aside>
:::
::::

:::: section
## 8.7 Joint modelling of longitudinal and survival data {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_7 .head1}

The association between the hazard of an event occurring at a given time
and an explanatory variable whose values are changing over time can be
assessed by including a time-dependent variable in a Cox regression
model. In the modelling process, the values of such a variable are
assumed to be constant between adjacent measurement times, so that it is
modelled as a step function. This is generally unrealistic, and the
resulting model can be a poor representation of the effect of a
time-dependent variable on the survival time. In addition, as with
baseline explanatory variables in a survival model, a time-dependent
variable is assumed not have measurement error, and that may also be
unrealistic.

Data that are recorded sequentially on the same individual over time are
known as *longitudinal data*, and a *longitudinal variable* is one whose
values evolve over time. The observed values of a longitudinal variable
at particular times can be regarded as snapshots of an underlying
continuous variable. A model for the longitudinal dependence of one or
more explanatory variables on time can be combined with a model for the
survival time to give a joint model for longitudinal and survival data.
A joint model can then be used []{#chapter8.xhtml_p270 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 270. " role="doc-pagebreak"}to examine the dependence
of a hazard function on the changing values of the longitudinal
variable.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_5" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 8.5]{.boxnum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam8_5){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival following aortic valve
replacement]{.boxht}

Thickening of the wall of the left ventricle of the heart chamber, known
as left ventricular hypertrophy, causes weakness, stiffening and loss of
elasticity, increasing the risk of cardiovascular disease. Replacement
of the aortic valve is designed to reduce this thickening, and a
reduction in the extent of hypertrophy is an important goal. The aim of
a study described by [Lim et al.
(2008)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib233){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib233 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} was to investigate changes in left ventricular
mass over a long period, to determine factors that may influence these
changes, and to assess the impact of changes on patient survival. The
study involved 256 patients who had undergone aortic valve replacement
using either a human or a porcine stentless valve. The patients were
followed up over a ten-year period, and during that time serial
echocardiographic measurements were used to calculate a left ventricular
mass index (LVMI). The time to death was also recorded, with those still
alive at the end of the follow-up period contributing a censored
survival time. Patients who had a second procedure were censored at that
time to ensure that they did not feature more than once in the data set.

The data used in this example includes a number of variables measured at
baseline, together with longitudinal measurements of the LVMI during the
follow-up period. The data can be found in the R data set
[heart.valve]{.monospace} that is part of the [joineR]{.monospace}
package, but a subset of the variables recorded, listed below, will be
used in this example.

  ----------- -----------------------------------------------------------
  *Id*:       Patient number
  *FUtime*:   Total follow-up time from date of surgery (years)
  *Status*:   Event indicator (0 = censored, 1 = death)
  *Time*:     Time of LVMI measurement after surgery (years)
  *LVMI*:     Standardised left ventricular mass index (LVMI)
  *Age*:      Age of patient in years
  *Sex*:      Sex of patient (0 = male, 1 = female)
  *Redo*:     Previous cardiac surgery (0 = no, 1 = yes,)
  *Emerg*:    Operative urgency (0 = elective, 1 = urgent or emergency)
  *DM*:       Preoperative diabetes mellitus (0 = no, 1 = yes)
  *Type*:     Type of valve (1 = human tissue, 2 = porcine tissue)
  ----------- -----------------------------------------------------------

Data for the first five patients are shown in [Table
8.10](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_10){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_10}. The
relationship between LVMI and time can be visualised by plotting LVMI
against time to give the response profile of each patient. [Figure
8.6](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_6){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_6} shows the
response profiles for all 256 patients.

  *Id*   *FUtime*   *Status*   *Time*   *LVMI*   *Age*   *Sex*   *Redo*   *Emerg*   *DM*   *Type*
  ------ ---------- ---------- -------- -------- ------- ------- -------- --------- ------ --------
