  1      4.956      0          0.011    118.98   75      0       0        0         0      2
  1      4.956      0          3.679    118.98   75      0       0        0         0      2
  1      4.956      0          4.696    137.63   75      0       0        0         0      2

  2      9.663      0          7.304    109.80   46      0       1        0         0      1

  2      9.663      0          9.307    114.93   46      0       1        0         0      1
  3      7.915      0          0.477    191.62   63      0       0        0         0      1
  3      7.915      0          1.321    168.38   63      0       0        0         0      1
  3      7.915      0          2.912    148.23   63      0       0        0         0      1
  3      7.915      0          5.482    180.18   63      0       0        0         0      1

  4      4.038      0          2.005     99.02   61      0       1        0         0      1
  4      4.038      0          4.038    112.92   61      0       1        0         0      1
  5      8.816      0          1.397    169.88   54      0       1        0         0      1
  5      8.816      0          4.638    183.43   54      0       1        0         0      1
  5      8.816      0          5.077    180.67   54      0       1        0         0      1

  [8.10]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_10){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times and longitudinal measurements of
  the LVMI for five patients who received an aortic valve
  replacement]{.table-title} {#tbl8_10}

<figure id="fig8_6" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_6.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_6" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.6</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Response profiles for each of the 256
patients in the valve replacement study.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

It is very difficult to discern any general trends in this figure, but
there is perhaps a suggestion of an initial decline in the value of the
index over time. The next step is to model the dependence of LVMI on
time to summarise the response profiles.

</aside>

::: section
### 8.7.1 Longitudinal modelling {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_7_1 .head2}

In this section, possible models are described for the relationship
between measurements on a longitudinal variable and time, for subsequent
use in a joint model for longitudinal and survival data. Particular
features that must be accounted for include the correlation between
successive measurements of the longitudinal variable in the same
individual, variation in the form of any relationship between
individuals, and random measurement error in the recorded values of the
longitudinal variable.

The simplest model for the relationship between a longitudinal variable
and time is a straight line relationship in which the slope and
intercept vary between individuals. Each []{#chapter8.xhtml_p271
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 271. Table on this page. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p272 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 272. " role="doc-pagebreak"}individual then has their
own profile for the expected response over time, and there is variation
between these individual profiles about an overall mean response.

Suppose that values $y_{ij}$ of a longitudinal variable *Y* are obtained
for the *i*th of *n* individuals at *n*~*i*~ successive times,
$t_{i1},t_{i2},\ldots,t_{i,n_{i}}$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,n_{i}$,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, where $y_{ij}$ can be expressed as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{y_{ij} = \beta_{0i} + \beta_{1i}t_{ij} + \epsilon_{ij}.}
\end{array}$$[(8.6)]{.equationnum}

In this model, the straight line relationship has different intercepts,
$\beta_{01},\beta_{02},\ldots,\beta_{0n}$, and different slopes,
$\beta_{11},\beta_{12},\ldots,\beta_{1n}$, for each of the *n*
individuals in the study. The quantity $\epsilon_{ij}$ in Equation (8.6)
is a residual term that summarises the deviation of $y_{ij}$ from the
assumed straight line model and may represent measurement error. The
intercept and slope for each of the *n* individuals are, for the moment,
assumed to have fixed values and are then termed *fixed effects*. The
expected value of $y_{ij}$ in this linear regression model is then
$\,\text{E}\,(y_{ij}) = \beta_{0i} + \beta_{1i}t_{ij}$, and the
$\epsilon_{ij}$ are generally assumed to be independent, with each
having a normal distribution with zero mean and common variance
$\sigma_{\epsilon}^{2}$.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_6" role="doc-tip">

[Example 8.6]{.boxnum} [Survival following aortic valve
replacement]{.boxht}

Suppose that the association between the left ventricular mass index
(LVMI) and time in the study on aortic valve replacement, described in
[Example 8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_5){#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam8_5}, is
modelled using a different linear relationship for each of the 256
patients. The expected value of the LVMI for the *i*th patient at time
$t_{ij}$ is then $\beta_{0i} + \beta_{1i}t_{ij}$, leading to variation
in the slopes and intercepts between the patients. This linear
regression model can be fitted to the data on the values of the LVMI at
different times for each patient, when data from at least two separate
times are available. In [Figure
8.7](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_7){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_7}, the fitted
relationships are plotted for the 256 patients in this study.

<figure id="fig8_7" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_7.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_7" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.7</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Individual straight line response variables
fitted to each of the 256 patients in the valve replacement
study.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

This plot is sometimes referred to as a *spaghetti plot* and shows that
there are clear differences in the fitted straight lines between
patients. For some patients, there is a positive slope and for others
the slope is negative. An overall linear regression model has estimated
expected value $162.126 - 0.701t$ at time *t*, and although there is an
overall downward trend in this relationship, suggesting that the LVMI
decreases over time, the slope is not significantly different from zero
$(P = 0.41)$.

</aside>

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p273 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 273. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}A particular disadvantage of the model in Equation
(8.6) is that there can be a large number of parameters to estimate. For
example, in the data on aortic valve replacement, there are 256 separate
intercepts and 256 separate slopes, together with the variance of the
$\epsilon_{ij}$. Moreover, the model assumes independent observations
within a patient, which is unlikely to be the case. To take account of
this, both the intercept and slope for each individual are now assumed
to be drawn at random from a large population of possible values, where
this population is summarised by a probability distribution. The
intercepts and slopes in such a model are then *random effects*. Models
for survival data that contain random effects, known as *frailty
models*, are described in [Chapter 10](#chapter10.xhtml) and [Section
10.1](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_1) of that chapter includes further details
on the nature of random effects.

We now assume that the intercepts $\beta_{0i}$ and slopes $\beta_{1i}$,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, for the *n* individuals, are normally distributed
random variables. The intercepts $\beta_{0i}$ are then assumed to be a
random sample of values from a normal distribution with mean *β*~0~ and
variance $\sigma_{0}^{2}$, written $N(\beta_{0},\sigma_{0}^{2})$, and
similarly, the slopes $\beta_{1i}$ are assumed to be randomly drawn from
a $N(\beta_{1},\sigma_{1}^{2})$ distribution. The underlying
distributions of the intercepts and slopes may be taken to be
independent, although this assumption can be relaxed. The mean values of
the random effects, *β*~0~ and *β*~1~, correspond to the average
intercept and slope across all the individuals and are sometimes termed
*population values*.

It is more convenient to use normal distributions with zero mean in the
formulation of a random effects model, and so we set
$\beta_{0i} = \beta_{0} + u_{0i}$ and $\beta_{1i} = \beta_{1} + u_{1i}$,
where $u_{0i}$ and $u_{1i}$ have independent normal distributions with
zero means and variances $\sigma_{0}^{2}$ and $\sigma_{1}^{2}$,
respectively. The model in Equation (8.6) then becomes

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{ij} & {= (\beta_{0} + u_{0i}) + (\beta_{1} + u_{1i})t_{ij} + \epsilon_{ij},} \\
 & {= \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t_{ij} + u_{0i} + u_{1i}t_{ij} + \epsilon_{ij}.}
\end{aligned}$$[(8.7)]{.equationnum}

By regarding the intercepts and slopes as random variables, observations
on the same individual will no longer be independent. To see this,
consider the covariance between the values of a longitudinal response
variable *Y* for the *i*th individual at times $t_{ij}$ and $t_{ij'}$,
for $j \neq j'$, defined by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{cov}\,(y_{ij},y_{ij'}) = \,\text{E}\,\lbrack(y_{ij} - \,\text{E}\,(y_{ij}))(y_{ij'} - \,\text{E}\,(y_{ij'}))\rbrack.}
\end{array}$$

Now, $\,\text{E}\,(y_{ij}) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t_{ij}$ and so
$y_{ij} - \,\text{E}\,(y_{ij}) = u_{0i} + u_{1i}t_{ij} + \epsilon_{ij}$,
and similarly for $\,\text{E}\,(y_{ij'})$, so that the covariance term
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{cov}\,(y_{ij},y_{ij'}) = \,\text{E}\,\lbrack(u_{0i} + u_{1i}t_{ij} + \epsilon_{ij})(u_{0i} + u_{1i}t_{ij'} + \epsilon_{ij'})\rbrack.}
\end{array}$$

Expanding this product, and using the fact that independence of $u_{0i}$
and $u_{1i}$ means that
$\,\text{cov}\,(u_{01},u_{1i}) = \,\text{E}\,(u_{0i}u_{1i}) = 0$, we get

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{cov}\,(y_{ij},y_{ij'}) = \,\text{E}\,(u_{0i}^{2}) + \,\text{E}\,(u_{1i}^{2})t_{ij}t_{ij'}.}
\end{array}$$

Since $\,\text{E}\,(u_{0i}) = 0$ and $\,\text{E}\,(u_{1i}) = 0$,
$\,\text{E}\,(u_{0i}^{2}) = \,\text{var}\,(u_{0i}) = \sigma_{0}^{2}$,
and similarly $\,\text{E}\,(u_{1i}^{2}) = \sigma_{1}^{2}$, so that the
covariance becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\sigma_{0}^{2} + \sigma_{1}^{2}t_{ij}t_{ij'}.}
\end{array}$$

The corresponding correlation between $y_{ij}$ and $y_{ij'}$ is obtained
by dividing the covariance by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \sqrt{}\left\{ {\,\text{var}\,(y_{ij})\,\text{var}\,(y_{ij'})} \right\} = \left. \sqrt{}\left\{ {(\sigma_{0}^{2} + \sigma_{1}^{2}t_{ij}^{2} + \sigma_{\epsilon}^{2})(\sigma_{0}^{2} + \sigma_{1}^{2}t_{ij'}^{2} + \sigma_{\epsilon}^{2})} \right\}. \right. \right.
\end{array}$$

This means that the model with random intercepts and slopes introduces a
correlation between any pair of observations within an individual that
depends on the variances of the random effects, as well as on the times
when the two observations were made. Moreover, []{#chapter8.xhtml_p274
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 274. " role="doc-pagebreak"}the variance of
the longitudinal variable increases with time, and the correlation
between two measurements decreases with increasing distance between the
measurement times $t_{ij}$ and $t_{ij'}$.

Often, there is some association between the intercept and slope for an
individual in a random effects model. For example, those with large
initial values of a longitudinal variable may experience a steeper
decline in the values of that variable over time. This can be
accommodated by allowing the random intercept and slope for each
individual in the model of Equation (8.7) to have correlation *ρ*, by
taking $\,\text{cov}\,(u_{0i},u_{1i}) = \rho\sigma_{0}\sigma_{1}$. The
vector of random effects for the *i*th individual,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i} = {(u_{0i},u_{1i})}'$, then has a bivariate
normal distribution with zero mean and a variance-covariance matrix,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}$, given by

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\mathbf{\mathbf{V}} = \left( \begin{array}{ll}
\sigma_{0}^{2} & {\rho\sigma_{0}\sigma_{1}} \\
{\rho\sigma_{0}\sigma_{1}} & \sigma_{1}^{2}
\end{array} \right).}
\end{array}$$

This leads to an additional term in the expression for the covariance
between the observed values of the longitudinal variable at different
times, $t_{ij}$ and $t_{ij'}$, as
$\,\text{cov}\,(u_{0i},u_{1i}) = \,\text{E}\,(u_{0i}u_{1i})$ is no
longer zero.

The longitudinal model in Equation (8.7) can also be extended to include
baseline variables as fixed effects, giving

$$\begin{array}{r}
{y_{ij} = \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t_{ij} + u_{0i} + u_{1i}t_{ij} + \epsilon_{ij},}
\end{array}$$[(8.8)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is a linear
combination of the values of the baseline explanatory variables for the
*i*th individual. This is a general linear mixed model in which $y_{ij}$
is an observation from a normal distribution with mean
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t_{ij}$
and variance

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\sigma_{0}^{2} + \sigma_{1}^{2}t_{ij}^{2} + \sigma_{\epsilon}^{2} + 2\rho\sigma_{0}\sigma_{1}t_{ij}.}
\end{array}$$

Additionally, the $y_{ij}$ themselves are correlated and the vector of
the *n*~*i*~ observations for the *i*th individual,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{y}}_{i}$, has a *multivariate normal distribution*.

A longitudinal variable can be regarded as a continuous function of *t*,
with observations made on the value of the variable at specific time
points, and can therefore be denoted by $Y(t)$. The model in Equation
(8.7) for $y_{ij}$, the observed value of a longitudinal variable $Y(t)$
at the *j*th time for the *i*th individual, $t_{ij}$, can then be
expressed as a model for the underlying longitudinal process, where the
observed value of $Y(t)$ at time *t* for the *i*th individual is
$y_{i}(t)$. The corresponding linear model for $y_{i}(t)$ can then be
written

$$\begin{array}{r}
{y_{i}(t) = m_{i}(t) + \epsilon_{i}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(8.9)]{.equationnum}

where $m_{i}(t) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t + u_{0i} + u_{1i}t$ is the true
underlying response profile that describes the trajectory of values
$y_{i}(t)$ for the *i*th individual, and $\epsilon_{i}(t)$ is the random
error term at time *t*. Under the model in Equation (8.9), the expected
value of the longitudinal variable is $\beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t$. The true
response profile, $m_{i}(t)$, is used to represent the relationship
between a longitudinal variable and time in a joint model for
longitudinal and survival data.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_7" role="doc-tip">

[Example 8.7]{.boxnum} [Survival following aortic valve
replacement]{.boxht}

A linear model with random intercepts and slopes is now used in
modelling the relationship between the longitudinal variable LVMI and
time in the data from [Example 8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_5). The LVMI
at time *t* will be denoted $Y(t)$, and the observed value of the index
for the *i*th patient at time *t* is as in Equation (8.9). Here,
$m_{i}(t) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t + u_{0i} + u_{1i}t$ is the true value
of the index at time *t*, and $u_{0i},u_{1i}$ are assumed to be
independent normal random variables with zero means and variances
$\sigma_{0}^{2}$, $\sigma_{1}^{2}$, respectively. The residual term
$\epsilon_{i}(t)$ is the difference between the observed and true value
of the LVMI and is assumed to have an $N(0,\sigma_{\epsilon}^{2})$
distribution.

</aside>

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p275 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 275. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}On fitting this model, ${\hat{\beta}}_{0} = 164.46$
and ${\hat{\beta}}_{1} = - 1.66$, so that the overall expected value of
the LVMI at time *t* is $164.46 - 1.66\, t$. In contrast to the fixed
effects model in Equation (8.6), the estimated slope is now almost
significant at the 5% level $(P = 0.060)$. The estimated variances of
the intercept and slope are ${\hat{\sigma}}_{0}^{2} = 3881.91$ and
${\hat{\sigma}}_{1}^{2} = 61.39$, so that corresponding 95% interval
estimates for *β*~0~ and *β*~1~ are respectively $(42.34,286.58)$ and
$( - 17.02,13.70)$. We would therefore expect that these intervals would
include most of the estimated slopes and intercepts for the 256 patients
in the study.

After fitting a longitudinal model with random effects, the intercepts
and slopes for each individual can be estimated, enabling the individual
response profiles to be obtained. The resulting linear relationships
between the LVMI and time, for each of the 256 patients in the study,
are shown in [Figure
8.8](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_8){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_8}.

<figure id="fig8_8" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_8.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_8" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.8</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Fitted random effects models for the 256
patients in the valve replacement study.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

Comparing this with [Figure 8.7](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_7), we see that
the slopes of the fitted lines are less extreme. The fitting process has
'shrunk' the slopes so that they are closer to zero. This is a well
known feature of random effects models, and occurs because the assumed
normal distribution for the slopes means that extreme values are less
likely. The individual slopes are now closer to the overall average
slope, with the more extreme slopes being shrunk the most.

Other variables measured at the outset of the study can also be added to
the random effects model, as in the model in Equation (8.8). When the
baseline variables, *Age, Sex, Redo, Emerg, DM* and *Type*, are included
in the model, only the gender effect is highly significant
$(P = 0.0008)$, with female patients having significantly lower values
of the LVMI. After allowing the intercepts in the random effects model
to depend on these baseline measurements, the time effect becomes more
significant $(P = 0.045)$.
:::
::::

::: section
## 8.7.2 A joint model {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_7_2 .head1i}

The dependence of a survival time on a longitudinal variable, $Y(t)$,
can be modelled by simply adding the observed value $y_{i}(t)$ to a Cox
regression model for the hazard of an event occurring at time *t* for
the *i*th individual. In this model, $Y(t)$ is fitted as a
time-dependent variable, using the methods described in [Section
8.2](#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_2) of this chapter. However, to take proper
account of the relationship between $Y(t)$ and time, a joint model for
the longitudinal variable and the survival time is required.

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p276 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 276. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In modelling the evolution of a longitudinal
response variable over time, the response profile $m_{i}(t)$ in Equation
(8.9) is used to describe this relationship for the *i*th individual. To
form a joint model, $m_{i}(t)$ is added to a model for the hazard of an
event occurring at time *t*, with a coefficient that summarises the
association between the hazard at time *t* and $m_{i}(t)$. Either a
semi-parametric or a fully parametric model for the hazard function may
be used. The hazard function for the *i*th individual, $h_{i}(t)$, can
then be modelled by taking

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \alpha\, m_{i}(t) \right\} h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(8.10)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is a vector of baseline explanatory
variables with coefficients $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$, and $h_{0}(t)$ is
the baseline hazard function. This model shows how the hazard of an
event occurring at time *t* depends on the value of $m_{i}(t)$, the
value of the response profile at that time. In this model, the parameter
*α* is a measure of the association between the value of the
longitudinal variable and the hazard of an event at time *t*. This
parameter can be interpreted as the adjusted log-hazard ratio of an
event occurring at time *t* for a unit increase in $m_{i}(t)$, the true
value of the longitudinal variable at time *t* for the *i*th individual.

The corresponding survivor function for the *i*th individual at time *t*
for the model in Equation (8.10) is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- {\int_{0}^{t}h_{i}}(v)\,\text{d}v} \right\} = \exp\left\{ {- {\int_{0}^{t}\exp}\left\{ \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \alpha m_{i}(v) \right\} h_{0}(v)\,\text{d}v} \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(8.11)]{.equationnum}

and this equation is needed to construct the likelihood function for the
survival model. In order to evaluate the integral in this expression,
values of $m_{i}(t)$ are required at all times up to time *t*.

The model in Equation (8.10) can be fitted by maximising the joint
likelihood derived from the models for the longitudinal variable and the
survival time. This enables the parameters in both parts of the joint
model to be estimated simultaneously. In outline, the likelihood for the
longitudinal part of the model is obtained from the joint distribution
of the *n*~*i*~ longitudinal observations on the *i*th individual,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{y}}_{i}$. Regarding the corresponding vector of random
effects, $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$, as fixed, $\mathbf{\mathbf{y}}_{i}$
has a multivariate normal distribution that is a function of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$ and a vector of model parameters, $\phi_{L}$.
Here, $\phi_{L}$ includes coefficients of the explanatory variables in
the longitudinal model, including the intercept and the coefficients of
terms associated with the time variable, and the variance of the
residual terms, $\sigma_{\epsilon}^{2}$. The corresponding likelihood
function for the *i*th individual is denoted
$L_{L}(\phi_{L},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i})$.

The likelihood function for the survival time of the *i*th individual,
*t*~*i*~, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}{(t_{i})}^{\delta_{i}}S_{i}(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$

from Expression (5.15) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), where *δ*~*i*~
is the event indicator. The hazard and survivor functions for the *i*th
individual can be found from Equations (8.10) and (8.11). Again
regarding $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$ as fixed, this likelihood will be
denoted by $L_{S}(\phi_{S},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i})$, where $\phi_{S}$
is a vector of model parameters. These include the coefficients of
explanatory variables in the survival model, the coefficient of
$m_{i}(t)$, *α*, and any unknown parameters in the baseline hazard
function.

The joint likelihood for the *i*th individual is then the product of the
two likelihood functions, $L_{L}(\phi_{L},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i})$ and
$L_{S}(\phi_{S},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i})$, over the *n* individuals.
Now, the components of $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$ are random quantities,
and so this joint likelihood is averaged over all possible values of the
random effects by integration with respect to their probability
distribution. The joint density function of $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$ is
written $f(\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i};\phi_{U})$, and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$ has a multivariate normal distribution with
zero mean and variance-covariance matrix that depends on the variances
and covariances of the $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}$ in the vector
$\phi_{U}$. The integrated likelihood function for the *n* individuals
is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{\int_{- \infty}^{\infty}L_{L}}}(\phi_{L},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i})L_{S}(\phi_{S},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i})f(\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i};\phi_{U})\,\text{d}\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}_{i}.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p277 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 277. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Estimates of the unknown model parameters are then
found by maximising the logarithm of this function, which of course can
only be done numerically. Standard errors of the estimates are found
from the information matrix, as described in [Appendix
A](#appA.xhtml_appA). Nested models with different sets of explanatory
variables in either the longitudinal or survival components can be
compared using the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic in the usual way, while
non-nested models with different formulations of the baseline hazard
function, for example, can be compared using the *AIC* statistic.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_8" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 8.8]{.boxnum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam8_8){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival following aortic valve
replacement]{.boxht}

A joint model that incorporates the dependence between the time to death
of patients who have received a heart valve replacement and the left
ventricular mass index (LVMI) is now fitted to the data from [Example
8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_5). Initially, both the model for the
longitudinal variable LVMI, and the survival model for the hazard of
death, will include all six baseline variables, namely *Age, Sex, Redo,
Emerg, DM* and *Type*.

The longitudinal variable for the LVMI will be modelled by using a
straight line relationship between the index and the time of
measurement, with random intercepts and slopes for each patient, that
may be correlated. First, a Cox regression model is used in the joint
model, fitted as a piecewise exponential model, as described in [Section
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), rather
than using partial likelihood methods. The estimated parameters and
their standard errors in this joint model are shown in [Table
8.11](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_11){#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_11}.

+-----------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------------+
| Terms in Model                                      | Cox Model             | Piecewise Model       | Weibull Model               |
+=====================================================+============+==========+============+==========+==============+==============+
| *Longitudinal process:*                             |            |          |            |          |              |              |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Intercept (${\hat{\beta}}_{0}$)                     | 151.106    | (13.037) | 155.060    | (15.879) | 155.323      | (15.873)     |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Time (${\hat{\beta}}_{1}$)                          | $- 1.245$  | (0.835)  | $- 1.264$  | (0.908)  | $- 1.285$    | (0.915)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Age                                                 | 0.266      | (0.212)  | 0.224      | (0.256)  | 0.220        | (0.254)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Sex: Female                                         | $- 34.367$ | (6.523)  | $- 33.495$ | (6.709)  | $- 33.383$   | (6.702)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Redo: Yes                                           | 6.810      | (8.378)  | 5.254      | (13.988) | 5.152        | (14.470)     |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Emerg: Urgent                                       | 8.461      | (8.464)  | 1.544      | (17.049) | 1.015        | (17.316)     |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| DM: Present                                         | $- 17.614$ | (10.083) | $- 20.731$ | (11.511) | $- 20.600$   | (11.483)     |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Type: Porcine                                       | 8.912      | (6.394)  | 9.017      | (6.752)  | 8.981        | (6.791)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| *Survival process:*                                 |            |          |            |          |              |              |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Age                                                 | 0.102      | (0.008)  | 0.106      | (0.019)  | 0.099        | (0.018)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Sex: Female                                         | 0.235      | (0.309)  | 0.199      | (0.314)  | 0.187        | (0.313)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Redo: Yes                                           | $- 0.171$  | (0.534)  | $- 0.184$  | (0.535)  | $- 0.231$    | (0.536)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Emerg: Urgent                                       | 0.055      | (0.423)  | 0.015      | (0.441)  | 0.015        | (0.440)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| DM: Present                                         | 0.554      | (0.618)  | 0.537      | (0.633)  | 0.463        | (0.634)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Type: Porcine                                       | 0.054      | (0.318)  | 0.080      | (0.321)  | 0.099        | (0.319)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| $m_{i}(t)$ $(\hat{\alpha})$                         | 0.008      | (0.003)  | 0.008      | (0.003)  | 0.008        | (0.003)      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+--------------+--------------+
| Weibull shape $(\hat{\gamma})$                      |            |          |            |          | $2.616 \times 10^{- 6}$     |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+-----------------------------+
| Weibull scale $(\hat{\lambda})$                     |            |          |            |          |  1.697                      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+------------+----------+------------+----------+-----------------------------+
| $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{0i})$                      | 3600.35               | 3541.92               | 3552.04                     |
+-----------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------------+
| $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{1i})$                      |  54.89                |  54.76                |  55.92                      |
+-----------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------------+
| $\hat{\rho} = \hat{\,\text{corr}\,}(u_{0i},u_{1i})$ |  $- 0.59$             |  $- 0.59$             |  $- 0.60$                   |
+-----------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------------+
| $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(\epsilon_{i})$                | 1292.36               | 1308.15               | 1305.89                     |
+-----------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------------+
| $- 2\log\hat{L}$                                    | 11069                 | 10887                 | 10892                       |
+-----------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------------+
| *AIC*                                               | 11107                 | 10937                 | 10934                       |
+-----------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------------+

: [[[Table]{.label}
[8.11]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter8.xhtml_b8tbl8_11){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Parameter estimates (standard error) in fitted
joint models using a Cox model, a piecewise exponential model with five
internal knots and a Weibull model for the survival
process]{.table-title} {#tbl8_11}

</aside>

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p278 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 278. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In the model for the longitudinal variable, the
only baseline variables that have a significant effect on the LVMI are
gender ($P < 0.001$) and preoperative diabetes mellitus ($P = 0.081$).
On average, female patients have a significantly lower value of the
index than males, and patients with preoperative diabetes have lower
index values. There is slight evidence that the LVMI decreases with time
($P = 0.136$).

In the survival model, each additional year of age increases the hazard
of death by 11% ($P < 0.001$), but none of the other variables measured
at baseline significantly affect patient survival. There is a
significant association between the true value of the LVMI at a given
time and the hazard of death at that time ($P = 0.001$), as measured by
the estimated value of the parameter *α* in the joint model. An increase
of 100 in the true value of the LVMI at a given time results in the
hazard of death at that time increasing by a factor of
$\exp(100\hat{\alpha}) = 2.30$.

From the estimated variances of $u_{0i}$ and $u_{1i}$, there is
considerable variation between the intercepts, even after allowing for
differences due to the baseline variables. The variation between the
slopes is much less marked, and considerably less than the variation in
the LVMI values due to measurement error. On fitting models in which the
random intercept and slope are independent within an individual, the
value of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic is 11094, which is considerably
greater than the value of 11069 in [Table
8.11](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_11), suggesting that these terms are indeed
correlated. From [Table 8.11](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_11), the random
intercepts and slopes are negatively correlated with a value around
$- 0.6$, so that the LVMI declines more quickly in patients who
initially have a higher value of the index.

A Cox regression model for the hazard function essentially has an
unknown parameter at each event time to describe the baseline hazard
function. As discussed in [Section 6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1) of
[Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), a more parsimonious model can be found by
limiting the number of cut points by specifying either the number or
location of these. For illustration, a piecewise model with five cut
points will be fitted, and the results are also given in [Table
8.11](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_11). The parameter estimates and their
standard errors, as well as the estimated variance components, are very
similar to those found on using a Cox regression model for the survival
component of the joint model.

Fully parametric models can be fitted in a similar way, and the results
of fitting a Weibull model for the baseline hazard function are also
shown in [Table 8.11](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_11). The model is
parameterised as a proportional hazards model, so that the baseline
hazard function is $\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$. Again the estimated
coefficients are similar to those found on fitting the other two models,
but here the hazard is a smooth function of time, rather than a step
function.

Comparing the values of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ and *AIC* statistics across
the three fitted models, we see that the Weibull model has the smallest
*AIC* value. The piecewise exponential model with five cut points has
the smallest $- 2\log\hat{L}$ value, but this model has six parameters
to represent the baseline hazard, rather than the two needed for the
Weibull model, resulting in a smaller value of the *AIC* statistic.
:::

::: section
## 8.7.3 Some extensions to the joint model {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_7_3 .head1i}

There are a number of ways in which the joint model for longitudinal and
survival data can be extended. The time variable in the linear component
of the longitudinal model can be modelled as a polynomial or spline term
so as to capture non-linear behaviour in the overall time trend.
Interactions between one or more factors measured at baseline and the
longitudinal variable can also be included to allow the effect of the
true level of the variable to vary across groups of individuals, such as
those of different ages.

In [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), flexible models for the log-baseline
hazard function based on B-splines, and for the log-cumulative hazard
function based on restricted cubic splines, were described and
illustrated. These models may also be used for the survival component of
a joint model.

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p279 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 279. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In the joint model given in Equation (8.10), the
true underlying value of a longitudinal variable at time *t*, $m_{i}(t)$
has a coefficient *α*. This coefficient summarises the association
between the value of a longitudinal variable at a given time and the
hazard of an event occurring at that time. In an obvious extension, *α*
may be formulated to depend on the values of certain baseline variables.
This enables models to be developed where the longitudinal response
impacts on survival in a way that depends on the baseline
characteristics of an individual.

In the model of Equation (8.10), the current value of a longitudinal
variable affects the current hazard of an event. This is sometimes
implausible, as there may be a period of time between the occurrence of
a critical value of the longitudinal variable and the event itself. This
can be accommodated by using a time lagged longitudinal variable to
allow the hazard of an event at time *t* to depend on the value of the
variable at an earlier time, $t - k$, for a given value of *k*. The
resulting model is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \alpha\, m_{i}(t - k)} \right\} h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $m_{i}(0)$ is used when $t < k$.

The slope of the trajectory of the longitudinal variable at a given time
may also affect the hazard of an event at that time. The slope of the
trajectory at time *t* is the derivative of the function $m(t)$ with
respect to *t*, $m'(t)$, say, and can be included in the joint model by
writing

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \alpha_{1}m_{i}(t) + \alpha_{2}m_{i}'(t)} \right\} h_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$

In this model, *α*~2~ measures the association between the hazard of an
event at *t* and the slope or rate of change of the true trajectory of
the longitudinal variable at that time, allowing for the association
between the value of the longitudinal variable and the hazard at that
time.

The hazard function for the survival process may also depend on the
history of the longitudinal variable up to time *t*. This can be
summarised using the area under the trajectory of the longitudinal
variable up to that time, which is the integral of the longitudinal
variable. The joint model for the hazard of an event at time *t* is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \alpha{\int_{0}^{t}m_{i}}(v)\,\text{d}v} \right\} h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where now *α* measures the strength of the association between the
hazard of an event at time *t* and the area under the trajectory of the
longitudinal variable to that time.

Many other extensions are possible, but a full treatment of longitudinal
data modelling is beyond the scope of this text.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam8_9" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 8.9]{.boxnum}](#chapter17.xhtml_b8exam8_9){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival following aortic valve
replacement]{.boxht}

In this example, some extensions to the joint model for longitudinal and
survival data will be illustrated using the data on the time to death
following aortic valve replacement, first given in [Example
8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_5). A number of baseline variables in the
longitudinal and survival components of the model were found not to be
significant in [Example
8.8](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_8){#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam8_8}. We will
therefore base this example on a joint model in which the longitudinal
response depends on the gender of the patient and their preoperative
diabetes status, and the survival component depends on patient age
alone. The longitudinal model for the value of the LVMI at time *t* for
the *i*th patient, $y_{i}(t)$, can then be expressed as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{y_{i}(t) = m_{i}(t) + \epsilon_{i}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $m_{i}(t)$ is the true, unobservable, value of the longitudinal
variable at time *t*, given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{m_{i}(t) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t + u_{0i} + u_{1i}t + \beta_{11}s_{i} + \beta_{12}d_{i},}
\end{array}$$[(8.12)]{.equationnum}

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p280 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 280. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and *s*~*i*~ and *d*~*i*~ are values of the
baseline variables *Sex* and *DM* for the *i*th individual, with
coefficients *β*~11~ and *β*~12~, respectively. Also, $u_{0i}$ is a
random $N(0,\sigma_{0}^{2})$ intercept and $u_{1i}$ is a random
$N(0,\sigma_{1}^{2})$ slope. For the moment, we will assume that the
random intercept and slope for each individual have correlation
$\rho = \,\text{corr}\,(u_{0i},u_{1i})$.

The joint model for the hazard of death at time *t* is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ \beta_{13}a_{i} + \alpha\, m_{i}(t) \right\} h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where *a*~*i*~ is age of the *i*th patient with coefficient *β*~13~,
$m_{i}(t)$ is given in Equation (8.12), and $h_{0}(t)$ is the baseline
hazard function.

In this example, a flexible parametric model for the survival times will
be used, where the logarithm of the baseline hazard function is modelled
using a cubic B-spline. This model was described in [Section
6.3](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_3) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), and from
Equation (6.10), the baseline hazard for a spline function with *m*
internal knots is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log h_{0}(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m + 4}\gamma_{j}}B_{j,3}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(8.13)]{.equationnum}

where *γ*~*j*~ is the coefficient of the *j*th cubic spline basis
function, $B_{j,3}(t)$, defined by the recurrence relationship in
Equation (6.6). This baseline hazard function has $m + 4$ unknown
*γ*-parameters.

On fitting joint models in which the baseline hazard function has
increasing numbers of internal knots, a model with $m = 5$ knots at
equally spaced percentiles of the event times has the smallest value of
the *AIC* statistic, namely 10913. The coefficients of the explanatory
variables in this model are very similar to those obtained for other
formulations of the baseline hazard function. The fitted model is such
that the overall estimated value of the LVMI for the *i*th patient is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{m}}_{i}(t) = 175.09 - 1.41t - 29.64s_{i} - 20.93d_{i} + {\hat{u}}_{0i} + {\hat{u}}_{1i}t,}
\end{array}$$

where ${\hat{u}}_{i0}$ and ${\hat{u}}_{i1}$ are estimates of the
individual random effects associated with the intercept and slope. The
estimated hazard function for the joint model is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ 0.0857a_{i} + 0.0051{\hat{m}}_{i}(t) \right\}{\hat{h}}_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$

where ${\hat{h}}_{0}(t)$ is an estimate of the baseline hazard function
in Equation (8.13). The estimated coefficient of ${\hat{m}}_{i}(t)$ in
this model is similar to that found for other models in [Example
8.8](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_8), shown in [Table
8.11](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_11).

It may be that the slope of the relationship between the LVMI and time
depends on the gender and preoperative status of a patient. Adding an
interaction between time and gender does not significantly improve the
fit of the model $(P = 0.75)$, and neither does an interaction between
diabetes and time $(P = 0.37)$.

The next step is to extend the model for the longitudinal variable to
allow a curvilinear relationship with time. For example, a quadratic
function of time can be included by taking

$$\begin{array}{r}
{m_{i}(t) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t + \beta_{2}t^{2} + u_{0i} + u_{1i}t + u_{2i}t^{2} + \beta_{11}s_{i} + \beta_{12}d_{i},}
\end{array}$$

where the coefficients of the linear and quadratic time effects are
normally distributed random variables. When there are a number of random
effects, it can be difficult to estimate all the covariance terms, and
so we will henceforth take the random effects to be uncorrelated.
Combining this quadratic expression for $m_{i}(t)$ with a B-spline model
for the logarithm of the baseline hazard function with five internal
knots gives a model that has an *AIC* value of 10926. This can be
compared with the corresponding value of the *AIC* statistic for the
[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p281 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 281. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}straight line model with uncorrelated random
effects, that is 10934. A cubic function of time gives a slightly
improved fit, with an *AIC* value of 10920.

As noted in [Section 3.8.2](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_8_2) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml), polynomial functions may perform poorly outside the
range of the data and a spline function of time may lead to an improved
fit. Here we will use a restricted cubic spline function of time for the
longitudinal variable LVMI with *k* internal knots, so that the spline
function is linear beyond the boundary knots. Restricted cubic splines
were described in [Section 6.2.2](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_2_2) of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml).

A joint model, with a B-spline representation of the logarithm of the
baseline hazard, and a restricted cubic spline function of time for the
longitudinal variable, LVMI, is now fitted. It turns out that modelling
the time effect by a spline with four internal knots does not
significantly improve the fit over a model with three, and so the latter
model is adopted. For this model, the *AIC* statistic is 10885. This is
considerably smaller than the corresponding *AIC* value for the model
where the LVMI is a cubic function of time.

Spline models are very flexible, but they are difficult to interpret
algebraically. We will therefore compare some of the fitted models for
the relationship between the LVMI and time graphically. [Figure
8.9](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_9){#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_9} shows the
estimated functions of time in a joint model for the hazard of death.

<figure id="fig8_9" class="image">
<img src="images/fig8_9.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter8.xhtml_b8fig8_9" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">8.9</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Some longitudinal models for the relationship
between LVMI and time. The solid line is the fitted cubic spline model
with 95% intervals given as dotted lines. Also shown are the fitted
straight lines for uncorrelated (- . -) and correlated (- - -) slopes
and intercepts, a quadratic model (<span class="math inline">−−</span>)
and a cubic model (<span
class="middle">.......</span>).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The figure relates to male patients without preoperative diabetes, and
shows the overall estimated expected value of $m_{i}(t)$, given by
${\hat{m}}_{i}(t) = {\hat{\beta}}_{0} + {\hat{\beta}}_{1}t + {\hat{\beta}}_{2}t^{2}$
for the quadratic model, for example, and similarly for other models.
Also shown in [Figure 8.9](#chapter8.xhtml_fig8_9) are estimated 95%
confidence bands for the fitted restricted cubic spline function.

The straight line models with uncorrelated and correlated random effects
for the intercepts and slopes are very similar to one another, although
the intercept is slightly greater in the model with correlated slopes.
The cubic longitudinal model is a better fit than either of the two
straight line models and the quadratic model, and has a similar pattern
to the cubic spline, that is the best fitting model of those tried. The
trajectories for all the fitted models are contained within the 95%
confidence bands for the fitted spline function, and so it is difficult
to discriminate between them. However, it does appear from the fitted
spline []{#chapter8.xhtml_p282 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 282. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}curve that there is a decline in values of the LVMI
in the two years following aortic valve replacement, but after that time
the values stabilise or possibly increase.

Finally, we will examine whether there is any evidence that the hazard
of death at a given time in the joint model depends on the slope of the
longitudinal trajectory of LVMI values at that time, or the accumulated
values of the index up to then. It is more straightforward to
differentiate or integrate a polynomial function of time than a cubic
spline term, and since the fit of a cubic polynomial is not so different
from that of the cubic spline model, we will use that model in this
illustration.

The slope of the cubic trajectory at time *t* for the *i*th patient is
obtained from the derivative of

$$\begin{array}{r}
{m_{i}(t) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}t + \beta_{2}t^{2} + + \beta_{3}t^{3} + u_{0i} + u_{1i}t + u_{2i}t^{2} + u_{3i}t^{3}}
\end{array}$$

with respect to *t* and is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{m_{i}'(t) = \beta_{1} + 2\beta_{2}t + 3\beta_{3}t^{2} + u_{1i} + 2u_{2i}t + 3u_{3i}t^{2}.}
\end{array}$$

The cumulative effect of the LVMI at *t* is the integral of $m_{i}(t)$,
given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\int_{0}^{t}m_{i}}(v)\,\text{d}v = \beta_{0}t + \beta_{1}\frac{t^{2}}{2} + \beta_{2}\frac{t^{3}}{3} + \beta_{3}\frac{t^{4}}{4} + u_{0i}t + u_{1i}\frac{t^{2}}{2} + u_{2i}\frac{t^{3}}{3} + u_{3i}\frac{t^{4}}{4}.}
\end{array}$$

Adding the slope to the joint model in addition to the term $m_{i}(t)$
gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ \beta_{13}a_{i} + \alpha_{1}m_{i}(t) + \alpha_{2}m_{i}'(t) \right\} h_{0}(t)}
\end{array}$$

and on fitting this model, we find that the estimated value of *α*~2~ is
0.0018 with a standard error of 0.0488 $(P = 0.97)$. The slope of the
LVMI trajectory does not have a significant effect on the hazard of
death after accounting for the effect of the LVMI at a given time on the
hazard of death at that time.

To model the cumulative effect of the LVMI rather than the value at *t*,
the joint model includes the integral of $m_{i}(t)$ giving

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ \beta_{13}a_{i} + \alpha{\int_{0}^{t}m_{i}}(v)\,\text{d}v \right\} h_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$

On fitting this model, $\hat{\alpha} = 0.0008$, with a standard error of
0.0006 that is not significant $(P = 0.21)$. The value of the *AIC*
statistic for this model is 10927, which is larger than that for the
cubic model that includes the effect of the LVMI at time *t*, 10920.
There is no evidence that the hazard of death at a given time depends on
the accumulated values of the LVMI to that time.

</aside>
:::

::: section
## 8.8 Further reading {#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_8 .head1}

The possibility of incorporating time-dependent variables in a
proportional hazards model was raised by [Cox
(1972)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib77){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib77 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. The appropriate partial likelihood function was
given in his paper, and discussed in greater detail in [Cox
(1975)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib78){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib78 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Kalbfleisch and Prentice
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib198){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib198 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} include a detailed account of the construction of
the partial likelihood function. The classification of time-dependent
variables outlined in [Section 8.1](#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_1) is due to
[Prentice and Kalbfleisch
(1979)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib302){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib302 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, who amplify on this in [Kalbfleisch and Prentice
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib198){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
[Andersen (1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib17){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib17
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reviews the uses of time-dependent
variables in survival analysis and includes an example on which the
hypothetical study of [Example 8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_4) is loosely
based. [Therneau and Grambsch
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib380){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib380 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Therneau
(2022)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib377){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib377 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describe some practical considerations and common
misconceptions when using time-dependent variables.

[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p283 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 283. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}A number of practical problems encountered in the
analysis of survival data with time-dependent variables are discussed by
[Altman and De Stavola (1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib13){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. A comprehensive analysis of data on primary
biliary cirrhosis, which includes an assessment of conditional survival
probabilities is also provided. See [Christensen et al.
(1986)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib64){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib64 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} for a further illustration. [Klein and
Moeschberger (2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib212){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} give the full data set on survival following bone
marrow transplantation, part of which was used in [Example
8.2](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_2){#chapter8.xhtml_b8exam8_2}, and use this
in a number of detailed illustrative examples.

The model described in [Section 8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_4) in
connection with organ transplantation was presented by [Crowley and Hu
(1977)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib89){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and
[Cox and Oakes (1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib81){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} in an analysis of the 'Stanford heart transplant
data'. This famous data set is given in [Crowley and Hu
(1977)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib89){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and an
update is provided by [Cox and Oakes
(1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib81){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. See
also [Aitkin, Laird and Francis
(1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib8){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib8 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and the ensuing discussion.

Relatively little work has been done on incorporating time-dependent
variables in a fully parametric model for survival data. However,
[Petersen (1986)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib290){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib290
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} shows how a parametric model with
time-dependent variables can be fitted. The Royston and Parmar model,
described in [Section 6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_4) of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml), can be extended to include time-dependent variables
by adding an interaction between potential time-dependent variables and
the spline variables, $\nu_{1}(y),\nu_{2}(y),\ldots$, in the baseline
cumulative hazard function of Equation (6.11) of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml).

A number of texts describe mixed models for use in longitudinal data
analysis, including [Crowder and Hand
(1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib87){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib87 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Diggle et al.
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib102){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib102 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Fitzmaurice, Laird and Ware
(2011)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib125){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib125 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Hand and Crowder
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib164){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib164 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Hedeker and Gibbons
(2006)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib172){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib172 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Molenberghs and Verbeke
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib265){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib265 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Verbeke and Molenberghs
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib395){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib395 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Weiss
(2010)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib406){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib406 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. A key text on the joint modelling of longitudinal
and time-to-event data is [Rizopoulos
(2012)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib317){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib317 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, who summarises the theory and provides many
practical illustrations using R software. [Elashoff, Li and Li
(2017)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib114){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib114 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} also provide an introduction to the theory and
practice of modelling with examples from clinical research. Review
papers include [Tsiatis and Davidian
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib392){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib392 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Tseng, Hsieh and Wang
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib390){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib390 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [McCrink, Marshall and Cairns
(2013)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib252){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib252 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Ritchie, Kalra and Diggle
(2015)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib315){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib315 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Henderson, Diggle and Dobson
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib174){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib174 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describe a general class of models for joint
modelling and illustrate some specific models, and flexible parametric
models for joint modelling are described by [Crowther, Abrams and
Lambert (2012)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib91){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib91
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. [Crowther et al.
(2016)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib92){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib92 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} show how delayed entry or left truncation can be
incorporated, and investigate the consequences of misspecifying the
longitudinal trajectory. [Long and Mills
(2018)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib242){#chapter8.xhtml_b8refbib242 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} show how joint models have been used in studies of
Huntingdon\'s disease.[]{#chapter8.xhtml_p284 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 284 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}
:::

[]{#chapter9.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter9.xhtml_p285 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 285. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 9]{.chapnum}[Interval-censored survival data]{.chaptitle} {#chapter9.xhtml_chapter9 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-9](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-9){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

In many studies where the response variable is a survival time, the
exact time of the event of interest will not be known. Instead, the
event will be known to have occurred during a particular time interval.
Data in this form are known as *grouped* or *interval-censored* survival
data, and commonly arise in studies where there is a non-lethal end
point. Examples include the time to emergence of permanent teeth in
children who visit the dentist fairly regularly, the time to recurrence
of cancer between successive follow-ups, and the time to the development
of AIDS in HIV infected subjects who have periodic blood tests. In fact,
most survival analyses are based on interval-censored data, in the sense
that the survival times are often taken as the nearest day, week or
month. In this chapter, methods for summarising and modelling
interval-censored data will be described and illustrated.

:::: section
## 9.1 Interval censoring {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_1 .head1}

Censoring is widely encountered in time-to-event data and occurs when
the end point of a study is not precisely known. This book contains many
examples of right censoring, where the actual time to some event is
greater than that observed. Left censoring occurs far less often, and
here the actual time to an event is known to be less than the observed
time. The time to an event is said to be interval-censored when the
event is only known to have occurred within a time interval.

To illustrate these different kinds of censoring, consider a study of
the age at which heart disease occurs, where individuals over 60 years
old are recruited and examined regularly over some period of time. Some
individuals may not have developed heart disease by the end of the
study, or may have died from other causes. The event times of such
individuals are right-censored. On the other hand, when individuals who
have been recruited are first examined, some may be found to have
already developed heart disease. The event times of these individuals
are then left-censored, as heart disease is only known to have developed
at some point before the initial screening. Other individuals will have
been found to be free of heart disease on one occasion, and to then have
developed the disease by a subsequent examination time. For them, the
time of occurrence is known to be in the interval between the two
examination times, and their event times are interval-censored.

Right and left censoring are in fact special cases of interval
censoring. Consider an event time *t* that is only known to occur within
the time interval from *L* to *U*, where *L* and *U* refer to lower and
upper limits, respectively. This interval will be denoted $(L,U\rbrack$,
which indicates that *t* is such that $L < t\leqslant U$. The interval
is said to be open at the lower limit and closed at the upper limit, and
resolves ambiguity about the interval in which a particular event time
occurs. For example, if time is measured in days and an event is known
to occur between 5 and 10 days, the interval $(5,10\rbrack$ signifies
that the event has occurred after day 5 but on or before day 10. On the
other hand, an event that occurs on or before day 5 will lie within a
time interval with an upper limit of 5 days.

[]{#chapter9.xhtml_p286 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 286. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this notation, if the event time *t* is known
precisely, $L = U$. If $L = t$ and $U = \infty$, so that the time to the
event is only known to exceed *t*, the event time is right-censored. The
upper limit of an interval for a right-censored event time may also be
denoted with an asterisk $(*)$. If $L = 0$ and $U = t$, the event is
known to have occurred by time *t* and so *t* is left-censored. An
observed set of time-to-event data may contain a mix of actual times as
well as right-, left- and interval-censored times.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam9_1" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 9.1]{.boxnum}](#chapter9.xhtml_b9exam9_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Recurrence of an ulcer]{.boxht}

In a double-blind clinical trial to compare treatments for the
inhibition of relapse after primary therapy has healed an ulcer,
patients are randomised to receive one or other of two treatments,
labelled *A* and *B*. Regular visits to a clinic were arranged for the
patients, and endoscopies performed 6 months and 12 months after
randomisation. A positive endoscopy result indicates that an ulcer has
recurred in the time since the last negative result. Information is
therefore obtained on whether or not an ulcer has recurred in the
interval from 0 to 6 months or in the interval from 6 to 12 months.
Additionally, some patients presented at the clinic between scheduled
visits, suffering from symptoms of recurrence. These patients had
endoscopies at these times in order to detect if a recurrence had in
fact occurred.

At entry to the trial, the age of each patient, in years, and the
duration of verified disease (1 = less than five years, 2 = greater than
or equal to five years) was recorded, in addition to the treatment group
(*A* or *B*). There are two variables associated with ulcer detection in
the data set, namely the time of the last visit, in months, and the
result of the endoscopy at that time (1 = no ulcer detected, 2 = ulcer
detected). Those with times other than 6 or 12 months had presented with
symptoms between scheduled visits. The study itself was multinational
and the full set of data is given in [Whitehead
(1989)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib413){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib413 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. In this example, the data from Belgium will be
used, and the relevant data are given in [Table
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_1){#chapter9.xhtml_b9tbl9_1}.

  Patient   Age   Duration   Treatment   Time of Last Visit   Result
  --------- ----- ---------- ----------- -------------------- --------
   1        48    2          *B*          7                   2
   2        73    1          *B*         12                   1
   3        54    1          *B*         12                   1
   4        58    2          *B*         12                   1
   5        56    1          *A*         12                   1
   6        49    2          *A*         12                   1
   7        71    1          *B*         12                   1
   8        41    1          *A*         12                   1
   9        23    1          *B*         12                   1
  10        37    1          *B*          5                   2
  11        38    1          *B*         12                   1
  12        76    2          *B*         12                   1
  13        38    2          *A*         12                   1
  14        27    1          *A*          6                   2
  15        47    1          *B*          6                   2
  16        54    1          *A*          6                   1
  17        38    1          *B*         10                   2
  18        27    2          *B*          7                   2
  19        58    2          *A*         12                   1
  20        75    1          *B*         12                   1
  21        25    1          *A*         12                   1
  22        58    1          *A*         12                   1
  23        63    1          *B*         12                   1
  24        41    1          *A*         12                   1
  25        47    1          *B*         12                   1
  26        58    1          *A*          3                   2
  27        74    2          *A*          2                   2
  28        75    2          *A*          6                   1
  29        72    1          *A*         12                   1
  30        59    1          *B*         12                   2
  31        52    1          *B*         12                   1
  32        75    1          *B*         12                   2
  33        76    1          *A*         12                   1
  34        34    2          *A*          6                   1
  35        36    1          *B*         12                   1
  36        59    1          *B*         12                   1
  37        44    1          *A*         12                   2
  38        28    2          *B*         12                   1
  39        62    1          *B*         12                   1
  40        23    1          *A*         12                   1
  41        49    1          *B*         12                   1
  42        61    1          *A*         12                   1
  43        33    2          *B*         12                   1

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [9.1]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter9.xhtml_b9tbl9_1){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Data on the recurrence of an ulcer following
  treatment for the primary disease]{.table-title} {#tbl9_1}

Once an ulcer is detected by endoscopy, a patient is treated for this
and is then no longer in the study. There were some patients who either
did not have an endoscopy six months after trial entry, or who dropped
out after a negative unscheduled endoscopy in the first six months.
These patients have been omitted from the data set on the grounds that
there is no information about whether an ulcer has recurred in the first
six months of the study. This means that those patients in [Table
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_1) whose last visit was greater than 6 months
after randomisation would have had a negative endoscopy at 6 months.

In modelling the data from this study, duration of disease is denoted by
an indicator variable *Duration*, which is zero when the duration is
less than five years and unity otherwise. The treatment effect is
denoted by a variable *Treat*, which takes the value zero if an
individual is on treatment *A* and unity if on treatment *B*. The
patients\' age is reflected in the continuous variate *Age*.

Data on the timing and result of the last visit in the last two columns
of [Table 9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_1) can be transformed to give
interval-censored times to ulcer recurrence. [Table
9.2](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_2){#chapter9.xhtml_b9tbl9_2} shows the
interval-censored times resulting from the various combinations of
values of the time and result of the most recent endoscopy. Event times
with an infinite upper bound are right-censored.

  Time of Last Visit   Result   Interval Censored Times
  -------------------- -------- -------------------------
   2                   2        (0, 2\]
   3                   2        (0, 3\]
   5                   2        (0, 5\]
   6                   1         (6, ∞\]
   6                   2        (0, 6\]
   7                   2        (6, 7\]
  10                   2          (6,10\]
  12                   1        (12, ∞\]
  12                   2         (6, 12\]

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [9.2]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter9.xhtml_b9tbl9_2){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Interval-censored data corresponding to the
  different combinations of the two variables in the final two columns
  of [Table 9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_1). Times with an infinite upper
  bound to the time interval are right-censored]{.table-title} {#tbl9_2}

After transforming the data in this way, they are now in the form of
interval-censored data.

One way of analysing such data is to simply focus on the times of a
detected recurrence. However, the data are a mixture of recurrences
detected at scheduled screening times, known as *screen-detected
recurrences* and recurrences diagnosed following the occurrence of
symptoms or *interval-detected recurrences*. This leads to a difficulty
in interpreting the results of the analysis. To illustrate the problem,
suppose that both treatments in this example have exactly the same
effect on the recurrence time, but that the new treatment suppresses
symptoms. The recurrence of an ulcer in a patient on the new treatment
will then tend to be detected later than that in a patient on the
standard treatment. Interval-detected recurrences will then be
identified sooner in a patient on the standard treatment and will
[]{#chapter9.xhtml_p287 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 287. Table on this page. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#chapter9.xhtml_p288 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 288. " role="doc-pagebreak"}then be shorter for this
group of patients. This would indicate an erroneous advantage of the new
treatment over the standard.

</aside>

In some circumstances it may be justifiable to ignore interval censoring
and use the upper or lower limit of the time interval in a standard
analysis. This can be satisfactory when the time intervals are quite
small relative to study duration. Another way of adjusting the
interval-censored data so as to permit standard analyses is to impute a
value within each observed time interval. For this, the midpoint of the
interval could be used, or more sophisticated multiple imputation
methods. However, these methods can lead to biased inference, and
methods developed specifically for the analysis of interval-censored
data are preferred.

::: section
### 9.1.1 Current status data {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_1_1 .head2}

A special case of interval-censored data arises when there is just one
observation time, and it is only known whether or not each individual in
the study has experienced some event by that time. Data of this type are
known as *current status data*. Individuals who have already experienced
the event have a left-censored event time, whereas those for whom the
event has yet to occur have right-censored times. Consequently, all the
observations in the resulting data set are either left-censored or
right-censored. Using the methods of analysis described in this chapter,
an estimate of the corresponding survivor function and associated
summary statistics can be obtained. Moreover, a modelling approach to
the analysis of interval-censored data can be used to identify factors
that may be associated with the event time in current status data.

To illustrate current status data, consider a carcinogenicity study in
which laboratory animals are exposed to a certain chemical and the time
to the occurrence of a tumour is of interest. The animals are sacrificed
30 days after the exposure and subsequently examined to determine
whether or not a tumour is present. Animals who are found to have a
tumour give rise to left-censored event times of 30 days, while animals
in whom a tumour is absent have right-censored event times of 30 days.
Percentiles of the distribution of time to tumour development can then
found from the estimated survivor function. Similarly, in
epidemiological studies to determine the age at menarche, the onset of
menstruation, data are recorded on whether or not menarche has occurred
in women who have been admitted to the study. The resulting data might
be summarised using the median age at menarche, and the dependence of
age at menarche on factors such as socioeconomic status can be assessed.
:::
::::

::: section
## 9.2 []{#chapter9.xhtml_p289 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 289. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Estimating the survivor function {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_2 .head1}

We saw in [Chapter 2](#chapter2.xhtml) how the survivor function of data
that may be right-censored can be estimated using either the
Kaplan-Meier or Nelson-Aalen estimates. These are step functions where
the estimated survivor function is constant between adjacent event
times, and only changes at the event times themselves. When a data set
includes interval-censored event times, the estimated survivor function
is also a step function, but this estimate is not defined within certain
time intervals, known as *Turnbull intervals*.

To determine the Turnbull intervals, all lower and upper boundary values
of interval-censored event times are arranged in ascending order and
labelled as *L* or *U* according to whether they are lower or upper
limits of the time intervals. Intervals with a *L* as a lower limit and
a *U* an upper limit are then identified. These are then the Turnbull
intervals and will then be such that there are no other lower or upper
limits of interval-censored times between them.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam9_2" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 9.2]{.boxnum}](#chapter9.xhtml_b9exam9_2){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Artificial interval-censored data]{.boxht}

To illustrate the construction of Turnbull intervals, suppose that the
event times of five individuals consist of one left-censored time, one
right-censored event time and three times that are interval-censored.
The observations in interval-censored format are as follows:

$$\begin{array}{r}
{(3,6\rbrack\,\,\,(0,7\rbrack\,\,\,(4,9\rbrack\,\,\,(8,10\rbrack\,\,\,(14,\infty\rbrack}
\end{array}$$

Here, the event time of the second observation, 7, is left-censored and
the interval has a lower limit of zero. The final observation of 14 is
right-censored and so has an infinite upper limit to the event time. The
ordered values of the interval boundaries are then:

$$\begin{array}{r}
{0\,(L)\,\,\, 3\,(L)\,\,\, 4\,(L)\,\,\, 6\,(U)\,\,\, 7\,(U)\,\,\, 8\,(L)\,\,\, 9\,(U)\,\,\, 10\,(U)\,\,\, 14\,(L)\,\,\,\infty\,(U)}
\end{array}$$

There are three time intervals where an *L* is followed by a *U*, namely
$(4,6\rbrack$, $(8,9\rbrack$ and $(14,\infty\rbrack$. These are the
Turnbull intervals over which the survivor function is not defined.
Notice that as with the Kaplan-Meier estimate, the survivor function for
this set of five observations is not defined beyond the longest event
time of 14 since this is right-censored.

</aside>

To estimate the survivor function, suppose that we have a sample of *n*
interval-censored event times in the form $(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, where the *i*th interval includes the upper limit,
*u*~*i*~, but not the lower limit, *l*~*i*~. If the *i*th observation is
known precisely, $l_{i} = u_{i}$, while if it is right-censored,
$u_{i} = \infty$, and if left-censored, $l_{i} = 0$. For an exact event
time, where $l_{i} = u_{i}$, the corresponding interval is
infinitesimal, leading to a step in the survivor function at this time.
This notation also resolves any ambiguity in identifying Turnbull
intervals when a data set includes intervals where the lower limit of
one is the same as the upper limit of another. Here, the lower limit is
at a time that is immediately after the upper limit and so is placed
after the upper limit when determining Turnbull intervals.

The estimated survivor function is constant between Turnbull intervals
and is derived from the probability, *θ*~*j*~, that an event time *t* is
in the *j*th of *m* Turnbull intervals, denoted $(a_{j},b_{j}\rbrack$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,m$. The estimated values of *θ*~*j*~,
${\hat{\theta}}_{j}$, are then the solution of the *m* simultaneous
equations

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\theta_{j} = \frac{1}{n}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\frac{\alpha_{ij}\theta_{j}}{{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}}},}
\end{array}$$[(9.1)]{.equationnum}

where $\alpha_{ij} = 1$ if the *j*th Turnbull interval
$(a_{j},b_{j}\rbrack$ is contained in *i*th observed interval
$(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and zero otherwise. An
outline of the derivation of this result is given in [Section
9.2.1](#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_2_1).

The estimated survivor function for a sample of interval-censored event
times is constant between Turnbull intervals and is not defined within
them, where it changes value. []{#chapter9.xhtml_p290 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 290. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Accordingly, the estimated
survivor function can be expressed as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{S}(t) = {\sum\limits_{j:b_{j} > t}{\hat{\theta}}_{j}},}
\end{array}$$[(9.2)]{.equationnum}

for a value of *t* that does not occur within a Turnbull interval, and
the summation is over all $\hat{\theta}$ s for which the upper limits of
the Turnbull intervals exceed *t*. This estimate, which is due to
[Turnbull (1976)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib393){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib393
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, is often referred to as *Turnbull\'s
estimate* of the survivor function. Equivalently, this estimate can be
written

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\hat{S}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
1 & {\text{if~}t\leqslant a_{1},} \\
