{1 - {\hat{\theta}}_{1} - {\hat{\theta}}_{2} - \cdots - {\hat{\theta}}_{j}} & {\text{if~}b_{j}\leqslant t\leqslant a_{j + 1},\, j = 1,2,\ldots,m - 1,} \\
0 & {\text{if~}t\geqslant b_{m},}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

for values of *t* that are not within Turnbull intervals.
:::

::: section
## 9.2.1 Derivation of the estimated survivor function {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_2_1 .head1i}

Suppose that we have a sample of *n* interval-censored event times
$(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and that the survivor
function at time *t* is $S(t)$. Since $S(l_{i}) - S(u_{i})$ is the
probability that the *i*th event time is contained in the interval
$(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$, the likelihood function of the *n*
interval-censored event times is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\lbrack {S(l_{i}) - S(u_{i})} \right\rbrack}.}
\end{array}$$[(9.3)]{.equationnum}

This likelihood can be expressed in a different form. Suppose that the
lower and upper time limits of each interval are arranged in ascending
order to give the set of unique values
$\tau_{0},\tau_{1},\tau_{2},\ldots,\tau_{r}$, which are such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\tau_{0} < \tau_{1} < \tau_{2} < \cdots < \tau_{r - 1} < \tau_{r},}
\end{array}$$

where $\tau_{0} = 0$ and $\tau_{r} = \infty$. Let *θ*~*j*~ be the
probability that an event time occurs within the *j*th interval
$(\tau_{j - 1},\tau_{j}\rbrack$ so that
$\theta_{j} = S(\tau_{j - 1}) - S(\tau_{j})$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,r$.

Also, set $\alpha_{ij} = 1$ if the *j*th interval
$(\tau_{j - 1},\tau_{j}\rbrack$ is contained in *i*th observed interval
$(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and zero otherwise. Then, the
likelihood function in Equation (9.3) can be written

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}} \right\}},}
\end{array}$$

which is maximised subject to the constraints
$0\leqslant\theta_{j}\leqslant 1$ and
${\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\theta_{j}} = 1$.

The corresponding log-likelihood function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\log}\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and to maximise this subject to the constraint
${\sum\limits_{j}\theta_{j}} = 1$, a *Lagrange multiplier* *λ* is
introduced. This involves adding the term
$\lambda({\sum\limits_{j}\theta_{j}} - 1)$ to the log-likelihood
function to give

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L_{0} = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\log}\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}} \right\} + \lambda\left( {{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\theta_{j}} - 1} \right),}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter9.xhtml_p291 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 291. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}which in view of the constraint does not change the
value of the log-likelihood function.

Differentiating $\log L_{0}$ with respect to *θ*~*j*~ and setting the
derivative to zero gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\frac{\alpha_{ij}}{{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}}} + \lambda = 0,}
\end{array}$$

and multiplying both sides by *θ*~*j*~ gives the equation

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\frac{\alpha_{ij}\theta_{j}}{{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}}} + \lambda\theta_{j} = 0.}
\end{array}$$[(9.4)]{.equationnum}

Summation over *j* and using ${\sum\limits_{j}\theta_{j}} = 1$ then
leads to ${\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}1} + \lambda = 0$, from which
$\lambda = - n$. Substituting for *λ* in Equation (9.4) then gives

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\frac{\alpha_{ij}\theta_{j}}{{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}}} - n\theta_{j} = 0,}
\end{array}$$

which leads to the set of equations with

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\theta_{j} = \frac{1}{n}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\frac{\alpha_{ij}\theta_{j}}{{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}}}.}
\end{array}$$

In fact,

$$\begin{array}{r}
\frac{\alpha_{ij}\theta_{j}}{{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\alpha_{ij}}\theta_{j}}
\end{array}$$

is the probability that an event time *t* in the interval
$(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$ occurs within the *j*th Turnbull interval, and
equating *θ*~*j*~ to the mean of these values gives the same result.

Since *θ*~*j*~ is only non-zero in the *m* Turnbull intervals, the
non-zero values are relabelled to give
$\theta_{1},\theta_{2},\ldots,\theta_{m}$, so that now *θ*~*j*~ is the
probability that an event time is contained in the *j*th Turnbull
interval, $(a_{j},b_{j}\rbrack$, for $j = 1,2,\ldots,m$. This is the
result in Equation (9.1).

This estimate is derived from a likelihood function without having to
specify a probability distribution for the survival times, and so it is
a *Non-Parametric Maximum Likelihood Estimator* (NPMLE). Also, this
estimation process stems from an expectation-maximisation (EM) algorithm
for obtaining the maximum likelihood estimate of *θ*~*j*~.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam9_3" role="doc-tip">

[Example 9.3]{.boxnum} [Artificial interval-censored data]{.boxht}

In this example, the calculations needed to obtain Turnbull\'s estimate
of a survivor function are illustrated using the artificial data from
[Example 9.2](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_2){#chapter9.xhtml_b9exam9_2}, where
the intervals are

$$\begin{array}{r}
{(3,6\rbrack\,\,\,(0,7\rbrack\,\,\,(4,9\rbrack\,\,\,(8,10\rbrack\,\,\,(14,\infty\rbrack}
\end{array}$$

In [Example 9.2](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_2), the Turnbull intervals were
found to be $(4,6\rbrack$, $(8,9\rbrack$ and $(14,\infty\rbrack$, and
the estimated survivor function is constant between these three
intervals.

[Table 9.3](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_3){#chapter9.xhtml_b9tbl9_3} gives the
values of $\alpha_{ij}$ for the intervals $(a_{j},b_{j}\rbrack$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,3$, which are unity if the interval
$(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$ includes $(a_{j},b_{j}\rbrack$ and zero otherwise.
The equations satisfied by the three non-zero *θ*-values,
${\hat{\theta}}_{1}$, ${\hat{\theta}}_{2}$ and ${\hat{\theta}}_{3}$, can
be obtained from Equation (9.1). For example,

+---------+-----------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Value   | Interval              | Values of $\alpha_{ij}$                                                       |
| of *j*  | $(a_{j},b_{j}\rbrack$ |                                                                               |
|         |                       +---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+
|         |                       | $\alpha_{1j}$ | $\alpha_{2j}$ | $\alpha_{3j}$ | $\alpha_{4j}$ | $\alpha_{5j}$ |
+=========+=======================+===============+===============+===============+===============+===============+
| 1       | $(4,6\rbrack$         | 1             | 1             | 1             | 0             | 0             |
+---------+-----------------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+
| 2       | $(8,9\rbrack$         | 0             | 0             | 1             | 1             | 0             |
+---------+-----------------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+
| 3       |  $(14,\infty\rbrack$  | 0             | 0             | 0             | 0             | 1             |
+---------+-----------------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+

: [[[Table]{.label}
[9.3]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter9.xhtml_b9tbl9_3){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Values of $\alpha_{ij}$ for use in calculating the
estimated survivor function for the artificial data set]{.table-title}
{#tbl9_3}

$$\begin{aligned}
{\hat{\theta}}_{1} & {= \frac{1}{5}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{5}\frac{\alpha_{i1}{\hat{\theta}}_{1}}{\alpha_{i1}{\hat{\theta}}_{1} + \alpha_{i2}{\hat{\theta}}_{2} + \alpha_{i3}{\hat{\theta}}_{3}}},} \\
 & {= \frac{1}{5}\left\{ {\frac{{\hat{\theta}}_{1}}{{\hat{\theta}}_{1}} + \frac{{\hat{\theta}}_{1}}{{\hat{\theta}}_{1}} + \frac{{\hat{\theta}}_{1}}{{\hat{\theta}}_{1} + {\hat{\theta}}_{2}}} \right\},}
\end{aligned}$$

[]{#chapter9.xhtml_p292 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 292. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}which simplifies to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\theta}}_{1} = \frac{1}{5}\left( \frac{3{\hat{\theta}}_{1} + 2{\hat{\theta}}_{2}}{{\hat{\theta}}_{1} + {\hat{\theta}}_{2}} \right).}
\end{array}$$

Similarly,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\theta}}_{2} = \frac{1}{5}\left( \frac{{\hat{\theta}}_{1} + 2{\hat{\theta}}_{2}}{{\hat{\theta}}_{1} + {\hat{\theta}}_{2}} \right),}
\end{array}$$

and the expression for ${\hat{\theta}}_{3}$ leads directly to
${\hat{\theta}}_{3} = 0.2$. The equations for ${\hat{\theta}}_{1}$ and
${\hat{\theta}}_{2}$ can be solved to give
${\hat{\theta}}_{1} = 0.5333$, $\theta_{2} = 0.2667$, and
${\hat{\theta}}_{1}$, ${\hat{\theta}}_{2}$, ${\hat{\theta}}_{3}$ satisfy
the constraint that they sum to unity.

The corresponding estimate of the survivor function is constant over
intervals where ${\hat{\theta}}_{j} = 0$, and undefined over intervals
where ${\hat{\theta}}_{j} > 0$. This estimate is found from Equation
(9.2), which leads to

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\hat{S}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
1.000 & {\,\,\,\, 0\leqslant t\leqslant 4,} \\
0.478 & {\,\,\,\, 6\leqslant t\leqslant 8,} \\
0.200 & {\,\,\,\, 9\leqslant t\leqslant 14,}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

and $\hat{S}(t)$ is undefined beyond $t = 14$. A graph of this estimate
of the survivor function for the artificial data is shown in [Figure
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_fig9_1){#chapter9.xhtml_b9fig9_1}.

<figure id="fig9_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig9_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter9.xhtml_b9fig9_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">9.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated survivor function for artificial
interval-censored data. The Turnbull intervals are
shaded.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#chapter9.xhtml_p293 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 293. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this figure, the Turnbull intervals are shaded
and the survivor function estimates joined by straight lines, even
though they are not defined within these intervals.

</aside>

Although it was possible to obtain estimates of the *θ*s in this simple
example without using numerical methods, this is rarely the case.
Moreover, the EM algorithm that underpins the estimation process is
often slow to converge. Alternative algorithms have therefore been
proposed, including the Iterative Convex Minorant (ICM) algorithm and
the EMICM algorithm, which is a combination of the ICM and EM
algorithms. In addition, the usual methods for obtaining standard errors
of an estimated survivor function cannot be used. This is because the
number of unknown *θ* parameters inevitably increases as the sample size
increases, which means that likelihood-based methods for obtaining
standard errors, described in [Appendix A](#appA.xhtml_appA), are no
longer applicable. Instead, resampling schemes based on the bootstrap
are widely used. This process was outlined in [Section
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml).

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam9_4" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 9.4]{.boxnum}](#chapter17.xhtml_b9exam9_4){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Recurrence of an ulcer]{.boxht}

For the data on times to recurrence of an ulcer, given in [Example
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_1){#chapter9.xhtml_b9exam9_1}, the survivor
function, $S(t)$, represents the probability of a recurrence after any
given time, *t*. For both treatments, the estimated survivor function,
$\hat{S}(t)$, is unity when $t = 0$ and undefined beyond 12 months. For
patients on treatment *A*, $\hat{S}(t)$ is 0.842 for
$2\leqslant t\leqslant 6$ and 0.777 when $t = 12$ months. For those on
treatment *B*$\hat{S}(t)$ is 0.917 for $5\leqslant t\leqslant 6$ and
0.708 and $7\leqslant t\leqslant 12$. The estimated survivor functions
are undefined elsewhere and are shown in [Figure
9.2](#chapter9.xhtml_fig9_2){#chapter9.xhtml_b9fig9_2}.

<figure id="fig9_2" class="image">
<img src="images/fig9_2.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter9.xhtml_b9fig9_2" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">9.2</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated survivor functions for patients
assigned to treatment <em>A</em> (—) and treatment <em>B</em> (—) for
data on the time to the recurrence of an ulcer. The Turnbull intervals
are shaded.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

These estimates do not differ by much, suggesting that the two
treatments have a similar impact on the time to ulcer recurrence. By
modelling the interval-censored data, we will be able to formally
examine the significance of any treatment difference.

</aside>
:::

::::: section
## 9.3 Semi-parametric proportional hazards models {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_3 .head1}

Models for interval-censored time-to-event data enable the impact of
factors and explanatory variables on the event time to be evaluated and
summarised. As with right-censored data, models are based on the hazard
of death at any given time and can be semi-parametric or
[]{#chapter9.xhtml_p294 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 294. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}fully parametric. In this section, semi-parametric
proportional hazards models are described, where no assumption is made
about the underlying distribution of the event times.

In a proportional hazards model, the hazard of death at time *t* for the
*i*th of *n* individuals is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $\beta'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is a linear combination of values
of explanatory variables for the *i*th individual,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, and $h_{0}(t)$ is the baseline hazard
function. The corresponding survivor function at time *t* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = S_{0}{(t)}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})},}
\end{array}$$

where $S_{0}(t)$ is the baseline survivor function, with $S_{0}(0) = 1$
and $S_{0}(\infty) = 0$.

Fitting a Cox regression model to survival data that may be
right-censored involves constructing a partial likelihood function that
does not involve $h_{0}(t)$, and maximising this to obtain estimates of
the *β*-parameters. This is not possible for interval-censored data, and
the *β*s and $h_{0}(t)$ need to be estimated simultaneously from a full
likelihood function. For a sample of *n* interval-censored event times
in the form $(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$, for $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, the full
likelihood function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}P}(l_{i} < T\leqslant u_{i}) = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {S_{i}(l_{i}) - S_{i}(u_{i})} \right\}},}
\end{array}$$[(9.5)]{.equationnum}

as in Equation (9.3). The contribution to this likelihood is
$S_{i}(l_{i})$ for a right-censored time, $1 - S_{i}(u_{i})$ for a
left-censored time and $f(t_{i}) = - \text{d}S_{i}(t_{i})/\text{d}t$ for
an uncensored event time, where $l_{i} = u_{i}$.

::: section
### 9.3.1 Semi-parametric Turnbull model {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_3_1 .head2}

One possible semi-parametric model is formed by taking the baseline
survivor function to be the NPMLE based on Turnbull intervals. In this
model, which is known as the *semi-parametric Turnbull model*, the
hazard, cumulative hazard and survivor functions are all constant
between adjacent Turnbull intervals. Specifically, if a set of
interval-censored event times leads to *m* Turnbull intervals
$(a_{j},b_{j}\rbrack$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,m$, then $S_{0}(t)$ is constant
for values of *t* in the intervals
$(0,a_{1}\rbrack,(b_{1},a_{2}\rbrack,\ldots,(b_{m - 1},a_{m}\rbrack,(b_{m},\infty\rbrack$.
The baseline survivor function for values of *t* in the *k*th of *r*
such intervals is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{0}(t) = \zeta_{k},\,\,\, k = 1,2,\ldots,r,}
\end{array}$$

for values of *t* in the *k*th interval between two consecutive Turnbull
intervals. The corresponding baseline cumulative hazard in the *k*th
interval is $- \log(\zeta_{k})$ and the hazard function can be formed
from increments in the cumulative hazard.

This model generalises the NPMLE of a survivor function to a regression
model for interval-censored data and is the analogue of a Cox regression
model for right-censored data. The full likelihood function for *n*
interval-censored observations, $(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$, is, from Equation
(9.5),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {S_{0}{(l_{i})}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})} - S_{0}{(u_{i})}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})}} \right\}},}
\end{array}$$[(9.6)]{.equationnum}

and the model is fitted by maximising this likelihood function. As for
the NPMLE of the survivor function, the EMICM algorithm is generally
used to fit this model, and the fitting process is implemented in a
number of software packages. Simulation methods such as bootstrapping
are used to obtain standard errors of the parameter estimates, described
in connection with the piecewise exponential model in [Section
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml). Although
right-censored data can be expressed as interval-censored event times,
parameter estimates []{#chapter9.xhtml_p295 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 295. " role="doc-pagebreak"}on fitting the
semi-parametric Turnbull model will not be the same as those found on
fitting a Cox model. This is because the fitting procedure is based on a
full rather than a partial likelihood function.

Once a model has been fitted, the quantity $- 2\log\hat{L}$, where
$\log\hat{L}$ is the maximised value of the log-likelihood function from
Equation (9.6) can be used to compare alternative models in the usual
way. In summary, differences in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ values are compared
with percentage points of a chi-squared distribution with degrees of
freedom equal to the difference in the number of *β*-parameters between
alternative nested models. For non-nested models, the *AIC* or *BIC*
statistics described in [Section 3.6.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_6_1) of
[Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml) may be used in model comparison.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam9_5" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 9.5]{.boxnum}](#chapter9.xhtml_b9exam9_5){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Recurrence of an ulcer]{.boxht}

Data on the recurrence times of an ulcer, given in [Example
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_1), are now used to illustrate fitting a
semi-parametric Turnbull model to interval-censored data. The model to
be fitted has a baseline survivor function that is constant between
adjacent Turnbull intervals and so the first step is to determine these
intervals. Using [Table 9.2](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_2) to place the limits
of the interval-censored observations in ascending order, there are
three Turnbull intervals, namely $(0,2\rbrack$, $(6,7\rbrack$ and
$(12,\infty\rbrack$. This means that there are just two parameters in
the baseline survivor function, corresponding to the time intervals
$(2,6\rbrack$ and $(7,12\rbrack$.

On fitting a model containing the variables corresponding to age,
duration and treatment, *Age, Duration* and *Treat* defined in [Example
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_1), the value of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$
statistic is 62.566. The corresponding value on fitting *Treat* alone is
62.884. As the difference of 0.32 is very small relative to percentage
points of a chi-squared distribution on 2 d.f., we conclude that neither
age nor treatment duration affect the time to recurrence of an ulcer in
these patients. In fact, $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for a null model is 62.982 and
so there is no significant treatment effect. However, this term will be
retained in the model so that the size of the effect can be estimated.

The estimated log-hazard ratio for patients on treatment *B* relative to
those on treatment *A* is 0.195 and the corresponding hazard ratio is
1.216. The standard error of the log-hazard ratio is estimated from
10,000 bootstrap samples. This involves simulating 10,000 data sets
where each is formed by randomly selecting 43 observations with
replacement from the data given in [Table 9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_1).
The semi-parametric Turnbull model containing *Treat* alone is then
fitted to each, leading to 10,000 estimates of the log-hazard ratio for
*Treat*. The standard deviation of these values is then a bootstrap
estimate of the standard error. The standard error is found to be 1.488,
and so a 95% confidence interval for the hazard ratio is
$\exp(0.195 \pm 1.96 \times 1.488)$, that is the interval from 0.066 to
22.442. Treatment *B* is estimated to lead to a 22% increase in the risk
of an ulcer recurrence, but this difference is not significant and the
corresponding interval estimate for the hazard ratio includes unity.

The estimated survivor functions for patients on each treatment are
given in [Table 9.4](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_4){#chapter9.xhtml_b9tbl9_4}.
The survivor function for a patient on Treatment *A* is the estimated
baseline survivor function, ${\hat{S}}_{0}(t)$, and the survivor
functions are not defined in the intervals $(0,2\rbrack$, $(6,7\rbrack$
and beyond 12 months. The difference in survivor functions is very
small, but does indicate that []{#chapter9.xhtml_p296 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 296. " role="doc-pagebreak"}there is a slight, though
non significant, increase in the probability of an ulcer recurring in
patients on treatment *B*.

+----------------+---------------------------------+
| Interval       | Estimated Survivor Function     |
+----------------+----------------+----------------+
|                | Treatment *A*  | Treatment *B*  |
+================+================+================+
| $(0,0\rbrack$  | 1.000          | 1.000          |
+----------------+----------------+----------------+
| $(2,6\rbrack$  | 0.896          | 0.875          |
+----------------+----------------+----------------+
| $(7,12\rbrack$ | 0.759          | 0.715          |
+----------------+----------------+----------------+

: [[[Table]{.label}
[9.4]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter9.xhtml_b9tbl9_4){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survivor function estimates from a semi-parametric
Turnbull model fitted to the data on ulcer recurrence]{.table-title}
{#tbl9_4}

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 9.3.2 Piecewise exponential model for interval-censored data {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_3_2 .head2}

An alternative semi-parametric model assumes that the baseline hazard
function is constant within each of a number of consecutive time
intervals, irrespective of the position of Turnbull intervals. This
leads to the piecewise exponential model described in [Section
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml) in which

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{0}(t) = \lambda_{j},}
\end{array}$$

for an event time *t* in the *j*th of *r* time intervals, where
$t_{(j - 1)}\leqslant t < t_{(j)}$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,r$, and
$t_{(0)} = 0$. For right-censored data, the intervals can be taken to be
such that there are approximately equal numbers of event times in each
interval. However, this is not possible for interval-censored data,
where the event times themselves are not observed. Instead, intervals
may be chosen to have similar numbers of unique boundary values in each
interval, or to cover similar time periods.

The corresponding baseline cumulative hazard function is, from Equation
(6.3) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{H_{0}(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\lambda_{j}}\Delta_{j}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(9.7)]{.equationnum}

where

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\Delta_{j}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
0 & {\,\,\, t < t_{(j - 1)},} \\
{t - t_{(j - 1)}} & {\,\,\, t_{(j - 1)}\leqslant t < t_{(j)},} \\
{t_{(j)} - t_{(j - 1)}} & {\,\,\, t\geqslant t_{(j)},}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

and the baseline survivor function is
$S_{0}(t) = \exp\left\{ - H_{0}(t) \right\}$. The likelihood function is
the same as that in Equation (9.6) and this is maximised over the *β*s
and the *λ* parameters in $h_{0}(t)$. In this formulation of the hazard
function, $\lambda_{j} > 0$, and so it is often parameterised by setting
$\phi = \log\lambda$ to ensure this. As usual, nested models are
compared using the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic, while non-nested models
are compared using the *AIC* or *BIC* statistic.

This proportional hazards model can be extended to allow coefficients of
the explanatory variables to differ between the chosen time intervals.
The difference in the value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ between these two models
then leads to a test of proportional hazards.

Although the piecewise exponential model can be fitted to
interval-censored data using the Newton-Raphson procedure described in
[Section 3.3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3_3) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml), faster efficient methods, such as the EMICM
algorithm are more commonly used. However, since the estimated survivor
function is undefined within a Turnbull interval, estimates based on
time intervals that overlap with these may not be unique. When the
number of intervals is relatively small, likelihood methods summarised
in [Appendix A](#appA.xhtml_appA) lead to the standard error of
parameter estimates, although bootstrap methods may be used instead.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam9_6" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 9.6]{.boxnum}](#chapter9.xhtml_b9exam9_6){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Recurrence of an ulcer]{.boxht}

In this example, a piecewise exponential model is fitted to the data on
the recurrence times of an ulcer first given in [Example
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_1). Since endoscopies to detect ulcer
recurrence were scheduled to take place 6 and 12 months after primary
treatment, it is natural to use the time intervals $(0,6\rbrack$ and
$(6,12\rbrack$ in a piecewise exponential model. The baseline hazard
function is then *λ*~1~ for $0\leqslant t < 6$ and *λ*~2~ for
$6\leqslant t < 12$. On fitting a piecewise exponential proportional
hazards model with the terms *Age, Duration* and *Treat*,
$- 2\log\hat{L} = 73.889$, and this increases to 74.258 when *Age* and
*Duration* are omitted. The estimated coefficient of *Treat* in this
model is 0.209, so that the hazard of a relapse on treatment *B*
relative to treatment *A* is 1.232 with a 95% confidence interval
ranging from 0.360 to 4.213. This []{#chapter9.xhtml_p297 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 297. " role="doc-pagebreak"}interval includes unity,
again indicating no significant treatment effect. The estimated values
of the parameters in the baseline hazard function are
${\hat{\lambda}}_{1} = 0.018$ and ${\hat{\lambda}}_{2} = 0.028$ in the
two intervals. The corresponding estimate of the cumulative hazard
function is found using Equation (9.7) which leads to

$$\begin{array}{l}
{{\hat{H}}_{0}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
{{\hat{\lambda}}_{1}t} & {\,\,\, 0\leqslant t < 6,} \\
{{\hat{\lambda}}_{2}t + 6({\hat{\lambda}}_{1} - {\hat{\lambda}}_{2})} & {\,\,\, 6\leqslant t < 12,} \\
{6({\hat{\lambda}}_{1} + {\hat{\lambda}}_{2})} & {\,\,\, t\geqslant 12,}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

and the estimated baseline survivor function is then
${\hat{S}}_{0}(t) = \exp\left\{ - {\hat{H}}_{0}(t) \right\}$. The
estimated survivor functions for patients on treatment *A* and treatment
*B* are shown in [Figure
9.3](#chapter9.xhtml_fig9_3){#chapter9.xhtml_b9fig9_3}.

<figure id="fig9_3" class="image">
<img src="images/fig9_3.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter9.xhtml_b9fig9_3" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">9.3</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated survivor functions on fitting a
piecewise exponential model for patients assigned to treatment
<em>A</em> (—) and treatment <em>B</em> (- - -).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The estimated survivor function is again slightly greater for patients
on treatment *A*, indicating that the time to recurrence of an ulcer is
a little longer for this group. The semi-parametric model with *Treat*
alone, fitted in [Example
9.5](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_5){#chapter9.xhtml_b9exam9_5}, and the
piecewise exponential model in this example, have three unknown
parameters, and so the value of the *AIC* statistic for these two models
is 68.884 and 80.258, respectively. The semi-parametric Turnbull is
therefore a better fit to the observed data.

Extending the model to have a different treatment effect within each
time interval, the model becomes

$$\begin{array}{l}
{h_{i}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
{\exp(\beta_{1}x_{i})\,\lambda_{1}} & {\,\,\, 0\leqslant t < 6,} \\
{\exp(\beta_{2}x_{i})\,\lambda_{2}} & {\,\,\, 6\leqslant t < 12,}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

where $x_{i} = 0$ if the *i*th of *n* patients is on treatment *A* and
unity otherwise. On fitting this model, $- 2\log\hat{L} = 71.821$, so
that the reduction in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ associated with having a
different treatment effect in each time interval is 2.437 on 1 d.f.,
with a *P*-value of 0.119. The estimated hazard ratio for treatment *B*
relative to treatment *A* is 0.415 in the first interval and 3.459 in
the second. Although this difference is only significant at the 12%
level, there is a suggestion that the treatment effect is greater
between 6 and 12 months after primary treatment for an ulcer.

</aside>
:::
:::::

::: section
## 9.4 []{#chapter9.xhtml_p298 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 298. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Parametric models {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_4 .head1}

The semi-parametric models described in the previous section lead to
discontinuous estimates of the hazard and survivor functions, which is
unrealistic in a practical setting. However, smooth estimates of these
functions can be obtained using the fully parametric models described in
[Chapters 5](#chapter5.xhtml) and [6](#chapter6.xhtml). It is also
straightforward to fit these models to interval-censored survival data.

In a fully parametric proportional hazards model, the hazard and
survivor functions for the *i*th individual, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, are
again given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t),\,\,\,\,\, S_{i}(t) = S_{0}{(t)}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})},}
\end{array}$$

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is a vector of values of *p* explanatory
variables, and $h_{0}(t)$, $S_{0}(t)$ are the baseline hazard and
survivor functions for which the components of $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$
are all zero. For the Weibull proportional hazards model, described in
[Section 5.6](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_6) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml),
$h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$ and
$S_{0}(t) = \exp( - \lambda t^{\gamma})$. This model can be fitted to
*n* interval-censored event times, $(l_{i},u_{i}\rbrack$,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, by maximising the likelihood function given in
Equation (9.6). For the Weibull model, this is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {{\lbrack\exp( - \lambda l_{i}^{\gamma})\rbrack}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})} - {\lbrack\exp( - \lambda u_{i}^{\gamma})\rbrack}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})}} \right\}},}
\end{array}$$[(9.8)]{.equationnum}

and the values $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$, $\hat{\lambda}$ and
$\hat{\gamma}$ that maximise this can be found using numerical methods,
such as the Newton-Raphson procedure described in [Section
3.3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3_3) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). The
standard errors of the parameter estimates are also found using the
results in [Appendix A](#appA.xhtml_appA). Writing $\hat{L}$ for the
maximised likelihood function under the fitted model, the statistic
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ can be used to compare alternative nested models.

Accelerated failure time models, also described in [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), allow models with a number of distributions for the
event times to be fitted, including a Weibull model. The general
accelerated failure time model has

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log T_{i} = \mu + \mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \sigma\epsilon_{i},}
\end{array}$$

where *ϵ*~*i*~ has a distribution that is such that *T*~*i*~ has a
Weibull, log-logistic or lognormal distribution, the *α*-parameters are
the coefficients of the explanatory variables in the model and *μ, σ*
are location and scale parameters. The likelihood function for *n*
interval-censored event times is again that given in Equation (9.5),
where, using Equation (5.65),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = S_{\epsilon_{i}}\left( \frac{\log t - \mu - \alpha_{1}x_{1i} - \alpha_{2}x_{2i} - \cdots - \alpha_{p}x_{pi}}{\sigma} \right),}
\end{array}$$

and *t* is taken to be *l*~*i*~ and *u*~*i*~. The function
$S_{\epsilon_{i}}( \cdot )$ corresponds to the chosen distribution of
the event times. For the Weibull AFT model,
$S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon) = \exp( - e^{\epsilon})$, and the resulting
likelihood function is equivalent to that in Equation (9.8), where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\lambda = \exp( - \mu/\sigma),\quad\gamma = \sigma^{- 1},\quad\beta_{j} = - \alpha_{j}/\sigma,}
\end{array}$$

for $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$. Full details are given in [Section
5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_11) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml).

These accelerated failure time models are fitted by maximising the
likelihood function over the parameters in $\mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}$,
*μ* and *σ* and alternative nested models compared using the
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic. The *AIC* statistic can be used to compare
non-nested models, such as models with different distributions for the
event times.

A disadvantage of parametric models is that the hazard and survivor
functions depend on relatively few unknown parameters in an assumed
distributional model that may be []{#chapter9.xhtml_p299 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 299. " role="doc-pagebreak"}difficult to validate with
interval-censored data. However, more flexible models such as the
Royston and Parmar restricted cubic spline model for a log-cumulative
baseline hazard function can overcome this. The likelihood function for
the model described in [Section 6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_4) is readily
obtained using Equation (9.5), and there are no new principles involved
in fitting and comparing alternative Royston and Parmar models.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam9_7" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 9.7]{.boxnum}](#chapter17.xhtml_b9exam9_7){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Recurrence of an ulcer]{.boxht}

This example illustrates fitting a Weibull proportional hazards model to
the interval-censored data on time to ulcer recurrence. The
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic for the Weibull model containing the
variables corresponding to age, duration and treatment, *Age, Duration*
and *Treat*, is 74.291 and omitting *Age* and *Duration*, leads to an
increase of 0.334 on 2 d.f. This non-significant change in
$- 2\log\hat{L}$, means that neither patient age nor duration of
treatment have a signifiant impact on the time to ulcer recurrence in
this group of patients, as was found in [Examples
9.5](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_5) and
[9.6](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_6){#chapter9.xhtml_b9exam9_6}. The hazard
ratio for treatment *B* relative to treatment *A* is 1.251, which is
again very similar to that found under alternative models in [Examples
9.5](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_5) and [9.6](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_6).
However, this is not significantly different from unity, as the
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic only increases from 74.625 to 74.754 when the
treatment term is omitted from the model.

In the accelerated failure time parameterisation of the model, the time
ratio is $e^{- 0.258} = 0.773$ which means that treatment *B* multiplies
the time to recurrence by 0.773. Those on treatment *B* have a
recurrence time that is estimated to be about 77% of that for patients
on treatment *A*, so that treatment *B* is effectively speeding up the
process that leads to the recurrence of an ulcer.

</aside>
:::

::: section
## 9.5 Further reading {#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_5 .head1}

Comprehensive coverage of statistical models and methods for analysing
interval-censored event times can be found in the book of [Sun
(2006)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib369){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib369 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Bogaerts, Komarek and Lesaffre
(2018)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib37){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib37 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} provide a practical introduction to analysing
survival data using R, SAS, and BUGS. See also the text edited by Chen,
Sun and Peace (2013). [Lindsey and Ryan
(1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib240){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib240 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Lesaffre, Komárek and Declerck
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib232){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib232 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Gómez et al.
(2009)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib150){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib150 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Zhang and Sun
(2010)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib427){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib427 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} provide review articles on methods for the
analysis of such data. [Jewell and van der Laan
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib192){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib192 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} review methods for the analysis of current status
data and describe extensions to competing risks and multivariate
survival data.

[Turnbull (1976)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib393){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} introduced what have come to be known as Turnbull
intervals and described a self-consistent algorithm for estimating the
survivor function from interval-censored data. The EM algorithm was
described by [Dempster, Laird and Rubin
(1977)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib100){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib100 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Groeneboom and Wellner
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib159){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib159 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} introduced the Iterative Convex Minorant (ICM)
algorithm. [Wellner and Zahn
(1997)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib410){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib410 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} proposed using a combination of the EM and ICM
algorithms. [Wang et al.
(2016)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib400){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib400 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} proposes using a spline function to approximate
the cumulative baseline hazard function. [Pan
(1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib283){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib283 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} shows how the ICM algorithm can be used to fit a
proportional hazard model. [Zeng, Mao, and Lin
(2016)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib425){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib425 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} show how an EM algorithm can be used to fit
proportional hazards and proportional odds semi-parametric models with
time-dependent variables.

[Sun (2001)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib368){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib368
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} described how Greenwood\'s formula for
obtaining the standard error of a survivor function in right-censored
data can be extended to interval-censored data, and also described how
the bootstrap approach can be used when likelihood-based methods break
down. [Goodall, Dunn and Babiker
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib152){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib152 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} describe three methods for calculating pointwise
confidence intervals for the non-parametric survivor function estimated
from interval-censored data.

The location of cut points in the piecewise exponential model for
interval-censored data has been discussed by a number of authors. These
include [Finkelstein
(1986)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib123){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib123 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} who suggests []{#chapter9.xhtml_p300 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 300. " role="doc-pagebreak"}that the expected number
of events be similar in each interval, and [Cai and Betensky
(2003)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib53){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib53 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, who describe a method that uses the quantiles of
the unique interval end points.

A number of non-parametric tests for comparing two or more groups of
right-censored survival times have been described in [Chapter
2](#chapter2.xhtml). Test statistics that are analogues of the log-rank
and Wilcoxon tests are readily constructed but the interval nature of
the data means that it is more difficult to obtain the variances and
asymptotic properties of the procedures. [Finkelstein
(1986)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib123){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}
suggested a score test, while [Sun
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib367){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib367 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Zhao and Sun
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib429){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib429 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} generalise the log-rank test to interval-censored
data. [Fay (1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib119){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib119
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} obtains a test under the proportional
odds model and describes a unified framework for comparing the survivor
functions for interval-censored data. Generalisations of weighted
log-rank tests, such as the log-rank and Wilcoxon tests, to
interval-censored data are also summarised by [Fay and Shaw
(2010)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib120){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib120 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, who describe an R package for their
implementation. Multiple imputation tests have been described in [Huang,
Lee and Yu (2008)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib188){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib188
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

[Lindsey (1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib241){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib241
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reviews the use of parametric models
for the analysis of interval-censored data. [Farrington
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib118){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib118 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} provides a comprehensive account of diagnostic
methods for use with proportional hazards models for interval-censored
data. The analysis of interval-censored data when there is informative
censoring has been considered by [Zhang et al.
(2007)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib426){#chapter9.xhtml_b9refbib426 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

[]{#chapter10.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter10.xhtml_p301 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 301. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 10]{.chapnum}[Frailty models]{.chaptitle} {#chapter10.xhtml_chapter10 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-10](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-10){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

In modelling survival data, we attempt to explain observed variation in
times to events such as death by differences in the values of certain
explanatory variables that have been recorded for each individual in a
study. However, even after allowing for the effects of such variables,
there will still be variation in their observed survival times. Some
individuals may have a greater hazard of death than others and are
therefore likely to die sooner. They may be described as being more
*frail*, and variation in survival times can be explained by variability
in this *frailty effect* across individuals.

Situations where the survival times amongst groups of individuals in a
study are not independent are frequently encountered. The survival times
of individuals within the same group will then tend to be more similar
than they would be for individuals from different groups. This effect
can then be modelled by assuming that the individuals within a group
share the same frailty. Since the frailty is common to all individuals
within a group, a degree of dependence between the survival times of
individuals within the group is introduced. Consequently, models with
*shared frailty* can be used in modelling survival data where some
association between the event times is anticipated, as in studies
involving groups of individuals that share a common feature, or when an
individual experiences repeated times to an event. Such models are
described in this chapter.

:::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## 10.1 Introduction to frailty {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_1 .head1}

Variation in survival times is almost always found amongst individuals
in a study. Even when a number of individuals share the same values of
demographic variables such as age and gender, or the same values of
other explanatory variables that have been measured, their survival
times will generally be different. These differences may be explained by
the fact that certain variables that influence survival were not
measured, or that an individual\'s survival time depends on variables
that it was not possible to measure. There may be many such variables
and our knowledge of these may be limited to the extent that we simply
do not know what the variables are that might explain this variability.

To illustrate, consider the survival times of patients who have been
diagnosed with a life-threatening cancer. Patient survival time
following diagnosis will depend on many factors, including the type of
cancer and stage of the tumour, characteristics of the patient such as
their age, weight and lifestyle, and the manner in which the cancer is
treated. A group of patients who have the same values of each measured
explanatory variable may nevertheless be observed to have different
survival times. This variation or *heterogeneity* between individuals
may arise because some individuals are not as strong as others in ways
that cannot be summarised in terms of a relatively small number of known
variables. Indeed, we can never aspire to know what all the factors are
that may have an effect on the survival of cancer patients, let alone be
able to measure them, but we can take account of them in a modelling
process.

Variation between the survival times of a group of individuals can be
described in terms []{#chapter10.xhtml_p302 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 302. " role="doc-pagebreak"}of some individuals being
more frail than others. Those who have higher values of a frailty term
tend to die sooner than those who are less frail. However, the extent of
an individual\'s frailty cannot be measured directly; if it could, we
might attempt to include it in a model for survival times. Instead, we
only observe the impact that the frailty effects have on the observable
survival times.

::: section
### 10.1.1 Random effects {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_1_1 .head2}

In the models described in earlier chapters, the effects corresponding
to factors of interest have always been assumed to be fixed. The
possible values of a *fixed effect* do not vary, and they are assumed to
be measured without error. For example, the factor associated with
gender has two distinct levels, male and female, that will not change
from study to study, and it will often be of interest to summarise
outcome differences between the two genders. On the other hand, a
*random effect* is assumed to have levels drawn from a population of
possible values, where the actual levels are representative of that
population. As an example, in a multicentre clinical trial, the centres
adopted might be considered to be drawn from a larger number of possible
centres. We would then model centre variation using a random effect, and
there would be little interest in comparing outcomes between particular
centres.

Random effects are assumed to be observations on a random variable that
has some underlying probability distribution, with the variance of this
distribution used to summarise the extent of differences in their
values. Such effects typically have many possible values, and it is
unrealistic to represent differences between them using fixed effects,
as this would introduce a large number of unknown parameters into a
model. By taking them to be random effects, there is just one parameter
to estimate, namely the variance of their assumed underlying
distribution.
:::

::: section
### 10.1.2 Individual frailty {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_1_2 .head2}

Since the unknown frailty values attributed to individuals in a study
are essentially drawn from a large number of possible values, we take
frailty to be a random effect. In situations where the variance of the
underlying distribution of random effects is small, the frailty effects
will not differ much and the effect on inferences will be negligible.
However, if the frailty variance is large, the observed survival times
of individuals may differ markedly, even after allowing for the effects
of other potential explanatory variables.

In the presence of frailty, individuals who are more frail will tend to
die earlier. This means that at any point in time when the survival
times of a sample of individuals are observed, those who are still alive
will be less frail than the corresponding population from which the
sample has been drawn. Since frailty is acting as a *selection effect*,
there are consequences for what can actually be observed. For example,
if each individual has a constant hazard of death, and frailty effects
are present, the more frail individuals will die earlier, while those
who are less frail will survive longer. The observed hazard function may
therefore appear to decline over time. As an illustration of this,
consider a group of individuals who have experienced a non-fatal heart
attack. For such individuals, the hazard of death is generally observed
to decline with time, and there are two possible reasons for this.
First, the individuals may simply adjust to any damage to the heart that
has been caused by the heart attack, so that the underlying hazard of
death does decline. Alternatively, the hazard of death may be constant,
but the observed decrease in the hazard may be due to frailty; higher
risk individuals die earlier, so that at any time, the individuals who
remain alive are those that are less frail. Data on the survival times
cannot be used to distinguish between these two possible explanations
for the apparent decline in the hazard of death. By the same token, when
allowance is made for frailty effects, estimated hazard
[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p303 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 303. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}ratios and their associated standard errors may be
quite different from the values obtained when frailty effects are
ignored. This feature will be illustrated in [Section
10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_3).
:::

::: section
### 10.1.3 Shared frailty {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_1_3 .head2}

Models that take account of individual levels of frailty can be used to
assess how the values of unmeasured variables may affect survival.
However, the notion of frailty is more widely used in situations where a
number of individuals share something in common. In a multicentre study,
for example, the survival experience of individuals from the same centre
may be more similar than that for individuals from different centres.
This could be because of different clinical teams in the different
centres, or different nursing practices across the centres. Similarly,
in an animal experiment, animals from the same litter will be more alike
than animals from different litters, because of genetic and
environmental influences. Their survival times will therefore be
correlated, and one way of modelling this association between survival
times is to assume that all the individuals within a group share the
same frailty. In paired organ studies involving eyes, ears or kidneys,
where the time to some event is recorded on each organ within the pair,
frailty might be included in a model to allow for any association
between the pairs of observed event times. Shared frailty models
therefore provide a method for modelling survival data when the survival
times are not independent.

Some studies lead to repeated or recurrent event times within an
individual. Examples of this type of situation include studies on the
times between successive adverse events, such as migraine or nausea, the
times to the failure of tooth fillings in an individual, and times to
the failure of transplanted kidneys in patients that receive more than
one transplant. Here again, the event times within an individual may not
be independent, and to model this we can assume that the frailty is the
same for each of these event times. The concept of a shared frailty can
therefore be extended to situations where there are repeated events
within an individual.
:::
::::::

::::: section
## 10.2 Modelling individual frailty {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_2 .head1}

Variation between the survival times of individuals can be modelled by
supposing that each person has his or her own value of a frailty. This
frailty term is assumed to act multiplicatively on the hazard of death
of an individual. Those with a frailty greater than unity will have
increased hazard, while those who have a frailty less than unity will
have a reduced hazard of the event occurring.

Consider a proportional hazards model for the hazard of an event
occurring at some time *t*, in an individual with a hazard function that
depends on the values of *p* explanatory variables
$X_{1},X_{2},\ldots,X_{p}$, and on some unknown baseline hazard function
$h_{0}(t)$. The hazard function for the *i*th of *n* individuals is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(10.1)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is the vector of values of the
explanatory variables for the *i*th individual, and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ is the vector of their unknown coefficients
$\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\ldots,\beta_{p}$. The baseline hazard $h_{0}(t)$
may be unspecified as in the Cox regression model ([Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml)) or a parametric function of *t*, such as a Weibull
model ([Chapters 5](#chapter5.xhtml), [6](#chapter6.xhtml)).

We now introduce a multiplicative frailty term, *z*~*i*~, and write

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = z_{i}\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$[(10.2)]{.equationnum}

The frailty term *z*~*i*~ cannot be negative, and the greater the
frailty, the greater is the hazard of the event occurring. A value of
$z_{i} = 1$ brings us back to the standard model of Equation (10.1),
corresponding to the situation where there are no frailty effects.
Values of *z*~*i*~ []{#chapter10.xhtml_p304 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 304. " role="doc-pagebreak"}between 0 and 1 correspond
to situations where the hazard is less than that in the underlying
proportional hazards model, and occur when individuals have increased
fortitude. Differences in the values of the *z*~*i*~ across the sample
of *n* individuals will then represent variation in the hazard of death
at any given time due to frailty effects.

It is generally more convenient to work with an alternative
representation of the frailty effect, obtained by setting
$z_{i} = \exp(u_{i})$. The model in Equation (10.2) can then be written

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + u_{i})h_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$[(10.3)]{.equationnum}

In this model, $u_{i} = \log z_{i}$ is a *random effect* in the linear
component of the proportional hazards model. Note that whereas a frailty
*z*~*i*~ cannot be negative, *u*~*i*~ can take any value, positive or
negative, and $u_{i} = 0$ corresponds to the case where $z_{i} = 1$ and
there is no frailty. In Equation (10.3), the linear component of the
proportional hazards model has been extended to include a random effect,
and this part of the model for survival times is now akin to mixed
models used in other areas of general and generalised linear modelling.

The *u*~*i*~ in Equation (10.3) are regarded as the observed values, or
*realisations*, of *n* independent and identically distributed random
variables *U*~*i*~, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, where each random variable,
*U*~*i*~, is assumed to have a common probability distribution. So
although the individual frailties across a group of individuals may all
be different, they are drawn from the same underlying probability
distribution.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam10_1" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 10.1]{.boxnum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10exam10_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

The data in [Example
1.2](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_2){#chapter10.xhtml_b1exam1_2} of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml) on the survival times of two groups of women, with
tumours that were negatively or positively stained, shows that there is
variability in the survival times of the women within each group. In the
negatively stained group, actual survival times range from 23 to beyond
224 months, while in the positively stained group, they range from 5 to
over 225 months. To summarise these data, a Weibull proportional hazards
model containing a factor associated with positive or negative staining
is fitted, as in [Example
5.6](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_6){#chapter10.xhtml_b10exam5_6}. The
estimated baseline hazard function is
${\hat{h}}_{0}(t) = \hat{\lambda}\hat{\gamma}t^{\hat{\gamma} - 1}$,
where $\hat{\lambda} = 0.00414$ and $\hat{\gamma} = 0.937$. The
corresponding estimated survivor function for the *i*th woman,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,45$, is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - \exp(\hat{\beta}x_{i})\hat{\lambda}t^{\hat{\gamma}} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

using Equation (5.43) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), where $x_{i} = 0$
for a woman with negative staining and $x_{i} = 1$ for one with positive
staining, and $\hat{\beta} = 0.934$. The corresponding hazard ratio for
a woman with a positively stained tumour, relative to one whose tumour
is negatively stained, is 2.545, with a 95% confidence interval of
(0.93, 6.98). The fitted Weibull survivor functions are shown in [Figure
10.1](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_1){#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_1},
superimposed on the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor functions. The
Weibull model does not appear to be a very good fit to the survival
times of women with positively stained tumours.

<figure id="fig10_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig10_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">10.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Observed and fitted Weibull survivor
functions for women with positive (<span class="middle">.......</span>)
and negative (—) staining.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In this figure, the data have been summarised in just two curves.
However, the introduction of frailty would lead to separate estimated
survivor functions for each woman, and we will return to this in
[Example 10.2](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_2){#chapter10.xhtml_b10exam10_2}.

</aside>

::: section
### 10.2.1 Frailty distributions {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_2_1 .head2}

In the model of Equation (10.3), it is common to assume that the random
variables *U*~*i*~, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, that are associated with the
random effects, have a normal distribution with zero mean and common
variance $\sigma_{u}^{2}$. If
$\left. U_{i} \right.\sim N(0,\sigma_{u}^{2})$, then
$Z_{i} = \exp(U_{i})$ has a lognormal distribution. This distribution
was introduced in [Section 5.1.4](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_4) of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml). Since $z_{i} = 1$ in the model of Equation (10.2)
corresponds to the situation where there is no frailty, it is desirable
for the frailty distribution to be centred on unity. By taking the
random frailty []{#chapter10.xhtml_p305 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 305. " role="doc-pagebreak"}effects, *U*~*i*~, to be
distributed about a mean of zero, the corresponding distribution of
*Z*~*i*~ has a median of unity. However, the expected value of *Z*~*i*~,
which is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{E}\,(Z_{i}) = \exp(\sigma_{u}^{2}/2),}
\end{array}$$[(10.4)]{.equationnum}

will be different from 1.0, and the variance of *Z*~*i*~ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(Z_{i}) = \exp(\sigma_{u}^{2})\left\{ \exp(\sigma_{u}^{2}) - 1 \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(10.5)]{.equationnum}

The results in Equations (10.4) and (10.5) show how the mean and
variance of a lognormal frailty distribution can be found from the
variance, $\sigma_{u}^{2}$, of the corresponding normally distributed
random effect. An alternative approach is to take *U*~*i*~ to have a
normal distribution with mean $- \sigma_{u}^{2}/2$ and variance
$\sigma_{u}^{2}$. Although *Z*~*i*~ would then have a mean of unity,
this formulation is not widely used in practice.

A distribution that is very similar to the lognormal distribution is the
gamma distribution, described in [Section
5.1.6](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_6) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), and so
this is an alternative distribution for the frailty random variable,
*Z*~*i*~. This frailty distribution is sometimes more convenient to work
with than the lognormal model, and can be used to investigate some of
the consequences of introducing a frailty effect, which we do in
[Section 10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_3).

If we take *Z*~*i*~ to have a gamma distribution with both unknown
parameters equal to *θ*, and density

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(z_{i}) = \frac{\theta^{\theta}z_{i}^{\theta - 1}e^{- \theta z_{i}}}{\Gamma(\theta)},\,\theta\geqslant 0,}
\end{array}$$

for $z_{i} > 0$, so that
$\left. Z_{i} \right.\sim\Gamma(\theta,\theta)$, this frailty
distribution has a mean of unity and a variance of $1/\theta$. The
larger the value of *θ*, the smaller the frailty variance, and as
$\left. \theta\rightarrow\infty \right.$, the frailty variance tends to
zero, corresponding to the case where the *z*~*i*~ are all equal to
unity and there is no frailty. The corresponding distribution of
$U_{i} = \log(Z_{i})$ has density

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(u_{i}) = \frac{\theta^{\theta}e^{\theta u_{i}}\exp( - \theta e^{u_{i}})}{\Gamma(\theta)},\,\theta\geqslant 0,}
\end{array}$$[(10.6)]{.equationnum}

for $- \infty < u_{i} < \infty$, and *U*~*i*~ is said to have an
*exp-gamma distribution*. This distribution is also referred to as the
log-gamma distribution, but this nomenclature is inconsistent with the
[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p306 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 306. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}definition of a lognormal distribution. The modal
value of *U*~*i*~ is zero, but the distribution is asymmetric and the
mean and variance of *U*~*i*~ are expressed in terms of *digamma* and
*trigamma functions*. Specifically,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{E}\,(U_{i}) = \Psi(\theta) - \log\theta,}
\end{array}$$[(10.7)]{.equationnum}

where the digamma function, $\Psi(\theta)$, can be obtained from the
series expansion

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\Psi(\theta) = - \lambda + {\sum\limits_{j = 0}^{\infty}\frac{\theta - 1}{(1 + j)(\theta + j)}},}
\end{array}$$

and here, $\lambda = 0.577216$ is Euler\'s constant. Also, the variance
of *U*~*i*~ is the derivative of $\Psi(\theta)$, written
$\Psi'(\theta)$, and known as the trigamma function. This function can
also be obtained using a series expansion, and

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(U_{i}) = \Psi'(\theta) = {\sum\limits_{j = 0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{{(\theta + j)}^{2}}}.}
\end{array}$$[(10.8)]{.equationnum}

The digamma and trigamma functions are available as standard functions
in many statistical software packages.

The lognormal and gamma distributions for frailty, and the corresponding
normal and exp-gamma distributions for the random effects, will be
compared later in Example 10.3.
:::

::: section
### 10.2.2 Observable survivor and hazard functions {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_2_2 .head2}

The survivor function for the *i*th individual, from Equations (1.6) and
(1.7) in [Chapter 1](#chapter1.xhtml), is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- {\int_{0}^{t}h_{i}}(t)\,\text{d}t} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where $h_{i}(t)$ is given in Equation (10.2). Therefore

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - z_{i}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t) \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(10.9)]{.equationnum}

where $H_{0}(t)$ is the baseline cumulative hazard function. This is the
survivor function for the *i*th individual conditional on the frailty
*z*~*i*~ and is termed a *conditional model* for the survival times.

The individual frailties *z*~*i*~ cannot be observed directly, and so
what we observe is the effect that they have on the overall survivor
function for the group of individuals. The observable survivor function
is therefore the individual functions averaged over all possible values
of *z*~*i*~, that is the expected value of $S_{i}(t)$ with respect to
the frailty distribution. If the corresponding random variables *Z*~*i*~
had a discrete distribution, where only a few specific values of the
frailty were possible, we would obtain this expectation by summing the
products of the possible values of $S_{i}(t)$ for the different *z*~*i*~
values and the probability that *Z*~*i*~ was equal to *z*~*i*~. However,
we generally assume a continuous distribution for the *Z*~*i*~, and in
this situation, the survivor function that is observed is found by
integrating $S_{i}(t)$ in Equation (10.9) with respect to the
distribution of *Z*~*i*~. The resulting survivor function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}^{*}(t) = {\int_{0}^{\infty}S_{i}}(t)f(z_{i})\,\text{d}z_{i} = {\int_{0}^{\infty}\exp}\left\{ - z_{i}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t) \right\} f(z_{i})\,\text{d}z_{i},}
\end{array}$$[(10.10)]{.equationnum}

where $f(z_{i})$ is the density function of the frailty distribution.
The quantity $S_{i}^{*}(t)$ is the *unconditional* or *observable
survivor function*, and Equation (10.10) defines an *unconditional
model* for the survival times.

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p307 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 307. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Once $S_{i}^{*}(t)$ has been obtained for a
particular model, the corresponding *observable hazard function*,
$h_{i}^{*}(t)$, can be found using the relationship in Equation (1.5) of
[Chapter 1](#chapter1.xhtml), so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}^{*}(t) = - \frac{\text{d}}{\text{dt}}\left\{ \log S_{i}^{*}(t) \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(10.11)]{.equationnum}

In general the integral in Equation (10.10) has to be evaluated
numerically, but as we shall see in the following section, this can be
accomplished analytically in the special case of gamma frailty effects.
:::
:::::

::::: section
## 10.3 The gamma frailty distribution {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_3 .head1}

The gamma distribution is often used to model frailty effects as it
leads to a closed form representation of the observable survivor and
hazard functions, as shown in this section.

Suppose that each individual in a study has a distinct frailty value,
*z*~*i*~, and that these are observations on independent and identically
distributed random variables *Z*~*i*~, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, where
$\left. Z_{i} \right.\sim\Gamma(\theta,\theta)$. Now, from Equation
(10.10), the observable survivor function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}^{*}(t) = {\int_{0}^{\infty}S_{i}}(t)f(z_{i})\,\text{d}z_{i},}
\end{array}$$

so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}^{*}(t) = {\int_{0}^{\infty}\exp}\left\{ - z_{i}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t) \right\}\frac{\theta^{\theta}z_{i}^{\theta - 1}e^{- \theta z_{i}}}{\Gamma(\theta)}\,\text{d}z_{i},}
\end{array}$$

and on some rearrangement, this becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\theta^{\theta}{\lbrack\theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t)\rbrack}^{- \theta}}{\Gamma(\theta)}{\int_{0}^{\infty}y_{i}^{\theta - 1}}e^{- y_{i}}\,\text{d}y_{i},}
\end{array}$$

where
$y_{i} = \left\{ \theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t) \right\} z_{i}$.
Then, from the definition of a gamma function in Equation (5.6) of
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\int_{0}^{\infty}y_{i}^{\theta - 1}}e^{- y_{i}}\,\text{d}y_{i} = \Gamma(\theta),}
\end{array}$$

and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}^{*}(t) = \left\{ 1 + \theta^{- 1}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t) \right\}^{- \theta}.}
\end{array}$$[(10.12)]{.equationnum}

Equation (10.11) leads to the observable hazard function, given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}^{*}(t) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}h_{0}(t)}{1 + \theta^{- 1}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t)}.}
\end{array}$$[(10.13)]{.equationnum}

In the next section, some of the consequences of introducing a frailty
effect are illustrated.

::: section
### 10.3.1 Impact of frailty on an observable hazard function {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_3_1 .head2}

To illustrate the effect that a frailty term has on the hazard function,
we will take the frailty random variable *Z*~*i*~ to have a
$\Gamma(\theta,\theta)$ distribution. Suppose that the baseline hazard
function $h_{0}(t)$ is a constant, *λ*, and that we are fitting a model
to a single sample of survival times so that there are no covariates.
Using Equation (10.13), the observable hazard function is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h^{*}(t) = \frac{\theta\lambda}{\theta + \lambda t},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p308 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 308. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}which declines non-linearly from the value *λ* when
$t = 0$ to zero. The presence of frailty effects therefore provides an
alternative explanation for a decreasing hazard function. Specifically,
the overall hazard of an event might actually be constant, but
heterogeneity between the survival times of the individuals in the study
will mean that a decline in hazard is observed.

Similar features are found if we assume that the underlying baseline
hazard is dependent upon time. In the case where the underlying baseline
hazard is Weibull, with $h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$, and
where there are no covariates,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h^{*}(t) = \frac{\theta\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}}{\theta + \lambda t^{\gamma}}.}
\end{array}$$

A plot of $h^{*}(t)$ against *t* for a Weibull hazard with
$\lambda = 1$, $\gamma = 3$ and various values of *θ* is shown in
[Figure 10.2](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_2){#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_2}.

<figure id="fig10_2" class="image">
<img src="images/fig10_2.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_2" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">10.2</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Observable hazard function for Weibull
baseline hazards with <span
class="math inline"><em>h</em><sub>0</sub>(<em>t</em>) = 3<em>t</em><sup>2</sup></span>
and gamma frailty distributions with <span
class="math inline"><em>θ</em> = 0.5, 2, 5</span> and
∞.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

One limitation of a Weibull baseline hazard function, noted in [Section
5.1.2](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_2) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), is
that it is monotonic. However, as we can see in [Figure
10.2](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_2), when there is a degree of frailty
present, we may observe unimodal hazard functions. Consequently, an
observed unimodal hazard may be the result of frailty effects, rather
than the intrinsic behaviour of the underlying baseline hazard.
:::

::: section
### 10.3.2 Impact of frailty on an observable hazard ratio {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_3_2 .head2}

Suppose that the hazard function for the *i*th individual is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = z_{i}\exp(\beta x_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where *x*~*i*~ is the value of a binary covariate *X* that takes values
0, 1 and *z*~*i*~ is a realisation of a $\Gamma(\theta,\theta)$ random
variable. The corresponding observable hazard function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}^{*}(t) = \frac{e^{\beta x_{i}}h_{0}(t)}{1 + \theta^{- 1}e^{\beta x_{i}}H_{0}(t)},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p309 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 309. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}from Equation (10.13), and the observable hazard
ratio for $X = 1$ relative to $X = 0$ is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\psi^{*}(t) = \frac{1 + \theta^{- 1}H_{0}(t)}{1 + \theta^{- 1}e^{\beta}H_{0}(t)}e^{\beta}.}
\end{array}$$[(10.14)]{.equationnum}

This hazard ratio is no longer constant but a function of time *t*, and
so proportional hazards no longer pertains. For example, in the
particular case where the underlying baseline hazard is assumed to be a
constant *λ*, $H_{0}(t) = \lambda t$ and the hazard ratio becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{1 + \theta^{- 1}\lambda t}{1 + \theta^{- 1}e^{\beta}\lambda t}e^{\beta},}
\end{array}$$

a non-linear function of *t*.

To illustrate the dependence of the observable hazard ratio on time,
[Figure 10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_3){#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_3}
shows the hazard ratio $\psi^{*}(t)$ for a Weibull baseline hazard with
$\lambda = 1$, $\gamma = 3$, $\psi = e^{\beta} = 3$ and various values
of *θ*.

<figure id="fig10_3" class="image">
<img src="images/fig10_3.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_3" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">10.3</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Observable hazard ratio for Weibull baseline
hazard with <span
class="math inline"><em>h</em><sub>0</sub>(<em>t</em>) = 3<em>t</em><sup>2</sup></span>,
<span class="math inline"><em>β</em> = log 3</span>, and gamma frailty
distributions with <span
class="math inline"><em>θ</em> = 0.5, 2, 5</span> and
∞.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The hazard ratio has a constant value of 3 when there is no frailty,
that is when $\theta = \infty$, but when the frailty variance exceeds
zero, the observable hazard ratio declines over time. This dependence of
the hazard ratio on time could be used to account for any observed
non-proportionality in the hazards.

These results show that the inclusion of a random effect in a Weibull
proportional hazards regression model can lead to hazards not being
proportional, and to a non-monotonic hazard function. Although this has
been illustrated using a Weibull model with gamma frailty, the
conclusions drawn apply more generally. This means that models that
include random effects provide an alternative way of modelling data
where the hazard function is unimodal, or where the hazards are not
proportional.
:::
:::::

:::: section
## 10.4 Fitting parametric frailty models {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_4 .head1}

Fitting the model in Equation (10.3) entails estimating the values of
the coefficients of the explanatory variables, the variance of frailty
distribution and the baseline hazard function. Models in which
$h_{0}(t)$ is fully specified, such as the Weibull proportional hazards
model or []{#chapter10.xhtml_p310 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 310. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}an accelerated failure time model, can be fitted
using the method of maximum likelihood. Denote the observed survival
data by the pairs $(t_{i},\delta_{i})$, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, where
*t*~*i*~ is the survival time and *δ*~*i*~ is an event indicator, which
takes the value zero for a censored observation and unity for an event.
If the random effects *u*~*i*~ in Equation (10.3) had known values, the
likelihood function would be

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left. {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ h_{i}( \right.}t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}S_{i}(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$[(10.15)]{.equationnum}

as in Equation (5.15) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml). In this
expression, the hazard function for the *i*th of *n* individuals is
given in Equation (10.3) and the corresponding survivor function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t_{i}) = \exp\left\{ {- \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + u_{i})H_{0}(t_{i})} \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(10.16)]{.equationnum}

where $H_{0}(t_{i})$ is the cumulative hazard function evaluated at time
*t*~*i*~.

However, the *u*~*i*~ are not known, but are independent and identically
distributed realisations of a random variable that has a probability
distribution with density $f(u_{i})$. In this situation, we integrate
the likelihood contributions over possible values of the random effects,
so that from Equation (10.15), the likelihood function becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left. {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{\int_{- \infty}^{\infty}\left\{ h_{i}( \right.}}t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}S_{i}(t_{i})f(u_{i})\,\text{d}u_{i},}
\end{array}$$[(10.17)]{.equationnum}

or equivalently, when working with the frailty terms *z*~*i*~,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left. {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{\int_{0}^{\infty}\left\{ h_{i}( \right.}}t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}S_{i}(t_{i})f(z_{i})\,\text{d}z_{i}.}
\end{array}$$[(10.18)]{.equationnum}

Numerical methods are generally needed to maximise this function, or its
logarithm.

Once the unknown parameters in a fully parametric model have been
estimated, it is possible to obtain estimates of the random effects. To
do this, we use a version of Bayes\' theorem, given later as Equation
(16.2) in [Chapter 16](#chapter16.xhtml), according to which the
probability of an event *A*, conditional on an event *B*, is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \text{P}(A\, \middle| \, B) = \frac{\left. P(B\, \middle| \, A)P(A) \right.}{P(B)}. \right.
\end{array}$$[(10.19)]{.equationnum}

We now write $\left. L(t_{i}\, \middle| \, u_{i}) \right.$ for the
likelihood of the *i*th event time *t*~*i*~ when the random effect
*u*~*i*~ is regarded as fixed, which from combining Equation (10.16)
with Expression (10.15), is

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. L(t_{i}\, \middle| \, u_{i}) = \left\{ \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + u_{i})h_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}\exp\left\{ - \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + u_{i})H_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}. \right.
\end{array}$$

This can be interpreted as the probability of *t*~*i*~ conditional on
the random effect *u*~*i*~. Next, the probability of a particular value
of the random effect is the density function $f(u_{i})$. Using Equation
(10.19), the probability of *u*~*i*~ conditional on *t*~*i*~ can be
expressed as

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \pi(u_{i}\, \middle| \, t_{i}) = \frac{\left. L(t_{i}\, \middle| \, u_{i})f(u_{i}) \right.}{p(t_{i})}, \right.
\end{array}$$[(10.20)]{.equationnum}

where $p(t_{i})$ is the marginal probability of the data, obtained by
integrating $\left. L(t_{i}\, \middle| \, u_{i})f(u_{i}) \right.$ with
respect to *u*~*i*~. This ensures that
$\left. \pi(u_{i}\, \middle| \, t_{i}) \right.$ integrates to 1, and so
defines a proper probability density function. The mean or mode of the
distribution of the random variable associated with the *i*th random
effect, *U*~*i*~, conditional on the data,
$\left. \pi(u_{i}\, \middle| \, t_{i}) \right.$, can then be obtained.
Finally, substituting estimates of the unknown *β*-coefficients and the
variance of the frailty distribution, we get an estimate of the mean or
modal value of the distribution []{#chapter10.xhtml_p311 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 311. " role="doc-pagebreak"}of *U*~*i*~,
${\hat{u}}_{i}$, say, which can be regarded as an estimate of the random
effect. Because of the link to Bayes\' theorem in Equation (10.19),
$\left. \pi(u_{i}\, \middle| \, t_{i}) \right.$ is referred to as the
*posterior density* of *U*~*i*~, and the estimates ${\hat{u}}_{i}$ are
termed *empirical Bayes estimates*. In a similar way, the posterior
variance of *U*~*i*~ can be obtained from
