$\left. \pi(u_{i}\, \middle| \, t_{i}) \right.$, which leads to the
standard error of ${\hat{u}}_{i}$. Corresponding estimates of the
frailty effects and their standard errors are obtained from
${\hat{z}}_{i} = \exp({\hat{u}}_{i})$.

Once frailty effects have been estimated, Equations (10.9) or (10.16)
can be used to obtain fully parametric estimates of the survivor
function. The median survival time for a particular individual can then
be found from the expression

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left\{ \frac{\log 2}{\hat{\lambda}\exp({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + {\hat{u}}_{i})} \right\}^{1/\hat{\gamma}},}
\end{array}$$[(10.21)]{.equationnum}

which is a straightforward adaptation of the result in Equation (5.48)
of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml).

Parametric survival models with frailty are most easily fitted when the
frailty effects have a gamma distribution, and so the fitting process
for this particular model is described in the following section.

::: section
### 10.4.1 Gamma frailty {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_4_1 .head2}

When the frailty random variable, *Z*~*i*~, is assumed to have a gamma
distribution, a closed form can be obtained for the integrated
likelihood in Equation (10.18). To show this, on substituting for the
hazard function, survivor function and density of *Z*~*i*~ into Equation
(10.18), the likelihood function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{\int_{0}^{\infty}\left\{ z_{i}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}h_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}}}\,\exp\left\{ - z_{i}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}\,\frac{\theta^{\theta}z_{i}^{\theta - 1}e^{- \theta z_{i}}}{\Gamma(\theta)}\,\text{d}z_{i}.}
\end{array}$$

Collecting terms in *z*~*i*~, this becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\frac{\theta^{\theta}}{\Gamma(\theta)}}\left\{ e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}h_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}{\int_{0}^{\infty}z_{i}^{\theta + \delta_{i} - 1}}\exp\left\{ - \lbrack\theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i})\rbrack z_{i} \right\}\,\text{d}z_{i}.}
\end{array}$$

Next, the density of a gamma random variable *Y* with parameters *r* and
*θ* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(y) = \frac{\theta^{r}y^{r - 1}e^{- \theta y}}{\Gamma(r)},}
\end{array}$$[(10.22)]{.equationnum}

and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\int_{0}^{\infty}y^{r - 1}}e^{- \theta y}\,\text{d}y = \frac{\Gamma(r)}{\theta^{r}},}
\end{array}$$

from which

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\int_{0}^{\infty}z_{i}^{\theta + \delta_{i} - 1}}\exp\left\{ - \lbrack\theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i})\rbrack z_{i} \right\}\,\text{d}z_{i} = \frac{\Gamma(\theta + \delta_{i})}{\left\{ \theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}^{\theta + \delta_{i}}}.}
\end{array}$$

Consequently, the likelihood function becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\frac{\theta^{\theta}}{\Gamma(\theta)}}\left\{ e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}h_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}\frac{\Gamma(\theta + \delta_{i})}{\left\{ \theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}^{\theta + \delta_{i}}}.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p312 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 312. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The corresponding log-likelihood function is

$$\begin{aligned}
 & {\quad{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {\theta\log\theta - \log\Gamma(\theta) + \log\Gamma(\theta + \delta_{i}) + \delta_{i}\lbrack\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + \log h_{0}(t_{i})\rbrack} \right\}}} \\
 & {\quad\text{\quad\quad} - {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{(\theta + \delta_{i})\log\left\{ \theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}}},}
\end{aligned}$$[(10.23)]{.equationnum}

and now that no integration is involved, standard numerica1 methods can
be used to maximise this function with respect to *θ*, the *β*s and the
parameters in the baseline hazard function, to give the maximum
likelihood estimates. Standard errors of these estimates are found from
the corresponding information matrix; see [Appendix
A](#appA.xhtml_appA). The estimated variance of the random effects can
then be found by using the maximum likelihood estimate of *θ*,
$\hat{\theta}$, in Equation (10.8).

To obtain estimates of the frailty effects when they are assumed to have
a gamma distribution, it is more convenient to work with the *z*~*i*~
rather than the corresponding random effects *u*~*i*~. Writing
$\left. L(t_{i}\, \middle| \, z_{i}) \right.$ for the likelihood of
*t*~*i*~ when the frailty *z*~*i*~ is regarded as fixed, the numerator
of Equation (10.20) is then
$\left. L(t_{i}\, \middle| \, z_{i})f(z_{i}) \right.$, which is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\lbrack z_{i}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}h_{0}(t_{i})\rbrack}^{\delta_{i}}\exp\left\{ - z_{i}e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}\,\frac{\theta^{\theta}z_{i}^{\theta - 1}e^{- \theta z_{i}}}{\Gamma(\theta)}.}
\end{array}$$

Ignoring terms that do not involve *z*~*i*~, the posterior density of
*Z*~*i*~, $\left. \pi(z_{i}\, \middle| \, t_{i}) \right.$, is
proportional to

$$\begin{array}{r}
{z_{i}^{\theta + \delta_{i} - 1}\exp\left\{ - \lbrack\theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i})\rbrack z_{i} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

From the general form of a two-parameter gamma density function, shown
in Equation (10.22), it follows that the posterior distribution of
*Z*~*i*~ is a gamma distribution with parameters $\theta + \delta_{i}$
and
$\theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i})$.
Then since the expected value of a gamma random variable with parameters
$r,\theta$ is $r/\theta$, the expectation of *Z*~*i*~ given the data, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \,\text{E}\,(Z_{i}\, \middle| \, t_{i}) = \frac{\theta + \delta_{i}}{\theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i})}. \right.
\end{array}$$

From this, an estimate of the frailty effect for the *i*th individual is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{z}}_{i} = \frac{\hat{\theta} + \delta_{i}}{\hat{\theta} + e^{{\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}{\hat{H}}_{0}(t_{i})},}
\end{array}$$

where ${\hat{H}}_{0}(t)$ is the estimated baseline cumulative hazard
function. Similarly, the variance of a gamma random variable is
$r/\theta^{2}$ and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \,\text{var}\,(Z_{i}\, \middle| \, t_{i}) = \frac{\theta + \delta_{i}}{\left\{ \theta + e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}}H_{0}(t_{i}) \right\}^{2}}. \right.
\end{array}$$

The estimated variance of *Z*~*i*~ reduces to
${\hat{z}}_{i}^{2}/(\hat{\theta} + \delta_{i})$ and so the standard
error of ${\hat{z}}_{i}$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,({\hat{z}}_{i}) = \frac{{\hat{z}}_{i}}{\left. \sqrt{}(\hat{\theta} + \delta_{i}) \right.}.}
\end{array}$$

Interval estimates for the frailty terms can then be found, and the
ratio of $\hat{z_{i}}$ to its standard error can be compared to
percentage points of a standard normal distribution to give a *P*-value
for a test of the hypothesis that the *i*th frailty effect is zero.
However, the results []{#chapter10.xhtml_p313 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 313. " role="doc-pagebreak"}of a series of unplanned
hypothesis tests about frailty effects must be adjusted to allow for
repeated significance testing, for example by using the Bonferroni
correction. With this correction, the *P*-value for the frailty terms of
*n* individuals are each multiplied by *n* before interpreting the
significance levels.

As this analysis shows, working with gamma frailties is mathematically
straightforward and leads to closed form estimates of many useful
quantities. In other situations, numerical methods are needed to
evaluate the summary statistics of the conditional distribution of the
frailty given the data.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam10_2" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 10.2]{.boxnum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10exam10_2){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

When a frailty term with a gamma distribution is included in a Weibull
model for the survival times of women with negatively and positively
stained tumours, outlined in [Example
10.1](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_1){#chapter10.xhtml_b10exam10_1}, the
resulting hazard function is
$h_{i}(t) = z_{i}\exp(\beta x_{i})\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$, and the
corresponding fitted survivor function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - {\hat{z}}_{i}\exp(\hat{\beta}x_{i})\hat{\lambda}t^{\hat{\gamma}} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

The variance of the underlying gamma distribution for the frailty is
${\hat{\theta}}^{- 1} = 3.40$, and estimates of the frailty effects
follow from the method outlined in this section.

In the presence of frailty, $\hat{\beta} = 2.298$, and the hazard ratio
for a positively stained woman relatively to one who is negatively
stained is 9.95. However, the 95% confidence interval for this estimate
ranges from 0.86 to 115.54, reflecting the much greater uncertainty
about the staining effect when account is taken of frailty. It is also
important to note that the estimated hazard ratio is conditional on
frailty, and so refers to a woman with a specific frailty value. The
revised estimates of *λ* and *γ* are 0.00008 and 1.9091, respectively,
and the individual frailty values are shown in [Table
10.1](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_1){#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_1}, alongside
the observed survival times. Note that women with the shortest survival
times have the largest estimated frailty effects.

: [[[Table]{.label}
[10.1]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times of women with tumours that were
negatively or positively stained and corresponding gamma
frailties]{.table-title} {#tbl10_1}

The individual estimates of the survivor functions are shown in [Figure
10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_4){#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_4}. This
figure illustrates the extent of variation in the estimated survivor
functions that stems from the []{#chapter10.xhtml_p314 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 314. " role="doc-pagebreak"}frailty effect, and shows
that there is some separation in the estimates for those women whose
tumours were positively or negatively stained.

<figure id="fig10_4" class="image">
<img src="images/fig10_4.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_4" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">10.4</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Fitted Weibull survivor functions for women
with positive (<span class="middle">.......</span>) and negative (—)
staining.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The median survival times, found using Equation (10.21), vary from 55 to
372 months for women in the negatively stained group, and from 16 to 355
months for those in the positively stained group. Of the 32 women with
positively stained tumours, 18 have estimated median survival times that
are less than any of those in the negatively stained group. This
confirms that once allowance is made for frailty effects, differences
between survival times in the two groups of women are not as pronounced.

The observable survivor function under this model, from Equation
(10.12), can be estimated by
$\left\{ 1 + {\hat{\theta}}^{- 1}{\hat{H}}_{0}(t) \right\}^{- \hat{\theta}}$
for a woman with a negatively stained tumour and
$\left\{ 1 + {\hat{\theta}}^{- 1}e^{\hat{\beta}}{\hat{H}}_{0}(t) \right\}^{- \hat{\theta}}$
for one with a positively stained tumour. These functions are shown in
[Figure 10.5](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_5){#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_5},
superimposed on the corresponding Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor
functions. Comparing this with [Figure 10.1](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_1),
we see that once allowance is made for frailty, the Weibull model is a
much better fit to the observed survival times. This figure also
provides a visual confirmation of the suitability of the fitted model.

<figure id="fig10_5" class="image">
<img src="images/fig10_5.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_5" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">10.5</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Fitted survivor functions for the Weibull
gamma frailty model, with the corresponding observed survivor functions,
for women with positive (<span class="middle">.......</span>) and
negative (—) staining.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The observable, or unconditional, hazard ratio, for a woman with a
positively stained tumour relative to one whose tumour was negatively
stained, derived from Equation (10.14), is given in [Figure
10.6](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_6){#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_6}. This
figure shows how the observable hazard ratio varies over time; the
hazard is much greater at earlier times, but declines quite rapidly.
This is due to the selection effect of frailty, whereby women who are
more frail die sooner. There are also many more early deaths in the
positively stained group, which is why the observed hazard ratio varies
in this way.

<figure id="fig10_6" class="image">
<img src="images/fig10_6.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_6" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">10.6</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Ratio of the hazard of death for a woman with
a positively stained tumour relative to one whose tumour is negatively
stained.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

</aside>
:::
::::

::::: section
## 10.5 Fitting semi-parametric frailty models {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5 .head1}

When no underlying parametric form is assumed for the baseline hazard
function, as in the Cox regression model, the procedure for fitting a
frailty model described in the previous section can no longer be used.
This is because the likelihood function in Expression (10.15) is no
longer fully specified. The approach that is now most widely implemented
in standard software packages involves maximising the sum of the partial
log-likelihood for the Cox []{#chapter10.xhtml_p315 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 315. " role="doc-pagebreak"}model that includes the
random effects,
$\log L_{p}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}})$, and the
log-likelihood of the random effects. This log-likelihood function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L_{p}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}) + {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\log}f(u_{i}),}
\end{array}$$[(10.24)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ is the vector of coefficients of *p*
explanatory variables in a Cox regression model, and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}$ is the vector of random effects for the *n*
individuals. The partial log-likelihood is []{#chapter10.xhtml_p316
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 316. " role="doc-pagebreak"}found by
replacing $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ in Equation
(3.6) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml) by
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + u_{i}$, to give

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L_{p}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}) = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}\left\{ {\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + u_{i} - \log{\sum\limits_{l \in R(t_{i})}\exp}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{l} + u_{l})} \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(10.25)]{.equationnum}

where *δ*~*i*~ is the event indicator and $R(t_{i})$ is the set of
patients at risk of death at time *t*~*i*~. The random effects,
*u*~*i*~, are the observed values of frailty random variables,
$U_{i} = \exp(Z_{i})$, where *Z*~*i*~ is usually taken to have either a
lognormal or a gamma distribution.

::: section
### 10.5.1 Lognormal frailty effects {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5_1 .head2}

When frailty effects are assumed to be lognormally distributed, the
random effects, *u*~*i*~, in Expression (10.24), are realisations of an
$N(0,\sigma_{u}^{2})$ random variable, with density

$$\begin{array}{r}
{f(u_{i}) = \frac{1}{\sigma_{u}\left. \sqrt{}(2\pi) \right.}\exp\left( {- \frac{u_{i}^{2}}{2\sigma_{u}^{2}}} \right).}
\end{array}$$

The log-likelihood function in Expression (10.24) is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L_{p}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}) - n\log\left\{ \sigma_{u}\left. \sqrt{}(2\pi) \right\} - \frac{1}{2\sigma_{u}^{2}}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}u_{i}^{2}}. \right.}
\end{array}$$[(10.26)]{.equationnum}

Since this log-likelihood is only used to estimate the *p* components of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ and the *n* components of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}$, the term involving $\sigma_{u}^{2}$ alone can be
omitted to give

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L_{pen}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}},\sigma_{u}^{2}) = \log L_{p}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma_{u}^{2}}{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}u_{i}^{2}}.}
\end{array}$$[(10.27)]{.equationnum}

This is known as a *penalised partial log-likelihood*, since the effect
of the second term is to assign penalties to the partial log-likelihood
function when the *u*~*i*~ have more extreme values, that is values that
are further from their expected value of zero. The $\sigma_{u}^{2}$ term
in Equation (10.27) essentially controls the relative importance of the
two components of this log-likelihood function.

The maximisation process proceeds iteratively by starting with an
initial estimate of $\sigma_{u}^{2}$ and finding estimates of the *β*s
and the *u*s that maximise
$\log L_{pen}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}},\sigma_{u}^{2})$.
Next, a *marginal likelihood*,
$L_{m}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\sigma_{u}^{2})$, is obtained by
integrating the likelihood,
$L_{p}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}})$ in Equation (10.25)
over the random effects *u*~*i*~, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L_{m}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\sigma_{u}^{2}) = {\int_{- \infty}^{\infty}L_{p}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}})f(\mathbf{\mathbf{u}})\,\text{d}\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}.}
\end{array}$$

[Ripatti and Palmgren
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib314){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib314
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} show that, at estimates
$\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$ of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$, the
logarithm of this marginal likelihood is well-approximated by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\sigma_{u}^{2}) = \log L_{pen}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}},\sigma_{u}^{2}) - \frac{1}{2}\log(\sigma_{u}^{2n}) - \frac{1}{2}\log \middle| \mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u} \middle| , \right.
\end{array}$$[(10.28)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u}$ is the $n \times n$ observed information
matrix for the random effects, formed from the negative second partial
derivatives of
$\log L_{pen}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}},\sigma_{u}^{2})$
with respect to the *u*~*i*~, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, evaluated at
$\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}}$, and
$\left| \mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u} \right|$ is the determinant of that
matrix. Using the estimates of the *β*s, the marginal log-likelihood in
Equation (10.28) is then maximised with respect to $\sigma_{u}^{2}$ to
give a revised estimate of $\sigma_{u}^{2}$. This process is repeated
until the difference between two successive estimates of
$\sigma_{u}^{2}$ is sufficiently small.

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p317 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 317. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Estimates of the random effects, ${\hat{u}}_{i}$,
and hence the frailty terms ${\hat{z}}_{i} = \exp({\hat{u}}_{i})$, are
also obtained from this iterative process. Moreover, the maximum
likelihood estimate of the variance of the random effect is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2} = n^{- 1}\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{\hat{u}}_{i}^{2}} + \,\text{trace}\,(\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u}^{- 1})} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where $\,\text{trace}\,(\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u}^{- 1})$ is the sum of
the diagonal elements of the inverse of $\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u}$. The
standard error of this estimate is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,({\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2}) = \left. \sqrt{}(2{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})\left\lbrack {n + \frac{1}{{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{4}}\,\text{trace}\,(\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u}^{- 1}\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u}^{- 1}) - 2\frac{1}{{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2}}\,\text{trace}\,(\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{u}^{- 1})} \right\rbrack^{- 1/2}. \right.}
\end{array}$$

Maximum likelihood estimates of variances tend to be biased, and instead
estimates based on the method of *restricted maximum likelihood (REML)*
estimation are preferred. For example, in the case of estimating the
variance of a single sample of observations, $x_{1},x_{2},\ldots,x_{n}$,
from a normal distribution, the maximum likelihood estimate of the
variance is the biased estimate
$n^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i}{(x_{i} - \overline{x})}^{2}}$ whereas the
corresponding REML estimate turns out to be the usual unbiased estimate
${(n - 1)}^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i}{(x_{i} - \overline{x})}^{2}}$.

REML estimates are obtained from a likelihood function that is
independent of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$, and the REML estimate of the
variance of the random frailty term is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\widetilde{\sigma}}_{u}^{2} = n^{- 1}\left\{ {{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{\widetilde{u}}_{i}^{2}} + \,\text{trace}\,({\widetilde{\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}}}_{u})} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where ${\widetilde{\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}}}_{u}$ is the estimated
variance-covariance matrix of the REML estimates of the *u*~*i*~,
${\widetilde{u}}_{i}$. The trace of this matrix is just the sum of the
estimated variances of the ${\widetilde{u}}_{i}$. This is the preferred
estimate of the variance of a normally distributed random frailty
effect. The standard error of ${\widetilde{\sigma}}_{u}^{2}$ can be
found from

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,({\widetilde{\sigma}}_{u}^{2}) = \left. \sqrt{}(2{\widetilde{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})\left\lbrack {n + \frac{1}{{\widetilde{\sigma}}_{u}^{4}}\,\text{trace}\,({\widetilde{\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}}}_{u}{\widetilde{\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}}}_{u}) - 2\frac{1}{{\widetilde{\sigma}}_{u}^{2}}\,\text{trace}\,({\widetilde{\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}}}_{u})} \right\rbrack^{- 1/2}. \right.}
\end{array}$$

Both ${\widetilde{\sigma}}_{u}^{2}$ and its standard error generally
feature in the output of software packages that have the facility for
fitting Cox regression models with lognormal frailty.

For fully parametric frailty models, the cumulative baseline hazard
function and the corresponding survivor function can be estimated using
the maximum likelihood estimates of the unknown parameters. However, in
semi-parametric frailty models, estimates of the baseline hazard and
cumulative hazard cannot be extended to take account of the frailty
terms, and so estimated survivor functions cannot easily be obtained.
:::

::: section
### 10.5.2 Gamma frailty effects {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5_2 .head2}

When the random variable associated with frailty effects is assumed to
have a gamma distribution with unit mean and variance $1/\theta$, the
corresponding random effects, *u*~*i*~, have an exp-gamma distribution,
introduced in [Section 10.2.1](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_2_1). Using the
density function in Equation (10.6), the log-likelihood function in
Expression (10.24) is now

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L_{p}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}) + n\theta\log\theta - n\log\Gamma(\theta) - \theta{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{(e^{u_{i}} - u_{i})}}.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p318 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 318. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}This log-likelihood function will be used to
estimate the components of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}$, and so terms involving *θ* alone can be omitted
to give the penalised partial log-likelihood function

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L_{pen}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}},\theta) = \log L_{p}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}},\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}) - \theta{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ e^{u_{i}} - u_{i} \right\}},}
\end{array}$$[(10.29)]{.equationnum}

in which
$\theta{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ e^{u_{i}} - u_{i} \right\}}$ is
the penalty term.

To obtain estimates of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$, $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}$
and *θ*, estimates of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}$ are first taken to be the values that maximise
Equation (10.29) for an initial value of *θ*. The marginal
log-likelihood for *θ*, at estimates $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$ and
$\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}}$ of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}$, is then used to obtain a revised estimate of *θ*.
This has been shown by [Therneau and Grambsch
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib380){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib380
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} to be given by

$$\begin{aligned}
{\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\theta)} & {= \log L_{pen}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}},\theta) + n\theta(1 + \log\theta)} \\
 & {\quad - {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ {(\theta + \delta_{i})\log(\theta + \delta_{i}) - \log\left\lbrack \frac{\Gamma(\theta + \delta_{i})}{\Gamma(\theta)} \right\rbrack} \right\}},}
\end{aligned}$$[(10.30)]{.equationnum}

where *δ*~*i*~ is the event indicator for the *i*th individual.
Maximising $\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\theta)$ in
Equation (10.30) with respect to *θ*, leads to $\hat{\theta}$. This
estimate is then used with Equation (10.29) to obtain updated estimates
of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ and $\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}$, and so on until
the process converges. No closed form estimate of *θ*, nor its standard
error, is available.

As the frailty variance, $1/\theta$, tends to zero, the marginal
log-likelihood in Equation (10.30) becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log L_{p}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}) - {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}},}
\end{array}$$

where $\log L_{p}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})$ is the maximised
partial log-likelihood for the Cox regression model when there is no
frailty. By taking the marginal log-likelihood to be
$\log L_{m}(\theta) + {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}$, the
maximised marginal log-likelihood in the presence of frailty is then
directly comparable to that for the model with no frailty. This is
helpful in comparing models, as we will see in [Section
10.6](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_6).
:::
:::::

:::: section
## 10.6 Comparing models with frailty {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_6 .head1}

In many cases, frailty acts as a nuisance term in the model. While it is
important to take account of its effects, the main interest is in
determining which explanatory variables are needed in a model, and
obtaining estimates of their effects, in the presence of frailty. The
methods described in [Section 5.7](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_7) of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), based on the change in the $- 2\log\hat{L}$
statistic, can be used to compare parametric models that incorporate
frailty. Model selection strategies for determining which factors to
include in a parametric model in the presence of frailty can then be
straightforwardly implemented. The fitted models can subsequently be
used to draw inferences about their effects, allowing for frailty.

The situation is more complicated for the Cox regression model. This is
because a penalised likelihood does not behave in the same way as the
usual likelihood or partial likelihood functions, since it contains
unobservable frailty effects. Instead, the maximised marginal
log-likelihood statistic is used, which is
$\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})$ or
$\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\theta})$ for lognormal
and gamma frailties, respectively. This statistic is generally given in
computer output from fitting Cox regression models that include frailty.
Differences in the values of
$- 2\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})$ or
$- 2\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\theta})$, or
$- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{m}$ for short, can be compared with percentage points
of a *χ*^2^ distribution in the usual manner. In the case of lognormal
frailty effects, the marginal likelihood based on the maximum likelihood
estimates of the *β*s is used, rather than that based on the REML
estimates.

::: section
### 10.6.1 []{#chapter10.xhtml_p319 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 319. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Testing for the presence of frailty {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_6_1 .head2}

In parametric models, the hypothesis of no frailty effects can be
evaluated by comparing $- 2\log\hat{L}$ values for models with and
without frailty effects, for a given set of covariates, with percentage
points of a chi-squared distribution on one degree of freedom,
$\chi_{1}^{2}$. However, some care is needed as the standard asymptotic
theory that underpins the result that differences in $- 2\log\hat{L}$
values have a chi-squared distribution, is no longer valid. Essentially
this is because the hypothesis of no frailty effect corresponds to
testing the hypothesis that the variance of the frailty term is zero,
and this is the smallest possible value that the variance can take.
Tests based on changes in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ then tend to be conservative,
and the resulting *P*-value for a difference will be larger than it
should be. In this situation, the difference in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ values,
when frailty is added, can be compared informally to percentage points
of a $\chi_{1}^{2}$ distribution. If the observed difference is
relatively large or small, conclusions about the degree of frailty will
be clear.

For a more precise determination of the significance of the frailty
effect, we can use the result that the asymptotic distribution of the
change in $- 2\log\hat{L}$, when a frailty term is added to a fully
parametric model, is an equally weighted mixture of $\chi_{0}^{2}$ and
$\chi_{1}^{2}$ distributions, written
$0.5(\chi_{0}^{2} + \chi_{1}^{2})$. A $\chi_{0}^{2}$ distribution has a
point mass at zero, and so if the random variable *W* has a
$0.5(\chi_{0}^{2} + \chi_{1}^{2})$ distribution, $\text{P}(W = 0) = 0.5$
and $\text{P}(W > w) = 0.5\text{P}(\chi_{1}^{2} > w)$ for $w > 0$.
Consequently, to apply this result, the *P*-value for testing the
hypothesis that the frailty variance is zero is just half of that
obtained from using a $\chi_{1}^{2}$ distribution.

In borderline cases, other methods may be needed, such as
*bootstrapping*. This procedure can be used to obtain an interval
estimate of the variance of a frailty term. Briefly, bootstrapping
involves resampling the data with replacement to give a new sample of
*n* survival times. The survival model that includes a frailty term is
then fitted to this sample, and the variance of the random effect is
then estimated. This is repeated a large number of times. The 2.5% and
97.5% percentiles of the distribution of the bootstrap variance
estimates then define a 95 per cent interval estimate for the variance.

In the case of the Cox regression model, fitted using the method of
penalised partial log-likelihood outlined in [Section
10.5](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5), the significance of a frailty term is
assessed using the $- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{m}$ statistic formed from the
maximised marginal log-likelihood. For lognormal frailty effects, the
maximised marginal log-likelihood for the model with frailty, obtained
from maximum likelihood estimation can be compared directly with the
maximised partial log-likelihood for the corresponding Cox regression
model without frailty. A similar process is used when gamma frailty
effects are assumed, but here the total number of events,
$\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}$, has to be added to the maximised
marginal log-likelihood, as explained in [Section
10.5.2](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5_2). As with parametric modelling, this
test procedure is only approximate, and bootstrapping may be required
for a more reliable assessment of the magnitude of the frailty effect.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam10_3" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 10.3]{.boxnum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10exam10_3){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival of patients registered for a lung
transplant]{.boxht}

The likely survival time of a patient from when they are assessed as
needing an organ transplant is of great interest to patients and
clinicians alike. In a study to quantify this survival time and to
identify factors associated with survival from listing for a lung
transplant, data were obtained on the UK patients who were registered
for a transplant in 2004. Survival times were measured from the date of
registration until death, regardless of all intervening treatments,
including transplantation. Survival was censored at the date of removal
from the list, the last known follow-up date, or at 30 April 2012 for
patients still alive. In addition, the age at registration, gender, body
mass index and primary disease were recorded, where primary disease was
categorised as fibrosis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD),
suppurative disease and other. There are 196 patients in the data set,
of whom 123 died during the study period. Data for the first 20 patients
are shown in [Table
10.2](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_2){#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_2}.

  Patient   Survival Time   Status   Age   Gender   BMI    Disease
  --------- --------------- -------- ----- -------- ------ -------------
   1        2324            1        59    1        29.6   COPD
   2         108            1        28    1        22.6   Suppurative
   3        2939            0        55    1        32.1   Fibrosis
   4        1258            0        62    1        30.0   Fibrosis
   5        2904            0        51    1        30.4   Fibrosis
   6         444            1        59    1        26.9   Fibrosis
   7         158            1        55    2        24.6   COPD
   8        1686            1        53    2        26.8   COPD
   9         142            1        47    1        32.2   Fibrosis
  10        1624            1        53    2        15.7   COPD
  11         16             1        62    1        26.4   Fibrosis
  12        2929            0        50    1        29.0   COPD
  13        1290            1        55    2        17.1   COPD
  14        2854            0        47    1        20.0   Other
  15         237            1        23    1        15.9   Suppurative
  16         136            1        65    1        16.0   COPD
  17        2212            1        24    2        19.5   Suppurative
  18         371            1        54    1        28.9   Fibrosis
  19         683            1        24    2        20.2   Suppurative
  20         290            1        53    1        25.2   Fibrosis

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [10.2]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_2){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times of 20 patients from listing for a
  lung transplant]{.table-title} {#tbl10_2}

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p320 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 320. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}We first fit a Weibull proportional hazards model
that contains age, gender, body mass index and primary disease. The
value of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic for this model is 2043.73. A
lognormal frailty term is now introduced, by adding a normally
distributed random effect to the linear component of the model, and
maximising the likelihood function in Expression (10.17). The Weibull
model with lognormal frailty has a $- 2\log\hat{L}$ value of 2026.85,
and the change in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ on adding the random effect is 16.88.
Comparing this reduction with percentage points of a $\chi_{1}^{2}$
distribution, the frailty effect is highly significant, with a *P*-value
of less than 0.001. This means that there is substantial heterogeneity
between the survival times of the patients in this study, after allowing
for the effects of the four explanatory variables. A less conservative
estimate of the significance of the frailty effect is found by referring
the change in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ to a $0.5(\chi_{0}^{2} + \chi_{1}^{2})$
distribution, equivalent to halving the *P*-value from a comparison with
a $\chi_{1}^{2}$ distribution, and it is then even more significant. The
variance of the normal random effect corresponding to the lognormal
frailty is ${\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2} = 2.254$. Using Equations (10.4) and
(10.5), the mean and variance of the lognormal frailty effect are 3.09
and 81.20, respectively.

We next fit a Weibull model that contains age, gender, body mass index
and primary disease, together with a gamma frailty effect. This model is
fitted by maximising the log-likelihood function in Expression (10.23),
from which the gamma frailty effect has variance
${\hat{\theta}}^{- 1} = 3.015$. The corresponding variance of the random
effect is calculated from Equation (10.8) and is 10.19. The Weibull
model with gamma frailty has a $- 2\log\hat{L}$ value of 2023.69, and so
addition of the frailty term leads to a reduction in the value of the
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic of 20.04. This change is highly significant
($P < 0.001)$, and greater than that when a lognormal frailty is
introduced.

In this example, the variance of the two distributions for the frailty
term, and the corresponding distributions of the random effect, are
quite different. To explore this in more detail, [Figures
10.7](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_7){#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_7} and
[10.8](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_8){#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_8} show the
fitted normal and exp-gamma probability density functions for the random
variable *U*, and the corresponding fitted lognormal and gamma
probability density functions for the frailty random variable, *Z*.

<figure id="fig10_7" class="image">
<img src="images/fig10_7.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_7" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">10.7</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Fitted probability density functions for the
normal (—) and exp-gamma (<span class="middle">.......</span>) random
effects.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig10_8" class="image">
<img src="images/fig10_8.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter10.xhtml_b10fig10_8" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">10.8</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Fitted probability density functions for the
lognormal (—) and gamma (<span class="middle">.......</span>) frailty
effects.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p321 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 321. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}[Figure 10.7](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_7) shows the
asymmetry in the exp-gamma distribution of the random effect, and
although the mode is zero, the mean, from Equation (10.7), is $- 2.05$.
This explains the disparity in the estimated variances of the normal and
exp-gamma distributions for the random effect. [Figure
10.8](#chapter10.xhtml_fig10_8) shows that the estimated density
functions of the frailty effect are quite similar when *z*~*i*~ exceeds
0.2. However, the density of the fitted gamma distribution tends to ∞ as
*z*~*i*~ tends to zero, whereas the fitted lognormal distribution is
unimodal. Also, the mean of the fitted gamma distribution is unity,
whereas from Equation (10.4), the fitted lognormal distribution has a
mean of 3.09, which is why the lognormal frailty variance is so much
greater than that for the gamma distribution.

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p322 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 322. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}To compare the parameter estimates for the various
terms and their standard errors, [Table
10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_3){#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_3} shows the
estimates for the different fitted Weibull models, including the
estimated variances of the random effects, *u*~*i*~, and the frailty
effects, *z*~*i*~, denoted $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{i})$ and
$\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(z_{i})$, respectively. Note that for a normal
random effect, the estimated variance is
$\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{i}) = {\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2}$, while for gamma
frailty, $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(z_{i}) = 1/\hat{\theta}$. Equation (10.5)
is then used to obtain the variance of a lognormal frailty term and
Equation (10.8) gives the variance of the random effect corresponding to
gamma frailty.

  Term                              No Frailty          Lognormal Frailty   Gamma Frailty
  --------------------------------- ------------------- ------------------- -------------------
  Age                                0.015 (0.012)       0.014 (0.011)       0.003 (0.020)
  Gender: Male                       0.080 (0.198)       0.226 (0.323)       0.433 (0.412)
  BMI                               $- 0.024$ (0.024)   $- 0.029$ (0.036)   $- 0.014$ (0.047)
  Disease: COPD                     $- 0.675$ (0.377)   $- 0.960$ (0.585)   $- 1.008$ (0.692)
  Disease: Fibrosis                  0.363 (0.344)       0.887 (0.564)      1.383 (0.722)
  Disease: Other                    $- 0.254$ (0.391)   $- 0.231$ (0.617)   $- 0.076$ (0.766)
  Weibull shape $(\hat{\gamma})$     0.663 (0.051)      1.040 (0.133)       1.304 (0.231)
  Weibull scale $(\hat{\lambda})$    0.0061 (0.0042)     0.0004 (0.0005)     0.0003 (0.0004)
  $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{i})$     --                  81.197              10.188
  $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(z_{i})$     --                  2.254               3.015
  $- 2\log\hat{L}$                  2043.73             2026.85             2023.69

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [10.3]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_3){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Parameter estimates (standard error) in fitted
  Weibull models]{.table-title} {#tbl10_3}

This table shows that there is a general tendency for the parameter
estimates to be further from zero when a frailty term is added, and the
standard errors are also larger. However, inferences about the impact of
the factors on patient survival from listing are not much affected by
the inclusion of a frailty term, and there is little difference in the
results obtained when either a lognormal or gamma frailty effect is
included. The only factor to have any effect on patient survival is
primary disease, with the hazard of death in patients with COPD being
less than that for patients with other diseases. Also, on the basis of
the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic, the model with gamma frailty might be
preferred to that with lognormal frailty.

To avoid making specific assumptions about the form of the underlying
baseline hazard function, we fit a Cox regression model that contains
age, gender, body mass index and primary disease, and that also includes
a lognormal or gamma frailty term. These models are fitted by maximising
the penalised log-likelihood functions in Equations (10.27) and (10.29),
respectively. To test the hypothesis that all frailty effects are zero
in the presence of the four explanatory variables, we compare the value
of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic for the fitted Cox model, which is
1159.101, with values of the maximised marginal log-likelihood
statistic, $- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{m}$, for Cox models with frailty.

When a lognormal frailty term is added, the value of the maximised
marginal log-likelihood statistic,
$- 2\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})$
from Equation (10.28), is 1157.480, and this leads to a change in the
value of the $- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{m}$ statistic of 1.62, which is not
significant ($P = 0.203$).

On fitting models with gamma frailty, the value of the
$\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\theta})$ statistic from
Equation (10.30) is $- 699.653$, and adding the observed number of
deaths, 123, to this and multiplying by $- 2$, gives 1153.306. This can
be compared with the value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for the Cox regression
model that contains the same explanatory variables, but no frailty
effects, 1159.101. The reduction in the value of this test statistic is
5.80, which is significant when compared to percentage points of a
*χ*^2^ distribution on 1 d.f. (*P* = 0.016). This now shows a
significant frailty effect after allowing for survival differences due
to primary disease and the other factors.

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p323 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 323. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The variances of the normal and exp-gamma random
effects are smaller in the Cox model than in the corresponding Weibull
model, and the frailty effect is less significant. This suggests that
the fitted Cox regression model explains more of the variation in
survival times from listing for a transplant than a Weibull model.

To compare the parameter estimates for the various terms in a Cox
regression model and their standard errors, [Table
10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_4){#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_4} shows the
estimates for the different models fitted, and the estimated variances
of the random effects, $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{i})$.

  Term                            No Frailty          Lognormal Frailty   Gamma Frailty
  ------------------------------- ------------------- ------------------- -------------------
  Age                              0.013 (0.012)       0.012 (0.013)       0.006 (0.018)
  Gender: Male                     0.078 (0.198)       0.114 (0.232)       0.306 (0.354)
  BMI                             $- 0.023$ (0.024)   $- 0.026$ (0.028)   $- 0.024$ (0.043)
  Disease: COPD                   $- 0.621$ (0.371)   $- 0.681$ (0.425)   $- 0.888$ (0.603)
  Disease: Fibrosis                0.377 (0.340)       0.553 (0.398)      1.227 (0.607)
  Disease: Other                  $- 0.218$ (0.386)   $- 0.198$ (0.448)   $- 0.089$ (0.668)
  $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{i})$   --                   0.522              2.155
  $- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{m}$          1159.10             1157.48             1153.31

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [10.4]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_4){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Parameter estimates (standard error) in fitted
  Cox models]{.table-title} {#tbl10_4}

As with the Weibull model, some parameter estimates are larger when a
frailty term is added and the standard errors are larger, but the
parameter estimates in [Tables 10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_3) and
[10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_4) are broadly similar. As for the Weibull
model, the gamma frailty model leads to the smallest value of the
$- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{m}$ statistic and is a better fit to the data.

Estimates of frailty effects can be obtained using the approach outlined
in [Section 10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_4). For example, from estimates
of the frailty terms, ${\hat{z}}_{i}$, for the Cox model with lognormal
frailty, there are 7 patients with values of ${\hat{z}}_{i}$ greater
than 3, namely patients 11, 36, 69, 70, 87, 113 and 163. The survival
times of these patients are 16, 21, 3, 4, 38, 22 and 35 days
respectively, and so the patients with largest frailty are those whose
survival is shortest, as expected.

</aside>
:::
::::

::::: section
## 10.7 The shared frailty model {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_7 .head1}

Models that seek to explain heterogeneity in the survival times of
individuals can provide improved estimates of the effects of covariates,
or help to explain features such as non-proportional hazards. However,
frailty models are particularly useful in modelling situations where
there is some characteristic whose values are shared by groups of
individuals. Some examples of potential areas of application were given
in [Section 10.1.3](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_1_3).

To formulate a shared frailty model for survival data, suppose that
there are *g* groups of individuals with *n*~*i*~ individuals in the
*i*th group, $i = 1,2,\ldots,g$. For the proportional hazards model, the
hazard of death at time *t* for the *j*th individual,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,n_{i}$, in the *i*th group, is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{ij}(t) = z_{i}\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(10.31)]{.equationnum}

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij}$ is a vector of values of *p*
explanatory variables for the *j*th individual in the *i*th group,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$ is the vector of their coefficients,
$h_{0}(t)$ is the baseline hazard function, and the *z*~*i*~ are frailty
effects that are common for all *n*~*i*~ individuals within the *i*th
group. The hazard function in Equation (10.31) can also be written in
the form

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{ij}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij} + u_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $u_{i} = \log(z_{i})$ and are assumed to be realisations of *g*
random variables, $U_{1},U_{2},\ldots,U_{g}$. The distribution assumed
for *U*~*i*~ is taken to have zero mean, and the normal distribution is
a common choice.

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p324 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 324. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The form of the baseline hazard may be fully
specified as in a Weibull model, or unspecified as in the Cox model. The
general parametric accelerated failure time model that incorporates a
shared frailty component is, from Equation (5.60) in [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), of the form

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{ij}(t) = e^{- \eta_{ij}}h_{0}(t/e^{\eta_{ij}}),}
\end{array}$$

where
$\eta_{ij} = \mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij} + u_{i}$.
Equivalently, extending Equation (5.61), this model can be expressed in
log-linear form as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log T_{ij} = \mu + \mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij} + u_{i} + \sigma\epsilon_{ij},}
\end{array}$$

where $T_{ij}$ is the random variable associated with the survival time
of the *j*th individual in the *i*th group, *μ* and *σ* are intercept
and scale parameters, and $\epsilon_{ij}$ has some specified probability
distribution.

::: section
### 10.7.1 Fitting the shared frailty model {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_7_1 .head2}

Suppose that the survival times for the *j*th individual in the *i*th
group are denoted $t_{ij}$, for $j = 1,2,\ldots,n_{i}$ and
$i = 1,2,\ldots,g$, and write $\delta_{ij}$ for the corresponding event
indicator, which is unity if $t_{ij}$ is an event time and zero
otherwise. Also, let $S_{ij}(t)$ and $h_{ij}(t)$ be the survivor and
hazard functions for the *j*th individual in the *i*th group,
respectively. For a fully parametric model, the likelihood function for
the observations in the *i*th group, when the frailty terms are known,
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L_{i}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}) = {\prod\limits_{j = 1}^{n_{i}}h_{ij}}{(t_{ij})}^{\delta_{ij}}S_{ij}(t_{ij}).}
\end{array}$$

As in [Section 10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_4), we next integrate this
likelihood over the *u*~*i*~ to give

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\int_{- \infty}^{\infty}L_{i}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})f(u_{i})\,\text{d}u_{i},}
\end{array}$$

where $f(u_{i})$ is the probability density function of *U*~*i*~. Over
the *g* groups, the likelihood function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}) = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{g}{\int_{- \infty}^{\infty}L_{i}}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})f(u_{i})\,\text{d}u_{i}.}
\end{array}$$

As in the case of individual frailty effects, the integration can only
be carried out analytically if the frailty effects have a
$\Gamma(\theta,\theta)$ distribution. In this case, the likelihood
function for the *i*th group is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{j = 1}^{n_{i}}\left\{ {e^{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij}}h_{0}(t_{ij})} \right\}^{\delta_{ij}}}\frac{\theta^{\theta}\Gamma(\theta + d_{i})}{\Gamma(\theta)\left\{ \theta + {\sum\limits_{j}\exp}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij})H_{0}(t_{ij}) \right\}^{\theta + d_{i}}},}
\end{array}$$

where $d_{i} = {\sum\limits_{i}\delta_{ij}}$ is the number of deaths in
the *i*th group. The corresponding log-likelihood over the *g* groups is

$$\begin{aligned}
{\log L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})} & {= {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{g}\left\{ {\log\Gamma(\theta + d_{i}) - \log\Gamma(\theta) - d_{i}\log\theta} \right\}}} \\
 & {- {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{g}{(\theta + d_{i})}}\log\left\lbrack {1 + \theta^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n_{i}}\exp}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij})H_{0}(t_{ij})} \right\rbrack} \\
 & {+ {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{g}{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n_{i}}\delta_{ij}}}\left\lbrack {\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij} + \log h_{0}(t_{ij})} \right\rbrack,}
\end{aligned}$$

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p325 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 325. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}which can be maximised to give estimates of *θ*,
the parameters in the baseline hazard function, and the *β*s.

Once this model has been fitted, estimates of the frailty effects,
${\hat{z}}_{i}$, can be obtained in the same manner as described in
[Section 10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_4), and we find that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{z}}_{i} = \frac{\hat{\theta} + d_{i}}{\hat{\theta} + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n_{i}}\exp}({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij}){\hat{H}}_{0}(t_{ij})},}
\end{array}$$

with standard error ${\hat{z}}_{i}/(\hat{\theta} + d_{i})$.

Cox regression models with frailty can again be fitted using penalised
partial log-likelihood methods, as in [Section
10.5](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5). To adapt the formulae given in that
section to the case of shared frailty models, the event indicator
*δ*~*i*~ is replaced by *d*~*i*~, the number of deaths in the *i*th of
*g* groups, and summations over $i = 1$ to *n* are replaced by
summations over $i = 1$ to *g*.
:::

::: section
### 10.7.2 Comparing shared frailty models {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_7_2 .head2}

Again, the results given in [Section 10.6](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_6)
apply equally to shared frailty models. In particular, fully parametric
models that include a shared frailty term can be compared using the
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic. To test for frailty, the change in the value
of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic on adding frailty effects can be
formally compared with percentage points of a
$0.5(\chi_{0}^{2} + \chi_{1}^{2})$ distribution, as in [Section
10.6.1](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_6_1).

For the Cox model with shared frailty that is fitted using penalised
partial log-likelihood methods, the marginal log-likelihood statistics,
$- 2\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})$
and $- 2\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\theta})$, can be
used to compare models with lognormal or gamma frailties, respectively.
These statistics can also be compared to the value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$
for the Cox regression model without frailty, to assess the extent of
frailty effects. However, the limitations of this approach, outlined in
[Section 10.6.1](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_6_1), also apply here. Note that
in the case of gamma frailty, the quantity
$\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{g}d_{i}$ needs to be added to
$\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\theta})$ to ensure
comparability with the maximised partial log-likelihood in the absence
of frailty, as for univariate frailty.

Estimates of the random effects can be found using the method described
in [Section 10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_4). Such estimates are
particularly useful when the frailty term represents centres in a
multicentre study, since the rank order of the estimated centre effects
provides information about the merits of the different centres in terms
of patient survival. But here, the estimated coefficients of the
explanatory variables in the model are interpreted as conditional on the
shared frailty. This means that hazard ratios relate to a comparison of
effects in individuals within the same group. Estimates of the random
effects also lead to estimates of the survivor function for individuals
with given characteristics, and median survival times can easily be
obtained. No new principles are involved.

We conclude with an example that illustrates some of these features.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam10_4" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 10.4]{.boxnum}](#chapter17.xhtml_b10exam10_4){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival following kidney transplantation]{.boxht}

Deceased organ donors generally donate both kidneys which are
subsequently transplanted into two different recipients, often by
different transplant centres. In modelling short-term graft and patient
survival, account needs to be taken of a number of factors associated
with the recipient, and factors associated with the transplant procedure
itself. This illustration is based on the outcomes of all kidney
transplants carried out in a particular year, using donors who are
deceased following circulatory death. The outcome variable is transplant
survival, defined as the earlier of graft failure or death with a
functioning graft. Of the many explanatory factors that were recorded,
patient age (years), diabetes status (0 = absent, 1 = present) and the
cold ischaemic time (CIT), the time in hours between retrieval of the
kidney from the donor and transplantation into the recipient, are used
in this example.

[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p326 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 326. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In addition to these recipient and transplant
factors, donor factors may also have an impact on the outcome of the
transplant. Although many factors related to the donor are routinely
recorded, it is difficult to take account of everything. Moreover, the
survival times of the patients who receive the two kidneys from the same
donor may be expected to be more highly correlated than the times for
recipients of kidneys from different donors. One way of taking account
of donor factors, while allowing for association between the outcomes
when organs from the same donor are used, is to include the donor as a
shared frailty effect. The frailty term is then the same in models for
the survival times of two recipients of kidneys from the same donor.

In this study there were 434 transplants using organs from 270 deceased
donors. Of these, 106 gave one kidney and 164 donated both kidneys. Data
for the 22 recipients of kidneys from the first 15 donors in the year
are shown in [Table
10.5](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_5){#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_5}.

  Patient   Donor   Survival Time   Status   Age   Diabetes   CIT
  --------- ------- --------------- -------- ----- ---------- ------
   1         1      1436            0        61    0          14.5
   2         2      1442            0        30    0           7.2
   3         3       310            1        62    0          14.3
   4         4      1059            1        62    0          10.6
   5         5       236            1        50    0          18.7
   6         6      1372            0        65    0          19.3
   7         6       382            1        71    0          12.2
   8         7       736            1        46    0          13.6
   9         7      1453            0        66    0          13.9
  10         8      1122            0        53    0          18.0
  11         8      1010            0        42    0          13.8
  12         9      1403            0        44    0          12.9
  13        10      1449            0        67    0          13.9
  14        11       843            1        34    0          16.6
  15        12      1446            0        32    0          10.3
  16        12      1397            0        51    0          19.0
  17        13      1301            0        75    0          12.5
  18        13      1442            0        47    0          11.3
  19        14      1445            0        69    0          18.8
  20        14      1445            0        53    0          23.2
  21        15      1445            0        46    0          15.5
  22        15      1445            0        49    0          17.2

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [10.5]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_5){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times of 22 patients following kidney
  transplantation]{.table-title} {#tbl10_5}

On fitting a Cox regression model containing cold ischaemic time, and
the age and diabetes status of the recipient, the value of the
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic is 831.87. To take account of donor effects,
a lognormal shared frailty effect is added, and the $- 2\log\hat{L}$
statistic decreases to 831.46. This reduction of 0.41 on 1 d.f. is not
significant ($P = 0.52$) when compared to a $\chi_{1}^{2}$ distribution
or a $0.5(\chi_{0}^{2} + \chi_{1}^{2})$ distribution. This indicates
that there are no frailty effects.

Parameter estimates in the fitted Cox regression model without frailty,
and with lognormal frailty included, are shown in [Table
10.6](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_6){#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_6}.

  Term                            No Frailty          Lognormal Frailty   Gamma Frailty
  ------------------------------- ------------------- ------------------- -------------------
  Age                              0.019 (0.010)       0.019 (0.010)       0.019 (0.008)
  Diabetes                        $- 0.220$ (0.436)   $- 0.254$ (0.449)   $- 0.258$ (0.458)
  CIT                              0.024 (0.021)       0.024 (0.022)       0.024 (0.020)
  $\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{i})$   --                   0.278               0.306
  $- 2\log{\hat{L}}_{m}$          831.87              831.46              831.51

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [10.6]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter10.xhtml_b10tbl10_6){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Parameter estimates (standard error) in Cox
  regression models fitted to the data on transplant survival following
  a kidney transplant]{.table-title} {#tbl10_6}

There is evidence that the age of a patient affects transplant survival,
but after taking account of this, there is no evidence that diabetes
status and the cold ischaemic time affect the outcome for this group of
patients. Also, the effect these three variables on transplant survival
is unaffected by donor effects.

When a gamma frailty is added to a Cox regression model that contains
the same explanatory variables, the value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ is very
similar to that of the other models, and as shown in [Table
10.6](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_6), the parameter estimates are practically
identical. Very similar results are obtained when a Weibull distribution
is assumed for the survival times. In summary, []{#chapter10.xhtml_p327
.pagebreak aria-label=" page 327. " role="doc-pagebreak"}taking proper
account of the donor effects does not materially affect inferences about
the impact of explanatory factors on short-term survival of these
transplant recipients.

</aside>
:::
:::::

::::::: section
## 10.8 Some other aspects of frailty modelling {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_8 .head1}

A number of other issues that arise in connection with frailty modelling
are discussed briefly in this section.

::: section
### 10.8.1 Model checking {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_8_1 .head2}

The techniques for model checking described in [Chapters
4](#chapter4.xhtml) and [7](#chapter7.xhtml) may reveal outlying
observations, influential values and inadequacies in the functional form
of covariates. In addition, an informal way of assessing the adequacy of
a parametric model with gamma frailty is to compare the observed
survivor function with the observable function derived from a frailty
model, as in [Example 10.2](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_2). In more complex
problems, the baseline survivor function estimated from fitting a Cox
model with the same explanatory variables as the frailty model, can be
compared to the observable survivor function for an individual with all
explanatory variables set to zero. For other choices of frailty
distribution the observable survivor function can only be determined
numerically, and so this approach is not so useful.

In frailty modelling, the validity of the chosen frailty distribution
may also be critically examined. Two particular distributional models
have been described in this chapter, the gamma and lognormal
distributions, but there are a number of other possibilities. Which of
these frailty distributions is chosen is often guided by mathematical
convenience and the availability of statistical software. One approach
to discriminating between alternative frailty distributions is to
consider a general family that includes specific distributions as
special cases. The *power variance function (PVF) distribution* is
widely used in this context. This distribution has a density function
that is a function of three parameters, *α, δ* and *θ*, and whose mean
and variance are given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{E}\,(Z) = \delta\theta^{\alpha - 1},\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{var}\,(Z) = \delta(1 - \alpha)\theta^{\alpha - 2},}
\end{array}$$

for $\theta\geqslant 0$, $0 < \alpha\leqslant 1$, $\delta > 0$. Setting
$\delta = \theta^{1 - \alpha}$ gives a distribution with unit mean and
variance $(1 - \alpha)/\theta$. When $\alpha = 0$ the distribution
reduces to a gamma density with variance $\theta^{- 1}$ and when
$\alpha = 0.5$ to an inverse Gaussian distribution. Tests of hypotheses
about the value of *α* can then inform model choice.

Another approach to model checking is to assess the sensitivity of key
model-based inferences to the specific choice of frailty model.
Comparing hazard ratios from a fully parametric models with those from a
Cox regression model that has the same frailty distribution can also be
valuable.
:::

::: section
### 10.8.2 []{#chapter10.xhtml_p328 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 328. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Correlated frailty models {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_8_2 .head2}

An important extension of shared frailty models is to the situation
where the frailties of individuals within a group are not identical as
in the shared frailty model, but are merely correlated. Such models are
particularly relevant when interest centres on the association between
event times, as might be the case when studying event times of paired
organs, or amongst twins for example. In the bivariate frailty case,
correlated frailty can be modelled by extending the model in Equation
(10.31) to have a separate frailty for each member of the pair. Then, in
the *i*th pair, the hazard function is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{ij}(t) = z_{ij}\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{ij})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

for $j = 1,2$, and a bivariate distribution is adopted for the
corresponding frailty random variables $(Z_{i1},Z_{i2})$. In the case of
lognormal frailties, a bivariate normal distribution could be assumed
for the corresponding random effects $U_{ij} = \log(Z_{ij})$ in the
linear component of the model, with
$\,\text{corr}\,(U_{i1},U_{i2}) = \rho$. This correlation need not be
the same for each group. The extent of this correlation can then be
evaluated. Such models may be fitted using likelihood or penalised
likelihood methods, but can also be fitted using Monte Carlo Markov
Chain (MCMC) methods, described in [Chapter 16](#chapter16.xhtml).
:::

::: section
### 10.8.3 Dependence measures {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_8_3 .head2}

When a shared frailty model is being used to account for association
between the event times of individuals within a group, a large frailty
variance corresponds to a high degree of dependence between the times.
In this sense, the frailty variance is a measure of dependence, and the
extent of this dependence can be compared in alternative models. For
example, on adding certain covariates, the frailty effect may diminish
and so a reduction in the frailty variance would indicate that the
revised model has been successful in reducing unexplained variation.

Other measures of dependence in parametric models can be based on
correlation. In the special case of gamma frailty distributions,
Kendall\'s coefficient of rank correlation, *τ*, is a useful measure of
association between a pair of survival times. Kendall\'s *τ* is simply
related to the frailty variance $\theta^{- 1}$ and is found from
$\tau = {(1 + 2\theta)}^{- 1}$. The advantage of this is that it yields
a summary statistic of dependence in the range $(0,1)$.
:::

::: section
### 10.8.4 Numerical problems in model fitting {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_8_4 .head2}

Frailty models are generally fitted using a combination of numerical
integration and optimisation. For this reason, it is important to
specify suitable initial values for the unknown parameters. These are
best obtained from fitting the corresponding model without frailty, and
using a small value for the initial frailty variance. Even then,
numerical procedures can lead to computational problems in the fitting
process, and instability in the resulting estimates. This is
particularly the case when the parameters being estimated have very
different scales. For instance, in [Example
10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_3){#chapter10.xhtml_b10exam10_3},
estimates of the Weibull shape parameter, *λ*, are much smaller than
estimates of the other parameters, leading to convergence problems. Such
difficulties can often be overcome by changing the time scale. For
example, converting a survival time in days to one in months or years,
by diving by 30.44 or 365.25, respectively, will often lead to
convergence, and this works for [Example
10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_3). Other possibilities include rescaling
certain parameters, for example by working with
$\lambda' = 10000\lambda$, or by reparameterising the model. Variance
parameters such as $\sigma_{u}^{2}$ in a normal random effect may be
recoded by estimating $\omega = \log\sigma_{u}^{2}$, and writing
$\exp(\omega)$ in place of $\sigma_{u}^{2}$ in the model. If changes
such as these are not successful, some changes may be needed to the
settings used in the numerical integration process, or the criteria that
define convergence of the optimisation process.
:::
:::::::

::: section
## 10.9 []{#chapter10.xhtml_p329 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 329. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Further reading {#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_9 .head1}

[Hougaard (2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib186){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib186
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} includes an extensive discussion of
frailty models, and a number of illustrative examples. More recently,
the books of [Hanagal
(2019)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib163){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib163
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Wienke
(2011)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib416){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib416
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Duchateau and Janssen
(2008)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib105){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib105
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} both give thorough accounts of frailty
modelling. General review papers include those of [Aalen
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib3){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib3 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Aalen
(1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib4){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib4 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Hougaard
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib184){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib184
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. [Balan and Putter
(2020)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib29){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib29 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} provide an overview of the theory and practice of
frailty modelling. General texts on mixed models include [Brown and
Prescott (2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib50){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib50
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Demidenko
(2013)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib99){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib99 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [West, Welch and Galecki
(2007)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib411){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib411
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Stroup
(2013)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib366){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib366
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Texts that include accounts of
restricted maximum likelihood estimation (REML) include those of
[McCulloch, Searle and Neuhaus
(2008)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib254){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib254
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Searle, Casella and McCulloch
(2006)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib345){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib345
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}

[McGilchrist and Aisbett
(1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib255){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib255
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} describe how a Cox model with normal
random effects can be fitted and illustrate this with a much discussed
example on times to the recurrence of infection following insertion of a
catheter in patients on dialysis. Their approach is generalised in
[Ripatti and Palmgren (2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib314){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} who show how random frailty effects that have a
multivariate distribution can be fitted using penalised partial
log-likelihood methods. [Therneau and Grambsch
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib380){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}
include a chapter on frailty models and describe how penalised partial
log-likelihood methods can be used to fit Cox models with frailty and to
test hypotheses about parameters in the fitted models; see also
[Therneau, Grambsch and Pankratz
(2003)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib382){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib382
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. An alternative fitting method is based
on the expectation-maximisation (EM) algorithm, described by [Klein
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib210){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib210
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Klein and Moeschberger
(2005)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib212){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib212
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

Instead of comparing Cox regression models with frailty using the
maximised marginal log-likelihood, Wald tests can be used. This approach
has been described by [Therneau and Grambsch
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib380){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and
uses the notion of generalised degrees of freedom, described by [Gray
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib157){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib157
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The result that the change in
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ on adding a frailty effect to a parametric model has an
asymptotic $0.5(\chi_{0}^{2} + \chi_{1}^{2})$ distribution was given in
[Claeskens, Nguti and Janssen
(2008)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib66){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib66 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

[Lambert et al.
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib226){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib226
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Nelson et al.
(2006)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib276){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib276
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} show how the probability integral
transformation can be used to fit parametric accelerated failure time
models with non-normal random frailty effects. This allows other
distributions to be adopted when using computer software designed for
fitting models with normal random effects.

There has been relatively little published on methods for checking the
adequacy of frailty models, with most emphasis on testing the validity
of a gamma frailty model. One of the first papers to develop a test for
the assumption of a gamma frailty distribution was [Shih and Louis
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib348){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib348
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and a goodness of fit test for use
with bivariate survival data was given by [Shih
(1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib347){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib347
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Some extensions are described by
[Glidden (1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib146){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib146
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. More recently a formal goodness of fit
test is given in [Geerdens, Claeskens and Janssen
(2013)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib135){#chapter10.xhtml_b10refbib135
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who also briefly review other work in
this area.[]{#chapter10.xhtml_p330 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 330 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}
:::

[]{#chapter11.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter11.xhtml_p331 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 331. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 11]{.chapnum}[Non-proportional hazards and institutional comparisons]{.chaptitle} {#chapter11.xhtml_chapter11 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-11](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-11){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

Proportional hazards models are widely used in modelling survival data
in medical research and other application areas. This assumption of
proportionality means that the effect of an explanatory variable or
factor on the hazard of an event occurring does not depend on time. This
can be quite a strong assumption, and situations frequently arise where
this assumption is untenable. Although models that do not assume
proportional hazards have been described in earlier chapters, some
additional methods are described in this chapter. A particularly
important application of non-proportional hazards modelling is the
comparison of survival rates between institutions, and so methods that
can be used in this context are also described.

:::: section
## 11.1 Non-proportional hazards {#chapter11.xhtml_sec11_1 .head1}

Models that do not require the assumption of proportional hazards
include the accelerated failure time model and the proportional odds
model introduced in [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), and the Cox regression
model that includes a time-dependent variable, described in [Chapter
8](#chapter8.xhtml). But often we are faced with a situation where the
assumption of proportional hazards cannot be made, and yet none of the
above models is satisfactory.

As an illustration, consider a study to compare a surgical procedure
with chemotherapy in the treatment of a particular form of cancer.
Suppose that the survivor functions []{#chapter11.xhtml_p332 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 332. " role="doc-pagebreak"}under the two treatments
are as shown in [Figure
11.1](#chapter11.xhtml_fig11_1){#chapter11.xhtml_b11fig11_1}, where the
time-scale is in years. Clearly the hazards are non-proportional. Death
at an early stage may be experienced by patients on the surgical
treatment, as a result of patients not being able to withstand the
surgery or complications arising from it. In the longer term, patients
who have recovered from the surgery have a better prognosis. A similar
situation arises when an aggressive form of chemotherapy is compared to
a standard. Here also, a long-term advantage to the aggressive treatment
may be at the expense of short-term excess mortality.

<figure id="fig11_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig11_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter11.xhtml_b11fig11_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">11.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Long-term advantage of surgery (—) over
chemotherapy (<span
class="middle">.......</span>).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In the study that leads to the survivor functions shown in [Figure
11.1](#chapter11.xhtml_fig11_1), it is clear that an analysis of the
two-year survival rate will be appropriate. Now consider a study to
compare the use of chemotherapy in addition to surgery with surgery
alone, in which the survivor functions are as shown in [Figure
11.2](#chapter11.xhtml_fig11_2){#chapter11.xhtml_b11fig11_2}. Here, the
short-term benefit of the chemotherapy may certainly be worthwhile, but
an analysis of the two-year survival rates will fail to establish a
