In a similar manner, since $\left. U \right.\sim N(\theta_{R}V,V)$ when
$\theta = \theta_{R}$,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(U < - k;\theta = \theta_{R}) = \Phi\left( \frac{- k - \theta_{R}V}{\left. \sqrt{}(V) \right.} \right) \approx 1 - \beta,}
\end{array}$$

and so we take

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{- k - \theta_{R}V}{\left. \sqrt{}(V) \right.} = z_{\beta},}
\end{array}$$

where *z*~*β*~ is the upper *β*-point of the standard normal
distribution. If we now substitute for *k* from Equation (15.6), we get

$$\begin{array}{r}
{- z_{\alpha/2}\left. \sqrt{}(V) - \theta_{R}V = z_{\beta}\left. \sqrt{}(V), \right. \right.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter15.xhtml_p411 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 411. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and so *V* needs to be such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{V = {(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})}^{2}/\theta_{R}^{2},}
\end{array}$$[(15.7)]{.equationnum}

to meet the specified requirements.

When the number of deaths is few relative to the number at risk, the
expression for *V* in Equation (15.3) is approximately

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\frac{n_{1j}n_{2j}d_{j}}{n_{j}^{2}}}.}
\end{array}$$[(15.8)]{.equationnum}

Moreover, if *θ* is small, and recruitment to each treatment group
proceeds at a similar rate, then $n_{1j} \approx n_{2j}$, for
$j = 1,2,\ldots,r$, and so

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{n_{1j}n_{2j}}{n_{j}^{2}} = \frac{n_{1j}n_{2j}}{{(n_{1j} + n_{2j})}^{2}} \approx \frac{n_{1j}^{2}}{{(2n_{1j})}^{2}} = \frac{1}{4}.}
\end{array}$$

Then, *V* is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{V \approx {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}d_{j}}/4 = d/4,}
\end{array}$$

where $d = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}d_{j}}$ is the total number of deaths
among the individuals in the study.

Finally, using Equation (15.7), we now require *d* to be such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{d}{4} = \frac{{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})}^{2}}{\theta_{R}^{2}},}
\end{array}$$

which leads to the required number of deaths being that given in
Equation (15.1).

At later death times, that is, when the values of *j* in Expression
(15.8) are close to *r*, the numbers of individuals at risk in the two
groups will be small. This is likely to mean that $n_{1j}$ and $n_{2j}$
will be quite different at the later death times, and so
$n_{1j}n_{2j}/n_{j}^{2}$ will be less than 0.25. This in turn means that
$V < d/4$ and so the required number of deaths will tend to be
underestimated.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam15_1" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 15.1]{.boxnum}](#chapter15.xhtml_b15exam15_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival from chronic active hepatitis]{.boxht}

Patients suffering from chronic active hepatitis rapidly progress to an
early death from liver failure. A new treatment has become available and
so a clinical trial is planned to evaluate the effect of this new
treatment on the survival times of patients suffering from the disease.
As a first step, information is obtained on the survival times in years
of patients in a similar age range who have received the standard
therapy. The Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function derived from
such data is shown in [Figure
15.1](#chapter15.xhtml_fig15_1){#chapter15.xhtml_b15fig15_1}.

<figure id="fig15_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig15_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter15.xhtml_b15fig15_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">15.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated survivor function for patients
receiving a standard treatment for hepatitis.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

From this estimate of the survivor function, the median survival time is
3.3 years, and the survival rates at two, four and six years can be
taken to be given by $S(2) = 0.70$, $S(4) = 0.45$, and $S(6) = 0.25$.

The new treatment is expected to increase the survival rate at five
years from 0.41, the value under the standard treatment, to 0.60. This
information can be used to calculate a value for *θ*~*R*~. To do this,
we use the result that if the hazard functions are assumed to be
proportional, the survivor function for an individual on the new
treatment at time *t* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{N}(t) = {\lbrack S_{S}(t)\rbrack}^{\psi},}
\end{array}$$[(15.9)]{.equationnum}

where $S_{S}(t)$ is the survivor function for an individual on the
standard treatment at *t* and *ψ* is the hazard ratio. Therefore,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\psi = \frac{\log S_{N}(t)}{\log S_{S}(t)},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter15.xhtml_p412 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 412. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and so the value of *ψ* corresponding to an
increase in $S(t)$ from 0.41 to 0.60 is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\psi_{R} = \frac{\log(0.60)}{\log(0.41)} = 0.57.}
\end{array}$$

With this information, the survivor function for a patient on the new
treatment is ${\lbrack S_{S}(t)\rbrack}^{\psi_{R}}$, and so
$S_{N}(2) = 0.82$, $S_{N}(4) = 0.63$, and $S_{N}(6) = 0.45$. A plot of
the two survivor functions is shown in [Figure
15.2](#chapter15.xhtml_fig15_2){#chapter15.xhtml_b15fig15_2}.

<figure id="fig15_2" class="image">
<img src="images/fig15_2.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter15.xhtml_b15fig15_2" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">15.2</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Estimated survivor functions for individuals
on the standard treatment (—) and the new treatment (<span
class="middle">.......</span>).</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The median survival time under the new treatment can be determined from
this estimate of the survivor function. Using [Figure
15.2](#chapter15.xhtml_fig15_2), the median survival time under the new
treatment is estimated to be about six years. A hazard ratio of 0.57
therefore implies an []{#chapter15.xhtml_p413 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 413. " role="doc-pagebreak"}increase in median
survival time from 3.3 years on the standard treatment to 6 years on the
new treatment.

To calculate the number of deaths that would be required in a study to
compare the two treatments, we will take $\alpha = 0.05$ and
$1 - \beta = 0.90$. With these values of *α* and *β*, the value of the
function $c(\alpha,\beta)$ from [Table 15.1](#chapter15.xhtml_tbl15_1)
is 10.51. Substituting for $c(0.05,0.1)$ in Equation (15.2) and taking
$\theta_{R} = \log\psi_{R} = \log(0.57) = - 0.562$, the number of deaths
required to have a 90% chance of detecting a hazard ratio of 0.57 to be
significant at the 5% level is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{d = \frac{4 \times 10.51}{0.562^{2}} = 133.}
\end{array}$$

Allowing for possible underestimation, this can be rounded up to 140
deaths in total. This means that approximately 70 deaths would need to
be observed in each treatment group.

The treatment difference that it is required to detect may also be
expressed in terms of the desired absolute or relative change in the
median survival time. The corresponding log-hazard ratio, for use in
Equation (15.1), can then be found by reversing the preceding
calculation. For example, suppose that an increase in the median
survival time from 3.3 years on the standard treatment to just 5 years
on the new treatment is anticipated. The survivor function on the new
treatment is then 0.5 when $t = 5$, and using Equation (15.9),
$S_{N}(5) = \left\{ S_{S}(5) \right\}^{\psi_{R}} = 0.5$. Consequently,
$\psi_{R}\log\left\{ S_{S}(5) \right\} = \log 0.5$, and since
$S_{S}(5) = 0.41$, $\psi_{R} = 0.78$. This reflects a less optimistic
view of the treatment effect than when *ψ*~*R*~ is taken to be 0.57. The
corresponding number of deaths that would need to be observed for this
hazard ratio to be declared significantly different from unity at the 5%
level, with 90% power, is then around 680. This is considerably greater
than the number needed to identify a hazard ratio of 0.57 as
significant.

</aside>

The calculations described above are only going to be of direct use when
a study is to be continued until a given number of those entering the
study have died. Most trials will be designed on the basis of the number
of patients to be recruited and so we must now examine how this number
can be obtained.
:::
::::

::::: section
## 15.3 Calculating the required number of patients {#chapter15.xhtml_sec15_3 .head1}

In order to calculate the actual number of patients that are required in
a survival study, we need to consider the probability of death over the
duration of a study. Typically, patients are recruited over an *accrual
period* of length *a*. After recruitment is complete, there is an
additional *follow-up period* of length *f*. The total duration of a
study will therefore be of length $a + f$. Notice that if *f* is small,
or even zero, there will need to be correspondingly more patients
recruited in order to achieve a specific number of deaths.

Once the probability of a patient dying in the study has been evaluated,
the required number of patients will be found from

$$\begin{array}{r}
{n = \frac{d}{\text{P}(\text{death})},}
\end{array}$$[(15.10)]{.equationnum}

where *d* is the required number of deaths found from Equation (15.2).
According to a result derived in the next section, the probability of
death can be taken as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ \overline{S}(f) + 4\overline{S}(0.5a + f) + \overline{S}(a + f) \right\},}
\end{array}$$[(15.11)]{.equationnum}

where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\overline{S}(t) = \frac{S_{S}(t) + S_{N}(t)}{2},}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter15.xhtml_p414 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 414. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}and $S_{S}(t)$ and $S_{N}(t)$ are the estimated
values of the survivor functions for individuals on the standard and new
treatments, respectively, at time *t*.

The above result shows how the required number of patients can be
calculated for a trial with an accrual period of *a* and a follow-up
period of *f*. Of course, the duration of the accrual period and
follow-up period will depend on the recruitment rate. So suppose that
the recruitment rate is expected to be *m* patients per month and that
*d* deaths are required. If *n* patients are to be entered into the
study over a period of *a* months, this means that $n/a$ need to be
recruited in each month. In practice, information is likely to be
available on the accrual rate, *m*, that can be expected. The number
recruited in an accrual period of length *a* is then *ma* and so the
expected number of deaths in the study is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{ma \times \text{P}(\text{death}).}
\end{array}$$

Values of *a* and *f* which make this value close to the number of
deaths required can then be found numerically, for example, by trying
out different values of *a* and *f*. This algorithm could be
computerised and an optimisation method used to find the value of *a*
that makes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{d - \left\{ ma \times \text{P}(\text{death}) \right\}}
\end{array}$$[(15.12)]{.equationnum}

close to zero for a range of values of $f$. Alternatively, the value of
*f* that yields the result in Equation (15.12) for a range of values of
*a* can be found. A two-way table giving the required number of patients
for different combinations of values of *a* and *f* will be particularly
useful in planning a study.

The following section gives details underlying the derivation of the
result in Equation (15.11), and can again be omitted without loss of
continuity.

::: section
### 15.3.1 Derivation of the required number of patients {#chapter15.xhtml_sec15_3_1 .head2}

We begin with the general result from distribution theory that the
marginal probability of a patient dying during the course of a study can
be obtained from the joint probability of death and entry to the study
at time *t* using

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = {\int_{0}^{a}\text{P}}(\text{death}\,\text{and}\,\text{entry}\,\text{at}\,\text{time}\, t)\,\text{d}t.}
\end{array}$$[(15.13)]{.equationnum}

The joint probability can in turn be found from the result

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}\,\text{and}\,\text{entry}\,\text{at}\,\text{time}\, t) = \text{P}(\text{death}\mid\text{entry}\,\text{at}\, t) \times \text{P}(\text{entry}\,\text{at}\, t),}
\end{array}$$[(15.14)]{.equationnum}

which is simply a version of the result that
$\left. \text{P}(A\, \middle| \, B) = \text{P}(AB)/\text{P}(B) \right.$.

We now assume a uniform recruitment rate over the accrual period. The
distribution of entry times to the study can then be taken to be uniform
over the time interval $(0,a)$. Therefore, the probability of an
individual being recruited to the study at time *t* is $a^{- 1}$, for
any value of *t* in the interval $(0,a)$. From Equations (15.13) and
(15.14), we have

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = {\int_{0}^{a}\text{P}}(\text{death}\mid\text{entry}\,\text{at}\, t)a^{- 1}\,\text{d}t,}
\end{array}$$

so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{a}{\int_{0}^{a}\text{P}}(\text{survival}\mid\text{entry}\,\text{at}\, t)\,\text{d}t.}
\end{array}$$

A patient entering the study at time *t* who survives for the duration
of the study, that is, to time $a + f$, must have been alive for a
period of length $a + f - t$ after entry. The conditional probability
P(survival ∣ entry at *t*) is therefore the probability of survival
beyond $a + f - t$. []{#chapter15.xhtml_p415 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 415. " role="doc-pagebreak"}This probability is the
value of the survivor function for that individual at $a + f - t$, that
is, $S(a + f - t)$. Consequently,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{a}{\int_{0}^{a}S}(a + f - t)\,\text{d}t,}
\end{array}$$

and on writing $u = a + f - t$, this result becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{a}{\int_{f}^{a + f}S}(u)\,\text{d}u.}
\end{array}$$[(15.15)]{.equationnum}

The integral of the survivor function is now approximated using
numerical integration. According to Simpson\'s rule,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\int_{u}^{v}f}(x)\,\text{d}x \approx \frac{v - u}{6}\left\{ {f(u) + 4f\left( \frac{u + v}{2} \right) + f(v)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\int_{f}^{a + f}S}(u)\,\text{d}u \approx \frac{a}{6}\left\{ {S(f) + 4S(0.5a + f) + S(a + f)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and hence, using Equation (15.15), the probability of death during the
study is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ {S(f) + 4S(0.5a + f) + S(a + f)} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

From this result, the approximate probability of death for an individual
in Group I, for whom the survivor function is $S_{S}(t)$, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death};\,\text{Group}\,\text{I}) = 1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ {S_{S}(f) + 4S_{S}(0.5a + f) + S_{S}(a + f)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

and similarly that for an individual in Group II is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death};\,\text{Group}\,\text{II}) = 1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ {S_{N}(f) + 4S_{N}(0.5a + f) + S_{N}(a + f)} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

On the assumption that there is an equal probability of an individual
being assigned to either of the two treatment groups, the overall
probability of death is the average of these two probabilities, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = \frac{\text{P}(\text{death};\,\text{Group}\,\text{I}) + \text{P}(\text{death};\,\text{Group}\,\text{II})}{2}.}
\end{array}$$

On substituting for the probabilities of death in the two treatment
groups, and setting
$\overline{S}(t) = \left\{ S_{S}(t) + S_{N}(t) \right\}/2$, the overall
probability of death can be written

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ \overline{S}(f) + 4\overline{S}(0.5a + f) + \overline{S}(a + f) \right\},}
\end{array}$$

as in Equation (15.11).

If the proportion of individuals to be allocated to Group I is *π*, the
overall probability of death becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\pi\,\text{P}(\text{death};\,\text{Group}\,\text{I}) + (1 - \pi)\,\text{P}(\text{death};\,\text{Group}\,\text{II}),}
\end{array}$$

and the result for the overall probability of death given in Equation
(15.11) can be modified accordingly.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam15_2" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 15.2]{.boxnum}](#chapter15.xhtml_b15exam15_2){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [[]{#chapter15.xhtml_p416 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 416. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Survival from chronic
active hepatitis]{.boxht}

In [Example 15.1](#chapter15.xhtml_exam15_1), it was shown that 140
deaths needed to be observed for the study on chronic hepatitis to have
sufficient power to detect a hazard ratio of 0.57 as significant, at the
5% level. Suppose that patients are to be recruited to the study over an
18-month accrual period and that there is to be a subsequent follow-up
period of 24 months. From Equation (15.11), the probability of death in
the 42 months of the study will then be given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ {\overline{S}(24) + 4\overline{S}(33) + \overline{S}(42)} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

Now, using the estimated survivor functions shown in [Figure
15.2](#chapter15.xhtml_fig15_2),

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\overline{S}(24) = \frac{S_{S}(24) + S_{N}(24)}{2} = \frac{0.70 + 0.82}{2} = 0.76,}
\end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\overline{S}(33) = \frac{S_{S}(33) + S_{N}(33)}{2} = \frac{0.57 + 0.73}{2} = 0.65,}
\end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\overline{S}(42) = \frac{S_{S}(42) + S_{N}(42)}{2} = \frac{0.45 + 0.63}{2} = 0.54,}
\end{array}$$

and so the probability of death is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ 0.76 + (4 \times 0.65) + 0.54 \right\} = 0.350.}
\end{array}$$

From Equation (15.10), the required number of patients is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{n = \frac{140}{0.350} = 400,}
\end{array}$$

and so 400 patients will need to be recruited to the study over the
accrual period of 18 months. This demands a recruitment rate of about 22
patients per month.

If it is only expected that 18 patients can be found each month, the
accrual period will need to be extended to ensure that there is a
sufficient number of individuals to give the required number of deaths.
The number of individuals that could be recruited in a period of *a*
months would be 18*a*. Various values of *a* can then be tried in order
to make this approximately equal to the value obtained from Equation
(15.10). For example, if we take $a = 24$ and continue with $f = 24$,
the probability of death over the four years of the study is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ \overline{S}(24) + 4\overline{S}(36) + \overline{S}(48) \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

From [Figure 15.2](#chapter15.xhtml_fig15_2), the survivor functions for
patients on each treatment at 24, 36 and 48 months can be estimated, and
we find that $\overline{S}(24) = 0.76$, $\overline{S}(36) = 0.65$, and
$\overline{S}(48) = 0.50$. The probability of death then turns out to be
0.357 and the required number of patients to give 140 deaths is now 393.
This is broadly consistent with an estimated recruitment rate of 18 per
month.

Now suppose that it is decided that the study will not have a follow-up
period, so that the accrual period is equal to the duration of the
study. If the accrual period is taken to be 20 months, so that $a = 20$
and $f = 0$, the probability of death is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\text{P}(\text{death}) = 1 - \frac{1}{6}\left\{ \overline{S}(0) + 4\overline{S}(10) + \overline{S}(20) \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

Now, $\overline{S}(0) = 1.00$, $\overline{S}(10) = 0.82$, and
$\overline{S}(20) = 0.79$, and the probability of death is 0.155. The
required number of patients is now $140/0.155 = 903$, and this would
just about be met by a recruitment rate of 45 patients per month. This
shows that the absence of a follow-up period leads to an increase in the
number of patients that must be entered into the study.

</aside>
:::

::: section
### 15.3.2 []{#chapter15.xhtml_p417 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 417. " role="doc-pagebreak"}An approximate procedure {#chapter15.xhtml_sec15_3_2 .head2}

A much simpler procedure for calculating the required number of patients
in a survival study to compare two treatments is outlined in this
section. The basis for this result is that
$\left\{ S_{S}(\tau) + S_{N}(\tau) \right\}/2$ is the average
probability that a patient in the study survives beyond time *τ*, where
$S_{S}(\tau)$ and $S_{N}(\tau)$ are the survivor functions at time *τ*,
for patients on the standard and new treatments, respectively. The
probability of death, in the period from the time origin to *τ*, can
then be approximated by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{1 - \frac{S_{S}(\tau) + S_{N}(\tau)}{2}.}
\end{array}$$

Using Equation (15.10), the required number of patients becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{n = \frac{2d}{2 - S_{S}(\tau) - S_{N}(\tau)},}
\end{array}$$

where *d* is the required number of deaths.

A natural choice for the value of *τ* to use in this calculation is the
average length of the follow-up period for patients in the study,
$f + (a/2)$, where *a* is the accrual period and *f* the follow-up
period. This procedure is very approximate because it does not take
account of patient follow-up times that extend beyond *τ*. As a
consequence, this result will tend to overestimate the required sample
size.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam15_3" role="doc-tip">

[Example 15.3]{.boxnum} [Survival from chronic active hepatitis]{.boxht}

The study on chronic active hepatitis is now used to illustrate the
approximate procedure for determining the required number of patients.
As in [Example
15.2](#chapter15.xhtml_exam15_2){#chapter15.xhtml_b15exam15_2}, suppose
that patients are recruited over an 18-month period and that there is a
further follow-up period of 24 months. The average length of the
follow-up period for a patient will then be $\tau = f + (a/2) = 33$
months. From [Figure 15.2](#chapter15.xhtml_fig15_2), the survivor
functions for patients in the two groups are given by $S_{S}(33) = 0.57$
and $S_{N}(33) = 0.73$, respectively. The approximate probability of
death in the period from 0 to 33 months is therefore 0.35 and the number
of patients required to give 140 deaths is $140/0.35 = 400$. In this
illustration, the sample size produced by the approximate result is
identical to that found using the more complicated procedure illustrated
in [Example 15.2](#chapter15.xhtml_exam15_2), but this will not usually
be the case.

</aside>
:::
:::::

::: section
## 15.4 Further reading {#chapter15.xhtml_sec15_4 .head1}

Full details on the issues to be considered when designing a clinical
trial are given in [Friedman, Furberg and DeMets
(2010)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib130){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"},
[Matthews (2006)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib250){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [Pocock
(1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib300){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and
the more general text of [Altman
(1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib12){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib12 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. [Whitehead
(1997)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib414){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib414
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} describes how a sequential clinical
trial can be designed when the outcome variable of interest is a
survival time, and [Jennison and Turnbull
(2000)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib190){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib190
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} describe group sequential methods.

Extensive tables of sample size requirements in studies involving
different types of response variable, including survival times, are
provided by [Machin et al.
(2009)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib244){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib244
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Julious
(2010)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib195){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib195
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Most statistical software packages
including SAS, R and Stata, have functions for sample size calculation.
In addition, a number of commercially available software packages for
sample size calculations, including PASS (Power Analysis and Sample Size
program) and nQuery Advisor, implement methods for calculating the
required number of patients in a survival study.

The formula for the required number of deaths in Equation (15.1) appears
in many papers, including Bernstein and [Lagakos (1978), Schoenfeld
(1981)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib222){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib222
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Schoenfeld and Richter
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib341){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib341
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Schoenfeld
(1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib342){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib342
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, although the assumptions on which the
result is based are different. [Bernstein and Lagakos
(1978)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib35){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib35 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} obtain Equation (15.1) on the assumption that the
[]{#chapter15.xhtml_p418 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 418. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}survival times in each group have exponential
distributions. [Lachin
(1981)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib220){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib220
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Rubinstein, Gail and Santner
(1981)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib328){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib328
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Lachin and Foulkes
(1986)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib221){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib221
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also discuss sample size requirements
in trials where the survival times are assumed to be exponentially
distributed. See also the earlier work of [George and Desu
(1974)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib142){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib142
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

[Schoenfeld
(1981)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib340){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib340
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} obtains the same result as [Bernstein
and Lagakos (1978)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib35){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and others when the log-rank test is used to
compare treatments, without making the additional assumption of
exponentiality. [Schoenfeld (1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib342){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} shows that Equation (15.1) holds when information
on the values of explanatory variables is allowed for.

The formulae for the required number of patients in [Section
15.3](#chapter15.xhtml_sec15_3) are based on [Schoenfeld
(1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib342){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. When
the assumption of exponential survival times is made, these formulae
simplify to the results of [Schoenfeld and Richter
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib341){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
Although the resulting formulae are easier to use, it is dangerous to
conduct sample size calculations on the basis of restrictive assumptions
about survival time distributions.

A variant on the formula for the required number of deaths is given by
[Freedman (1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib128){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib128
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Freedman\'s result has
$\left\{ (1 + \psi)/(1 - \psi) \right\}^{2}$ in place of
$4/{(\log\psi)}^{2}$ in Equation (15.1). However, for small values of
$\log\psi$,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left\{ (1 + \psi)/(1 - \psi) \right\}^{2} \approx 4/{(\log\psi)}^{2},}
\end{array}$$

and so the two expressions will tend to give similar results. The
approximate formula for the required number of patients, given in
[Section 15.3.2](#chapter15.xhtml_sec15_3_2), is also due to [Freedman
(1982)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib128){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

[Lakatos (1988)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib223){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib223
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} presented a method for estimating the
required number of patients to compare two treatments which can
accommodate matters such as staggered entry, non-compliance, loss to
follow-up and non-proportional hazards. [Lakatos and Lan
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib224){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib224
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} show that the Lakatos method procedure
performs well in a variety of circumstances. This approach is based on a
Markov model, and requires a computer program for its implementation.

Approximate sample size formulae for use in modelling the
subdistribution hazard function in the presence of competing risks,
described in [Section 12.5](#chapter12.xhtml_sec12_5) of [Chapter
12](#chapter12.xhtml), have been given by [Latouche, Porcher and Chevret
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib228){#chapter15.xhtml_b15refbib228
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

[]{#chapter16.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter16.xhtml_p419 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 419. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 16]{.chapnum}[Bayesian survival analysis]{.chaptitle} {#chapter16.xhtml_chapter16 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-16](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-16){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

Most of the modelling techniques described in this book are based
constructing a likelihood or partial likelihood function of the observed
time-to-event data. This likelihood function is then maximised over the
unknown parameters in the model that is being fitted to obtain estimates
of quantities such as hazard ratios. Standard errors of these estimates
are then used to construct interval estimates and perform hypothesis
tests. This approach regards sample data as observed values of random
variables and underpins what is known as the classical approach to
statistical inference. An alternative approach is to regard the unknown
model parameters as random variables. This approach is known as
*Bayesian inference*, and the associated methods are now widely used in
many areas of application, including the biomedical sciences. This
chapter describes and illustrates the essential features of the Bayesian
approach to modelling survival data.

::: section
## 16.1 Bayes\' theorem {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_1 .head1}

Underpinning Bayesian inference is a theorem from conditional
probability known as Bayes\' theorem, published by the Rev. Thomas Bayes
in 1763. A simple version of this is the expression for the probability
that an event *A* has occurred, conditional on another event *D* having
occurred, which is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \text{P}(A\, \middle| \, D) = \text{P}(AD)/\text{P}(D), \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.1)]{.equationnum}

where $\text{P}(AD)$ is the joint probability that both events *A* and
*D* occur. Similarly, the conditional probability of *D* given *A* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, A) = \text{P}(DA)/\text{P}(A), \right.
\end{array}$$

and on substituting for the joint probability $\text{P}(DA)$ from this
equation into Equation (16.1), we obtain

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \text{P}(A\, \middle| \, D) = \frac{\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, A)\text{P}(A) \right.}{\text{P}(D)}. \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.2)]{.equationnum}

This result is now extended to the case where at least one of *k*
mutually exclusive events, $A_{1},A_{2},\ldots,A_{k}$, necessarily
occurs in conjunction with some other event *D*. The conditional
probability of any one of these *k* events, given that *D* has occurred,
is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \text{P}(A_{i}\, \middle| \, D) = \frac{\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, A_{i})\text{P}(A_{i}) \right.}{\text{P}(D)}, \right.
\end{array}$$

for $i = 1,2,\ldots,k$. The overall probability of the event *D* is the
sum of the probabilities that *D* occurs with any one of the *k* events
$A_{1},A_{2},\ldots,A_{k}$, and so the denominator is

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \text{P}(D) = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{k}P}(DA_{i}) = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{k}P}(D\, \middle| \, A_{i})\text{P}(A_{i}). \right.
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p420 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 420. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}This result that means that the conditional
probabilities $\left. \text{P}(A_{i}\, \middle| \, D) \right.$ sum to
unity, and the full version of Bayes\' theorem is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \text{P}(A_{i}\, \middle| \, D) = \frac{\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, A_{i})\text{P}(A_{i}) \right.}{\left. {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{k}P}(D\, \middle| \, A_{i})\text{P}(A_{i}) \right.}. \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.3)]{.equationnum}

This theorem provides the basis for the Bayesian approach to inference.
:::

::: section
## 16.2 Bayesian inference {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_2 .head1}

In Bayesian inference, Equation (16.3) is reinterpreted to show how
prior information about an unknown parameter, *θ*, can be combined with
observed data to give a posterior probability distribution of that
parameter. When *θ* is discrete, the particular values that it may take
correspond to the events *A*~*i*~ in Equation (16.3). When *θ* is
continuous, as it more often is, knowledge about *θ* that is external to
the data can be summarised in a prior probability density function,
$\pi(\theta)$. In survival analysis, the data are *n* observed survival
times, $t_{1},t_{2},\ldots,t_{n}$, some of which may be censored. These
data are assembled into a vector $\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}$, and correspond
to the event *D* in Equation (16.3). The data depend on the value of *θ*
in an assumed model for the data generating process, from which a
likelihood function is constructed,
$\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\theta) \right.$,
corresponding to $\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, A_{i}) \right.$ in
Equation (16.3). Regarding *θ* as continuous, the sum in the denominator
of Bayes\' theorem is replaced by an integral over the range of possible
values of *θ*. The resulting combination of data and prior density is
the posterior density function of *θ*,
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$, so that
from Bayes\' theorem in Equation (16.3),

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = \frac{\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\theta)\pi(\theta) \right.}{\left. {\int_{\theta}L}(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\theta)\pi(\theta) \right.}. \right.
\end{array}$$

Generally, a model will depend on more than one parameter, and the set
of *p* unknown parameters, $\theta_{1},\theta_{2},\ldots,\theta_{p}$
will be represented by the vector $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$. The
posterior density function of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ given the data,
$\left. \pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,
is then proportional to the product of the sample likelihood function
$\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) \right.$
and the prior density of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$,
$\pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}})$, so that again from Equation (16.3),

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = \frac{\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}})\pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) \right.}{p(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})}. \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.4)]{.equationnum}

The denominator $p(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})$ is a normalising constant to
ensure that the posterior density
$\left. \pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
integrates to unity and is also the *marginal distribution* of the
sample data, given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. p(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = {\int_{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}L}(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}})\pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}})\,\text{d}\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}. \right.
\end{array}$$

This multidimensional integral over the possible values of the unknown
model parameters is generally very difficult to evaluate. However, we
will see in [Section 16.6](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_6) that a posterior
distribution can be estimated using numerical methods, without the need
to obtain $p(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})$.

Often, we are interested in specific parameters in a model, while others
are merely nuisance parameters that are not of interest in their own
right. For example, in fitting a Weibull model to survival times, we may
want to have information about some or all of the regression
coefficients or hazard ratios, but not necessarily the underlying scale
and shape parameter of the Weibull model. The posterior marginal
distribution of *θ*~*j*~, the *j*th model parameter in
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, can be found by integrating out the other
$p - 1$ parameters so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \pi(\theta_{j}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = {\int_{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}}\pi}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}, \right.
\end{array}$$

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}$ denotes the set of parameters
in $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ excluding *θ*~*j*~.

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p421 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 421. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In Bayesian inference, the parameters in an assumed
model for survival times are regarded as random variables, and inference
about these parameters is based on their posterior distribution. This
posterior distribution then leads to Bayesian point estimates, interval
estimates and hypothesis tests.
:::

:::: section
## 16.3 Bayesian models for survival data {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_3 .head1}

A Bayesian approach to modelling survival data is readily implemented
when the likelihood function can be fully specified. Suppose that a
proportional hazards model is to be fitted, where the hazard and
survivor functions for the *i*th individual at time *t*,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, are given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t),\text{\quad\quad}S_{i}(t) = S_{0}{(t)}^{\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})}.}
\end{array}$$

In these expressions, $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is the vector of values
of explanatory variables for the *i*th individual, $h_{0}(t)$ is the
baseline hazard function and $S_{0}(t)$ the baseline survivor function
found from $S_{0}(t) = \exp\left\{ - H_{0}(t) \right\}$, where the
baseline cumulative hazard is
$H_{0}(t) = {\int_{0}^{t}h_{0}}(u)\,\text{d}u$. In a parametric model,
the baseline hazard function is a fully specified function of unknown
parameters. For example, the Weibull model introduced in [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml) has a baseline hazard that depends on a shape
parameter *γ* and a scale parameter *λ*. Consequently, the likelihood
function used in fitting a parametric model depends on parameters
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ that include the *β* coefficients and the two
parameters in the baseline hazard. Using Expression (5.15) of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), the likelihood function for a parametric model is
given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = {\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\{ h_{i}( \right.}t_{i}) \right\}^{\delta_{i}}S_{i}(t_{i}),}
\end{array}$$[(16.5)]{.equationnum}

where *δ*~*i*~ is an indicator variable that is unity when *t*~*i*~ is
an event and zero otherwise, and $h_{i}(t_{i})$ and $S_{i}(t_{i})$ are
the hazard and survivor functions at time *t*~*i*~. Details of the form
of the hazard and survivor functions for particular models were given in
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml). The likelihood in Equation (16.5) can then
be used directly in the expression for a posterior probability in
Equation (16.4), and combined with any prior knowledge about the unknown
parameters in the model. Moreover, any fully parametric model, including
accelerated failure time models, proportional odds models, flexible
models based on spline functions, models for interval censored data, and
many others, can readily be accommodated in the Bayesian framework.

The Cox regression model can also be fitted using the Bayesian approach,
with the partial likelihood function described in [Section
3.3](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), and given
in Equation (3.4), used in place of
$\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
in Equation (16.4). This likelihood only depends on the *β*-coefficients
in the model and prior information about these coefficients can also be
incorporated. Once Bayesian estimates of the unknown *β* parameters in
the model have been obtained, estimates of the baseline cumulative
hazard and survivor functions can be found using the results in [Section
3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_10) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml).

A drawback to this approach is that it does not allow prior information
about the baseline hazard function to be incorporated. To do this, a
model is needed for the baseline hazard itself. Suppose that the data
contains *r* ordered event times $t_{(1)} < t_{(2)} < \cdots < t_{(r)}$.
The Cox regression model assumes a constant baseline hazard between
adjacent event times, and so a corresponding parametric model would have
$h_{0}(t) = \lambda_{j}$, for $t_{(j - 1)}\leqslant t < t_{(j)}$, where
$t_{(0)} = 0$ and $\lambda_{j} > 0$ $j = 1,2,\ldots,r$. This hazard
function is constant in each time interval and is the *piecewise
exponential model* described in [Section 6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1) of
[Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml).

The corresponding likelihood function for a proportional hazards model
with

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\eta_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p422 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 422. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where
$\eta_{i} = \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is a linear
combination of the values of *p* explanatory variables for the *i*th
individual, is found from Equation (16.5). The hazard and survivor
functions are found from $h_{i}(t) = \exp(\eta_{i})\lambda_{j}$ if
$t_{(j - 1)}\leqslant t < t_{(j)}$, for $j = 1,2,\ldots,r$,
$S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - H_{i}(t) \right\}$, and
$H_{i}(t) = \exp(\eta_{i})H_{0}(t)$, where the baseline cumulative
hazard is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{H_{0}(t) = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}\lambda_{j}}\Delta_{j}(t),}
\end{array}$$

and

$$\begin{array}{l}
{\Delta_{j}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
0 & {t < t_{(j - 1)},} \\
{t - t_{(j - 1)}} & {t_{(j - 1)}\leqslant t < t_{(j)},} \\
{t_{(j)} - t_{(j - 1)}} & {t\geqslant t_{(j)}.}
\end{array}~ \right.}
\end{array}$$

With large data sets, the number of intervals and the corresponding
number of parameters in $h_{0}(t)$ becomes prohibitively large. Wider
intervals are then defined over which the hazard is assumed constant.
Most computer software for Bayesian semi-parametric modelling adopts a
piecewise exponential baseline hazard model rather than the standard Cox
model.

::: section
### 16.3.1 Bayesian version of the simple exponential model {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_3_1 .head2}

In [Section 5.4.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4_1) of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), it was shown how an exponential distribution with
mean $\lambda^{- 1}$ can be fitted to a single sample of censored
survival data using classical likelihood-based methods. The
corresponding Bayesian approach is now described.

Suppose that the observed data consist of a sample of *n* exponentially
distributed survival times $t_{1},t_{2},\ldots,t_{n}$, where *r* of
these are event times and the remaining $n - r$ are right-censored.
Using the indicator *δ*~*i*~ to denote whether or not the *i*th
observation is censored, with $\delta_{i} = 0$ corresponding to a
censored observation, the likelihood of the data was shown in Equation
(5.16) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml) to be

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\prod\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\lambda^{\delta_{i}}}e^{- \lambda t_{i}}.}
\end{array}$$

This will now be written as
$\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda) \right.$ to
emphasise that this is the likelihood of the data $\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}$
under an exponential model with parameter *λ*, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda) = \lambda^{r}e^{- \lambda S}, \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.6)]{.equationnum}

where $r = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}}$ and
$S = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}}$.

The Bayesian inference process proceeds by formulating a prior
distribution for *λ*. Since *λ* must be positive, any prior information
must be summarised in the interval $(0,\infty)$. If we have a good idea
about possible values of *λ* we could use a distribution that is centred
on the most plausible value of *λ*, with a range that encompasses this
value. For example, we could take the prior to be a gamma distribution
with mean equal to the prior estimate of *λ*, and a variance that is
either high or low, depending on our confidence in this estimate. In
this example, we will assume that no prior knowledge about *λ* is
available. Anticipating a result in [Section
16.4](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_4), a suitable prior density function of
*λ*, $\pi(\lambda)$, would be proportional to $\lambda^{- 1}$, and we
take $\pi(\lambda) = c\lambda^{- 1}$. Since the constant *c* appears in
both the numerator and denominator of the expression for the posterior
density function in Equation (16.4), this constant can effectively be
ignored. The posterior distribution of *λ* given the sample data, is
then given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \pi(\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = \frac{\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda)\pi(\lambda) \right.}{p(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})}, \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.7)]{.equationnum}

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p423 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 423. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. p(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = {\int_{0}^{\infty}L}(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda)\pi(\lambda)\,\text{d}\lambda. \right.
\end{array}$$

Using Equation (16.6), the numerator in Equation (16.7) is

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda)\pi(\lambda) = \lambda^{r - 1}\exp( - \lambda S). \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.8)]{.equationnum}

Next, using the gamma function defined in Equation (5.6) of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), where

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\Gamma(r) = {\int_{0}^{\infty}u^{r - 1}}e^{- u}\,\text{d}u,}
\end{array}$$

and setting $u = \lambda S$, so that $\text{d}u = S\,\text{d}\lambda$,
the denominator of Equation (16.7) becomes

$$\begin{array}{r}
{p(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = {\int_{0}^{\infty}\lambda^{r - 1}}\exp( - \lambda S)\,\text{d}\lambda = \frac{\Gamma(r)}{S^{r}}.}
\end{array}$$

From Equations (16.7) and (16.8), the posterior density function of *λ*
is then given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \pi(\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = \frac{\lambda^{r - 1}S^{r}\exp( - \lambda S)}{\Gamma(r)}. \right.
\end{array}$$

This is the probability density function of a random variable *λ*, and
comparison with the density of a gamma distribution in Equation (5.11)
of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml) shows that *λ* has a gamma posterior
distribution with parameters *r* and $S = {\sum\limits_{i}t_{i}}$. The
mean of this distribution is $r/S$ and the mode is $(r - 1)/S$, so long
as $r > 1$. Notice that the mean of the posterior distribution of *λ* is
the same as the maximum likelihood estimate of *λ* derived in [Section
5.4](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_4) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml). This will
often be the case when we use a prior distribution that conveys little
or no information about the value of an unknown parameter.

In this simplest of models, it has been possible to obtain an explicit
form for the posterior distribution, so that all the information about
*λ* from both prior information and data is conveyed in a gamma
distribution. However, this will rarely be possible, and numerical
methods for obtaining the posterior distribution will generally be
required.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam16_1" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 16.1]{.boxnum}](#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam16_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Time to discontinuation of the use of an
IUD]{.boxht}

In this example, Bayesian methods are used to analyse the data of
[Example 1.1](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_1){#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam1_1} on
the times to discontinuation of an IUD for 18 women. Specifically, the
discontinuation times will be assumed to have an exponential
distribution with mean *λ*, so that from Equation (16.6), the likelihood
of the sample data is

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda) = \lambda^{r}\exp\left\{ - \lambda S \right\}, \right.
\end{array}$$

where *r* is the number of event times and
$S = {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}t_{i}}$.

A non-informative prior for *λ* will be used with density
$\pi(\lambda) \propto \lambda^{- 1}$. There are $r = 9$ event times in
the data and the sum of all 18 discontinuation times is 1046. The
posterior density of *λ* is then proportional to the product of the
likelihood and the prior density function of *λ*, which is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\lambda^{8}\exp( - 1046\lambda).}
\end{array}$$[(16.9)]{.equationnum}

In this case we can evaluate the constant of proportionality that makes
this expression integrate to unity over *λ*, and as in [Section
16.3.1](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_3_1), the posterior distribution of *λ*
is gamma with parameters 9 and 1046, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \pi(\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = \frac{\lambda^{8}1046^{9}\exp( - 1046\lambda)}{\Gamma(9)}, \right.
\end{array}$$

for $\lambda > 0$. This posterior distribution can be used to construct
Bayesian point estimates, interval estimates and hypothesis tests, and
we return to this in [Section 16.5](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_5).

</aside>

We next consider in greater detail how prior information can be
specified.
:::
::::

:::::: section
## 16.4 []{#chapter16.xhtml_p424 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 424. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Incorporating prior knowledge {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_4 .head1}

Bayesian inference requires that prior information about unknown model
parameters be quantified for incorporation in the inferential process.
If such information is available, and can be summarised in a prior
density function, the prior is said to be *informative* about the
unknown model parameters. On the other hand, if little or no such prior
information is available, the prior is described as *vague* or
*non-informative*, respectively. The prior density function will then be
essentially flat.

Prior distributions will be needed for each of the parameters
$\theta_{1},\theta_{2},\ldots,\theta_{p}$ that make up the vector
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$. When these prior distributions are taken to
be independent of one another, the overall prior is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) = \pi_{1}(\theta_{1})\pi_{2}(\theta_{2})\cdots\pi_{p}(\theta_{p}),}
\end{array}$$

where $\pi_{j}(\theta_{j})$ is the prior density of the *j*th parameter
*θ*~*j*~, $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$. If this independence assumption cannot be
made, $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ may be taken to have a multivariate
distribution, such as the multivariate normal. Prior information about
any intercorrelation between the individual parameters can then be taken
into account. This adds another layer of complication, but there is no
difficulty in principle in taking $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ to have a
multivariate distribution, rather than the product of *p* independent
univariate prior distributions.

We now consider the various degrees of prior information in turn.

::: section
### 16.4.1 Non-informative prior information {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_4_1 .head2}

Even when there is no prior knowledge about the value of an unknown
parameter in a model, this lack of knowledge must be quantified by a
*non-informative* prior density function. A prior distribution of this
kind assigns similar probability density to all possible values of an
unknown parameter and has a large variance. The posterior distribution
will then be dominated by the form of likelihood, so that the resulting
inferences are then driven almost entirely by the data.

The prior distribution used to summarise a lack of knowledge about a
parameter depends on its range of possible values. For parameters that
can take any value in the range $( - \infty,\infty)$, such as the
coefficient of an explanatory variable in a survival model, the prior
density function $\pi(\theta)$ can be taken to be constant over this
range. Then, *θ* has a uniform distribution, denoted
$U( - \infty,\infty)$. However, this is an *improper distribution*,
because the integral of this uniform density function,
${\int_{- \infty}^{\infty}\pi}(\theta)\,\text{d}\theta$, is not defined.
However, this is not a problem so long as the integral in the
denominator of Equation (16.4) is finite.

For positive valued parameters that can take values in the range
$(0,\infty)$, such as a hazard ratio or a scale parameter, $\pi(\theta)$
can be assumed constant over this range. A uniform prior on $(0,\infty)$
might then be adopted, although this is also an improper prior
distribution. A parameter *θ* that is strictly positive is such that
$\log\theta$ is defined over the range $( - \infty,\infty)$. This
suggests that an improper uniform distribution over $( - \infty,\infty)$
be used for $\log\theta$. This corresponds to a distribution for *θ*
that is proportional to $1/\theta$ in the range $(0,\infty)$. Notice
that different prior distributions can result from quantifying ignorance
about *θ*, or a function of it such as $\log\theta$.

Sometimes, a prior distribution is needed for a parameter *θ* that is
defined over $(0,1)$, such as a probability. Here, a common choice of
prior is a form of the beta distribution which has a density function
proportional to $\theta^{\alpha - 1}{(1 - \theta)}^{\alpha - 1}$ for
$0\leqslant\theta\leqslant 1$ and chosen values of *α*. This
distribution has mean 0.5 and a variance that decreases as *α*
increases. For $\alpha = 1$, $\pi(\theta) = 1$, corresponding to a
uniform prior over $(0,1)$. When $\alpha = 0$,
$\pi(\theta) \propto \theta^{- 1}{(1 - \theta)}^{- 1}$, which
corresponds to the logistic transform of *θ*,
$\,\text{logit}\,(\theta) = \log\left\{ \theta/(1 - \theta) \right\}$,
having an improper $U( - \infty,\infty)$ distribution. Both of these
choices for a non-informative prior have intuitive appeal.

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p425 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 425. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The difficulty of specifying prior information in
an accurate and non-controversial manner, coupled with a desire to use
priors that have little or no influence on the final inference, means
that non-informative priors are widely used. However, the use of a flat
prior distribution to represent lack of prior knowledge, can be
criticised. For example, suppose that a hazard ratio for a treatment
effect is a key parameter. A flat prior that is uniform on $(0,\infty)$
could be used to express prior ignorance about the value of the hazard
ratio. However, this is saying that our prior information is such that a
hazard ratio in the range from 0 to 10 is just as plausible as a hazard
ratio in the range from 1000 to 1010. This is rather unlikely to be the
case.

Instead of just using a default specification of a non-informative
prior, it is often valuable to give some thought to the likely range of
values of unknown parameters, so that this can be summarised in a vague
or informative prior distribution.
:::

::: section
### 16.4.2 Vague prior information {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_4_2 .head2}

A distribution that conveys very little information about the prior
value of a parameter is said to be *vague, diffuse* or *weakly
informative*. Vague priors are based on distributions that are proper
but highly dispersed, and so they will still tend to be swamped by the
data. Just as with non-informative priors, inferences about *θ* will
then be based almost exclusively on the observed data.

For a parameter that can take any value in the range
$( - \infty,\infty)$, a common choice of vague prior is the normal
distribution with zero mean and a large variance, *σ*^2^, denoted
$N(0,\sigma^{2})$. How large *σ*^2^ needs to be depends on the scale of
the unknown parameter, but taking *σ* to be at least double the
anticipated maximum value of *θ* will lead to a very flat prior. In
fact, for suitably large values of *σ*^2^, the normal distribution is
very similar to the improper $U( - \infty,\infty)$ distribution. Other
distributions that are commonly used in this context include the
*t*-distribution with a small number of degrees of freedom and the
Cauchy distribution. Of course, taking anything other than a uniform
distribution over the range of possible values of *θ* is building in a
small amount of prior information, but the posterior distribution will
still be overwhelmingly dominated by the likelihood function.

Vague prior knowledge about a positive valued parameter, *θ*, can be
specified using a lognormal distribution, which is equivalent to a
normal distribution for $\log\theta$. A prior distribution that is often
used for the scale parameter of a parametric model is the *half-normal
distribution*. This is formed by truncating a normal distribution with
zero mean so that it is defined for positive values only. The
distribution has a mean of $\sigma\left. \sqrt{}(2/\pi) \right.$ and
variance $\sigma^{2}(1 - 2\pi^{- 1})$, so that diffuse half-normal
priors have large values of *σ*^2^. Another option is a gamma
distribution with a large variance. For example, a gamma prior with
parameters $r = \lambda = 0.001$ has a mean of 1 and variance 1000 which
is also a diffuse but proper prior.

Although different prior distributions will necessarily lead to
different posterior distributions, any such differences may not be of
practical importance in terms of inferences to be drawn. For example,
whether a non-informative prior distribution for a log-hazard ratio,
*β*, is taken to have either a uniform distribution on
$( - \infty,\infty)$, or a diffuse normal distribution, may not
materially affect inferences about the value of *β*. This is
particularly the case for larger datasets, but differences may be more
noticeable in smaller ones.

Just as an improper prior can be an unrealistic representation of the
prior state of knowledge about a parameter, vague priors can be
criticised on similar grounds. Specifically, a normal prior distribution
with a large variance may still attach substantial probability to values
of a parameter that are most unlikely to occur. For example, taking the
prior distribution for a log-hazard ratio for a treatment effect, *β*,
to be $N(0,10)$ means that there is about a 20% chance that *β* is
outside the range $( - 4,4)$, which is quite substantial for a rather
unlikely value.
:::

::: section
### 16.4.3 []{#chapter16.xhtml_p426 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 426. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Substantial prior information {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_4_3 .head2}

When prior information is *substantial*, or *informative*, the posterior
distribution may depend heavily on the assumed form for the prior
distribution. Careful consideration must therefore be given to eliciting
prior information to ensure that it is accurate. Prior information about
the value of some or all of the unknown parameters in a model is
sometimes available from previous knowledge. On other occasions, a
combination of a literature search and expert opinion may lead to a
suitable numerical summary. It may also be possible to use estimates of
key parameters obtained from previous studies to formulate a suitable
prior distribution, which is often the case when a study is similar to
an earlier one.

Typically, an informative prior is based on a probability distribution
such as the normal or gamma, depending on the range of possible values
of the parameter. Informative priors can be rather subjective, in that
one persons numerical specification of a prior may differ from
another\'s. However, it may be possible to agree that the hazard ratio
for a particular explanatory variable, such as a treatment effect, is
likely to be centred on 1.5 and most unlikely to exceed 4, for example.
An asymmetric prior distribution with a median of 1.5 and variance that
leads to a very small probability that the effect exceeds 4 could then
be constructed and adopted.

There is always some degree of arbitrariness when choosing a prior
distribution, and there is often concern about the extent to which a
posterior distribution is dependent on the choice that is made. For this
reason, a range of plausible priors can be used in an analysis, and the
sensitivity of the posterior distribution to these different choices
evaluated.
:::
::::::

:::::: section
## 16.5 Summarising posterior information {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_5 .head1}

The posterior distribution of a parameter *θ* summarises all the
available information in the prior distribution and the likelihood of
the sample data, and so underpins inferences about *θ*. Point
estimation, interval estimation and hypothesis testing are now
considered in turn.

::: section
### 16.5.1 Point estimates {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_5_1 .head2}

The most appropriate point estimate of *θ* is the value that maximises
the posterior density, that is the mode of the distribution. The mode
can sometimes be obtained analytically by setting the derivative of the
posterior density, or its logarithm, to zero, and finding the value of
*θ* that satisfies this equation. When this is not possible, a numerical
method for function maximisation will be needed.

The mean of the posterior distribution may also be used as a point
estimate of location if the posterior distribution is reasonably
symmetric. The spread or dispersion of the distribution is then
conveniently summarised by the variance or standard deviation of the
distribution. When the posterior distribution is asymmetric, the median
value will be a more suitable point estimate of location, and a suitable
measure of spread is then the semi-interquartile range, defined in
[Section 2.4](#chapter2.xhtml_sec2_4) of [Chapter 2](#chapter2.xhtml).
In general, the median or mean are more convenient summaries of location
than the mode.
:::

::: section
### 16.5.2 Interval estimates {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_5_2 .head2}

An interval estimate generally provides a more informative summary of
the posterior distribution of a parameter than a point estimate. An
exact $100(1 - \alpha)$ per cent interval estimate is one where *θ* is
contained in the interval with a pre-specified probability *α*. The
lower and upper limits of the interval, *θ*~*l*~ and *θ*~*u*~, are then
such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. {\int_{\theta_{l}}^{\theta_{u}}\pi}(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\theta = 1 - \alpha, \right.
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p427 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 427. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where *α* is suitably small. This is often called a
*probability interval*, as it expresses the posterior probability that
*θ* lies in the interval $(\theta_{l},\theta_{u})$. Taking *α* to be
0.05 or 0.01 leads to probability intervals of 95% or 99%.

There are a number of ways that the interval $(\theta_{l},\theta_{u})$
can be defined. One possibility is to ensure that the posterior
probability density is the same at both *θ*~*l*~ and *θ*~*u*~, so that
no value of *θ* outside this interval has higher probability density
than any other value inside the interval. An interval constructed in
this way is known as a *highest probability density* (HPD) interval and
is the most natural way to obtain an interval estimate in Bayesian
inference.

A HPD interval usually needs to be obtained numerically. In outline,
values of *θ* that satisfy
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = \phi \right.$,
for a range of values of *ϕ* are identified. The probability coverage
associated with a particular value of *ϕ* is then found from

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. {\int_{\theta}\pi}(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\theta, \right.
\end{array}$$

where the integration is over those values of *θ* for which
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) > \phi \right.$.
This leads to the value of *ϕ* that gives the required *α, ϕ*~*α*~, say,
and the limits of a HPD interval are then the values *θ* for which
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = \phi_{\alpha} \right.$.

An alternative approach is to adopt an interval with equal tail areas. A
$100(1 - \alpha)$ per cent interval estimate would then have probability
$\alpha/2$ in each tail of the posterior distribution. The corresponding
limits of the interval are readily obtained from the upper and lower
$\alpha/2$ points of the posterior distribution. An interval estimate
constructed in this way is termed a *credible interval* and is often
much more straightforward to obtain than a HPD interval. These two
intervals are illustrated in [Figure
16.1](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_1){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_1}.

<figure id="fig16_1" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_1.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_1" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.1</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Posterior distribution of <em>θ</em> with a
mode at <span class="math inline"><em>θ̃</em></span> and two types of
<span class="math inline">100(1 − <em>α</em>)</span> % interval
estimate.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

In this figure, a $100(1 - \alpha)$ % HPD interval is formed from values
of *θ* with
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) > \phi_{\alpha} \right.$
leading to $(\theta_{l}^{H},\theta_{u}^{H})$, and a $100(1 - \alpha)$ %
credible interval for *θ* is $(\theta_{l}^{C},\theta_{u}^{C})$. Notice
that the intervals are quite different, even though they both have the
same probability coverage.

Although Bayesian probability intervals are the equivalent of confidence
intervals in classical inference, there is a very important difference
in their interpretation. A confidence interval is such
[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p428 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 428. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}that there is a prescribed probability that the
(random) interval contains the true (fixed) value of the unknown
parameter. However, a probability interval is such that the random
variable *θ* has a given probability of being included within it.
Additionally, Bayesian probability intervals are obtained directly from
the posterior density function and do not require the calculation of a
standard error. Consequently, they do not depend on large samples for
their validity and so they are also exact.
:::

::: section
### 16.5.3 Bayesian hypothesis tests {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_5_3 .head2}

Hypothesis tests are widely used in classical inference to assess the
validity of a null hypothesis, and their associated *P*-values are used
as a measure of the strength of evidence against this null hypothesis.
If a null hypothesis is rejected on evidence from the data, then the
alternative hypothesis is the basis for any subsequent action. The
likelihood ratio test underpins the construction of most hypothesis
testing in survival analysis, through the use of the statistic
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ to compare alternative models. However, likelihood
ratio statistics only have a known asymptotic chi-squared distribution
and rely on large samples, that is a relatively large number of events,
for their validity.

In the Bayesian approach, there is no need to distinguish between a null
and alternative hypothesis, and we simply calculate the posterior
probability that *θ* has a specific value, or range of values, using
probability intervals. This leads to a direct and exact evaluation of
the probability that a hypothesis is true.

Suppose that we hypothesise that *θ* has some value greater than *θ*~0~.
This probability is then found from the posterior density of *θ*,
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$, using

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. P(\theta > \theta_{0}) = {\int_{\theta_{0}}^{\infty}\pi}(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\theta. \right.
\end{array}$$

For example, if *θ* is a hazard ratio, so that $\theta > 0$, we might
test the hypothesis that the hazard ratio is greater than 2, say, by
simply computing the probability that the posterior density of *θ*
exceeds 2.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam16_2" role="doc-tip">

[Example 16.2]{.boxnum} [Time to discontinuation of the use of an
IUD]{.boxht}

In [Example
16.1](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_1){#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam16_1}, the
posterior distribution of *λ* in an assumed exponential distribution for
the data on IUD discontinuation times was found to be a gamma
distribution with parameters $(9,1046)$. The mode of this distribution,
the value of *λ* with highest posterior density, is $8/1046$, that is
0.00765. A 95% probability interval for *λ* is the interval
$(\lambda_{l},\lambda_{u})$ and is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. {\int_{\lambda_{l}}^{\lambda_{u}}\pi}(\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\lambda = 0.95. \right.
\end{array}$$

A highest probability density interval is difficult to obtain, as the
values $\lambda_{l},\lambda_{u}$ that satisfy this equation cannot be
found algebraically. Using numerical methods, the 95% HPD interval is
$(0.0035,0.0143)$, and means that there is a 95% chance that *λ* is in
this interval. The lower and upper $2.5\%$ points of the posterior
distribution of *λ* are more easily found. From the distribution
function of a gamma distribution with parameters $(9,1046)$, they are
0.0039 and 0.0151. The interval $(0.0039,0.0151)$ is then a 95% credible
interval for *λ*. In this example, the HPD and credible intervals are
very similar because the posterior density function of *λ* is quite
symmetric.

A test of the hypothesis that $\lambda < 0.01$ is found by evaluating
the probability that the posterior probability of *λ* is less than 0.01
from the integral

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. {\int_{0}^{0.01}\pi}(\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\lambda. \right.
\end{array}$$

This is found to be 0.717 from the distribution function of a gamma
distribution, so that there is a 72% probability that *λ* is less than
0.01.

</aside>

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p429 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 429. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The Bayesian approach to inference leads to point
estimates and exact interval estimates for an unknown parameter, as well
as exact hypothesis tests about the values of this parameter. Their
ready interpretation in terms of direct probabilistic statements about
parameter values means that this approach has considerable practical
appeal.
:::
::::::

::::: section
## 16.6 Evaluating a posterior distribution {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_6 .head1}

Although Bayesian inferential techniques have many advantages, their
widespread practical adoption has been held back by limitations in
computer power. This is no longer the case, and efficient computational
methods for the numerical evaluation of posterior density functions are
now readily available. This means that it is possible to determine the
form of a posterior density, and associated summary statistics, in
situations where the posterior density cannot be expressed in closed
form, or when the necessary integration to obtain the posterior
distribution of a parameter is not algebraically possible. These
techniques rely on simulation to obtain random samples from a posterior
distribution.

Suppose that *N* simulated values of a single parameter *θ* can be
obtained from the posterior distribution of *θ*,
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$. For
sufficiently large values of *N*, a histogram of these values will
approximate the posterior distribution from which they have been
sampled. Also, inferences about this posterior distribution can be based
on these simulated values. For example, the posterior mean will be
estimated by their sample mean, and probability intervals can be
obtained from the proportion of sample values in a prescribed range.
These estimates will get closer and closer to the corresponding true
values as *N* increases.

There are a number of possible simulation methods, but here we describe
a very general technique, known as *rejection sampling*, that is
particularly useful when the posterior distribution has a complex form.

::: section
### 16.6.1 Rejection sampling {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_6_1 .head2}

This straightforward simulation method can be used to sample values from
a posterior density function of a single parameter,
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$.
Importantly, rejection sampling can be used when
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is only
known up to a constant. Since from Equation (16.4),
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is
proportional to
$\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\theta)\pi(\theta) \right.$,
the product of the sample likelihood and the prior density, we can use
rejection sampling to sample from
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ without
having to perform the required integration to obtain the normalising
constant. This is a considerable advantage.

The basic idea is that instead of sampling from
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$, we sample
from a distribution with density function
$\left. f(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ that is then
scaled to envelop
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$. The *base
density function*
$\left. f(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is chosen to
be such that it is straightforward to simulate sample values from it.
The scaling factor, *K*, is taken to be such that
$\left. Kf(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is never
less than $\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
at any value of *θ*, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{K = \max_{\theta}\left\{ \frac{\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.}{\left. f(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$[(16.10)]{.equationnum}

The simplest base density function is one that is constant between two
values *a* and *b*, that is a uniform density, denoted $U(a,b)$. The
corresponding base density function is
$\left. f(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = 1/(b - a) \right.$
for $a\leqslant\theta\leqslant b$ and zero otherwise, where the limits
*a,b* are chosen to be beyond the plausible range of posterior values of
*θ*. From Equation (16.10), the scaling factor is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{K = (b - a)\max_{\theta}\left\{ \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p430 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 430. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The value of *θ* that maximises
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is the
mode of this posterior distribution, $\widetilde{\theta}$, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. K = (b - a)\pi(\widetilde{\theta}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}). \right.
\end{array}$$

The value of $\widetilde{\theta}$ may be found by differentiating
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ with
respect to *θ* and equating the derivative to zero. When the posterior
density is not easy to differentiate, as is often the case, a numerical
method for function maximisation may be required.

To illustrate, suppose that we wish to sample from the posterior
distribution of a non-negative parameter *θ* with the density shown in
[Figure 16.2](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_2){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_2}. A
uniform base density in the range $(a,b)$, is shown as a dashed line in
this figure, which clearly does not enclose the distribution from which
we wish to sample from. The uniform density is therefore scaled by
multiplying $\left. f(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
by
$\left. K = (b - a)\pi(\widetilde{\theta}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
to give a function that is constant at
$\left. \pi(\widetilde{\theta}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$.
This function is shown as a solid line in [Figure
16.2](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_2).

<figure id="fig16_2" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_2.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_2" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.2</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Illustration of rejection sampling with a
<span class="math inline"><em>U</em>(<em>a</em>, <em>b</em>)</span> base
density function.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The next step is to simulate a random number, *θ*~*s*~, from the base
density, $\left. f(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$. We
also simulate a further random number *u* from a $U(0,1)$ distribution.
Then if

$$\begin{array}{r}
{u\leqslant\frac{\left. \pi(\theta_{s}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.}{\left. Kf(\theta_{s}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.},}
\end{array}$$

we accept *θ*~*s*~ as a sample value from
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ and reject
*θ*~*s*~ otherwise. This is equivalent to simulating a random number,
*v*, from a uniform distribution on
$\left. (0,Kf(\theta_{s}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})) \right.$ and
accepting *θ*~*s*~ if
$\left. v\leqslant\pi(\theta_{s}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$.
The simulated value *θ*~*s*~ is therefore accepted with probability
$\left. \pi(\theta_{s}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})/Kf(\theta_{s}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
and is a sampled value from
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$. This is
also illustrated in [Figure 16.2](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_2). Since the
*x*-coordinate of a uniform random number under the graph of
$\left. f(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is the same
as that of a uniform random number under the graph of
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$, this
simulation process works with posterior densities that are only known up
to a constant.

This process is now repeated many times until we obtain a sufficiently
large sample of *θ* values from the posterior distribution. These values
are then assembled into a histogram to summarise the posterior density.
In addition, the mean of the sampled values can be used as the mean of
the posterior distribution of *θ*, and probability intervals for *θ* can
also be obtained from the simulated values.

Using a uniform envelope, illustrated in [Figure
16.2](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_2), leads to many sampled values *θ*~*s*~
being rejected, because the base density function is not very close to
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$. A better
choice for the base density might be based on a gamma distribution, or
even a normal distribution. More detailed refinements would include
using a piecewise base function and an adaptive approach where
$\left. f(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is changed
as information about
$\left. \pi(\theta\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
accumulates.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam16_3" role="doc-tip">

[Example 16.3]{.boxnum} [[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p431 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 431. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Time to discontinuation of
the use of an IUD]{.boxht}

To illustrate rejection sampling, values from the posterior distribution
of *λ* in [Example 16.1](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_1) will be simulated.
Although we know the actual density of the posterior distribution in
this case, we will in this example assume that
$\left. \pi(\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is only
known up to a constant. Then, the posterior density is proportional to
the product of the likelihood of the data
$\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda) \right.$ and the
prior distribution on *λ*, $\pi(\lambda)$, so that from Equation (16.9)
