in [Section 16.3](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_3),

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. \pi(\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \propto L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda)\pi(\lambda) = \lambda^{8}\exp( - 1046\lambda). \right.
\end{array}$$

A graph of this function against *λ* is shown in [Figure
16.3](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_3){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_3}. On
differentiating the logarithm of this function, and setting the
derivative to zero, we find that it has a maximum at
$\widetilde{\lambda} = 8/1046 = 0.00765$, and the density is
$3.927 \times 10^{- 21}$ at $\widetilde{\lambda}$. From the graph in
[Figure 16.3](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_3), we also see that the function
is practically zero for $\lambda > 0.02$, and so a uniform base density
on $(0,0.02)$ is used. This is multiplied by
$\left. K = 0.02\,\pi(\widetilde{\lambda}\, \middle| \, t) \right.$ so
that the function $\lambda^{8}\exp( - 1046\lambda)$ is then enclosed by
a rectangle with a maximum at $3.927 \times 10^{- 21}$.

<figure id="fig16_3" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_3.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_3" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.3</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">The function <span
class="math inline"><em>λ</em><sup>8</sup>exp (−1046<em>λ</em>)</span>.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

Next, 10,000 values from a uniform distribution on $(0,0.02)$ are
simulated and each simulated value of *λ, λ*~*s*~, is accepted as a
value from the posterior distribution of *λ*,
$\left. \pi(\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$, if a
second uniform random number, *u*, in the interval
$(0,3.927 \times 10^{- 21})$ does not exceed
$\lambda_{s}^{8}\exp( - 1046\lambda_{s})$. Notice that we are not using
the full posterior density here, and yet the simulated values will be a
sample from the posterior distribution.

From the simulated values, we get the histogram shown in [Figure
16.4](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_4){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_4}. The
estimated density values have been obtained from the frequency
distribution of the simulated values by dividing these by their sum and
the interval width, which is 0.0005 here.

<figure id="fig16_4" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_4.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_4" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.4</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Histogram of simulated values from the
posterior distribution of <em>λ</em> in an exponential model fitted to
data on discontinuation times, together with the actual posterior
density function.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

Superimposed on this histogram is the actual gamma posterior density
function. There is very good agreement between them and this would be
further improved with a larger number of simulations. The sample mean
and median of the simulated values from the posterior distribution of
*λ* are 0.00861 and 0.00832, respectively. A 95% credible interval for
*λ*, obtained from the lower and upper 2.5% points of the distribution
of simulated values, is $(0.0040,0.0149)$. The hypothesis that *λ*
exceeds 0.01 can be examined using the proportion of simulated *λ*
values greater than 0.01, which is 0.284. The corresponding exact values
for the mean and median of the posterior gamma distribution are 0.00860
and 0.00829, the 95% credible interval is $(0.0039,0.0151)$ and
$\text{P}(\lambda > 0.01) = 0.283$. The agreement is excellent.

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p432 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 432. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}As only 23% of the 10,000 simulated values were
accepted in the sampling process, this procedure is relatively
inefficient. A base density that is closer to the function formed from
$\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\lambda)\pi(\lambda) \right.$
would be expected to lead to improved agreement with the same number of
simulations.

</aside>

This example illustrates how a simulation method can be used to obtain
the marginal posterior distribution of a single parameter. The next step
is to determine the joint posterior density of *p* unknown parameters in
a vector $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, and again this is done using
simulation.
:::

::: section
### 16.6.2 Sampling from a posterior distribution using MCMC {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_6_2 .head2}

The posterior distribution of a vector of parameters,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$,
$\left. \pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,
can be simulated by drawing independent samples from
$\left. \pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
through a process of updating previously sampled values. These simulated
values are then used to identify and summarise key features of the
posterior distribution.

Denote the set of parameters in $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ excluding
*θ*~*j*~ by $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}$ and let
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*}$ be the most recently sampled
values of the components of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}$. The
posterior density of *θ*~*j*~, conditional on the values
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*}$ and the data
$\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}$, is then
$\left. \pi(\theta_{j}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,
and using Equation (16.4), this is proportional to

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\theta_{j},\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*})\,\pi(\theta_{j},\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*}), \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.11)]{.equationnum}

for $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$. In this expression,
$\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\theta_{j},\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*}) \right.$
is the sample likelihood and
$\pi(\theta_{j},\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*})$ is the prior
density function of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, evaluated at *θ*~*j*~,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*}$. The constant of proportionality
ensures that the conditional posterior density,
$\left. \pi(\theta_{j}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,
integrates to unity over *θ*~*j*~. Values of the components of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ can be simulated from this posterior density,
without knowing the constant of proportionality, using rejection
sampling described in [Section 16.6.1](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_6_1).
Starting with initial values of the components of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*}$, a value $\theta_{j}^{*}$ is
sampled from
$\left. \pi(\theta_{j}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)}^{*},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$.
This is then used in updating the remaining components of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, and the process repeated to give a sequence
of simulated values of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$.

In this simulation process, the latest simulated value of a parameter in
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ depends only on the most recent value. This
property is referred to as the *Markov property*, and the consecutive
simulated values, which are found using fixed transition probabilities,
$\left. \pi(\theta_{j}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{( - j)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,
[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p433 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 433. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}in the updating, form a *Markov chain*. The
simulation process is known as *Markov Chain Monte Carlo*, or MCMC. From
the theory associated with Markov chains, the distribution from which
the sequence of simulated values,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(1)},\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(2)},\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(3)},\ldots$,
is drawn converges to
$\left. \pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$
under certain conditions that generally hold when simulating values from
a posterior distribution. Consequently, once the stream of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ values has become stable, successive values
are taken as simulated values from the posterior distribution
$\left. \pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,
and these are then used to summarise the characteristics of this
posterior distribution. Also, the posterior density of any component of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ can be estimated from the histogram of the
sequence of sampled values of this parameter, which may be smoothed
using density estimation techniques.

One of the most widely used algorithms to simulate values of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ is a particular MCMC process known as *Gibbs
sampling*, that is summarised by the following steps.

1.  Specify initial values of the *p* unknown parameters in
    $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, denoted
    $\theta_{1}^{(0)},\theta_{2}^{(0)},\ldots,\theta_{p}^{(0)}$, and
    assembled into the vector $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(0)}$.

2.  Sample a value of each parameter in turn from its conditional
    posterior distribution, conditioning on the immediately preceding
    values of all the other parameters. Therefore,

    1.  $\theta_{1}^{(1)}$ is sampled from
        $\left. \pi(\theta_{1}\, \middle| \,\theta_{2}^{(0)},\theta_{3}^{(0)},\theta_{4}^{(0)},\ldots,\theta_{p}^{(0)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,

    2.  $\theta_{2}^{(1)}$ is sampled from
        $\left. \pi(\theta_{2}\, \middle| \,\theta_{1}^{(1)},\theta_{3}^{(0)},\theta_{4}^{(0)},\ldots,\theta_{p}^{(0)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,

    3.  $\theta_{3}^{(1)}$ is sampled from
        $\left. \pi(\theta_{3}\, \middle| \,\theta_{1}^{(1)},\theta_{2}^{(1)},\theta_{4}^{(0)},\ldots,\theta_{p}^{(0)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,

    and so on until

    1.  $\theta_{p}^{(1)}$ is sampled from
        $\left. \pi(\theta_{p}\, \middle| \,\theta_{1}^{(1)},\theta_{2}^{(1)},\theta_{3}^{(1)},\ldots,\theta_{p - 1}^{(1)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$.

    This leads to the first set of simulated values of the components of
    $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(1)}$.

3.  This process is then repeated, so that

    1.  $\theta_{1}^{(2)}$ is sampled from
        $\left. \pi(\theta_{1}\, \middle| \,\theta_{2}^{(1)},\theta_{3}^{(1)},\theta_{4}^{(1)},\ldots,\theta_{p}^{(1)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,

    2.  $\theta_{2}^{(2)}$ is sampled from
        $\left. \pi(\theta_{2}\, \middle| \,\theta_{1}^{(2)},\theta_{3}^{(1)},\theta_{4}^{(1)},\ldots,\theta_{p}^{(1)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,

    3.  $\theta_{3}^{(2)}$ is sampled from
        $\left. \pi(\theta_{3}\, \middle| \,\theta_{1}^{(2)},\theta_{2}^{(2)},\theta_{4}^{(1)},\ldots,\theta_{p}^{(1)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,

    and so on until

    1.  $\theta_{p}^{(2)}$ is sampled from
        $\left. \pi(\theta_{p}\, \middle| \,\theta_{1}^{(2)},\theta_{2}^{(2)},\theta_{3}^{(2)},\ldots,\theta_{p - 1}^{(2)},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$.

    This gives a second set of simulated values,
    $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(2)}$.

4.  This process is continued *N* times until a vector
    $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(N)}$ of values
    $\theta_{1}^{(N)},\theta_{2}^{(N)},\ldots,\theta_{p}^{(N)}$ is
    obtained, where *N* is sufficiently large to ensure that the
    sequence of values of each parameter has converged.

At each step in this simulation process, a sample value from the
conditional distribution in Expression (16.11) can be drawn using the
rejection method described in [Section
16.6.1](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_6_1).

The sequence of simulated values usually takes a while to settle down
and so the first *M* sample values of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ are
usually discarded. This initial period is called the *burn-in*. The
simulation is then run a further *N* times, and these *N* sampled values
are then regarded as being drawn from the posterior distribution of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, and form the basis for posterior inferences.
Specifically, the posterior distribution of each parameter *θ*~*j*~,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, is summarised by the histogram of the *N* simulated
values $\theta_{j}^{(1)},\theta_{j}^{(2)},\ldots,\theta_{j}^{(N)}$.
Bayesian point estimates such as the mean and median of the posterior
distribution of *θ*~*j*~ and interval estimates can similarly be found.
Hypothesis tests about *θ*~*j*~ can be performed by calculating the
proportion of the *N* simulated values that are in accord with a
particular hypothesis, which estimates the probability that the
hypothesis is true.

The number of samples discarded and the number used as sample values
from the posterior distribution depends on the speed of convergence of
the $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ values. Typical choices for the
[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p434 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 434. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}number of discarded samples are between 10 and
1000, but it may be much larger. The actual value chosen depends on the
accuracy of the initial estimates and how well-behaved the sequence is.
If initial estimates are taken to be estimates from fitting a survival
model using a non-Bayesian approach, the sequence will tend to converge
quite quickly. The number of samples used after the burn-in period is
typically between 1,000 and 20,000.

By monitoring the initial sequence of *M* simulated values of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, and the subsequent *N* values after the
burn-in period, convergence can be assessed. This monitoring process is
facilitated by graphical procedures, such as a plot of successive
simulated values of each parameter, known as the *trace*. This plot
gives a visual assessment of the stability of the sequence of parameter
values generated.

The above description summarises the essential features of the Gibbs
sampler, but this algorithm is subject to many refinements that render
it computationally efficient. In addition, checks may be incorporated
into the algorithm to monitor and ensure convergence of the simulated
values. The sample values of a particular parameter generated in this
way will be correlated, and this means that a larger number of values
may be needed than would otherwise be the case. Again, this
autocorrelation can be monitored and the equivalent sample size for
autocorrelated values can be evaluated. The MCMC process may also
require an additional *adaptive phase*, to fine-tune the algorithm to
optimise performance. Samples generated during this phase are discarded.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam16_4" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 16.4]{.boxnum}](#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam16_4){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

To illustrate how a posterior distribution is estimated in a parametric
model, we return to the data first given in [Example
1.2](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_2){#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam1_2} of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml) on the survival times of women with breast cancer.
In [Example 5.6](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_6){#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam5_6}
of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), a Weibull model was fitted to these
data according to which the hazard of death at time *t* for the *i*th
individual is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\beta x_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $x_{i} = 1$ if the tumour was positively stained and zero
otherwise. The log-hazard ratio for the staining effect is *β* and
$h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$ is the baseline Weibull hazard
with scale parameter *λ* and shape parameter *γ*. The corresponding
survivor function is $S_{i}(t) = S_{0}{(t)}^{\exp(\beta x_{i})}$, where
$S_{0}(t) = \exp\left\{ - \lambda t^{\gamma} \right\}$. Using these
results, the likelihood of the sample data can be found directly using
Equation (16.5). This likelihood function is combined with prior
information about *β, λ* and *γ*, where the prior density functions are
assumed to be independent. Diffuse priors will be used for each
parameter. Specifically, we take $\left. \beta \right.\sim N(0,10^{2})$,
and $\left. \log\lambda \right.\sim N(0,10^{2})$, which corresponds to a
lognormal distribution for *λ*. A half-normal prior with
$\sigma^{2} = 10^{2}$ will be used for *γ*, which is a distribution with
mean 8 and variance 36.

A MCMC simulation method will be used to fit the model with a burn-in of
1000 simulations and a subsequent run of 10,000. A trace of individual
posterior values of the three unknown parameters in the model is shown
in [Figure 16.5](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_5){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_5}.
The trace plots in this figure suggest that the simulated values are
very stable, so that the modelling process has successfully converged to
the relevant posterior distributions.

<figure id="fig16_5" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_5.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_5" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.5</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Trace plots of the posterior values of <em>β,
λ</em> and <em>γ</em> from 10,000 simulations.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The median and a 95% credible interval for *β, λ* and *γ* can be found
from the 10,000 simulated values from their respective posterior
distributions and are $1.010\,(0.060,2.098)$, $0.003\,(0.001,0.018)$ and
$0.951\,(0.677,1.287)$, respectively. The corresponding estimate and
credible interval for the hazard of a positive stain, $\exp(\beta)$, is
$2.745\,(1.062,8.067)$. As these estimates are found from a simulation
modelling process, they will not be exactly reproducible.

The credible interval for the shape parameter *γ* includes unity,
suggesting that an exponential model may well be suitable here. We will
return to this in [Example
16.6](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_6){#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam16_6}. The full
posterior distribution of the hazard ratio can be summarised using the
histogram of the 10,000 simulated values of $\exp(\beta)$, which is
shown in [Figure
16.6](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_6){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_6}.

<figure id="fig16_6" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_6.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_6" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.6</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Simulated posterior distribution of the
hazard ratio for the staining effect in women with breast
cancer.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p435 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 435. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}From the simulation, 9,822 values of $\exp(\beta)$
are greater than unity, so the probability that the hazard ratio for the
staining effect is greater than unity is 0.98. Indeed, the probability
that this hazard ratio exceeds 4 is 0.24. There is therefore a very
strong effect of staining. These estimates are in broad agreement with
those found when fitting a Weibull model to the data using the classical
approach, given in [Example
5.5](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_5){#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam5_5} of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml). However, the credible intervals and the results of
the hypothesis testing procedures are now described using probability
statements.

To further illustrate this process, a Cox regression model with a
piecewise exponential baseline hazard function, described in [Section
16.3](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_3), will be fitted to the data using a
Bayesian approach. The prior distribution of *β* will be taken to be
$N(0,10^{2})$ and gamma priors with $r = \lambda = 0.001$ will be used
for the parameters in the baseline hazard function. The likelihood
function used in the modelling is that described in [Section
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1). A MCMC simulation process with a burn-in
of 1000 and subsequent run of 10,000 simulations will again be used. The
resulting median of the posterior values of the hazard ratio,
$\exp(\beta)$, []{#chapter16.xhtml_p436 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 436. " role="doc-pagebreak"}together with a 95%
credible interval is $2.504\,(0.988,7.571)$. Comparing this to the
corresponding posterior estimate on fitting a Weibull distribution, we
see that they are quite similar, although the credible interval for the
Cox model just includes unity.

</aside>
:::
:::::

::: section
## 16.7 Predictive distributions {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_7 .head1}

Once a model has been fitted, we may wish to use this to obtain
estimates of functions of the unknown parameters, such as the estimated
survivor function or hazard function for an individual with specified
values of explanatory variables in the model. Bayesian point estimates
of model parameters from a MCMC simulation process, such as their mean
or median, can be used to obtain such estimates directly by substituting
these values for unknown parameters in a survivor or hazard function.

For example, suppose that an exponential model is fitted to data
consisting of *n* survival times and the values of a single explanatory,
*X*, that takes the value *x*~*i*~ for the *i*th individual,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$. The survivor and hazard functions for the *i*th
individual are then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - \exp(\beta x_{i})\lambda t \right\},\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, h_{i}(t) = \exp(\beta x_{i})\lambda,}
\end{array}$$[(16.12)]{.equationnum}

using results in [Section 5.1.1](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_1) of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), where *β* is the coefficient of the explanatory
variable, *λ* is the constant baseline hazard under this model and
$t > 0$. Following a MCMC simulation process, *N* sets of values
$\beta^{(\nu)}$ and $\lambda^{(\nu)}$, $\nu = 1,2,\ldots,N$, will be
obtained. If $\widetilde{\beta}$ and $\widetilde{\lambda}$ are Bayesian
point estimates of *β* and *λ* from this simulation, such as the their
sample mean, an estimate of the hazard and survivor functions is
obtained by plugging these estimates into the equations in (16.12), to
give

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\widetilde{S}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ - \exp(\widetilde{\beta}x_{i})\widetilde{\lambda}t \right\},\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\widetilde{h}}_{i}(t) = \exp(\widetilde{\beta}x_{i})\widetilde{\lambda}.}
\end{array}$$

A full Bayesian analysis requires that the uncertainty in the estimated
functions is also taken into account, and for this we need a *predictive
distribution*. Suppose that we want to predict values of a survivor or
hazard function at some time *t* for a new or existing individual with
values $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{0}$ of the set of explanatory variables in
the model. The *posterior []{#chapter16.xhtml_p437 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 437. " role="doc-pagebreak"}predictive distribution*
of a function $g(t)$ for this individual is the density function of
$g(t)$, conditional on $\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{0}$ and the survival times
$\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}$, and given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. p\left\{ g(t)\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{0},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}} \right\} = {\int_{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}p}\left\{ g(t)\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{0},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}},\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}} \right\}\,\pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}. \right.
\end{array}$$

That is, we integrate the density of $g(t)$, conditional on
$\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{0}$, $\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}$, and the unknown model
parameters in $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, with respect to the posterior
distribution of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, so that the distribution of
$g(t)$ does not involve the unknown parameters. This accounts for the
uncertainty in $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$.

When a MCMC method has been used to simulate values of the model
parameters, the *ν*th set, $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)}$, can be
used to obtain the value of the function $g(t)$ at these simulated
values, $g_{\nu}(t)$, say, for $\nu = 1,2,\ldots,N$. The integral can
then be approximated by the distribution of these *N* values of $g(t)$.

For example, in the exponential model for survival times with a single
explanatory variable *X*, the predictive distribution of the survivor
function $S(t)$ when $X = x_{0}$ is

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. p\left\{ S(t)\, \middle| \, x_{0},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}} \right\} = {\int_{0}^{\infty}{\int_{- \infty}^{\infty}p}}\left\{ S(t)\, \middle| \, x_{0},\mathbf{\mathbf{t}},\beta,\lambda \right\}\,\pi(\beta,\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\beta\,\text{d}\lambda, \right.
\end{array}$$

where
$\left. \pi(\beta,\lambda\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is
the joint posterior density of $\beta,\lambda$. To approximate this
double integral, simulated values $\beta^{(\nu)}$ and $\lambda^{(\nu)}$,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,N$, are obtained using MCMC, and these will lead to *N*
simulated values of $S(t)$, given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{S^{(\nu)}(t) = \exp\left\{ - \exp(\beta^{(\nu)}x_{0})\lambda^{(\nu)}t \right\},}
\end{array}$$

for $\nu = 1,2,\ldots,N$. These simulated values form the predictive
distribution of $S(t)$. A point estimate of the survivor function at any
value of *t* is the mean of the simulated values of $S(t)$, that is
$N^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{\nu}S^{(\nu)}}(t)$. By using a range of values of
*t*, a Bayesian estimate of the survivor function can be found. Bayesian
probability intervals at any value of *t* can also be found directly
from the predictive distribution of $S(t)$ in the manner described in
[Section 16.5.2](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_5_2).

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam16_5" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 16.5]{.boxnum}](#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam16_5){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

Bayesian estimates of the parameters in a Weibull model fitted to the
data on the survival times of women with breast cancer were obtained in
[Example 16.4](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_4){#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam16_4}.
We now use the results in [Section 16.7](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_7) to
obtain the posterior survivor functions for patients with positively and
negatively stained tumours. The estimated survivor function for the
*i*th patient at time *t* is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\widetilde{S}}_{i}(t) = \exp( - e^{\widetilde{\beta}x_{i}}\widetilde{\lambda}t^{\widetilde{\gamma}}),}
\end{array}$$

where $x_{i} = 1$ if the tumour is positively stained and zero
otherwise, and $\widetilde{\beta},\widetilde{\lambda}$ and
$\widetilde{\gamma}$ are Bayesian estimates of the parameters. For a
given value of *t*, the individual estimates of $S_{i}(t)$, for each
simulated value of $\beta,\lambda$ and *γ*, then form the posterior
predictive distribution of $S_{i}(t)$. One hundred values of *t* in the
range from 0 to 225, the longest observed time in the data, were used.
The median, lower and upper 2.5% points of the resulting 10,000
simulated values of the survivor function, for each value of *t* and
$x_{i} = 0,1$, are then used to estimate the survivor functions of women
with positive and negative staining, together with uncertainty limits
formed from the 95% credible intervals at each value of *t*. The
resulting plot is shown in [Figure
16.7](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_7){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_7}. The
survivor functions are well separated, and show clearly that a patient
with a positively stained tumour has a less favourable prognosis.

<figure id="fig16_7" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_7.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_7" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.7</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Weibull survivor functions for women with
negatively (—) and positively (-- - --) stained tumours. Corresponding
95% credible intervals are also shown for negative (- - -) and positive
(<span class="middle">.......</span>) staining.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

A similar analysis can be carried out after fitting a Cox regression
model, to give [Figure
16.8](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_8){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_8}. In this
figure, the estimated survivor functions are constant between event
times and lead to stepwise functions. The stepwise survivor functions
are very similar to those shown in [Figure
16.7](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_7).

<figure id="fig16_8" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_8.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_8" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.8</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Stepwise survivor functions from fitting a
Cox model with piecewise exponential baseline hazard for women with
negatively (—) and positively (-- - --) stained tumours. Corresponding
95% credible intervals are also shown for negative (- - -) and positive
(<span class="middle">.......</span>) staining.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The next step is to formally compare these Bayesian models to examine
whether an exponential parametric model may fit as well as a Weibull,
and to compare models with and without the explanatory variable that
corresponds to the staining effect.

</aside>
:::

::::: section
## 16.8 []{#chapter16.xhtml_p438 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 438. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Bayesian model comparison {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_8 .head1}

In many practical applications of survival analysis, there is a need to
compare alternative models. Suppose that two different models are being
entertained, labelled *M*~1~ and *M*~2~, which need not necessarily be
nested. In a Bayesian framework, it is natural to compare these two
models in terms of the posterior probability of each, given the data.
From Equation (16.2), the probability of a model *M*~*j*~ given data *D*
is
$\left. \text{P}(M_{j}\, \middle| \, D) = \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, M_{j})\text{P}(M_{j})/\text{P}(D) \right.$,
for $j = 1,2$. The choice between two models can then be based on the
ratio of the conditional []{#chapter16.xhtml_p439 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 439. " role="doc-pagebreak"}probabilities of each,
given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\left. \text{P}(M_{1}\, \middle| \, D) \right.}{\left. \text{P}(M_{2}\, \middle| \, D) \right.} = \frac{\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, M_{1})\,\text{P}(M_{1}) \right.}{\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, M_{2})\,\text{P}(M_{2}) \right.}.}
\end{array}$$

This is simply the ratio of the marginal probabilities of the sample
data under each model, multiplied by the ratio of prior probabilities.
The quantity

$$\begin{array}{r}
{B = \frac{\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, M_{1}) \right.}{\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, M_{2}) \right.}}
\end{array}$$

is called the *Bayes factor*, and summarises the weight of evidence in
favour of model *M*~1~ over model *M*~2~. This is the basis for model
comparison in Bayesian inference. Typically, a value of *B* between 1
and 3 is regarded as weak evidence in favour of *M*~1~, a value between
3 and 20 constitutes reasonable evidence, values between 20 and 150
represent strong evidence, and if *B* exceeds 150, there is overwhelming
evidence in favour of Model *M*~1~.

The Bayes factor, *B*, is the likelihood ratio used in comparing models
under the classical approach to model comparison, described in [Section
3.5](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_5) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). However,
instead of maximising the likelihood over unknown model parameters
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j}$ in Model *M*~*j*~, $j = 1,2$, to obtain
estimates of these parameters, we integrate over
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j}$ to give the marginal probability of the
data which is independent of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j}$.
Consequently, $\left. \text{P}(D\, \middle| \, M_{j}) \right.$, the
marginal distribution of the data under model *M*~*j*~, is found from

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. {\int_{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j}}L_{j}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j})\,\pi_{j}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j})\,\text{d}\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j}, \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.13)]{.equationnum}

where
$\left. L_{j}(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j}) \right.$
and $\pi_{j}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}_{j})$ are the likelihood and prior
density functions under Model *M*~*j*~, $j = 1,2$.

The integral in Equation (16.13) involves the prior density function,
and so a Bayes factor cannot be obtained if an improper prior
distribution is used. Moreover, the Bayes factor is often difficult or
impossible to calculate because of the need for multiple integration
over the model parameters. Consequently, alternative statistics for
summarising model fit are generally adopted.

::: section
### 16.8.1 *DIC* statistic for comparing models {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_8_1 .head2}

One of the most widely used statistics for Bayesian model comparison is
very similar to the Bayesian Information Criterion (*BIC*) defined in
[Section 3.6](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_6) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml)
and is called the *Deviance Information Criterion* (*DIC*). As with the
*BIC* statistic, it is a combination of a term summarising the fit of a
model and a term that represents model complexity.

The *DIC* is based on a quantity
$\left. D(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) = - 2\log L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) \right.$,
which is sometimes called the *deviance*. In classical inference, the
model parameters in $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ are replaced by their
maximum likelihood estimates to give $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}$,
and $D(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$ is then the $- 2\log\hat{L}$
statistic introduced in [Section 3.5](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_5) of
[Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), and used as a basis for model comparison.

The complexity of a model is measured by an estimate of the effective
number of model parameters, *p*~*D*~. Writing
$\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}$ for the vector of posterior means
of the parameters in $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, obtained using Bayesian
point estimates, *p*~*D*~ is given by

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{D} & {= \,\text{E}\,\left\{ {- 2\log\frac{\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) \right.}{\left. L(\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}\, \middle| \,\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}) \right.}} \right\},} \\
 & {= \,\text{E}\,\left\{ D(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) \right\} - D(\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}),}
\end{aligned}$$[(16.14)]{.equationnum}

where the expectation is with respect to the parameters in
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$. The *DIC* statistic is then defined, by
analogy with the *BIC* statistic, as

$$\begin{array}{r}
{DIC = D(\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}) + 2p_{D}.}
\end{array}$$[(16.15)]{.equationnum}

Models with smaller *DIC* are preferred.

[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p440 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 440. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}When a MCMC method has been used to simulate values
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)}$, $\nu = 1,2,\ldots,N$, from the
posterior distribution of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$,
$\,\text{E}\,\left\{ D(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) \right\}$ can be
estimated using the mean deviance over the simulated values of
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, given by
$\overline{D(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}})} = N^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{\nu}D}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)})$.
Also, $\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}$ in Equation (16.14) is the
mean of the simulated $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$ values, that is
$N^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{\nu}\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)}}$, so that
*p*~*D*~ is found from
$\overline{D(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}})} - D(\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}})$,
which is the posterior mean deviance minus the deviance of the posterior
mean.
:::

::: section
### 16.8.2 *WAIC* statistic for comparing models {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_8_2 .head2}

Although widely used, the *DIC* statistic has a number of limitations,
including the fact that a reparametrisation of the model can lead to
slightly different values of the statistic. For example, different *DIC*
values may be obtained when using the same set of explanatory variables
in a proportional hazards or accelerated failure time representation of
the Weibull model, considered in [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml). Also, the
*p*~*D*~ measure of model complexity can be negative. This has led to a
number of alternative proposals, including the *Watanabe-Akaike
Information Criterion*, also known as the *Widely Applicable Information
Criterion, WAIC*.

The *WAIC* statistic is similar to the *DIC* statistic in Equation
(16.15), except that the likelihood is averaged over the posterior
distribution of $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$,
$\left. \pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$,
instead of plugging in the mean value
$\overline{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}$. This leads to the statistic

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. WAIC = - 2{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\log}p(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) + 2p_{W}, \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.16)]{.equationnum}

where $\left. p(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) \right.$ is the
*posterior marginal density* of *t*~*i*~, given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
\left. p(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) = {\int_{\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}}L}(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}})\,\pi(\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}})\,\text{d}\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}. \right.
\end{array}$$[(16.17)]{.equationnum}

The measure of model complexity, *p*~*W*~ in Equation (16.16), is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{p_{W} = 2{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\left\lbrack \log p(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{t}}) - \,\text{E}\,\left\{ \log L(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) \right\} \right\rbrack},}
\end{array}$$[(16.18)]{.equationnum}

where $\left. L(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}) \right.$ is
the contribution that the *i*th survival time makes to the likelihood
function and the expectation is taken over the model parameters in
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$. Models with smaller values of the *WAIC*
statistic are preferred. Notice that individual contributions to the
likelihood are used in the calculation of *WAIC*.

When a MCMC simulation method is being used to generate sample values
from the posterior distribution of a set of parameters
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}$, the *WAIC* statistic can be obtained from
the likelihood contribution of each observation *t*~*i*~,
$i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, calculated from each set of sampled values
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)}$, $\nu = 1,2,\ldots,N$. The posterior
marginal density in Equation (16.17) for each observation can then be
estimated by the average value of these likelihood contributions over
the $\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)}$, that is
$\left. N^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{\nu}L}(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)}) \right.$.
Also, the second term in Equation (16.18) can be estimated from the
average values of the log-likelihood of *t*~*i*~ over the
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)}$, that is
$\left. N^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{\nu}\log}L(t_{i}\, \middle| \,\mathbf{\mathbf{\theta}}^{(\nu)}) \right.$.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam16_6" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 16.6]{.boxnum}](#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam16_6){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Prognosis for women with breast cancer]{.boxht}

In [Example
16.5](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_5){#chapter16.xhtml_b16exam16_5} a
Bayesian Weibull model was fitted to the data on the prognosis for women
with positively and negatively stained tumours. To explore the
dependence of the hazard of death on the effect of staining, a Weibull
model that includes the staining effect will be compared with one that
does not. On fitting the Weibull model in [Example
16.5](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_5), the value of the *WAIC* statistic for
model comparison is 319.5. The corresponding measure of model complexity
for this model is $p_{W} = 2.9$, which is very close to 3, the number of
unknown parameters in the model ignoring those in the prior
distributions. When a model []{#chapter16.xhtml_p441 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 441. " role="doc-pagebreak"}with a common Weibull
distribution for each group of women is fitted, $WAIC = 321.3$ with
$p_{W} = 1.7$. Since this *WAIC* statistic is larger than that for the
model that includes the staining effect, we conclude that inclusion of
this effect does improve the model. The corresponding values of the
*DIC* statistic for comparing these two models are
$319.3\,(p_{D} = 2.9)$ and $321.5\,(p_{D} = 1.9)$, which are very
similar to the values of *WAIC*.

We can similarly compare Weibull and exponential models using the *WAIC*
statistic. On fitting an exponential model with the stain effect
included, $WAIC = 318.3$ with $p_{W} = 2.4$. This is less than the
corresponding value of *WAIC* for the Weibull model, suggesting that an
exponential baseline hazard would suffice for this data set. When the
staining effect is removed from the exponential model, the *WAIC*
statistic increases to 320.2 with $p_{W} = 1.2$, again confirming the
importance of including this term.

The value of *WAIC* on fitting a Cox regression model with piecewise
exponential hazards, also described in [Example
16.4](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_4), is 331.9 with $p_{W} = 18.1$. The
value of *WAIC* here is much larger than for the Weibull model, so this
model is not as good. However, this is because there are many more
parameters being fitted in the Cox model. It may well be that models
with fewer steps in the hazard function will perform better, and so
additional models with fewer parameters will be considered.

[Table 16.1](#chapter16.xhtml_tbl16_1){#chapter16.xhtml_b16tbl16_1}
gives the values of *WAIC* and *p*~*W*~ for piecewise exponential models
with different numbers of parameters in the baseline hazard function.
The times where the baseline hazard changes are placed at equally spaced
percentiles of the uncensored survival times between the smallest and
largest. A piecewise exponential model with *k* parameters will have a
baseline hazard with $k - 1$ steps. The model with $k = 1$ corresponds
to a constant baseline hazard, that is an exponential model.

  Value of *k*   *WAIC*   *p*~*W*~
  -------------- -------- ----------
  1              318.3    2.4
  2              319.1    3.1
  3              320.0    3.9
  4              320.9    5.0
  5              319.7    5.8
  6              324.7    6.7
  7              325.9    7.5

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [16.1]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter16.xhtml_b16tbl16_1){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Values of *WAIC* and *p*~*W*~ on fitting
  piecewise exponential models with *k* intervals, where $k = 1$
  corresponds to fitting the exponential model]{.table-title} {#tbl16_1}

This table shows that models with $k = 1,2,3,4,5$ intervals are
comparable, but adding more intervals leads to inferior values of
*WAIC*. Notice that the values in [Table 16.1](#chapter16.xhtml_tbl16_1)
are all less than the value of 331.9 for the model where the baseline
hazard changes at each event time. The fitted piecewise exponential
models also indicate that the baseline hazard is roughly constant up to
time 70, and decreases thereafter. Also, for $k\leqslant 5$, the values
of the *WAIC* information criterion are similar to that for the Weibull
model. Since an exponential model was found to have a very similar
*WAIC* value to that for a Weibull model, it is not surprising that a
piecewise exponential model with few knots is comparable.

</aside>

We end this chapter with a further example that illustrates the Bayesian
approach to the analysis of times to an event.

<aside class="box" epub:type="tip" id="exam16_7" role="doc-tip">

[[Example 16.7]{.boxnum}](#chapter17.xhtml_b16exam16_7){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Health evaluation and linkage to primary
care]{.boxht}

In a study on health evaluation and linkage to primary care, known as
the HELP study, a clinical trial involving patients in a residential
detoxification programme was conducted. A total of 470 patients
undergoing detoxification for alcohol, heroin or cocaine, who had no
[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p442 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 442. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}primary care physician, were randomised to receive
help from a HELP clinic in linking them to primary medical care, or no
such help. The ultimate aim of the programme was to enable the patients
to benefit from primary care, and so the response variable was the
number of days from randomisation to linkage. Some patients were never
linked to primary care and so had censored event times. Many additional
variables were recorded on each patient, but in this example we will use
age, gender and whether or not the patient was homeless when admitted to
the trial. Fuller details on the study are given by [Samet et al.
(2003)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib330){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib330
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and the data are in the R data set
[HELPmiss]{.monospace} which is part of the [mosaicDATA]{.monospace}
package. The variables in the data set are as follows:

  ------------ -------------------------------------------------------------
  *Time*:      Time to linkage to primary care in days
  *Status*:    Event indicator (0 = censored (no linkage), 1 = uncensored)
  *Age*:       Age of patient in years
  *Gender*:    Gender of patient (0 = female, 1 = male)
  *Housing*:   Homelessness status (0 = homeless, 1 = housed)
  *Help*:      Assistance in linking to healthcare (0 = no, 1 = yes)
  ------------ -------------------------------------------------------------

The first 15 lines of the data set are shown in [Table
16.2](#chapter16.xhtml_tbl16_2){#chapter16.xhtml_b16tbl16_2}.

  *Patient*   *Time*   *Status*   *Age*   *Gender*   *Housing*   *Help*
  ----------- -------- ---------- ------- ---------- ----------- --------
   1          225      1          37      1          1           1
   2          365      0          26      1          1           0
   3          343      0          39      0          1           0
   4           57      1          32      1          0           0
   5          365      0          47      0          1           1
   6          334      0          49      0          1           0
   7          365      0          28      1          0           1
   8          365      0          50      0          0           0
   9          382      0          39      1          0           1
  10          365      0          34      0          1           1
  11          365      0          58      0          1           0
  12          365      0          58      1          0           0
  13           22      1          60      1          0           1
  14          443      0          36      1          1           1
  15           41      1          28      0          0           1

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [16.2]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter16.xhtml_b16tbl16_2){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times of 15 patients in the HELP
  study]{.table-title} {#tbl16_2}

The factor of primary interest is the impact of assistance from the HELP
clinic on the time to linkage to primary care. A Bayesian approach will
be used to estimate the hazard ratio for the HELP clinic effect,
adjusting for the other three factors.

A Weibull distribution will be considered first. Vague prior
distributions will be used for the coefficients
$\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\beta_{3},\beta_{4}$ of the variables associated
with age, gender, housing status and whether or not the HELP clinic
provided assistance. Specifically, we will take these to have $N(0,1)$
distributions, which means that we anticipate that there is a 95%
probability that each *β*-coefficient lies in the range $( - 2,2)$, and
no hazard ratio is likely to exceed $e^{2} = 7.4$. A diffuse
$N(0,10^{2})$ prior will be assumed for the logarithm of the Weibull
scale parameter, $\log\lambda$, as there is much uncertainty about this
parameter. The shape parameter *γ* will be taken have a diffuse
half-normal prior derived from a $N(0,10^{2})$ distribution, and the
MCMC simulation process will use a burn-in of 1000 simulations, followed
by 10,000 runs.

On fitting a Bayesian Weibull model that contains the variables *Help,
Age, Gender* and *Housing*, the simulated posterior distributions of the
model parameters lead to hazard ratios and 95% credible intervals. These
are []{#chapter16.xhtml_p443 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 443. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}$5.155\,(3.619,7.503)$, $1.024\,(1.004,1.045)$,
$1.404\,(0.969,2.100)$ and $0.864\,(0.642,1.169)$, for *Help, Age,
Gender* and *Housing*, respectively. The value of the *WAIC* statistic
for this model is 2344.2 with $p_{W} = 5.6$. The credible interval for
the hazard ratio for the housing effect includes unity, and from the
10,000 simulated values of the hazard ratio for housing status, the
posterior probability that the hazard ratio is less than 1.0 is 0.83. We
therefore conclude that housing status does not affect the time taken to
link to primary care. When this variable is removed from the model,
$WAIC = 2343.3$ with $p_{W} = 4.6$, suggesting a slightly improved
model. The credible intervals for the hazard ratios for the three
remaining terms exclude unity, and so these variables are retained.

The next step is to see if the Weibull baseline hazard can be improved
upon by using a spline function to model the hazard function. For this,
we will use a cubic spline with a B-spline basis function for the
logarithm of the hazard function. This model, which was described in
[Section 6.3](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_3) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml),
ensures that the resulting estimate of the log-cumulative hazard will be
an increasing function of time. This is not necessarily the case when
using a restricted cubic spline model for the log-cumulative hazard, and
this can lead to difficulties in the MCMC simulation process.

From Equation (6.10) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), the cubic B-spline
model for the log-hazard function for a model with *m* internal knots is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log h_{i}(t) = \eta_{i} + {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{m + 4}\alpha_{j}}B_{j,3}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where *α*~*j*~ is the coefficient of the *j*th B-spline basis function,
$B_{j,3}(t)$, for $j = 1,2,\ldots,m + 4$, and
$\eta_{i} = \mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is a linear
combination of the values of the explanatory variables. The models to be
fitted all include the three explanatory variables that were found to be
important in the Weibull model, namely *Help, Age* and *Gender*.

A $N(0,10^{2})$ prior distribution will also be used for the
coefficients in the spline basis functions, as again there is
uncertainty about their precise values. Knots will be set at percentiles
of the distribution of the event times, as described in [Section
6.2.3](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_2_3) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), and
various numbers of knots will be used so as to identify the most
suitable model. Cubic spline models with increasing numbers of internal
knots, *m*, are now fitted, from a minimum of two boundary knots and no
internal knots. The results are tabulated in [Table
16.3](#chapter16.xhtml_tbl16_3){#chapter16.xhtml_b16tbl16_3}. In this
B-spline model for the log-hazard function, $m = 0$ does not correspond
to a Weibull distribution as it does in the restricted cubic spline
model for the log-cumulative hazard.

  Value of *m*   *WAIC*   *p*~*W*~
  -------------- -------- ----------
  0              2273.4    7.2
  1              2260.1    9.9
  2              2263.2   11.1
  3              2262.0   11.5

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [16.3]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#chapter16.xhtml_b16tbl16_3){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Values of the *WAIC* statistic for B-spline
  models for the log-hazard function with *m* internal
  knots]{.table-title} {#tbl16_3}

The spline models all have values of *WAIC* that are less than that for
the corresponding model with a Weibull baseline hazard function, where
$WAIC = 2343.3$, and so they provide a better description of the
underlying baseline hazard. However, the *WAIC* statistic starts to
increase when a second internal knot is added, and *WAIC* for the model
with three internal knots is still greater than that for the model with
just the one. This suggests that the increase in model complexity is not
outweighed by a marginally improved fit. We therefore use a B-spline
model for the log-baseline hazard that has just one internal knot.

On fitting this model using a MCMC simulation process, the hazard ratios
and 95% credible []{#chapter16.xhtml_p444 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 444. " role="doc-pagebreak"}intervals for the
variables *Help, Age* and *Gender* are, respectively,
$4.846\,(3.440,7.079)$, $1.024\,(1.004,1.045)$ and
$1.398\,(0.969,2.074)$. Consequently, every increase of one year in age
increases the chance of being linked to the healthcare system by a
factor of 1.024 or 2.4% and there is a 40% greater chance that male
patients will be so linked. Patients who have been given assistance in
accessing healthcare have nearly five times the chance of linkage. The
estimated hazard ratios based on the spline model are very similar to
those for the Weibull model, but the spline model gives a better
representation of the underlying hazard of linkage.

From the MCMC simulation, we can estimate the posterior hazard functions
and survivor functions for patients with particular characteristics who
were and were not given HELP clinic assistance, using the methods
described in [Section 16.7](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_7). The predictive
distributions of the hazard and survivor functions show the hazard of
linkage at a given time and the probability that linkage occurs after
that time, respectively. These functions are illustrated for a 30 year
old male patient in [Figures
16.9](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_9){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_9} and
[16.10](#chapter16.xhtml_fig16_10){#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_10}.

<figure id="fig16_9" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_9.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_9" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span class="ordinal">16.9</span></span></a>
<span class="figure-title">Hazard functions for a 30 year old male
patient who had no assistance (—) and who has had assistance (- - -) in
accessing primary care.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="fig16_10" class="image">
<img src="images/fig16_10.jpg" />
<figcaption><p><a href="#chapter16.xhtml_b16fig16_10" class="backlink"
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"><span class="figurenum"><span
class="label">Figure</span> <span
class="ordinal">16.10</span></span></a> <span
class="figure-title">Survivor functions for a 30 year old male patient
who had no assistance (—) and who has had assistance (- - -) in
accessing primary care.</span></p></figcaption>
</figure>

The chance of linkage to healthcare peaks at around 28 days after the
start of the study, and decreases thereafter. The figures clearly show
that those who have had assistance in accessing healthcare have a much
greater chance of actually accessing such care. Beyond 100 days, the
chance of linkage changes little.

</aside>
:::
:::::

::: section
## 16.9 Commentary {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_9 .head1}

In this chapter, we have seen how a Bayesian approach to inference can
be used in modelling survival data What then are the advantages and
disadvantages of this approach in relation to the classical approach
described in the previous chapters?

Bayesian methods provide a means of fitting complex realistic models to
large data sets using a process that makes it possible to combine new
data with any available information from external knowledge or previous
studies. This is achieved through a unified framework for inference,
that proceeds from specifying a model from which a likelihood is
constructed, through formulating prior distributions for all unknown
parameters in that model, to estimating and summarising the posterior
distribution of each parameter for use in inference.
[]{#chapter16.xhtml_p445 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 445. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Moreover, unlike classical inferential procedures,
this methodology does not rely on large samples for the validity of
point and interval estimates and hypothesis tests. A further advantage
is the ease of interpreting results. In Bayesian inference, credible
intervals and hypothesis tests are based directly on statements about
the probability that a parameter such as a hazard ratio lies in a
certain interval, or the probability that certain hypotheses are true.
This is much more easily understood than confidence intervals and
*P*-values.

Of course, there are also disadvantages. Foremost amongst these is the
necessity of incorporating prior knowledge about each unknown parameter
by formulating a prior probability distribution. Although this is
mitigated by the possible use of vague and non-informative prior
distributions when there really is no prior information, concern often
remains about the subjectivity of this. Also, the process of obtaining a
posterior distribution generally involves using simulation techniques
based on MCMC methods, which can be a computationally intensive process.
There may also be issues surrounding the selection of numerical methods,
the setting of iteration control options, and the need to identify and
remedy convergence issues. Although this is all supported by modern
computer software, the process does require a greater degree of
expertise than that need to fit a Cox regression model using the
classical approach, for example.

In summary, Bayesian approaches to the analysis of survival data provide
a powerful way to fit realistic models that can account for those
complicating features that are often found in actual studies.
:::

::: section
## 16.10 Further reading {#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_10 .head1}

A comprehensive account of the theory and practice of the Bayesian
approach to survival analysis is given in the book of [Ibrahim, Chen and
Sinha (2001)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib189){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib189
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. There are many general textbooks on
Bayesian inference. Among the introductory texts are [Bolstad and Curran
(2016)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib38){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib38 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Hoff
(2009)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib181){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib181
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Lunn et al.
(2012)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib243){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib243
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Lambert
(2018)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib225){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib225
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. More advanced texts include those of
[Bernardo and Smith
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib34){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib34 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Box and Tiao
(1973)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib39){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib39 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and [O\'Hagan and Forster
(2004)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib280){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib280
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. [Gelman et al.
(2013)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib138){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib138
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} provide a comprehensive text on
Bayesian data analysis, while [McElreath
(2020)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib257){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib257
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} illustrates Bayesian methodology with
examples from the software packages R and Stan. The recent text of [Sahu
(2022)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib329){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib329
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} contains a detailed discussion on many
aspects of []{#chapter16.xhtml_p446 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 446. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Bayesian methods. [Barnett
(1999)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib31){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib31 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} gives an overview of Bayesian inference and
compares it with other approaches to inference.

These books all describe MCMC methods for simulating values from a
posterior distribution in varying degrees of detail. More comprehensive
descriptions of this methodology can be found in [Gilks, Richardson and
Spiegelhalter
(1996)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib143){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib143
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Gamerman and Lopes
(2006)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib133){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib133
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. For more on the Gibbs sampler, see
[Geman and Geman
(1984)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib141){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib141
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Gelfand and Smith
(1990)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib137){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib137
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. A tutorial on the methodology has been
provided by [Casella and George
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib55){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib55 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and a more complete and advanced summary is given
by [Tierney
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib389){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib389
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Diagnostic methods for assessing
convergence of a chain include [Brooks and Gelman
(1998)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib48){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib48 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Gelman and Rubin
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib140){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib140
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Raftery and Lewis
(1992)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib310){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib310
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

Bayesian model selection and comparison is described in [Raftery
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib309){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib309
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Kass and Raftery
(1995)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib201){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib201
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The *DIC* statistic is due to
[Spiegelhalter et al.
(2002)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib358){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib358
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Spiegelhalter et al.
(2014)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib360){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib360
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compare *DIC* with some other measures
and review how their statistic has been received. The *WAIC* statistic
was introduced by [Watanabe
(2010](#refbib.xhtml_refbib401){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib401 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, 2013) and further developed by [Gelman, Hwang and
Vehtari (2014)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib139){#chapter16.xhtml_b16refbib139
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

[]{#chapter17.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#chapter17.xhtml_p447 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 447. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 17]{.chapnum}[Survival analysis with R]{.chaptitle} {#chapter17.xhtml_chapter17 .chaplabel}

DOI:
[10.1201/9781003282525-17](https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003282525-17){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

There are a number of statistical software packages that can be used to
implement the techniques described in this book, and some have been
mentioned in [Section 1.5](#chapter1.xhtml_sec1_5) of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml). Of these, R ([R Core Team,
2021)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib307){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib307 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} is probably the most widely used, for at least
three reasons. First, R 'packages' have been developed for a huge
variety of statistical tasks, and these are maintained and updated by
their authors. Second, there is a relatively short lead time between the
development and publication of new statistical techniques and their
implementation in R, and R code often accompanies the publication of
novel statistical methods. Third, R is freeware and available to
everyone. On the other hand, R is not an easy package to learn, and some
previous programming experience is an advantage. The output also tends
to be rather terse, and it can sometimes be difficult to interpret. The
material in this chapter should help to resolve these issues, insofar as
the analysis of survival data is concerned.

The chapter begins with information on R coding and nomenclature and an
outline of the basics of data input and editing. This is followed by
material on the R packages for modelling survival data using the methods
described in [Chapters 2](#chapter2.xhtml) to [16](#chapter16.xhtml). In
each case, sufficient detail is included to allow the reader to quickly
get started. Although there are often a number of different R packages
that might be used for any particular task, this chapter concentrates on
those used by the author to illustrate the techniques throughout this
book. In some instances, the R code that is shown here may not be the
most elegant or succinct, but it should be relatively easy to
understand. No attempt is made to provide a comprehensive review of the
software, and it is inevitable that some of the material in this chapter
will become outdated as new packages are developed and others are
deprecated.

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## 17.1 Introduction to R {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_1 .head1}

The R software can be downloaded from the Comprehensive R Archive
Network (CRAN) at [<https://www.r-project.org>]{.monospace}, which links
to sites in many countries from which R code for Linux, Windows or macOS
can be downloaded. The interface with R is rather basic, but this is
enhanced by using RStudio Desktop. This is an integrated development
environment for R that is also available as an open-source edition that
can be downloaded from
[[https://www.rstudio.com/products/rstudio/download/](https://www.rstudio.com)]{.monospace}.
As well as having a source editor that is used to enter R commands
before running them, RStudio simplifies saving and retrieving files and
accessing help on the syntax of commands. When code is run, output
appears in a separate window, and there are also windows for graphics, a
list of variables in the workspace and a history of commands used. Once
R and RStudio have been installed, R can be started by opening RStudio.

Interaction with R is through the command line that has a
'[\>]{.monospace}' prompt. In the code given in the sequel, R commands
will be prefixed with '[\>]{.monospace}', although this symbol is not
part of the command. R commands are entered into R workspace, either
directly or through the source editor in RStudio. It is often convenient
to start by specifying a working directory or folder
[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p448 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 448. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where R code can be stored, using
[setwd(]{.monospace} *name of working directory* [)]{.monospace} and the
name of the current directory can be checked using
[getwd()]{.monospace}.
:::

::::: section
## 17.2 Data input and editing {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_2 .head1}

Data sets for survival analysis will typically have columns
corresponding to the survival times, a censoring or status indicator,
and explanatory variables or factors, while the rows contain the values
of the variables for each individual. A data set that is to be analysed
using R is called a *data frame* and is a rectangular array with columns
corresponding to variables and rows to the observations.

The values of a variable can be read in directly, using for example

<figure id="comp17_1" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_1.jpg" />
</figure>

where [\<-]{.monospace} is the assignment operator. In many cases, an
equals ([=]{.monospace}) sign can be used instead. The function
[c]{.monospace} combines the arguments of the function to form a vector.
R can then be used as a calculator to manipulate the values of a
variable, as in

<figure id="comp17_2" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_2.jpg" />
</figure>

to form $y = x\log(x) + \exp(x^{3}/20)$, and the result can be output by
typing [print(y)]{.monospace} or simply [y]{.monospace} at the command
prompt. Statistical functions can also be used in calculations, as in

<figure id="comp17_3" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_3.jpg" />
</figure>

To create an R data frame containing the variables [x]{.monospace} and
[y]{.monospace}, the values are assigned to a data frame name as in

<figure id="comp17_4" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_4.jpg" />
</figure>

Comments can be added to R code by using the prefix '[\#]{.monospace}'.
The output resulting from this series of calculation steps is given
below.

<figure id="comp17_5" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_5.jpg" />
</figure>

Notice that some commands, such as [data.frame]{.monospace} are made up
of two words with a dot separating them. Variable names can also include
dots, as in [newdata.x]{.monospace}, and all variable names and command
names are case-sensitive. There are a very large number of mathematical
and statistical functions that can be used to manipulate data in this
way, including commands for conditionally executing statements,
subsetting data, matrix algebra, and so on. Help on any command, for
example [data.frame]{.monospace}, can be obtained using
[help(data.frame)]{.monospace}.

::: section
### 17.2.1 []{#chapter17.xhtml_p449 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 449. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Reading and manipulating data from a file {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_2_1 .head2}

The data sets in this book are all available from the publishers web
site at [<https://www.routledge.com/>]{.monospace}, and can be read into
R and formed as a data frame using the function
[read.table]{.monospace}. The data sets to be used in this chapter will
be assumed to be in the current working directory.

The data from [Example
1.2](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam1_2} on the
survival times of women following a diagnosis of breast cancer can be
input and assigned to a data frame named [bcancer]{.monospace} using

<figure id="comp17_6" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_6.jpg" />
</figure>

Here, the filename in quotes is the full path name or just the name of
data file itself if this is stored in the current directory. The option
[header=TRUE]{.monospace} means that the variable names have been
included in the data set and that these are then to be used as the
variable names in the data frame.

Notice that in order to render the data originally presented in [Table
1.2](#chapter1.xhtml_tbl1_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl1_2} amenable to
computer-based analysis, the two variables [time]{.monospace} and
[status]{.monospace} represent the right-censored data in which
[status=1]{.monospace} if an observed time is an event and
[status=0]{.monospace} otherwise. The data frame can be inspected by
using the [print]{.monospace} function, as in
[print(bcancer)]{.monospace}, or by just typing the data frame name at
the prompt symbol, as in

<figure id="comp17_7" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_7.jpg" />
</figure>

in which the output has been curtailed after the first five
observations. Another useful command is [summary]{.monospace} which
gives descriptive statistics for the variables in a data frame as in

<figure id="comp17_8" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_8.jpg" />
</figure>

It quite often happens that variables with the same names are used in
different data frames. To refer to a particular variable in a specific
data frame a '[\$]{.monospace}' symbol is used to link the data frame
name and the variable name. For example, to form a new variable
[stain1]{.monospace} by converting the values of the variable
[stain]{.monospace} from 1,2 to 0,1, use

<figure id="comp17_9" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_9.jpg" />
</figure>

Another procedure commonly required when manipulating data sets is the
facility to create a factor. In the breast cancer data set, stain is a
variable with values 1,2. To set this as a factor with two levels, and
label them "Negative" and "Positive", respectively, use

<figure id="comp17_10" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_10.jpg" />
</figure>

In this code, the factor labels are assigned in the same order as the
data, so that when [bcancer\$stain=1]{.monospace} the factor level is
labelled "Negative".

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p450 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 450. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}To quit an R session, use [quit]{.monospace}, which
gives you the option of saving the contents of the current workspace for
future use. However, to specifically save a workspace in a file of type
RData, and restore it later, the following code may be used.

<figure id="comp17_11" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_11.jpg" />
</figure>

Further manipulations that can be carried out are described in the many
text books on R, some of which are mentioned in the further reading
section at the end of this chapter.
:::

::: section
### 17.2.2 R packages {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_2_2 .head2}

The R program comes with many facilities for manipulating, analysing and
displaying data, some of which have been outlined in this section.
However, the power of R is realised through the use of *packages*. A
package consists of code, sample data and documentation that can be
downloaded from the Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN). Typically, a
package contains a number of functions that are used for particular
tasks within the package. There are literally thousands of packages made
available by their authors, covering statistical techniques, data
visualisation, data manipulation, machine learning, report generation
and so on. Packages can be incorporated in R when needed for some
specific purpose by first installing a package. Installed packages are
then loaded into the current workspace to enable them to be used in an R
session.

A particularly important package for survival analysis is the
[survival]{.monospace} package. Full documentation is included on the
CRAN site, and the author has provided an accompanying vignette that
describes the package, [Therneau
(2022)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib377){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib377
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The package implements a great variety
of methods for survival analysis, including descriptive techniques, Cox
regression modelling, parametric modelling, modelling with
time-dependent variables, frailty modelling and techniques for model
checking.

The base R software includes a number of widely used packages, including
[survival]{.monospace}, but this, and any other package, can be
installed and loaded using the following commands

<figure id="comp17_12" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_12.jpg" />
</figure>

The list of packages in a library can be checked using
[library()]{.monospace}. Many packages require others in order to work
properly, and these will be downloaded with the main package.

In the following sections, R code is given for implementing particular
techniques in modelling survival data, following the ordering of
chapters in this book.
:::
:::::

::: section
## 17.3 Non-parametric procedures {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_3 .head1}

The first step in analysing survival data is often to plot the
Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function for two or more groups of
data. To do this in R, the [survfit]{.monospace} function in the
[survival]{.monospace} package is used. The following code can be used
to estimate the survivor functions for the two groups of women in the
data on breast cancer prognosis.

<figure id="comp17_13" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_13.jpg" />
</figure>

The [survfit]{.monospace} function includes a *survival object*,
[Surv]{.monospace}, which is used to describe the response variable in a
*model formula*. It has arguments that are the survival time and
censoring indicator, which in this example are [time]{.monospace} and
[status]{.monospace}. This is then linked to a model formula using the
'$\left. \, \right.\sim\,$' symbol. The model formula may consist of
just '[1]{.monospace}' if there are no terms in a model, but otherwise
it is a string of variable and factor names joined with a '$\,$
[+$\,$]{.monospace}' symbol. Interaction terms can also be included
using a colon ([:]{.monospace}) to link terms, so that a model where
coefficients of a variable in [x]{.monospace} depend on the levels of a
factor in [a]{.monospace}, would be represented by [a:x]{.monospace}.

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p451 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 451. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In the preceding code, the requirement for a
Kaplan-Meier estimate for each of the two staining groups is indicated,
but an overall estimate ignoring the two groups would be obtained using
[Surv(time,status)$\left. \, \right.\sim\,$ 1]{.monospace}. The
[summary]{.monospace} function gives a numerical summary of the
estimated survivor functions for each value of [stain]{.monospace},
which can either be a variable or factor. The summary output for
[stain=1]{.monospace} is as follows.

<figure id="comp17_14" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_14.jpg" />
</figure>

In this code, the results of using [survfit]{.monospace} are saved to
the [survfit]{.monospace} object [km]{.monospace}. This object contains
information that can be extracted, or used in supplementary commands.
For example, a graph of the two survivor functions is obtained using the
plot function with [km]{.monospace}, as in

<figure id="comp17_15" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_15.jpg" />
</figure>

and leads to a plot very similar to that shown in [Figure
2.9](#chapter2.xhtml_fig2_9){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig2_9} of [Chapter
2](#chapter2.xhtml). The [plot]{.monospace} function has many options
for customising the plot that are described in the CRAN documentation.
There are also other plotting functions available, including
[xyplot]{.monospace} in the [lattice]{.monospace} package and the
package [ggplot2]{.monospace}.

The R function [survdiff]{.monospace} in the [survival]{.monospace}
package can be used to perform the log-rank and Peto-Peto tests
described in [Section 2.6](#chapter2.xhtml_sec2_6) of [Chapter
2](#chapter2.xhtml). The test statistic used in this function is a
weighted sum of differences between $d_{1j}$ and $e_{1j}$, the observed
and expected numbers of deaths in one group at the *j*th of *r* event
times and is given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{U_{T} = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{r}w_{j}}(d_{1j} - e_{1j}).}
\end{array}$$

The weights are
$w_{j} = {\lbrack{\hat{S}}_{KM}(t_{(j)})\rbrack}^{\rho}$, where
${\hat{S}}_{KM}(t_{(j)})$ is the overall Kaplan-Meier estimate of the
survivor function, on the assumption that there is no difference between
the survivor functions of the two groups, at the *j*th ordered event
time, $t_{(j)}$, $j = 1,2,\ldots,r$. The log-rank test is obtained when
$\rho = 0$, which is the default value of *ρ*, and $\rho = 1$ gives the
Peto-Peto test.

For the data on breast cancer prognosis, where the data are contained in
the R data frame [bcancer]{.monospace}, a log-rank test can be performed
using [survdiff]{.monospace} with the following code.

<figure id="comp17_16" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_16.jpg" />
</figure>

Using [print(lr)]{.monospace} results in the following output, which is
in agreement with the calculations in [Example
2.12](#chapter2.xhtml_exam2_12){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam2_12} of
[Chapter 2](#chapter2.xhtml).

<figure id="comp17_17" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_17.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p452 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 452. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The output gives observed and expected numbers of
events for each staining group, with the test statistic in the final
column headed [(O-E)\^2/V]{.monospace}. The test statistic is the same
whether either group is used in the calculation. As in this example, the
