default number of decimal places is sometimes not sufficient. The number
of digits in the output can be changed by using the
[digits=]{.monospace} option of the [print]{.monospace} function, as in
[print(lr,digits=5)]{.monospace}. The resulting output gives a
chi-squared value of 3.515 $(P = 0.0608)$.

The Wilcoxon test is not implemented in the [survival]{.monospace}
package, but as the results are usually very similar to those from the
Peto-Peto test, this can be used instead. To do this, we include the
option [rho=1]{.monospace} in [survdiff]{.monospace}. The necessary code
and output to conduct a Peto-Peto test is as follows

<figure id="comp17_18" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_18.jpg" />
</figure>

and the results agree with the calculations in [Example
2.13](#chapter2.xhtml_exam2_13){#chapter17.xhtml_b2exam2_13}.

If a Wilcoxon test is specifically required, the R function
[gehan.wilcoxon.test]{.monospace} in the
[PHInfiniteEstimates]{.monospace} package can be used in the following
manner, after first installing [PHInfiniteEstimates]{.monospace}.

<figure id="comp17_19" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_19.jpg" />
</figure>

This again is in agreement with the calculations in [Example
2.13](#chapter2.xhtml_exam2_13).

The R code for these tests can be extended to include more than one
factor, and stratified tests described in [Section
2.8](#chapter2.xhtml_sec2_8) of [Chapter 2](#chapter2.xhtml) can be
implemented by including a [strata]{.monospace} term in the model
formula.
:::

:::::: section
## 17.4 The Cox regression model {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4 .head1}

The Cox regression model for the hazard of an event at time *t* for the
*i*th of *n* individuals is given

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t)}
\end{array}$$

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is a linear
combination of the values of explanatory variables for the *i*th
individual, $i = 1,2,\ldots,n$, and $h_{0}(t)$ is an unspecified
baseline hazard function. This model can be fitted using the R function
[coxph]{.monospace} in the [survival]{.monospace} package. The syntax
for specifying the model is the same as that used in the function
[survfit]{.monospace}, in that a [Surv]{.monospace} object is linked to
a model formula.

An important issue is the treatment of tied survival times, discussed in
[Section 3.3.2](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_3_2) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml). Many software packages for Cox regression analysis
use Breslow\'s method as the []{#chapter17.xhtml_p453 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 453. " role="doc-pagebreak"}default, primarily because
it is computationally straightforward. However, the R
[survival]{.monospace} function [coxph]{.monospace} defaults to using
Efron\'s method, as this can be more accurate. This means that default
output from R may not match that from other software packages. To change
the method of dealing with ties in [coxph]{.monospace}, the option
[ties="breslow"]{.monospace} is included, as in the illustration below.
Output will then correspond to the examples in this book, where
Breslow\'s method has been used.

To illustrate the use of the [coxph]{.monospace} function, data on the
survival times of 48 patients with multiple myeloma, given in [Example
1.3](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_3){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam1_3} of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml), will be used. R code to input the data and fit a
Cox regression model containing the variables *Hb* and *Bun* is given
below.

<figure id="comp17_20" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_20.jpg" />
</figure>

This results in the following output, obtained using
[summary(coxregfit)]{.monospace}.

<figure id="comp17_21" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_21.jpg" />
</figure>

The estimated coefficients of the variables *Hb* and *Bun* in the fitted
Cox regression model are $- 0.1336$ and 0.0186, with standard errors of
0.0620 and 0.0057, respectively. The output includes Wald tests based on
the statistic $z = \hat{\beta}/\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\beta})$ and
corresponding *P*-values. Also given are the hazard ratios,
$\exp(\hat{\beta})$, and corresponding 95% confidence limits in the
columns headed [lower. 95]{.monospace} and [upper. 95]{.monospace}. This
is then followed by a concordance measure, which is the *c*-statistic
described in [Section 3.12.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_1) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml), and the results of likelihood ratio, Wald and score
tests of the hypothesis that all coefficients in the fitted model are
zero. These tests were defined in [Section A.2](#appA.xhtml_secA_2) of
[Appendix A](#appA.xhtml_appA), but the Wald and score tests are rarely
used.

The output from [coxph]{.monospace} does not included the value of the
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic, but this can be extracted from the
[coxph]{.monospace} object [coxregfit]{.monospace} using

<figure id="comp17_22" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_22.jpg" />
</figure>

The two values from this output are the values of the statistic for the
model with no explanatory variables, that is the null model, and the
fitted model, respectively. The values of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ agree with
those in [Table 3.9](#chapter3.xhtml_tbl3_9){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl3_9}
of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml).

The result of a likelihood ratio test for comparing the fitted model
with the null model, shown in the output from [coxph]{.monospace}, is
the difference in the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ values for the null and
[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p454 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 454. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}fitted models, which is
$215.940 - 202.938 = 13.00$. This can be compared with percentage points
of a chi-squared distribution on 2 d.f., to test whether both *Hb* and
*Bun* are needed in the model. As described in [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml), the model building approach generally includes
variables sequentially, and the result of this test is not usually of
interest. Instead, the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic is obtained for each
model and changes due to the inclusion or omission of terms are compared
to percentage points of a chi-squared distribution. However, differences
in the likelihood ratio test statistic given in the R output will be the
same as those between the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ values when two different
models have been fitted.

Values of the *AIC* or *BIC* statistics for a model fitted using
[coxph]{.monospace} can be obtained directly by adding 2*q* or
$q\log d$, respectively, to the value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$, where *q* is
the number of *β*-parameters in a model and *d* is the number of
uncensored observations. Alternatively, the function
[extractAIC]{.monospace} can be applied to a [coxph]{.monospace} object.
The default is to give *AIC*, but for *BIC*, the option [k=]{.monospace}
is set to be $\log d$. Using [k=2]{.monospace}, the default, gives the
standard formula for *AIC*.

For example, the data on survival times of patients diagnosed with
multiple myeloma has $d = 36$ events. This value is accessible from the
[coxph]{.monospace} object [coxregfit]{.monospace} as
[coxregfit\$nevent]{.monospace}, and the code below gives the *AIC* and
*BIC* statistics for the model containing *Hb* and *Bun*.

<figure id="comp17_23" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_23.jpg" />
</figure>

In the output, the first number is the value of the number of parameters
in the model, *q*, and $AIC = 206.94$, $BIC = 210.10$.

::: section
### 17.4.1 Variable selection and the lasso {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4_1 .head2}

R does not include automatic routines for variable selection as these
have a number of disadvantages, outlined in [Section
3.6](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_6) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). However,
the [anova]{.monospace} function in the [survival]{.monospace} package
gives the values of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ found when terms are added
sequentially to a model in the order in which they appear in the model
formula. For example, output from [anova(coxregfit)]{.monospace} is as
follows.

<figure id="comp17_24" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_24.jpg" />
</figure>

Values of the maximised partial log-likelihood are given, which can be
multiplied by $- 2$ to give the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic. Starting
with the null model, *Hb* is added followed by *Bun*. The *P*-values in
the column headed [Pr( \> \|Chi\|)]{.monospace} refer to the
significance of the change in $- 2\log\hat{L}$ on adding each term
sequentially. Adding *Hb* to the null model leads to a significant
reduction in $- 2\log\hat{L}$, as does adding *Bun* to the model that
contains *Hb*. The *P*-value of 0.0043 for *Bun* is the effect of this
variable on the hazard of death after allowing for the effect that *Hb*
has.

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p455 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 455. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}To see the significance of *Hb*, adjusted for
*Bun*, the two variables are given in reverse order in the
[coxph]{.monospace} call, as in

<figure id="comp17_25" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_25.jpg" />
</figure>

Variable selection in a Cox regression model using the lasso was
described in [Section 3.7](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_7) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml) and can be implemented using the R function
[penalized]{.monospace} in the [penalized]{.monospace} package. Suppose
that the lasso procedure is to be used to identify which of the seven
potential explanatory variables is needed in a Cox regression model
fitted to the data on the survival of multiple myeloma patients, as
illustrated in [Example
3.7](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_7){#chapter17.xhtml_b3exam3_7} of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml). Once the [penalized]{.monospace} package has been
installed and loaded, as described in [Section
17.2.2](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_2_2), the lasso procedure is implemented
using

<figure id="comp17_26" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_26.jpg" />
</figure>

The survival object [Surv(time,status)]{.monospace} is again used as the
response, but the model formula is given in the [penalized=]{.monospace}
option. Note that a '$\left. \, \right.\sim\,$' symbol is needed at the
start of the formula. If only some parameters are to be penalised, the
remainder can be declared in the [unpenalized=]{.monospace} option. The
option [lambda1=0]{.monospace} specifies that the *L*~1~ norm penalty is
to be used, which corresponds to the lasso, with a minimum value of
zero. The [steps=]{.monospace} option specifies that 20 values of *λ*
are to be used. The option [steps="Park"]{.monospace} gives an automatic
choice of step lengths using the method described in [Park and Hastie
(2007)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib284){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib284
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The option [standardize=]{.monospace},
if set to [TRUE]{.monospace}, sets the explanatory variables on similar
scales of measurement, as described in [Section
3.7.2](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_7_2). By assigning the
[penalized]{.monospace} object to [penfit]{.monospace}, the R commands

<figure id="comp17_27" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_27.jpg" />
</figure>

give the partial log-likelihood values for each value of the lasso
parameter, *λ*, and also a trace of the estimated coefficients for the
values of *λ*, as shown in [Figure
3.2](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig3_2}.
:::

::: section
### 17.4.2 Measures of predictive ability and explained variation {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4_2 .head2}

Various measures of predictive ability and explained variation were
summarised in [Section 3.12](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml). Harrell\'s *c*-statistic is found using the
[concordance]{.monospace} function in the [survival]{.monospace}
package, and Gönen and Heller\'s measure of predictive ability, together
with its standard error, can be found using the function
[phcpe]{.monospace} in the [CPE]{.monospace} package, using

<figure id="comp17_28" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_28.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p456 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 456. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The *c*-statistic was also included in the output
from using [summary(coxregfit)]{.monospace}.

The *D*-statistic that summarises predictive ability, and the measures
of explained variation denoted $R_{P}^{2}$ and $R_{D}^{2}$, can be
obtained using the [royston]{.monospace} function in the
[survival]{.monospace} package. The values of these statistics for the
model containing *Hb* and *Bun* fitted to the data on multiple myeloma
are found as follows.

<figure id="comp17_29" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_29.jpg" />
</figure>

In this output, the first two items refer to the value and standard
error of the *D*-statistic, [R.D]{.monospace} is $R_{D}^{2}$ and
[R.KO]{.monospace} is $R_{P}^{2}$ in the notation of [Section
3.12](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml). Also,
[R.N]{.monospace} is Nagelkerke\'s *R*^2^ value ([Nagelkerke,
1991](#refbib.xhtml_refbib271){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib271 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}) and [C.GH]{.monospace} is the Gönen and Heller
measure of concordance. The values of these measures agree with those
shown in [Table
3.20](#chapter3.xhtml_tbl3_20){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl3_20} of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml).
:::

::: section
### 17.4.3 Time-dependent ROC curves {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4_3 .head2}

The function [survivalROC]{.monospace} in the package of the same name
can be used to estimate time-dependent receiver-operating characteristic
curves after fitting a model to survival data. The ROC curve is formed
from the risk score or linear predictor for the fitted model and by
default, the unique values of the risk score are used as threshold
values for calculating true- and false-positive rates. Since the
[survivalROC]{.monospace} function only requires the risk score to
estimate a time-dependent ROC curve, the function can be used with any
parametric survival model as well as a Cox regression model.

After fitting a Cox regression model to the multiple myeloma data, the
risk score, or linear predictor, can be extracted from the
[coxph]{.monospace} object [coxregfit]{.monospace} using

<figure id="comp17_30" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_30.jpg" />
</figure>

However, the resulting values of the risk score in [lp1]{.monospace} are
based on the values of the variables *Hb* and *Bun* that are centred
about their mean. This means that the linear predictor, or risk score,
is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{- 0.13364\,(Hb - 10.2521) + 0.01857\,(Bun - 33.9167),}
\end{array}$$

since the means of the *Hb* and *Bun* values are 10.2521 and 33.9167,
respectively. The score based on the untransformed variables is
$- 0.13364\, Hb + 0.01857\, Bun$, and to calculate this from the values
of variables that are centred on zero, the following code can be used.

<figure id="comp17_31" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_31.jpg" />
</figure>

The effect of this is to add the sum of the products of the coefficients
and sample means of each variable to the linear predictor formed from
the centred values of the variables.

After installing and loading [survivalROC]{.monospace}, the coordinates
of a time-dependent ROC curve at 12 months can be obtained using the
[survivalROC]{.monospace} function in the following manner.

<figure id="comp17_32" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_32.jpg" />
</figure>

In this code, [Stime=]{.monospace} and [status=]{.monospace} have been
assigned to the survival time and censoring indicator in the myeloma
data frame, [marker=]{.monospace} has been set to the risk score in
[lp0]{.monospace} and the time point set to 12 months using the option
[predict.time=12]{.monospace}. The Kaplan-Meier method of estimation has
also been specified using [method="KM"]{.monospace}. The true and
false-positive rates for each threshold value can be extracted from the
[survivalROC]{.monospace} object [mroc]{.monospace} using [TP12
\<-mroc\$TP]{.monospace}, [FP12 \<-mroc\$FP]{.monospace}, and the area
under the ROC curve obtained from [mroc\$AUC]{.monospace}. Following
this, [TP12]{.monospace} can be plotted against [FP12]{.monospace} to
give the ROC curve at 12 months, shown in [Figure
3.10](#chapter3.xhtml_fig3_10){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig3_10}.

The nearest neighbour method of estimation can also be implemented,
using the option [method="NNE"]{.monospace}. In addition, the value of
the smoothing parameter, *ω* in Equation (3.44), is set to 0.05 using
the option [span=0.05]{.monospace}.
:::
::::::

:::::: section
## 17.5 []{#chapter17.xhtml_p457 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 457. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Model checking in the Cox regression model {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_5 .head1}

Many of the model checking diagnostics described in [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml) are based on various types of residual. To
illustrate how residuals can be obtained using R, data on the survival
times of patients with multiple myeloma, first given in [Example
1.3](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_3), will again be used.

::: section
### 17.5.1 Analysis of residuals {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_5_1 .head2}

Residuals are easily obtained by applying the [residuals]{.monospace}
function in the [survival]{.monospace} package to a
[survival]{.monospace} object. In [Section
17.4](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4), a Cox regression model was fitted to
the multiple myeloma data and the results stored in the
[survival]{.monospace} object [coxregfit]{.monospace}. The martingale
and deviance residuals can then be found, and assigned to the variables
[martres]{.monospace} and [devres]{.monospace}, using

<figure id="comp17_33" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_33.jpg" />
</figure>

Cox-Snell and modified Cox-Snell residuals can be found from the
martingale residuals using Equations (4.6) and (4.4) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml) with

<figure id="comp17_34" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_34.jpg" />
</figure>

The score, Schoenfeld and scaled Schoenfeld residuals can be obtained
using the options [type=c("score")]{.monospace},
[type=c("schoenfeld")]{.monospace} and
[type=c("scaledsch")]{.monospace}, respectively, in the
[residuals]{.monospace} function. The martingale and deviance residuals
have a single value for each individual and are provided as vectors.
However, the score, Schoenfeld, and scaled Schoenfeld residuals are
matrices of values for each individual and each explanatory variable in
the model.

When the [residuals]{.monospace} function is used to obtain the score
residuals for each of the two variables *Hb* and *Bun*, in the data on
multiple myeloma, the matrix of score residuals has 48 rows in the same
order as the data frame, one for each patient, and columns for the score
residuals for *Hb* and *Bun*. These columns are labelled with the
variable names in the data frame, that is [hb]{.monospace} and
[bun]{.monospace}, but it is usually convenient to change these names to
avoid confusion with the variables in the original data. To obtain the
score residuals and change the column names to [score.hb]{.monospace}
and [score.bun]{.monospace}, use

<figure id="comp17_35" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_35.jpg" />
</figure>

It is also useful to place the residuals in a data frame with the
original data, so that they can easily be manipulated. To do this,
columns containing residuals are *bound* using the [cbind]{.monospace}
function. For example, to bind columns containing Cox-Snell, martingale,
deviance and score residuals, calculated using the
[residuals]{.monospace} function, to the data frame named
[myeloma]{.monospace}, use

<figure id="comp17_36" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_36.jpg" />
</figure>

The values of the variables in the object named
[myeloma.res]{.monospace} can be inspected using
[print(myeloma.res)]{.monospace} and the first 10 values of some of
these are shown below.

<figure id="comp17_37" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_37.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p458 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 458. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}For some diagnostic plots, it is useful to have the
ranks of the survival times and the risk score in the same data frame as
the other residuals. The ranks can easily be found by applying the
[rank]{.monospace} function to the times, using for example [ranktime
\<-rank(time)]{.monospace}. The linear predictor,
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, can be
obtained from a [coxph]{.monospace} object in the manner described in
[Section 17.4.3](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4_3). The resulting score is
centred on the means of the variables, but apart from this shift in the
horizontal axis, plots of residuals against the risk score will be the
same as those shown in [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml). However, risk
scores that are not centred, used in this book, can be obtained using

<figure id="comp17_38" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_38.jpg" />
</figure>

which can then be bound with the data frame [myeloma.res]{.monospace}
using

<figure id="comp17_39" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_39.jpg" />
</figure>

Residual plots are then found using, for example,

<figure id="comp17_40" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_40.jpg" />
</figure>

which gives a plot of the deviance residuals against the risk score, as
in [Figure 4.6](#chapter4.xhtml_fig4_6){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig4_6}. A
cumulative hazard plot of the Cox-Snell residuals, described in [Section
4.2.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_2_1) can be obtained by using the
[survfit]{.monospace} function with the residuals specified as the time
variable. That is,

<figure id="comp17_41" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_41.jpg" />
</figure>

The estimated cumulative hazards can be found from the
[survfit]{.monospace} object [cskm]{.monospace} as
[cskm\$cum]{.monospace}, which gives the Nelson-Aalen estimate of the
cumulative hazard function defined in [Section
2.3.3](#chapter2.xhtml_sec2_3_3). The cumulative hazard function can
also be estimated from $- \log\hat{S}(r_{Ci})$, where $\hat{S}(r_{Ci})$
is the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function of the Cox-Snell
residuals, calculated using [ch \<\--log(cskm\$surv)]{.monospace}. The
cumulative hazard can then be plotted against the values of the
Cox-Snell residuals, obtained as [cskm\$time]{.monospace}, using
[plot(ch $\left. \right.\sim$ cskm\$time)]{.monospace}. The resulting
plot is similar to that shown in [Figure
4.5](#chapter4.xhtml_fig4_5){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig4_5}.

The matrix of Schoenfeld residuals has one row for each event time in
the data set, and one column per variable. The rows are ordered by the
variable associated with the event times. These cannot easily be
combined with other residuals, but the matrices of the Schoenfeld and
scaled Schoenfeld residuals include the event times as row labels. For
example, the Schoenfeld residuals for the multiple myeloma data can be
found using

<figure id="comp17_42" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_42.jpg" />
</figure>

and the first 10 rows of the matrix [schoenres]{.monospace} are shown
below

<figure id="comp17_43" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_43.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p459 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 459. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Again, the columns can be relabelled, and it is
also useful to convert the column of row labels, which are the event
times, into a variable. The matrix is then converted to a data frame for
further manipulation. This is done using

<figure id="comp17_44" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_44.jpg" />
</figure>

and results in the data frame [schoenres]{.monospace} including the
variable [time]{.monospace}.

The matrix of scaled Schoenfeld residuals can be similarly obtained and
manipulated. This results in a data frame that can easily be used to
produce a plot of the scaled Schoenfeld residuals against the event
time, to examine the assumption of proportional hazards, as described in
[Section 4.4.2](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_2) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml).
:::

::: section
### 17.5.2 Identification of influential observations {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_5_2 .head2}

A delta-beta, $\Delta_{i}{\hat{\beta}}_{j}$, is the approximate change
in the *j*th parameter estimate when the *i*th observation is omitted
from the model fitting process. This quantity can also be scaled to give
a standardised delta-beta. Both these quantities were described in
[Section 4.3.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_3_1) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml) and are used to explore how particular observations
influence the parameter estimates and their significance. Both
statistics can be obtained from the [residuals]{.monospace} function in
the [survival]{.monospace} package. For example, delta-betas and scaled
delta-betas for the two variables *Hb* and *Bun* in the fitted Cox
regression model for the multiple myeloma data are found using

<figure id="comp17_45" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_45.jpg" />
</figure>

Both function calls lead to a matrix with rows corresponding to the 48
patients and columns corresponding to the two explanatory variables *Hb*
and *Bun*. The column names can be changed and the matrices merged with
the data frame [myeloma.res]{.monospace} that contains other diagnostics
in the same way as for score residuals, described in [Section
17.5.1](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_5_1).

The two statistics that measure the influence that individual
observations have on the set of parameter estimates, $LD_{i}$ and
$\left| \mathbf{\mathbf{l}}_{\max} \right|_{i}$, introduced in [Section
4.3.2](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_3_2) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml), are
readily obtained using the matrix algebra facilities of R. Specifically,
the likelihood displacement, $LD_{i}$, is the *i*th diagonal element of
the matrix $\mathbf{\mathbf{B}}$ in Equation (4.16), and
$\left| \mathbf{\mathbf{l}}_{\max} \right|_{i}$ is the eigenvector
corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{\mathbf{B}}$. For
the multiple myeloma data, these diagnostics can be obtained from the
matrix of score residuals, [scoreres]{.monospace}, and the
variance-covariance matrix of the parameter estimates in the fitted
model, which is obtained from the [coxph]{.monospace} object
[coxregfit]{.monospace} as [coxregfit\$var]{.monospace}. The following R
code achieves this.

<figure id="comp17_46" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_46.jpg" />
</figure>

In this code, matrix multiplication is performed using the
[%\*%]{.monospace} operator and the function [t]{.monospace} yields the
transpose of a matrix. The matrix operation [diag]{.monospace} gives the
diagonal elements of [B]{.monospace}, and the function
[eigen]{.monospace} returns a list of components including the
eigenvalues in decreasing order and the corresponding standardised
eigenvectors. Assigning [e]{.monospace} to this list, the eigenvector
corresponding to the largest eigenvalue is the first, and can be
accessed as the first column in the matrix formed by the eigenvectors,
using [e\$vectors\[,1\]]{.monospace}. The absolute value of these is
then assigned to [lmax]{.monospace}. The variables [LD]{.monospace} and
[lmax]{.monospace} can be added to another data frame using the
[cbind]{.monospace} function, for further analysis.
:::

::: section
### 17.5.3 []{#chapter17.xhtml_p460 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 460. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Testing the assumption of proportional hazards {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_5_3 .head2}

A log-cumulative hazard plot of the data, described in [Section
4.4.1](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_1) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml), can be
found from the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor function. In
[Example 4.9](#chapter4.xhtml_exam4_9){#chapter17.xhtml_b4exam4_9} of
[Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml), the variable *Hb* associated with serum
haemoglobin level was redefined as a factor with four levels, and the
assumption of proportional hazards over these levels was examined. The
factor [hbfactor]{.monospace} can be defined as having levels 1,2,3,4 in
the [myeloma]{.monospace} data frame using

<figure id="comp17_47" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_47.jpg" />
</figure>

and a [survfit]{.monospace} object [km1]{.monospace} generated using

<figure id="comp17_48" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_48.jpg" />
</figure>

The [survfit]{.monospace} object includes the variables
[km1\$time]{.monospace} and the Kaplan-Meier estimate of the survivor
function, [km1\$surv]{.monospace}. To plot the log-cumulative hazards,
the factor levels for *Hb* have to be attached to the [km1]{.monospace}
object. As this object has a different length to the original data
frame, the [km1]{.monospace} object cannot just be combined with the
factor levels in [hblevels]{.monospace}. However, [km1]{.monospace}
includes the variable [km1\$strata]{.monospace} that is associated with
the haemoglobin levels and that can be used to generate the factor
levels for *Hb* using

<figure id="comp17_49" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_49.jpg" />
</figure>

This command assigns values 1, 2, 3, 4 to [hblevels]{.monospace} and
repeats each level according to the number of observations in each of
the four strata. A log-cumulative hazard plot can be obtained using
either the Nelson-Aalen estimate of the log-cumulative hazard or the
Kaplan-Meier estimate, with the following [plot]{.monospace} functions,
respectively.

<figure id="comp17_50" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_50.jpg" />
</figure>

The option [pch=]{.monospace} in [plot]{.monospace} provides a different
plotting symbol for each level of *Hb*. The two plots are very similar,
but the second version leads to [Figure
4.13](#chapter4.xhtml_fig4_13){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig4_13}.

A plot of the scaled Schoenfeld residuals against survival time,
described in [Section 4.4.2](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_2) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml), can also be used to assess the validity of the
proportional hazards assumption. This plot can be obtained from the
matrix of scaled Schoenfeld residuals in the manner described at the end
of [Section 17.5.1](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_5_1).

The test statistics described in [Section
4.4.3](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_3) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml) can be
found using the function [cox.zph]{.monospace} in the
[survival]{.monospace} package on the [coxph]{.monospace} object. For
example, for the data on multiple myeloma,

<figure id="comp17_51" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_51.jpg" />
</figure>

gives the values of chi-squared statistics and their *P*-values for the
individual Grambsch and Therneau tests for the variables *Hb* and *Bun*
in the fitted model as well as the overall *zph* test.
:::
::::::

::: section
## 17.6 Parametric survival models {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_6 .head1}

Parametric models for survival data, described in [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml), can be fitted using the [survreg]{.monospace}
function in the [survival]{.monospace} package. This function fits
accelerated failure time models using the log-linear representation of
the model described in [Section 5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_11), and can
be used to model survival times that are assumed to have an exponential,
Weibull, lognormal or log-logistic distribution, amongst others.

In the accelerated failure time model for a random variable *T*~*i*~
associated with the *i*th of *n* survival times,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\log T_{i} = \mu + \alpha_{1}x_{i} + \alpha_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + \alpha_{p}x_{pi} + \sigma\epsilon_{i},}
\end{array}$$[(17.1)]{.equationnum}

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p461 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 461. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}where *μ* and *σ* are intercept and scale
parameters, respectively, the *α*s are the coefficients of the *p*
explanatory variables in the model and *ϵ*~*i*~ is a random variable
that has a probability distribution that depends on the distribution of
*T*~*i*~. The effect of an explanatory variable on survival can be
summarised as a time ratio, $\exp(\alpha)$, which is the amount by which
a survival time is multiplied for a unit increase in the value of the
corresponding explanatory variable.

The syntax for the [survreg]{.monospace} function is very similar to
that of [coxph]{.monospace}, and the default is to fit a Weibull model.
For example, the following code can be used to fit a Weibull model to
the data on survival times following a diagnosis of multiple myeloma,
first given in [Example 1.3](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_3) of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml).

<figure id="comp17_52" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_52.jpg" />
</figure>

The output obtained using [summary(weibfit)]{.monospace} is shown below.

<figure id="comp17_53" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_53.jpg" />
</figure>

From this output, the parameter estimates in the accelerated failure
time model of Equation (17.1) are $\hat{\mu} = 2.704$,
$\hat{\sigma} = 0.899$, and the estimated coefficients of the variables
*Hb* and *Bun* are 0.1146 and $- 0.0158$, respectively. The
[Log(scale)]{.monospace} parameter in the output is just
$\log\hat{\sigma}$. This part of the output gives the standard error of
each parameter estimate, the value of the Wald statistic computed as
$z = \hat{\alpha}/\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\alpha})$, and the corresponding
*P*-value. Both *Hb* and *Bun* significantly affect survival. A unit
increase in the value of *Hb* increases the survival time by a factor of
$\exp(0.1146) = 1.121$, that is by 12%, and a unit increase in the value
of *Bun* decreases survival by 1.4%.

The log-likelihood for the model, [Loglik(model)]{.monospace}, is
computed using Equation (5.46) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), and the
value of the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic is found from
[-2\*weibfit\$loglik]{.monospace}, which gives 306.996. This statistic
is used to compare alternative models. The log-likelihood for the model
with an intercept only, labelled [Loglik(intercept only)]{.monospace},
is that for the null model, and the chi-squared statistic in the output
compares the fitted model to this null model, a comparison that is
usually of little interest. The output also gives the number of
iterations needed to fit the model and the number of observations in the
data frame, [n]{.monospace}.

The Weibull accelerated failure time model can also be expressed as a
proportional hazards model where the hazard of death at time *t* for the
*i*th individual is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t).}
\end{array}$$

The survival time of an individual for whom all the explanatory
variables are zero is assumed to have a Weibull distribution with scale
parameter *λ* and shape parameter *γ*, and the baseline hazard function
is given by $h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$. The
correspondence between the parameters of the accelerated failure time
and proportional hazards versions of the Weibull
[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p462 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 462. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}model was given in Equation (5.53) of [Chapter
5](#chapter5.xhtml). In particular, the log-hazard ratio in the
proportional hazards version of the model is $\beta = - \alpha/\sigma$.
The estimated log-hazard ratios for unit change in the values of *Hb*
and *Bun* are then $- 0.1146/0.899 = - 0.1275$ and
$0.0158/0.899 = 0.0176$, leading to hazard ratios of 0.88 and 1.02,
respectively.

Accelerated failure time models with survival time distributions other
than the Weibull can be fitted using [survreg]{.monospace} with the
option [dist=]{.monospace} that has values ["exponential"]{.monospace},
["loglogistic"]{.monospace} or ["lognormal"]{.monospace}, amongst
others. In each case, the intercept, scale and *α*-parameters in the
log-linear model in Equation (17.1) are given. The correspondence
between these and other parametrisations of the models are described in
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml). In each case, the output is very similar
to that obtained for the Weibull model, and alternative models are
compared using the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic. For example, to fit a
log-logistic accelerated failure time model to the data on multiple
myeloma, the following code is used.

<figure id="comp17_54" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_54.jpg" />
</figure>

and [summary(llfit)]{.monospace} gives

<figure id="comp17_55" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_55.jpg" />
</figure>

The time ratio and log-likelihood values for the Weibull and
log-logistic models are very similar.
:::

:::::: section
## 17.7 Flexible parametric models {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_7 .head1}

Piecewise exponential models and flexible parametric models based on
spline functions, presented in [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), are readily
fitted using R.

::: section
### 17.7.1 Piecewise exponential model {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_7_1 .head2}

The piecewise exponential model described in [Section
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_1) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml) can be
fitted using the [phreg]{.monospace} function in the [eha]{.monospace}
package. Data from [Example
3.4](#chapter3.xhtml_exam3_4){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam3_4} of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml) on the survival times of 36 patients with
hypernephroma will be used to illustrate this function, and piecewise
exponential models were fitted in [Example
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_1){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam6_1} of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml). The data on the survival times, survival status,
and the variables *Age* and *Nephrectomy*, are first input and the
variable *Age* defined as a factor with levels $< 60$, $60 - 70$ and
$> 70$, using the following code.

<figure id="comp17_56" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_56.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p463 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 463. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}To fit a model with 22 intervals for the baseline
hazard function, so that it is equivalent to a Cox regression model, use

<figure id="comp17_57" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_57.jpg" />
</figure>

The function [phreg]{.monospace} can be used to fit a number of
distributions, and defaults to the Weibull. A piecewise exponential
model is fitted using the option [dist="pch"]{.monospace}, and the
[cuts=]{.monospace} option specifies the cut points for the baseline
hazard function. Using [print(pefit)]{.monospace} leads to the following
output.

<figure id="comp17_58" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_58.jpg" />
</figure>

The entries in the column headed [W.mean]{.monospace} in the output are
the weighted means of variables or the relative frequency of factor
levels. The weights are the proportions of the total observed survival
time accounted for by patients with each level of a factor, or each
value of a variable. In this data set, the total survival time over the
36 patients is 1340 months, which is labelled [Total time at
risk]{.monospace} in the output. For level 1 of the factor *Age*, there
are 19 patients and the survival times of this group add up to 862. The
proportion of the overall survival time experienced by patients in the
first age group is then $862/1340 = 0.643$, as shown. Similarly, for the
variable *Nephrectomy*, the total survival time of patients with
$Nephrectomy = 0$ is 88 and that for patients with $Nephrectomy = 1$ is
1252. The weighted mean of this variable is then
$(88 \times 0 + 1252 \times 1)/1340 = 0.934$, as shown in the output.

The next block of output gives the coefficient and hazard ratio for each
term in the model, along with their standard errors and a Wald *P*-value
that is derived from the statistic
$z = \hat{\beta}/\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\beta})$. Hazard ratios for factors
are relative to the first level, and agree with those quoted in [Example
6.1](#chapter6.xhtml_exam6_1). The statistic [Max. log.
likelihood]{.monospace} is the maximised log-likelihood for the fitted
model, and the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic is obtained by multiplying
this by $- 2$, using [-2\*pefit\$loglik]{.monospace}. The [LR test
statistic]{.monospace} and the corresponding [Overall
p-value]{.monospace} is a comparison between the fitted model and the
null model.

The [phreg]{.monospace} object [pefit]{.monospace} contains a number of
other variables, including the values of the baseline hazard function in
the 22 intervals, found using [pefit\$hazards]{.monospace}.
:::

::: section
### 17.7.2 Models for the hazard function {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_7_2 .head2}

B-spline models for the logarithm of the baseline hazard function are
more widely used in Bayesian survival analysis and will be illustrated
later in [Section 17.15](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_15).
:::

::: section
### 17.7.3 []{#chapter17.xhtml_p464 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 464. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Models for the log-cumulative hazard function {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_7_3 .head2}

The Royston and Parmar model that uses a restricted cubic spline to
model the log-cumulative hazard, described in [Section
6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_4) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml), can be
fitted using the function [flexsurvspline]{.monospace} in the package
[flexsurv]{.monospace}. Once this package has been installed and loaded,
models with different numbers of knots are easily fitted. Consider again
the data on survival following diagnosis with multiple myeloma,
introduced in [Example 1.3](#chapter1.xhtml_exam1_3) of [Chapter
1](#chapter1.xhtml), and also used in [Sections
17.4](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4)--[17.6](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_6). As
noted in [Section 6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_4) of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml), the Royston-Parmar model with no internal knots
corresponds to a Weibull model. The option [k=]{.monospace} is used to
specify the number of knots and so a Weibull model containing the
variables *Hb* and *Bun* can also be fitted using

<figure id="comp17_59" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_59.jpg" />
</figure>

This results in the following output.

<figure id="comp17_60" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_60.jpg" />
</figure>

The first part of the output gives the sample means of the variables
*Hb* and *Bun*, followed by the estimated parameters in the model. In
the notation of [Section 6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_4) of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml), ${\hat{\gamma}}_{0} = - 3.0077$,
${\hat{\gamma}}_{1} = 1.1123$, and the estimated *β*-coefficients of the
two variables in the linear component of the model are $- 0.1274$ and
0.0176, respectively. Since the proportional hazards version of the
Weibull model is used here, these estimated coefficients are log-hazard
ratios, and their values are quite similar to those found on fitting a
Cox regression model, in [Section 17.4](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4). The
*γ*s relate to the parameters in the Weibull baseline hazard,
$h_{0}(t) = \lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$, and from [Section
6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_4) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml),
$\gamma_{0} = \log\lambda$ and $\gamma_{1} = \gamma$. The standard error
of the coefficients and their lower and upper 95% confidence limits are
shown. The column headed [exp(est)]{.monospace} gives the exponential of
the coefficients, which are hazard ratios, together with their 95%
confidence limits. After a summary of the numbers of observations,
events and censored values in the data, the sum of the survival times is
shown as [Total time at risk]{.monospace}. The maximised log-likelihood
for the fitted model and the number of degrees of freedom, which is the
number of unknown model parameters, is followed by the *AIC* statistic
used in model comparison.

Models with increasing numbers of knots can readily be fitted using a
sequence of [flexsurvspline]{.monospace} functions with different values
for the option [k=]{.monospace} and models compared using *AIC*.
Proportional odds models with a restricted cubic spline function,
described in []{#chapter17.xhtml_p465 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 465. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Section
6.5](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_5) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml) can also be
fitted using the [flexsurvspline]{.monospace} function with the option
[scale="odds"]{.monospace}. The output is very similar, but the
estimated coefficients in the fitted model are now log-odds ratios.
:::
::::::

::::: section
## 17.8 Model checking in parametric models {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_8 .head1}

Residuals for parametric models, described in [Section
7.1](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1) of [Chapter 7](#chapter7.xhtml), are
readily obtained. The [survreg]{.monospace} object includes the
estimated linear component of the accelerated failure time model, given
by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\eta}}_{i} = \hat{\mu} + {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}x_{i} + {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}x_{2i} + \cdots + {\hat{\alpha}}_{p}x_{pi},}
\end{array}$$

from which the standardised residuals can be found. On fitting a Weibull
accelerated failure time model to the multiple myeloma data, the
[survreg]{.monospace} object [weibfit]{.monospace} contains the linear
predictors, ${\hat{\eta}}_{i}$, in
[weibfit\$linear.predictors]{.monospace} and the scale parameter,
$\hat{\sigma}$ in [weibfit\$scale]{.monospace}. Standardised residuals,
$r_{Si}$, are then found using

<figure id="comp17_61" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_61.jpg" />
</figure>

Cox-Snell residuals are obtained using Equations (7.3) and (7.2) of
[Chapter 7](#chapter7.xhtml), where for the particular case of a Weibull
distribution, $S_{\epsilon_{i}}(\epsilon) = \exp( - e^{\epsilon})$, as
in Equation (7.7), so that $r_{Ci} = \exp(r_{Si})$. These residuals can
simply be calculated using [csres \<-exp(stres)]{.monospace}. Martingale
and deviance residuals are then found using

<figure id="comp17_62" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_62.jpg" />
</figure>

Deviance residuals may also be obtained directly using

<figure id="comp17_63" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_63.jpg" />
</figure>

However, the resulting deviance residuals will have the opposite sign to
those calculated from the martingale residuals as [devres]{.monospace}.
The reason for this difference in sign is explained in [Section
7.1.4](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_1_4) of [Chapter 7](#chapter7.xhtml).

As with residuals obtained from a fitted Cox regression model, columns
of residuals can be bound with the data frame for use in plots and
further analyses in the manner described in [Section
17.5.1](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_5_1).

::: section
### 17.8.1 Influential values {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_8_1 .head2}

The influence of observations on a parameter estimate, $\hat{\alpha}$,
in a fitted parametric survival model can be investigated using the
delta-beta statistics described in [Section 7.4](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_4)
of [Chapter 7](#chapter7.xhtml). The unscaled and scaled delta-betas can
be extracted from a [survreg]{.monospace} object using the
[residuals]{.monospace} function with [type=c("dfbeta")]{.monospace} or
[type=c("dfbetas")]{.monospace}. Values of the likelihood displacement
statistic described in [Section 7.4.2](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_4_2) of
[Chapter 7](#chapter7.xhtml) can be found using
[type=c("ldcase")]{.monospace}.
:::

::: section
### 17.8.2 Comparing observed and fitted survivor functions {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_8_2 .head2}

Comparison of a model-based estimate of the survivor function with the
observed Kaplan-Meier estimate provides an informal means of evaluating
model fit, as described in [Section 7.3](#chapter7.xhtml_sec7_3) of
[Chapter 7](#chapter7.xhtml). Some manipulation is needed to obtain the
fitted survivor function for individuals with different levels of risk
score, and the process will be illustrated using data on the survival
times of 26 women following one of two types of chemotherapy treatment
for ovarian cancer, given in [Example
5.11](#chapter5.xhtml_exam5_11){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam5_11} of
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml).

The data are first input and assigned to the data frame
[ovarian]{.monospace}. A Weibull model containing the variables *Age*
and *Treat* is then fitted and saved to the [survreg]{.monospace} object
[ofit]{.monospace}. This can be done using

<figure id="comp17_64" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_64.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p466 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 466. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The risk score for each patient is then extracted
from the fitted accelerated failure time version of the Weibull model
and saved in the variable [eta]{.monospace}. In addition, the rank of
each score is obtained, so that the patients can be divided into three
risk groups according to whether the rank of the score is in the range
from 1 to 8, 9 to 17 or 18 to 26. This variable is added to the data
frame, and leads to the grouping shown in [Table
7.2](#chapter7.xhtml_tbl7_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl7_2} of [Chapter
7](#chapter7.xhtml). The code to accomplish this is given below.

<figure id="comp17_65" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_65.jpg" />
</figure>

Next, the fitted survivor function under the Weibull model, for each of
the 26 women, is calculated for values of the survival time *t* from 0
to 1200 in steps of 50. This is done by repeating each block of 26 rows
in the augmented data frame [ovarian]{.monospace}, 25 times, and then
adding a variable that contains the times [tt]{.monospace} at which the
estimated survivor function is to be computed, using

<figure id="comp17_66" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_66.jpg" />
</figure>

The result is a new data frame, [sdata]{.monospace}, with
$25 \times 26 = 650$ rows. Finally, the estimated survivor function is
calculated using

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\hat{S}(t_{i}) = \exp\left\{ {- \exp\left( \frac{\log t_{i} - {\hat{\eta}}_{i}}{\hat{\sigma}} \right)} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where
${\hat{\eta}}_{i} = \hat{\mu} + {\hat{\alpha}}_{1}Age_{i} + {\hat{\alpha}}_{2}Treat_{i}$
is the linear predictor, and $Age_{i}$ and $Treat_{i}$ are the values of
*Age* and *Treat* for the *i*th woman. The R code for this is based on
the function [aggregate]{.monospace} to form the group means for
combinations of values in [tt]{.monospace} and [group]{.monospace} and
is shown below.

<figure id="comp17_67" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_67.jpg" />
</figure>

The data frame [avsurv]{.monospace} then contains the average of the
survivor function estimates in [survest]{.monospace} by risk group
[group]{.monospace} and time [tt]{.monospace}. Finally, the Kaplan-Meir
estimate of the survivor function for each risk group is readily
obtained using

<figure id="comp17_68" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_68.jpg" />
</figure>

The mean survivor functions can then be superimposed on a plot of the
Kaplan-Meier estimates for each group, obtained from the
[survfit]{.monospace} object [KMest]{.monospace}, using

<figure id="comp17_69" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_69.jpg" />
</figure>

This gives a plot similar to [Figure
7.5](#chapter7.xhtml_fig7_5){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig7_5}, but using
additional options in the [plot]{.monospace} function to control the
layout would lead to a more pleasing graph.
:::
:::::

::::: section
## 17.9 Time-dependent variables {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_9 .head1}

Variables whose values change over time can be incorporated in a Cox
regression model by expressing the data in counting process format. This
data format was outlined in [Section 8.3](#chapter8.xhtml_sec8_3) of
[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p467 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 467. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}[Chapter 8](#chapter8.xhtml), and described in
greater detail in [Section 13.1](#chapter13.xhtml_sec13_1) of [Chapter
13](#chapter13.xhtml). A certain amount of data manipulation is often
required to achieve this, and care is needed to avoid coding errors.

The R function [tmerge]{.monospace} in the [survival]{.monospace}
package can be used to express data sets in counting process format. The
function is described in an R vignette on time-dependent variables
([Therneau, Crowson and Atkinson,
2022)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib377){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. In
summary, the function is used to specify a base data set to which
time-dependent variables are added. Time-dependent variables are then
defined in a subsequent call of the function, and merged with the base
data set. The process is best illustrated in an example, and the data on
bone marrow transplantation in the treatment of leukaemia will be used
for this. This data set was described in [Example
8.2](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam8_2} of [Chapter
8](#chapter8.xhtml), and the data were presented in [Table
8.2](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl8_2}. The data set
contains missing values in the variable *Ptime*, denoted by a
'$\, \cdot \,$ ', when a patient dies before the platelet level has
returned to normal. Consequently, the option
[na.strings=c(".")]{.monospace} is appended to the [read]{.monospace}
statement to create [NA]{.monospace} for any missing value. The variable
*Precovery* is not needed here and so is dropped from the data frame. A
factor associated with the three disease groups is also defined and the
original variable for disease group is dropped, using the following
code.

<figure id="comp17_70" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_70.jpg" />
</figure>

The [tmerge]{.monospace} function is then used to express the data in
counting process format using

<figure id="comp17_71" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_71.jpg" />
</figure>

This results in three new variables being added to the base data frame,
[leukaemia]{.monospace}. The variables [tstart]{.monospace} and
[tstop]{.monospace} give the start and stop times for each interval,
which here are just zero, and the values in the variable *Time*,
respectively. The option [id=]{.monospace} specifies the identifier to
be used in merging data sets created with [tmerge]{.monospace} and the
option [death=event(time,status)]{.monospace} specifies that the new
variable [death]{.monospace} is to denote the status of an individual at
the end of each time interval from [tstart]{.monospace} to
[tstop]{.monospace}. The first five lines of the data frame
[tdvdata]{.monospace}, which has 23 rows, are as follows.

<figure id="comp17_72" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_72.jpg" />
</figure>

Next, a second call of the [tmerge]{.monospace} function is used to
specify that *Ptime* is the variable denoting time to platelet recovery.
A time-varying platelet level indicator derived from this variable is
then merged with the previously created data frame
[tdvdata]{.monospace}. The [tdc=]{.monospace} option specifies that
[Plate]{.monospace} is to be the new time-dependent variable, and adds
this to the data from the first call of [tmerge]{.monospace}. The code
to do this follows.

<figure id="comp17_73" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_73.jpg" />
</figure>

The resulting data frame, [tdvdata]{.monospace}, now has two rows for
each patient, except for the two whose platelets did not recover to
normal levels. The data are now in counting process format, and the
first six lines are as follows.

<figure id="comp17_74" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_74.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p468 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 468. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this format, the first patient has a platelet
level indicator of zero until day 29, when their platelet level has
recovered and the platelet level indicator becomes unity, and so on.

A Cox regression model containing the new time-dependent variable can
now be fitted. To do this, the [Surv]{.monospace} object in the
[coxph]{.monospace} function is used to specify the start and stop
times, and the status of the patient at the end of each time interval. A
model containing the factor associated with disease group and the
time-dependent variable is fitted using the following function call.

<figure id="comp17_75" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_75.jpg" />
</figure>

Part of the resulting output, shown below, is very similar to that
described earlier in [Section 17.4](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4).

<figure id="comp17_76" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_76.jpg" />
</figure>

From this output, the coefficient of the time-dependent variable is
$- 2.3196$. The three levels of the factor [fgroup]{.monospace}
correspond to ALL, low risk AML and high risk AML, respectively, and the
effect due to the first level, [fgroup1]{.monospace}, is set to zero.
The hazard ratio for an ALL patient relative to a low risk AML patient,
at a given time, is then $\exp(0 + 2.0761) = 7.97$ and that for a high
risk AML patient relative to a low risk one, also at a given time, is
$\exp(0.3894 + 2.0761) = 11.77$, as found in [Example
8.2](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_2).

As a further illustration of the use of the function
[tmerge]{.monospace}, R code is given for the analysis of the data of
[Example 8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_4){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam8_4} from
a hypothetical study on the treatment of liver cirrhosis. The first step
is to combine the basic data in [Table
8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_4){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl8_4} with the data
from [Table 8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_5){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl8_5}
that gives the time-varying log(bilirubin) values. The two data sets are
first input using

<figure id="comp17_77" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_77.jpg" />
</figure>

The time-dependent variable to be created needs to include the
log(bilirubin) value at time zero, that is the baseline value from
[Table 8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_4). This can be done by constructing a
data set containing the patient number, a time of zero and baseline
log(bilirubin) value alone, combining this with the data from [Table
8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_tbl8_5), and ensuring that the combined data set is
in patient and follow-up time order. Code to achieve this follows.

<figure id="comp17_78" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_78.jpg" />
</figure>

The data frame [lbrdata]{.monospace} is now merged with the baseline
data to produce a data set that is in counting process format. This can
be done using the following two calls of the [tmerge]{.monospace}
function.

<figure id="comp17_79" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_79.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p469 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 469. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Patients 5 and 8 have log(bilirubin) values
recorded on the same day as their death. When using a time-dependent
variable, values recorded at a given time apply immediately after that
time, and as a result the new values of log(bilirubin) recorded on day
341 for patient 5 and day 182 for patient 8 will not feature in the
analysis. The merged data frame then has 52 observations, with the
baseline values of the log(bilirubin) level in the variable
[lbr]{.monospace} and the time-dependent values in [lbrt]{.monospace}.
On fitting a Cox regression model that includes treatment and the
time-dependent log(bilirubin) values, the following output is obtained.

<figure id="comp17_80" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_80.jpg" />
</figure>

The estimated coefficients and the value of $- 2\log\hat{L}$ for the
fitted model agree with the values quoted in [Example
8.4](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_4) of [Chapter 8](#chapter8.xhtml).

Models that do not contain the time-dependent variable can be fitted to
the expanded data set in counting process format, [lbrdata]{.monospace},
or to the 12 observations in the data frame [liverbase]{.monospace}. The
same results will be obtained.

::: section
### 17.9.1 Time-varying coefficients {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_9_1 .head2}

Models with time-varying coefficients have been described in [Section
4.4.4](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_4) of [Chapter 4](#chapter4.xhtml) in
connection with testing the assumption of proportional hazards, and in
[Section 11.2](#chapter11.xhtml_sec11_2) on modelling a treatment effect
by a step function. Such models can also be fitted using the function
[coxph]{.monospace}.

In [Section 4.4.4](#chapter4.xhtml_sec4_4_4) of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml), a Cox regression model with a coefficient that was
a function of time was used in connection with assessing proportional
hazards. The *time transform facility* of the [coxph]{.monospace}
function can be used to include the term $\beta(t) = \beta t$ in a
model. To do this, a function [tt]{.monospace} is defined within
[coxph]{.monospace} to form the product of a variable with some function
of time, using [tt=function(x,t,...)x\*$g(t)$]{.monospace}, where $g($
[t$)$]{.monospace} is a specified function of [t]{.monospace}, such as
[t]{.monospace}, [log(t)]{.monospace}, or even a spline function.

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p470 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 470. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}To illustrate this, we return to the data on the
incidence of infection in dialysis patients, given in [Example
4.1](#chapter4.xhtml_exam4_1){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam4_1} of [Chapter
4](#chapter4.xhtml). In [Example
4.12](#chapter4.xhtml_exam4_12){#chapter17.xhtml_b4exam4_12}, a Cox
regression model containing the variables *Age* and *Sex* was augmented
by time-dependent coefficients for both these variables to examine
whether age and gender effects were constant over time. After inputting
the data to a R data frame named [dialysis]{.monospace}, the following
code may be used to fit a Cox regression model that includes *Age* and
*Sex*, together with a time-varying coefficient of *Sex*.

<figure id="comp17_81" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_81.jpg" />
</figure>

Part of the output from using [summary(tdcfit)]{.monospace} follows.

<figure id="comp17_82" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_82.jpg" />
</figure>

Using [-2\*logLik(tdcfit)]{.monospace} gives 34.104 for the value of
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ for this model, and when the term [tt(sex)]{.monospace}
is omitted, $- 2\log\hat{L}$ increases by 0.364 to 34.468, as in
[Example 4.12](#chapter4.xhtml_exam4_12). The fitted model has a
time-varying coefficient of *Sex* that is estimated by
$\hat{\beta}(t) = - 1.281 - 0.074t$. This indicates that the gender
effect increases with time, but not significantly so.

A term that has different coefficients over consecutive time intervals
leads to a piecewise Cox model used in modelling non-proportionality
between treatments, described in [Section
11.2.1](#chapter11.xhtml_sec11_2_1) of [Chapter 11](#chapter11.xhtml). A
step function for a time-varying coefficient can be incorporated in a
Cox regression model using the [survSplit]{.monospace} function in the
[survival]{.monospace} package. This function is used to divide the time
scale into the required number of intervals, and the resulting data
frame is used to fit a stratified model.

The process is illustrated using data on the survival times of patients
with gastric cancer, given in [Example
11.2](#chapter11.xhtml_exam11_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam11_2} of
[Chapter 11](#chapter11.xhtml). In that example, the treatment effect
was assumed constant in the time intervals 1--360, 361--720, 721--1080
and beyond 1080 days, but allowed to vary between them. The data are
first input to form the R data frame [gcancer]{.monospace} using

<figure id="comp17_83" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_83.jpg" />
</figure>

In the resulting data frame, the treatment effect is represented by the
variable [treat]{.monospace}, which is zero for chemotherapy alone and
unity for the combined treatment. The time period is then stratified
using the [survSplit]{.monospace} function with

<figure id="comp17_84" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_84.jpg" />
</figure>

This coding leads to the [survSplit]{.monospace} object
[gcancer1]{.monospace}, with the time period cut at 360, 720 and 1080
days. The option [episode=]{.monospace} specifies the variable name to
be used for labelling the time period in the resulting data frame. The
data for patients 32, 33 and 34 in rows $50 - 58$ of the original data
frame are as follows.

<figure id="comp17_85" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_85.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p471 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 471. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The data are in counting process format with the
time periods and status defined using the new variable
[tstart]{.monospace} and the original variables [time]{.monospace} and
[status]{.monospace}. The time-varying treatment effect can be modelled
by stratifying by period, and including a treatment by period
interaction. The interaction between treatment and the stratifying
variable is represented as [treat:strata(tperiod)]{.monospace} in the
model formula, and a Cox regression model containing [treat]{.monospace}
and the interaction term is fitted using

<figure id="comp17_86" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_86.jpg" />
</figure>

Part of the output is given below.

<figure id="comp17_87" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_87.jpg" />
</figure>

From this output, the log-hazard ratios for the combined treatment
relative to chemotherapy alone in the four time intervals are 0.877,
$0.877 - 1.129 = - 0.25$, $0.877 - 1.978 = - 1.10$ and
$0.877 - 2.060 = - 1.18$, respectively. Also, [anova(gcfit)]{.monospace}
leads to a test of whether the treatment effect is constant over the
four periods. This model is overparameterised, which is why no term is
given for [treat:strata(tperiod)tperiod=1]{.monospace}. The results are
more easily interpreted by just using

<figure id="comp17_88" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_88.jpg" />
</figure>

The interaction term [treat:strata(tperiod)]{.monospace} now leads to a
separate treatment effect in each period, and part of the output is
shown below.

<figure id="comp17_89" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_89.jpg" />
</figure>

The hazard ratios and interval estimates can be read directly from this
output and agree with those presented in [Table
11.3](#chapter11.xhtml_tbl11_3){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl11_3}.
:::

::: section
### 17.9.2 Joint modelling of longitudinal and survival data {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_9_2 .head2}

Joint models for longitudinal data and survival times can be fitted
using the package [JM]{.monospace}, but others are available, such as
the [joineR]{.monospace} package. Initially, the longitudinal variable
is modelled using a linear mixed model, using the function
[lme]{.monospace} in the [nlme]{.monospace} package. This function
requires the fixed effects and random effects in the model to be
specified. The survival times are then modelled using
[coxph]{.monospace} to produce a survival object. The function
[jointModel]{.monospace} is then used to combine these separate
longitudinal and survival models to give a joint model.

The process will be illustrated using data on patient survival following
aortic valve replacement, given in [Example
8.5](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_5){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam8_5} of [Chapter
8](#chapter8.xhtml). This data set includes patient survival times and
longitudinal values of a variable associated with the left ventricular
mass index, LVMI, as well as explanatory variables measured at the
outset of the study. The data are first input to a data frame
[valve]{.monospace} using

<figure id="comp17_90" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_90.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p472 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 472. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The longitudinal variable will be modelled using a
straight line relationship with time that has a random intercept and
slope for each patient. Variables representing the sex and preoperative
diabetic status of a patient will also be included, as these were shown
to be important in [Example
8.8](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_8){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam8_8} of [Chapter
8](#chapter8.xhtml), and the [lme]{.monospace} function to fit this
model is as follows.

<figure id="comp17_91" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_91.jpg" />
</figure>

In this function, the [random=]{.monospace} option specifies that the
model for the random effects is '[$\left. \right.\sim$
time]{.monospace}', and the grouping variable, [id]{.monospace}, is
specified after the '[\|]{.monospace}' symbol.

A data frame with one row per patient is needed to model the survival
times, and to construct this from the data frame [valve]{.monospace},
which contains multiple rows for each patient, the following code is
used.

<figure id="comp17_92" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_92.jpg" />
</figure>

The '[!]{.monospace}' symbol is used to select the rows of the data
frame [valve]{.monospace} for which a value of the variable
[id]{.monospace} is not duplicated. The variables [lvmi]{.monospace} and
[time]{.monospace} are also dropped as they are not needed, using

<figure id="comp17_93" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_93.jpg" />
</figure>

This leads to a data frame [lsurv]{.monospace}, whose first 5 lines are
as follows.

<figure id="comp17_94" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_94.jpg" />
</figure>

In [Example 8.8](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_8), patient age was the only
variable to significantly affect survival. A Cox regression model
containing this variable alone is then fitted in the usual way, but
including the option [x=TRUE]{.monospace} to retain the matrix of values
of the explanatory variables in the model for later use.

<figure id="comp17_95" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_95.jpg" />
</figure>

The function [jointModel]{.monospace} then combines the
[lme]{.monospace} object [lmefit]{.monospace} with the
[coxph]{.monospace} object [coxfit]{.monospace}, and the variable
associated with the longitudinal measurement times is specified using
the [timeVar=]{.monospace} option. At this stage, there are a number of
models that can be used for the survival times in the joint model,
including the Cox regression model, piecewise exponential models, fully
parametric models and flexible models based on splines. A spline model
for the logarithm of the baseline hazard function will be used here, as
in [Example 8.9](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_9){#chapter17.xhtml_b8exam8_9} of
[Chapter 8](#chapter8.xhtml), leading to the following code.

<figure id="comp17_96" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_96.jpg" />
</figure>

The [method=]{.monospace} option specifies that a proportional hazards
spline model with a default of five internal knots is to be used and
that the required numerical integration is to be performed using
Gauss-Hermite quadrature. A different value for the number of internal
knots can be set using the option [lng.in.kn=]{.monospace}. Alternative
proportional hazards models can be specified in a similar manner, using
["Cox-PH-GH"]{.monospace}, ["piecewise-PH-GH"]{.monospace}, or
["weibull-PH-GH"]{.monospace}.

Partial output from [summary(jmod)]{.monospace} follows.

<figure id="comp17_97" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_97.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p473 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 473. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Following the values of the maximised
log-likelihood, $\log\hat{L}$, *AIC* and *BIC* statistics, the estimated
standard deviations of the random intercepts, $u_{0i}$, and slopes,
$u_{1i}$, are given together with the estimated standard deviation of
the residual terms, *σ*~*ϵ*~, on the line labelled
[Residual]{.monospace}. The next column gives the correlation between
the intercept and slope as $- 0.582$.

The coefficients for the longitudinal process are estimates of the
*β*-coefficients in the fitted model for $m_{i}(t)$, the underlying
response profile for the *i*th patient. Here, the fitted model is linear
in time *t*, and includes the sex and diabetic status of the *i*th
patient, *s*~*i*~ and *d*~*i*~, and is given in Equation (8.12), from
which

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{m}}_{i}(t) = 175.09 - 1.41t - 29.64s_{i} - 20.93d_{i},}
\end{array}$$

as in [Example 8.9](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_9) of [Chapter
8](#chapter8.xhtml). The standard errors of the estimated coefficients,
*z*- and *P*-values lead to interval estimates and hypothesis tests.

The estimated hazard function for the joint model is obtained from the
section labelled [Event Process]{.monospace}, where the estimated
coefficient of $m_{i}(t)$ in the joint model is labelled
[Assoct]{.monospace}. As in [Example 8.9](#chapter8.xhtml_exam8_9), of
[Chapter 8](#chapter8.xhtml), this is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{h}}_{i}(t) = \exp\left\{ 0.0857a_{i} + 0.0051{\hat{m}}_{i}(t) \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

The [Event Process]{.monospace} section of this output also contains the
coefficients of the 9 cubic B-spline basis functions needed for the
specification of the spline model for the log-baseline hazard, but these
have been omitted.

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p474 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 474. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Following this, a number of operations can applied
to the [jointModel]{.monospace} object, including [predict]{.monospace}
to obtain the fitted values of the longitudinal variable,
[plot]{.monospace} to obtain various graphical summaries of the model,
and [residuals]{.monospace} to derive various kinds of residual for the
fitted joint model.
:::
:::::

:::::: section
## 17.10 Interval-censored data {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_10 .head1}

As described in [Section 9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_1) of [Chapter
9](#chapter9.xhtml), exact, left- and right-censored event times can all
be expressed in the form of interval-censored data. A right-censored
event time has an interval with an infinite upper limit, a left-censored
time has a zero lower limit, and exact event times have the same lower
and upper interval limits. Interval-censored data with the lower limits
in a variable *L* and upper limits in *R* can be expressed as a survival
object for use in a model formula using
[Surv(L,R,type="interval2")]{.monospace}. An infinite upper limit can be
specified as [Inf]{.monospace} or [NA]{.monospace}.

This section will be illustrated using the data on the time to
recurrence of an ulcer, given in [Example
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_1){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam9_1} of [Chapter
9](#chapter9.xhtml). The first step is to input the data in [Table
9.1](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_1){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl9_1} and perform the
necessary manipulations to express it in interval-censored format,
summarised in [Table
9.2](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl9_2}. The values of
the variables *Treat* and *Dur* are also transformed so that *Treat* is
unity for treatment *B* and zero otherwise, and *Dur* is unity if the
disease duration is at least five years and zero otherwise. A 'baseline'
individual is then one on treatment *A* with a disease duration of less
than five years. This can be accomplished in the following manner.

<figure id="comp17_98" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_98.jpg" />
</figure>

The first ten lines of the data frame [ulcer]{.monospace} are then as
follows.

<figure id="comp17_99" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_99.jpg" />
</figure>

::: section
### 17.10.1 Non-parametric maximum likelihood estimate of the survivor function {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_10_1 .head2}

A non-parametric maximum likelihood estimator of the survivor function
for interval-censored data was described and illustrated in [Section
9.2](#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_2) of [Chapter 9](#chapter9.xhtml). This
estimate can be obtained using the [icfit]{.monospace} function that is
part of the [interval]{.monospace} package with the following code.

<figure id="comp17_100" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_100.jpg" />
</figure>

Using [summary(icsurv)]{.monospace} leads to the following output.

<figure id="comp17_101" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_101.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p475 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 475. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}If desired, an overall survivor function can be
fitted using [icfit]{.monospace} with the [Surv]{.monospace} object
[Surv(left,right,type="interval2") $\left. \right.\sim$ 1]{.monospace}.

The output from the [summary]{.monospace} function gives the survival
probabilities in the Turnbull intervals for each treatment group. From
these, the estimated survivor function for patients on treatment *A*
([treatment=0]{.monospace}) is unity at time zero, $1 - 0.1579 = 0.8421$
for recurrence times between 2 and 6 months,
$1 - 0.1579 - 0.0648 = 0.7773$ at 12 months, and undefined beyond 12
months, as in [Example
9.4](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_4){#chapter17.xhtml_b9exam9_4} of [Chapter
9](#chapter9.xhtml).

The function [getsurv]{.monospace} in the [interval]{.monospace} package
can also be used to obtain estimates of the survivor function at
specified times for each treatment. Since the survivor function is not
uniquely defined within Turnbull intervals, this function uses linear
interpolation to obtain estimates from points on the line linking
survival estimates at the start and end of a Turnbull interval. The
following code captures the estimates for each treatment group at event
times of $0,1,2,\ldots,12$, and forms a matrix of these values in
[surv_ests]{.monospace} for printing and further analysis.

<figure id="comp17_102" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_102.jpg" />
</figure>

This leads to the following output, which can be reconciled with the
survivor function estimates obtained from the survival probabilities in
the output from [summary(icsurv)]{.monospace}.

<figure id="comp17_103" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_103.jpg" />
</figure>

The [icfit]{.monospace} object [icsurv]{.monospace} can also be plotted
directly using [plot(icsurv)]{.monospace}, which leads to a graph
similar to that shown in [Figure
9.2](#chapter9.xhtml_fig9_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig9_2}.

A non-parametric maximum likelihood estimator of the survivor function
for interval-censored data can also be found using the R function
[ic_np]{.monospace} in the [icenReg]{.monospace} package. To
[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p476 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 476. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}use this function, a matrix of values of the left
and right limits of the intervals has to be specified, instead of a
[Surv]{.monospace} object, in the following manner.

<figure id="comp17_104" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_104.jpg" />
</figure>

The output from a [summary]{.monospace} function is minimal, but the
[ic_np]{.monospace} object [icsurv1]{.monospace} includes
[icsurv1\$scurves]{.monospace} that can be displayed using
[print(icsurv1\$scurves)]{.monospace}, and the resulting output includes
estimates of the survivor functions.
:::

::: section
### 17.10.2 Semi-parametric models for interval-censored data {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_10_2 .head2}

The semi-parametric Turnbull model described in [Section
9.3](#chapter9.xhtml_sec9_3) of [Chapter 9](#chapter9.xhtml) can be
fitted to interval-censored data using the [ic_sp]{.monospace} function
in the [icenReg]{.monospace} package. For example, to fit a model
containing the treatment effect alone to the data on ulcer recurrence
times, use

<figure id="comp17_105" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_105.jpg" />
</figure>

The option [bs_samples=]{.monospace} specifies the number of bootstrap
samples to be used in computing bootstrap estimates of the standard
errors of the parameter estimates in the fitted model. This process was
outlined in [Example
9.5](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_5){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam9_5} of [Chapter
9](#chapter9.xhtml). The output can be obtained using
[summary(icfit)]{.monospace} and gives

<figure id="comp17_106" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_106.jpg" />
</figure>

From this output, the estimated log-hazard ratio for a patient on
treatment *B*, relative to one on treatment *A* is 0.195, with a
standard error of 1.488. This standard error has been obtained using
bootstrap simulation, and so is not exactly reproducible. The value of
the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic is found from [final llk]{.monospace} as
$- 2( - 31.442) = 62.884$, as in [Example 9.5](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_5).

The [getSCurves]{.monospace} function can be used to obtain survivor
function estimates for each treatment group using

<figure id="comp17_107" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_107.jpg" />
</figure>

which leads to the following abbreviated output.

<figure id="comp17_108" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_108.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p477 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 477. "
