role="doc-pagebreak"}The Turnbull intervals and survivor function
estimates correspond to those given in [Table
9.4](#chapter9.xhtml_tbl9_4){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl9_4} of [Chapter
9](#chapter9.xhtml). Note that the default setting of the
[getSCurves]{.monospace} function is to produce an estimate of the
survivor function at the average of the values of variables in a fitted
model. Therefore, [getSCurves(icfit)]{.monospace} gives a survivor
function for [treatment $= 0.5881$]{.monospace} since 0.5881 is the
sample mean of this variable over the 43 patients, which is not
meaningful.
:::

::: section
### 17.10.3 Parametric models for interval-censored data {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_10_3 .head2}

Parametric models can be fitted to interval-censored data using the
[survreg]{.monospace} function in the [survival]{.monospace} package.
The [Surv]{.monospace} object used in this function is the same as that
used in other functions for interval-censored data, and the output is
similar to that described in [Section 17.6](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_6) on
fitting parametric models using [survreg]{.monospace}. The only feature
to be wary of is that a left-censored time of zero has to be replaced by
[NA]{.monospace}. For example, to fit a Weibull model to the
interval-censored data on ulcer recurrence times, use

<figure id="comp17_109" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_109.jpg" />
</figure>

The double equal sign in the [ifelse]{.monospace} statement is needed as
a logical expression is being evaluated to give a result of either true
or false. See [Section 17.6](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_6) for details on
interpreting this output. The results agree with those given in [Example
9.7](#chapter9.xhtml_exam9_7){#chapter17.xhtml_b9exam9_7} of [Chapter
9](#chapter9.xhtml).
:::
::::::

:::::::: section
## 17.11 Frailty modelling {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_11 .head1}

Models for survival times that include individual random effects for
individuals, or random effects that are shared by individuals within a
group, are known as frailty models. Such models may be parametric or
semi-parametric and are described in [Chapter 10](#chapter10.xhtml).

::: section
### 17.11.1 Fitting parametric frailty models with individual frailty {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_11_1 .head2}

A proportional hazards model for the hazard of an event at time *t* that
includes a random effect *u*~*i*~ for the *i*th of *n* individuals is
such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i} + u_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$

where $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ is a linear
combination of values of explanatory variables,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$, with coefficients $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$,
and $h_{0}(t)$ is the baseline hazard function. This model can also be
written

$$\begin{array}{r}
{h_{i}(t) = z_{i}\exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})h_{0}(t),}
\end{array}$$[(17.2)]{.equationnum}

where $z_{i} = \exp(u_{i})$ is termed a frailty. This distinction
between a random effect, *u*~*i*~, and the corresponding frailty term,
*z*~*i*~, will be preserved in the sequel, although some R packages use
the term frailty to mean either *u*~*i*~ or *z*~*i*~.

Parametric frailty models can be fitted using the R function
[parfm]{.monospace} in the [parfm]{.monospace} package. This function
can be used in modelling data assumed to have an exponential, Weibull,
log-logistic or lognormal distribution, amongst others, with frailty
distributions that include the gamma and lognormal.

The baseline distributions in the [parfm]{.monospace} function are
parameterised in a different manner to that used by the
[survreg]{.monospace} function. In particular, the Weibull baseline
hazard is expressed as $\lambda\rho t^{\rho - 1}$, so that *ρ* replaces
*γ* in Equation (5.4) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), and the
exponential baseline []{#chapter17.xhtml_p478 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 478. " role="doc-pagebreak"}hazard has $\rho = 1$. The
log-logistic baseline hazard is parameterised as in Equation (5.8) of
[Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), but using *α* instead of *θ*, and the
lognormal distribution is specified as in [Section
5.1.4](#chapter5.xhtml_sec5_1_4) of [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml). The
frailty distributions are parameterised with *θ* being the variance of
the frailty terms, *z*~*i*~, in a model for the *i*th individual.
Therefore, *θ* in the gamma frailty distribution, described in [Section
10.2](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_2), is replaced by $\theta^{- 1}$, and the
lognormal frailty in [Section 10.2.1](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_2_1) of
[Chapter 10](#chapter10.xhtml) has variance
$\,\text{var}\,(z_{i}) = \theta$, so that the frailty effects *u*~*i*~
have variance $e^{\theta}(e^{\theta} - 1)$. Full details of the range of
models and the parameterisations used in [parfm]{.monospace} are given
in the R vignette associated with the package ([Munda, Rotolo and
Legrand, 2017)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib270){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib270
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

To illustrate the use of the [parfm]{.monospace} function, data from
[Example 10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_3){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam10_3}
on the survival of patients awaiting a lung transplant will be used. The
data are first input to a R data frame and gender is declared as a
factor with levels corresponding to male and female patients, while
disease is a factor with four levels, corresponding to COPD, fibrosis,
suppurative disease and other diseases. The baseline level is also set
to be for a male patient with suppurative disease.

<figure id="comp17_110" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_110.jpg" />
</figure>

The syntax for the function [parfm]{.monospace} is similar to that for
other R functions used in modelling survival data, in that a model
formula is expressed using a [Surv]{.monospace} object. In addition, the
[dist=]{.monospace} option is used to specify the parametric
distribution of the survival times, the [cluster=]{.monospace} option
specifies the term to be used for the frailty, which is regarded as a
factor, and the [frailty=]{.monospace} option is used to describe the
frailty distribution. To fit a Weibull model with gamma frailty that has
individual frailty values for each of the 196 patients, the following
code is used.

<figure id="comp17_111" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_111.jpg" />
</figure>

Unfortunately, the model fitting procedure fails to converge, as the
estimate of the Weiubull scale parameter becomes very small. This issue
can be resolved by working with a different timescale for the survival
times. Here, after converting the survival times from days to months by
division by 30.44, the model fitting process converges. With this
rescaling, the original Weibull baseline hazard becomes
$30.44h_{0}(t/30.44)$, that is $30\lambda\rho{(t/30.44)}^{\rho - 1}$,
and so the scale parameter estimated from fitting a model to the time in
months is $\lambda^{*} = 30.44^{\rho}\lambda$. Consequently, the Weibull
scale parameter, on the original scale, is
$\lambda = \lambda^{*}/30.44^{\rho}$. Values of the log-likelihood
statistic $- 2\log\hat{L}$ will also differ, but differences in their
values for alternative models will be preserved by any rescaling of
survival times.

To change the timescale from days to months and fit a Weibull model with
gamma frailty, the following code is used.

<figure id="comp17_112" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_112.jpg" />
</figure>

and [print(f_lung)]{.monospace} leads to the following output.

<figure id="comp17_113" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_113.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p479 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 479. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}From this output, the $- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic
used when comparing nested models is $- 2( - 591.709) = 1183.418$, which
can also be found as [-2\*logLik(f_lung)]{.monospace}. Also,
[theta]{.monospace} refers to the frailty variance, that is the
estimated variance of the *z*~*i*~ in Equation (17.2), and
[rho]{.monospace} and [lambda]{.monospace} refer to the shape and scale
parameters in the Weibull baseline hazard. Note that the value
$\hat{\lambda}$ in [Table
10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_3){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl10_3} of [Chapter
10](#chapter10.xhtml) is found as $0.023/(30.44^{1.304}) = 0.00027$.
These estimates are followed by the estimated coefficients of the terms
in the model, which agree with the entries in the fourth column of
[Table 10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_3). However, the value of
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ differs because of the rescaling of the survival time.
Estimated frailty effects can be obtained using the
[predict]{.monospace} function, so that [predict(f_lung)]{.monospace}
gives the 196 individual estimates of the gamma frailties,
${\hat{z}}_{i}$. By changing the [frailty=]{.monospace} option to
[frailty="lognormal"]{.monospace}, the entries in the third column of
[Table 10.3](#chapter10.xhtml_tbl10_3) are found.
:::

::: section
### 17.11.2 Fitting parametric frailty models with shared frailty {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_11_2 .head2}

The function [parfm]{.monospace} can also be used to fit parametric
models with shared frailty. The coding is very similar to that described
for individual frailty, except that the [cluster=]{.monospace} option
now specifies the variable associated with the groups of individuals
that share a common frailty.

As an illustration, consider the data from [Example
10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_4){#chapter17.xhtml_b10exam10_4} on
survival following kidney transplantation. Here, a kidney donor may
provide for one or two transplants, so that two individuals may share a
common donor, leading to shared frailty. The following code is used to
input the data, transform the time scale and fit a Weibull model with
gamma frailty.

<figure id="comp17_114" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_114.jpg" />
</figure>

Using [print(f_kidney)]{.monospace} leads to the following output.

<figure id="comp17_115" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_115.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p480 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 480. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}This output is interpreted in the same way as that
from fitting an individual frailty model. However, the log-likelihood
values again differ from those quoted in [Example
10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_4) because of the rescaling of the
survival time. Output from fitting models with gamma frailty also
includes Kendall\'s *τ*-statistic, a measure of the association between
pairs of survival times, referred to in [Section
10.8](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_8) of [Chapter 10](#chapter10.xhtml).
Similar code can be used to fit other combinations of parametric and
frailty distributions.
:::

::: section
### 17.11.3 Fitting semi-parametric models with individual lognormal frailty {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_11_3 .head2}

Models with a lognormal frailty term can be fitted to survival data
using the [coxme]{.monospace} function in the package of the same name.
This function uses the penalised partial likelihood method to fit models
that contain frailty effects, described in [Section
10.5](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5) of [Chapter 10](#chapter10.xhtml).

Consider again the data on survival times while awaiting a lung
transplant. The data frame [lung]{.monospace}, used in [Section
17.11.1](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_11_1), contains the data with gender and
disease set as factors. Survival time in days will be used in this
analysis. To fit a Cox regression model containing the terms *Age,
Gender, BMI* and *Disease*, together with an individual frailty,
*z*~*i*~, for the patient effect that is assumed to have a lognormal
distribution, we use

<figure id="comp17_116" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_116.jpg" />
</figure>

In this code, the frailty term is specified by adding
[(1\|patient)]{.monospace} to the model formula, and the frailty
variable is automatically assumed to be a factor. Using
[summary(lnfcox)]{.monospace} leads to the following output.

<figure id="comp17_117" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_117.jpg" />
</figure>

The first part of this output gives three log-likelihood values,
referred to as [NULL]{.monospace}, [Integrated]{.monospace} and
[Fitted]{.monospace}. The null log-likelihood is that for a model
containing no explanatory variables or frailty. This is just the
maximised partial log-likelihood for a Cox regression model that has the
same baseline hazard function, $h_{0}(t)$, for each individual.
[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p481 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 481. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The integrated log-likelihood is the maximised
marginal log-likelihood,
$\log L_{m}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})$,
given in Equation (10.28) of [Chapter 10](#chapter10.xhtml). The fitted
log-likelihood is the maximised partial log-likelihood for the fitted
model, $\log L_{p}(\mathbf{\hat{\beta}},\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}})$,
obtained from Equation (10.25). Of these three log-likelihood values,
the integrated log-likelihood is the most useful, as it is used in
comparing alternative models.

The line [Integrated loglik]{.monospace} in the next part of the output
gives chi-squared values for comparing the integrated log-likelihood
with that for the null model. That is, $2(587.616 - 578.74) = 17.75$.
The line labelled [Penalized loglik]{.monospace} compares the fitted or
maximised partial log-likelihood with that for the null model, so that
$2(587.616 - 529.304) = 116.62$. The number of degrees of freedom refers
to the effective number of independent parameters in the fitted model.
The maximised partial log-likelihood includes estimates of the random
effects, $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}}$, whereas they have been integrated
out of the marginal log-likelihood function. This explains the
difference in the effective numbers of degrees of freedom associated
with the two tests. The *AIC* and *BIC* statistics in the columns
labelled [AIC]{.monospace} and [BIC]{.monospace} values are calculated
from the [Chisq]{.monospace} value by subtracting either twice the
number of degrees of freedom, or $q\log d$, where *q* is the degrees of
freedom and *d* the number of events, respectively. These statistics
have limited use when interest centres on comparing alternative models.

The next part of the output gives the parameter estimates, hazard
ratios, standard errors, *z* and *P*-values to test the hypotheses that
the coefficients are zero. This is followed by estimates of the standard
deviation and variance of the random effect terms in the model, that is
$\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{i})$.

Once a frailty model has been fitted, a [coxme]{.monospace} object can
be used to extract the values of additional quantities. For example,
[lnfcox\$frail]{.monospace} gives the individual frailty estimates,
${\hat{u}}_{i}$, that can be used to identify groups of more frail
individuals, as described in [Section 10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_4).
However, when [lnfcox\$loglik]{.monospace} is used to produce maximised
log-likelihoods, the following output is obtained.

<figure id="comp17_118" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_118.jpg" />
</figure>

Here the [Penalized log-likelihood]{.monospace} is the maximised value
of the penalised partial log-likelihood for the fitted model, that is
$\log{\hat{L}}_{pen}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}},{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})$
from Equation (10.27). This is found by subtracting the penalty term,
${(2{\hat{\sigma}}_{u}^{2})}^{- 1}{\sum\limits_{i}{\hat{u}}_{i}^{2}}$,
from the maximised partial log-likelihood,
$\log L_{p}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}})$.
The value of this penalty function can be found from
[lnfcox\$penalty]{.monospace} and is 22.8272, so that the maximised
penalised log-likelihood is $- 529.3037 - 22.8272 = - 552.1309$, as
given.
:::

::: section
### 17.11.4 Fitting semi-parametric models with individual gamma frailty {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_11_4 .head2}

Models with gamma frailty distributions cannot be fitted using
[coxme]{.monospace}, but the [frailty]{.monospace} function in the
[survival]{.monospace} package can be used with [coxph]{.monospace} to
incorporate gamma frailty effects. R code to fit a Cox regression model
with gamma frailty to the data on survival times of patients registered
for a lung transplant is given below.

<figure id="comp17_119" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_119.jpg" />
</figure>

Here, the frailty term is specified by the addition of a
[frailty]{.monospace} function to the model formula and
[summary(gfcox)]{.monospace} leads to the following output.

<figure id="comp17_120" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_120.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p482 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 482. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this output, the estimated coefficients of the
terms in the model are given together with their standard errors, a
chi-squared statistic calculated as
$\left\{ \left. (\hat{\beta}/\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\beta}) \right\}^{2} \right.$,
and the corresponding *P*-value. The quantity labelled [se2]{.monospace}
is a robust estimate of the standard error that accounts for uncertainty
in the specification of the model. The exponentiated coefficients, that
is the hazard ratios, are also given together with 95% interval
estimates.

The line labelled [frailty.gamma(patient)]{.monospace} gives a
chi-squared test statistic for the hypothesis of no frailty. In outline,
this is based on a Wald test statistic, calculated as
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}^{- 1}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}})\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}}$,
where $\mathbf{\mathbf{V}}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}})$ is the
variance-covariance matrix of the frailty estimates in the vector
$\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}}$, obtained from the matrix of second
derivatives of the penalised partial log-likelihood, as described in
[Appendix A](#appA.xhtml_appA).

After information on the number of iterations needed to fit the model,
the estimated variance of the random effect is given, that is
$\hat{\,\text{var}\,}(u_{i})$. On the same line is the integrated or
marginal log-likelihood for the fitted model, labelled
[I-likelihood]{.monospace}. When multiplied by $- 2$, this is the
$- 2\log\hat{L}$ statistic used in comparing alternative models with
gamma frailty. The value of the likelihood function includes the
additional term $\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}\delta_{i}$, referred to at the
end of [Section 10.5](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5) of [Chapter
10](#chapter10.xhtml), so that models with frailty can more easily be
compared to those without. The effective number of degrees of freedom
for each term in the model, including the frailty effect, is followed by
an estimate of concordance, measured by the *c*-statistic described in
[Section 3.12.1](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_12_1) of [Chapter
3](#chapter3.xhtml). The statistic labelled [Likelihood ratio
test]{.monospace} compares the fitted model with a null model, where the
likelihood for the fitted model is the maximised partial log-likelihood
at the estimates of the *β*s and the frailty effects *u*~*i*~, but
without a penalty term, that is
$\log L_{p}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}})$ in
the notation of [Section 10.5](#chapter10.xhtml_sec10_5).

The [frailty]{.monospace} function in the form
[frailty.gaussian(patient)]{.monospace} can also be used to add
lognormal frailties to a model. Although the [coxme]{.monospace}
function is generally regarded as being more reliable, the two
procedures often give similar results.
:::

::: section
### 17.11.5 []{#chapter17.xhtml_p483 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 483. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Fitting semi-parametric models with shared frailty {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_11_5 .head2}

The R functions [coxme]{.monospace} and [coxph]{.monospace} with
[frailty.gamma]{.monospace} can be used to model shared lognormal and
gamma frailty, respectively. The coding is very similar to that for
individual frailty described in the previous section and will be
illustrated with the data on survival following a kidney transplant,
used to illustrate parametric shared frailty models. The following code
is used to fit a lognormal frailty for the donor effects using the
function [coxme]{.monospace}

<figure id="comp17_121" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_121.jpg" />
</figure>

and [summary(slnfcox)]{.monospace} leads to the following output.

<figure id="comp17_122" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_122.jpg" />
</figure>

This output is in the same format as for individual frailty models
fitted with [coxme]{.monospace} and is in accord with the results given
in [Example 10.4](#chapter10.xhtml_exam10_4) of [Chapter
10](#chapter10.xhtml).

The function [coxph]{.monospace} with a [frailty.gamma]{.monospace}
function is used to fit a model with shared gamma frailty using

<figure id="comp17_123" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_123.jpg" />
</figure>

The resulting estimates of the coefficients of the explanatory variables
in the model are very similar to those found when using a
semi-parametric model with lognormal frailty.
:::
::::::::

:::::: section
## 17.12 Competing risks {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_12 .head1}

Models for competing risks are used in situations where individuals in a
study may experience one of a number of different end points, and were
described in [Chapter 12](#chapter12.xhtml). In this section, the use of
R to estimate the cumulative incidence function, and to fit models for
cause-specific hazards and cause-specific incidence will be described.
Data on time to graft failure following a liver transplant, described in
[Example 12.1](#chapter12.xhtml_exam12_1){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam12_1}
of [Chapter 12](#chapter12.xhtml), will be used to illustrate the R code
and output. In this data set, the graft failure times of many patients
are censored, and there are four potentially fatal end points, namely
rejection, thrombosis, recurrent disease and other causes. In the data
set, the variable associated with cause of failure is coded 0, 1, 2, 3
and 4, respectively. The data are input to an R data frame and both
[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p484 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 484. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}gender and liver disease are set as factors, with
female gender and alcoholic liver disease as the reference levels, using

<figure id="comp17_124" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_124.jpg" />
</figure>

::: section
### 17.12.1 Estimating and modelling cause-specific hazard functions {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_12_1 .head2}

Competing risks data can be summarised using the survivor function for
each event type in the manner described in [Section
12.2](#chapter12.xhtml_sec12_2) of [Chapter 12](#chapter12.xhtml). This
involves using the event times of each cause of failure in turn,
treating the times of all other events as censored, to give
cause-specific data. This can be done by creating a new variable
indicating that the time to a given cause of liver failure is an event,
while times to all other causes are censored. For example, to define a
variable that is unity if the cause of death is thrombosis, for which
the variable [cof]{.monospace} in the [liver]{.monospace} data frame is
equal to 2, and zero otherwise, use

<figure id="comp17_125" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_125.jpg" />
</figure>

The variable [status2]{.monospace} can then be used in the
[survfit]{.monospace} function to obtain estimated survivor functions.
For example, Kaplan-Meier estimates of the survivor function for each
liver disease, when rejection is the cause of graft failure, are found
using

<figure id="comp17_126" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_126.jpg" />
</figure>

Subsequently, Cox regression models for the hazard of each event type
can be fitted using the [coxph]{.monospace} function, as described in
[Section 17.4](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_4). For example, to obtain the
cause specific hazard ratios in [Table
12.5](#chapter12.xhtml_tbl12_5){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl12_5} of [Chapter
12](#chapter12.xhtml), after fitting a Cox regression model with graft
failure caused by rejection as the event, use

<figure id="comp17_127" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_127.jpg" />
</figure>

This leads to the hazard ratios for Thrombosis shown in [Table
12.5](#chapter12.xhtml_tbl12_5). Other causes of graft failure can be
handled in a similar manner.
:::

::: section
### 17.12.2 Estimating the cumulative incidence function {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_12_2 .head2}

The function [cuminc]{.monospace} in the package [cmprsk]{.monospace}
can be used to estimate the cause-specific cumulative incidence
function, where the options [ftime=]{.monospace} and
[fstatus=]{.monospace} are used to specify the variables containing the
survival times and status indicator. For example, to obtain the overall
cumulative incidence of thrombosis, the following function call is used.

<figure id="comp17_128" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_128.jpg" />
</figure>

This can be extended to provide cumulative incidence functions for each
indication for transplant, using the [group=]{.monospace} option to
specify the grouping variable, as in

<figure id="comp17_129" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_129.jpg" />
</figure>

Following this, the cumulative incidence of alcoholic liver disease, for
example, and the corresponding time points, can be obtained from the
variables [cuminc2\$"ALD 1"\$est]{.monospace} and [cuminc2\$"ALD
1"\$time]{.monospace}. These can be assigned to variable names for use
in further analysis []{#chapter17.xhtml_p485 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 485. " role="doc-pagebreak"}or graphical summaries.
For example, a plot of the cumulative incidence functions can be
obtained using [plot(cuminc2)]{.monospace} which gives a graph similar
to that shown in [Figure
12.2](#chapter12.xhtml_fig12_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17fig12_2}.
:::

::: section
### 17.12.3 The Fine and Gray model for cumulative incidence {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_12_3 .head2}

The Fine and Gray model, described in [Section
12.5](#chapter12.xhtml_sec12_5) of [Chapter 12](#chapter12.xhtml) is
used to model the cumulative incidence of an event in the presence of
competing risks. The model can be fitted using the function
[crr]{.monospace} in the [cmprsk]{.monospace} package. This function
does not allow model formulae to be specified, and instead, a matrix of
the values of the explanatory variables to be included in a model must
be provided. For factors, indicator variables need to be defined, but
this is helped by the [model.matrix]{.monospace} function. For example,
the function [model.matrix( $\left. \right.\sim$
A+B)\[,-1\]]{.monospace} will generate indicator variables for the two
factors, [A]{.monospace} and [B]{.monospace}. The final
[\[,-1\]]{.monospace} removes a constant term from the output of the
function to avoid overparameterising the indicator variables.

In the case of the data on time to graft failure following a liver
transplant, indicator variables for the factors associated with gender
and liver disease, whose levels are in the columns [fgender]{.monospace}
and [fdisease]{.monospace} of the [liver]{.monospace} data frame, are
generated using

<figure id="comp17_130" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_130.jpg" />
</figure>

The age of the patient is also to be included in the model and so this
must be combined with the indicator variables in [indvars]{.monospace}
to give a matrix [xvars]{.monospace} that contains age and the indicator
variables for gender and disease. The columns of the resulting matrix
are also renamed. This is done using

<figure id="comp17_131" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_131.jpg" />
</figure>

The first 10 lines of the matrix [xvars]{.monospace} are as follows.

<figure id="comp17_132" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_132.jpg" />
</figure>

The indicator variables generated by the [model.matrix]{.monospace}
function are such that [PBC]{.monospace} or [PSC]{.monospace} are unity
if the disease is PBC or PSC, respectively, and zero otherwise, and
[male]{.monospace} is unity for a male patient and zero otherwise.

The [crr]{.monospace} function can now be used to fit a model for the
cumulative incidence of a particular event. The variables containing the
event times and the code for each event type and censored observations
are specified using the options [ftime=]{.monospace} and
[fstatus=]{.monospace}. These are followed by the matrix of explanatory
variables to be included in the model, specified using
[cov1=]{.monospace}. The code for a censored time can be specified using
the [cencode=]{.monospace} option and the [failcode=]{.monospace} option
is used to specify which event type is to be modelled, with all other
events as competing risks. In the [liver]{.monospace} data frame, the
variable [cof]{.monospace} contains the event codes with zero for a
censored time and 2 for thrombosis. Using [failcode=2]{.monospace}
specifies that the incidence of thrombosis is to be modelled. The option
[cencode=0]{.monospace} specifies that the variable [cof]{.monospace} is
zero for a censored time, but this is unnecessary as this is the
default.

To model the dependence of the incidence of thrombosis on a patient\'s
age, gender and primary liver disease, the following function call of
[crr]{.monospace} is used.

<figure id="comp17_133" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_133.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p486 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 486. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Using [summary(cifit2)]{.monospace} leads to the
following output.

<figure id="comp17_134" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_134.jpg" />
</figure>

The output is largely self-explanatory, and gives the estimated values
of the coefficients of the explanatory variables in the model, the
corresponding subhazard ratios, [exp(coef)]{.monospace}, the standard
errors of the coefficients, *z*- and *P*-values. The subhazard ratios
are then repeated and the two columns labelled [2.5%]{.monospace} and
[97.5%]{.monospace} are the 95% confidence limits for them. These
correspond to the values given in the column headed "Thrombosis" in
[Table 12.6](#chapter12.xhtml_tbl12_6){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl12_6}.
These estimates are followed by log-likelihood values on fitting the
model. The [Pseudo Log-likelihood]{.monospace} is the maximised partial
log-likelihood obtained from Equation (12.12) of [Chapter
12](#chapter12.xhtml), and [Pseudo likelihood ratio test]{.monospace}
compares this with a null model, that is a model with no explanatory
variables.

Various quantities can be extracted from the [crr]{.monospace} object
[cifit2]{.monospace} for use in further analysis. Using
[failcode]{.monospace} values of 1, 3 and 4 will enable the incidence of
the other three event types to be modelled.
:::
::::::

::: section
## 17.13 Multiple events and event history modelling {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_13 .head1}

Models for recurrent events, and in particular those due to Anderson and
Gill (AG), and Prentice, Williams and Peterson (PWP), can be fitted
after expressing the relevant data in counting process format, as
illustrated in [Example
13.2](#chapter13.xhtml_exam13_2){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam13_2} of
[Chapter 13](#chapter13.xhtml). This can be straightforwardly
accomplished using the [tmerge]{.monospace} and [survSplit]{.monospace}
functions, described in [Section 17.9](#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_9). In
addition, The [cumtdc=]{.monospace} option in [tmerge]{.monospace} can
be used to enumerate the number of recurrent events for each individual.
This is needed to stratify by recurrence number when fitting the PWP
model using the [coxph]{.monospace} function.

Event history models are fitted by defining a variable associated with
the different transitions, and using this to form the data in counting
process format with [tmerge]{.monospace}. The data manipulation needed
to transform a data set into the required format can be complex, and
depends on the format of the data provided. Because of this, further
details are not given here.
:::

::: section
## 17.14 []{#chapter17.xhtml_p487 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 487. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Dependent censoring {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_14 .head1}

A Cox regression model with dependent censoring, described in [Section
14.3](#chapter14.xhtml_sec14_3), can be fitted in the following manner.
First, the probability of censoring is estimated by fitting a survival
model to the time to censoring. This is done by reversing the usual
event time indicator so that it takes the value unity for a censored
time and zero for an event time. As noted in [Section
14.3](#chapter14.xhtml_sec14_3), it is more convenient to fit a
parametric model, and so the [survreg]{.monospace} function is used to
model the hazard of censoring. Parameter estimates in this model lead to
the linear predictor and scale parameter, which are saved to the data
frame for later use.

The time-to-event data are then expressed in counting process format
using the [survSplit]{.monospace} function. This is facilitated by
creating a variable with the unique event times to use in the
[cuts=]{.monospace} option of the function. From the extended data
frame, estimated values of the survivor function for the time to
censoring are calculated at each interval end point. Their reciprocals
are the weights needed in modelling dependent censoring. Finally, a Cox
regression model is fitted to the counting process data, using the
[coxph]{.monospace} function with the option [weights=]{.monospace} used
to specify the variable associated with the calculated weights.

To illustrate the code required, data on the time to death while
awaiting a liver transplant from [Example
14.1](#chapter14.xhtml_exam14_1){#chapter17.xhtml_b17exam14_1} will be
used to fit a Cox regression model that allows for dependent censoring.
The data are first input to give a data frame [livertx]{.monospace} that
matches [Table
14.1](#chapter14.xhtml_tbl14_1){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl14_1} using

<figure id="comp17_135" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_135.jpg" />
</figure>

The probability of censoring is then modelled by fitting a Weibull
distribution to the censoring times, using the censoring status variable
[cstatus]{.monospace}. Estimates of the linear predictor and scale
parameter are assigned to [lp]{.monospace} and [sigma]{.monospace} and
saved in the [livertx]{.monospace} data frame, using the following code.

<figure id="comp17_136" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_136.jpg" />
</figure>

The next step is to construct a variable [survtime]{.monospace}
containing the unique values of the event times in the data set, and use
this in the [survSplit]{.monospace} function to give a new data frame
[livertx1]{.monospace} in counting process format.

<figure id="comp17_137" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_137.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p488 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 488. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The variable [time]{.monospace} is now the end
point of each time interval. The estimated survivor function at each of
these end points can now be found from the log-linear form of the
parametric model in Equation (17.1). From Equation (14.5) of [Chapter
14](#chapter14.xhtml), the estimated probability of censoring beyond
time *t* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{S}}_{ci}(t) = \exp\left\{ {- \exp\left\lbrack \frac{\log t - {\hat{\eta}}_{ci}}{{\hat{\sigma}}_{c}} \right\rbrack} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

where the linear predictor in the model is
${\hat{\eta}}_{ci} = {\hat{\mu}}_{c} + {\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}}}_{c}'\,\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$,
and ${\hat{\mu}}_{c}$ is the intercept,
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\alpha}}}}_{c}$ is the vector of coefficients of
the explanatory variables, and ${\hat{\sigma}}_{c}$ the scale parameter
in the log-linear form of the Weibull model. Code to obtain values of
the estimated survivor function at the end point of each interval in the
counting process data and to compute the weights follows.

<figure id="comp17_138" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_138.jpg" />
</figure>

The variables [cstatus]{.monospace}, [lp]{.monospace},
[sigma]{.monospace} and [csurv]{.monospace} are no longer needed and can
be dropped from [livertx1]{.monospace} using

<figure id="comp17_139" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_139.jpg" />
</figure>

The first 8 rows of [livertx1]{.monospace} are then as follows.

<figure id="comp17_140" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_140.jpg" />
</figure>

A weighted Cox regression model can now be fitted using

<figure id="comp17_141" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_141.jpg" />
</figure>

and part of the output from [summary(wcoxfit)]{.monospace} follows.

<figure id="comp17_142" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_142.jpg" />
</figure>

The estimated coefficients of the four explanatory variables in this
output, and their robust standard errors, agree with the weighted
estimates in [Table
14.4](#chapter14.xhtml_tbl14_4){#chapter17.xhtml_b17tbl14_4}. The
unweighted version of the model can be fitted using

<figure id="comp17_143" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_143.jpg" />
</figure>

If desired, robust estimates of the standard errors of the coefficients
in this model can be obtained by defining a cluster identifier for each
individual observation and adding [cluster(id)]{.monospace} to the model
formula, as in.

<figure id="comp17_144" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_144.jpg" />
</figure>

The output from this code gives the unweighted estimates in [Table
14.4](#chapter14.xhtml_tbl14_4).
:::

:::::: section
## 17.15 Bayesian survival analysis {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_15 .head1}

The package [rstanarm]{.monospace} enables a wide range of regression
models to be fitted using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods with
the R modelling syntax. This package is in turn an addition to the
package [rstan]{.monospace}, which provides an R interface to
[Stan]{.monospace}, a [C++]{.monospace} library for
[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p489 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 489. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}Bayesian modelling and inference. Bayesian survival
analysis, described in [Chapter 16](#chapter16.xhtml), can be
implemented using the function [stan_surv]{.monospace} in
[rstanarm]{.monospace}, and the function is described in the article of
[Brilleman et al.
(2020)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib46){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib46 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. Data from the HELP study on health evaluation and
linkage to primary care, described in [Example
16.7](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_7){#chapter17.xhtml_b16exam16_7} of
[Chapter 16](#chapter16.xhtml), will be used to show how the function
[stan_surv]{.monospace} can be used in Bayesian survival analysis, and
to illustrate the resulting output. The data are first input to a data
frame named [help]{.monospace} using

<figure id="comp17_145" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_145.jpg" />
</figure>

and after installing the [rstanarm]{.monospace} package, it can be
loaded using [library("rstanarm")]{.monospace}.

::: section
### 17.15.1 Bayesian parametric modelling {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_15_1 .head2}

The [stan_surv]{.monospace} function can be used to fit Bayesian
versions of exponential, Weibull and Gompertz models for the baseline
hazard function, described in [Chapter 5](#chapter5.xhtml), and B-spline
models for the log-baseline hazard, described in [Section
6.3](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_3) of [Chapter 6](#chapter6.xhtml). Prior
distributions need to be specified for the unknown parameters in a
model, as well as information about the MCMC simulation process. A
Bayesian Weibull proportional hazards model that contains the four
variables in the data set, *Age, Gender, Housing* and *Help*, can be
fitted to the data from the HELP study using the following code.

<figure id="comp17_146" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_146.jpg" />
</figure>

The model is specified using a [Surv]{.monospace} object in the usual
way, and a Weibull baseline hazard is specified using
[basehaz="weibull"]{.monospace}. With [stan_surv]{.monospace}, the
Weibull proportional hazards model is parameterised as
$h_{i}(t) = \exp(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{x}}_{i})\lambda\gamma t^{\gamma - 1}$,
with $\log\lambda$ referred to as the intercept and denoted
[(Intercept)]{.monospace} in the output, and *γ* as the shape parameter,
labelled [weibull-shape]{.monospace} in the output. A MCMC sampling
process is initiated using [algorithm=c("sampling")]{.monospace}, but
since this is the default for the [stan_surv]{.monospace} function, this
option has been omitted. Details on the number of chains to be used, the
total number of simulations required, and the number of these to be used
for the burn-in are given using the options [chains=]{.monospace},
[iter=]{.monospace} and [warmup=]{.monospace}, respectively. The
[seed=]{.monospace} option can be used to initialise the pseudo-random
number generator for the simulation process, and enables the random
number sequence to be reproduced.

Next, the prior distributions are specified. A wide range of prior
distributions can be accommodated, including the normal, Cauchy,
exponential and Laplace distributions. These are described in the
vignette of [Brilleman et al. (2020)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib46){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}. In the above code, a normal distribution with
zero mean and variance 10^2^ is specified for the intercept of the
Weibull model, that is $\log\lambda$. The [prior_aux=]{.monospace}
option is used to specify the prior distribution of a shape parameter,
which is non-negative. In this case,
[prior_aux=normal(0,10)]{.monospace} specifies a half-normal
distribution for the Weibull shape parameter *γ*. The
[prior=]{.monospace} option is used to specify prior distributions for
the *β*-coefficients in the linear part of the model, and here they have
all been assigned $N(0,1)$ distributions. This choice of prior
distributions was used in [Example 16.7](#chapter16.xhtml_exam16_7) of
[Chapter 16](#chapter16.xhtml). Different location and scale parameters,
or different distributions, could be used if the *β*-coefficients were
not to have the same prior distribution.

The function call of [stan_surv]{.monospace} leads to the following
output. The functions [summary]{.monospace} and [print]{.monospace} give
different levels of information, and so output from both is described.
Output from [summary(hweib)]{.monospace} follows.

<figure id="comp17_147" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_147.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p490 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 490. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}This output gives the mean and standard deviation
of the coefficients of the explanatory variables, and the Weibull
intercept and shape parameters, over the 10,000 MCMC simulations,
together with the 10%, 50% and 90% percentiles of their distributions.
The contents of the output labelled [MCMC diagnostics]{.monospace} are
helpfully described in the output.

Abbreviated output from using [print(hweib)]{.monospace} follows.

<figure id="comp17_148" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_148.jpg" />
</figure>

This output gives the median values of the estimates from the MCMC runs,
together with the median absolute deviation, labelled
[MAD_SD]{.monospace}. This is a robust measure of the variability in an
estimate, calculated as the median of the absolute differences between
each simulated []{#chapter17.xhtml_p491 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 491. " role="doc-pagebreak"}value and the overall
median. The exponential of the median in the column
[exp(Median)]{.monospace} can be used as an estimate of the hazard ratio
for each variable in the model.

The function [prior_summary]{.monospace} provides a useful way of
obtaining a summary of the priors used in the [stan_surv]{.monospace}
function, and leads to the following output.

<figure id="comp17_149" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_149.jpg" />
</figure>

This confirms the chosen priors for the intercept and shape parameters
of the Weibull model and the coefficients of the variables.
:::

::: section
### 17.15.2 Bayesian semi-parametric modelling {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_15_2 .head2}

The function [stan_surv]{.monospace} can also be used to fit a Bayesian
version of the model for the log-hazard function based on B-splines,
which was described in [Section 6.3](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_3) of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml). In the special case where B-splines of degree zero
are used, the model is equivalent to a piecewise exponential model where
the baseline hazard function is $h_{0}(t) = \exp(\alpha_{j})$ in the
*j*th time interval, $j = 1,2,\ldots,m + 1$, where *m* is the number of
internal knots, that is knots between the shortest and longest event
times. However, the R function [stan_surv]{.monospace} includes an
intercept term, *μ*, so that the baseline hazard actually being fitted
is $h_{0}(t) = \exp(\mu + \alpha_{j})$. Since there are now $m + 2$
unknown parameters in this model, associated with $m + 1$ time
intervals, the model is overparameterised and *α*~1~ is set to zero. The
baseline hazard is then *e*^*μ*^ in the first time interval and
$\exp(\mu + \alpha_{j - 1})$ in the remaining *m* intervals,
$j = 2,3,\ldots,m + 1$. There will then be just *m* B-spline
coefficients for a model with *m* knots.

The required code is quite similar to that for fitting parametric models
to the data from the HELP study, except that the [basehaz=]{.monospace}
option is set to [bs]{.monospace}, which stands for B-spline. To fit a
Bayesian version of a piecewise exponential model, the degree of the
B-spline function and the positions of the knots where the baseline
hazard changes, are expressed using the option
[basehaz_ops=list(degree=0,df=5)]{.monospace}. Here, the option
[df=5]{.monospace} means that there are to be five internal knots
between the shortest (0) and longest (456) event times, and the end
points for the time intervals are chosen to be at equally spaced
percentiles of the event times. This leads to internal knots at 18, 32,
42, 74 and 136 days. The option [df=5]{.monospace} could be replaced by
a list of event times to be used for internal knots, so that
[basehaz_ops=list(degree=0,knots=c(18,32,42,74,136))]{.monospace} would
give similar output. A model with a different baseline hazard for each
interval between event times would correspond closely to the Cox
regression model and could be fitted by specifying the number of knots
to be equal to the number of distinct event times, or by listing the
unique event times in the [knots=]{.monospace} option.

To illustrate Bayesian semi-parametric modelling, the following code is
used to fit a Bayesian piecewise exponential model with 5 internal knots
to the data from the HELP study. All four explanatory variables have
been included in the model. A $N(0,1)$ prior is used for each
coefficient of the explanatory variables, and the prior distribution for
the intercept and each of the B-spline parameters is taken to be
$N(0,10)$. The MCMC simulation process has a burn-in of 1000 followed by
10,000 simulations.

<figure id="comp17_150" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_150.jpg" />
</figure>

[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p492 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 492. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}The medians of the estimates of the parameters, and
corresponding hazard ratios, found using [print(bayespe)]{.monospace},
are as follows.

<figure id="comp17_151" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_151.jpg" />
</figure>

The estimated coefficients of the explanatory variables in the model
obtained as the medians of the MCMC simulations are very similar to
those found on fitting a Bayesian version of the Weibull model. The
intercept, [(Intercept)]{.monospace}, and the coefficients labelled
[b-splines-coef]{.monospace} are estimates of *μ* and the coefficients
*α*~*j*~, $j = 2,3,4,5,6$, of the spline basis functions. The baseline
hazard function is $\exp(\mu)$ in the first interval and
$\exp\left\{ \mu + \alpha_{j} \right\}$ in the five remaining intervals.
These quantities will need to be derived from the individual 10,000 MCMC
simulations, rather than from the medians in this output.

The function [posterior_survfit]{.monospace} can be used to obtain the
posterior predictive survivor and hazard functions, amongst others, for
individuals with given characteristics, from the individual estimates of
the model parameters in the MCMC simulations. Specifically, the baseline
functions can be obtained from

<figure id="comp17_152" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_152.jpg" />
</figure>

and plotted using [plot(basesurv)]{.monospace}, for example.

The *WAIC* statistic for Bayesian model comparison, described in
[Section 16.8](#chapter16.xhtml_sec16_8) of [Chapter
16](#chapter16.xhtml), for a model fitted using the
[stan_surv]{.monospace} function is easily found using the
[waic]{.monospace} function. For example, [waic(bayespe)]{.monospace}
gives

<figure id="comp17_153" class="image1">
<img src="images/comp17_153.jpg" />
</figure>

from which the *WAIC* statistic is 2237.4 for the fitted piecewise
exponential model, and the corresponding estimate of model complexity is
$p_{W} = 9.6$. The row labelled [elpd_waic]{.monospace} is the expected
log-pointwise predictive density, a measure of prediction accuracy, and
multiplying this by $- 2$ gives the *WAIC* statistic.
:::

::: section
### 17.15.3 Flexible models for the hazard function {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_15_3 .head2}

Bayesian versions of flexible cubic B-spline models for the log-hazard
function, described in [Section 6.3](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_3) of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml), can be fitted using similar code. The only change
that is needed is []{#chapter17.xhtml_p493 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 493. " role="doc-pagebreak"}to use
[degree=3]{.monospace} in the [basehaz_ops=]{.monospace} option. Models
with increasing numbers of internal knots (degrees of freedom) can then
be fitted and the most suitable determined using the *WAIC* criterion,
for example.

Restricted cubic spline models for the log-cumulative hazard, described
in [Section 6.4](#chapter6.xhtml_sec6_4), of [Chapter
6](#chapter6.xhtml), are not often used in Bayesian survival analysis,
as there can be difficulties in ensuring that the log-cumulative hazard
is an increasing function of time, leading to difficulties in the MCMC
simulation process.
:::
::::::

::: section
## 17.16 Further reading {#chapter17.xhtml_sec17_16 .head1}

In this chapter, it has only been possible to introduce a small number
of the many R packages for survival analysis, and to illustrate just
some of the many facilities for data manipulation, vector and matrix
algebra. In addition, there has been very limited coverage of R software
for data visualisation and the graphical representation of data.
However, there are many books that provide an introduction to the R
software and its use in statistical analysis. Among them are the texts
of [Crawley (2013)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib84){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib84
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Dalgaard
(2008)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib93){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib93 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Davies
(2016)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib95){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib95 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, [Field, Miles and Field
(2012)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib121){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib121
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Verzani
(2014)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib397){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib397
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wickham
(2019)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib415){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib415
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. There is also an [R for Dummies book
(de Vries and Meys,
2015)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib101){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib101 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and one in the same series on statistical analysis
using R ([Schmuller,
2017](#refbib.xhtml_refbib338){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib338 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}).

[Moore (2016)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib267){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib267
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} describes the principles of survival
analysis, illustrated with data that have been analysed using R.
[Broström (2022)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib49){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib49
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} presents an introduction to survival
and event history analysis, with examples from the social sciences
analysed using the R package [eha]{.monospace}.

All R packages are described on the CRAN website, but for some a longer
form of the documentation, known as a vignette, is available. There are
vignettes for the survival package ([Therneau,
2022)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib377){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"},
modelling with time-dependent variables, ([Therneau, Crowson and
Atkinson, 2022a](#refbib.xhtml_refbib378){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib378
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the [parfm]{.monospace} package
([Munda, Rotolo and Legrand, 2017)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib270){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"} and multi-state models and competing risks
([Therneau, Crowson and Atkinson,
2022b](#refbib.xhtml_refbib379){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib379 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}). See also the earlier paper on
[parfm]{.monospace}, by [Munda, Rotolo and Legrand
(2012)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib269){#chapter17.xhtml_b17refbib269
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The [JM]{.monospace} package for the
joint modelling of longitudinal and time-to-event data is described by
Rizopoulos (2022), and Bayesian survival analysis using the
[rstanarm]{.monospace} package is described by [Brilleman et al.
(2020)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib46){.biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.[]{#chapter17.xhtml_p494 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 494 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}
:::

[]{#appA.xhtml}

::::: {.section .appendix role="doc-appendix"}
# []{#appA.xhtml_p495 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 495. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Appendix A]{.chapnum}[Maximum likelihood estimation]{.chaptitle1} {#appA.xhtml_appA .chaplabel}

This appendix gives a summary of results on maximum likelihood
estimation that are relevant to survival analysis. The results presented
apply equally to inferences based on a partial likelihood function, and
so can be used in conjunction with the Cox regression model introduced
in [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml), and the fully parametric models
described in [Chapters 5](#chapter5.xhtml) and [6](#chapter6.xhtml). A
full treatment of the theory of maximum likelihood estimation and
likelihood ratio testing is given by [Cox and Hinkley
(1974)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib80){#appA.xhtml_brefbib80 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}.

::: section
## A.1 Inference about a single unknown parameter {#appA.xhtml_secA_1 .head1}

Suppose that the likelihood of *n* observed survival times,
$t_{1},t_{2},\ldots$, *t*~*n*~, is a function of a single unknown
parameter *β* and is denoted $L(\beta)$. The *maximum likelihood
estimate* of *β* is then the value $\hat{\beta}$ for which this function
is a maximum. In almost all applications, it is more convenient to work
with the natural logarithm of the likelihood function, $\log L(\beta)$.
The value $\hat{\beta}$, which maximises the log-likelihood, is the same
value that maximises the likelihood function itself and is generally
found using differential calculus.

Specifically, $\hat{\beta}$ is the value of *β* for which the derivative
of $\log L(\beta)$, with respect to *β*, is equal to zero. In other
words, $\hat{\beta}$ is such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\text{d}\log L(\beta)}{\text{d}\beta}\left| {}_{\hat{\beta}}~ \right. = 0.}
\end{array}$$

The first derivative of $\log L(\beta)$ with respect to *β* is known as
the *efficient score* for *β* and is denoted $u(\beta)$. Therefore,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{u(\beta) = \frac{\text{d}\log L(\beta)}{\text{d}\beta},}
\end{array}$$

and so the maximum likelihood estimate of *β*, $\hat{\beta}$, satisfies
the equation

$$\begin{array}{r}
{u(\hat{\beta}) = 0.}
\end{array}$$

The asymptotic variance of the maximum likelihood estimate of *β* can be
found from

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left( {- \,\text{E}\,\left\{ \frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\beta)}{\text{d}\beta^{2}} \right\}} \right)^{- 1},}
\end{array}$$[(A.1)]{.equationnum}

or from the equivalent formula,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\left( {\,\text{E}\,\left\{ \frac{\text{d}\log L(\beta)}{\text{d}\beta} \right\}^{2}} \right)^{- 1}.}
\end{array}$$

The variance calculated from either of these expressions can be regarded
as the approximate variance of $\hat{\beta}$, although it is usually
more straightforward to use Expression (A.1). When the
[]{#appA.xhtml_p496 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 496. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}expected value of the derivative in Expression
(A.1) is difficult to obtain, a further approximation to the variance of
$\hat{\beta}$ is found by evaluating the derivative at $\hat{\beta}$.
The approximate variance of $\hat{\beta}$ is then given by

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\beta}) \approx \left. ~{- \left( \frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\beta)}{\text{d}\beta^{2}} \right)^{- 1}} \right|_{\hat{\beta}}.}
\end{array}$$[(A.2)]{.equationnum}

The second derivative of the log-likelihood function is
[]{#appA.xhtml_p497 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 497. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}sometimes known as the *Hessian*, and the quantity

$$\begin{array}{r}
{- \,\text{E}\,\left\{ \frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\beta)}{\text{d}\beta^{2}} \right\}}
\end{array}$$

is called the *information function*. Since the information function is
formed from the expected value of the second derivative of
$\log L(\beta)$, it is sometimes called the *expected information
function*. In contrast, the negative second derivative of the
log-likelihood function itself is called the *observed information
function*. This latter quantity will be denoted $i(\beta)$, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{i(\beta) = - \left\{ \frac{\text{d}^{2}\log L(\beta)}{\text{d}\beta^{2}} \right\}.}
\end{array}$$

The reciprocal of this function, evaluated at $\hat{\beta}$, is then the
approximate variance of $\hat{\beta}$ given in Equation (A.2), that is,

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\beta}) \approx \frac{1}{i(\hat{\beta})}.}
\end{array}$$

The standard error of $\hat{\beta}$, that is, the square root of the
estimated variance of $\hat{\beta}$, is found from

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\beta}) = \frac{1}{\left. \sqrt{}\left\{ i(\hat{\beta}) \right\} \right.}.}
\end{array}$$

This standard error can be used to construct confidence intervals for
*β*.

In order to test the null hypothesis that $\beta = 0$, three alternative
test statistics can be used. The *likelihood ratio test statistic* is
the difference between the values of $- 2\log L(\hat{\beta})$ and
$- 2\log L(0)$. The *Wald test* is based on the statistic

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\beta}}^{2}i(\hat{\beta}),}
\end{array}$$

and the *score test statistic* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\left\{ u(0) \right\}^{2}}{i(0)}.}
\end{array}$$

Each of these statistics has an asymptotic chi-squared distribution on 1
d.f., under the null hypothesis that $\beta = 0$. Note that the Wald
statistic is equivalent to the statistic

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\hat{\beta}}{\,\text{se}\,(\hat{\beta})},}
\end{array}$$

which has an asymptotic standard normal distribution.
:::

::: section
## A.2 Inference about a vector of unknown parameters {#appA.xhtml_secA_2 .head1}

The results in [Section A.1](#appA.xhtml_secA_1) can be extended to the
situation where *n* observations are used to estimate the values of *p*
unknown parameters, $\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\ldots$, *β*~*p*~. These
parameters can be assembled into a *p*-component vector,
$\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}$, and the corresponding likelihood function is
$L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})$. The maximum likelihood estimates of the
*p* unknown parameters are the values
${\hat{\beta}}_{1},{\hat{\beta}}_{2},\ldots,{\hat{\beta}}_{p}$, which
maximise $L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})$. They are therefore found by
solving the *p* equations

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\text{d}\log L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})}{\text{d}\beta_{j}}\left| {}_{\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}~ \right. = 0,}
\end{array}$$

for $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, simultaneously.

The vector formed from
${\hat{\beta}}_{1},{\hat{\beta}}_{2},\ldots,{\hat{\beta}}_{p}$ is
denoted $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$, and so the maximised likelihood
is $L(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})$. The efficient score for *β*~*j*~,
$j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{u(\beta_{j}) = \frac{\text{d}\log L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})}{\text{d}\beta_{j}},}
\end{array}$$

and these quantities can be assembled to give a *p*-component vector of
efficient scores, denoted
$\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})$. The vector of maximum
likelihood estimates is therefore such that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}) = \mathbf{0},}
\end{array}$$

where $\mathbf{0}$ is the $p \times 1$ vector of zeroes.

Now let the matrix $\mathbf{\mathbf{H}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})$ be the
$p \times p$ matrix of second partial derivatives of the log-likelihood
function, $\log L(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})$. The $(j,k)$ th
element of $\mathbf{\mathbf{H}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})$ is then

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\frac{\partial^{2}\log L(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})}{\partial\beta_{j}\partial\beta_{k}},}
\end{array}$$

for $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$, $k = 1,2,\ldots,p$, and
$\mathbf{\mathbf{H}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})$ is called the *Hessian
matrix*. The matrix

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}) = - \mathbf{\mathbf{H}}(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})}
\end{array}$$

is called the *observed information matrix*. The $(j,k)$ th element of
the corresponding *expected information matrix* is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{- \,\text{E}\,\left( \frac{\partial^{2}\log L(\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}})}{\partial\beta_{j}\partial\beta_{k}} \right).}
\end{array}$$

The variance-covariance matrix of the *p* maximum likelihood estimates,
${\hat{\beta}}_{1},{\hat{\beta}}_{2}$, ..., ${\hat{\beta}}_{p}$, written
$\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})$, can then be
approximated by the inverse of the observed information matrix,
evaluated at $\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}$, so that

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\,\text{var}\,(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}) \approx \mathbf{\mathbf{I}}^{- 1}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}).}
\end{array}$$

The square root of the $(j,j)$ th element of this matrix can be taken to
be the standard error of ${\hat{\beta}}_{j}$, for $j = 1,2,\ldots,p$.

The test statistics given in [Section A.1](#appA.xhtml_secA_1) can be
generalised to the multiparameter situation. Consider the test of the
null hypothesis that all the *β*-parameters in a fitted model are equal
to zero. The likelihood ratio test statistic is the value of

$$\begin{array}{r}
{2\left\{ {\log L(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}) - \log L(\mathbf{0})} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

the Wald test is based on

$$\begin{array}{r}
{{\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}'\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}(\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}})\,\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}},}
\end{array}$$

and the score test statistic is

$$\begin{array}{r}
{\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}'(\mathbf{0})\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}^{- 1}(\mathbf{0})\mathbf{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{0}).}
\end{array}$$

Each of these statistics has a chi-squared distribution on *p* d.f.
under the null hypothesis that $\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}} = \mathbf{0}$.

[]{#appA.xhtml_p498 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 498. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}In comparing alternative models, interest centres
on the hypothesis that some of the *β*-parameters in a model are equal
to zero. To test this hypothesis, the likelihood ratio test is the most
suitable, and so we only consider this procedure here. Suppose that a
model that contains $p + q$ parameters,
$\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\ldots,\beta_{p},\beta_{p + 1},\ldots,\beta_{p + q}$,
is to be compared with a model that only contains the *p* parameters
$\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\ldots,\beta_{p}$. This amounts to testing the null
hypothesis that the *q* parameters $\beta_{p + 1}$,
$\beta_{p + 2},\ldots,\beta_{p + q}$ in the model with $p + q$ unknown
parameters are all equal to zero. Let
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}_{1}$ denote the vector of estimates
under the model with $p + q$ parameters and
${\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}_{2}$ that for the model with just *p*
parameters. The likelihood ratio test of the null hypothesis that
$\beta_{p + 1} = \beta_{p + 2} = \cdots = \beta_{p + q} = 0$ in the
model with $p + q$ parameters is then based on the statistic

$$\begin{array}{r}
{2\left\{ {\log L({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}_{1}) - \log L({\hat{\mathbf{\mathbf{\beta}}}}_{2})} \right\},}
\end{array}$$

which has a chi-squared distribution on *q* d.f., under the null
hypothesis. This test forms the basis for comparing alternative models,
and was described in greater detail in [Section
3.5](#chapter3.xhtml_sec3_5) of [Chapter 3](#chapter3.xhtml).
:::
:::::

[]{#appB.xhtml}

:::::::: {.section .appendix role="doc-appendix"}
# []{#appB.xhtml_p499 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 499. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Appendix B]{.chapnum}[Additional data sets]{.chaptitle1} {#appB.xhtml_appB .chaplabel}

This appendix contains a number of data sets, together with some
suggestions for analyses that could be carried out. These data sets may
be downloaded from the publishers\'s web site, at the location given in
the preface.

::: section
## B.1 Chronic active hepatitis {#appB.xhtml_secB_1 .head1}

In a clinical trial described by [Kirk et al.
(1980)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib208){#appB.xhtml_brefbib208 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, 44 patients with chronic active hepatitis were
randomised to the drug prednisolone, or an untreated control group. The
survival time of the patients, in months, following admission to the
trial, was the response variable of interest. The data, which were given
in [Pocock (1983)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib300){#appB.xhtml_brefbib300
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, are shown in [Table
B.1](#appB.xhtml_tblB_1){#appB.xhtml_btblB_1}.

: [[[Table]{.label}
[B.1]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#appB.xhtml_btblB_1){.backlink
aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times of patients suffering from chronic
active hepatitis]{.table-title} {#tblB_1}

Summarise the data in terms of the estimated survivor function for each
treatment group. Compare the groups using the log-rank and Wilcoxon
tests. Fit Cox and Weibull proportional hazards model to determine the
significance of the treatment effect. Compare the results from these
different analyses in terms of the significance of the treatment effect,
and, for the model-based analyses, the estimated hazard ratio and
corresponding 95% confidence limits. Obtain a log-cumulative hazard plot
of the data, and comment on which method of analysis is the most
appropriate.
:::

::: section
## B.2 Recurrence of bladder cancer {#appB.xhtml_secB_2 .head1}

In a placebo-controlled trial concerning bladder cancer, conducted by
the Veterans Administration Cooperative Urological Research Group,
patients with superficial bladder tumours first had their tumour removed
transurethrally. They were then randomised to a placebo, or to the
chemotherapeutic agent, thiotepa. The initial number of tumours in each
patient, and []{#appB.xhtml_p500 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 500. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}the diameter of the largest of these, was recorded
at the time of randomisation. The original data set, included in
[Andrews and Herzberg
(1985)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib24){#appB.xhtml_brefbib24 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, gave the times to up to nine tumour recurrences,
but the data presented in [Table
B.2](#appB.xhtml_tblB_2){#appB.xhtml_btblB_2} refer to the time to the
first recurrence, in months. Patients who had not experienced a
recurrence by the end of the follow-up period contribute censored
observations. The variables in the data set are as follows:

  ------------ --------------------------------------------------
  *Patient*:   Patient number (1--86)
  *Time*:      Survival time in months
  *Status*:    Status of patient (0 = censored, 1 = recurrence)
  *Treat*:     Treatment group (1 = placebo, 2 = thiotepa)
  *Init*:      Initial number of tumours
  *Size*:      Diameter of largest initial tumour in cm
  ------------ --------------------------------------------------

  [B.2]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#appB.xhtml_btblB_2){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Time to first recurrence of a tumour in bladder
  cancer patients]{.table-title} {#tblB_2}

Using a Cox proportional hazards model, determine whether the recurrence
times depend on the explanatory variables *Init* and *Size*. After
adjusting for relevant variables, examine the significance of the
treatment effect. Check if there is any evidence of an interaction
between treatment group and the two explanatory variables. Using
model-checking diagnostics, comment on the suitability of the fitted
model. In particular, by fitting a suitable time-dependent variable,
test the assumption of proportional hazards with respect to treatment.
Estimate the hazard ratio for the treatment effect and give a 95%
confidence interval for the ratio. Compare this estimate with that
obtained from fitting a Weibull proportional hazards model.
:::

::: section
## B.3 Survival of black ducks {#appB.xhtml_secB_3 .head1}

In the first year of a study on the movements and overwintering survival
of black ducks, *Anas rubripes*, conducted by the US Fish and Wildlife
Service, 50 female black ducks from two locations in New Jersey were
captured and fitted with radios. The ducks were captured over a period
of about four weeks from 8 November 1983 to 14 December 1983, and
included 31 hatch-year birds, that is, birds born during the previous
breeding season, and 19 birds of at least one year of age. The body
weight and wing length were measured for each duck. The status of each
duck, that is, whether it was alive, missing or dead, was recorded daily
from the date of release until 15 February 1984, when the study was
terminated. The data set, which is included in [Hand et al.
(1994)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib166){#appB.xhtml_brefbib166 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, contains the following variables:

  ----------- -------------------------------------------------------------------
  *Time*:     Survival time in days
  *Status*:   Status of bird (0 = alive or missing, 1 = dead)
  *Age*:      Age group (0 = hatch-year bird, 1 = bird aged $\geqslant 1$ year)
  *Weight*:   Weight of bird in g
  *Length*:   Length of wing in mm
  ----------- -------------------------------------------------------------------

The values of each of these variables for the ducks in the study are
shown in [Table B.3](#appB.xhtml_tblB_3){#appB.xhtml_btblB_3}.

  *Time*   *Status*   *Age*   *Weight*   *Length*   *Time*   *Status*   *Age*   *Weight*   *Length*

  : [[[Table]{.label}
  [B.3]{.ordinal}]{.tablenum}](#appB.xhtml_btblB_3){.backlink
  aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. "
  role="doc-backlink"} [Survival times of 50 black ducks]{.table-title}
  {#tblB_3}

Using a proportional hazards model, examine the effect of age, weight
and length on survival time. Investigate whether the coefficients of the
explanatory variables, *Weight* and *Length*, differ for the ducks in
each age group. Is there any evidence that non-linear functions of the
variables *Weight* and *Length* are needed in the model?
:::

::: section
## B.4 Bone marrow transplantation {#appB.xhtml_secB_4 .head1}

Following the treatment of leukaemia, patients often undergo a bone
marrow transplant in order to help bring their blood cells back to a
normal level. A potentially fatal side effect of this is
graft-versus-host disease, in which the transplanted cells attack the
host cells. []{#appB.xhtml_p501 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 501. Table on this page. "
role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#appB.xhtml_p502 .pagebreak
aria-label=" page 502. " role="doc-pagebreak"}In a study described by
[Bagot et al. (1988)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib28){#appB.xhtml_brefbib28
.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, 37 patients who were in complete
remission from acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) or acute lymphocytic
leukaemia (ALL), or in the chronic phase of chronic myeloid leukaemia
(CML), received a non-depleted allogeneic bone marrow transplant. The
age of the bone marrow donor, and whether or not the donor had
previously been pregnant, was recorded, together with the age of the
recipient, their type of leukaemia, an index of mixed epidermal
lymphocyte reactions, and whether or not the recipient developed
graft-versus-host disease. The variables in this data set are as
follows:

  ------------ -----------------------------------------------
  *Patient*:   Patient number (1--37)
  *Time*:      Survival time in days
  *Status*:    Status of patient (0 = alive, 1 = dead)
  *Rage*:      Age of patient in years
  *Dage*:      Age of donor in years
  *Type*:      Type of leukaemia (1 = AML, 2 = ALL, 3 = CML)
  *Preg*:      Donor pregnancy (0 = no, 1 = yes)
  *Index*:     Index of cell-lymphocyte reactions
  *Gvhd*:      Graft-versus-host disease (0 = no, 1 = yes)
  ------------ -----------------------------------------------

The data, which were also given in [Altman
(1991)](#refbib.xhtml_refbib12){#appB.xhtml_brefbib12 .biblioref
role="doc-biblioref"}, are presented in [Table
B.4](#appB.xhtml_tblB_4){#appB.xhtml_btblB_4}.

  *Patient*   *Time*   *Status*   *Rage*   *Dage*   *Type*   *Preg*   *Index*   *Gvhd*

  24           112     1          33       43       3        0        1.50      1
